The kneading and letting them sit instructions are pretty vital as well as having hands wet with cold water. This was quick and easy with inexpensive and easily found ingredients. I love Sohla’s recipes!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I've never commented on NYT cooking before, but this recipe has left me no choice it's that good. I cook with Japanese curry roux blocks often. The chicken meatballs take it to the next level. The ginger and garlic bring a freshness that balances the richness of the roux perfectly. Plus they reheat so much better in the microwave than, say, cubed chicken thighs. It's a great meal prep option.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: This is truly a 5/5 star recipe.  I only made one modification suggested by others - using 3/4 cup of panko bread crumbs.  The ground chicken is usually very moist and this was the right amount of panko to permit the mixture to make meatballs that hold together.  The meatballs had great flavor and the medium-spicy Golden Curry roux I used had great flavor as well.  I used Japanese sweet potato which seems perfect for this recipe as opposed to the typical sweet potato varities.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: @Matt I also used the Japanese sweet potatoes and they’re perfect. All if you have a Trader Joe’s they usually carry them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
Made this for my boomer parents who loved it! Used pork instead of chicken and bok choi instead of spinach as I had both of those and didn’t want to go to the shops. 

1/2 cup panko crumbs was plenty for the ground pork.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Sohla is the GOAT (curry)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I added 1/3 of a grated apple and some chopped carrots while I let the potato’s simmer and it turned out great!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Upped the panko as many others did and baked the meatballs for about 20 min at 425 flipping once so they got some browning on both sides. Otherwise cooked as directed and it was absolutely delicious! Will be making again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: This was pretty good. Texturally, it was all too soft without variation. We agreed with another poster that the sugar either needed to be reduced or eliminated. Too sweet. Also, needed acid. We increased the panko to about 3/4 C and probably could have gone to 1 C, and used chicken stock, not water. Next time, maybe add pickled ginger to the meatballs and pickled ginger juice to the stock. The sweet potatoes were pretty, but added to the overall softness. I like the idea of carrots instead. We topped it with sweet potato chips, which was a good idea.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
my husband and teenage sons don't like sweet potatoes. so I used a small honeynut squash (mostly b/c I like squash and sweet potatoes) and added cut-up fingerling potatoes and chunks of peeled apples (potatoes and apples typically go in Japanese curries). for greens, threw in bok choy and some leftover broccoli rabe. YUM!

the meatballs were the star - followed the tips to increase panko to 3/4 cup and let mixture rest for 10-15 mjns before forming the balls. I also chilled the meatballs on a sheet pan covered in parchment for about an hour. to keep them intact as much as possible when cooking them, use plenty of oil, give them enough room, so fry in two batches, don't turn until they release on their own (2 mins for the first "side"), then also brown a second side and even one or two more surfaces, so they are kind of "sealed." they'll finish cooking in the curry. 😋😋😋

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
I haven’t been this happy with a recipe in a long time. The meatballs were soft and flavorful, the sauce was luscious. I took Mark P’s advice and did 3/4 cups of panko - I also stuck the balls in the fridge to chill while I prepped the veg for the curry. 

Instead of the sweet potato, I did a mix celery root, carrot, and turnip. The swiss chard at the end was chef’s kiss. Thank you for this wonderful recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Yum!! So good. I only had one bunch chard so I added a chopped-up cabbage (added at same time as the potatoes), which I actually liked more than the chard. I also used 3/4 C panko in meatballs and broth instead of water, as commenters suggested. Next time (def will make again), I think I’ll try just cabbage and maybe peppers — no need for the sweetness of the potatoes, though I love sweet potatoes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
I made my own roux :
	•	2 Tbsp butter or oil
	•	2 Tbsp flour
	•	1 Tbsp curry powder 
	•	1 tsp garam masala
	•	1 tsp soy sauce
	•	1 tsp honey
	•	Optional: ½ tsp grated ginger or garlic
I followed the rest of the recipe as is and my meatballs were intact and perfect. I used a lot of avocado oil and got it very warm before putting the meatballs  a dutch oven. Delicious! 

How:
Make a roux with olive oil + flour, then stir in spices, soy sauce, and sweetener. Add directly to your curry and simmer until thick.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
Good, but probably won't make again. It's Japanese curry, so the real difference is in swapping out the meat and veggies.  As to those changes:

-Meatballs were good, but not really worth the added effort over chicken/beef for me personally.
-Sweet potatoes are great in this!
-Spinach is really good the first day, less so after.  Will probably sub in a heartier green.

Solid variation on a standard Japanese curry, and I'll def. integrate some of what I liked here into my standard version.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and simple dish. My first time using Japanese curry roux cubes (Golden Curry medium hot). I added the entire 3.5pz box, and the flavor is strong but good. It also allows for adding water, which I did as the greens made it all too thick. One caveat: the chicken meatballs are definitely wet and struggled to hold round. It was tempting to add more panko.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: The curry roux blocks had little actual curry spices, so I used S&B curry powder along with a Japanese togarashi hot chili paste added when sautéing the onions and garlic. Opened the fridge and threw in broccolini, chard with some of the chicken left over from last night’s dinner. Chicken broth was used to make the sauce. Really lovely meal over rice and bound to be cooked again. Thanks for the inspiration!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I’m new to curry flavored dishes. I would like to make this dish but don’t like anything too hot/spicy. Should I stay away from this recipe? TX

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: @Janet Finkel most Japanese curry bricks come in several levels of spice- the Golden brand, which is available at my supermarket, has a mild option that has no heat at all. I say try it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
Japanese style curry is usually not very spicy. It's usually more sweet and mild. If you search  the internet for japanese curry roux or mix you should  find some reviews that compare different brands.

If you substitute regular curry for Japanese curry like several commenters here have done then it will be spicier.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
@Katie
I prefer the mild, which has great flavor as followed. We had Gochugaru Korean chile flakes at the table.  Choose your own adventure!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
This was fine. Using homemade curry roux would be much better. Check out Just One Coolbook’s recipe. But the store bought stuff is much faster. 

Also the meatballs were ok. They held their shape easily, but weren’t special in any way.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: This was very good! I increased the bread crumbs to 3/4 cup as others suggested. The meatballs still seemed too wet to stay together when frying so I instead baked them in the oven at 350 F for 20 minutes. This worked well for me and was an easier prep.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Prepared as written. Delicious. Just the right amount of kick from the roux. Definitely will make again

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: We thought this was quite delicious, but alas it won’t be a reapeat.  After dinner I happened to glance at the ingredients in Golden Curry.  Palm oil, hydrogenated rapeseed oil, MSG, disodium this and disodium that…I’m also not sure if the chicken meatballs were cooked all the way through.  Hope we emerge unscathed.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: @Laurel W. There are products that don’t contain those ingredients, in case you’re interested in trying the recipe again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
@andi
What are the other curry products that don’t have MSG?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: @andi Would love to know the healthier options because I did like the recipe a lot.  Though maybe it was the taste of all those forbidden ingredients I enjoyed!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Make the meatballs first and precook on a sheet pan do say 12 min at 400-425. Then toss into the curry once the sweet potatoes are cooked and simmer for a few. Toss in spinach at the end to wilt last minute.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Upped the breadcrumbs to 1c and added 2 more cups of water after the roux. So good!! Definitely making again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
As others have said, you probably don't have to double the bread crumbs, just let the mixture sit for 15 minutes or so. I also think mixing up the curry brick in broth ahead of time is a very good idea. Made it super easy to incorporate.

Was surprised by how not overly salty this was. Will make again probably, but it didn't knock my socks off.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I put the meatballs in the freezer for a bit, which helped, along with additional panko. I also added some carrots, as well as a bit more water. This recipe takes more time than listed - anything with meatballs does, normally - but it was good and I would make it again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: This might be my favorite recipe from NYT. I don’t have the biggest Dutch oven so my meatballs were a little bigger, which required an additional 5 minutes of cooking at the end to make sure they were cooked through. One of the few recipes where I didn’t have to prep everything at the beginning: you can cut the onion and sweet potato while the meatballs are cooking, and clean and trim the spinach/chard while everything is simmering.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely make this. I used carrots instead of sweet potatoes and upped the bread crumbs. Totally delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight and it was delicious. Followed other reader’s suggestions and upped the panko to 3/4 for every pound of ground chicken. Also ground chicken thighs myself, highly recommend. I tripled the meatballs, as I had a family pack of Vermont Curry to use up. Added 3 tablespoons of hoisin to the meatballs, and baked for 16 mins at 400. Used mushrooms and chard, out of sweet potatoes. Also added ginger and garlic to the sautéed onions, and put in another 2 tablespoons of hoisin sauce to the curry. Served over fresh udon noodles. So going on the rotation.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I agree with everyone else about the Panko not being enough.  But I also added tofu to the mix, as well as chicken consume.  That added some good nutritional content, without sacrificing the flavor.  I also cooked the meatballs in my air fryer, and increased the amount of liquid in the curry.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I mostly made this according to the directions, though I was out of panko (no idea how that happened) so I used crushed saltines instead and added in bok choy in place of spinach and upped the vegetable matter with the addition of carrots and green beans. I also used a small disher to make the meatballs because they were a bit wet and it worked like a charm. I also put the meatballs in the oven after I browned them as recommended in another post. It was delicious and the meatballs were perfect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Excellent. The meatballs come out tender and delicately flavorful (go for white pepper if you have it). The fond from browning them first really deepens the flavor of the onions and sauce. And this recipe really lends itself to frozen spinach — I dumped in a 12-ounce bag of leaf spinach. I had no trouble keeping the meatballs intact with 1/2 cup of breadcrumbs, but you do need to resist the urge to flip them too soon. It also helps to use tongs (carefully) to position and turn them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
Amazing! A few adjustments
- increased breadcrumbs to nearly 1 cup per earlier suggestions
- let the meatballs sit for 15 minutes 
- increased water added to 5.5 cups and also used 7/8 of a golden curry package (used the medium spice)
- used Tuscan kale instead of spinach since that’s what I had in the fridge and used two sweet potatoes

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Used this recipe to make curry roux. It uses a can of "Oriental Spice Mix" that has no salt and no MSG. I bought it on Amazon. I cut down the cayenne, but I don't like it very spicy: https://www.justonecookbook.com/simple-chicken-curry/

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I couldn’t find the roux cubes so I followed the link in the recipe and made my own.  They were very easy to make and they came out really well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Used 3/4c panko and let the mixture sit for a bit really helps make the meatballs easier to make. I needed an extra cup of water otherwise I found the curry too thick and salty. Tried it with and without the sugar in the meatball mixture and while you can’t taste it I think it adds some balance. This recipe readily accepts extra Swiss chard and sweet potato!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Part Indian curry, part stew, 100% delicious. Cooking the meatballs on a sheet pan in a hot oven solves the problem of disintegrating meatballs—just set them in the sauce at the very end, or simply spoon the curry over the meatballs and rice as you serve. Double the recipe if you have any hopes for leftovers.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I've made this several times now, and it's a favorite! I leave out the sugar because it makes the meatballs weirdly sweet, and with the sweet potato it's too much. By all means, up the panko to 3/4 c and they do hold together but they're delicate. When little bits break off and carmelize it's delicious. Chop the curry bar finely before adding to the pot and it melts beautifully, and I always need the extra half cup of water (probably because my veg measurements are always inflated).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I’m finding it difficult to buy a no msg curry.  Msg leaves me a terrible headache the next morning.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I used a delicata for my winter squash of choice. Lovely flavor and texture!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: My family loves it.  I'm not sure how difficult it is to find the Japanese curry paste where you all are, but I've found it at the super Fry's in Phoenix and at the Mekong Supermarket in Mesa. White guy tip at Mekong, it isn't in the aisle that says curry. It's 2 aisles over to the west and half an aisle to the north on the right.  There are several heat options in 7 oz packages that are split in to two 3.5oz packs.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have Japanese roux blocks but did have regular curry powder (not a turmeric heavy one). here's how i Japan-i-fied it. for 3oz final paste, melt 3tbsp butter, simmer with 3tbsp flour until nutty brown. Add 3tbsp curry powder and mix into paste. add 4tbsp soy sauce, 3tbsp apple sauce or grated apples, and a few dashes of cinnamon and nutmeg. Voila! sweet cozy curry paste :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I used the SB brand roux, 2 blocks (out of 5 for the package) about 36 grams or 1.3 oz was enough.  Using a pair of scissors to cut the roux over the pot worked well.  This was delicious, but it did take me a lot longer than 15 minutes of prep time.  I guess I am a slow chopper, have two kittens hovering over me didn't help.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I baked the meatballs at 350F for 45 mins. I added a tbsp of olive oil to the turkey mince to keep it moist.  It was delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I read other comments before cooking and increased panko bread crumbs to 3/4 of a cup. I increased ginger and garlic as well. The texture was great. I left the mix stand for about 10 minutes before shaping into meatballs and cooking. They were perfect. I wasn’t able to find Japanese curry roux so I substituted green curry paste. I used coconut milk instead of water. So quite different from the recipe! It was delicious! I loved the meatballs and it worked with the curry. I’d make it again this same way until I am able to find Japan curry roux.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Double the bread crumbs. Mix curry cubes first with a little hot water to crush and mix

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: It was delicious and I will certainly make it again. No problems with making the meatballs or that it  was to moist. Perfection in the Dutch oven

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Upped the panko to 3/4 cups per many comments and it worked out well. I had carrots lying around so used them instead of sweet potatoes - I think they take longer to cook even though I tried cutting them not too big. Used lacinato kale for greens. I was surprised by how much the curry block thickens the dish! Used mild curry for my toddler but I sprinkled some cayenne on my plate and it worked well. Next time I’d use less sugar in the meatballs as the whole dish was pretty sweet - I know it’s part of Japanese flavor profiles but we don’t have sweet teeth. Great recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Really amazing meal.   I added some sleeks since I had  them.   Added a little miso for extra flavor boost.  For meatballs, I followed advice here with just under a cup of panko, but also added a touch of heavy cream for fat, and put them in fridge for ten minutes to solidify a bit.    All in all, a really good recipe and tasty dinner.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Doubled the recipe & used my mixer stand and a small ice cream scoop to form the meatballs. Much easier. Served for 8 & had yummy leftovers. Will add more veg next time cuz that’s how I roll.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: flavorful but time consuming to make as others have mentioned. the tips to increase bread crumbs and let the meatball mixture rest for 15-20 mins was helpful. overall I think this recipe was worth trying but for a quicker, simpler meal, I might prefer it with tofu or chicken instead of the meatballs. the garlic and ginger are a nice touch though! leftovers reheat well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, filling, and green! Served with a healthy amount of rice, it gave us 5 servings (and we are hungry people).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I made this in my work, which worked really well for frying the meatballs and adding the huge bunch of greens at the end. Some of my eaters are not fans of sweet potato or pumpkin so I used potatoes, carrots and daikon instead. I also subbed pork for chicken, as it's what was on hand.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was a complete winner! I did have to make some substitutions; my grocery store didn't have mature spinach or swiss chard, so I bought kale. The ground chicken available was "extra lean" and extra expensive, so I bought a higher-fat ground turkey instead. The meatballs were very wet, but I was able to cook them no problem and they came out tender and delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: @Denise You can make your own ground chicken. I did this for the first time for this recipe. Just pulse cubed chicken breasts a few times in a blender or food processor (I put the breast in the freezer for about 20 minutes beforehand).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: @Denise You can make your own ground chicken, which I did for the first time for this recipe. Pulse cubed chicken in a blender or food processor a few times. I put the breasts in the freezer for about 20 minutes beforehand.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Wow this was a hit. I cook regularly for guests and they commented on the aroma and flavor. The meatballs were really soft and sticky to work with initially but browning was easy and they tasted amazing. I would not change a thing about this recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I may have made mine a bit too spicy but delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Cooked as written, but used kale as my greens and put them in with the sweet potatoes. I liked the combination of the curry sauce with the veggies. The meatballs were tender but overly salty with the curry sauce, I’ll reduce the soy sauce next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I loved this recipe, but I was a bit turned off by the ingredients in the Golden Curry brand.  I ended up making my own curry sauce and it turned out to be delicious and similar in texture and flavor.  To do this, I ended up adding chopped potatoes, corn starch, flour, a little bouillon paste, chicken broth, fresh and powdered ginger, chopped garlic, and garam masala.  My vegetarian daughter even made an exception to her rule of no meat.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: This looks delicious, but I can’t find Japanese curry that doesn’t have MSG.  Any suggestions?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: No suggestions. MSG is completely safe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: @Ella Thank you!  I’m go for it & use the pkg.  Recipe sounds too good to pass up

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I agree with the person who said MSG is fine, but in the note, there is a link to a recipe for making your own curry brick. A much bigger pain than just buying the stuff, but doable if you're trying to avoid something specific.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: This was an instant hit. I might reduce the size of the sweet potato if I want to fit more greens.  Used the green curry box, not so spicy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious and a huge it. (I doubled the panko as recommended) I think in future I will fold in greens before adding the meatballs, it was difficult not to break apart the tender meatballs with all those greens. Served with Japanese pickles. So good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe was a hit in our house. Like others suggested I upped the panko to 3/4 cup. 
We have a lot of Korean markets in our area and like the Ottogi Mild curry. It comes in a bag, no need to break up any cubes. Just sprinkle it in the pot and stir gently. It mixes very easily.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night, and definitely going to add more breadcrumbs to the mix. I added around 1/3 cup more, but the meatballs were still too wet and fell apart while pan frying.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: WOW this was good. I followed the tip on using 3/4 cup of breadcrumbs and recommend that others do the same.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is so good! It was quick and easy and is healthy. My husband loves Japanese curry so this is about to become a very regular staple for us!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is AMAZING! I made one change to it by using Impossible Ground Beef rather than ground chicken and it was still great. I would recommend watching the cook on the meatballs if you do that though, when I left them sit for 3 mins they were a smidge burnt. Still really good with the curry, but something to be aware of.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: The curry roux came ~6.5 oz, so I doubled the recipe. It was delicious, and my husband said, " This is a keeper. " I hardly ever make the same dish twice, but I will abide by his wishes. I used the Java brand and it was very flavorful and a teensy bit spicy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
Personal mods: 

- increase panko to 3/4 cup
- add carrot chunks, shiitake mushrooms
- use veggie or chicken stock (2:1 stock to water ratio)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recommendations to double the panko.  This was delicious!  I couldn't find Japanese curry so I used Massamam curry with a bit of coconut milk.  Really great!  Even the family members who are foodies loved it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I judge recipes based on how many spoonfuls I eat directly from the pot instead of a proper plate/bowl, and let’s just say I lost count with this one. I followed the recipe exactly except I only used half of an 11 oz container of spinach, which seemed like plenty. Next time, I’d add a serrano or jalapeño to the meatballs, as I like a little spice in a curry. I used the S&B Golden Curry brand roux. Due to the sodium content, I don’t think this will go into the regular rotation, but it will definitely be revisited at some point. Thank you Sohla!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: added swiss chard. upped the bread crumbs like other commenters recommended. DELISH

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: This is a keeper!  But took me 1.5 hours not one hour.  And I worked steadily!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Made it as written and it was excellent! Let the meatball mixture sit for about 10-15 min while I prepped the other ingredients before rolling into balls — definitely helped with the binding and marination. Will probably double the batch next time and freeze half for an even easier meal.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Try 3/4 c panko skip sugar did carrot instead

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Great quick weeknight meal! We made as written, but added shiitake mushrooms (put in to lightly saute before adding sweet potato) and it adds a nice extra punch to the dish!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
fantastic :) definitely a staple for me now. 
yes to using 3/4 to 1 cup of panko breadcrumbs and letting the mixture sit for 15ish minutes. I add a bit of tofu (about half of a 15oz container) to the meatballs, drained, pressed, and crumbled up, partly for some additional protein & partly to stretch out the ground chicken a bit, I think it works very well as a binder, haven't had them fall apart on me at all

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Meatballs were flavorful and delicious. I served them separate from the curry. I would make these again and incorporate into other dishes!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Oh my goodness, this is soooo delicious. My son, who was cooking the curry, texted me at work and said “Mom, this is amazing, it’s going into the regular meal rotation!!” At our local Asian market, we found the best roux to be the Java curry, hot. It is rich and adds some spice but not too much.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
After giving this recipe my first attempt (always stay very close to recipe on first try), I would try ¾ cup bread crumbs and opt for the spicier roux rather than the mild. Great suggestion on mixing in the roux in smaller bowl before moving to Dutch oven (I also need a much larger Dutch oven!)

All in all a massive success and this attempt garnered my 11yr old daughter to confess it is her all-time favourite dinner I’ve made! Wow!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
Wow… fantastic. Only change was using 3.9 oz of curry roux ( half a pack of golden curry as others have mentioned)

Otherwise I followed the recipe to a tee and it was superb. Will definitely make again. Thanks Sohla El-Waylay

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: dissolving the curry brick in a mixture of hot water and sake made a big difference in bringing everything together once it was added to the pot. Used a chicken bone broth instead of water ....bit more protein and gave the curry nice body. Those mentioning the need for added panko might not have kneaded the panko with aromatics to make a paste before adding the chicken and egg because I did not face this issue .....good dish!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
10/10! Not sure why other commenters said their meatball mixture was too wet. I had no trouble shaping them. And as noted, keeping my hands wet helped greatly in preventing the mix from sticking to them. Also, if you're struggling to get the Swiss chard cooked down, just cover the pot let steam help you, it practically disappears into the mix.

Never used curry roux before but WOW it's delicious (used Vermont Curry). This will be a great meal to make again when the weather gets cold.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
Made this using the ground chicken 
YUM
going to try this again but using tofu instead of the chicken

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: This is my first Japanese curry but won’t be the last - family enjoyed it.  I used turkey instead of chicken for the meatballs and baked them instead of pan frying while preparing the rest of the dish.  I used a “mild” curry, but would try the medium next time as it could have used more heat.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Nobody liked.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I used 3/4 cup of panko and refrigerated the uncooked meatballs while I worked on the rest of the prep. No problems with cooking the meatballs, though I cooked them longer than directed. 16oz of spinach would have been 4 bunches. I bought 3. Washed 2 bunches (abt 8oz) and saw that was plenty. Actually added 2/3 of what I washed. It was still too much. When I tried folding the spinach in (one large handful at a time), some of the meatballs got mashed. I should have added the meatballs last maybe?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I don’t understand the high reviews, this was extremely bland- I think the brand of curry roux really matters here, any grocery store curry isn’t going to cut it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody:
absolutely increase to 3/4 cups of panko
chill ping pong size meatballs for 15 mins
don't be shy coating pan with oil
meatballs will release from pan, have faith! similar to browning a chicken thigh
follow recipe and method as written
I subbed sweet potatoes for carrots and red potatoes (both chunked)
also, meatballs will be moist, mix as directed, as opposed to some recipes that warn against over mixing

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely love this dish. First time made it as written. Second time subbed carrots for the sweet potatoes and komatsuna (a type of Japanese green) for the spinach. Perfect low effort high reward dish!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Ran out of time and motivation so bought trader joes turkey meatballs. Browned them then sauted onion, added regular potato but added carrots.  The boiling ensured then are tender then the chopped up curry block and spinach last.  Whole family loved it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Used 3/4c panko and let the meatballs mixture sit a spell before shaping. Came out perfect. I found this very salty and wish there had been more curry sauce. It was a massive hit with my hu

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Used 3/4c panko (per everyone’s advice) and let the meatballs mixture sit a spell before shaping. They came out perfect. I found this very salty and wish there had been more curry sauce. However, it was a massive hit with my generally very picky husband though and he just asked me to make it again. Will experiment with using slightly less curry roux/more water. I also skipped the sugar and will add it back in next time to see if that mellows and balances out the perceived saltiness.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I was excited to make this Japanese curry.  The chicken "meatballs" were really good.  Love yams and greens too.  But the Golden cubes left a really unpleasant aftertaste and kind of ruined the dish. I would have preferred if the recipe included a list of spices to add fresh instead of using a commercial mix. Will add some red pepper flakes tonight to see if that helps - there's a lot leftover!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic recipe.  Made it exactly as written.  First time I had used the Japanese curry roux.  Didn’t even realize that was such an easy to find ingredient.    Will be using that again for sure!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Adding a new recipe to my “cozy semi-instant meatball recipe” canon - in good company with rice-a-roni meatballs (the OG recipe from the side of the beef RaR box). I let the meatballs sit in the fridge for 20 minutes or so before browning, I think that helped keep them together. Took others’ advice and whisked the roux with some hot broth before dumping it in. Garnished with sesame seeds - didn’t add much but they made it look nice. Incredibly delicious and satisfying and my first experience with Japanese curry - going to get this stocked in the pantry and looking forward to making a double batch when I have friends over this winter! P.S. I loved the sugar in the meatballs.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: purchased  16oz of baby spinach leaves and only used 6oz.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Substituted ground turkey for the ground chicken and added juice of a full lime at the end as it needed some acid. Otherwise made as written- meatballs held together well with the 1/2 cup of panko. Use a good amount of oil and cooked first side a bit more, maybe 5mins, to brown and release from pan. Would make again (with the other half of my golden curry packet)!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Ingredient prep took 45 minutes, overall took 1.5 hours. Cooked meatballs unpleasantly soft (used 1 cup panko). Overall okay, but chunked chicken thighs are just as good and much easier. Won't repeat.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I used butternut squash and baby spinach. I also found the meatballs wet, and many of them broke apart, but I didn’t mind at all. I added a whole cup of water after adding the greens to thin it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and easy. I used Impossible instead of chicken and the 1/2c of panko worked great. I also baked them in the oven instead of pan frying. I think it made everything come together quicker too since I was working on the sauce while the meatballs were cooking. I added 1/4lb of shiitake mushrooms also because I had them. I will definitely make this again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I made this with impossible meat, gluten free bread crumbs and coconut aminos instead of soy sauce and it turned out amazing. Thanks for the recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Thank you for this suggestions! Sounds delicious.@Mark P

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Is it possible to cook the meatballs first and then add them to the curry mixture?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Yum! The meatballs would be great in other dishes too. I’m thinking of a banh mi style sandwich. I subbed carrots for sweet potatoes and used frozen spinach.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Delicious flavors! I prefer Japanese curry powder, so I incorporated it with the onions and oil, reduced the water to 2 cups, and added a potato starch slurry before adding the meatballs to the pan. Came out great!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious and my whole family loved it. I had ground turkey instead of chicken and it worked very well. I doubled the recipe to do some meal prep sharing with friends and everyone raved about this dish.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. I used a full thing of "Medium" curry sticks as well as a lot of the "Mild". First time ever making meatballs - so they were not pretty. But, the flavors were OUT OF THIS WORLD.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I tried the method of baking the meatballs per another reviewer's comment. I don't think this is wise to do as the meatballs will fall apart nor do they get that browned flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Made as written except followed other readers' lead and doubled the panko.  While I suspect that the curry roux (S & B Golden Curry, medium hot) is intended for beef or lamb, it works with the chicken meatballs.  For future experiments, virtually any vegetable would probably work here.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: This was really flavorful. You have to like curry obviously. My meatballs were a little sticky but they turned out ok though not quite round. I didn't have as much thick liquid as in the picture but I still felt the dish was outstanding. I served it with brown rice.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I know lots of people had issues with the breadcrumbs but it was fine for mine, you just really do have to knead! I used twice as much butternut squash as recommended and it was still good, delicious and fun to make :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: Another delicious Japanese curry recipe! I followed closely except I added a few dashes of fish sauce to the meatballs and threw in a handful of chopped cilantro in with the spinach. Served over udon noodles and we have plenty of leftovers for the week. Will make again! Served with some cold sake and it was the perfect Sunday meal.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: I might just keep that meatball recipe and make them without the curry because they are that good! I was too lazy to shape them after work so I just used two spoons to portion the mixture into the pot, the craggly bits got extra crispy and brown :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
commentBody: This one is a keeper.  I taught middle school in Japan in 1990’s and curry was a school lunch staple.  It’s economical, relatively easy and very comforting.  Based on comments I added about an extra 1/4 cup of panko and let the meatballs absorb the panko for about half an hour.  This recipe took me about an hour not counting the rest time for the meatballs.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was good, but it needed a little tweaking to make it less sweet and give it more of a queso flavor. I added extra nutritional yeast, a bit of white vinegar to give it extra tang, and a can of green chilis. It was delicious and a good healthy alternative to normal cheese dip!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was skeptical at first but this was surprisingly good! And healthy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I add white miso in addition to the nooch to give it more umami depth and make it more cheese-like in flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not bad, but not queso. I can see using this as a sandwich spread if you keep it on the thicker side.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Tried this for the first time last week and I have been raving to vegan and non-vegan friends!

Made it as is and used it as a warm drizzle on some crispy black bean tacos I made. 

Saved the rest (about a quart) and used to make a very quick stovetop vegan mac and cheese through the week. Added some chopped pickled jalapeños and a touch of the pickle brine to the queso while the pasta boiled. 

I adjusted some seasonings to taste (added more nooch and a touch more MSG) but overall a really solid interpretation of a vegan queso. 

It’s now a part of my rotating vegan sauces that make meals more interesting!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I doubled the recipe and cooked everything in a 4 quart stockpot.  It was pretty good, I had to cook the mixture a lot longer to make the cashews soft enough.  Same texture as queso, tasted somewhat like cheese.  I used a combination of nutritional yeast powder and flakes.  I steamed some broccoli and poured a bunch of queso over it. I enjoyed it.  I'll make it again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I liked it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Made a version of this for some vegan mac and "cheese." I added three seeded, dried New Mexico chili peppers and around 2 tbs white miso. Only used one garlic clove (not sure why). Next time will use more. The chili peppers added some good zip. 

I made 12 oz of GF Jovial pasta and I have enough leftover sauce for at least another 12-oz box.

Overall, a good recipe. I'll make it again and will experiment some. Maybe more chili peppers. Might also try a little more oil.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was actually pretty convincing as queso! It probably took like a quarter cup of salt though, haha (not really, but I aggressively salted and blended it like 5 times before it tasted right)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I make a variation of this every day (ordered something like 45 pounds of organic pea protein too to save in money, if not space!) 

(1) oat milk (any milk is fine obvi) 
(2) 2 dates 
(3) 1 ripe frozen banana 
(4) 1-2 scoops of pea protein (we have a ton right now, see alone,  so I’m drunk with power here) 
(5) cocao powder 
(6) splash of vanilla 
(7) salt! (Adds SO much!) and 
(8) mesquite bean powder (makes it taste more like a malt!) 

…. BLEND! Then add some (9) ice and blend some more! (This lets the ice not melt the first time around). 

Mesquite, and good dates, are more common in Phoenix, where we lived for a year. I recommend mesquite and especially good dates as much as I do NOT recommend Phoenix.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @MaddyChaddy dying lol. My sister moved to Phoenix about 6 months ago and is already making plans to escape.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this as written except I used regular milk instead of buttermilk because that’s what I had lying around and honestly, it was the best smoothie I’d had in a long time! Totally delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used regular milk and added a shot of espresso. Tasty! Didn't include flax seeds.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I make a version of this with low-fat Kefir instead of buttermilk. Recommend.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Loved this.
The dates add that idea of sticky, sweet glaze, slightly hardened after cooling. The ground flaxseed is the idea of the slightly gritty, crunchy inside of the baked roll’s cinnamon, sugar inside. I see both as almost necessary for the texture, which is truly a cinnamon roll in liquid form.

Taste-wise, I found I preferred to mix milk in with more yogurt instead of buttermilk, but I see that as a personal preference thing, not a recipe fault. For folks looking for a sweeter dessert smoothie adding brown sugar (or maple syrup!) might be necessary. I found that with a very ripe banana, no more sweetness is needed. What a fun smoothie!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What could "1/2 cup ice cubes" possibly mean? About one cube fits into my half cup measure. Would be nice to have this by #cubes or weight or something rational.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @skip I figured roughly a handful would work

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@skip I think it’s critical to the recipe to have exactly 1/2 cup of ice cubes. So fill up a plastic 1/2 measuring cup with water, then pour the water into a larger container and weigh it. Then pour out the water, put the container back on the scale, and put ice cubes in the container until you get the same weight as before. Viola! Well, you may have to adjust for the difference between the volume of frozen versus liquid water. 

Or just eye ball it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Drew My morning chuckle! Thank you!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used 2% milk and a room-temp banana and my kids swore I had blended a cinnamon bun in milk. It was delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This reminds me of the date shakes we had in Palm Springs about 30 years ago.  They were delicious so we will certainly try this.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Peter I had one of those 13 years ago and have been thinking about it ever since! Can’t wait to try this one.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Celia. Is it still possible to get a Date Milkshake in Palm Springs?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made it as the recipe instructed, but used normal milk. (I didn’t have buttermilk) The cinnamon is there, but feels odd. This tastes weird, never making this again. 2 out of 5

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just got back from Palm Springs last night and wanted to keep the date shake thing going. I made exactly as written - delicious! My dates are small so I might put 3 in next time. As for the comments that the dates don't puree, this is the beauty of the date shake, the little bits of date you get to chew. I love it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really struggled to get the dates blended in. First try they were mostly whole. Second try I chopped them first and there were still lots of date pieces at the bottom—not well integrated like in the pic. I assume the testers used a high performance blender like a Vitamix. My cheap, lower-powered version didn’t seem up to the task

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Try (chopping and) freezing dates, lightly dusted ground flax seeds or cocoa powder ahead of time. Makes blending easier.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made as directed and it was too tangy and not sweet enough.  Added a touch of brown sugar and it was good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made it exactly as written and it was delicious - others note leaving out the flax seed or using regular milk instead, but the former is definitely giving it nuttiness, and the latter tang, so it really wouldn't be the same.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Made as directed and found it had a tangy taste and overall was not unpleasant but not amazing either.  It lacked richness too, and I’m not fond of doctoring reasonably healthy smoothies with extra sweeteners just for flavor.  Ok but not a keeper for me.

My go-to smoothie:

3/4 cup whole milk
1 ripe banana
1 T peanut butter
1 T cacao (unsweetened)
1 date (optional)
2 scoops plain whey protein (optional)
6 ice cubes

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: you should add some frozen spinach. It tastes like nothing and adds so many nutrients

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I am dairy free (womp womp) and so used almond milk instead of buttermilk and kite hill Greek style almond milk and soy yogurt and it was still an unbelievable smoothie. I threw in a little bit of cacao flavored protein powder and a tablespoon of almond butter and it was to die for. I think the salt really makes it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Pretty good. Some tahini takes it to eleven.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Man oh man did this make me slurp! It was so daggone delicious! highly recommend. Now the tanginess of the buttermilk was wonderful IMHO but I could see why some of y'all wanted to tame the tang a little. For me though this was a total zippedeedoodah; can't wait to make for ma and sneak in some Benefiber!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
If you like a thicker smoothie try only 3/4 cup buttermilk. 

You can make a buttermilk substitute with 1 cup milk and 1 tbsp white vinegar.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @NC You could, but it really will just taste like sour milk. That approach is best for baked goods when you need an acid to react with baking soda. In this case, yogurt thinned with water would be a better substitute since you get that complex cultured flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used to make this recipe as a healthy breakfast smoothie. When my husband tried it, he asked me if I was sure this was "healthy" because it tasted "sinful!"

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: pears instead of bananas. Kefir. Sweetener as needed

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This is SO good. I think the buttermilk really is key for the tangy taste that makes it reminiscent of a cinnamon roll with a cream cheese glaze. Really recommend folks try it with.
I did need to make one sub -- I am allergic to banana, so used steamed, frozen cauliflower to make up the bulk of the smoothie (bonus: it has a more neutral/not noticeable taste). I also added vanilla protein powder (partly for the protein boost, partly to add some sweetness the cauliflower doesn't have).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Blitzed half a recipe in the Nutribullet, then used it as the base for overnight oats. I needed to soak, then chop the date, before the blender could do anything with it…does taste just like a cinnamon roll. Great idea - will do again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I had some leftover buttermilk and used it in this smoothie. What a great way to use leftover buttermilk! I did not know buttermilk in a smoothie could taste this good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made as written. This is ridiculously good

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Buttermilk (full fat) is not optional. But used walnuts instead of flax. Kids loved it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Best smoothie recipe ever! I replaced the buttermilk with 1/2 c kefir and 1/2 c almond milk. I didn’t have a frozen banana so used an unfrozen one. Phenomenal!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Excellent with plant milk of any kind (oat, soy, almond, or hemp), which will help avoid cholesterol, saturated fat, IGF1, and, now, bird flu virus (H5N1), found in cow milk. Come on NYT! Dairy is NOT healthy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
For everyone aghast that the NYT would specify low-fat anything, buttermilk is naturally low fat. It’s the milk leftover from butter production. If you find a “whole milk” buttermilk, that means that fat has been added back in. 

My family all really enjoyed this smoothie! Yum. For myself, I might do half buttermilk, half almond milk or something next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @McKenzie Although that was how buttermilk originated, in the American South they use “buttermilk” to describe whole sweet milk that’s soured by lactic acid producing bacteria. Buttermilk is both the watery tangy leftover liquid from butter making and the whole milk based drink.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed this recipe exactly and it was delicious. The buttermilk adds a lot of flavor to the smoothie. Made a great breakfast! I'll try using some protein powder or maybe add a little almond butter next time to make it more filling.. but it's perfect as it is.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I didn't have butter milk so I made my own by putting a splash of vinegar into the milk. I also did not have flax seeds. The rest was followed. This turned out delicious! I will definitely be making this again. Possibly with a shot of espresso!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this today. Refreshing and cure for a sweet tooth. I used regular milk. The salt is a great addition.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious! I was scared of the full cup of buttermilk so I opted for 1/2 C buttermilk and 1/2 C oat milk. Just enough tang for me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I didn't have buttermilk so I made my own, using the cheater method of 1 tbsp of white vinegar to 1 cup of milk. (This also works with plant milks.)

The whole smoothie was absolutely delicious and I didn't need a quart of buttermilk to go to waste in the fridge.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This was so tasty! I have had it twice in two days. I made it as directed and also made it with plain Greek yogurt and whole milk in place of the buttermilk- I prefer the latter. 
No need for a high speed blender here, as the little bits of dates and flax make it even more reminiscent of a cinnamon bun.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Loved this. No buttermilk so used combo of almond milk and 2% milk. Used chia seeds instead of flax, and added 1/2 cup Greek yogurt for protein. Everything else as directed.  No lie, this tasted just like a delicious cinnamon bun without the cloying sweetness and carb overload. Will be in my regular rotation going forward.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was skeptical of this recipe but holy moly I was wrong. Subbed Greek yogurt for the buttermilk to get more protein in and swapped hemp hearts for the flax seeds because I didn’t have any. I also added some powdered peanut butter on top. It was completely delicious. I’ll be adding this to my rotation.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this with whole milk because it was what I had and regular dates from Aldi's because it was what I had. I added a spoonful or two of Greek yogurt into the smoothie before mixing. It was pretty good. The dates didn't get super smooth; I've heard soaking them in hot water for a few minutes can help with that. This was good though.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I add almond butter and then it’s pretty much the Second Cup date and almond smoothie, which I love when I want a not too fruity smoothie.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: As a Sohla stan I will always give her concoctions a try. I had all the ingredients, to boot! This was a true diversion from my usual berries and greens smoothies, and a revelation. Sweet, nutty, tangy, with the warmth of cinnamon and the occasional chewy bit of date. Yum!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I omitted the salt. Added half cup of walnuts for fiber and espresso shot for caffeine. Delicious, despite my non-artistic "yogurt swirl."

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: swapped buttermilk for kefir (just what I had in the fridge), I really enjoyed the tang!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delightful! Made with whole milk kefir since that's what I had on hand and it was the perfect amount of tang.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Great with plain Kefir in place of buttermilk for a little tang and health boost. Don’t miss the salt - it definitely enhances the flavor. One of the best smoothies I’ve had in a good while!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used oat milk here, which worked just fine. Per another commenter, I added additional ice after my initial round of blending and that really thickened the whole thing up nicely. This is delicious and tastes just like a cinnamon roll. Next time, I'll add a scoop of protein powder or maybe even some silken tofu just to get a little protein. This is getting added to the regular rotation of morning smoothies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This smoothie is out of control good. I was going to skip out on the buttermilk and then saw how much protein it has in it, which sold me. I have a hard time eating in the morning, and I wake up craving this smoothie. I am going to add some espresso powder next to see how it changes things.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I can’t believe how good this tastes even without any sweetener. It really does taste like a cinnamon bun, and there’s nothing that’s outright "bad for you" in it. 10/10, no notes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made recipe as written— one of the best smoothies I’ve ever had.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used oat milk, a scoop of protein powder, added a tablespoon of chia, and substituted cinnamon for pumpkin pie spice. I also reduced to a half of a frozen banana. This was delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was delicious and I’m going to be honest, I was a little skeptical at first thinking it would not be that sweet. It was so good, I might make it everyday! I think my picky kids would even love it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made this with with 1 substitution: peanut butter instead of flax seeds because buying two specialty ingredients for a smoothie was enough for me.  I thought it was tasty and creamy a nice way to start a summer day, but generally unremarkable.  (And unfortunately it reminds me of the sad banana and peanut butter powder smoothies I ate for breakfast in college) 
Now that I have the dates I'd make it again but I would make it again but subbing diluted  yogurt for the buttermilk

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used whole milk. Added vanilla protein and used hemp seeds instead of flax. Wow so good! Salt makes the dates pop off, so don’t skip that!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My husband made this for me. The smoothie is very tasty

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is one of my faves. I love it as is but if Im out of dates I find that a handful of raisins works too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I really love this. Have yet to purchase buttermilk so can’t really speak to the recipe exactly. In place of buttermilk I use plain yogurt mixed/oat milk then follow recipe. I drink this way too often. Yum.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Did not, in all transparency, taste like a cinnamon roll. But tasty nonetheless! I added protein powder for a little extra boost.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Katastic I agree, it was tasty, but had nothing to equate it with a cinnamon roll.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is so so so good. I love how there are little bits of un blended dates. One thing I found was I tried this with my immersion blender and almost broke it. From then on I have had to use my standing blender which is a little more of a pain to clean but worth it. I like to add a spoonful of peanut butter and a scoop of chocolate protein powder and a scoop of vanilla protein powder. Sometimes I put spinach in it and although the color can be off putting, you can’t even taste the spinach! This flavor combo is so versatile, I have made an overnight oats version with it. Perfect for when you get tired of chocolate or banana peanut butter smoothies, but want a similar vibe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What a great use of leftover buttermilk! (I ALWAYS have extra buttermilk after using it for a recipe because the perfect amount of buttermilk is never to be found in a grocery store.) Thank you for helping me achieve my goal of 0 food waste!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wish I knew what to sub for banana.....

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Didn't have very much flavor despite making exactly as instructed

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I am allergic to bananas, and almost every smoothie recipe calls for them either mashed or frozen. Substitute suggestions, please and thanks?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm also allergic to bananas so I use avocados instead to give similar texture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I hate bananas.  I just leave them out, no subbing anything.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: frozen cauliflower--just by cauliflower rice and put it in there...even healthier.  You might need another date!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very tasty and pretty sweet. Nice way to use up buttermilk, I only had a half a cup but the cinnamon roll vibes were still shining.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Very tasty! Try this even if you are skeptical! It's really good. (I used half yogurt and half buttermilk.)

Even though I used a good quality blender, I could not get the dates completely pureed, so there were some flecks of date in my drink. Didn't bother me, but it might bug some people. I don't know if pre-soaking the dates might help, to soften them more.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is - DELICIOUS!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Great smoothie and tastes so much like a cinnamon roll! I didn’t have buttermilk so increased plain Greek yogurt to 1/2 c. Added some spinach and collagen powder for more protein, then cold water for liquid. No extra sweetener, it is already quite sweet. Finally, tossed in a few toasted pecans at the end because I love a little crunch!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: DELICIOUS! Used chia seeds instead of flax. 10/10

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Super yummy!
Not reallllly a cinnamon roll. But a really yummy date smoothie!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Extremely delicious and easy to make! I followed the recipe almost exactly,doubling it for two servings, using one frozen and one unfrozen  banana and tossing in six dates. Husband loves it and so did I!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious! If you have a vitamix, it really pulverizes both the dates and ice. Used more frozen banana and cinnamon.  A hit!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this on a snowy day and it was pretty good! My blender is old so I was left with some date chunks still in mine but I’m not mad because they add a fun texture. I left out the flax seed as I didn’t have any and it was still good, although a little watery. Tastes like cinnamon and banana :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My son calls this the “vanilla milkshake” smoothie. 10/10 would recommend.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The dates did not blend in well and the smoothie ended up more sour than expected.  I was using a spoon to scoop up the dates at the bottom of the glass and then I understood the hype better.  I added espresso before I got to the end.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sounded delicious and I was interested so I clicked, but the photo of the glass with the yogurt decoration sealed the deal: I am trying this :D

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Don't skimp on the buttermilk! Use whole farm fresh buttermilk for the real taste. Don't use pre ground cinnamon... dry fry a few sticks and grind them in a spice grinder while hot. Use extra large eggs and add an extra cube of ice to compensate for the thickness of whole buttermilk. I make my own vanilla out of bourbon and vanilla pods... try it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Accidentally made this with CUMIN instead of cinnamon and somehow it still tasted delicious… absolutely fool proof!!!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very good. I used three dates. Next time it will be five. Other than that I followed the recipe

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a really unique smoothie, well thought out. Very satisfying.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I enjoyed this. I made it exactly as written. I wouldn’t go out of my way to buy buttermilk to make this, but it’s definitely a good way to use up leftover buttermilk from other recipes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used 2% milk. I found the taste to be mild. Added 2 more dates (4 dates total), dash more cinnamon and it was perfect!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used unsweetened soy milk, and a slice of silken tofu, to increase the plant protein.  I also add some psyllium husk and plant sterol powder.  Didn't use flax seed or ice cubes.  Tasted very good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Somehow, I ran out of cinnamon and I used pumpkin pie spice as a substitute last minute. I think it (or apple pie spice) would make for a really good fall smoothie!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The drink in the photo sure looks prettier than the real thing.  I'm not sure how they got the color of the smoother to appear so dark...and to provide such nice contrast to the swirls of yogurt?  And I even added twice the amount of dates, seeing as a mere two dates didn't really make sense to me, seeing as it's called a 'Cinnamon Date' smoothie.  Either way, very tasty, and quite sweet ....maybe too sweet?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This smoothie intrigued me when it was suggested one day. I saved it to try when I next had dates on hand. I finally made it last week and my kids loved it. They said it reminded them of a blended, frozen king cake. We've made it a couple times since then too and it's always great. We do add extra cinnamon, though.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I like cinnamon buns but even though I used less than the called for amount of cinnamon I found it a little too cinnamon forward and almost astringent - that may be due to the type of cinnamon I have though. Otherwise it was nice!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I swapped out the buttermilk for kefir that I got from my local farmers market. Other than that I followed the recipe as is and it is DELICIOUS. It tastes just like a cinnamon roll with cream cheese icing. Next time I may add an extra date. My only question is- why or how does the picture look the way it does? lol.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was pretty good and a similar flavor to a cinnamon bun. But it is very sweet and syrupy. I enjoyed it, but probably wouldn’t make it again. Maybe cut the dates and banana by half? Very creative idea and pretty finishing touch to add the swipes of yogurt on the glass!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thoughts on a banana substitute?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: both my toddler and I loved this one. we doubled the dates for extra sweetness.  my only complaint is that the dates didn't blend up smoothly but that is probably an operator-error. Instead of buttermilk I used normal milk with a splash of white vinegar.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: what would be a good sub for the flax?  Im not gonna buy flax seeds for a smoothie recipe.  Peanut butter? Sunflower seeds? almond flour? Just skip it?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This came out a lot thinner than I’m used to for smoothies. Sweetness level was perfect. Next time will try it with plain Greek yogurt and protein powder. I think cottage cheese would also add a salty tang.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’ve been making this with dairy free-yogurt and oat milk instead of buttermilk and adding cinnamon almond butter for more protein. So good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really nice! I used full fat buttermilk and subbed the flax for some peanut butter. It really does remind you of a cinnamon roll! My one regret is that the overripe banana I used gave it a kind of acidic, cloying sweetness. Would def make again

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Doesn't the recipe call for flax seed?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @ML Yes, it does. I used peanut butter because that’s what I had on hand. Although after making it again with flax seeds, I think flax seeds do make the smoothie taste more cinnamon roll like

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used a few strawberries since I was out of bananas. Couldn’t even taste them. This was seriously so good! I’m always amazed when something that seems so straightforward is so delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have a cheap blender which struggles with blending the dates - I found that if I pit and freeze them beforehand they blend a little easier.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Try chopped dates.  That's what I have so that's what I'm going to do!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hello all, we loved this cake; however, it only rose to 1 inch tops. More like brownie than cake. I followed instructions to a t.  I followed ingredients list and measured carefully…with one exception. I used a plant-based “butter” instead of the real thing because I had it available. Do you think that was culprit or something else?  Thankful for your comments.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Absolutely love this. Definitely don’t understand the people saying it’s not rich enough or not sweet enough or lacks flavor. Crazy. 

It really does remind me of a cinnamon bun with cream cheese frosting. It’s hard to get medjool dates here, we mostly just get California dates so I used three since they’re much smaller but I think it’s would honestly be sweet enough without them. I made exactly as written but added collagen and creatine since I’m trying to incorporate them into my diet but don’t have a lot of ways to do so. Having this as a before bed snack feels like a true treat! 

I do think you could leave out the ice cubes and not notice, it’s not very thick with current proportions and doesn’t need any thinning in my opinion. I might try it with kefir next time just for the added health benefits but I feel like it won’t be as good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
WOW I went into this not knowing what to expect. I had some bananas that were about to go bye bye then I saw this recipe. This is amazing. I had no idea it would be this good and I know exactly what’s in it.
Now I need more over ripe bananas.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Make healthy 
Buttermilk -> fair life milk

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love this recipe, I feel like I’m drinking a milkshake! I use a few dashes of cardamom instead of the cinnamon and 1 tbs of peanut butter instead of the flax seeds.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow, I happened to have some cinnamon rolls in my kitchen when I made this, so I could compare back-and-forth. There is a clear similarity in both taste and texture. Love it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The cinnamon is the star in this smoothie -- so good. I made it with coconut milk. Such simple ingredients but so good for you. Thank you!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The smoothie was delicious using the exact recipe. I will make it again without any modifications.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This smoothie is my go to! So good. I do have a word of warning. Not only is this dangerously good, but it’s downright dangerous if not consumed properly. If you choose to garnish with a lovely sprinkle of cinnamon… DRINK WITH A STRAW.

*if you don’t use a straw you risk the fate of inhaling cinnamon and trust me, you don’t want that experience.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very tasty treat, and super sweet with dates and bananas, even with buttermilk. Next time, I’ll use more yogurt to balance that out. Yet, it is definitely a dessert rather than a smoothie, which I think of as a meal.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What would someone use instead of banana? I’m allergic and am so sad.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Tried once with only 1/4c buttermilk and the rest reg milk, and it was nice.  Today I tried with the full amount of buttermilk and it is BONKERS delicious.  Follow this as written, it’s perfect!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Total fave; great tang and almost rich. Yogurt on glass essential.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My one year old tasted it and declared “cookie!”. It’s very good. I used nonfat Greek yogurt and a little whole milk to thin it in place of buttermilk. I had trouble with the dates blending properly but used an immersion blender. Flax seed essential.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm not normally a sodium hawk, but 1231 mg sounds like A LOT!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
- 1/4 cup buttermilk and 3/4 cup regular milk, perfectly tangy for me. 
- a spoonful of tahini, which increases the richness
- date paste, which blends more smoothly

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Side note: I guess substitutes are nice to know in a pinch, but there’s a reason why she used buttermilk!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I make this exactly as written and i must have made it 20 times. I love this smoothie. I buy my dates at Costco...fresh and soft so they blend well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Oh that does taste like a cinnamon roll!  Used non-dairy milk & yogurt b/c those are easier on my digestion but still found it very decadent smoothie.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really nice - I'd also recommend a shot of espresso (helps melt some of the ice if you have sensitive teeth like me) and a drizzle of maple syrup for a little extra sweetness.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: According to Google, half a cup of “standard” ice cubes should weigh 60-70 grams.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My monocle popped out while reading this. I’ll need to buy more houses on Boardwalk to afford this recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: grocery store caviar isn't expensive

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I've made this for years and always use less expensive lumpfish caviar black or red, sometimes even salmon or trout roe). Makes a great impression for a crowd.
I save the good stuff to be eaten as it should be, with toast points or blini and other garnishes optional.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Joe
I make mine with fake caviar-comes in a jar, about $5. Works just fine. (look for this in Russian, Slavic, or Middle Eastern stores.)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow ! This is the almost exact recipe from the First (Red) Women’s Exchange of Memphis, TN cookbook from years ago! My Mom had this every year at holiday parties and it was soooooo good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The stuff legends are made of! My Christmas dinner companions were fighting over who would get to lick the bowl. Thank you, Sohla.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
