commentBody: My uncle is famous for his caviar pie and his recipe is very similar but uses scallions instead of chives. We serve it with wheat thins because the sweet nuttiness balances the rest of the flavors.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I just made this as a triple decker layer pie (think layer cake), with each layer a new type of caviar that is progressively more expensive and exquisite. The hardest part was pairing with sufficiently delicate and appropriately rare bottles of champagne. By the time we finished, I served a standalone caviar tasting on great aunt’s mother of pearl spoons, they came over when she transferred her assets out of Swiss vault. My guests loved this experience. Highly recommend.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Bill Whoa and wow! Thumbs up. How did the caviar fare when layered?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Bill this sounds incredible! And inspired.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
For previous comments, so many type Os!
That is Chevre' cheese to replace the cream cheese. Rinsing the shallots did not diminish the flavor. I did not have chives, so substituted finely minced scallion greens.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: To Paul D - it's very good made a day ahead, just don't put the caviar on until close to serving time. I'm making mine right now for NYE tomorrow!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
this is a fantastic idea!…i wonder if there could be a lightly buttered bellini “crust” on the bottom?
maybe extravagant for a large party but sweet for. party of two with a bottle of nice V….happy new years!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Made it for Christmas - easy, relatively economical (the recipe notes are accurate: you can get away with a less-expensive caviar with this dish), and an easy win. Party guests raved and it went fast.

Only tip I'd add is to taste the mixture carefully for salt: the caviar I used was on the salty side, and so were the chips I used. A delicious salt-bomb, but next time I'll be more careful.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very similar idea to mom’s recipe (Columbus OH, c 70s) but the lower level was pate, then the cream cheese, iced with caviar… with rings of minced onion, minced yolks, diced egg white, then finally a layer of curly parsley… very pretty on the buffet, with bremner wafers… May need to try this for new years! Ty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can this be made the day before serving?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Paul D I would say to make it up to the point before adding the caviar. Just wrap tightly in plastic and refrigerate so it doesn’t dry out. Add the caviar before serving.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You won’t be disappointed making this recipe! We couldn’t get enough!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Oops, previous comments. For lactose intolerance, goat yogurt instead of sour cream. Chevre instead of cream cheese.
 Gently mixed the egg yolk mixture, shallots and finely chopped egg whites because I misread the instructions and it turned out fine. Served in a shallow bowl, made it easier to transport and serve. Topped with Salmon roe before serving. A giant hit with the crispy veg and assorted thin crackers. Next time, will be easier.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: delicious as a dip with potato chips. almost even better slathered onto a thick slice of toasted sourdough for breakfast.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I've been making this star of the show for years. Because it contains so many ingredients, it doesn't suffer if you use a less expensive caviar (lumpfish, either black or red, salmon caviar, American Golden). I always serve it with lightly toasted baguette slices. And good champagne or top shelf iced vodka, of course!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could I double the recipe and put it in a springform pan?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Trout roe typically costs between $40 and $65 for 4 oz.  Since this feeds 12 people, the cost per person is between $3 and $5.  This is an affordable way to make food for a gathering seem luxurious even when you're on a budget.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Nothing in the world beats sturgeon caviar with blini and sour cream. And a glass of chilled vodka.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this with Imperia Caviar as I feared the mixture of flavors would overpower grocery store caviar which tends to be lower quality. It was excellent, and sparked a full 30 minutes of conversation at my dinner. Definitely not too expensive and delicious (if you know how find quality ingredients).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made for Thanksgiving gathering. It was delicious. Served with potatoe chips, taro chips and blini. Yummy and a big treat.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this for a Christmas appetizer (feast of the seven fishes)— everyone was skeptical, but they LOVED it. Made with salmon roe to keep costs down, assembled in a glass pie pan (let’s not get tooooo fancy), and served with salt and vinegar kettle chips. I second the recommendation to enjoy with an ice-cold martini, my pref is Grey Goose with an extra twist!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
A big appetizers hit! For lactose intolerance I substituted cheered and goat yogurt.
For me, I made and served it in a shallow bowl, which made it easy to transport and serve. I misread the instructions and laid the yolk and butter mixture down, and then patting on the prepped onions(oops). So I just added the chopped whites and tossed all together, then pressed down, and proceeded with the creamy ingredients. I used Salmon roe. Served with breakfast radishes, sliced cakes, and very crisp crackr

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I had this at a catered holiday party one year and when I saw it what it cost when planning my own party (almost $100), I decided to make it myself. Found this recipe. Very easy and delicious. Sometimes, for home use, I just put all the layers on a flat bowl and don't worry about the looks. I have also just made the egg salad with sour cream topping and green onions, skipping the caviar. Makes a great sandwich.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My mother used to make something similar as a dip. She would use a very thick sour cream and mix in the grated zest of a lemon and fresh lemon juice, finely chopped shallots, and some garlic salt and celery salt. She would let that sit overnight for the flavours to mingle. She potted the dip into a festive bowl, added the chopped hard boiled eggs on top (whites and yolks separated for colour), and then added lumpfish caviar on top. She served it with Carr’s water crackers.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Back in the day, this caviar dish was a star at my annual Christmas open house! It is that good - and it looks spectacular on the table.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My dad introduced us to caviar with a simple version of this - shape a disc of cream cheese and place in the center of a "fancy" plate. Surround it with a ring of minced onion, a ring of chopped egg white, and a ring of smashed egg yolk. Top the cream cheese with the caviar and serve with your choice of crackers or toasts.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Served with matzo so as not to interfere with the flavors. It was a very fun and festive snack to come home to after dancing all evening. I under seasoned the egg and cream cheese layers, reasoning that the ikura would be plenty salty, but they could have used a little more.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It was great and looked so festive and special. I will make it again but will cut the cc/sour cream layer in half.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The Junior League of San Francisco published a cookbook circa 1978-81 that had this recipe. I save it for  holidays and always , always get rave reviews.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm going to use small individual ramekins to reduce use of caviar. I also might top a few with smoked salmon.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could this be done in reverse in a bowl or mould and then inverted onto a platter for a prettier look?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
PS - I tried this when I made the recipe for a wine night with friends yesterday and it worked well. I lined an 8in round bowel with plastic wrap, lightly sprayed with non-stick. Then I added the layers in reverse, starting with the cream cheese mixture, then shallots, then the egg. Let set in the fridge and inverted onto a plate when ready to serve. 
Delicious recipe, even if you just use the more available lumpfish roe

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just wondering… Why do you rinse the shallot after chopping it?

commentBody: It cuts the 'oniony-ness' by rinsing away the sulfer.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love this for breakfast on rye toast!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this, used decent caviar but the result was  meh. Nice presentation though.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I make this with not too expensive black caviar and salmon roe.  It's always a hit. The recipe I use is a little simpler.  Put the hard boiled eggs (cold) in a food processor with 4 tablespoons butter.  No fuss, no muss.  I use a smallish springform pan instead of a ring mold

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Good but everything overpowered the caviar for me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: How is this with just the cheese and caviar layer, or an alternative to the egg layer? It looks so festive for a party, but I’m not the biggest fan of boiled eggs.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Perhaps it was me but the egg cook time was not quite sufficient for me - would have preferred a firmer yolk for this one to hold its shape better!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: How do you get this out of the mold again, if the caviar is on top?  It’s not hard.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Excellent and easy to make ahead of time. Made this for a large cocktail party. I used 6” mold, but will use 8” next time. Provided mini blini, Carr’s crackers and endive spears. Store bought lumpfish eggs were fine for this recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What is speculoos cookie butter?  Not something we have in Sweden, so how do you make it at home?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Deborah T I’m thinking you MIGHT have the cookie… in America we do get it in one of the airlines… I thought it was from Denmark but I could be mistaken.  It’s a little brown sugar type cookie with some fun spices in it.  So good

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Deborah T Biscoff cookie spread, is what the recipe is referring to.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Deborah T in Europe, I think you can try lotus Biscoff butter, should yield same effect

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love that folks are discovering rocky road! My only tip is DON'T use foil for the sling. It works sometimes and other times it tears and you are stuck gouging out little bits of foil from your beautiful rocky road. You really can take it any direction. For example I love some tahini and a touch of rosewater in the chocolate, with turkish delight, pistachios, marshmallows, raspberry gummies and dried apricots as mix ins, and then decorate the top with some dried rose petals and gold sprinkles.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this together with my 7 year old son! Had a blast.  Totally kid friendly! Thank you for incorporating a recipe that kids can make! We used Fruit snacks, Nutella, peanut butter, marshmallows, and honey roasted cashews!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What is cookie butter?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I think Trader Joe's sells a version. Also, if your local stores carry it, Biscoff (like the cookies) makes a jar of cookie butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It is just cookies in a blender with butter (and sometimes other things).  Google it for recipes if you can't find it in your neighborhood stores.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Stephanie
Trader Joes does sell it- it is delicious I get it all the time!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For all the people who aren’t familiar with speculoospasta (they’re translating the Dutch “pasta,” which means “paste,” as “cookie butter,” which confused me), or cannot easily find it in stores, the video says you can use Nutella, peanut butter, or tahini instead, so worry not.  But ChatGPT says that the Biscoff brand is available in the US, as well as in France and Germany, so you may be able to find it if you’d like.  It essentially tastes like crushed gingerbread.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Laura Kay If you live in the US then look for Biscoff cookies or Biscoff cookie butter. They have it at Kroger grocery stores, Target, etc. They also give out the cookies on domestic flights with United, American, Delta, etc. I believe Trader Joe’s has them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What is speculoos? Never heard of it

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Karonday have you heard of cookie butter from Trader Joe’s?  That is one version of it… it’s those little cookies called speculoos  that you get on airplanes!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You have probably seen them as Lotus brand Biscoff cookies. Speculoos cookies are Belgian Speculaas and are traditionally made with spices, butter, and brown sugar and baked until completely crispy giving them a very caramelised flavour. They make a "cookie butter" spread from the ground up cookies; it's kind of like peanut butter. The Lotus Biscoff spread is the most common but stores like Walmart and Trader Joe's have homebrand versions.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m FIRST! Ok but seriously, this was a messy delight to make with little kids. The flavor combo was unappealing as written, but after dutifully following the recipe, the result just tasted like more. Just don’t go wild with chunks like I absentmindedly did because it’ll ruin the chunk-to-glue ratio. Easily fixed, at least!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is impressive! How did you get the recipe and all the ingredients together so quickly? I stocked up on pretty much every baking ingredient but marshmallows, speculoos and gumdrops?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Startbakingca Are you also in DK? Funny you mention as I was one proud expat once I had it all! Føtex had melting chocolate (I combined mostly dark with some milk), and large gum drops were among their Christmas candy (Carletti brand, generically called “Konfekt med frugtsmag”). I halved the gum drops. Normal had bags of colored mini marshmallows and Biscoff cookie butter. I used brunkage for the seasoning blend: different ratios of the spices, but it’s a subtle flavor anyway. Have fun!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: what is cookie butter??????

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Rainashine I assume that they are referring to the Biscoff spread, which you would find alongside  Nutella at the grocery store.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are pretty. look delicious and sound like they are fun to make. Congrats to the folks who had speculoos and  cookie butter in their pantry and made these right away.  Like many of the other commenters, but for google, I had no idea of what a speculoo was nor did I have any idea what cookie butter was. Though I found a recipe to make cookie butter, it called for crushed speculoos. A few suggested substitutions  at the end of the recipe would have helped quite a bit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
My wife and I waited all year for Cookie Week. This was our first cookie of this year’s Cookie Week. The anticipation in our house was palpable. 

We made these, split a single piece, exchanged a look and threw them in the trash.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Matt this comment cracked me up. I’m excited to make these tonight but am subbing with some dried fruits, nuts and darker chocolate. Will report back if these get tossed in the bin! lol

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What is cookie butter?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Steve Pakiz https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cookie_butter

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It is like peanut butter/nutella but made of speculoos cookies. The most common brand is Lotus Biscoff Cookie Butter but there are a lot of dupes. If you are in the USA Trader Joe's sells one called "Speculoos Cookie Butter" but the original Lotus brand Biscoff speculoos spread/butter is fairly easy to find in Europe, North America, Australia, UK etc.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Steve Pakiz If you live in the US then look for Biscoff cookies or Biscoff cookie butter. They have it at Kroger grocery stores, Target, etc. They also give out the cookies on domestic flights with United, American, Delta, etc. I believe Trader Joe’s have them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Great twist on (British) Rocky Road! I am from the UK and used to make this all the time. Adding Speculoos spread to the chocolate makes the base more Christmassy than the normal version! 

If this looks a bit too sweet for you... try these swaps:
-  good quality dark chocolate instead of choc chips
- digestive biscuits for the cookies (like in the British version)
-  leave out the candies OR swap for dried cranberries for an extra christmassy feel!

Enjoy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have fond memories of making these 50+ years ago.  We called them Stained Glass Windows and   we used multicolor mini marshmallows (do they even have those anymore?) in the mix.  My kids loved them, of course.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Joan exactly! Instead of cookie butter we used ground graham crackers as a binder. No spices the recipe in many 70’s cookbooks. Everything old is new again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cookie butter is made from ground up Biscoff or speculoos cookies.  In the U.S. it is found in the peanut butter section of most grocery stores.  Biscoff is a brand of speculoos cookies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Name Janet
At Costco cookies in a 4 pk box 5.99 on sale and 7.99 regular price.  We are giving a box to each of our neighbors for Christmas… did I say these cookies are addicting?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Name Janet Trader Joe’s also carries it too but they have been having some stock issues with it lately.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I live in the Netherlands, so speculoos is ubiquitous here.  But I have never seen it in stores in NYC, let alone in other parts of the US.  Can one now buy speculoos in many places in the US?  As a funny aside, it took me a minute to figure out that “speculoos cookie butter” must mean plain old Dutch speculoos.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Laura Kay yeah, I live in California, and all our local grocery stores carry it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I know Trader Joe’s has it in the US, maybe other grocery stores too?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can absolutely find it in the USA. The Lotus brand is available widely and Trader Joe's makes a dupe that they call "Cookie Butter"

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What is speculoos sugar? Is it something one makes?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I've read hundreds of NYT recipes and never commented. But I'm under strict orders from my pickleball group to say how DELICIOUS this is. Also that subbing Australian red and green licorice tubes for the spice drops was Christmasy and perfect (the tubes slice up  like gum drops with a fairly neutral flavor profile, technically apple and strawberry--Darrell Lea brand). I was slammed with requests for the recipe. It was EASY to make. Go Sohla!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cookie butter is made from ground up spice cookies, like Biscoff, that’s native to Belgium and the Netherlands. It should be findable in most US grocery stores, it’s definitely available at Trader Joe’s, Walmart, and World Market.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious! I love all the textures. Be careful with the gum spice drops. You do need a chewy gooey component. But next time I would pick out the wintergreen peppermint spice gum drops, tastes a bit weird with these ones in the mix.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Daisy Thanks for this tip! After seeing cinnamon, peppermint, spearmint, sassafras, licorice, and clove as the flavors in the spice drops I bought for this recipe, I was wondering how that would work. Before this recipe, I was unfamiliar with spice drops.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'd never tried a 'spice drop' before, but was not a fan of the taste! Also, the addition of pumpkin spice just seems weird to me. I'm going to make a version with marshmallows, pecans, dried cherries, pistachios, and candied orange peel -- and cross my fingers!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: speculoos is the generic term for a spicy-sweet Belgian cookie - Sohla uses the Biscoff brand of speculoos cookies and speculoos butter. Google it, folks. Serious Eats has recipes for each. But I'm sure I heard Sohla say that using your own ginger cookies could work - that's what I'll do.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sub dried fruit for the gumdrops (yuck) and skip the cookie butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Marie
The cookie butter makes it almost fudge like

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this for my first holiday cookie party, and it was a HIT. Everyone raved. One lady seemed to think I was so amazing for it that she asked if it was MY recipe that the NYT had published. I accidentally burned some of the pecans a bit and added a little extra salt (used coarse kosher salt, and added about 40% extra—def don’t do that with fine salt—and also salted the pecans), and multiple people noted that they liked the saltiness.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Miri Great job Miri! So glad you got such glowing reviews!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Made today with my 9 year old daughter, who has a short attention span, but we watched the video and then put it together and it was great. We used nutella instead of cookie butter, and she insisted on mixing dark and white chocolate chips, which I thought would be -meh- but it was delish. Just tossed in some cinnamon, ginger, cardamom and nutmeg.

I would say be a little picky about your gumdrops. We bought some run of the mill gumdrops and the lemon and lime are kinda blech in this mix.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Speculoos cookie butter can be found at Trader Joe’s in the US, for those who are inquiring

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I just made this with my 4 year old daughter. The only note is the gum drops don't add much other than a too chewy element, I would probably omit the next time we make it. I enjoy everyone else's suggestions on here, love the idea of dried fruit, would be so much better than gum drops.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So, first I have to find out what speculoos butter is, which I had to look up, during which I had to look up what on earth speculoos is (are?), for which Biscoff cookies reportedly may be substituted. Then I have to make speculoos butter, and add pumpkin or apple pie spice to chocolate? And ruin perfectly good gumdrops by placing them in a matrix with marshmallows? Fuggedaboutit! I'll just eat a handful of chocolate chips with a handful of gumdrops and call it good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We made these two ways: first, we followed the recipe as closely as we could, but burnt the pecans, so went with pretzels for salty crunch. Second, we made an alternative based on Sohla el-Waylly’s comments in the video to try other mixes and went with Nutella, Rice Krispies and marshmallows. Both were a lot of fun to make and to eat. I liked the texture of the chocolate/spread mix, which was firm, but not hard to bite into. It’s very easy and I’ll definitely make it again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Looking forward to this recipe. For speculos butter — I’ve found it at Trader Joe’s if that’s available to you. I think it’s better than Bischoff. I don’t think I’ve seen speculos cookies, but we have a Dutch windmill cookie here in Michigan that would be a good substitute.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Speculoos or Cookie butter can be found at Trader Joe’s

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In Australia this would typically not be fudgy but be a jumble of large marshmallows and other mix ins glued together with melted chocolate. To me the ratio of marshmallows in the picture looks way too small. Typical mix ins might be peanuts, broken shortbread pieces or pretzels, coconut, but you can do just about anything. I love glacé ginger and pistachios. Recipe tin eats has a pretty typical Australian version for anyone keen to try a different take on rocky road. I’m keen to try this version but not sure I can get spice drops here!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cookie butter can be bought at Trader Joe's. It tastes like pre-made cookie dough you can buy at the grocery, but has the consistency of peanut butter. Just add it as specified and don't eat it out of the jar or you will not be able to put the spoon down. It is delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Victoria
Biscoff cookies sold at Costco large 4 pk  5.99 on sale if not 7.99

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
For those asking about speculoos, Google is a thing. It's neat and you'll get an answer super fast.

Has anyone actually made this and tried it yet?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Let me google that for you. Asking questions here builds a sense of community. Yeah google might be faster, but we are all people looking for human connection, why not let that be here?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If you have Wegmans near you, they have Biscoff brand - for cookie butter & cookies. Trader Joe’s also has at least a variation of the cookie butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I just made this! I skipped the spice drops (I'm not a fan) and went with the tastes I like. I used mini marshmallows, walnuts, shortbread cookies, cookie butter, and gold sprinkles on top for some flare. It tastes great! I think I'll add pistachios next time. Bringing this as a Christmas gift to my family this year.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Oh my, the cookie butter on a spoon, right out of the jar, is delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love how flexible this is! We swapped in peanut butter, animals crackers, and Trader Joe’s fruit jellies. We also added Rice Krispies which add a great crunch. This is so simple and fun to make with kids.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Oh good Lord, this is a sweet, salty, crunchy, chocolatey treat. So easy. Be sure to include the gumdrops as their cool sweetness balances the richness of the other flavors.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are so pretty and festive looking and were a HIT at a holiday party! They are so easy and adaptable and turned out great. I used a combo of dark and milk chocolate along with bittersweet (in the video she talks about different options), and it was velvety and yummy with the cookie butter but definitely a little melty in the hand. I couldn't find spice drops so I used fruit flavored gum drops, which I was worried might be weird- nope, it was delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I did not add the gum drops, as others have said. Instead I added dried cherries and jujufruit gummy candies. The dry cherries gave the cookie a tangy kick that worked really well with the other ingredients. The jujufruits produced the beautiful stained glass effect, like the gum drops. The cookie butter and cut up speculoos were so tasty in this cookie! I was surprised at how nicely all of flavors worked together and how easy this cookie was to make.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was a huge hit this year when we gave it as gifts. Biscoff is the name brand for the generic Speculoos. We had no problem finding them (cookie and butter) in the first supermarket we looked, in the US, ShopRite. This will definitely be requested again next year and easy to make.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I am not a gum drop fan. Swapped them out with crystalized ginger and this is our new go-to!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I wonder if this could be made with Nesslerode mix subbed in for gumdrops (and maybe lose the pumpkin pie spice, plus maybe sub walnuts for pecans). Sohla said we could put anything we like in, but I wonder if the Nesslerode mix is too wet or too high proof and might interfere with the mixture setting up…. When Sees used to put Nesslerode in some of their filled chocolates, those were my favorites. https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/6221-nesselrode-mix

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Gorgeous - I can't wait to try these. What about storage? Do they need to be kept in the fridge/freezer? Do they get melty at room temp?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My kid called this crazy cake. Co-workers asked if I was stoned when I made it. This recipe is a good time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow! I made these because they were quick and simple, and they turned out to be my favorite of my Christmas cookies! I used the Biscoff cookies/butter, winter-y cinnamon spices, almonds and the rest of the recipe. I think the Biscoff and spiced gum drops make it! I sent a batch to my son for his birthday and am going to make them with my little "grand-nephews" on Christmas Eve. A surprising (to me) winner!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have found cookie butter at Trader Joe’s in the past.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Greetings all! Trader Joe’s sells cookie butter. It’s by the peanut butter. I discovered it years ago when it was one of their free samples. I thought I had died and gone to heaven. Heat some in the microwave and pour it over ice cream and you will never be the same.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made a gluten-free version.  I substituted Oat Haus Granola Butter for the cookie butter and gluten-free ginger snap cookies for the speculoos cookies.  It came out fantastic!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’ve been making these cookies for decades only we called them Gum Drop Cookies.  Best made with Brach’s Spice drops.  Don’t taste quite right with any other brand and now Brach’s doesn’t make all of the colors.  Cut the gum drops in half before using. It’s easier.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I am making this for the second time and getting a little NYE with the ingredients. I bought thick pretzel sticks and crushed those in - as well as some ginger cookies and those green cherry Christmas things. I found spiced gumdrops at the dollar store.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I found Biscoff (same as speculoos) cookies and cookie butter at World Market Cost Plus.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I adore Sohla and have her cookbook—but this isn’t a cookie. I don’t bake for children though so maybe that’s why I’ll skip this one. All Cookie Week creators—I eagerly await this feature every year but I wish it was a bit earlier. My friend and I have our cookie bake early December (we make I over 1500 cookies) so I already have 10 doughs prepped. Can’t wait to see what else you all post!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I promise you that plenty of adults enjoy Rocky Road! You can make a more adult versions with nuts (almost any nut works!), alcohol soaked dried fruits, candied fruits, marshmallows, gummy candies like turkish delight, coconut, honeycomb/sponge toffee etc. It's quite common to find gourmet versions specifically for adults around the holiday season.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Please! Someone educate all of us asking about speculoos! Can we make it? Even doing web search all dem to require a brand name cookie - Bischoff?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m making this! It looks like fruit cake when you cut it into sections!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Would sub in Lotus biscoff butter for speculoos cookie butter (but in Australia so not sure how easy that is to find elsewhere).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For everyone asking, speculoos (or speculaas) is a type of spiced shortbread cookie. The brand here in the US is usually Biscoff, and you're likely to find the spread or butter at Trader Joe's.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: An internet search seems to say that Biscoff is a type of speculoos cookie and that you can make speculoos cookie butter with it if you can't buy the premade variety locally.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For those asking about "cookie butter": It is like peanut butter/nutella but made of speculoos cookies. The most common brand is Lotus Biscoff Cookie Butter but there are a lot of dupes available. If you are in the USA Trader Joe's sells one literally called "Speculoos Cookie Butter" (dark green label) but the original Lotus brand is called Biscoff speculoos spread/butter (red and white label) is what Sohla is using and it is fairly easy to find in Europe, North America, Australia, UK etc.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Speculoos is internationally more known under the brand name Biscof - the brand also has the biscof spread or speculoospasta, which is I guess what she means with cookie butter

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cookie butter is Biscoff Spread? Pretty sure it is a thing. Trader Joe’s also has Speculoos Cookie Buttet Spread https://www.traderjoes.com/home/products/pdp/speculoos-cookie-butter-096761

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cookie butter is the spiced ginger cookie mixed with butter and sugar to form a "butter."  It's available at Walmart.  It used to be at Trader Joe's and appears to have been discontinued.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made my version today. Watched the video and was inspired to change up the smoosh, crunch, and jelly elements. I made mine with half and half biscoff and tahini instead of either/or. Them used honey sesame sticks, mini marshmallows, and candied ginger. I’m currently waiting for it to set but i tried a spoonful before it went into the fridge - it is great. Can’t wait to try other combos!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What is speculoos cookie butter?  is it similar to anything we can find easily? What would be a reasonable substitute, if not?  These look spectacular and I'd LOVE to make them as close to recipe as possible!...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: To everyone who keeps asking what is speculoos butter, watch the video! She tells you what you can sub it with! She even says you can leave it out altogether.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love Sohla in her mom era! Me too, girl. This looks utterly delightful. Core memories in the making!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Biscoff cookie butter is the same as Speculoos cookie butter.  Trader Joe’s sells it.  It’s delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Cookie butter is made by Biscoff. No actual butter involved. I can buy it at Target in the Dallas suburbs. If you watch the video she does say you could use any nut butter or Nutella or leave it out.
Speculoos are windmill cookies that you can buy anywhere with a decent grocery store. Or Amazon unless you are boycotting them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cookie butter or speculous is also sold at Trader Joe’s.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Was intrigued by the strangeness of the recipe and they turned out to be way better than I expected. All the flavors and textures are perfect together!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Great effort to deliciousness ratio!! The gumdrops look great in it, but I don’t like the flavor. Next time, I will double the marshmallows and omit gumdrops. I’ll be taking them to an office party tomorrow so we will see how they hold up at room temp for a few hours - wish me luck.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Speculoos is a Belgian/Dutch cookie similar to a graham cracker or digestive biscuit. Cookie Butter is a spread made out of ground speculoos and oil with extra fat and sugar. It also comes from Belgium/the Netherlands, but has been popular in the US for nearly a decade. The original brand, which is widely available in North America and Europe, is Biscoff.

@Deborah T I can get Biscoff cookies and Biscoff Spread "kakkräm" at my local Willy's, so yes, it is available in Sweden.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In the USA - during the holiday season (aka now), Aldi has speculoos cookies in many shapes for very reasonable prices.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Speculoos Cookie Butter Recipe Source: https://www.liveeatlearn.com/wprm_print/how-to-make-speculoos-cookie-butter Ingredients 1 8.8 oz package Biscoff cookies 250 g ½ cup water 120 mL 2 Tbsp brown sugar ¼ tsp cinnamon ¼ tsp salt ½ cup coconut oil solid Instructions: Make Crumbs: Add cookies to a blender or food processor and pulse until they become fine crumbs. Make Paste: Add the water and continue blending into a smooth paste. Add all remaining ingredients and blend until smooth.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Incidentally on holiday at Christmas markets in Germany, so in love with the idea of cookie butter. Think graham cracker in Nutella form. I would swap the gumdrops for maybe bits of kids fruit rolls or leather or a Haribo of your choice.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can’t say I liked this treat after making it. I subbed fruit snacks for gumdrops, but still found it too sweet. Good base, may attempt another time with different mix-ins.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are good. very easy to make. I cut them much smaller because they are extraordinarily sweet. Mine were not quite as vibrant because I used the Trader Joes candy that is a lighter color and square. The cookie butter and cookies added a nice flavor and texture. I found both at Trader Joes. It kind of reminds me of the seven layer cookies but was easier to make.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this exactly as is! Well, I used Biscoff cookies and Biscoff cookie butter because I think that’s the same thing as Speculoos, so if I’m wrong I made an accidental, but successful, substitution. It’s great! I don’t usually care for fruity candies but the spice drops just lend a little hint of flavor and a fun texture. I’m going to make another batch with gummy cherry candy because I think it’ll have a bit of a Black Forest Cake vibe. Really fun and easy addition to the holiday baking list!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used Mott’s Fruitsations for the gumdrops, gave the slice a nice chewy fruity flavour. The cookie spread in Canada is Biscoff, very tasty. We found these cookies to be quite addictive.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cookie butter is like peanut butter. They sell it at Trader Joe's. It's like gingersnap peanut butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My football fan group makes Rocky Road with mini marshmallows and whole macadamia nuts. Always a sellout at potlucks. Sometimes sprinkle with flaky smoked salt. It’s ultra.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these with my 2 year old. Easy and delicious. Spice drops added a cool texture, but the flavor was odd and overpowering, so I’d leave them out next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m setting out to make this today. The ingredients aren’t hard to locate in the US honestly. I searched cookie butter and found it at Target, Walmart, the local grocery and even tiny jars at Dollar Tree! I got the cookies in expensively ($2?)  at Aldi on a whim the other day so I’m happy I have something to do with them now.I'm just not sure about the gumdrops. They look pretty but I’m afraid they’ll taste weird

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What would happen if you store it at room temperature. Could I include it in a tin box of cookies to send in the mail?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 10/10 no notes. Make this candy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this last night with my 7 year old son. It was great fun and a perfect kid-friendly project. We used Nultella, marshmallows, crispy honeycomb, m&ms, and maraschino cherries. In the future, we will up the mix-ins so that the chocolate is barely holding everything together. I think it will be more balanced that way, as the chocolate is super rich and sweet. Nuts would be welcome here. We also agreed to skip the m&ms : )

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was able to find speculoos butter and speculoos cookies both at Walmart in the US - labeled as  the Biscoff brand. Gumdrops were the hardest to find and after making I would replace these with something softer. Overall loved the results though.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Biscoff cookies  and cookie butter readily available in the Boston area and I thought were delicious here. I made exactly as written and thought they were easy and festive. Next time i think i will leave out the gumdrops, they were a discordant note I thought, though they looked great when sliced. 
My only concern is whether the chocolate will melt at room temp in my cookie tin? As these are gifts, i don’t have control over storage.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was so easy to make an absolutely delicious. And it doesn’t hurt that it’s gorgeous to look at! I would not seek out spice drops as a stand-alone treat, but the flavor works so well in this rocky road.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In West Michigan we have Steenstra's Windmill Cookies at Meijer. They work great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I thought that this was delicious, easy and I will make it again but without the spiced gumdrops.  I think that regular gumdrops will make this recipe perfect.  The challenge is finding regular gumdrops.  I couldn’t find them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I would love to make these with spice drops but can't find them anywhere in NYC. They used to be everywhere. Anybody know where to find them in Manhattan?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: they are hard to find everywhere now. Chain grocery stores and convenience stores/pharmacies (like Walgreens) are your best bet.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm surprised to read "but, in Australia and Britain, it’s a fudgelike candy" - it's a fudgelike candy here in the US as well. It's usually an option at candy shops with a fudge bar, and it's especially common at the holidays. My grandmother always made it. I've never seen gumdrops in it though.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Have made multiple versions- my favorite subs/changes have been gingersnaps, haribo gummi bears for the spice drops, increased ratio of chocolate/biscoff butter base to the "rocks", which make it look nicer and held together better. Tried pistachios but think flavor is a bit mild and so it's a waste. When I have time would like to make marshmallows since I'm really not crazy about the store-bought ones.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cookie butter, or speculoos is found at trader Joe's, some regular grocery stores with the peanut butter. Think Nutella with slightly different flavor.  Airlines serve Biscoff cookies, same thing, same flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cookie butter and the Biscoff cookies they are based on don't taste anything like Nutella. They are more like a spiced shortbread cookie.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used this as a rough guideline. I was going to make my Bubby’s haystacks this morning and I went sideways! Butterscotch for the chips, peanut butter instead of the speculoos, 5 oz Chinese chow mein noodles instead of cookies, marshmallows, the end of a bag of mixed nuts, some tiny cinnamon chips, and a cinnamon spice blend topped with a gold and pearl sprinkle with snowflake sprinkles. All stuff I’ve had around and am always looking for ways to use up. What a fun combination and looks so pretty!  Cooling now but I intend to slice smallish for a party tray of sweets.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For folks from Florida and other southern states, Publix carries Biscoff Cookie Butter. Look for it near the peanut and almond butters.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these with Trader Joe’s Cookie Butter, a package TJ’s Fruity Gummy Candies, and pistachios. Presentation didn’t look as stunning compared to the spice drops, but it’s still pretty. Overall very easy recipe and will hit the spot for a potluck.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Like another commenter, I am gluten free so used granola butter instead of cookie butter. There's a good amount of spice in granola butter, so I left out the added spices in the recipe. Used gluten free gingersnaps and replaced the spice drops with sour cherries - turned out delicious and fun!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Easy and fun recipe. Easily made vegan for a friendly gift. I used vegan marshmallow, biscoff Cookies, marshmallows and pecans. Looking forward to trying with different components. Maybe chopped Reese’s? Yum. Slicing was a little tough, but maybe that was due to what I put inside.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sohla absolutely crushed it with this dessert! My dad and I made this ahead of Christmas—and as a chef of 45 years, he was blown away by this fun and easy holiday recipe! The only sub we made was Swedish fish (red only) for the gum drops, and they turned out beautifully in the cross section cut. Tasted great too!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Loving the rocky road and have made it like 4x this year. Tried a variation with crystallized ginger and extra pumpkin pie spice and a mix of choc and butterscotch chips, another with fruit jellies and salted pistachios. All great!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Margie S thanks for some great ideas for variations! I made this too and followed the recipe but now I’ve got the hang of it I’ll be brave enough to try new combinations! Happy Holidays!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this with my 10 year old - it was very easy which is a great relief this time of year.  We have nut allergies so I tried rice krispies instead. They didn't give a crunch but I still liked the textural result. Because they were more absorbant I had to make a more of the melted chocolate than the recipe normally calls for. They look festive when cut up. I brought them to a work party and received positive reviews.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made and gifted 6 batches of this recipe, stealing a thin slice for myself from each batch. Yummy and so easy to make.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Quick and easy. I subbed craisins for the gum drops, and cut each slice into about 8 small pieces about the size of a large caramel. With the combination of textures in each piece, the result reminded me of a Chunky candy bar. My husband loved it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was fun, easy, and yummy! Will definitely be making again next holidays. I used half the cookie butter so the texture would firmer and more like the rocky road we had in Sydney. Thanks

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Vegan option!
Did this with my grandson (4). Spectacular!
added quinoa puffs... really cute.
Forgot the marshmallows, so, now it's vegan.
(I already only buy vegan chocolate and candies; speculoos is vegan, the spread too. There are of course vegan marshmallows, so might try that next time. Though, those are usually, weaker, meltier, so not sure about that)
Posting from the Netherlands

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We call this crack at my house.  The combination of chocolate, gummies (Target worms that I cut into 1/4" pieces), salty, spicy is addictive.  I have made this 4 times.  Found the speculous bitter.  The Nutella version was much better.  I liked the milk chocolate version better than semisweet though the chips don't melt well.  Ended up putting it in the food processor to get it smooth. Though I am not usually a fan of sprinkles they add a crunchy texture that really complements the candy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Although the intro to the recipe rightly points out that you can swap in any kind of nut butter or mix-ins you want, I highly recommend giving this a try as written (I could not find Speculoos-brand cookies or cookie butter, but in North America the Biscoff brand of is almost the same thing and readily available in supermarkets). The spiced cookies and cookie spread, and the pumpkin pie spice, really add a special, moreish flavour to the whole thing that feels a bit more festive than usual.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: the easiest and most crowd pleasing recipe. Take this to all of your holiday parties people will be so impressed and they look amazing! Truly idiotproof.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
1. I don't have speculoos cookie butter in my local stores, we found Biscoff cookie butter and that works great. 
2. My picky kids, who refuse to eat nuts in any form, will eat any that I put in this, it's so good they don't notice. 
3. Genuinely, it has no business being this delicious. It's now a part our official Christmas cookie line up.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made a version with Oreo’s, Nutella, and gummy worms for my 2025 Cookie Box. Stoner and kiddo favorite alike, most of 2nd most talked about this year. We do 40-50 boxes each solstice and this one helps ease the baking pains a bit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What the heck are speculoos cookies?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Mmm,… fudgey goodness. I’m thinking of using some leftover candied fruits from fruitcake baking instead of the gumdrops, plus mini-marshmallows, almond butter, and whole roasted almonds.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I looked up cookie butter and speculoos. These are expensive ingredients so I'm hesitant to try this recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Similar to peanut butter but made from speculoos cookies.  Trader Joe's has a great version.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In the U.S., we mostly know speculoos by the brand name Biscoff. Biscoff makes a jarred spread out of the cookies, with the consistency of peanut butter, known generally as cookie butter. Not sure how to make your own, though.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We've made this since I was a child (I'm 53), with butter, chocolate chips, small multicolored marshmallows, and chopped pecans. We call it "stained glass windows"; the recipe came from a "Cookies and Candies" book from the 60s or 70s. I'm excited to try this variation!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In the US you can find “Biscoff” brand speculoos-type cookies in most grocery stores. “Cookie butter” refers to Biscoff cookie butter sold in small jars similar to Nutella—it’s a creamy, Biscoff cookie flavored spread.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In the video, she uses a jar of Biscoff cookie butter—and says you can sub in peanut butter or tahini. Never seen it Biscoff in stores, but will look!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Trader Joe’s usually has cookie butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: To those asking about cookie butter - Biscoff is the main brand!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Trader Joe carries Speculoos  (cookie butter).  It is a sweet cinnamon forward spread.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If you live in the US and have a Trader Joe's near you, they have cookie butter year round. In any store that has it, it will likely be near the peanut butter. It is a bit thicker than peanut butter and is basically made with ground up speculoos (spiced, kind of like a crunchy gingerbread) cookies and a few other ingredients. I've never made my own cookie butter but Google would probably have an answer on that one.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: People are asking about the cookie butter - I presume they mean something like the biscoff cookie spread (?), found next to Nutella and nut butter in well-stocked stores.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You can buy cookie butter at Trader Joe’s.  My local grocery has it too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Trader Joe’s makes a speculoos cookie butter for those of us lucky enough to live near one.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: To our friends asking about cookie butter, Trader Joe's makes a product called Speculoos Cookie Butter Spread. I'm assuming that is the ingredient that is called for here. Could brand names be specified when specialty items are used in NYT recipes?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yes, what is speculoos cookie butter and where in the world do you find it besides Australia.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Sohla, you are a wonderful presenter! Fantastic video demo. 

These are super cool, I definitely want to try making them (I doubt my upstate chain grocery store has the cookie butter, but Nutella and PB will be available).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Lots of folks asking where to get this. Trader Joe’s sells this, next to the peanut butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can you freeze this? How long can you make it ahead and keep it in the fridge or freezer? Thanks!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Dear Sage, re: "What is cookie butter?" Cookie butter is found with the Peanut Butter in the grocery store. Brand name is "Biscoff Cookie Butter", named after their yummy Biscoff cookies. There are also store-brand versions called "Speculoos" Cookie Butter. Both brands are found at Kroger's (Fred Meyer), Safeway, and Winco. Good Luck.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For those asking about cookie butter: https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cookie_butter A lot of supermarkets have this on the shelf next to peanut butter. Trader Joe’s sells a version. I’ve also found it on line. It’s delicious…..

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Trader Joe's has a Speculoos Cookie Butter, and I have seen the Lotus Biscoff Cookie Butter in large USA grocery stores in the European/imported foods section.  The Biscoff cookies that Sohla uses can be found in that section as well.  They're also available on Amazon.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
My 5 year old and I watched all the new cookie week videos, and she voted for this one as the first recipe we try. We took Sohla’s fancy route, with Nutella and mixed quality dark/milk chocolate bars. Very rich and surprisingly delicious - appealing to kids and adults alike! They don’t carry speculoos cookies at our grocery store so subbed Dare spice snaps and worked in a pinch. Would highly recommend!

Next up, Lemon-Tumeric Crinkles…

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I live in suburban Florida, and our local Publix stocks speculoos cookies and butter under the Biscoff brand. Trader Joe’s also stocks cookie butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Interesting that the recipe calls for chocolate chips. In Australia chocolate chips don't melt very well, which makes them hold shape when baked. Why not use blocks of chocolate, which melt easily?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could you use dulce de leche instead of the biscoff?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In the US I found speculaas from Bostonspice and king Arthur baking co. Hope to make these for family Christmas party

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Speculoos = Biscoff. Speculoos Cookie Butter is Biscoff Spread. Look for it next to Nutella and Peanut Butter. It’s in most larger supermarkets these days, but also at places like Target.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The classic UK version that i grew up with includes both Nutella and also some golden syrup, which would make the set-up chocolate more malleable, and just biscuits, and is sliced as a cake.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For everyone asking what cookie butter is, its a strange and wonderful American invention. If I were y'all, I'd maybe sub with Nutella, peanut butter, or some other type of sweet spread. Marmite need not apply.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Would these freeze well?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I just learned my Meijer store in a small town in Michigan has Lotus Biscoff cookies AND Biscoff cookie butter!   This recipe is totally doable!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cookie Butter is in every Trader Joe’s and almost every other grocery store I’ve ever been in. By the peanut butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Question: would this melt/soften too much if left at room temperature?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not sure where I went wrong. I followed the ratios but turned out so dense could barely cut through it.  Maybe because used Nutella?  Expected mine wouldn't be as pretty because used dried cranberries instead of gum drops but since there's no separation between elements there's none of the mosaic beauty.  That said it is really delicious with balance of salt from the pecans & tartness of cranberries.  Nice crunch from the Amaretti I used but no flavor & not a hint of the pumpkin pie spice.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I just made this with what I had on hand, trusting that the recipe is a flexible as promised. My ingredients included: almonds, dried cherries, tahini, coconut cookies, Swedish fish candies, marshmallows. I doubled the recipe, eyeballed a bunch of measurements, and topped with holiday sprinkles. It’s chilling now, and fingers crossed for success! We have a cookie swap at work today so these are headed that way.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Maria - how did the cookies go over at the swap?!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These were fantastic!
I have made them twice this week both times with sun butter instead of speculoos butter it adds so much rich fudginess. The first time I made it pretty much as written the second time I added mint extract and trador joes holiday mint stars. Wow great recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was weird and delicious and so much fun. They look beautiful, and, even though I knew the ingredients going in, I was so surprised by just how much it tasted like THE HOLIDAYS.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: SO GOOD.  I omitted the pumpkin spice (I didn't have any) (yes I know I can make some, TYVM) and my mix-ins were Biscoff cookies, toasted pecans, dried cherries, and marshmallows.  A full slice was a bit too much so I've cut mine into squares, as you would for fudge.  Thanks Sohla for this recipe - I'm sure this will be a forever holiday indulgence!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We loved these! Simple and delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: After cutting, do you store the slices in the fridge or at room temperature?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Meow I have had mine out at room temp for a couple of days now and there’s no melting or stickiness.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm in Mexico City, so it's weird that cookie butter is more widely available here than in NYC?, if not ubiquitous or well known. Mercado Libre has a Great Value version and Soriana down the street has Biscoff on the shelves.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My 6 year old and I had fun making this.  We used Christmas (peppermint) gum drops and walnuts instead of pecans. We bought the cookie butter and Speculoos cookies at TJ’s. My 6 year old claimed to like it, but I have never seen him not finish a cookie.  I thought it was just ok. I think the peppermint gum drops ruined it.  I would make it again with dried fruit and a higher quality chocolate.  I do love how easy it is to make and that it is super kid friendly.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this tonight and had a lot of fun!  I love cookie week!  Couldn't find the gumdrops so substituted Skittles gummies and used chili crisp cashews for the nuts.  If you are in the USA, the speculoos (biscoff) cookies and the cookie butter are sold in Target stores if you have one near you.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For those asking what is 'cookie butter', I found Biscoff Cooke Butter on the same aisle as peanut butter, almond butter, nutella, and used Biscoff cookies as the cookie crumble. I think this is fun and beautiful, and super easy to make.  Possibly up the pumpkin pie spice, maybe add candied ginger?  It was lovely and festive on the serving tray, I can also see it being very pretty in little treat bags.  Yes, to someone else's comments, this would be a great make with kids and seniors recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I don't like biscoff or cookie butter. What would be a good substitution?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: After our travels to the Netherlands and Belgium, we fell in love with speculuss. I find it many places, Trader Joe’s  has cookies butter , even Kroger.  You can make the spice blend yourself and have around similar to pumpkin pie spice .  The spice blend is different. Many spices including pepper.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: While visiting New Zealand over Christmas last year, I was very confused as to what Rocky Road and Lollie Slices actually were (they were often seen next to each other).  When I asked about them, one was often used to describe the other, adding to my confusion.  However, after making Sohla's recipe for Rocky Road, I am a happy convert!  An easy cookie to make and share.  And now I have a use for my Bischof Cookie Butter that does not involve simply eating it by the spoonful! Thanks Sohla!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For those who love an insanely sweet treat, this recipe is just right! Made it with my kids who had loads of fun, although they now refuse to eat it as it's very sweet indeed. Would recommend omitting the speculous cream and add some dark chocolate in the mix. Used cinnamon and coffee spice in the chocolate which achieved a great result. T

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this as described except my add-ins were marshmallows, glace cherries, Nilla wafers, and pecans. Cut into bicotti-like slices and then into thirds. VERY sweet but also addictive. One loaf pan makes more than enough—we are keeping a quarter of the batch and giving the rest away, otherwise we’d never get through it all. A fun and fast recipe, highly indulgent, looks pretty, good for potlucks or cookie exchanges.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I could not find any spice drops that weren't mint flavor (I didn't think mint would go well with all these flavors) so I used a holiday version of the candy "dots." I was still a little weary so I only used a few of them but it turned out great! I also added pretzels and it added another salty element that we liked.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Standard containers of Biscoff cookie butter, Biscoff cookies, spice drops, and marshmallows make enough for this recipe X3. So I would recommend getting enough chocolate and pecans to triple the recipe! It is so so easy and tasty. The slices of rocky road are adorable and very festive looking. It's the perfect low effort, high reward recipe for holiday cookie giving.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Omitted pecans and increased amount of cookies and marshmallows to compensate, a new holiday tradition!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I found speculoos cookie butter at Walmart (of all places). I ordered the cookies, but the order was canceled due to "location." Trader Joe's allegedly has them, so I'll look there today. Sounds like this recipe is endlessly flexible, so if I have to use gingersnaps, I will.

Bon appetit!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Trader Joe's does carry it.  It's called Speculoos Crunchy Cookie Butter Spread.  It's what I used in this recipe and it worked well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made as written.  Used DOTS for the gum drops.  A big hit for something gooey and sweet at the end of dinner. I think this would be great to make with kids.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You can purchase the cookie butter at Trader Joe's.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Looks delightful! Trader Joe’s sometimes has a product called Speculoos Cookie Butter (it’s hit and miss.)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Biscoff and Trader Joes both have this kind of cookie butter. You can buy both on Amazon.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'd like to make a gluten free version of this recipe.  It looked promising until the "speculoos" and "speculoos cookie butter".

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Like numerous other people, I came here to look for info on cookie butter and substitutes. Here you go: https://www.foodnetwork.com/how-to/packages/food-network-essentials/what-is-cookie-butter I have some of the Trader Joe's one, but not enough so I'm going to use some almond butter or tahini. I like the idea of this but don't want gumdrops, so I'm just gonna break up a bunch of cookies I found on sale, marshmallows and pecans.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was able to find both Speculoos butter and cookies on Amazon.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this with Golden Grahams cereal, mini marshmallows, mini-Junior Mints and some macadamia nuts left over from Thanksgiving. Great pop of peppermint from the mints. So easy to put this together with anything you might have in the pantry.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Rocky Road is also known as a candy in the U.S., not just in Australia and U.K. See's has been selling it for decades.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Trader Joe's sells speculoos Cookie Butter in my area,

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Oh no, my  chocolate chips seized, with lots of things mixed in.  Even though I microwaved it all do only 10 seconds at a time.  What can I do now?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Nope. Made these as described and just NOPE. Too sweet, strange flavor combination, and a waste of perfectly good chocolate.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Oh Man! The only thing I did different on this recipe (I did watch the video) was substitute dried cherries and pistachio pieces for the red and green gum drops. And it came out like cardboard. I have a friend that is a chef who suggested that using chocolate chips was the wrong kind of chocolate to use for this recipe and that my chocolate had seized. So she suggested I melt the whole mess and add nearly scalding whipping cream to see if that would loosen it up. It did just a tiny bit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this the other day, and like others had to substitute based on what I had. I used salted pistachios instead of walnuts and Welch’s fruit chews instead of gum drops - not as pretty! But the end result is pretty interesting. I also added cinnamon to round things out. Looking forward to reactions from the people I bring it to :-)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I had a hard time locating the spice drops, finally found them in our CVS store. Personally, I didn’t like the flavor they added, I did like the texture though. Maybe, next time I would use gummies or dots

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Terri I swapped gum drops for red Swedish fish and it was the perfect mild flavor! Same texture and look achieved too!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Trader Joes has speculoos with all of their other cookies and speculoos cookie butter is in their peanut butter aisle.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these with Tony’s Chocolonely - 1dark, 1 milk bar combined. Next time I’ll probably use a nut butter and dried fruit with pecans. I didn’t like the spice flavors, especially with the gumdrop/cookie bits. These remind me of the cathedral cookies from the 60’s.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were fun to make! The first time with spice drops. The second batch we swapped them out for M&Ms.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was a great recipe for kids to work on together as a kick off to winter break! A great recipe for young bakers who are learning to be independent in the kitchen. So forgiving and the best part for the kids was they could lick the bowls and spoons clean afterwards for a change.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I did biscoff cookies, sponge toffee, praline nut mix and dried tart cherries. Instead of the biscoff spread I added a tablespoon of fine espresso powder to cut some of the sweetness. Turned out great! Will make again next year.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not all mini marshmallows are equal in weight when measured by volume, & vice versa. Strongly recommend measuring the volume for the marshmallows, and if you like that ratio, stick with it. I first weighed the marshmallows and came up with about 2/3 cup.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was so fun to make and really delicious! The salt at the beginning really enhanced all the other fun flavors that were added after the chocolate and cookie butter was melted and incorporated. For friends asking what cookie butter is or having trouble finding it, I used Biscoff cookie butter and the Biscoff cookies. It was readily available in my Midwest grocery store.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: YUM. This was my first rocky road experience and thanks to Sohla’s whimsical recipe and charming video, it was a resounding success. This was so little effort, and really good. The crunchy cookie bits, the squishy marshmallow buts, and the chewy gummy bits were texturally so fun.  Don’t try to slice it too thin, like I did, or you risk a falling apart mess.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I overdid the add-ins. Next time I may double the chocolate base but start with the additions for a single batch.  I can always add more.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Will they store or melt at room temperature?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This turned out great! It’s definitely going to become one of my go to holiday recipes. The speculoos cookies/butter and the pumpkin pie spice really punch up the flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is totally worth making. Couldn’t find spice drops, so used holiday Dots and it was good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Made this tonight to take to a friend’s place for Christmas Eve dinner tomorrow. My one change was to use Graham Crackers instead of Speculoos, because they were out. ( the y were out of marshmallows too…was it because of this recipe?!) 
The recipe reminds me (in appearance and general concept) of a more child- friendly version of a recipe from the Green & Blacks cookbook — Konditor & Cook’s Chocolate Biscuit Cake. That one is made with 60% dark chocolate, unsalted butter, golden syrup, digestive biscuits, walnuts, sultanas, glacé cherries and an egg. 
This recipe is much simpler - easy for kids to make, and suitably whimsical.  
Can’t wait to taste it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wonderful to see Rocky Road in the NYT! As an Australian overseas who has been eating Rocky Road for 40  years, I’ve never had it with speculoos or cookie butter. So don’t sweat it if you can’t find specific ingredients, you can sub in any plain cookie. One of the many wonderful things about Rocky Road is that you can easily vary the ingredients to match your pantry or even food allergies - gluten free pretzels are a yummy addition!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I added dried cherries instead of gumdrops drops, and lightly salted the edge of the bars. So good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I loved this recipe and will make it again. Great for gifting. Next time I won’t use Dots gumdrops (too chewy, too blah), but a sugared, soft gummy of some kind. Candied ginger might be the ticket. It’s probably just as well I went with the Dots because I would have eaten it all in one sitting if it were any more delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I don't know where this easy candy has been all my life! I, too, know many of the people I cook for would object to spice drops. And I couldn't find any near me, so I used a mix of crystalized ginger, dried cherries, and Annie's fruit snacks.    And the second batch, I swapped the Biscoff cookies and cookie butter for Gluten Free Tates Zinger Ginger Cookies and Tahini.  So good!  And super fun to make.  A great addition to the cookie box.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My 6 year old son enjoyed making these alone! He was able to pick out different ingredients from the pantry to add. The fatter type sprinkles look much prettier then the tiny ball ones so I would go with those. We added chocolate covered pretzels and everyone love that.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These were initially unappealing with the gum drops, etc. I wrote them off as kid food. But I watched the video and took Sohla's suggestion for a variant... which was excellent!
--Used a blend of good dark and milk chocolate bars (mostly dark)
--2 big spoonfuls of peanut butter
--3/4 t cinnamon
--1/4 t kosher salt
--1 cup each: salted peanuts, marshmallows, Corn Chex, speculoos

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This version of Rocky Road is so unique and was a great addition to our Christmas dessert table.  It’s very rich though, so I cut the 12 pieces into thirds for a yield of 36 pieces. In the U.S. Lotus markets speculoos as Biscoff cookies and cookie butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Made these with my 9 year old and the recipe was very easy and adaptable.
We used Nutella, roasted pecans, rice krispies, marshmallows and lots of sprinkles.
Our mix was way pared down from the original recipe, but it was delicious and will definitely be a part of our holiday baking rotation.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was a grownup type of rocky road. The cookie / speculo0s butter made all the difference. A lovely presentation in the the cookie boxes I gifted this Christmas. Easy to execute. Will be making these again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Absolutely no one in my family liked these and I ended up throwing the whole thing out. What a waste of my speculoos cookies and paste, not to mention my good chocolate! We love all the ingredients but not together, apparently. I think it was the pumpkin spice flavor that really was off-putting in these so if I did these again, I would leave that out but I honestly doubt I’ll ever do them again. We love pumpkin spice but I wish I’d omitted it this time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Kathryn M or getting tinfoil between your teeth! I still have some old style fillings! OUCH!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these last holiday season for gifting. I got VERY creative with them. My favorite mixi-in? Pretzels! These are so easy and so much fun. I would highly recommend making these with children. Or better yet let your children make these on their own! Everyone who received them wanted the recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Amazing delicious, great for those types of events when you don’t know what to bring. I put a few in clear gift bags during the holidays for presents

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this last year and it was SO good. Definitely don’t skip the gingerbread pieces. Question: Has anyone put this in a cookie box? If so, did the flavors take over the entire box like peppermint does?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I know it’s a kid friendly recipe or whatever but it would be way better with butter or something in the chocolate to be more fudgey and less like just hard chocolate. And pretzels would also be nice in it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For those wondrring about speculoos, it's dutch.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Nigella Lawson version is much better

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The effort - low. Result - spectacular.  A real show stopper when the cookie box is open.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used peanut butter instead of speculoos cookie butter, but did have Biscoff's for the special taste. These are delicious, easy to make, very versatile, and incredibly festive. A big hit!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can I make this on a half sheet pan for more of a “bark” or drop it on wax paper lined cookie sheets?  First time it was hard to slice as a loaf shape.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these with pomegranates instead of gum drops and they were amaaaaazing

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was surprised by the comment that Rocky Road might be synonymous with ice cream in the US. I've always associated it with a soft chocolate cookie, with chunks of semi-sweet chocolate, walnuts, and marshmallow. No speculoos, pumpkin spice, or gumdrops. Archway's Rocky Roads (now, I believe, discontinued) used to be my favorite cookie.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'd like to give this to neighbors as a holiday gift. Would you make one loaf per family? Would one loaf be a decent amount for 2 families? Thanks for an guidance.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this with chopped candied ginger this year instead of gumdrops. Last year I found that grocery store gumdrops were unpleasantly chewy. Anything soft and fruity would make a nice substitute taste-wise, if not for the cute color. I’ll try adding candied pineapple next. Just gotta find something with a similar texture that is colorful.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these as a part of a holiday cookie table I like to set up for my coworkers.  I make about 12 - 14 types of cookies to have a variety and serve with homemade hot chocolate and homemade peppermint marshmallow.  These were a hit, and they were gone quite fast.  Personally, the spiced gumdrops were not my favorite, but they look beautiful and festive when they are sliced.  I might try it with candied citrus peels sometime.  This recipe can be changed up to fit your tastes.  The marshmallows, crunchy cookies and nuts add such great texture.  Mix it up.  They’re fun and festive!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious! I thought it would look pretty on my cookie plate, but I’ll make it again because it’s a delight to eat.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hol-ee Cow!!! This is the best new bread-y thing I've enjoyed this much!! From the muffin bottoms to the pull-apartness to that amazing dough, these are the greatest! And the tops are browned more, plus the flaked salt, so they taste VERY pretzelly (if the pretzel had a recessive gene for croissantism)!! Parker House WHAT?!?! Nope, these gorgeous buns are more fun to make than they are to eat, and they are sublime to eat! WOW.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Louise Thompson. I had NO plans to makr dinner rolls yesterday, but this recipe came up and based on your candid - and inspiring - review, I made a double batch for no particular reason other than to freeze half.   I share your "Hol-ee-COW" opinion!  However for those trying to use free-standing silicone muffin cups instead of a muffin tin.....it doesn't work.   The rolls  don't brown and puff up well.  When I "properly" made another batch (because I had doubled the recipe) with a popover pan, they came out so good that nobody ate much of anything else I served lolololol.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love it... these are my new dinner rolls. I only baked for half the time and froze them in freezer bags and I take them out and cook them for the remaining time to serve.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Funny that it refers to rolling into a square, then says 11" wide (which would be an 11 inch square). Then it says to cut lengthwise and squares don't have length. So... confusing for a second.
Roll 1/2 the dough into an 11 inch square and cut  the square into 6 even strips... Stack the strips... and cut the stack into 6 even pieces...    Repeat with other 1/2 of the dough.
