Fun and easy recipe with option to add herbs, garlic, or cinnamon sugar to tweak. Looks fancy for special dinner.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: That confused me for a bit, too. :-)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these using the linked enriched dough recipe. They puffed up more than the picture when baking and taste great. I'd recommend putting the tin on a rimmed sheet as the butter from the rolls leaked out and dripped. Some layers fell away when I removed them from the muffin tin and, despite buttering everything, I did have some sticking to the tin. Don't skip the salt on top. It is essential. Fussy and a lot of work but I will happily make again for Thanksgiving.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these rolls yesterday. I have made bread with Clair Saffitz All-Purpose Enriched Bread dough recipe, and loved the bread for toast and sandwiches. However using this dough to make these rolls produced a beautiful looking roll but one that was disappointing.  The rolls stuck to the buttered pan, the vertical slices  easily separated  and even with all the butter and salt the roll was not satisfying. The next day they were even less tasteful. Make the bread, not the rolls.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The times do not add up to 7 hours 40 minutes.  Where is step 6?  How do I brush the top of the muffin tin (Step 1)?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Ann Marie
You have to make the All Purpose Enriched Dough recipe linked in the Ingredients list. That’s where the time is. Also, there is no Step 6. Step 5 is the completion.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Ann Marie you’re meant to brush the butter in the muffin cups and on the metal that connects all the cups. The whole pan has to be painted with butter so the rolls don’t stick when they overflow the cups.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
The step 6 they are referring to is in the recipe for the All-Purpose Bread Dough.

One of the ingredients for THIS recipe is the dough that you make in the All-Purpose Bread dough recipe.  Follow that recipe to make the dough (but only go up to Step 6 in that recipe) and then come back to this recipe to finish making these rolls with that dough.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I've made these twice now and they are awesome! First time I used a metal muffin pan and the second time I used a silicone muffin pan. I had to use the full 22 min to get the right color in the metal tin. I had to go up to 27 min for color on the bottom in the silicone pan. Worth every moment either way!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My husband wished for  a soft bread for dipping in olive oil, remembering doing this at a favourite restaurant. This is the perfect bread for that. I made a whole charcuterie board. The way the bread split apart was perfect for all the condiments ready to eat with it: cheese, pate, prosciutto. I enjoyed the process of making them. I imagine substituting a whole wheat dough using the same assembling mode.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Oh my!! The pictures for this recipe got me. I first saw the recipe for Thanksgiving, but it seemed too labor intensive to tackle. So I waited until I was asked to bring rolls for Christmas.

I prepared by reading and rereading the recipe, and watching the tutorial twice, then reading all comments.

What I learned: much easier than I thought. Make the rolls on the smaller side (3 layers each) make 2 dozen instead of 12, I don’t have a rolling pin so a wine bottle worked just fine and my 11 inches was only approximate, can make a few days ahead.

Absolutely would make again (in the beginning I swore I would NEVER make them again) the finished project was fabulous!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can you make these with vegan butter?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @dh. if you are asking me, NO.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These rolls are delicious, but absolutely massive. They melded together on top of the muffin pan and looked a little grotesque. 

Because they are much too large as an accompaniment to a meal, I would like to try again and limit the layers to three, doubling the amount made.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these for Thanksgiving and they were a huge hit! I made sure to butter the pan all around the edges so they wouldn’t stick, and they came out pretty well although a couple spots needed some gentle wiggling to remove them. Most of them did also fall apart when I put them in a bread bowl as others have mentioned, but my guests were happy because they could choose how much they wanted (a big roll or just a few pieces). I served them with softened salted butter for spreading and everyone raved about them. Don’t skip the flaky salt! Maldon brand worked great — I bought mine on Amazon.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Do you think I could make and refrigerate the dough two days before I cook these?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Excellent, and a huge hit with the family. I used my favorite enriched dinner roll recipe (Williams Sonoma bread cookbook, buttermilk and eggs) and divided each half into 12 rolls, not 6, because when I did 6, the rolls totally overwhelmed my muffin pan. Used lots of butter but I skipped the egg wash and sprinkled kosher salt onto the last butter application, which worked just fine.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I am obsessed!  The fan-tans were difficult for me to arrange and cut, so I changed the shape to crescents for the remaining half, and those are the ones that flew out of the bread basket.  I also thought that the portions were too big, so I'm now making 24 crescents.  I've also replaced ½ cup of the all-purpose flour with ½ cup of who!e-wheat flour.  Cool, freeze, then thaw and reheat for only 5 minutes.  My spouse is now asking if I could sprinkle cinnamon sugar on a few. Sure!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I did a trial run last week to see how these would work for Thanksgiving. First off, these things are huge! Im probably going to cut them smaller and make 24 rather than 12. They are really too big to serve with such a big meal and they looked a bit deformed in the pan because of the size. They almost reminded me of a popover. I froze them after they were baked and they reheated beautifully.  I placed them on a sheet pan covered half time with foil and then uncovered to crisp up about 15 min at 400. Over all they were really good albeit a little more work on the front end but once they are in the fridge you dont have to worry about them for 24 hours then can either bake them fresh or reheat from the freezer which is what I am going to do!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: How does the dry yeast get activated? I thought you need to dissolve the yeast in water that is about 110F to activate it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Karen the activation that many recipes call for is meant to prove that your yeast is alive. It’s absolutely not necessary if you know a particular jar isn’t old, though the instant yeast does a little better at hydrating in a dough  without the activation period than the active dry

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these for Thanksgiving for my white roll loving husband.  Since I was going to be in the kitchen all day it did not seem a problem to keep track of them for all of that time.  As I was making them I was thinking “well it is fun to try a new recipe/method for making rolls but this is sooo time consuming”.  I doubted I would make them again.  I followed the directions exactly.  OMGosh!  These are amazing!  We loved them.  The recipe is now in my Thanksgiving folder and they will appear again on our table…maybe for his birthday?  We are splitting the leftover slices and toasting them gently in the toaster oven.  No butter needed (:  Thanks for this recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could I use frozen bread dough and start there ?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @GD - that kind of defeats the purpose of this recipe - the beginning step, creating the enriched dough, is critical to this bread.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sure, I don't see why not. Whatever works for you. Pick a bread dough you like, then just roll out, butter, slice, etc. This recipe more about a technique for using dough than for actually making dough, so go ahead! Use good butter and salt the top, let them rise enough in tins to become puffy, should be great. Enjoy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Any ideas why mine nearly all came unstuck fan--wise? The taste is great, the recipe not too hard for a novice bread-baker,  but it what would make them  fall into 4 pieces?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Currently making these and they are not rising in the muffin tin :(. Anyone know if they will still bake okay?  I followed the enriched dough recipe yesterday and the dough chilled overnight..

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Give them time in a warm place, best covered with slightly damp plastic wrap. Will warm up and rise, just needs time. Good luck!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really great recipe and crowd pleaser! Will be a new recipe tradition

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hi! I used Claire Saffitz's All-Purpose Enriched Bread recipe as base. I do not have a stand mixer but made the tangzhong, made the dough then left in fridge over night. Next day, left on counter to rise, punched down when rising above bowl. At night, back in fridge. Did this again next day. On third day, rolled out but forgot to spread with butter. Cut into strips, shaped, another rise and baked. Forgetting butter did not matter. These are delicious! Soft, salty, yellow, great! Recommend!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I did a test run of these and they are tasty but huge. Also my layers came apart due to the size of muffin. Where did I go wrong in the stacking?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made these for thanksgiving and they were a hit. The recipe is pretty simple, but I screwed up the first batch and forgot to halve the dough before cutting into strips. I managed to salvage the rolls, but that’s a gotcha I need to keep in mind next time. 

Also, the bottoms of the rolls did not all fuse together. I I’ll try compressing the bottoms next time before baking. 

Definitely making these for Christmas as well. They’re delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these rolls last year for thanksgiving and fell in love. This year, I decided to double the recipe. Don’t do this if yon have a standard kitchen aid mixer - it’s far too much dough for the bowl. I ended up cutting the enriched dough into two batches and working them separately and they turned out great, but overall I didn’t end up saving myself time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Absolutely incredible! Mouth wateringly good rolls! I used a cheese scraper on cold butter and sandwiched that between the layers. Soft. Buttery. Very very good. Anyone tried cinnamon, sugar and butter? And what about laminating 3 times?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can’t figure out the 7 hour proving time.  I don’t see it in the recipe.  Am I missing something? Thought my grandchildren would love these.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Alison
Click on the Enriched Dough Recipe in the ingredients list. The making of that dough is where the rest of the time is accounted for.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Marcy, Did you halve the recipe for the enriched bread for these rolls?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: They tasted amazing but they fell apart when I removed them from the muffin pan - they didn't stick to the pan, they just didn't stick together. I'd sure like some suggestions for keeping them together. I need to figure this out before Thanksgiving.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hi! Try skipping the butter between the layers. I forgot to butter the dough after rolling it out and before cutting into strips. So, the strips had no butter between them. I just rolled out, cut into strips, layered the strips, put in muffin tins, let rise again and baked. They did not fall apart and were a big hit. So maybe skip the last  butter layer (on purpose!). Hope it worked out. Happy Thanksgiving!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I had not used the enriched bread recipe before but decided to take a stab at these rolls. Delicious! If you haven’t made a tang zhong before, I recommend watching the video. It’s a good visual.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: LOVE THESE! I made these for Thanksgiving and they were a particular hit with the little ones. I used oil on the muffin tin instead of butter and it was still totally delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really good. Used the linked dough recipe (with some mods). Easy and fun.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Best to remove these from the pan after they have cooled, or while still warm?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A success! I took other's advice and made them smaller, (I also used some mini muffin tins). They took longer to cook than I thought, but it could be that the oven door kept getting opened during our family Thanksgiving, everyone needed the oven for something! I made biscuits last year and they were awful, this one was very good. Don't forget the salt!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was so excited about this based on all the reviews… they did not beat the classic Parker house rolls in my opinion, I will not be making this recipe again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these for Thanksgiving and they were a huge hit! I made a double batch (giving my new 7 quart KitchenAid a run for its money) and after reading the comments here decided to make some in jumbo muffin cups and some in popover pans. The popover pans worked the best. I used half bread flour and a little extra yeast (to quicken the process). So delicious! Adding it to my faves!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Novice bread baker here who has only ever made Mom’s dinner rolls - with and without bread flour. There is a noticeable difference when I use bread flour. Any reason it won’t work in this recipe? Can’t wait to try them and don’t want to screw them up.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are insanely good and surprisingly easy! Just dedicate some time to making that enriched dough (my mixer went through a workout) and the rest is a breeze. These would even be good with some fresh rosemary or thyme on top.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I've made these rolls twice - both times with excellent results. Second time used only half the enriched dough (kept other half in fridge for cinnamon rolls following day) - 10 rolls from half the dough (vs 6 rolls). Smaller rolls stayed together better and were just as pretty. Best when timed to be served warm right out of the over.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: "To serve, crumble over the fried sage leaves" EDIT: To serve, crumble the fried sage leaves over the chicken. (I've never tried to crumble fried chicken, but it sounds tough!)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @ChrisK thanks! Made me laugh and that is tough these days.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This sounds brilliant. I dislike turkey because of its taste and love great fried chicken like what I grew up eating. Pray tell how do you discard all that oil after cooking? I can handle the messy frying cooking. Thank you

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Georgia Burg You are in luck! Wirecutter has an article on that in today’s NYT.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Georgia Burg you might want to try Fry Away. It’s plant based and you mix it into the hot oil. As the oil cools it becomes a solid so you can throw it out in the garbage. I get mine on Amazon but am sure you can get it from a ton of places.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Georgia Burg perhaps in the container you brined the chicken in?? Or I use gallon ziplock bags to discard the fat

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can the recipe be adapted for white meat/breasts?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Virginia My family made this for Thanksgiving 2024 with chicken tenders, and it worked great. You may have to adjust the cooking time for smaller pieces, but the recipe (as written with 00 flour and peanut oil) was delicious with the tenders.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@ES

Just an update to say we made this again for Thanksgiving 2025, and it is now cemented as a family tradition! Everyone loves it. We used chicken tenders and ended up adding the spice blend to the flour instead of adding it after frying. We added extra salt and cayenne, too. We also skipped the fried sage leaves, as we felt they didn’t add much last year. Great alternative to turkey for the holiday meal!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Clean and return oil to container and reuse. https://www.seriouseats.com/clean-cooking-oil-with-gelatin-technique

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Chioeke Wow! Thanks for the link.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Chioeke can confirm this works well. The oil was cloudy the next day in the fridge, but cleared right up when heating.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Chioeke our compost service takes used oils…

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I am from SoCal, I smiled when you mentioned Claim Jumper! Remember their chocolate cake? I always wanted to try it! Can the chicken be made a day ahead for a large party? If so, how would you reheat?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: How about adjustments for using breasts?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If trying to do ahead, would this recipe work if you dredged and flowered all pieces first and left in the fridge for an hour or until ready to fry?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @KC I cooked a big batch and flour’d everything then fried. The earlier pieces got gummy and I had to re-flour it. They still turned out great! But maybe there’s a way to dry the chicken more before flouring or keep the chicken dry after flouring?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If you use kosher chicken, it has already been brined (soaked and salted), eliminating a lot of time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe is fantastic! I'm not sure what exactly made it turn out to be the best fried chicken I've ever made, but it was. I left out the msg, used salt instead of the chicken bouillon powder, brined just thighs (opted to forego drumsticks) for 48 hours because I needed to cook it a day later than anticipated, but otherwise made it exactly. My guy is a fried chicken fanatic and he was very, very happy with how this turned out.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: As a food safety person, the idea of adding previously used chicken brine to the flour grossed me out. Recommend reserving some brine before adding the chicken during the marinating process. Otherwise, looks amazing!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Fay i was too at first but the you fry the chicken so that should be safe

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For all the users asking about cooking breasts, there is nothing fundamentally different about this recipe than other fried chicken recipes on NYTC. Use the ten-piece method of cutting bone-in breasts in half to get more surface area. Brine and fry per recipe and remove from oil around 165 deg instead of the higher finish temp for dark meat.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I couldn't wait until Thanksgiving. I made this for dinner last night, and it was delicious!  I also made mashed potatoes, fried cabbage, and cornbread. I fried extra sage leaves and put them in the mashed potatoes. My family luv'd it.  Thank you for the recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My mom’s fried chicken was the best!!! Instead of all the dredging, wet hand/dry hand, she put all the flour and seasonings in a paper bag, tossed in the chicken and shook vigorously until all pieces (including breasts!) were well coated. She was barely five feet tall. This was simultaneously a feat of physicality and act of love. She knew her family loooved fried chicken. As I read the recipe, I knew that Mom’s paper bag method would reign, and it did. Clean up is much easier too. Just toss!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: thats what I do! 1 step baby!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could you airfry this?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hands down the most thorough guidance on how to handle your oil temperature, and the results were perfect. Using the pizza flour as suggested and following her technique to go straight from brine to flour resulted in a shatteringly crisp exterior and a juicy, succulent interior. And the spice seasoning … up the cayenne a bit for a nice heat at the back of your palate … brings so much flavor! This is a winner. Once you do fried-chicken Thanksgiving, you’ll never go back!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: for a vegan take, swapped out the poultry in favor of seared and brined tofu that I tore into nuggets, then dredged and fried. I played around with brines and arrived at a familiar conclusion: Simple is best. Some brown sugar, some salt, some peppercorns, some sage. But an overnight brine is a must. I replaced the bouillon in Sohla’s original herb blend with nutritional yeast, and before adding it used about half of the blend to season the flour.  Tanya Sichynsky

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can I substitute turkey? How fun would that be?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: On that I am not sure as the cut up turkey parts don't seem as common as chicken. I will add that I used to deep fry turkeys for thanksgiving and the process, while intensive (and you need to buy a deep frying rig and do it outside so you don't burn the house down), is well worth it. We used to refer to the friend turkey skin as "turkey bacon". I know places where you can have them do it for you. Someone stole our rig which is a bummer but so it goes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
HMart (and other stores, I'm sure) sells tempura mix, which is cornstarch and fine flour. It's a fantastic option if you can't find 00 flour. 

I'm looking forward to making this instead of Turkey.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I was all excited until I realized there is no provision for the breast. 
Not everyone loves dark meat.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Do not use bare hands for the suggested steps. Either use tongs or wear gloves. Brine is so hard on the delicate skin of hands, fingers and especially cuticles.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is the first fried chicken I've ever made and also the best fried chicken I've ever had. Sohlae's recipes are the best with just the right amount of detail. I ended cooking the first batch probably closer to 11-12 minutes per side. We did all thighs because that's my favorite and I halved the recipe because it was just three of us. Everyone loved it, including my mostly vegetarian husband and my picky 7 year old.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: OMG!  That chicken was so much fun to make, especially with Sohla’s reassuring presence on the video next to me. A I went through what would otherwise be a scary frying process, Sohla calmed me down so I could enjoy it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Absolutely AMAZING. 
Have made this three times so far, twice for guests, each time gets the rave:  BEST FRIED CHICKEN EVER!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can you get good fried chicken if you remove the skin before dredging the parts through the flour mixture? Poultry skin really skeeves me out.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Jenny Not really. Your best bet is probably removing skin and baking in a crunchy coating.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yes, I've been making the Pickle-brined fried chicken sandwich recipe from NYT for a few years now, and it calls for boneless skinless thighs - it turns out amazing every time : )

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yes, NYT cooking's recipe for Pickle-Brined Fried Chicken sandwiches calls for boneless skinless chicken thighs, and they turn out great every time!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: When calibrating your thermometer note that higher altitudes have lower boiling temps. I live in Denver where water boils at 202°F/94.4°C.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If you can’t eat chicken for religious reasons, pork works just as well!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What religion permits pork but not chicken? Serious question (asking as a Jew who loves chicken and never touches the "other white meat")!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I believe Jasmine is a representative for the Society for the Preservation of Chickens


I suspect she might be a chicken herself...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could you air fry the chicken?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Has anyone any experience replacing wheat flour with a gluten free alternative?  If so, what did you use and how did it turn out?  THANKS

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I think that an instant flour with a powdery texture, such as Wondra, could be used.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: THANK YOU for this recipe!  We keep kosher and I cannot use buttermilk to brine.  I can't wait to try this!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hi Rusty: If you will be using a kosher chicken, the brining step isn't necessary.  Kosher chickens have already been salted in the koshering butchery process.

author: Sohla El-Waylly

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Jess I have not yet made this, but I make fried chicken with rice flour all of the time. In fact, my gluten eating food loving teenagers prefer it!
I suppose you could use a one-to-one gluten-free flour, but I typically do not, and find that rice flour fries up nice and crispy. It doesn’t always get the same beautiful shade of golden, but if that’s a problem to, you could add a little paprika.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: "Great, but what do you do with all that leftover oil?". If you used EVO, throw it away. If you used peanut oil or some other high-smoke oil IT IS STILL GOOD. Strain through a fine mesh strainer and put back on shelf. The oil should be good for at least a few fry sessions provided you do not abuse it. Label the bottle with what it was used for. My experience is that fried chicken oil can be used for french fries, etc. Oil used for fried fish should only be used for frying fish.

author: Sohla El-Waylly

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This. Recipe. Rocks. End.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I cooked two pieces last night and was very disappointed with the blandness. So last night I took the rest out of the brine, made more spice mix, double it really, and worked it into the chicken, covered overnight and the results today, Thanksgiving was way more what you would expect. I will make it again with this adjustment. Also needed to be salted coming out of the oil. I tried the lemon, didn't care for it, who puts lemon on fried chicken? not me...........

author: Sohla El-Waylly

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this for a Thanksgiving table of 8 tonight.  Amazing, and multiple guests declared it "the best fried chicken I've ever had".  I have to agree.  It even looked just like the NYT photos!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We made this for Thanksgiving and it was so good we might have to change our tradition. It needed far less flour than called for.  We also made chicken strips with boneless breasts and they were delicious.  Brined it less than 24!hours, no problem. Didn’t have all the spices, still the best fried chicken ever!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was the best fried chicken. Period. Crispy. Craggy. Perfectly spiced. I had never made fried chicken before and now I want to have a whole fried chicken party!! It was insanely good. Had to go to a few stores, but finally found a Knorrs powdered chiicken buillon. Just follow along and do everything Sohla tells you to do and you too, will be very happy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! Even my pescatarian son succumbed and had two thighs. From now on, this will be a Thanksgiving dinner tradition. I included some bone-in breasts (halved), but they took forever to fry, really putting us behind schedule. If you must include breasts, I'd advise going with the boneless.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Phenomenal. I’ve never deep fried before. Recipe is fantastic. Will make for friends Birthday.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this for the night before Thanksgiving and then tonight I modified it by using skinless chicken breasts that I cut and pounded so they would all be roughly the same size.  Total success both times—especially thanks to being able to watch the video and absorb the counsel about how to anticipate and carefully respond to the temperature.  I feel like a cooking genius!!!!!  I’m going to make this when we get together with friends at a rental house next summer.  Thank you so much Sohla !!!!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed this recipe precisely and I will say it's exceptional. I did a half batch for just the two of us and we absolutely loved it. It helps we're both fans of fried sage and have it growing all the time, and that completes this recipe. This would be great even if you don't have fresh sage to fry. I can't wait to make this for Thanksgiving. I don't care for turkey, and most of our guests don't usually, so how excited will they be for fried chicken next November!?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this last year and we used Bob's Red Mill 1 to 1 Gluten Free Flour. Even the non-Celiacs loved it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Never just discard oil!  It is considered a hazardous material.  Cool it, poor it back into the container and take to your local dump that takes hazardous materials.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What we do is cool it in the freezwer in a plastic container, then simply put it in the garbage. Since oil is not considered a hazardous substance by the EPA (see https://www.hazardouswasteexperts.com/is-used-oil-hazardous-waste/), and https://www.epa.gov/emergency-response/vegetable-oils-and-animal-fats, you do not need to take it to a dump that takes hazardous materials. "Hazardous materials" are very carefully defined and used cooking oil does not fall within the definition.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Nancy if your community takes food waste, they will take the used oil.  Our community has curbside food waste and a location has large containers for food waste, that’s where I would drop off my used oil.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
The nutritional info is way off the mark. Each person is consuming TWO pieces of chicken (or 1 leg), so the calories should be nowhere close to 2000 (daily avg intake.)

The issue is the oil. It is used for frying, but the nutrition software thinks it is fully consumed. 

Calories should be around 600.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Lots of questions and comments about what to do with the leftover oil. You can simply strain it and use it again.  You should be able to use it four or five more times before it’s no longer viable.  Oggi makes a great stainless steel oil storage container with a built-in strainer.  Its seals well and keeps the oil in good shape for months.   You can find it on Amazon.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: made it today and it is so good yet so simple! I am always leery of fried stuff since I want the crunch but don't wish to double fry - this worked perfectly and stayed super crunchy even though I didn't have the same degree of craggy surfaces as shown on the photo. However I think my candy thermometer is crucial in monitoring the heat since I had to cook mine in 4 batches - basically just follow the recipe and you will get crunchy juicy flavourful fried chicken.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was outstanding - made it for a Friendsgiving and it was an absolute hit. We did a combo of legs, thighs, and boneless breasts and everything came out perfectly. The seasoning on top was really what made it, though. It did take a bit longer (~15 min) to get that nice golden brown color but that was partially due to me overcrowding the pot a bit. We did use 00 flour and I think it really made for a lovely, light and crisp crust. Def try this recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was amazing!!!  We decided to have this as opposed to turkey.  It was absolutely delicious!!  Just be aware of how to get rid of the oil you use to fry the chicken in.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Excellent! We used chicken breasts. Amazing how the spice mixture at the end adds a nice blast of favor and the fried sage with lemons made for a beautiful platter. Will make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My husband found this post and video. Decided after 27 years, scrap the turkey. Getting ready to brine the chicken, Happy Thanksgiving!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Would anyone happen to know why the herb seasoning is not used to season the flour itself?

commentBody: the herb mix would be lost in 5 cups of flour

author: Sohla El-Waylly

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It doesn't, per the instructions. It is, instead, sprinkled on after frying. However, my concern would be that some pieces could be unevenly seasoned so when I make this later today, I may blitz the herbs and spices in a mini food processor and add it directly to the flour then hit it with a little sea salt after pulling it from the fryer. Will report back if this method works out favorably.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Exactly the kind of recipe that I avoid, even though my mom made fried chicken every week. But it sounds delicious. I wish someone would invite us.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this for our Thanksgiving dinner tonight. Well, I tried, but gave up half way through and went back to the way my mom makes it. Did a buttermilk brine instead with seasonings added in (so they could infuse into the meat), then double battered with a seasoned flour/egg/flour again combo. I know this is one of those annoying comments that’s like- do a different recipe instead, I just think this method is ultimately bland. But, the idea of fried chicken on Thanksgiving, amazing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Was delicious! Got comments that suggested this is the best fried chicken ever!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this yesterday for Thanksgiving and it was a HIT! The herb seasoning on top with the fresh lemon... fantastic! It was my first time frying chicken and Sohla's video and recipe instructions are so clear and helpful. I'm so excited I now have the skill to fry chicken. This recipe very well could become a Thanksgiving staple.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was a huge hit on Thanksgiving. Thank you. Was cooking for friends and they were sceptical....but we won!!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this for thanksgiving after watching Sohla’s video! It was delicious and a huge hit. I’d never deep fried and only had a digital meat thermometer but it worked fine. I would probably make much more of the herb seasoning and zest it more heavily next time. I don’t cook much meat, and when I do, it’s chicken breast or ground beef, so I was pleasantly surprised how cheap the chicken legs were! 6$ for five full legs which fed five people.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving for family of 4. The directions were very clear, especially the frying part which was new to me. Used regular all purpose flour and breading was very crispy and not heavy. The spice mix was terrific (used fresh instead of dried sage because had it on hand) and the lemon zest was surprisingly perfect and added a fresh note. An elegant tasty fried chicken recipe! Appreciated other comments about how to clean up used oil. I feel empowered now having made fried chicken! Wasn’t difficult or too messy at all.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was my first time making homemade fried chicken and deep frying and this 100% worth it! So delicious and extra crispy! Although it tasted too salty to me so I will probably reduce the MSG and salt when I make it again. I brined bone in chicken thighs for about 36 hours.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Game changer! A lot of work but well worth it. Iso delix’d recommend an electric fryer and cook outside or in the basement as we did since it was raining. Otherwise your house will smell like fried food for days.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A game changer! So delicious. I’d recommend cooking outside in an electric fryer or in the basement as I did since it was raining. Otherwise your house will smell like fried chicken for days. Our guests were ecstatic. I didn’t tell them about the MSG, I remember that being a thing but it really enlivens the flavor. Also get some good chicken bouillon powder from a Chinese market.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is my first Sohla recipe-  would give this 3.5 stars. It’s easy to make and flavorful, but I found the flavor profile to be more unique than your standard fried chicken. To the point where after eating 2 pieces, I’m tired of eating this flavor. It’s like if you ate a m extravagant chocolate sundae with all the fixings, you could only take a handful of bites before you call it quits

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made the whole menu - excluding only the sweet potatoes - and it was DIVINE. Garnered raves from my beloved Gen Z offspring and my equally beloved yet far more grumpy Gen X husband. Can't gush enough!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this and it was delicious!  I followed every step to the letter, and also made some tenders too.  Everyone loved it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Nancy R same! Made it for Thanksgiving and it was perfect and delicious. Everyone raved. Her instructions and the video was invaluable. I set up a fry station just as she did and had zero trouble.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these without MSG (because of a dietary restriction) but followed the recipe to a T otherwise. Be aware that the cayenne pepper level may be aggressive for children or spice-averse adults, but I loved it. It was my first time frying chicken and I made these for Thanksgiving instead of a Turkey: HUGE HIT! We will be repeating this recipe for Christmas.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving and it was excellent. Brined for a bit more than 24 hours, and the meat was juicy and tender with a crispy, flavorful breading. We used an induction cooktop to keep the temperature consistent, which worked very well. Would make this again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: With the skin, the chicken is juicier; without the skin, it has a slightly more delicate flavor and is less fatty - I prefer the second option.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Big mistake not using baking powder to cut the flour. Makes a huge difference in the crust. I've been doing fried chicken turkey for years at Thanksgiving by combining Samin Nosrat's buttermilk turkey recipe with serious eats fried chicken recipe. Highly recommend.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used cooking oil should be taken to your local Tox-away site.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: First time I've tried frying anything. I was always scared of handling the oil but Sohla's video gave me an extra boost of courage. The temperatures and times worked out exactly as scripted. The chicken was crispy on the outside with lots of tasty craggy bits. Inside was juicy, well-seasoned, and flavourful. By far - BY FAR - the best fried chicken I've ever had. I'm a longtime Sohla fan and this recipe was truly next level - both in deliciousness, and in making the technique accessible.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious. Will reduce sugar in brine next time and change up the sprinkle seasoning to fit the mood.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I typically don’t eat fried food through out the year. So, this recipe was new and exciting to make for the holidays! My husband and I typically enjoy none traditional proteins (duck, lamb, or ribs) for the holidays so fried chicken was perfect. Instead of the salt water brine, I used buttermilk and fried the chicken in sunflower oil. It was SO juicy, then skin was crispy and crunchy. Best idea ever!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made the dish without msg and couldn’t tell I actually brined the chicken. With such a small amount of salt in the brine it did nothing for the chicken flavor. How about suggesting a different amount of salt if not using msg? (Who has msg lying around?)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Came out exactly as described in the video. Used the 00 Italian flour and was watchful to start temp at 325 and not get below 275. Cooked perfectly. Stayed crisp for hours, juicy, perfectly seasoned inside and out. It was a revelation, first homemade fried chicken that was better than what I’ve had at restaurants. The herb seasoning a big part of the revelation, can never have fried chicken without it. Would love variations on the seasoning.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow. Surprisingly crispy. I've made fried and oven-fried chicken many times before and usually need to do a very goopy/messy coating to get a crunchy skin. I think the 00 flour really makes a difference. Will be making this again. Brine was easy and so was the coating and the seasoning.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We made with half the batch chicken, half the batch tofu. Excellent on both accounts!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I've made this 3 times in the past year.  Twice for Thanksgiving, and once just because it's great!  Although it takes some time to marinate, the actual cooking part is very straightforward.  "Best fried chicken I've ever had" seems to be the top eval from my table guests.
I did the "00" flour this past Thanksgiving, and the chicken was even more crispy than my previous times making the dish.
I've never used MSG in this recipe yet, but I can't imagine it being better than the results I've gotten

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I didn’t have legs but had a whole chicken. I cut it apart and used it instead. It was delicious. The fried sage, while delicious, was unnecessary since sage was in the herb mix already. I saved the fried sage for other recipes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is really delicious. I substituted maple syrup for the corn syrup and it was no problem. The cake was light and fluffy, and the topping was great, I will use it when I make pumpkin bread as well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This might be the best cake I’ve ever made. So incredibly fluffy, and the caramel pecan topping adds the right level of sweetness.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Allison I agree that the technique of whipping the eggs, sugars, and corn syrup yields such a fluffy and moist cake. I made the cake with 1 tablespoon of pumpkin pie spice as I like a spicy cake. It was delicious. The caramel topping came out easily and perfectly adding a unique topping to a wonderful cake! I’m making this for Thanksgiving!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This cake is delicious but should come with a warning for possible dental work. I, too, cooked the caramel mixture according to the recipe for 4-6 minutes not realizing that would result in a very hard candy coating. Luckily, I was the first to try it when serving my guests but unluckily, I lost a crown in the process! Lucky again the next day when my dentist was able to just reglue the crown- perhaps using the toffee that was left on the tooth. LOL

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have been baking the pecan pie recipe in Rose Beranbaum's The Pie and Pastry Bible for years. That recipe calls for Lyles Golden Syrup https://www.lylesgoldensyrup.com/ a welcome alternative to the over the top sweetness of Corn Syrup - I bet you could replace it here as well...

author: Sohla El-Waylly

You can find it on The NY Times Cooking channel or there’s a link in the feature.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I misread the instructions in step 6. You’re supposed to bring the frosting to a vigorous boil, which will take 4-6 minutes, and then take it off the heat. I brought it to a full boil and then kept it boiling 4-6 minutes resulting in a caramel that cooled to the consistency of a Sugar Daddy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It uses a whole can of pumpkin!  Hallelujah! I hate to measure that stuff or use only part of a can; the rest always goes bad in the 'fridge. Can't wait to make this.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In other recipes, I have successfully used both brown rice syrup and/or Lyle's Golden Syrup as 1:1 substitutions for corn syrup - specifically to those recipes where I want a caramelized or gooey result.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The cake itself is divine, but, like Merideth I had problems with the caramel.  I let it boil for about a minute then cool for several minutes until it settled.  The cake had risen to a domed shape so when I poured the caramel onto the cake, it ran off towards the edges.  I spent about 5 minutes scraping sauce back toward the center of the cake until it had cooled and thickened enough to stay in place.  The end texture of the caramel was pretty rubbery. Any suggestions on where I went wrong?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @betsy I ALWAYS use cake strips around my pans round or rectangle.  Wet them thoroughly, size appropriately with adjustable Velcro and place tightly around pan before placing in oven.  The strips are fabric and metallic on the outside  so they keep the edges of the cake cooler so the middles doesn’t rise  but stays level with the more cool edges.  Works every time and I bake perfectly level cakes even at 5000 feet elevation.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Would this work if using gluten free flour as substitute?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Marishka I used Bobs red mill gf flour and the big difference is texture of the cake. It came out more dense than expected.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Plainsgal: I think you could use the glaze without the pecans… they are just sprinkled on top. The glaze (without the pecans) is basically a version of penuche frosting… it doesn’t include cream, but is otherwise the same, so you could make a traditional penuche which should have a very similar flavor to the recommended glaze.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Measured everything by weight and this turned out perfectly, no notes. My spouse claims to not like pumpkin but he had two slices and was talking about how I'll have to make it again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Hmmm…I have a super cute Nordicware pumpkin cake pan. Thinking of chopping the pecans and putting them into the bottom of the pan before adding the batter. Then, I’d glaze the unmolded cake before serving.
Think I’ll play with this before the big day…

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very tasty cake, came together quickly. Fun to watch her video and see the process. I would definitely make again. Don’t be intimidated by this one, just pay close attention to the ingredients and instructions. We loved this for thanksgiving and leftovers!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: First, I refuse to use corn syrup so I subbed pure maple syrup and it turned out perfectly!  I agree those who experience the topping hardening must have stirred it after it’s directed in recipe.  I accidentally mixed up my orange fruit/vegetable and this got sweet potato.  Every raved, ate seconds and requested some to go.  Served with fresh whipped cream.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So dang delicious and worth the effort.  I made this in my MIL's kitchen, so it was a hassle finding everything I needed. I almost gave up before I started, but I'm glad I finished it. Used a hand mixer, salted butter, dark Karo syrup, and two 8" square foil pans because that's what I had to work with, and it came out perfectly. 10/10.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I never post reviews, but this was a great cake and a big hit at work. The video was extremely helpful. It is not too sweet, and so convenient to use a whole can of pumpkin. The texture is light, fluffy, and not heavy like some pumpkin desserts can be. It was hard to judge the right amount of flaky salt because it is hard to see on the background of chopped pecans. I might put some salt in the caramel next time. A keeper!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just finished making this for the first time for Thanksgiving.  It looks and smells amazing.  Have to wait to taste tomorrow.  The other bakers comments about the topping were very helpful!  It's important not to overcook it or it will be too gooey hard.  Total cooking time for me was about 7 minutes total.  Once I saw it expanding and foaming I removed from heat. I toasted my pecans for 8 minutes on a timer while the oven was preheating.  I made sure they were fragrant when I removed them. Thank you to all who took the time to share your experiences.  Happy baking!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made as directed except maple syrup subbed for corn syrup and the cake and topping turned out perfect. Maybe corn syrup makes this cake last longer and nothing else will do that?  I can’t think of any other reason not to use a substitute. For my family I don’t have to concern myself with this cake lasting a few days, they loved it and it’s going fast!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this gluten free using King Arthur's 1:1 measure GF flour. Recipe worked beautifully. Fantastic crumb. Big hit with celiac and non-celiac guests alike.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have a family full of nut allergies.  Any ideas for an alternative frosting?  I don't think leaving the nuts out of this would end well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
You could leave out the nuts altogether, maybe making 1 1/2 times the caramel.  Or you could try using toasted coconut shreds.  Coconut and caramel are a good combination and sound pretty tasty with pumpkin cake.

If you do make it without the pecans, please, please tell us how it went!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@TriciaPDX
I haven’t made this yet but I have made another pumpkin cake that uses pepitas sautéed in butter in the topping. It’s delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this today, and it would be delicious even without the pecans! A cream cheese icing would be great too!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I don't have pumpkin spice, but I have allspice, cinnamon, cardamom, ginger, probably everything that pumpkin spice contains.  Anyone know proportions to simulate pumpkin spice?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Carol for 1 TBSP of pumpkin pie spice, I use 1.5 tsp cinnamon   .5 tsp ginger   .25 tsp nutmeg   .12 tsp cloves

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Recipe is easy to follow, delicious, uses a whole can of pumpkin, and can be made a day ahead. My kind of recipe.   Accidentally added the vanilla at the beginning making the topping and it turned out fine, phew. I think I should have let it cool a little longer cause it spread more to the edges. Next day, the topping slid off some of the sides but it is a forgiving cake and I was able to put it back on and cut it fine. Maybe FL humidity? Enjoyed watching Sohla cook the fried chx Thanksgiving!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I enjoyed the cake, very moist. I didn’t like the topping’s texture with the corn syrup. It felt a little rubbery to me. I suggest the salted caramel glaze from Melissa Clark’s banana snacking cake instead.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made it with an excellent gluten-free flour blend and it was fantastic. 5 stars

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Nicole which flour did you use?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Was this ever delicious! The only exceptions I used was to bake a butternut squash for the recipe and used Lyles Golden Syrup. It was moist and came out perfectly. Thank you!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made it for Thanksgiving and it is DELICIOUS! Many guests commented how good it was and asked for more. I'm making more for Christmas and will probably be making this every Thanksgiving and Christmas from now on.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not enough pumpkin flavor if I made them again, I would double the spices. I also added chocolate chips to the topping, which helped a lot.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Tend to add more salt than called for in desserts but this was still just too sweet for most guests to have more than a small piece. I get that pies are very sweet as well but maybe the crust helps to balance more than the pumpkin cake does in this case

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this cake for 2025 Friendsgiving. Unfortunately, I didn’t love it. I felt like the cake tasted too eggy and it just wasn’t flavorful enough. I would add more pumpkin spice if I make it again. Also, wish I had read the comments because the corn syrup topping is way too sweet and incredibly unhealthy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can the cake be frozen, and if so, before or after glazing?  Thanks

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I don't think caramel, penuche-type frostings freeze well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I would love to know if this would freeze well if I make it ahead.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: can anyone advise whether light or dark corn syrup would be better, or is it personal preference?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In the video, Sohla uses dark corn syrup but she also says you can use any type of corn syrup you have. :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used light, and it came out great!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is so good. Not too sweet, not too spiced but perfectly balanced. We all really liked it. I will definitely keep this one to repeat.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Regarding freezing, I’d consider freezing just the cake, defrosting it, and then making the topping. But it’s big — I’d have to cut the cake in half to fit in my freezer.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: OK, I think the sheet pan thing has jumped to the shark. I’m off for convenience, but why not just bake it in a regular baking pan?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I would let the caramel cool for longer than the 3 minutes. You’d need to experiment, but I’ll try a 5 or 6 minute rest next time. My cake didn’t even have much of a dome but so much of it went down the sides and I had to keep pushing it back up. Applying when it’s a bit cooler would solve that.

The cake itself is divine. The topping is a bit sweet for my tastes, so I’ll probably cut back the amount used next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed this recipe almost exactly (added an extra half teaspoon of pumpkin spice in the cake) and it turned out perfectly. It took about 3 minutes for the caramel to boil and as soon as it was roiling and rising I turned off the heat and let it sit. Gave it the perfect gooey but solid texture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yum!!! I had no corn syrup- I substituted a combination of some pancake syrup (main ingredient is corn syrup) and sugar syrup. Followed the instruction to remove the syrup from heat immediately after the coming to a rising boil. The texture was perfect (chewy & soft).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’d like to incorporate chocolate in some way - a drizzle on top or folded into the batter. Not sure which is the best way to go.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can this be made dairy-free by substituting margarine for the butter?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: When do you add the oil?  Recipe says pour into measuring cup.  Does not say when to mix into ingredients.  Or did I miss something?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @PK it is before you add flour/pumpkin and fold. Add oil in a thin stream while mixing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: absolutely lovely cake. subbed the corn syrup for agave and otherwise followed the recipe to a T. came out moist and delicious. will definitely make again and a very welcome substitution for traditional pumpkin pie which we all know can be boring 🧡

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @pk...step 3

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Phenomenal. As usual I thought I knew better than the author and I was wrong. Follow their instructions, timing and order and you will not be disappointed. I cooked the caramel too long and added the pecans to it before pouring on which was like spreading viscous praline on top. You will enjoy this!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The cake came out light and about inches high, but after adding the topping, it compressed and got soggy. The final product was a dough-y and dense cake level that barely tasted like pumpkin with gooey topping. Could this be because I made it the day before? Any other ideas? I did use Malden salt flakes and so the topping was a hit with a little ice cream.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Aviva
I agree. I believe the sauce was too liquidy and changed the texture of the cake into a leaden mass. The sauce also made the cake too sweet.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used the can of pumpkin and pumpkin pie spice.,I took the cake to a community Thanksgiving dinner. Someone had made two pumpkin pies with fresh sugar pumpkins and classic spices, cloves, allspice and cinnamon. We all preferred the mellow taste of those pumpkin pies and full agreement that this recipe would not be repeated.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe didn’t work well for me. The cake was not only soggy but also it had an “egg-y” taste that overwhelmed the pumpkin flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this Wednesday night for Thanksgiving. It was a perfect dessert. It is now Friday morning and I’m eating it as my coffee cake.  I’m obsessed. My sister in law asked me to make it for Christmas. I didn’t change anything. I think I was not as careful about not stirring the brown sugar mix after removing from the heat but that did not seem to matter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not a fan of this. The liquid topping soaked in and made the cake a leaden mass. Perhaps it’s ok if eaten immediately (still warm), but it is definitely not a good cake to make a day ahead. The Times guide has much better pumpkin recipes. I recommend the pumpkin scones.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a lovely cake with the perfect level of warm spices and an easy gooey pecan caramel topping. I baked a half-recipe in an 8x8 pan with great results. Just don’t stir the topping once it’s off the heat so you don’t crystallize the sugar, and for heaven’s sake, use the small amount of corn syrup. Ya want gooey? That’s the only way you’re gonna get it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The icing- delicious but I did not really enjoy the texture of the cake and will make again without the corn syrup and see if I love it! Otherwise, pretty tasty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Spectackular success! Everyone looked a bit skeptical when I explained it was a combination of pumpkin and pecan but then they all raved. The cake was light and fluffy but somehow it felt like pumpkin pie in the mouth. And I saw some comments about tooth breaking so I made sure the caramel topping was not too firm. Excellent dessert.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Absolutely delicious and a big win for our thanksgiving this year. My family loved this cake and appreciated not having an excess of crust on their plates from all those pies!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Absolutely wonderful!  I microwaved some that had been put in the fridge and I think l like it even better warm!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this for a Friendsgiving.  I substituted agave for 1/3 of the corn syrup in the batter and 3/4 of the corn syrup in the topping.  It worked beautifully.  The result was a softer topping that was not cloyingly sweet.  My guests raved about it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm not sure how... but this cake only gets better with time! I made this for Thanksgiving and it was divine on the day of but then I let it set, covered loosely with plastic wrap, on the counter. The topping helped keep the cake decadently moist while also creating an even more pecan-pie like flavor. It's been six days of me sneaking little bites every time I pass it and it still tastes amazing! Definitely one I will be returning to again and again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Everyone LOVED this cake at Thanksgiving. 
Awesome recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were a big hit!  I don't really like pumpkin pie, but they were delicious and I will make them again to take for special people.  They were a bit tricky putting them together, but well worth the trouble!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: To watch the video of the cake, start at 18:00. Enjoy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this for a crowd of 20 with dark corn syrup and dark brown sugar. There was a single little square left, and I got a lot of compliments.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I sent my partner to the store for corn syrup and he came back with dark corn syrup.  I imagine it would work the same way but what about the taste.  Think it would make much difference?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious! I made it another time with some substitutions to make it a little healthier but still tasty… used allulose instead of the sugars, added more pumpkin, used whole wheat pastry flour, cut the oil in half but then supplemented with whole fat yogurt for moisture, and cut the pecans by 1/3rd. I left the butter and corn syrup. The topping didn’t caramelize as much (but it did have a nice caramelized flavor) so I poked small holes the cake for it to soak into the cake. It came out super moist and delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Everyone loved this! Deliciously moist, good crumb (not too dense), and not overly sweet (unless you devour the edges of caramel 🙃). I used a hand mixer as I had cut the recipe in half. Beating took FOREVER or what seemed like forever, so will use a stand mixer next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Baked this cake for the first time for Thanksgiving.  Total silence and rapture while all ate!  This is the best cake I have ever eaten or made.  I found the flaky salt was crucial--it offset the very sweet syrup topping.  Highly, highly recommend.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was a last-minute addition to the dessert table.  I replaced the white sugar w/ allulose/monkfruit sugar- I had run out of white sugar. Used organic corn syrup that was in the pantry for 3 years & added more spices. (No one died.) The final product was slightly underwhelming the first day, but was fantastic the following day. Not sure if my helper didn't get the caramel to the right temperature, so it should have hardened a bit to a crackle...but it seeped into the cake overnight & yum!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Got pumpkin? Do this cake. The cake is very good. The topping sets it apart. Done right - the cake becomes quite fluffy, almost custardy. Pecans sit on top with just a bit of syrup, most of which soaks into the cake. And, as said, an entire can of pumpkin. If you like pumpkin, you gotta do this cake.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: I smashed the green beans in a single layer on a cutting board to save a plastic bag; I needed to chase a few errant beans across the counter, but it otherwise worked. The beans didn't taste raw or grassy after overnight salting. This was a fresh take on green beans that I'd make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: Sounds tasty but I’m thinking of blanching the green beans. Personally I am not eating a scoop of raw green beans!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: @Leslie I have made a different recipe either smashed green beans. It was delicious. The beans softened a bit so the texture was similar to blanched green beans.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: I think if you use the thinner, french green beans it'll work. I'm going to try iti!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: Those concerned about the "raw" green beans should also watch Sohla's video that goes along with the menu featured in: You Know What You Need? A Fried Chicken Thanksgiving. Sohla explains about salting the green beans. Her video also shows that the green beans were not haricot verts.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: The dressing looks interesting but i will probably blanche the beans and use less salt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: I’m really amazed how good this was. Green beans were crunchy, loved  the sweet and tang of the dressing. Very easy as long as you give yourself time to do it. We let the beans sit overnight. Will definitely make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: Made this for thanksgiving along with Sohla’s other dishes- overall delicious meal with great flavors! This salad didn’t really come out though. The green beans were way too salty and still raw, I ended up eating around them. I think green beans blanched or straight out of a can would have been much better. Also I wish I had tossed the whole salad in the dressing, the stacking idea is fun but wasn’t easy to eat on a plate full of other things. The dressing was delicious though, I also added extra dried cranberries and fresh pomegranate seeds which added a fun pop. Thanks Sohla for the fun menu, we had a great time executing it!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: We were ok with taste and texture of the uncooked green beans, but did not find the flavor of the dressing appealing.  Also, the dressing pooled on the plate and its flavor was too "weak".  It really needs a higher viscosity to help it adhere to the greens.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: Raw green beans?  I was taught that raw green beans can severely upset the digestive system.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: Raw green beans?  I thought raw green beans cause digestive upset.  Is that a myth?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: @MarianK It’s totally a myth! I eat them all the time for a snack, just like raw carrots.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: @MarianK thin haricot verts are delightful raw. But even if they’re not the thinnest, salting and sitting also breaks them down somewhat like a blanching would.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: It depends on the individual. It is usually not a problem, but everyone is different.  I don't tolerate raw zucchini well, green beans I am fine with.  Eat a few and try them.  The salt breaks down or alters the cellular structure so it will not taste raw.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: The raw beans left a bit to be desired. I decided to use French beans and blanching would be a much better idea than pounding and leaving with salt. I skipped the layering of lettuce because no one in my family was interested in the 'salad' part. Just put a layer of lettuce on bottom for decoration.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: I have to admit, I was prepared to be underwhelmed  as we subbed this for a green bean casserole. But I should have known Sohla wouldn’t disappoint. This was absolutely fantastic!!! Made it as written with canned cranberry sauce. Will be a staple in future years.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody:
If you do not like crunchy green beans, skip. Although the salt removed the raw flavor, it did not soften the green beans. People had a hard time serving themselves with the layered salad set up. I had to cut the salad up on my plate to get bites. 
The cranberry dressing was great.
This was not a popular item at the Thanksgiving table. Most people avoided it and those who did try it told me gently, “it wasn’t their favorite.”

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: My family loved this recipe for thanksgiving. It was a novel cooking method. Family fun!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: The cranberry sauce based dressing looks interesting but I am not sure heavily salted raw green beans is a good idea.

recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: what kind of green beans? round? runner?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: This is a great recipe, will definitely make again! My 7 year old even asked me "how I made the beans so good".

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody:
10/10 brilliant recipe.
Will be using this method to prep green beans all the time now. (Mine spent less than 6 hours in the fridge and still came out great.)
All hail Sohla!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
commentBody: Went over well at Thanksgiving dinner. A fresh take on green beans. My only change was to blanche the green beans instead of salting. Will be making again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Pardon me, but would Grey Poupon arriving in my 1979 Rolls work just as well?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Any suggestions for something vegetarian to put this gravy on? Whole roasted cauliflower perhaps?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made the recipe as instructed and unfortunately it was a disappointment. We served it with mashed potatoes, green beans, and fried chicken. It added a punch of color to the plate, so that was nice visually. Perhaps I made mistake but I’m usually good with instructions… very little flavor and as someone who likes mustard, this was flat.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I agree with others that this recipe fell flat. So, when adding the prepared mustard, I added 1 TBSP apple cider vinegar and 1 tsp sugar. Stir well and then strain. The vinegar & sugar help the other flavors pop.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m sad to say I was very excited for this and it was really gross. I’m an ex chef and I was thrilled with the idea of it, but it just didn’t work and curious if others had very different results because they are so few ingredients I don’t know how that could be.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Okay, this is a bit quirky and maybe not for everyone, but if you follow Sohla’s instructions closely, you get a lusciously smooth gravy … with an undeniable but not overwhelming overtone of hot mustard. And it totally works! Served with mashed potatoes and friend chicken, it’s just what that combo needs to refresh your palate.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: turned out really really well. had it on a few different options

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I liked this gravy. I used No Chik’n broth. Served it over mashed potatoes, greenbeans, and fried chik’n fillets. It’s interesting and pretty. I imagine I will make this one occasionally to change things up a bit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What to add if I've only got normal American mustard?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'd say make a different recipe. They're not remotely the same.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Did not like this at all.  Couldn't think of anything to do that would fix it.  Tossed it and made a different make ahead gravy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was tasty enough, but I think I was hoping it would have more of a punch, it was kind of like a typical gravy with a mustardy kick, so not bad, but probably won't make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I believe it's the blossom end, not the stem end, that one should cut off before pickling because it has enzymes that can soften the pickles after they are brined.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Rob  That’s exactly what my dad did in every batch he made. And he made enough every fall to last the whole winter. Including green tomatoes and celery. Perfect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are amazing! So easy to make, and I love the freshness of these pickles. They are slightly sweet with amazing crunch. I used Diamond salt. If one were to use a different kosher brand, I'd recommend cutting down on the amount, or, better still, weighing the salt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could one leave out the sugar?  It doesn't sound like it would go with mustard seed or dill... and I HATE sweet pickles...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’ve read that Kosher salt is best for refrigerator pickles because it has no additive to make the salt free flowing, and that additive is bad for some reason.  Also I was instructed to use filtered or distilled water, not tap water.  But I don’t boil the brine so maybe these precautions are moot in this context?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
If you are making "real" pickles--the kind that depend on lactic acid-producing bacteria culled from the environment--the chlorine in tap water can kill the bacteria that are responsible for fermentation. It shouldn't matter for this recipe though. It calls for distilled vinegar, so there are no live bacteria cultures to start with. 

Personally, I refuse to buy water. I dechlorinate by letting it stand for a few days, or by bringing it to a boil for a few minutes and then letting it cool down.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Filtered water is a nice touch, but by no means necessary.  You use kosher salt rather than table salt because the iodine added to table salt can make the pickles turn blue.  So can using raw garlic - I prefer to use granulated garlic.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Rather than refrigerate them, water bath can them for 15-20 minutes.  The pickles will be shelf-stable for at least a year.  When processing, grate in some horseradish and a few small, Japonica peppers, and to ensure crispness, if you've got some, a fresh grapeleaf.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Kirbys are hard to find this time of year, but the local Kroger has 6 packs of small Persian cukes that worked very well!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These sound more like bread and butter pickles. Is the sugar necessary?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It is definitely the blossom end that gets cut. Don’t think it matters what you do to the stem end.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love these so much. Can I reuse the brine by boiling it and pouring it over a fresh batch? They only last a week in the fridge before they are all eaten

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Blsam, I have that same question. Can you reuse the brine?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I would honestly rename this recipe as “Quick Sweet Dill Pickles”. I have never made pickles, so I didn’t realize how much sugar this recipe has in it. Not what I wanted. Otherwise it was nice and easy to follow.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These have great flavor. However, a little too sweet for my liking and teeters on the edge of a bread and butter for me. I’ll likely cut the sugar to 1/8 cup next go-around.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Where can you find Kirby cukes at this time of year? I haven't seen one in weeks.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Lei-Lei Market, Tucson, AZ  $1.49/lb   Fresh dill, two bunches for $1.99

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are superb! Has anyone had success waterbathing them? I'd love it if they could be shelf stable.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Way too salty for me, I used kosher salt but would maybe use just 1 tablespoon

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed the basic directions the first time while skipping the coriander and mustard seeds and substituting a couple small handfuls of freeze dried dill weed for the dill sprigs into the jar with the cucumbers and garlic.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were good but more like a blend between sweet and dill pickles. I haven’t made pickles very often so I couldn’t remember if sugar was standard. I’ll use a different recipe next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have to say this is a great recipe for my three kids to make! They are nine, seven and six, and they were excited to help out. They enjoyed monitoring the pickles to see when they would be ready. This easily pairs well with the smash burger, which I made a few days later for dinner. I’m not a big pickle fan but wanted to enjoy them with my kids, and I even put my pickle on my smash burger, which added great flavor to my dinner. Easy to make, easy to clean, and kid friendly- can’t beat that!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Sour Cream and Onion Mashed Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: Sohla, can we leave the skin on the Yukon gold potatoes also without affecting the result (other than the flecks of gold skin amidst the red)?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Sour Cream and Onion Mashed Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: @A Reader  Yes! I always leave the skins on.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Sour Cream and Onion Mashed Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: @A Reader I always leave skins on. The contain most of the nutrients.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Sour Cream and Onion Mashed Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: Any mashed potatoes, or potato puree, van be made more interesting by adding finely chopped purple onions. Just add some at a time and tasting in between, making sure you do not make the onions overwhelming.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Sour Cream and Onion Mashed Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: @GS Sounds more flavorful than the scallions. Thanks!  Do you add them raw?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Sour Cream and Onion Mashed Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: @GS Yes I didn’t have scallions so used very finely diced yellow onion, O% fat greek yogurt, a bit of cream and olive oil. I’ll try red onion next time

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Sour Cream and Onion Mashed Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: This is similar to the way I make my mashed potatoes.  My kids would even devour any leftovers  straight from the refrigerator. However, add butter and garlic powder and omit the onion powder and salt.  Simply delicious!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Sour Cream and Onion Mashed Potatoes Recipe
