commentBody: We made this tonight for two, with a 3.2 lb. chicken. It was easy an delicious. Used onion, lemon, thyme, parsley and rosemary for aromatics, cooked at 450º F for 40 minutes. Temperature was more than 175º. Meat was moist and flavorful. We had NYT Radicchio Caesar Salad, Italian Roast Potatoes and some cheap red wine. We have enough of everything for tomorrow night. Can't wait!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Totally agree with Laurie. I have grilled or roasted a chicken (always Mary’s whole bird, as big as what’s available at Taylor’s or the co-op) at least 36 weeks out of every year for a decade or better. Not once did I think of using a rack over a tray - brilliance.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Did this last night. I'm a big fan of spatchcocking, and while this was my first chicken (been doing the Thanksgiving bird for years) it cooked as expected. 

But once again, regardless of how many things you shove under the skin (I used fresh sage and rosemary) and how much oil and garlic you rub on the outside, it always tastes like a baked chicken.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Often roast a well seasoned spatchcocked chicken over cubed bread. When chicken is done, remove to platter. Remove bread to bowl and reserve. On stove top, add some vinegar of your choice and heat with your favorite greens until wilted. Toss with some of the bread. Serve with the chicken.
I use the extra flavorful bread with eggs.
Bet this is from a NYT article or recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For a little finesse run your thumb along both sides of the breastbone and pop it out. Use a small knife and separate the wishbone. This lets the seasoning get better contact with the breast.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I always spatchcock the chicken to roast it, but good olive oil will smoke at high temperatures. Use canola oil instead, and as someone else pointed out 400 degrees works just as well and your oven won’t get as messy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love this cooking method. I like to line the pan with potatoes to catch the chicken drippings while they cook. Or, taking a page from Zuni Cafe, placing large chunks of bread under the chicken halfway through the cooking to absorb the drippings, then using them as croutons for a salad.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I wish I’d read the notes before I prepared the chicken as I would have left the backbone in, only cutting along one side. Also agree the temp is way too high. I checked after 30 mins and turned down to 400. I had a small bird from Trader Joe’s, it was juicy and delicious and did finish up in under an hour. I added more seasoning and was glad for it. It’s a basic inexpensive way to cook chicken and have material for a homemade stock if that’s your thing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I have one question for Times cooking. Where in this day and times can you find a 3 to 4 pound chicken?
I live in a "well supplied" small city out west and can't remember the last time I found a 4 pound chicken. I've quit buying whole birds.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: After splitting, and seasoning the bird, I heat a cast iron pan on the stove top on high. Let it get hot. Put bird in the pan, skin side down, for 5 minutes. Then place the pan in a hot, 425 degree oven for 15-20 minutes, depending on the size of the bird. Take out the pan, flip the bird over so the skin side is up, place back in the oven for 35-40 minutes more. This will result in crispy skin, evenly cooked, delicious chicken.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Use convection oven, 350, and no need for any oil or butter just sprinkle with kosher salt....freeze backbones and use for chicken stock

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Upside: Tasty chicken
Downside: Filthy oven, smokey kitchen

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Agreed. Set off apartment smoke alarm 20 minutes in, even with external ventilation set to max.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I spread achiote paste all over the chicken (under skin too) and let it marinade in the fridge for a half a day. Then use the cast iron pan/oven method.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If you remove the backbone, "pope's nose," and wing tips, add them to the neck and gizzard (in the giblets package), you can make a small amount of stock, which will serve as a base for a gravy or sauce while the bird is resting. Or just add them all to your chicken bits bag in the freezer for future use in stock.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For those who can't/don't want to spatchcock the chicken themselves, the butcher at many supermarkets will do it for you at no additional cost. Also, Bell & Evans sells an already spatchcocked chicken.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sliced ranges, lemons and onions (skin on the citrus) are all excellent under the chicken, and lovely to eat, as well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Turkeys, no matter how large, can be spatchcoked, too.  The butcher will have to accomodate the backbone removal; but the method reduces the time spent by literally hours.  An 18 - 21 lb. turkey takes about 90 minutes (or a bit more), and with the proper butter/wine basting, there is absolutely no sacrifice in color.  Turkey emerges lovely brown to mahogany.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I did a quick version of this because I was in a rush.... so I didn't marinate, I just did the dry rub and roasted right away. I also didn't see the part about the oil (rushing!), so I didn't do that and.... It was delicious anyway!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Perfection!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very moist, but needs a fair amount of salt. Would probably stuff some lemon slices under the skin as well next time

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wishbone? Aren’t you cracking the sternum?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Under the dry brine step it says 450°F oven. I find this exceptionally high unless quicker us needed. Otherwise, start with 450 then turn down to 325-350°F once the skin starts to brown. If it's getting too dark, tent with foil and SLOW ROAST the rest of the way.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: With a weight on top--"en brique"-- it's the be-all bird

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Also, it works fine without a wire rack for me, and less cleanup. I like to be able to stir the onions/carrots/etc. that I put alongside in a sheet pan.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I leave the back bone in just cutting close to it on one side and season lightly with garlic salt on the inner surface before cooking. I cook the bird at 400 degrees on roasting pan with a layer of water under the rack. The water not only provides moist heat but it captures juices that enrich the stock made with the leftover carcass. Waste not, want not.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This comes out soooooooo yummy and moist. I rubbed the added poultry seasoning under the skin like oiling a freshly washed baby and the flavor of the meat was chef's kiss awesome.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Over 30 years ago, I watched Julie Child butterfly a chicken on her program.  She started with a single slice down the backbone, and carefully carved away along the ribcage on both sides.  She 
cautioned against piercing the skin as it compromises the juices while roasting.  The trickiest part is separating the leg and wing bones from the ribs.  Laid out and covering a layer of dressing, it cooks and presents beautifully, in less time as mentioned here.  I've done the Thankgiving turkey since.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My local store now carries spatchcocked Bell & Evan’s chicken of the size mentioned here and I roast one in a 12-inch cast iron skillet, over a bed of garlic cloves slicked in a neutral oil. A 1-pint container of peeled cloves is about right. The garlic comes out confit under a mantle of rich chicken fat, drain the garlic, store it in a covered jar in the refrigerator and use it for up to a week. Both the bird and the garlic benefit greatly.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love any and all forms of spatchcock cooking. I roast at 425° and use a Meater 2 probe (inserted in thickest part of the breast) and remove at 155° - more than adequate to kill any bacteria while retaining exceptional moisture

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used preserved lemon instead of fresh lemon because of the additional aromatics, and I built my bed with fresh shallots!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I believe that most butchers will cut the backbone out of the chicken if you want to skip this step

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Part of the value of buying a whole chicken is making stock with the bones.  Does the spine need to be roasted before going in the stock pot??

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Doesn't need to, but it's good. Just throw it on a sheet pan in the oven and roast till brown. I make spatchcocked chicken often, and so I put all the backs in the freezer until I'm ready to make stock. Then I put them on a sheet pan with cut up veg for the stock, including onions with skin on, and roast till brown. They are a wonderful addition to the stock.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: i followed this verbatim (my aromatics - onion, lemon, parsley, oregano, garlic) -- did add some sliced, small, redskin potato on top of the grate.  Best roasted chix i've ever made.  Loved the crispy skin.  Loved the short cooking time.  Only downside, my smoke alarm went off repeatedly.  That being said -- well worth it.  (and the redskin potatoes were awesome, be sure to slice them thin).  Definite 5 stars.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For those having trouble flattening the breast, it helps as noted to use a knife to remove the breastbone and wishbone.  If it is hard to disjoint the breastbone, cover the chicken with plastic wrap and use a rubber mallet from your workbench to do it.  I consider ours a kitchen tool!  I usually need it to get the bowl off the mixer stand after kneading bread dough.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Excellent. Be creative with aromatics! I used all I had on hand before we went on a trip; parsley, onion, shallots, celery, lemon, lots of dried herbs and put carrots on the rimmed sheet pan.  SO GOOD!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We use a broiler pan, line it with foil and place sliced potatoes and onions in it. The fat dips down and flavors the potatoes and onions. Sometimes we make an herb butter and work it in between the breast/thighs and skin. Simple, fast and delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I put about a half can of beer in the sheet pan. Made the juiciest chicken ever!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’d never made a spatchcock chicken before this and found it actually surprisingly easy with day-ahead planning (which I’m not good at generally). The results were wonderful!  Brined an 8-lb stuffing chicken with dry rosemary, salt and pepper for 22 hrs. When ready to roast, placed cut onions, carrots, fresh fennel, and 1/2 lg lemon in sheet pan with salt, pepper, rosemary, then the rack and olive oiled spatchcock chicken. Used auto-roast function at 400 degrees. Roasted maybe 1hr 15? Delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: There's no indication here about whether one roasts a spatchcocked bird entirely on one side (skin side up as pictured) or flips it once about halfway through. Also, I find that gently loosening the skin (I use the handle of a silicone spoonula) and spreading an olive-oil based mix of chopped fresh herb under the skin adds a lot of flavor to the finished product.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I dry brined my chicken for about 6 hours and followed the recipe exactly.  it came out very juicy and tasty! I will do this again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Great way to roast a chicken, breast meat stays moist and the thigh and drums are done at the same time. I used a roasting chicken so lots of leftovers for sandwiches and soup - including my favorite Melissa Clark soup that has farro, chickpeas and Indian seasonings in it. Note:, if you think you will be using this technique fairly often, consider investing in a quality pair of poultry shears. I have a good pair of kitchen shears but they were not equal to the task of cutting out the backbone.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is the best roast chicken I have ever made. I watched the video and dry brined for 24 hours. As per the video I used an organic free range chicken and followed the recipe/video exactly. Thank You!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wonder what type of salt folks use? Flake or other? Mine came out pretty salty the first time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
The chicken turned out well cooked and juicy, but lacked flavor.  Next time we'd add more salt to start and then pair it with a gravy or a spicy sauce like chimichurri.  We got out a jar of minced Giardiniera and it improved the taste.
My husband reported that cutting out the backbone wasn't hard as he had good kitchen shears.
Our chicken was on the large side ~4 lbs and it took about 1 hour to cook to temperature.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Oil started smoking at about 20 minutes of 450 and set of apartment smoke detector, even with ventilation fan set to max.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I am a lazy person who waits until the spatchcock chickens are on sale at a certain store and I buy two and freeze them. They are the best! I never roast a chicken, however, without sticking some onions, carrots and potatoes, etc., underneath. It's a waste of the drippings otherwise. I have a pair of my grandmother's poultry shears that my mother gave me and they must be from the 1930s or earlier. I have never used them but they are very pretty and HEAVY.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For those looking for someplace to purchase a 3-4 pound chicken, Publix sells Murray’s brand although it is sometimes not in stock. Whole Foods sells several brands that fit the bill. The cost is higher, but the meat is absolutely much better. I recently read an article comparing brands saying that Bell And Evans is the best.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Re bird size and weight: Costco size birds are raised with growth hormones etc. Try a yard-raised, air-chilled bird and you taste a deeper chicken flavor. I find the white meat of todays chickens to be virtually tasteless.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The oven temperature IS confusing.  I have to assume that it is 450 for both methods.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Fantastic, loved this!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: When dry brining in the fridge, should the chicken be covered fairly tightly with plastic wrap to protect the rest of the foods in the fridge from contamination?  Or covered loosely with plastic wrap? Or not at all?  I learned the hard way not to use aluminum foil to cover the raw chicken in the fridge.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For PR: Best to leave it uncovered in the fridge. The skin will dry, making it crispy when baked. The plastic wrap will hold in moisture and make the skin less browned and possibly mushy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very good. Make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very good. I added more maple surup

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The chicken was fantastic.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This worked great. They ran out of chicken in the supermarket on Dec 23rd in the evening so I replaced it with two chicks and it worked well. I accidentally stored them in the freezer, so defrosted in the fridge for 24h first, then 6hrs dry brining. The lemon-thyme-onion based kicked in really nicely with the peppery salt on the skin. I accidentally covered only the upper part of the chicken in salt and pepper but it was still good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have done this a number of times and the chicken always comes out perfect. Some variations, the last one I did I dry brined with kosher salt and brushed mayonnaise over the top of the chicken.  It was the most moist chicken I ever made.  I roast at 450 and cover the bird , if needed, with aluminum foil if it's browning too fast.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The roasting method result was delicious, juicy chicken with crackly skin using the dry brine method for the chicken for 24 hours in the fridge, uncovered.  I used ghee in the "oil or clarified butter" step.  But the roasting method result also made a mess of my oven.  Good as it was, for that reason, as I clean my oven this morning I think this is probably one and done for me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed this recipe and added compound butter with thyme, rosemary, and some sauteed garlic under the skin just before roasting. My chicken was 4.5 lbs and I cooked it at 425 for about an hour. It was perfect, and my date said ti was so good he nearly cried.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What are aromatics?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Usually onions, celery, carrots, herbs, garlic.  Anything that goes in a mirepoix or that you use to add flavor to a roast or stew.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The author provides specific suggestions for aromatics in the ingredient list.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Dry brining is a definite NO…. Takes moisture OUT of the bird.
BUT wet brining in a water salt , sugar and flavourings over night will ensure  moist delicious bird… .. don’t believe a real chef wrote this recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I've dry brined a turkey and it turned out very moist and flavorful.  And the skin crisips nicely.  You should try it with the chicken.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I have bbq a spatchcock chicken for years.   Fifteen minutes skin side up.   Then 30 minutes skin side down with weights on the chicken.  Comes out perfect every time.   Excited to try this in the kitchen.   No one wants to stand outside in the cold!!!!

I make a brine of two tablespoons olive oil, one tablespoon salt and then add whatever herbs look the best from the garden.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Never been able to get past the word or the jump-scare appearance. Still, Sam Sifton remains the best food writer out there.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I advocate the "reverse spatchcock". A chicken has breast meat and dark meat. Breast meat is properly cooked to 155°F. Dark meat is properly cooked to 175°F. It is nearly impossible to get both right when cooking them at the same time. So, remove both breasts and the breast bone. You now have a "reverse spatchcock". Flatten and proceed with this recipe with a final temperature of 175°F. Save the breasts for another day.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The only way I cook poultry - no more dry white meat

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have been making Mark Bittman's Chicken Under a Brick for years. Spatchcocked, but also weighted, first breast side down, then up. We like it dry brined, and then with butter, herbs, garlic, whatever, under the skin. But I am totally going to try it on the rack with the aromatics underneath!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is all too much work. Cook’s illustrated has the easiest and most foolproof method- no spatchcocking required. Preheat a heavy frying pan, preferably castiron, at 450.  Rub whole chicken with olive oil and the salt/spices. Tie back legs. Place chicken breast side up onto heated pan. Cook for 25-30 minutes until breast is ~120 and thigh is ~135. Turn off oven. Keep chicken in oven for another 30 minutes or so.Remove chicken and tent with foil and rest for 20 minutes. Make gravy with juices.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: That's not a spatchcock chicken, is it?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Like another poster, I cut along just one side and flatten it. (Self-interest; arthritis in my hands makes  "snip along the backbone" a more difficult and painful task than it used to be, whether scissors or heavy knife.) Roasting the backbone along with the rest gives you more goodness to make easy stock out of, along with the picked carcass.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I've recently tried airfrying a spatchcocked chicken in my oven/airfryer. The basket is large enough to fit the whole bird but the wrong parts of the bird are under the fan so I needed to separate the legs and thighs so everything gets done close to the same time.
I used 325F and 50 mins for a 3.5 lb bird. The breasts are much thicker than the thighs so even though the fan was blowing directly on them they could have used another 5 min and maybe a 5F bump.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: How does one go about replying to a particular  individual?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Do you mean privately? You can't do that.

To reply to an individual comment for all to see, click on the Reply button.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Here’s a much easier method - no cutting through bone! https://www.nytimes.com/2021/01/27/t-magazine/peter-som-roast-chicken-recipe-cooking.html He shows the cut at about :40 in the video. The chicken is good too - best if you marinate overnight.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Easiest way. Ask your butcher to spatchcock the chicken. Most are happy to do it with a little advance notice

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Ask your butcher to spatchcock the chicken. Most of them are happy to do it with a little advance notice.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For households of one or two, spatchcock the bird, then divide it in two.  Roast half using your recipe of choice and freeze the other half for next week.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love a stuffed roasted bird. I'm sure some stuffing can be put under a spatchocked chicken, but is it enough to feed a family of 4?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: That will depend on how much stuffing you put under the spatchcocked chicken.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Please tell me how refrigerating poultry or any food, uncovered, is safe (from a food safety perspective)?   I've always wondered this, but never asked.  Thanks in advance.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Rita, bacteria cannot survive salt because salt draws moisture out of them, along with drying out the skin of the bird. Bacteria grows in moisture. Salt has been used as a preservative since before we have records.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I believe it is much easier to cut the bird in the “leaping frog” style, no cutting through bones required. Cooks the same as a spatchcock bird.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The only issue with this is that it results in an oven that will need to be cleaned. It makes a delicious chicken but there is no way around the mess if you cook it on an open sheet pan.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Itried this and will never go back to the old method.  The meat is moist and tender, simply perfect!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These clear and adaptable instructions revolutionized my weeknight roast chicken. I might never roast a chicken another way again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wonderful

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is also the best way ever to cook a turkey!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Didn’t brine. Sit on the bed of sliced onions and lemons and Rosemary didn’t have onions or would have added. Overdone at 40 minutes try 30. What is a 4 pound chicken.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The only problem with this recipe is the difference between dark meat and white meat cooking times. I remove the leg/thighs and start them12 minutes before the breast section. It may not look as pretty but it avoids the rubbery dark meat or the overcooked white meat problem.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Remove the leg/thighs and start them 12 minutes ahead of the breast portion. This avoids the underdone rubbery dark meat or the dry overdone white meat. Works like a charm.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed the directions exactly but I found it impossible to crack the wishbone. Maybe I’m just not strong enough? I ended up flipping the bird back over and just cutting the wishbone with my shears. Seemed to work just as well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I make a simple stuffing of wild rice, chestnuts, a bit of old bread, and herbs to roast my spatchcoked chicken on. I stick it back in the oven to keep baking while the chicken rests to be sure it is cooked through.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I also am wondering where to find a 3-4 pound chicken.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’ll add a plea for smaller birds — impossible to find a  three to four pound chicken, even with the flocks decimated by avian flu and ranchers trying to catch up financially. Everything is five pounds and up, regardless where we shop.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
The chicken itself was delicious but I wish the recipe suggested how to handle/use the drippings. 

I used misc aromatics from my fridge under the bird - some old celery, parsley stems, half an onion - but then i felt that these got all the delicious drippings in and on them, but weren't actually things I wanted to eat!  So I threw them into the pot later with the bones, but since they had gotten a little singed, the broth now has a bit of burned flavor that I don't like.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Excellent, so moist!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Overnight dry brine 5lb spatchcock chicken still cooked in less than an hour after setting on counter half hour out of fridge; great with planked russets ala AGA Blake & bull parboil but no flour

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly (and watched the video). In my opinion it results in chicken that oddly tastes like salty chicken soup - not roast chicken. The skin becomes thin and peculiarly chicken soup-ish in taste. It did not cook evenly as promised.The entire bird is so overly salted, I found it inedible. I've made spatchcocked chicken several times and had high hopes for this recipe but, sadly, this one is not worth all the effort. I would never make it again and do not recommend it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I put the chicken over a bed of fennel, shallots, lemons, garlic, and herbs. Preheated convection oven to 425, cooked 45 minutes. Internal temp read 180. I thought it would be totally dead but it was still great!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If you have weak or arthritic hands, cutting out the backbone with kitchen shears may be too hard.    A pair of tin snips from the hardware store makes the job much easier.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For most beaten egg dishes, like a frittata, I mix 1 tablespoon of corn starch to 3 tablespoons water into a slurry, add seasoning and aromatics and add to the beaten eggs.  It yields an excellent binder, distributes the seasonings in the mixture as well as fluffing and puffing the eggs for increased volume.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Skyeenter Cornstarch is a highly processed food, has no nutritional value, and is not needed whatsoever. I’ve made hundreds and hundreds of frittatas over the years and they are great without it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@michaluna

So I guess it depends on what you’re looking for — if you want something fluffier, you could try the cornstarch slurry tip. But if you want to avoid the processed food, you could skip it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
If you have a glass cooktop, do NOT give the pan a “firm tap” against the stovetop unless you want to shell out hundreds of dollars for a new glass cooktop.

Shaking the pan on a glass cooktop is likewise a bad idea.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My favorite filling, in a one to one-half ration:  fresh cup up asparagus for the main, Then 1/2 each of diced onions and red peppers, to equal the amount of asparagus.  Sauté all until veggies are just beginning to get tender and remove from heat.  Mix eggs and milk, salt & pepper in a bowl, add grated cheese (half Cheddar and half Gruyere are good) and stir into eggs.  Then place in a well buttered casserole; bake in oven at 350 until set.  Much easier than dealing with a cast iron pan.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In my experience of making a weekly frittata, there is no need to stir things. Start with sauteing onions, add the other veg. (leftovers or uncooked), once they are browned and softened, pour eggs over the lot, add some cheese, leave on the stove top for a few minutes (covered may help) then transfer to the broiler. Perfection. (Dollops of Labneh are my newest cheese addition.)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @MC what’s your favorite pan to use for this? Cast iron? Love the one pan style

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have just one question: what are leftover french fries??

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Ally
They are in the same category as leftover wine, I imagine!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: After they're sautéed, I'll toss all the non-egg goodies into a large bowl with the cheese and eggs and give a good mixing. This generally ensures good distribution of ingredients. That gets put back in the pan over medium-low heat and cooked until the sides are set and small bubbles begin to form in the middle. After that I'll stick it in a 350º oven for 12-15 minutes. Perfection.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Don't tap especially if you are using a cast iron pan on a glass stove top!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I don't use a broiler. I've always flipped my frittata by inverting the skillet over a pizza pan and then sliding the frittata back into the skillet to cook/brown what was the top side.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is the method for a Spanish tortilla.  Traditionally, it is made with thinly sliced potatoes and, for some cooks, onions.  It is equally adept at using leftovers and bits & bobs.  I favorite is ham, onions and marinated artichoke hearts.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Italians and Spaniards cook all the stuff you want to add first and then add it to the scrambled eggs in a bowl. Let it sit and the heat makes them stick together. Then clean the pan and start over to make the frittata.  Don't burn it.  When crust forms flip it.  When done it looks like a pie.  In Spain you will see Tortilla Espanola served by the slice for lunch with bread and a salad.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I make a frittata for the two of us every Friday with whatever leftovers I have from the week: in an 8" nonstick cake pan goes whatever I'm using along with 4 well-beaten eggs, and everything goes in the pre-heated air fryer for 12 minutes at 360. Golden, puffed and always delicious: not to mention super easy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sohla, your video lesson on cooking eggs so many different ways is fab. Sharing it with every egg lover I know. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MPqnS1K4vgw

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this recipe this morning, as is, and loved it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this night with leftover cooked veg plus sautéed onions and red peppers.  As some cooks suggested, I popped the whole business in a  casserole dish, put chucks of goat cheese on top and baked till golden brown and delicious.  No broiler. It was delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I prepared this morning and thought it was delicious and easy. Using an assortment of vegetables it came together quickly. More broiling time was needed for browning.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was delicious! I was skeptical of some aspects of the technique, but I followed the instructions exactly and my frittata came out perfectly. This will be my new go-to method! The only thing I did differently was started sautéing onions ten minutes before everything else to give them time to caramelize, then added red peppers, asparagus and halved cherry tomatoes for the recommended quicker sauté. Added a bunch of fresh chives with the eggs, tipped with cheddar and goat cheese, et viola!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: With our 12-inch cast iron, 9 eggs, 1/2 t fine sea salt, a large onion caramelized in 2T olive oil, close to two cups diced chard stems, a tablespoon fresh thyme, over a half cup chunked feta, and a sprinkling of torn tarragon leaves to finish. Excellent!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m afraid I don’t understand what is done in the broiler and what on the stove. Can you clarify?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Jmuscato: You turn the broiler on in Step 2, but all of the cooking is done on stovetop until Step 6--when you broil after dotting with cheese.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hi, any thoughts about the best mix-ins if you want to serve at room temp?  Thanks.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I really like to use cherry tomatoes cut in half if I'm going to serve at room temp. Other veggies (spinach, kale, mushrooms) eg, and proteins (fam fave is bacon) are usually fine, but adding tomatoes give a room temp frittata a bit of freshness and pop!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I thought a Frittata was baked to cook...this is just scrambled eggs with mix-ins and cheese broiled on top...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Recommendations for a broiler safe nonstick skillet?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Cast iron. Every cook needs at least one and a 10 1/2 inch skillet is the perfect size for many recipes. 
Search cast iron and you'll get a gazillion choices!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: ScanPan. Highly recommend.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have one that I got for under $20 bucks at Ikea.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have a slightly different technique, as I mix the vegetables into the beaten eggs, quickly wipe out the pan in case anything has stuck to it, add a little more oil if necessary, and then pour the egg/vegetable mixture back in. I don't usually use cheese but instead top my cooked frittata with salsa verde, zhoug, or pesto base (i.e. herbs, garlic, oil - no nuts or cheese). But I'll try this.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love these "clean out the refrigerator" recipes!!  I just put more than 2 cups in!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In this time of climate chaos, energy conservation is necessary as well as efficiency.  Inverting the frittata instead of turning on the broiler is worth doing—especially on my induction cooktop.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was amazing. I had leftover pork tenderloin, roasted potatoes and added bacon and tomato and Gouda, Cheddar and Feta cheese. Sooooo good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My version, not looking for a browned end product, sauté mix-ins, mix eggs with a cup of cottage cheese, shredded cheese of choice and mix-ins. Pour in a pie pan and cook in the oven at 350° for about 30 minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We were iced in and I had no frozen mixed veggies, so I used match stick carrots, red pepper, and shredded red cabbage. I had frozen peas so used those. Sautéed the raw veggies before the aromatics. Tasted better after some sesame oil and Maggie sauce. Did not care for ketchup so used some Sriracha as was all we had. Definitely comfort food on a cold icy night! Looks like some great leftovers.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: When does this go into the broiler? Am I missing something?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Step 6 says to remove the pan from the heat, dot with cheese, and broil.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was taught that adding salt to beaten eggs and leaving it before cooking caused the eggs to separate. Anyone got any science/further thoughts on that?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
As a friend once suggested to me many years ago… The best made omelets are made from last night’s leftover (“everything”) pizza.      
He said “Just scrape the toppings off and you’ve got a great start.”  I think this would work for frittatas too!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: How can I incorporate frozen spinach?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Best to thaw it first: place in a glass bowl, add a little water, and microwave 3-4 minutes. Drain in colander, pressing very well to get all the green liquid out. then use that.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have sautéed it in the pan, straight from the freezer bag; the water cooks off while you sauté.  (In my case, the spinach had been in the freezer for a while, and I sprinkled with veeeery little garlic powder just in case there was a freezer flavor, lol.  Turned out fine!)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We do “clean up the refrigerator omelets and frittatas regularly.  Use spices and herbs and seasonings as appropriate for the ingredients and your taste.  We ALWAYS have a variety of cheeses, which sort of marry the ingredients.  Hot, nutritious, tasty, no waste, what’s to not like?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The farro texture was surprisingly meaty. I even did a double take, thinking it was sausage. My family assumed the same thing… delicious

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I needed to cook this significantly longer than stated. Using a cast iron pan and an induction stovetop, I found that the center was solidifying faster than the edges.  Next time I would prewarm the pan in the oven if I used the cast iron again.  All in all, I would make it again. It still turned out well and the kids gobbled it up. I added the butts from whole grain bread, frozen peppers from last summer’s garden and some baby Swiss chard past it’s prime.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Great cues for putting together a "whatever you want" frittata. I used 12 eggs (for weekday leftovers), a small amt of olive oil, a number of veggies, cheese and a couple slices of leftover ham. I was worried that sauteing the mix-ins all in the same pan as the egg mix would make it stick but it didn't- came out in perfect slices.  Very quick, easy and delicious, adapts to whatever you have on hand.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love this clean out the fridge method. And I did and it was super tasty. I did overcook the frittata somehow. Maybe cuz one of my add-ins was breadcrumbs. Or maybe cuz I let it go past the "jiggly" phase when it was on the desktop or perhaps it overcooked in the broiler phase, not sure. I'll make this again, but would watch that.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If you have neither broiler nor air fryer, how do you finish? Very hot oven?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Frittata is my go to for feeding two to four people easily for weeknights or even entertaining! My fav add in is leftover roast potatoes. Killer!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Why in the world would you run your broiler on high for up to 15 minutes while you cook the frittata on the stovetop? Simply turn it on when you get to that stage in the recipe — or, as others have suggested, simply flip the semi-hard eggs in the pan, either with a deft wrist flick or the safer inverted-plate method.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe ended up being one of this holidays' favourites. I found out some confusion about stove top degrees during the process: 5 on my stove cannot be medium, because it does nothing to the eggs and 3 cannot be medium-low. I improvised somehow, replaced the chicken with Wiener sausage, and the recipe still became a success.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Having been a life long steak person (as was my mother and both grandmothers) I can pass on that most steaks (rib eye, NY strip, filet, porterhouse) should only be flipped once. Never mash the meat, never oil the pan (salt your seasoned cast iron pan with coarse salt) …. Skirt steak, hanger and the like are a but different…but on finer cuts, this is the standard. Butter, sauces, and toppings are served on the side after the steak has rested allowing the crust to fully form.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Insisting on only flipping your steak once is the single best guarantee for an overcooked steak. That's why modern recipe writers, chefs, and now home cooks don't do it that way. You risk a lot and gain little. https://www.seriouseats.com/the-food-lab-flip-your-steaks-and-burgers-multiple-times-for-better-results

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Hula frances that’s actually a myth. People live by it but you can flip steaks more than once especially when they’re very thick. It won’t cause it to be overcooked if you don’t overcook it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We should all make a New Year Resolution to be thoughtful on when and why to comment.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I follow America’s Test Kitchen reverse sear method.  First, salt and pepper the steak, then warm it in a 265-degree oven for 10 minutes, then flip for another 10 minutes.  Then into the preheated hot cast iron skillet.  No oil, just Kosher salt.  Used to swear by 4 minutes on a side.  Recent research shows that flipping the steak every 60 - 90 seconds gets better results — the crust isn’t as thick and dense.  I have a Thermoworks instant read thermometer to check the temperature.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Obviously, there is more than one path to the perfect steak, and that’s a good thing. My version is salt the hot skillet, flip once, watch for the juice to bubble on top, double check with thermometer sideways through the end, deglaze pan with white wine, add one T of good butter, proceed with gilding the potatoes. Divine and 500 calories less.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Karen a question.  Is it ok to deglaze a cast iron frying pan with wine.  Does it damage the finish built up in the pan?   Does it extract iron into the wine affecting the taste?   Thanks.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Drgonzo777 no,it does not. Not for a cast iron pan. And yes that’s the best way to deglaze a pan. Also a seasoned cast-iron pan shouldn’t get anything in your food but if it does, it won’t be iron. It’s going to be the seasoning.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Never done a steak like this, it was perfectly cooked and I liked the outside browning. Thyme is delicate and I'd add more next time for a stronger presence without being overwhelming.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed instructions precisely - perfect! Could cut my steak with a butter knife

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Get the smallest potatoes possible. Salt the water like the sea

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My nit to pick is wiping out the pan before the butter baste step. That's a lot of flavor going to waste; much better to let the taters bathe in it combined with the butter, no?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Rather than dedicate 30 minutes of constant administration and flipping, I always reverse sear the beef, pork or lamb. The difference between the two outcomes are minimal to the point of questionable.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A great steak tutorial!  Had a beautifully marbled steak demoniac from a local Vermont farm that I did NOT want to over/under cook.  Perfect medium rare, enjoyed by all.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This comment from Hula Frances thoroughly refutes the recipe-how confusing!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
The comment to which you refer did seem to be less than helpful insofar as it indicates that the information presented in the recipe was not taken into account.

As some other commenters point out, research has been done, better techniques now deliver superior results. Notably: 1. Flip Often (this recipe). 2. Reverse Sear. 

Oh, and always use a good digital thermometer. This recipe explains why in the head-notes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Umm,,, skirt steak, cut from the diaphragm of the cow, is almost never as thick as rib-eye. Or almost any other steak.  I'd say that this recipe is perfect for most steaks, except for skirt!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My best steaks now come from my air fryer. Start with the best cuts. Brush with olive oil, massage in S&P, refrigerate for 2  hrs, bring to room temp, 400° in fryer, 10 minutes, flip at 8. No butter needed. Rest for 5 minutes, in or out of the fryer, off, depending on your doneness preference. Crisp char.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Low rating only because I made this with skirt steak and declare there are better uses for that cut than this recipe. The flavor was great, but the texture with all that butter and a thinner cut of meat wasn't appetizing at all.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Who has 30 minutes to cook a steak?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Another method that I have used, to great success  (from various reputable sources including the NYT and Cook's Illustrated) is; pre-heat a cast iron skillet in a 500 degree oven. Season a room temperature steak (T-bone, Porterhouse, or bone-in Ribeye) with salt and pepper. Open a window or turn on the exhaust fan! Put the steak in the skillet and close the oven door. Cook 3 to 5 minutes (depending on the kind of steak and the thickness), then flip and cook another 2 - 3 minutes. Let it rest.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I found I’d use less salt. A sprinkle or two rather than coating the steak. I’m glad I didn’t use as much as the recipe indicated. 

The cooking method did result in an amazingly tender and flavorful steak though. I’ve never employed this  frequent-flipping method and I must say I was impressed.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: How many does this recipe serve?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Under ingredients:  Yield: 2 to 3 servings

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I can tell if a steak is medium rare, the way I like it, just by poking the steak with my finger. It depends on the give of your poke if it's done. Then if that fails, 125F is the right temp for doneness.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Great taste, very rich!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I just applied this method to loin lamb chops with great success.  They were enjoyed with sautéed veg and potatoes fried in duck fat.  Washed down with a valpolicella.  YUM.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Super good and classic. I don't cook steak often but the instructions produced a noticable crust and great texture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: 425 degrees for 12-15 minutes?  I beg to disagree. I followed this NYT cooking advice a few times only to be disappointed and frustrated that the salmon came out overcooked to the point of being dry and tasteless.  I suggest at least checking it after cooking for 6-8 minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody:
I preheat the oven to 400, then get my skillet hot on the stove.  Season the salmon as you like. Then add some olive oil to the pan. Sauté the salmon flesh side down for 15 seconds, just to give the flesh a little crispness. Then flip it skin side down, and immediately pop in the oven for 8 minutes.  
Crispy exterior, buttery soft interior. If you like firmer cooked salmon, give it another minute in the oven.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: If the fish is already properly cooked when I take it out of the oven, wouldn't I overcook it if I stick it back under the broiler for 2-3 minutes? Or did you intend for the skin to be removed and put under the broiler? Otherwise, wouldn't it make more sense to slightly undercook the fish and then finish under the broiler to crisp the skin?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Remove the skin and place that under the broiler.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: The classic rule of thumb for oven-roasting Pacific salmon is 10 minutes per inch of thickness at 450 degrees. I have to assume that would work for the Atlantic variety as well.  If using the broiler to crisp the skin for 2-3 minutes, I’d dial back the roasting time by a minute or two, as the oven will retain a lot of heat and continue to roast the fish while broiling.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: The cooking time for salmon depends on whether it is wild or farmed.  Wild salmon takes about 1/3 to 1/2 the cooking time of farmed (depending on size).   This recipe must have been based on farmed salmon.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: You can also quickly use a blow torch to crisp the skin

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Kenji's "Dry-Brined Salmon" recipe from this site recommends a minimum of 8 hours of salting to achieve "ideal results". Is the difference the amount of salt being used? He gives a specific amount of salt to be used in his recipe and this just says "heavily salt". Can you please clarify?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Can this dry brining technique be used on all fish? Skinless fish, like a cod loin?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: I have tried this technique with dry brining a few times and despite rinsing thoroughly, I find the fish comes out saltier than I like it. Should I be using less salt?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: the cucumber salad deserves to be its own recipe :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Way to salty.  Didn't salt the second time after rinsing and still to salty.  Afraid my landlord will find my mumfied corspe in the morning.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was great! We loved this at our house. I am not a dried fruit fan, so I swapped the cranberries for some fresh pomegranate. Highly recommend - makes for a bright and flavourful side salad.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Hey all y’all. If you only want to cook salmon the way you already cook it, why even read this recipe??

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: This is way too salty even with rinsing. i cook salmon every week and do not find it dry ever.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Trust the recipe and process with this one.  The salad especially seems very strange but all put together delicious and they compliment each other.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: I have to agree with previous commenters who questioned the time needed to cook the fish. Mine was overdone after 12 minutes and probably would have been perfect at 8 or 9 minutes. Also, I salted the fish as directed after rinsing off the brine, and the overwhelming taste was of salt rather than of salmon. If I made this again, I would not use the second dose of salt. But sadly, I won’t make it again; I have more successful methods in my repertoire.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Do you return salmon to broiler skin side up under broiler?  Seems that would cook salmon more than you want?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Wait, did I miss it? What do you do with the cucumbers, dill and cranberry mixture?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: "Rinse the salmon under cool water to remove the salt... Lightly season with salt." Perhaps just tweak the dry-brine step so you don't have to rinse and re-salt

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Brett - the cucumber, dill, cranberry part is just a side dish suggestion (cold salad). It doesn’t mix with the salmon unless you place them overly close on your plate. ;-)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Better cooking in a saute pan lightly oiled on medium high skin side down.  Get pan hot beforehand.   Flip to skinless side after five minutes or so.  Then watch the color change rising up side of fish to exactly your preference (mr to mw).  Lift off with spatula and skin will be crispy!  Love the dry brine idea can't wait to try...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: I think Alison Roman’s recipe for salmon and herbs is a much more efficient method for cooking/crisping by broiling it for 5-7 mins . The recipe itself is simple and outstanding . After reading this recipe thoroughly and the comments I think I will stick with AR’s recipe and double and reduce her sauce / marinade which can be  refrigerated and used again when the mood strikes !

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: I have tried this recipe. No matter how long one washes the salmon, it remains overly salty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: why not just wipe the salt off the salmon when ready to cook instead of flushing the fish with water after all the salt is removing moisture and firming up the fish isn't it?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Does everyone eat the "brown stuff" underneath the skin? I have avoided because it seemed to have an off-putting taste?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: I followed the instructions, marinating the filet for 25 minutes and rinsed it well.  It turned out to be very salty and had a hard texture, not at all like it usually turns out, whether pan or oven roasted.  I wont be trying this again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: This far too complicated and results in dry tasteless salmon.  The suggestions in feedback say it all.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: You could also just pull the fish from the stove or oven when it's somewhere around 110-115. It will finish cooking as it rests. 125 degrees is the magic number if you hate overcooked salmon.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: I've never cooked salmon skin up before. It makes it hard to check for doneness. I set the timer for 8 minutes and cut into it. It still seemed uncooked, so I set the timer for another two minutes. Shame on me, from the PNW, I know better!  It ended up overcooked. Nonetheless, we cooked some brown rice and had bowls with the rice, cucumber salad, and salmon. Delish! Will make again, just cooking less.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: It would be helpful to specify where in the oven the rack should be. The top of the oven will be much hotter than the middle or bottom.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: NYT Cooking has some wonderful recipes, but for those of us on a low/no salt diet, the recipes fail to suggest either a decent salt substitute or herbs. I miss salt, but I would rather do without than have another heart attack!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Salmon is oily enough. Lightly sprinkle greek seasoning and smashed garlic all over, roast at 425 on "airfry" if you have it for the 15-20 minutes, depending upon how pink you want it inside. I recently had a mixed citrus "dip" at a steakouse in NYC. Made my own using lime/orange/lemon rinds, butter and a pinch of sugar. Exceptional. P.S. If you can find King/Chinook salmon, undercook. You'll be glad you did.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: This recipe must be for incredibly thick and fatty salmon, because my wild caught keta salmon was a hard, dense lump after only 8 minutes at that temp. Broiling it after didn’t improve anything. I’m surprised NYT writers don’t talk about different varieties of salmon, and just assume everyone is cooking the thickest and most fatty varieties.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Lovely recipe! Nice hack with with the rolling pin and the cucumbers. I found 220°C for 15min a very adequate time. The salmon came out just the way I love it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Are the dry brining instructions to salt both the skin and meat sides of the fish?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: I agree with other readers.  I cook it for Max 5 minutes and crisp the skin separately

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Remove the skin, or buy it skinless, It tastes & smells fishy. Pan sear it in a hot skillet with butter until brown & crispy, about 2 minutes, flip it over & finish it in the oven for 5 minutes, ( 425 ) and it's a perfect medium that is cooked through but not dry. I prefer M/MR so 3 minutes works.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Totally different preparation so totally different taste.   I've been cooking salmon with skin on for the past decade.  It does not smell fishy and is easy to cook in a variety of ways.  This sounds interesting and easy.  I think I'll try it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Would this work in my air fryer?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Yes. 11-12 minutes, 400F, flesh side up, no flipping.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: I love Sohla and hate to be negative, but this is waaaayyyy more done than I ever want to eat salmon. I always dry brine, usually also add some cane sugar. If I want crisp skin, the start in a cold skillet method works. Or you can just broil, skin up for 8 minutes. But my favorite is to cook the skin separately. I do the meat at 275 for 15 minutes and then toast the skin on a piece of foil in the toaster oven until it is like a potato chip, which takes about 10 minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: At the risk of sounding like a boor, I loathe cranberries and wonder what an acceptable substitute might be? It's a texture thing/phobia, I guess...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: The Washington Post has a salmon recipe to broil salmon skin side up to get it cooked and crisp. https://www.washingtonpost.com/recipes/broiled-salmon-horseradish-sauce/

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Given the industrial farming of most salmon and the fact that it’s shipped to the consumer by airplane, I don’t get it, while this fish still is recommend. There are so many alternative fishes with less environmental impacts.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: After I dry brined I marinated salmon in maple syrup and dash of soy for a few hours (my usual method). Rinsed off salt, towel dried before popping back into marinade, adding more maple syrup on top and cooked for about 10 minutes before popping under the broiler. Worried fish was going to be overdone but not yet crispy, so stripped off skin and returned under the broiler on its own. End result was delicious, fish perfectly done, tender and moister than usual: modify recipe to your own liking.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: The fish came out perfectly! Mine was thinner so I roasted for ten and broiled for one.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: The dry-brining method was so easy & far superior to standard oven baked!  425 convection for 25 minutes...salmon came out excellent flavor AND texture!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Don't know about others, but instructions as written gave us a completely yummy dinner.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: really easy, delicious way to cook the salmon.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: I have been wondering how to achieve nice crispy skin on salmon. I was excited about trying this recipe until I read the notes. I'm confused how it got a 4-star rating. Can anyone offer some insight on whether the notes tend to be overly critical/picky or do the reviews skew toward being overly positive?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Nub - best ever way to cook salmon. It was perfect, thank you!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Too much salt!!! Unhealthy....

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: After trying a number of methods of cooking salmon, I've come to rely on my air fryer. I prefer farm raised Atlantic salmon; center cut skin-on filets of 8 oz. I spray the screen, season the flesh side, and air fry without flipping for 11-12 minutes at 400F. Comes out perfectly each time

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: I cooked a 7oz piece for 5 minutes and it was perfect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Did not work for me. Used the flavor profile successfully for a canned salmon puree for flatbread roll ups. Subbed greek yogurt for oil, left the non pureed cranberries and cucumbers to scatter on last. So good on hot day. Chives next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: I cooked at 425 for 10 minutes, then 3 min under the broiler to crisp the skin.  Perfect.  However, the additional salt called for after the fish had been dry brined put it over the top of saltiness for me.  The cucumber dish was yummy.  I substituted sultanas for cranberries.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: Would you all do yourselves a favor and use a meat thermometor?  145 degrees for salmon will be medium.  I like it rare, so 125. All of the anxious comments about cooking times for meat and fish, when a simple thermometer produces perfect results every time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
commentBody: whaat if you cant get cucumbers

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Fine idea, except why bother with the broiler? Put the cruciforms back in the still-hot pan, and stir around while they blacken. I add a little water at the end and put a lit on to get a little steam cooking if needed. Add the other ingredients for some more heat just before serving. One pan, no waiting.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody:
@Lisa 
Great idea!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: I prefer to roast the broccoli at @425* for about 20 minutes to deepen the flavor while remaining crunchy. Also provides a larger margin of error to avoid over charring.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: This nut-date topping is fantastic. I used pecans and pistachios, charred the broccoli and served it over farro. Delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Roasted broccoli is much easier in an Air Fryer if you have one. 360 for 3-6 minutes depending on quantity

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: I have made this several times, roasting at 425F instead of broiling.  I microwave the broccoli, misted with a little water, for 2-3 minutes before roasting.  It retains its bright color and crunch but I don’t have to watch it as closely as I would have to if broiling.  Last night I used a combination of walnuts, pecans, and a few pistachios, using walnut oil instead of olive oil.  That was the best version so far.   Going to try this with cauliflower next.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody:
@Kit
I love all of your ideas. Cauliflower would taste good too and maybe adding butternut squash or roasted sweet potatoes too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: @Kit genius move with microwaving; I debated blanching/shocking to get it cooked through beforehand, but microwave was perfect before getting some roasted edges.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: What a lovely addition to the broccoli rotation. I used pecans and pepitas with the dates. Easy and delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: To simplify, roasted broccoli in the oven at 400F for 30 mins. Delish. Next time, will smear whipped ricotta on baked sweet potato and top with the broccoli and seasoned nuts and dates. Sprinkling of fresh lemon zest to finish. Bliss.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Awesome served over farro.  Added a little date syrup and chili oil.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Paired nicely with pork tenderloin.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: This was so incredibly good! I like broccoli extra done so I roasted it at 400 for about 25ish minutes with a can of chickpeas and half a red onion. The dressing is OMG delicious. I used pistachios walnuts pepitas and pinenuts and followed the recipe after that!! I could eat this every day!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. You can air fry the broccoli to save time, but nothing beats a slow roast in the oven for taste. Used szechuan peppercorns for an extra kick!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: This was a hit with my family. So much so that I re-made the nut and date topping the next morning and we have been throwing it on everything (and, I confess, eating it by the spoonful).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Expect those sizzled nuts to show up in other dishes! Breakfast oatmeal with a maple drizzle to finish? Yip. Even with all that black pepper.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: You can totally broil the broccoli while toasting the nuts. Do not omit lemon juice. Mix with white beans for a full meal.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody:
A delightful surprise - easy to prepare, delicious (used pepitas and pecans), and devoured! 

Also appreciate the tip on using our salad spinner to dry the broccoli - not something I would've tried for fear that spinning something so chunky would break it. It didn't, and worked like a charm.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: I used sliced almonds, walnuts and dates., plain California were fine. More the merrier. The lemon juice should be stirred into the mixture off the heat and set aside. 8 minutes under a close hi broiler is fine.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody:
Absolutely delicious . different and super healthy and a versatile side dish. Could also be a main dish with the addition of  tofu or chicken.
I made Spanish mackerel fillets  escabeche and it went great together.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight, outstanding way to liven up sometimes boring broccoli. I took another poster’s advice and charred the broccoli in the same cast iron pan used for toasting the nuts, simple and delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Delicious.  Used cashews and pistachios.  I took another reviewers suggestion and microwaved the broccoli 3 minutes and then roasted it in convection oven at 400 for about 10 minutes.  It was perfectly charred and just soft enough.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: I used roasted mixed nuts and pumpkin seeds. I cooked the broccoli as instructed in the air fryer. Favorably received

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody:
This reminds me of the “Sunny Broccoli” deli salad of the 90’s made with raisins and sunflower seeds.
Other combinations of nuts and fruits could be interesting as well. I might try craisins for a pop of color.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight and loved the nut and date topping! Worth repeating the recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: We served this on a plater surrounded by the NYT recipe "Air-Fryer Tofu" and it was beautiful and tasty. This recipe pairs nicely with other recipes because it doesn't compete with strong flavors...it is subtle but wow!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: This tasted even better than it looks.  2 - 4 servings and I had to restrain myself from eating it all. Guess what I'm having for breakfast today!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Perfect as written for me. Browning nuts in a pan is important since you then add the dates to plump up a bit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: The topping doesn't adhere, so you'll be chasing it around your plate to try and balance it on your fork with the broccoli. It's so good tho that you won't care. Just eat it with your fingers.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Put all cooked components in a bowl and added a quarter cup of a sauce composed of olive oil, honey, Dijon, and harissa (about a tsp of the latter two). It made the flavors pop without overpowering. Guests loved it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: I (mostly) made this Saturday and loved the topping!  I used pecans & Pepitas, & it was delicious. I roasted the broccoli as I usually do, rather than broiling, & I served it atop yogurt mixed with salt, pepper, cumin, & lime (I was out of lemons).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Loooved this! The combination of nuts / dates / black pepper is excellent. I roasted my broccoli and used a mix of almonds and pecans. Served it with edamame beans, baked tofu, and chilli crisp for lunch.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody:
Excellent!
Steamed broccoli in the MW first, then broiled about 8-10 minutes 
Used pecans and walnuts
Like the ratio but may have had less broccoli than called for
Lemon is a must

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: I was skeptical about this one, even though I love all the ingredients but is delicious and now it in our family rotation.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Like several other commenters, I roasted the broccoli (25 mins @ 400F) along with, on a separate sheet pan, quartered cremini mushrooms (17 min) I’d tossed in olive oil and seasoned with sea salt. I thought the combination worked well with the date topping, which really does only need a minute to cook.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: The topping is dangerous! Easy to eat it all up before it makes it on to the broccoli! I will be making it regularly and keeping it in the refrigerator! Highly recommend the European blend peppercorns from Penzey’s for that ingredient.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: I used sesame and sunflower seeds with almonds. Added some creamy goat cheese crumbles and chili pepper flakes to the top. Combined with the dates and charred broccoli, the flavors are a perfect mixture of sweet, hot and salty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: I must admit that I steamed the broccoli.  Perhaps I don't know how to properly roast it, because every time I've tried, it comes out simultaneously burned and raw.  I only had walnuts, so that's what I used, along with some dates I found in the back of the pantry.  I devoured the whole thing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Roast 425x20min

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: I agree with Lisa.  Just blacken the broccoli in the hot pan.  (In Texas, I like to avoid the oven if possible--too hot in the kitchen!)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious, beyond simple. I roasted the broccoli, used pecans, walnuts and sunflower seeds and made a meal of it by putting it over sweet potato glass noodles.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody:
My broccoli hating husband absolutely devoured this. I added sesame seeds, cashews and walnuts. I realized cashews roast quickly so maybe add it a minute later than the walnuts. 
I will add few more dates and probably chop up the broccoli after cooking so i get all bits in one spoonful 🥹

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody:
Made this for Mother's Day. Was a huge hit. The nut & date topping will be a go-to for me as it's easy to make and will work well over other veggies or even salads.  I happened to have dates at home. But if out, dried cranberries or raisins would work well.

I wasn't able to get the broccoli stems crispy, even on broil high for 12 min. They were bit soft. Crowns were fine. I'll try drying it even more next time.  And I might skip the step to peel the stems.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Am I crazy or do the sizzled nuts and dates need a pinch of brown sugar and maybe even some red pepper flakes?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: This was really good. I realized (as others have commented) that the nut mixture would work well with broccoli just roasted instead of broiled and I'll do that next time just because I'm more used to cooking broccoli that way. For me, seasoning with a little salt was almost too much. On the other hand I only used a little bit of pepper and it wasn't enough. Also, I amost overcooked the nuts - have to be careful there. I made this with figs because I forgot to by dates. Still good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Vegetarian go to

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: everyone, i mean everyone at our dinner last night loved this recipe.  even the dog, oops, we don't have a dog.  i slowly toasted the nuts and seeds because i have burned them too many times before in other recipes.  i also took my time when adding the dates.  i will be making this recipe again and again.  i used bagged broccoli florets.  my husband doesn't like black peppercorns and so i just lightly sprinkled the broccoli with freshly ground black pepper.  it was delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Toasting nuts in a toaster oven, if you have one, for about 6 to 7 minutes results in perfectly toasted and not-burned nuts.  Pan toasting requires constant attention and if you turn away for a minute you've got char.  Different toaster ovens may vary in the time needed but once you've got it down bob's-your-uncle.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: For a bachelor & senior citizen, this set of instructions is very confusing. "Trim the dry end off the broccoli & peel the stem. Cut broccoli stem in half lengthwise, then pull it apart to break the head in half. Cut into individual florets, each with a long spear of stem attached." I understood none of this. The dry end? Peel the stem? Perhaps with detailed instructions like those using terminologies that I found confusing, & perhaps others did too, we could've used a video to show us the steps

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: I guess this is close to how I prepare broccoli anyway so it made sense to me.  Here is another way to say it:  Remove the tough end of the stem and discard it.  Peel the stem up to the crown.  Cut through the stem lengthwise but leave the crown intact; then pull apart the two stem halves to gently separate the two halves of the crown.  Cut the two half-heads lengthwise several times to make slender spears, each having some stem and crown.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: The sizzled nuts & dates are so delicious! I haven’t even put them over broccoli yet, but I’ve been making knockout breakfasts of plain whole milk yogurt, sliced banana, a drizzle of honey and topped with the nuts/dates. Sounds strange, but trust me, the sweet/salty/creamy/crunch is divine! I think this topping will become as much a staple now as the homemade chili-garlic crunch!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
