recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody:
- Can put back in the still-hot pan, stir around while they blacken. Add a little water at end w a lid on to get a little steam cooking if needed. Add other ingredients for some more heat just before serving. One pan!
- 425 for 20 min or Air Fryer @ 360 for 3-6 min
- used pecans and pepitas with the dates

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: not for me. too hard to eat , the nuts and dates don't stay on the fork with the broccoli.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Easy, delicious and nutritious.  I cooked the broccoli in the air fryer with 2 Tbs evoo, used a mix of walnuts, pistachios and pine nuts (based on what we already had in the house), slightly increased the dates and added a sprinkling of cayenne.  This will go into our rotation from now on.  Yum!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: This is one of the best things ever if you love brassicas and dates, crunch and acid. Yeah, I roasted the broccoli. Otherwise, as written. Brilliant. Thank you, Soha!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody:
Very special flavors.. husband raved. I doubled the nuts/ dates/ lemon. Used some Costco mixed nuts plus sunflower seeds
Due to timing issue, I added the charred broccoli into the cast iron pan with nuts- coating everything nicely and then a quick reheat…my new fav broccoli recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: We all loved this!  A perfect blend of nutty toastiness, char, and sweetness!  I used pistachios, slivered almonds, walnuts, pecans, sunflower seeds, black and white sesame seeds with only 2T olive oil. After the broccoli and nut blend was assembled, I sprinkled a few tablespoons of pomegranate seeds over it all which added tiny bursts of sweet/tart moisture. Delish!  And easy to make.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Roasting the broccoli first would help to get it cooked through and then finish under broiler if needed but mine usually gets toasty anyway.  I used whole raw cashews but next time I’ll break them up.  Date/nut mix was delicious but I doubt I used 2 teaspoons of pepper and it was quite peppery!  This mix was also good in some coconut curry rice we had with it and I can imagine it with a lot of other things!  You could add pasta for a meal as well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Would this be good with olives?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe! I was skeptical of the dates, but they were really good in combination with the toasty nuts. I suspected that two tsp of peppercorns was going to be excessive so I halved it and was glad I did, but other people may be bigger peppercorn fans.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: This dish was ok. The nut topping was delicious, but the preparation suggestions need work, I think.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody:
Can Cauliflower be substituted for the Broc, using the same recipe (but I assume a longer cooking time, due to the density of the Caulis)?

How about figs, rather than dates?  Anyone try that?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: This is a really interesting dish. I served it on a bed of mashed potatoes. In the future I might cut the spears into bite-sized pieces before broiling for easier serving.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: I am switching to more plant-based meals and found this pretty delicious over cooked farro.  However, I wish it were a little sassier. Does anyone have any suggestions how to have the topping have a little more liquid? Thanks.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious!! Easy and I look forward to keeping it in the broccoli rotation.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: I discovered that the last of my dates had been consumed so roasted some sweet potato cubes instead. Delicious! Love the toasted and seasoned nuts. What a great idea to combine with roasted broccoli. Next time I’ll try the dates.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Quite tasty. The dates and broccoli are a great combination and the nuts complement the dish well. The problem is getting the nut and date mixture to adhere to the broccoli. Basically, one has to fork a piece of broccoli and the scoop up the nut mixture but the flavors are great

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: I've made this a few times. Interesting recipe.  I might add a pinch of Korean pepper to the next batch.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: This can easily be done in a wok, skipping the broiling, but giving a similar result. I did it in two stages with the nuts and dates first, then setting that aside to dry sauté the broccoli before recombining all. Two things to clean. Pretty good, if not identical.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: An amusing aside. I totally botched cooking this recipe. Too hot broiler, nuts mixture added to broccoli. Result: incinerated veg. Live and learn

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: I used the nuts/seeds I had on hand - cashews, sesame seeds, pecans and pine nuts, and wound up eating half a head of a huge roasted cauliflower. This is bonkers good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious.  I too modified how I cooked the broccoli by roasting it at 450 degrees instead of broiling. The combination of flavors and textures is perfect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: We all really liked this but the dates never really melted to make a coating on the broccoli similar to the pictures. I used a combo of pecans, pepitas and pistachios. My guests asked for the recipe so it was all good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Amazing - the combo of textures and flavors is fantastic. Had it for dinner all by itself and was very very happy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious nut, date & lemon sauce. Trust the 2 teaspoons of pepper. It gives a nice kick without using capsicum. 
I added a bit of lemon zest last minute with a tiny sprinkle of parsley just before serving. 
Roast any cruciferous veg any way then top with this. Yum!

I had freshly roasted pepitas on hand so warmed the nuts gently & skipped to step 2 without the 2T. oil. Easy, interesting texture, great leftovers.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Par boil  the Broccoli and then in oven - shorter control time in oven

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: The date nut addition (I only had cashews) was great.  Lemon juice didn't come across as lemony, just as brightness.  I messed up the prep and made small florets with some chopped (peeled) stems and it was still great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Everyone in my family loved this! The date/nut mixture adds such a lovely texture. I used half cashews half peanuts. Watch the nuts carefully when sauteeing, they burn quickly!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: sohla did it again! this nut-date topping is incredible. we grilled our broccoli.. but why stop there? serve over salads, your favorite protein bowl, leave out the pepper and add it to yogurt or pancakes. a new favorite topping in our house!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: This topping is phenomenal. Dates and broccoli really go hand-in-hand. I ended up microwaving briefly first to cook the broccoli through (instead of blanching/shocking) then roasting which I would continue to do. I will be making this regularly.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Anyone try roasting a day ahead & doing a quick reheat? I've got too much going on in the oven.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Could you make the topping a day ahead and then warm it during prep?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: A big favorite at our Thanksgiving.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: My first comment here because this is THAT delicious. Absolutely genius blend of flavors and textures. Yum!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Very good and easy to make! It was a hit at Thanksgiving!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: One of the best vegetable (and vegan) dishes we've ever had.  We made it exactly as written, with the nuts being a mix of cashews and pecans.  Yum, thank you Sohla El-Waylly!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: The delicious topping is also excellent on roasted Brussels sprouts.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Change out the dates to slim slices of red pepper and red onion to keep the sweet vibe with less calories. Healthier, too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
commentBody: Great and easy recipe! I increased the date/nut mixture a bit and added scarlet runner beans and roasted butternut squash with cumin and a little chili powder. Made a great dinner that happened to be vegan!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I'm Armenian and all of my grandparents were from Turkey. We made it like this: 

First, lightly brown very thin egg noodles with butter in a saucepan (my grandmother and mother used to use an entire stick of butter, but of course, that was the 60s. Three tablespoons works just fine) - just don't let them get too dark. Add the rice, thoroughly coat in the butter and then add chicken broth to cover. Simmer covered until liquid has been absorbed. Season to taste. Fluff. Perfect. Every. Time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Jacquie, your recipe is very close to mine. I’m GF and use GF spaghetti, broken into the butter. Cook to golden, add rice and mix into the butter, then chicken broth! Delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
My Armenian grandparents were also from Western Armenia, Dikranagerd, before they were driven out by the Turks. Had to add that for accuracy sake! :)
We would add shaydeh (thin egg noodles) and cook as you've described except my mom would pour in boiling water or heated chicken broth to the sauted rice and noodles. Btw, a heavy bottomed pot is essential.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Laurig That’s the way this non Armenian (“odar”) was taught to make it by my Dikranagerd mother in law.  I still use a stick of butter to 2 cups of rice.  But I discovered a diffuser. I put that under the pot when I lower the heat.  No more rice stuck to the bottom (but that is a treat for whoever empties the pot!).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The repeated rinsing , soaking and discarding of the water in this recipe will effectively extract much of the B vitamins remaining in the white rice or that have been added to it via enrichment.  My practice with white rice is to limit the rinsing, live with the starch and keep the B vitamins.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Parching brown rice until aromatic and colored causes it to open and be fluffier, more like white rice in texture.  A neat trick for the brown rice adverse. Cook as you would normally after the toasting.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @BJ. The recipe here doesn’t call for enriched rice. In most cases, normal rice grains such as aren’t enriched and should always be rinsed before cooking. There is light traces of arsenic from the soil found in rice even in organic rice and should be rinsed off. Rinsing the rice also reduces the excessive starch in rice making the resulting cooked rice lighter and fluffier.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can this be adapted to a rice cooker?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Raba Zaki
Yes.  I used to do this in a small heavy pot as a side dish, or a one dish meal. When I had a rice cooker, I made it in the rice cooker.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is the best rice I’ve ever made. I followed the recipe exactly. Thank you.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m Indian born in Kolkata, India. My family members cooked this dish on all the religious occasions. And we loved it all the time cause It’s great. And it’s one of the most tasty foods that you’ll ever taste.  Try it and feel the smell of heaven in your nostrils. Good luck….

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I am assuming that after step 2, all ingredients can be transferred to a rice cooker.  Step 5 has detailed instructions, it seems, for those uninitiated in rice cooking.  Any thoughts on why this might not transfer to a rice cooker successfully?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
My Mother, from Northern Illinois, learned to make rice this way while working in Brazil.  

Back in the States, American cooks were astonished to see her “fry the rice before boiling it”.   And all the more astonished about the fluffy outcome.  (“It’s not even the instant rice!”)

As suggested in the recipe, she also added chopped onions and minced garlic.  The rice was ready to be steamed when it had become opaque.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Ever since I left my mother's house (she relied on Uncle Ben's Instant Rice, which I hated) and began cooking for myself, I have always cooked rice like this. I just thought this was how rice was cooked. Now I am going to make myself sound fancy and say I am cooking pilaf!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thanks for this useful recipe but would like to point out it is never advisable to use water from the hot tap for consumption as this water contains containments (even 2 Tbs from the step to take if your rice has not cooked sufficiently after 18 min).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Good tip. Never drink water from a hot tap as it might contain dissolved minerals and metals due to the heating process.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Use filtered cold water.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I do a cheater parching where I spread the dry rice on a toaster over tray and toast it then proceed as for regular stove top rice.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Living g in San Antonio TX rice is a major part of Tex Mex cooking. I received a tip from someone to place a tea towel between the pan and lid during the steaming process at the end. I get fluffy rice every time whether I toast it first or not. Have also used this technique with other grains.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yes! I do the tea towel for steaming/resting at the end, EVERY time I cook any kind of rice, and it never fails. People always want more of the fluffy, perfect rice, whether it's a pilaf or just plain rice.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love this recipe -- it's similar to how I cook rice, except that (I can hear the horrified intake of breath already) I don't soak. I just measure it out, plunk it in a pot, add 1 tsp of Diamond Crystal Kosher salt per cup, 1 tablespoon of good butter per cup of rice, & 1 1/2 cups boiling water per cup. I cover it & peek in at about 15 minutes, when it's usually done, & then I turn off the heat, fluff it, cover it with a tea towel & then the pot lid. 5 minutes later it's gorgeous perfection.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Rinsing enriched white rice removes the added vitamins and minerals. Do not rinse unless you have reason to believe it's dirty. I generally add onions, garlic, peppers and celery when I make a pilaf. The green makes it pretty. Cumin can also be toasted with the rice. Use imagination. I've been cooking this way for close to 60 years.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe is the first time that a NYT recipe was a bust! For me, at least.  I followed the instructions for rinsing and letting sit covered in water, just 15 minutes.  Then sauteed in the butter per the instructions and added only 3 cups of chicken broth for 2 cups rice.  Even with less water, this turned into a mushy concoction.  I can't imagine what another cup of liquid would have done.  Probably turned it into a porridge. Next time, no soaking.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I would suggest that your pot that is used will be a big determiner in how much moisture is left or isn't left at the end. I have one pot, I only use for rice. It isn't a wonderful pot it just works. I try not to use it for anything else and that helps keep it "working" as my rice pot. I have bought several upgrades thinking that they would be the shiny new rice pots. They were all a disappointment. How to find the right pot? Look for one without "hot spots" but mostly try different pots.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I added some toasted broken angel hair for extra flavor and authenticity.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a lovely, but hardly foolproof, recipe. I find that baked rice is easier, more forgiving, and always turns out beautifully.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My mother's friend cooked rice this way, she used Uncle Ben's and it was the most delicious rice I've ever had.

author: Sohla El-Waylly

author: Sohla El-Waylly

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I have never seen a pilaf which differed from cooked rice solely by browning the rice in butter first.  My pilafs feature a flavored liquid, often broth, with spices, aromatics,  and/or vegetables.  Sometimes I throw in dried fruit like raisins, dried apricots, dried mango bits.  Been cooking for over 50 years, am not a newbie.  

Dictionary definition of pilaf isn’t as Sohla claims.  But don’t just take my word for it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I added some more butter at the end and my spouse didn’t like it as much, though my daughter did. So 50/50. I felt like the effort didn’t result in anything more different than my rice cooker. 

Maybe I’ll try again with more salt and less butter. I also want to try it with more aromatics and extras.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’ve been making basmati rice like this for years and always turns out well. I use 1:1 rice to water. One trick that help keep rice (and other grains cooked in water) fluffy is when you turn off heat, let it rest for 5-10 minutes before serving but put a tea towel over the top and then the lid on top of that. The towel soaks up the extra steam and prevents the rice from getting mushy from condensing steam.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I will try this method, but I wonder what the purpose of rinsing the rice is? Doesn't that make it mushy?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I use long grain rice ( basmati).
Butter is nice but parching in olive oil or ghee works fine too.
For extra flavor stir fry some whole cumin seed and a big black cardamom with the onions and garlic before parching the rice.
Because parching gets the cooking process going I prefer to use 1.5 cups of water for every cup of rice. Also live to add greens like chopped coriander and/or thinly sliced scallions to the finished product. 
For a veg  ‘biryani’ just mix in frozen peas and carrots.

Very delicious with the addition of shallots with the rice while parching, coriander stems laid on top while cooking, and chicken broth as the cooking liquid.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I use a container, cup or glass filled with long-stem rice.  The liquid I add is just about twice the size of the rice container. Ex: If I use one cup rice, I will add one cup water and one cup broth. I add a Tbsp. butter and a few squirts of olive oil, to the pan plus salt.  I put a lid on the pan and cook on high until rice comes to a full, long boil. Then I turn off the heat, leave the lid on the pan and let it set until liquid is absorbed.  Fluff with a fork.  Works perfectly every time!

commentBody: This is just how I make a Pilaf. I like more salt, but that’s a taste preference, and I use half butter and half olive oil for the fat. author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
NEVER rinse cooked pasta! OMG, I can't believe people do this. 

First, it's unnecessary: it's just been in boiling water; why are you rinsing water with water?

Second, you're rinsing off the starch, which is an essential pasta ingredient, not only for taste, but so that any sauce or condiment you put on it will adhere to it. 

And never just put cooked pasta aside with no oil on it, or keep it separate from whatever sauce you're using. It will turn into a gloppy, gluey mess.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Rinsing the cooked noodles is a normal step in preparing Chinese noodle dishes, where you *don't* want the starch from the noodles to gum up the dish and you *do* want the noodles to be cool before adding to the wok/pan for later stir-fry.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Lisa Simeone that’s a very one-sided view.  Different people and cultures cook noodle dishes differently.   Remember from where pasta originates, it wasn’t italian.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Rinsing pasta can be appropriate under certain conditions. In this recipe, the pasta is rinsed to cool it down and stop it from cooking further.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The pasta is still cooking when you turn off the heat, when you carry it to the sink to dump it into the colander, when you set it down afterwards. So just do all of that a little sooner. I still say never rinse pasta.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
The choice to rinse or not rinse your pasta depends on your ultimate goal. If your goal is to help emulsify any fats into a sauce, then, yes, do not rinse your pasta. Leave it wet, even reserving the starchy water to aid in the formation of the sauce.

If your goal is to substitute pantry-staple spaghetti for fresh lo-men noodles, then you should rinse the pasta. This dish should be loose, the noodles separate.

commentBody: yum my Bengali mom makes this too! she never rinses the noodles. she also sautés some green chilies with the ginger and garlic, and also adds cut and lightly steamed potatoes with the other veggies. mushrooms taste so good in this!

It was a filling meal that took me about 40 minutes to get to the table. My impression was that this was a wonderful dish for children. I striped it with ketchup as pictured and the hit of sweet tomatoey-ness definitely helped. If I made it again, I’d double the aromatics and the sauce ingredients and also add some chili garlic oil or chili crisp with the final mix. author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I recommend watching the video. It answers the questions that are here and likely will be here, plus it's entertaining.
- there's a ton of sugar in these recipes, that's the point
- use whatever mix-ins you want
- imitation vanilla is used with the other extracts to get "that nostalgic packaged cookie vibe"
- rolling the dough in mix-ins yields prettier cookies
- the dough is formulated around being mixed and baked. Making the dough ahead is likely to dry it out and yield less chewy cookies

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Erin is correct!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I had difficulty getting the toppings rolled onto the cookie dough. Too dry! I read all the comments and watched the video but no other bakers commented on this issue.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I sprinkled the mini m&ms on top to get them to show.   the sprinkles stuck well for me

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cookies were too salty and ended up very hard, wouldn’t make again

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Mine were perfectly salted with Diamond brand kosher salt and still had great chewiness with about 10-11 minutes in the oven at 375. I wonder if you used something other than diamond kosher which might explain the saltiness?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love this recipe and am with the other cooks who called it "the perfect cookie." I'm a horrible baker, but these made me feel and look like a star! And I LOVE that the dough doesn't have to chill before baking. So fast!! I didn't have imitation vanilla on hand, so I used vanilla paste in its place. Don't skip the final roll in the sprinkles before baking. My boyfriend wanted some with fewer sprinkles, so he made me do some without the roll. They look so sad and naked.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Under cook these slightly! I didn’t let them get a golden edge and they were still a little crunchier than I would have liked and would have benefited from a minute less in the oven.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: As others have noted, the suggested baking time was far too long. I set for the minimum of 14 minutes but should have checked sooner - the first batch was quite burned and crisp. I froze the rest of the dough and will check after 10 minutes when I got to bake those.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
So fun for Pride month! Kids loved them. I see any instruction to melt butter as a requirement to brown it.  I did that here and 1. I came up short 2 cookies doing everything by weight; 2. 14 mins was way too much baking time; 3. Cookies were delicious!
I let my batter sit, per the instructions, and had no problem with sprinkle color bleeding. I used Wilton Sprinkled/Colored Jimmies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If ever there was a school bake sale recipe, this is it. Kids always pick this over all else.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was a great option for getting rid of a lot of my kid's Halloween candy! The video I'd very helpful.  These cookies are meant to be extremely sweet.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My batch were hard at 14 minutes. I wish have followed reviews that said 12 but I was distracted……following the recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made exactly as written for a pride party. I was worried they would taste like food coloring and canuba wax but people inhaled them! Definitely a crowd pleaser

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are just plain amazing! mine came out a bit to crunchy after 12 minutes, so I would bake for a little less than that.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
My changes (which I was pleased with):
-Sugar cut in half
-Used table salt, cut in half since it’s lower volume than kosher
-Also baked at 350 for 12-14 minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I had trouble rolling the dough into balls, and then wasnt able to roll them in the candy because they were too dry. I watched the video and didn't rest the dough - I may try weighing the flour next time, maybe I had too much?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I had this same problem and added an extra egg the second time - worked perfectly.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is my favorite kind of cookie, crispy on the edges, chewy in the middle, made in one bowl, and endlessly customizable. Per other's recommendations, I cut the salt by a bit since I wasn't using diamond crystal, and baked the cookies for 11-12 minutes. I also made mine slightly larger at 45g each.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My cookies were delish but too crispy.  Did that happen to anyone else?  I want to make these again, so I want to learn what changes to make.  Thx!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Hollymine too and I was disappointed by that. I think creaming the butter & sugar til fluffy, rather than letting it sit for 30 and stirring twice, might be the key to softer cookies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Holly I should have trusted my instincts and baking experience to pull these cookies at 10 minutes. They came out crispier than I wanted, not terrible, but not the chewy cookies I was expecting.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The Lisa Frank of cookies! These were the number one pick of all the cookies I made. Follow the instructions  as written, make them all the same size and don’t overtake.  They are the perfect blend of chewy, crunchy, colorful sweetness.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I can not wait to try these. They will add a perfect pop of color to our cookie boxes this year. Thank you Sohla!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For those of us with an allergy to almonds…should we substitute or just skip?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You should know about imitation extracts if you have nut allergies! Of course, do your research before ingesting anything you're potentially allergic to.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Ememem - My partner has a nut allergy and I’ve found the McCormick Culinary imitation almond extract to work really well in baked goods. It gives that wonderful amaretto flavor I missed all the years I didn’t think to look for imitation.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: There is no cookie more PARTY than these! So pretty, fun, and delicious. I can’t wait to make them again. I needed to adjust the baking time to 10 mins— mine browned in that quickly.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love that--'there is no cookie more PARTY than these!--tells me all I need to know about these little gems.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Where should I look for "large" rainbow sprinkles? Is there a recommended brand?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A sugar bomb, as advertised. So delicious! My cookies took about 12-14 minutes. Huge payoff for minimal work and mess.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Might have been my oven, but 14 minutes was way too long to cook these.  I made other recipes and the timing was right, just not this one. I was definitely in the 9-10 minute range to avoid hard and overdone cookies.  Maybe try a test one or two before a full batch? When i worked out the right timing, they were delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed the recco for 350 oven at ten minutes… got the perfect, soft, chewy cookie. Definitely do the 30 minute rest, too!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Could I brown the butter or would that affect the outcome? 
These cookies are very good, but they need that something extra. As written I would give them a 4/5. 
 I weighed all ingredients, opted not to do the 30 min rest and baked for 10-12 minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Imitation vanilla is both more economical and far more sustainable/environmentally friendly, and in a recipe like this, the lightly artificial flavor is the entire point. Save the real stuff for custards and sauces only and make the world a better place while you're at it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Don't make the mistake I made with the "let this mixture sit for 30 minutes" step! That's at **room temperature**, not in the fridge. You will be sad if you put it in the fridge. You will be more satisfied if you leave it on the counter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made by my adult daughter.  She reported that the dough was crumbly and she needed to add more butter to form cookies.  Also, they hardened prematurely.  (Followed recipe.) She suggested it might be better to experiment with adding sprinkles and m&ms to a favorite sugar cookie recipe instead. Won’t make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thank you for mentioning vegan butter as an alternative in the recipe. We are not a vegan household, but we are strictly dairy free due to an allergy. A note to others who might be making these cookies for loved ones in the same boat: watch your mix-ins! M&Ms aren't vegan and contain milk.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are easily over-baked! The problem with the direction about how long to bake them is that the dough has a deep colored tone to it before you bake it, so looking for a "barely golden edge" is futile -- the dough isn't pale enough to show a golden edge. The directions should say: Bake for 14-15 minutes (I did 16 and it was TOO long); the cookies won't change color but will have a pliable edge when you take them out. I sadly over-baked mine.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 9 mins in the oven and 1-2 on the cookie sheet before a cooling rack gave me the chewy cookie I wanted! Any more made the cookies super hard (I think the cookie itself doesn’t have a lot of fat and more sugar). If I were to make them again, I’d also focus less on the mix ins and make sure my dough balls were completely rolled in the candy, less sickly sweet and get the texture and look!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Kids are very excited about these, but I did have to take them out at 10 minutes and would probably cut the salt a bit- I'm a salt wimp, but it is a great, super fun recipe :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
-Mine baked for 11 minutes. MAYBE even 10, at 12 they were BURNT
-i added pretzels in addition to MnMs/sprinkles, worked really well

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You must watch the video. So entertaining and really helped me understand the steps of the recipe and made me buy the app for a year lol. We ate these cookies up in 24 short hours!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The cookies surely will last longer than 3 days!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I added some chopped mini pretzels because I was craving a little more salt and crunch. So good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used a sprinkle mix with larger shapes - big pools of melty sugar once baked. Stick with the waxy, rod shaped sprinkles.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I also found the mix quite dry and I had a hard time rolling into balls as they came apart.  Just scooped and flattened.  Also shortened the cooking time.  Omitted the almond extract due to allergies but upped the vanilla.  Everyone loved them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Well, too salty and too cake like. Let me stress, I expected that with baking powder in the dough, they would be more cakey than crisp.  Twelve minutes baking time was pretty spot on but again...these were cakey (not chewy and yes I let the dough rest for half an hour, stirring twice)...and my God salty. An 18 year old cookie expert in my home tasted one and said to my husband "why are these cookies so salty?".

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Glad to see it wasn't just me thinking that they are too salty. If I make again I will definately cut the salt in half!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were fun to make and a festive treat for a solstice party, but ultimately were too sweet for my taste. Did have nice chew, though. Be careful not to over bake!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I am the type of person that the recipe writer talks about, who always cuts sugar in half in recipes. I have preschooler who loves rainbow everything and sprinkles but she doesn’t need a giant amount of sugar. I did that here and don’t regret it at all! Sweetness level just right considering the outside is coated with more sugar. I used extra crunchy sprinkles and it tasted like the entire outside was a candy shell. Heartily recommend cutting sugar in half to all who are debating it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: delicious cookies!! They make the most delectable ice cream sandwiches ever!! Used choc chip cookie dough ice cream and I was in Heaven!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were tasty and easy to make. I took the advice from other commenters and baked them for 12 minutes and it was good but I'll probably try 11 minutes next time because they are still a bit on the crunchier side.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were highly disappointing. Baked them for a party and felt I had to bake something else last minute to bring instead. While they look colorful and cute, they are hard as rocks, even when shortening the cooking time. Followed the recipe/video to a T but just lacking. Huge bummer.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies were delicious on day 1, but did not keep well unfortunately. By day 3 they were hard and inedible, I tossed the rest. I would only make them if you can make them fresh and know the whole batch will be eaten!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
As Sohla says in the video, these are best eaten the same day. If you want to keep them past that, she counsels, store them in the freezer and temper them in the oven just before eating.
BTW, her videos are a treat. I watch them even when I'm not that interested in the recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: With the first batch I followed the recipe and was having a really tough time mixing in the sprinkles and m&ms. The dough got tough and the cookies turn out pretty hard…they were also a bit salty. The second batch I halved the salt and did 1 and 1/3 cup flour instead of 1 and 2/3 cup. The dough was more buttery and everything mixed in and stuck on much better. The cookies were chewy and delicious. Second times a charm!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Now, that's a cookie! Sweeter than sweet. I'm sure kids would love these; I know I do, as I really like sweet cookies. It takes a lot of sprinkles to coat the outsides and I ran out of tiny M&Ms, but these are really great. Make them the way Sohla says; don't try to make them "healthy".

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: major fail for me. maybe I misunderstood the directions, but I rolled the discs on all sides in the m&m sprinkle mixture, and all of the m&ms that were on the bottom of the disc touching the pan burned to a crisp. I even baked the cookies for less time than stated and the cookies were incinerated on the bottom and had to toss them all. a shame because they look so pretty in the picture :(

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I thought I followed the directions to a tee and the cookies came out as hard a rock- but obviously I must have overcooked them ( and if so only by 3 to 4 minutes). Way too much sugar for me. I’m sure sea gulls at beach will enjoy them. Will stick to my tried and true recipes handed down in my family.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Well at the last minute I relented on my vow to use 1/2 sugar and i regret. Kids hopefully like this (little,  sweet-toothed kids) but I found them too sweet as is. Used dark brown and maybe would’ve been less cloyingly so with light (also prettier). 
I had to use more than double the sprinkles and m&ms to get coated. Maybe that didn’t help.. 
so much sugar couldn’t even taste the imitation vanilla!
Baked 10, rested 2 on sheet - perfect consistency!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Did anyone try these without the almond extract or with a different extract. Im sure they are still delicious but was curious about other bakers experience. I have a couple of friends with nut allergies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I have a severe nut allergy to all nuts and I used Maraschino cherry liquid, which was recommended as a substitution. I'm also allergic to stone fruits (cherries), but when cooked alters the pectin or something and I can handle it fine.

These cookies are extraordinarily as advertised... a true candied sugary confection and just as stated pure "joy" to make and eat!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This is a good recipe, in concept, but I think it needs some adjusting. I had to add a decent amount more flour than the 200g to get the dough to a state it could be portioned and rolled. Also, the cookies were well done (but not burned) after the 14 minute minimum for baking.
Gonna try again soon, addressing those issues

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I had made the required amount of everything, and the cookies were put on two trays. The first tray came out amazingly! But as I was waiting for the first batch to come out, I let the second batch just sit there, for she said “Do NOT put cookies in for cooling!” I needed to put the second batch in the fridge, for the butter had melted too much in the second batch, and got burned. I think the recipe needs to be tweeked

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these tonight with 4 T of "Just Egg" instead of an egg. Next time would use just 3 T; I think the texture was a little too runny. I accidentally added an extra 1/4 t of salt so they were definitely salty, but in the finished version, kind of addictive. I ended up cooking mine for just 7 minutes-- any longer and they were too dark.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these the night before my kiddo's birthday and sent him to school with the batch. These are officially my go-to birthday cookies! Absolutely delicious with tiny celebrations in each bite! I found sprinkles with hearts and stars and it made them even more fun.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I thought the batter was really dry.  It was falling apart when I tried to roll them.  Also, I baked the first batch according to the recipe and they were almost burnt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I unfortunately did not like these cookies. They weren't overtly sweet (which I was surprised) but I baked them at the minimum time range and they cooled down hard as crackers, lol. My oven was at temperature, maybe running a little lower. They do sound interesting, though! They looked great in the video. I like that this recipe is not trying to be over-the-top, and I enjoy the idea of a mix-and-bake cookie.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I have three daughters and they all love this recipe
its so easy that they can do it themselves!
I love how its not a TOO sweet depending on your mood 
over all its a 10 out of 10

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are delicious. I did… lower the sugar amounts (don’t hate me). Lowered to 130g and 90g. Freeze beautifully, defrost in 20 minutes. Make it!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a wonderful recipe to make with a group of kids. I scooped the dough, they rolled the balls and rolled in sprinkles. So much fun!  Cookies are sweet and perfect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Definitely put the batter in the refrigerator for a bit to firm it up before molding and rolling in the sprinkles. This keeps the cookies from spreading out too much when cooking and they form neat circles.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made a mash up of these with Yossy Arefi’s malted sugar cookies and they were amazing.  Basically used the malted sugar cookies as the base (using half brown and Half white sugar) and the extracts and mix ins from this recipe. Yum! Baked 10-12 min at 350.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Love these fun cookies. Yes, they are very sweet, but I would think that would be obvious from the sprinkles & M&Ms. If you want some sort of fancy, nuanced, sweet/savory cookie, find another recipe.
     These are EASY, fast, and yummy! Followed the recipe exactly, including the 30 min suggested rest time (before adding flour). I agree with everyone else about the cook time - I baked my first batch for 14 mins (way too long). Second tray baked for about 11 mins & were perfect!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I watched the video, read the comments & followed the recipe & these cookies turned out perfectly! I made 8 dozen for a cookie exchange. They’re fun, colorful, tasty & easy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Fun, festive, and super easy to make! I ran out of time to prepare something fussier for our neighborhood cookie exchange so I made these and they were a hit. Love the rolling step since it makes much prettier cookies!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were the worst cookies I ever made.  They are teeth curdling sweet with a bitter chemical aftertaste, presumably from the imitation vanilla extract.  No matter how cute they are, I found them inedible.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These cookies make the best ice cream sandwiches!! I use cookie dough ice cream and people go nuts! 
They also freeze exceptionally well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I am not clear on why there are 2 bacthes of M&M's.  
1/2 cup of M&M's left intact for the batter, then added 1/4 cup M&M's crushed with sprinkles to edcorate cookies before baking,  Di I get that right??

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made exactly as directed without the 30 min wait time. They were absolutely perfect. If they’re misshapen it’s super easy to shape them when they come out. A keeper!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
For being so sweet, these are actually pretty good! My 4-yo daughter picked this recipe out and my wife and I were pleasantly surprised. 

Only change I’d suggest is baking at 350 for 14 mins. The video claims that if you like them soft bake them as the recipe calls for, but our oven made them too crispy to eat at 16mins@375. Other than that, great and would do again

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I rested the batter for 30 minutes as recommended and it was so loose and sticky that it was difficult to scoop and roll in the toppings.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious! I baked them for 12 on the second batch, 14 made them a little too hard for me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: O.M.G. - the moment I saw the picture I knew I had to have these. Just baked two batches because we were so excited to get a taste. Will these keep until Christmas Day if we pop them in the freezer?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: She states in the video that they should be frozen if you aren’t eating them within a few days.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Someone asked about freezing these. I freeze everything and these seem to take it quite well.
They are not my favorite but I’m expecting the kids will love them.
They WERE super easy and quick to make and add really fun color. For Christmas, next time I’d just use red and green sprinkles.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 375 in oven browned the cookies far too much by minute 11. Lowered the temp to 350 and they still browned quite a bit by minute 14.  Not sure what temp they should cook at and for how long.. These are tasty, but just never felt I got the consistency of the final product right for the recipe. Not sure I would make these again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I baked mine for 15 minutes and when they cooled for a good 20 minutes they got really hard. Would it be better to reduce cooking time or adjust the butter to flour ratio? I felt my dough was not ‘soft’ so perhaps that was my issue. Any suggestions welcome because the flavors are great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hi! Did you let the dough rest for 30 mins as suggested? I did and I thought the texture was excellent. I wonder if that might make a difference for you.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wondering if this could be made with pure butter (cream and salt) Unsalted butter has undefined “natural flavors” added that I can’t eat. Thanks

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I almost always bake with salted butter because it's what I keep on hand and I just like it better. I made these with salted butter and just decreased the salt to a pinch. They were great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were quick and easy in addition to being delicious. Great party cookie; they were a big hit. I made them as instructed but didn't have imitation vanilla so just used more pure vanilla. I don't love almond extract so debated omitting but was glad I didn't. It adds a little something extra but they don't taste "almond-y". No issues with the rainbow sprinkles bleeding. Will make again this season for sure!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Followed directions with exception of using all natural vanilla, as that is what I had.  Fourteen minutes and they were ready at 375.  I just returned from our local rec center potluck and they were a big hit.  I saw one man with three in his hand!
Will make again to give out to neighbors. Thanks for a fun and tasty cookie.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are everything Sohla promised. Sugarbombs, nostalgic and fun to make. I think the less time in the oven, the better the chew of the cookie is (as others stated they can become hard fast!) 9 minutes in, with 2 mins on the sheet was good. If you are highly sensitive to the sugar content, I would do less mix ins and focus on coating the entire ball with candy. Makes for great texture, look without sacrificing the dough’s intention.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed the recipe to a T and they were over baked at 14 mins. Probably should’ve done 12 mins.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies were delicious, but ended up VERY flat. I recommend upping the flour until it yields a texture that is more dough-like. The flavor is perfect however. If you don’t have imitation vanilla, just make do with the recommended around of real vanilla. Any mix-ins work well!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Christina Tosi of Milk Bar fame always uses clear vanilla in her bakes. It gives the the flavor of a real bakery made product.  I keep my Madagascar vanilla for recipes where vanilla should really shine. These are super fun and festive!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Agree with others—14-16 minutes is too long to achieve the pale color in the photo. I used the 35-gram weight to portion and 12 minutes resulted in browned crispy cookies (overdone) with 10 minutes being about right.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
OMG I love these exactly as they are! I over baked them a bit the first time. We suffered the pain of eating them immediately and I’m baking another batch today! 
Thank you for my new favorite I-Want-a-Cookie-Right-NOW recipe. Forget Christmas - I’ll be baking these all year!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this as written — grateful for a use for the imitation vanilla my husband bought by accident some time ago. Delicious and fun! Maybe I’m too generous, but I ended up using twice as many mini M&Ms as called for during the rolling phase. Thirteen minutes of bake time worked for my cookie scoop.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are delicious and fun to make. I didn't have an imitation vanilla,  so I just left it out. Crispy and chewy- the perfect cookie!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow!  This cookie base is outstanding!  I had so much fun making it and my teenage and her friends went wild!  What I like best about this cookie recipe is that you can incorporate many different combinations, the “flair “ (mm/sprinkles) could be anything.  Next time I’m going to let my imagination run wild!   Thank you NYT- making this recipe truly brought joy into my world and put smiles on my other people’s faces!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: As many have said, Sohla explains all in the NYT Cooking YouTube video. I was initially put off by the imitation vanilla and multiple extracts but there are reasons for each based on the flavours she was looking to recreate. Just give it a try. I made these with Smarties (for non-Canadians, I don't mean the tiny sour sugar disks no one likes, but a chocolate candy very similar to M&Ms). They are so fun. They spread a lot so definitely be mindful of spacing on the pans.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed the instructions exactly (including weighing each cookie before rolling in sprinkles!) and I have a thermometer for my oven, and I definitely overdid the first batch at 14 min, I found it to be closer to 12 min.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Do not over bake—they won’t look done but they are. These babies spread out, so give ‘em some room on the baking sheet.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The commenter who suggested freezing the rolled balls and then baking was spot,on. Far less fussy. I used ancho chili powder rather than cayenne and they were great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are easy to make, fun festive cookies that are delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I was disappointed with these. Either the bake time is too long or the temp is too high, or both! 
They weren’t really worth it to me to try again though because I’m not a huge fan of cookies with candy in them anyway. 
But you’ve been warned- watch them closely in the oven and take them out before you think they’re done. They brown further while cooling which means they won’t be as soft. This isn’t the kind of cookie you want to be hard and crunchy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Only altitude adjustments (we live at 7500’) was to skimp on baking powder and baking soda.  Used stand mixer with paddle attachment on low (thank you, Meg Greer).  12 min at 375 in convection oven, rotating at 6 min.  Perfect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The 69 and 70 year olds who have sampled these for me absolutely love them. Sized down to 25 grams for a better holiday box size. When rolling and dunking in sprinkles, the cookie feels exactly like a scallop. And they’re so FUN and FESTIVE!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Fun for A but taste like candy

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My kids went absolutely wild for these and kept saying things like “I’m in candy heaven!” They can’t wait to make them again. When we do, I’ll probably add another tablespoon or two of flour because they were thinner than I was expecting. Definitely keep an eye on these as they can get overbaked quickly. .

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were pretty and tasted good, but were way too hard. I did let them sit for a bit to let the sugar dissolve, but there was still zero chewiness. Don’t think I’ll make them again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are delicious cookies - a sweet treat. And gorgeous. If dough gets too warm they’ll spread so be aware. They are also very large - visually a delight but practically may want to make them smaller and adjust cooking time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies were a huge hit, even when made vegan and gluten free with King Arthur GF flour, Just Egg and Imperial veg. oil baking sticks. I did cut 1/4 cup of granulated sugar and skipped the imitation vanilla (didn't have any). Even the non-vegan and gluten loving friends and family gobbled these up.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hi! what did you substitute for the m&m’s? Can’t find vegan minis.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Watch the video, it's cute and funny! :D I love that these are named Lisa Frank cookies. You can totally get away with regular M&Ms, maybe even just roll them in the rainbow sprinkles but do rainbow sprinkles and regular M&Ms inside, that's what I did! Turned out great and were a family hit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I baked these with red and green M&Ms and similar sprinkles for a Christmas theme. I baked my first dozen for 15 minutes and found them overdone. I followed other commenter recommendations for the 2nd dozen and baked them for 12 minutes. Much better!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these for my Christmas cookie boxes for my friends with kids who complain my cookies are too adult for them.     I added about 3/4 of Cristina Tosi's corn flake crunch and it gave a nice salty crunch that breaks up the sweetness a bit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: After carefully weighing each dough disk to 35grams, the recipe yielded 20 cookies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: People have mentioned “the video” - there’s no link in the recipe, I guess that was when it was in a Times article?  I followed the directions as best as I could, although my cookies ended up ½ to ¾ “ thick.  Using two tablespoons per cookie, I got SIXTEEN, and they were huge, like 6” in diameter.  Next time, one tablespoon per cookie.  I also didn’t get the inference that sprinkles and m&m’s went on the top, it says to roll the cookies after you’ve made them discs.  Perfect kid cookie though!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Substituted the imitation vanilla with a little more normal vanilla and almond extract, turned out lovely. We also used gluten free cup for cup flour, and had lovely results.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed the recipe but took the advice of others and took them out around between 10-12 and they were perfectly chewy. Any longer and they would I’ll have been over done.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The video is very useful. My cookies only needed 12 minutes to bake & I worried that I had over baked them as they were more crunchy than chewy the first day. However, these cookies get better a few days later & are perfectly crispy on the edges & chewy in the center. I did not have problems with the sprinkles bleeding on the cookie.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The FUN factor in making these cookies is over the top!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made these with 2t real vanilla and no imitation extract.  Accidentally switched the sugar quantities so mine were brown sugar heavy.  Best baking outcome across 3 batches was 12 mins in oven, 2 mins on sheet, then to a rack.  I have a gas oven, for what it's worth.

They are really sweet and pretty one note but my Secret Santa (who is a self described maximalist) loved them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m planning to make these with gluten free flour. Any tips?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just baked these. They’re easy, fast, and fun. Make sure to give them the space on the cookie sheet the recipe suggests; they really do spread as they bake! Trust fingertip touch and appearance over time; my Thermador oven took 18 minutes for two sheets, and I rotated them on the shelf about 12 minutes in. And don’t skimp on the sprinkles to roll in. You will use and want the whole half cup for rolling with the mini M&M’s!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
For
Anyone maybe wanting to know, I veganized these pretty successfully by just replacing the egg with a flax egg, the butter with vegan butter (as the recipe does allow), and adding to extra tablespoons of flour to compensate for the flax egg.

Also, why are we hating so much on imitation vanilla?  It’s fantastic.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I couldn't buy imitation vanilla in my large local grocery store.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thank you thank you these cookies really deliver a delicious memory. Spot on with all the extracts combined to perfection. Kids love love love em. Crispy edges and softer centers with a sweet vanilla warmth in every bite. Nostalgia in a cookie form.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I was able to make these vegan by subbing the egg for Bob’s Red Mill Egg Replacer. They came out delicious! 

I did not chill the butter/sugar mixture for 30 minutes but the cookies still came out chewy. I think I’ll try chilling the mixture next time to see how they compare.

I agree with other commenters: bake for 11-12 minutes. That’s the sweet spot for getting perfectly baked cookies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In the video, Sohla says to pop it in the fridge for an hour or two before forming the balls, but the recipe omits this step. Any thoughts/helpful experience on whether or not to chill this cookie?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Don't put it in the fridge. She says that will dry it out.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thank you McKlem. I did end up chilling them the first time because my dough was so crumbly before doing so, and because she mentioned it in the video. I didn't do it the second time around.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were really fun to make, although they came out a little crisp. I baked them for 10 minutes, but I will probably cut that back next time. (And there will definitely be a next time!)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Great taste and look! If you’re not planning to do exactly 35 grams per cookie dough ball and closer to two tablespoons, you’ll need to double cooking time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made using 2-1/2 t real vanilla.   Needed more sprinkles & M&Ms for rolling the cookies.   Per other comments, 12-14 minutes cooking time is more than adequate.  I’d say remove the cookies before they start looking cooked because they get hard when cooled.   Wonder if this could use a bit more butter?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love the fun look and delicious taste of these but also agree that the bake time should be 11-12 min. My batch baked to the full time was rock hard.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were such a quick and easy recipe, I swapped out for king Arthur’s measure for measure gluten free blend and these were the first cookie recipe I have found that was still able to produce a flat, chewy/crisp cookie while using a gf blend! Perhaps 14 mins a bit long for a bottom line, I found that mine were ever so slightly over…. Great recipe that will probably become my standard now for a “chocolate chip/mixin” recipe

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Be careful with the sprinkles that you use. Mine melted, ran off the cookie and blackened on contact with the cookie sheet. I learned that there are different types of sprinkles. Not all are meant for baking.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Because the butter is melted the dough is very warm when finished. It does need chilling. I needed a little more flour to make it firm enough to roll.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The recipe says to form the dough  into balls and flatten into disc's before coating them in the M&M and sprinkle mixture but it doesn't seem that Sohla does that in the video. I flattened mine and baked for 14 minutes. They were too crispy and not chewy. Will attempt again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What a fun recipe and delicious! I couldn’t find mini M Ms so I used regular sized in red and green and got red and green jimmies. Very festive! As for the imitation vanilla naysayers, I paid $0.99 for a bottle in Target. Read about imitation vanilla sometime. It’s not as horrible as you may think. Highly recommend this recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Our family loves these cookies. Our only changes are to skip the almond and imitation vanilla extracts, upping the regular vanilla to compensate, and baking on the shorter side like other commenters suggest. With an adult eye on the oven use, my kids can otherwise make these cookies themselves. That alone is worth an extra star. My kids love the video too and crack up at "he doesn't understand joy."

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 35 g is not the same as 2 Tbsp!! gotta bake them for like 5 min less than it says

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is also just a killer dough for if you want to make fast and easy chocolate chip cookies!! It's so versatile and delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I baked them first with no mods except for time in oven (13 mins). I made a 2nd batch, this time I excluded the almond extract and replaced about half (60 grams) of the mini M&M's with Guittard Extra Semi Sweet chips (55%). I kept the sprinkles and imitation vanilla but I did not roll the cookies in extra M&M's or sprinkles. I reduced the temp and baked them at 350 for 15 mins. They turned out great! Still sweet but little more balanced. Not hard/crunchy but just the right crisp/chewy texture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have to preface this by saying cookies are not my forte.  Because of this, I watched the video.  I weighed my ingredients, and weighed the cookie balls.  After baking for 9 minutes they were still raw.  At 10 minutes they were brown, and I took them out. After cooling, they were as hard as a rock. I wouldn’t bother to make them again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: SO good. I made as written except didn’t have imitation vanilla so I upped the regular vanilla. They were easy, came out beautiful, and once cooled are pleasantly crispy (kind of like a Tate’s but a little bit chewier). Absolutely will be repeating this recipe and trying different mix-ins!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: as usual, sohla does a brilliant job of walking us through her recipe.  i added less minis to the batter because i didn't have enough but didn't change one other thing about them.  it took a total of 12 minutes for me. they are gloriously playful and colorful and just the right foil for all the more sophisticated cookies out there. thank you, sohla.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are RIDICULOUSLY GOOD. They are (mostly) going to my nearest neighbors in the goodie bag I do most Christmases.  IF we can stop ourselves from eating them. Thanks for the recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The first time I made these my dough was very crumbly, even though I followed the recipe to the letter. It didn't look like the dough in the video, and I'm not sure why. These still were very tasty. The second time I added a second egg so the dough would be easier to work with and it worked perfectly and were delicious. Only thing I can think is perhaps the eggs I used were smaller than the ones Sohla used? Anyway,  I love these and will definitely make them again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Great recipe! Instead of rolling the entire cookie in toppings, I just dipped the tops in the sprinkles and pressed 5 mini m&ms on top. Baked at 10 minutes for crisp and chewy perfection!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious taste but I'm so grateful for the readers who mentioned that the baking time was closer to 12 minutes.  The cookies were a tad too dark after only 12 minutes.  Beware!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are a great, nostalgia-inducing cookie - which Sohla explains she was trying to accomplish. I think the almond extract is what brings that "artificial" box cake/cookies vibe - so don't skip that unless you need to. I think these may be my new go-to quick cookie as I usually have the ingredients and the bright colors make everyone excited to eat them. I used Great Value sprinkles and mini M&M's and had no color bleeding issues.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Agree with the need to reduce cook time. We added nuts after multiple suggestions by enthralled participants, and they were a great addition. Will be a fixture at gay pride next year.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Had to reduce the time on these in my oven to like 9-10 minutes even - but they were good and super fun- hoping I won't forget this next time!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m mad about how good these are. I did half mini m&ms and half chopped up Heath bar and it did not disappoint.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I hate that I made these cookies - they are so dang delicious & easy to make. I made them for the holidays and they were a total hit. No modifications needed, but also recommend watching the video.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
After making these a second time-
I will take out at 9 min in a 350 convection oven. They will not look done on top but they are. Bottoms were brown.
Absolutely chill the dough about 30 min.I have never met a cookie dough that can’t be chilled or that chilling dried out. Wrap it in plastic wrap if you’re worried.
I did take out 1/4 c of sugar from the recipe with no affect on taste and far less spread that the first batch.
I weighed the flour the 2nd time and it was perfect. No extra needed.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Good and cute but very sweet! I need to bake a couple minutes less!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love these cookies, but also had trouble with the baking time as others have mentioned too. I baked for 13 minutes and they turned out very crispy (think Tate’s). I will bake another batch and start checking at 11 minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
maybe I made then smaller than suggested but needed much less cooking time, 9 minutes tops.  otherwise great.








=

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love Sohla but the predominant flavor of these cookies is sweet. I couldn’t really detect much chocolate flavor from the mini M&Ms. If I did make these again I would use peanut butter M&Ms for flavor. 14 minutes at 375F was perfect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
3/4 tsp orange extract
Use tbsp scoop
Bake for 11 min

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yum!  Made 2x so far. Second time added mini-marshmallows and black pepper potato chips with the mini m&ms and rainbow sprinkles. Very fun combo!  Formed 36g flattened balls baked at 375 for 15 minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Will it cookie cake? Yes! Made this dough exactly as instructed and pressed it into a standard 10 inch cake pan. I used a round of parchment and lots of butter so it wouldn't stick. I pressed extra sprinkles and m&ms into the top since I couldn't really roll it. Decorated with white icing. It's giving Great American Cookie Co. Took a while to bake (30 min or so?) and got a little over cooked at the edge while waiting for the center to finish up. Next time, I'll divide the dough into 2 pans.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is the sixth cookie from cookie week that I’ve made.  I’m so glad at least one of those recipes is super quick and easy!  I read all the comments about the reduced bake time. You won’t believe it—mine only baked for TEN minutes! Go figure.  Just keep an eye on your batch and I’m sure they’ll be fine.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This cookie is divine. The only change I made was using pecans instead of sprinkles because I don’t like sprinkles. My cookies were perfect at 12 minutes. I just ate the first warm one that came out of the oven and I had to go to another room to keep from eating two or three more.  The saving grace of a cookie this sweet is that I can’t eat more than one or two at a time although they are addictive. The video was very helpful.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It should say that it should look like appel sauce not wet sand

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Loved these! Used 1 tsp cake batter flavoring instead of the imitation vanilla and almond to give them a more birthday cake vibe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’ve already made a different variation of this recipe using Sohla’s cookbook (the corn chip/marshmallow/chocolate ones, also great!) but these really shined. Yeah, they’re super sweet, that’s the point! These taste like something I’d be served at a birthday party as a kid and I loved them. I only got 12 cookies out of the batter but they were pretty large. The only change I made was using my homemade vanilla extract for both extracts since I didn’t wanna buy imitation just for this one recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these cookies exactly as the instructions and video describe and they were chewy and crunchy and beautiful and people couldn't believe I made them! One batch was a few minutes more done than the other and the slightly underdone cookies were my favorite. I served them the same day although the next day they were still chewy too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I actually had a similar experience to many other reviewers. I baked the cookies for 16 minutes and they came out way too hard to bite into. I lowered the cooking time to 14 and they were a bit softer, but still had quite the chew to them. I don't know if I would make these again, but if I did I would try to lower the baking time to see if that would help.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I loved these! Crispy and colorful and baked up fast. Subbed out imitation vanilla bec I just couldn’t find it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used Almond flour in these to make them gluten free. I tried subbing 1 to 1, but the dough seemed a little soft, so i added more until it became more of a stiff cookie dough texture. I didn’t measure, but probably added another quarter cup or a bit more than that. Tasted great! Cookies probably a bit thinner than they otherwise would have been, but not a bad substitution.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: i love the way you make this cookie with m&m's and sprinkles i just love it !!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Definitely need to chill for 30 minutes before forming the cookies or they will spread too much. Also, I baked for ten minutes and they were done. Any longer and they would be burnt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I didn’t see & watch the video until I was too far in.  Wish I had seen it.  I did refrigerate the dough for a couple of hours and that wasn’t good.  Had to go down to 13 minutes on cooking time, and they were still too hard.  Still delicious though!!  This saved the day for my wife for her office cookie exchange.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: super sweet- not surprised as the sugar content is crazy high- but a nice sense memory taste and for a holiday treat, why the heck not....had one with my morning coffee and do not feel worse for it

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I thought it was really hard to add on the extra toppings, but it was overall a very simple recipe! They also taste delicous! I reccomend.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: its hard 2 get the extra toppings on, but they taste delicious so its WORTH IT! I reccomend watching the video first. They are super easy overall, and I'd make them again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 11 minutes in a convection oven at 375 is perfect. Halve the amount of sprinkles to roll in. I had lots left over.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Too much salt, didn’t enjoy this recipe

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wonderfully sweet and delicious with such a nostalgic flavor and satisfyingly chewy texture! My first round of cookies burned very quickly so if your oven runs a little hot, I'd recommend lowering both the temperature and the baking time (mine only took 11-12 minutes at 360 but every oven is different).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 375 for 13 minutes yielded a brittle over-cooked cookie in the first batch. Dropped to 350 for first 5 minutes then 375 (to get crisp edges) for 4 final minutes. Perfect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is the absolute best recipe for kids, and brings joy to them cooking. We've used any and every kind of mix-in and it always turns out perfectly. We don't use imitation vanilla and just go heavy on the real stuff. My daughter loves to pick out what goes in and at this point she probably could make these cookies herself. We love adding dried coconut! We use a stand mixer but that works just fine.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were delicious

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were delicious. I did not use the clear vanilla, just added more pure extract. Do not over-bake unless you like really crunchy cookies. Everyone loved them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I loved these cookies. Usually I’m a “not too sweet” person but these were so fun to make, look at and eat. And I love having this as a base recipe for spontaneous bakes bc you don’t need to wait for butter and eggs to reach room temp. I’d like to try it with salty mix-ins like pretzels and potato chips.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made a batch of these yesterday. Agree with 11 minute cooking time. Because I've had mixed results making cookies with melted butter, I used half room temp and half melted (because I already had some at room temp). I decreased the granulated sugar by 1/4 cup, decreased Diamond salt to 1 scant tsp (only because I watch my sodium intake).
Additions: mini M&Ms, peanuts, chopped rolos, a handful of potato chips. I did not roll them in toppings. Appearance is fine and they are delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really loved this recipe. It wasn't a firm enough dough to roll into the sprinkles, but an ice-cream scoop then dip into the sprinkles worked fine. I feel like a touch more vanilla would really bring that flavour out a little more, but otherwise perfection. Such a fun biscuit to have for parties.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So...I had so many issues with this recipe. My batter looked nothing like the video, my cookies were too small and I used the wrong size scoop, and my toppings would not go on as easily as demonstrated. But, when I did get it right, the cookies had this lovely crisp edge, and were amazingly chewy in the middle. It took me a couple takes, but once you master the recipe, it is magical.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I actually couldn't *find* imitation vanilla at my local grocery, so used extra vanilla (2 t total), extra almond extract (1/2 t total), and rum extract (1/4 t).  Cookies were delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m not sure if it was my fault, but I followed the recipe to a tee I think. But the dough came out very crumbly… I just added some milk to it and it cooked well though.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used 2 tsp vanilla extract and omitted the other 2.  Excellent

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Made this recipe exactly as written, and I weighed all the ingredients, but was disappointed in the results. I opted for dark brown sugar and unsalted butter (Tillamook brand.) My cookies spread thinner than pictured, turned out much darker, and were already overbaked at the suggested 14 minute mark. The sprinkles and M&Ms also sank into the dough during baking, dulling the colors.

I wouldn't make this again, but if I did, I'd use light brown sugar and check them at 10 minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Matthew S I used 1/2 light and 1/2 dark and cookies have no height to them. All my leavenings are working, so not sure why they’re so flat. They don’t look like the picture!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are so joyful! They are tasty and whimsical - and can't we all use more whimsy? I subbed in Christmas M&Ms and sprinkles to add to my holiday cookie collection and replaced almond extract with Fiori di Sicilia because it's delicious and what I had on hand.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This cookie has entered into my heavy rotation.  It’s 1) super easy without a mixer if you don’t have one and 2) super nostalgic with the sprinkles and clear vanilla extract evoking your childhood birthday cakes.    I’ve found I’ve needed slightly more sprinkles/m and m’s for rolling, maybe about 50g extra on both ingredients.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Be very, very careful when baking. I baked as directed on got browned, hard cookies. With the sprinkles especially they are very hard when overdone. They spread out quite a bit on the pan.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was contemplating using peanut butter m&m minis has anyone ever tried those or similar?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
