commentBody: These are great to make with kids. They love all the colors and you don’t have to be too precise when measuring all the fun add-ins. The kids loved eating them too ;-) Great recipe to teach/practice baking with the littles.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m giving these 4 stars instead of 5 because I have this underlying issue with sprinkles; like jello and Cool-whip, they aren’t really food. Neither is imitation vanilla, so I used double the real thing. But the cookies are good and the flavor is great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used King Arthur 1:1 GF flour.  Simple Truth sprinkles with natural dye (not quite as vibrant colors).  These came out very crisp but were well received.  Next year will make with red, green and white sprinkles!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Be careful not to use too much almond extract. I would even cut it in half. Overall, great texture and fun to make!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I make these for my Christmas cookie boxes every year and I add cashews along with gems(indian m&ms) and they are truly loved for their cute appearance and delicious taste.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Perhaps I have the palette of a 12 year old but these are the best cookies ever. Froze the baked batch and took them out one at a time to eat- still perfect once thawed, especially if thrown in the oven for a couple of minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The dough was really dry for me, so I added a bit more melted butter. Still quite crumbly once I started rolling them in my hands, but they tasted great and were a big hit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The first batch we baked was too crispy. In order to make these taste like the Insomnia Cookies confetti cookie, need to bake at 350 for only about 12 minutes. we left out the chocolate, again to try and re-create the Insomnia Cookies confetti cookie.  Turned out great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: omitted m&ms (didnt have any), upped the amount of regular vanilla by 1/2 teaspoon (2teaspoons total) using vanilla bean paste instead, and baked for 12 minutes as suggested in comments. they came out perfect and delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: They turn crispy when cool, not soft and chewy like I’d expected. Pretty basic. Not my favorite.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these with my 7 yr old. Honestly they aren’t really my thing but I respect them for what they are. They tastes like a sugar fueled, 90’s Lisa Frank fever dream. My son was delighted. They did spread a lot in the oven and went from being almost done to almost too done very quickly. There was a nice chew when they were still warm but as they cooled they were crisp all the way through. If it were up to me I probably wouldn’t make them again, but I’m sure that my son will insist.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Way too hot and for too long. 190° for 14 mins resulted in some burnt/all hard cookies. 190° for 12mins was better, and next time I'll try 180° for 12 mins. Tasted and looked great, otherwise.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this for lunch today and enjoyed it! It’s easy and offers textural interest thanks to the bread crumbs, crunchy veggies, etc. Grating a tomato by hand is so satisfying. Looking forward to making it again tomorrow (I saved half the dressing and crumbs to make another salad).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very easy to prepare and eat. I used turkey pepperoni slices and forgot to add 3 tablespoons of EVOO and used small mozzarella balls and it still came out great. I will used this as my go-to don't feel like cooking meals.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This salad is very good. So much flavor. I made extra of the pepperoni breadcrumb mixture and put it on top of the Chile Crisp Fettucine recipe from the NYT. Incredible.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Have made this a couple of times and love it. Since I’m always looking for ways to make things easier and cut down on mess, I’ve found that “sheetpanning”(new word) the bread and pepperoni together with a little oil (on parchment so you don’t have to wash the sheet pan) works very well. I only cook these items, then toss with everything else.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I really enjoy adding to a large tortilla with hot pepper hoagie spread and maybe a little ranch for a filling “pizza” wrap that’s great for a more filling lunch.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was a good Sunday night dinner on a humid day. It was delicious and quick, we enjoyed all of the different textures. I would halve the breadcrumbs next time and maybe add a little vinegar to the dressing, the brine in my pepperoncini wasn’t punchy enough.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really tasty, and very simple.  As my girlfriend said, it tastes and smells like a pizza joint in Jersey.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made for dinner. Kept pepperoni slices whole and lightly fried to remove some of the fat   add something crispy. I did not do the whole thing w/the pepperoni crumbs but instead toasted some baguette and seasoned those for crunchy croutons. For the dressing, grater was in the dishwasher, so stick blended chopped tomato, small clove garlic, a couple pepperoncini, the pepperoncini vinegar and olive oil. (No sugar.) Halved a few cherry tomatoes, few grinds of pepper, some torn basil, and tossed.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Some twists that improved this:
• Rather than grating the tomato(es), slice into bite-sized wedges, drizzle with EVOO, salt, and a bit of red wine vinegar. Marinate 15+ mins as you prep the other things, and they'll leave a wonderful umami juice you can use as the base for the dressing. The pieces of tomato can now be used in the salad as an added element.
• Put a few slices of good bread in the blender to make your own bread crumbs. They toast up more easily than panko and taste better too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is excellent! I used 15 cherry tomatoes and blended w immersion blender for dressing. Also added halved tomatoes to the salad.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this for brunch today as part of my summer project to make all 100 easy summer recipes. This is the first time I ever grated a tomato and the result was satisfying. I'd make this again. My definition of easy doesn't include two sautes and two gratings and 4 different vessels to clean. Lucky my dishwasher is working. Paired it with the NYT cold Buttermilk cucumber soup that sat in the fridge overnight. Lots of complex flavors and textures to start the day.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This really does taste like pizza!! Delish, no notes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Rarely are there decent tomatoes where I live, perhaps august and September, period. What is an acceptable substitution for the beefsteak? Tomato paste?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is such a delicious recipe and very adaptable. I didn’t have a tomato on hand so I added some acid and sweet umami by adding about 2tbs balsamic to the dressing. I also used marinated bocconcini with a little of the oil which was so good. Really you can put pepperoni breadcrumbs on a stack of dryer sheets and they’d be delicious, but they make this salad exceptional. This recipe is literally everything I want in a salad: crunchy, flavorful, salty, and perfectly balanced.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yum! I liked the interest of the grated tomato; I’ll have to remember that for other salads. I added chopped yellow pepper but otherwise made as described. Easy and filling enough for dinner. Will make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Double the amount of pepperoni you crisp, and toss the extras into the salad, while they are still warm.  One of the easiest and best recipes ever from NYT Cooking!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: As I worked on this salad, I listened to NPR, and guess who they were interviewing on Jen White's show?! Sohla!  This salad is fun and a treat as something different.  I used up some cherry tomatoes since I didn't have a beefsteak.  Even missing the purple onion, this is savory and tasty.  I bet I could also substitute cabbage (a favorite of Sohla's) for the romaine!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this for dinner last night and it was great! For the Super Bowl, I’m contributing some of the food for 50+ teenagers. I told them I’d bring “walking tacos” but these flavors will really improve the idea!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Back in the 1980s, in Bon Appetite I found a recipe of pizza salad that built it in a pre baked pizza crust. Lightly dressed lettuce. Pepperoni, mozzarella balls, etc. delicious

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Do not make the mistake I made and assume it will have enough flavor without plenty of salt.  I expected the pepperoni, oregano and garlic powder to do the heavy lifting but....no.  Also thought the panko to pepperoni ratio was off- will probably add salt and subtract panko next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really tastes like a pepperoni pizza! I thought 3/4c was too much breadcrumbs. I would use 1/4c less next time and double the pepperoncini and black olives. Be careful with adding salt to the pepperoni and bread crumb mixture because most pepperoni is already very salty!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I liked this, but the breadcrumbs turned to utter mush as soon as they made contact with the very wet dressing. I imagine something more like a crouton would hold up better.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The pepperoni crisps and bread crumb additions are stellar and give the salad a pizza flavor. However I thought the tomato dressing was lackluster. It needs something more.  I even used a ripe from the garden brandy wine tomato and it was still missing something. Maybe some Italian herbs?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Delicious! Made this as a giant salad for my whole
family. All 4 kids loved it which rarely happens. And most of my kids don’t even like pizza! Like how it’s full of pizza flavors without the heaviness of cheese.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My husband said this is the best salad he’s ever had.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I take leftover pizza, slice it into small pieces with my pizza cutter, add whatever salad dressing and greens and voila - a pizza salad. The olives and pepperocini are a nice addition and I always have those available.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wish I had seen this last summer when I was getting fabulous Beefsteak Tomatoes in my garden.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thought my husband would like it. And he did. But if I'm going to eat anything with this much oil, it might as well be real pizza.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We made this salad twice, first following the directions as written and then using croutons from some ciabatta in place of panko. Seasoned the croutons with the garlic powder, oregano, salt, and some red pepper flakes and added a bit more olive oil to the dressing. The result was a bit more like panzanella. Love the idea of pepperoni crumbs for eggs or pasta, but for this salad croutons in a tomato vinaigrette will be our version of a fun and flavorful salad.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Terrific. Will make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could use couscous instead of breadcrumbs.  Needs more garlic. Fresh oregano use mozzarella don’t substitute.  Use fresh ripe tomato…maybe more than one or throw in cherry tomatoes.  Maybe more less crispy pepperoni?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I didn't have pepperoni but did have plant based hot Italian sausage sliced into thin rounds as a substitute.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Solid recipe with room for lots of modifications! I lightly sauteed the red onion and then the grated tomato for a more mild taste to both. Was wonderful!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was great as written, but with the leftovers, I threw a few spoonfuls of crushed Calabrian chiles in with the dressing and it really amped up the flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is genius. I love the grated tomato in the dressing (rather than in pieces) and of course crispy pepperoni strips and pepperoni flavored breadcrumbs. I think this would be good as a pasta salad too. Plenty spicy without extra red pepper flakes but my pepperoni was on the spicy side.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made 2x. 1st time, followed the recipe. Pepperoni was more burnt than crisped by sautéing in oil, so the 2nd time, I just sliced it up and added it as is. Much better, and cut out 2 Tbsp. of the oil as well. The other change I made the 2nd time was to use GF panko breadcrumbs (guest with celiac). They were excellent!  Did take a few extra minutes to brown. No one could tell they were GF.  Tomato dressing is excellent as is.  Just like with pizza, you can add or subtract based on preferences.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Too much oregano!!! If I make this again I will use at least half the amount of oregano and will not fry the pepperoni—that seemed to really make the flavor of the really good pepperoni I bought disappear. The crunch of the breadcrumbs is plenty of crunch. Did I buy super potent oregano or what? We couldn’t even finish our salads.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I hate sliced black olives. Would kalamatas work?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It was alright but Mark didn’t like it that much

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. We did not sauté the pepperoni nor did we add breadcrumbs, and it was still extremely good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Such a fun and delicious salad! Make this asap

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’ve never left a comment ever- but safe to say this may be the recipe I’ve made the most of the entire repertoire of NYT recipes. My husband devours it and it’s great for two for dinner with no leftovers!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 10/10 salad, perfect for a Super Bowl Sunday starter. Used banana peppers instead of pepperoncinis, turkey pepperoni, and gluten free Panko.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this as written & it’s fantastic. It’s pretty low effort considering there are a few steps. I think it needs parmigiano in the crumbs to make it the real pizza experience. Recommended!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This salad was delicious. I substituted burrata for the fresh mozzarella and it combined with the dressing in a lovely way. Did not use any sugar in the dressing, it was not needed for the flavor. Will definitely make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: There are enough sardines in the sea to feed the world, and we should be eating more of them. Quality counts--most grocery-store sardines come from Morocco and are industrially processed. Those from Spain and Portugal are generally better quality. A separate note: Italians would never put six garlic cloves in puttanesca. We brown a couple cloves in the oil, then remove them so as not to overpower the other flavors.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Max Alexander, Rome Leave a few sardines for the dolphins, please. Oh, and everything else that feeds on them. Aim for multiple days each week focusing vegetarian. This is every creatures world.Thanks.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Max Alexander, Rome we recently went to a Portuguese store at the base of Cape cod, where they specialize in sardines from all over the world… Bought about seven tins of them… Now I know what we can do with some  of them!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Max Alexander, Rome

Perhaps you wouldn’t add six garlic cloves. I definitely would, did, and am happy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love this general technique and use it often, but a warning: careful when you smash those tomatoes! They're little juice-bombs and will happily make a mess of you and your stove and anything else within spitting distance. I pierce each one with a knife before tossing them into the pan to mitigate their explosiveness.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Su No mess with with tomatoes!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love this recipe.  A huge hit.  My only modification was that I let the tomatoes sauté in the oil a bit longer than the recipe calls for before I added the pasta water.  Otherwise, I think the tomatoes sort of boil instead of fry.  NB: As with all dishes that cook the pasta along with the other ingredients, for a few moments it seems like you've added too much water and are inadvertently making a soup. Don't despair. A moment later the pasta absorbs everything and all is well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this with no fresh ‘maters as we didn’t have- but we had lovely sardines. Instead, I used a little Raos marinara, tomato paste properly caramelized, and diced tomatoes, drained, plus all other ingredients. The oil from the sardines was wonderful. Will make this following the recipe next time. The time estimate of 40 minutes was right on the mark. Everyone loved it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @karoma What do you mean by “properly caramelized?”

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Anne Add the tomato paste to the pan early in the process and saute it, smashing it into the bottom of the pan a bit, until it turns a darker red.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow, this was FABULOUS!! When you’re craving that salty-fishy-OOOMPH-on-your-palate-dish, make this. Followed directions pretty exactly, using dry-cured black olives (which added to the ooomph factor), and Portugese sardines thanks to another commenter’s advice. A whole small tin of anchovies. Only needed about 3 cups pasta water. Easy to keep most of the ingredients on hand. Will make again and again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If these ingredients have too much salt/sodium for you, try rinsing the capers and olives and omitting the added salt. Drain well before adding to the pan. This helps remove the extra sodium without having to omit any of the tasty ingredients.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @BJ I would not add anchovies to this (although I haven’t yet cooked it but know and cook anchovies in other recipes).   Sardines capers and olives enough IMHO.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Let's be clear, when they say black olives, it typically means kalamatas (or something similar), not mild black olives that you'd use in Mexican dishes or are ubiquitous on pizza. Substituting with these mild olives is not an option. If you don't have the right ones, wait until you do to make the dish.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@The Cannoli,

Green can work too. I used amazingly good Gordals to a fine result. But agreed: no bland black olives!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love pasta with tuna and love sardines but was a little leery of sardine pasta.  It was surprisingly delicious.  I halved the recipe for the two of us and improvised by using a 14 oz can of cherry tomatoes (didn’t have fresh) plus some tomato paste.  I halved everything except I used the full amount of anchovies. I only needed to add a splash of the pasta cooking water at the end.  It was an easy, delightful meal.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Simply outstanding - tremendous flavor. I followed the recipe and was rewarded with a meal everyone raved about.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Tasty! This dish would generally be a hard no for my husband so I left out the olives, he dealt with the capers and said it is a make again dish! We are lower sodium so I did not salt the pasta water (I know I know) and I did use an entire can of anchovies and did not use any seasoning salt. Satisfying and bright. Definitely needs the spice without the olives though, gotta have some punch.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was pretty good. I added some freshly chopped oregano when adding the tomatoes to the olive oil mixture and cooked the tomatoes without adding water. Doing this the tomatoes will burst out of their skins and caramelize a little so the flavors are better and you don't get splattered trying to smash them up in water. I think the flavor of the dish would be improved by then adding white wine instead of pasta water so I will try that next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Jane S Wait! You'd substitute white wine for 2 cups or more of the pasta water? I like wine in recipes as much as the next person, but I urge others NOT to follow that suggestion!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This is a fantastic, flavorful weeknight meal that will be part of our regular rotation.

Added 1 tsp of oregano along with the chile flakes for extra flavor, and some baby spinach to make this a one pot balanced meal. There are lots of briny flavors, but we found the sauce still benefited from a 1/2 tsp of kosher salt and lots of fresh black pepper.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really nice dish.  I'm not someone who would normally be interested in a recipe with sardine in the name but was looking for any excuse to use up the massive quantity of cherry tomatoes coming out of the garden now.  Made  exactly as written and was pleasantly surprised--just lovely.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really needed a spritz of lemon to finish it off, but with that, made a great pantry meal!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have always loved puttanesca and this recipe hits all those happy places on my tongue!  Instead of parsley, I lined a bowl with some baby arugula and served the hot puttanesca and pasta right on top of it, so it would wilt and lend its peppery flavor to the dish.  The only thing I had to leave out was the black olives (drat, didn't have any).  No matter, it was delicious anyway, and will make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: interesting technique to smash the tomatoes! I went for 1/2 tsp red pepper flakes, probably could have been 1tsp. all other measurements worked for me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very delicious. Have made several times and the best was today.  The differences from prior were 1) I used only the oil in the sardine can, which was the right amount for my taste; 2) halved the cherry tomatoes and sauteed a bit for adding the reserved pasta water and 3) added a few glugs of passata.  Boil the pasta, drain, then use the same pot for the sauce for less mess,  Living in Portugal I am spoiled for great quality tinned sardines for €2-3, so thrilled to have such a good recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Benn adding good quality tuna in olive oil to puttanesca for years but never thought of sardines.  Will try that next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Absolutely loved this recipe! We had everything we needed in the pantry or fresh from the garden. Parents: this recipe is child’s play. My daughter has been helping cook it with me since she was 3, and now at nearly 7 she can do it mostly by herself. Our only regular addition is a bit of thinly sliced fresh basil when we plate it up. Such a pleasure!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I make a similar dish frequently - but use grape tomatoes (sweeter) and sardines in lemon with olive oil. Delish and easy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is SO good. And so flexible. We had jarred tomato sauce from the summer and this seemed a good use for them and it was, together with some higher end tinned sardines and anchovy paste, since that's what I had. Fast, simple and better than you can imagine.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I've got to admit that I've never eaten canned sardines and was skeptical, but my wife lobbied for this recipe, and... it was delicious! Complex, deeply flavorful, aromatic, and healthy. I imagined that I was eating this in a trattoria in Sicily  Followed recipe as described, and it turned out fabulous. And yes, don't worry about undercooking the pasta - it softens up with the tomato sauce.  Ciao bella!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was excellent. Made exactly as written.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this a while back. Stuck to the recipe and it was great. Use good quality tinned fish. Added parmesan, even though I know that's often omitted with fish.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In lieu of black olives, I used kalamata.  They added a wonderful more complex flavor to a terrific dish.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Great alternative for a regular puttanesca. Economical, fast, nutritious and delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used some passata as I hadn’t any fresh tomatoes… we loved it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Honestly, after eating this two nights now, I think I'd prefer it without the sardines. I find it fishy. (I like fish.) I'm not UNHAPPY eating it, but I won't make it again, at least not with sardines included.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Excellent recipe, allows for a lot of substitutions and additions, for example more sardines and added mushrooms.  Didn't have enough cherry tomatoes so used canned crushed tomatoes...tho fresh would definitely taste better.  Also didn't have spaghetti noodles, but fusilli is not a terrible option.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We really liked this, including the kids. Our three year-old had seconds and kept demanding more sardine pieces.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Beware! I love seafood but this recipe was way too fishy for my taste. I would only recommend this for the biggest of tinned fish aficionados

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Every Puttanesca I’ve ever prepared came from the oven. Mix everything but the pasta on a baking sheet. I also add three small onions sliced as thin as I can to melt into the sauce during baking. 190c/375f for 30 minutes. Meanwhile cook pasta al dente, drain and hold back 1/2 - 1 cup pasta water. Return pasta and finished sauce from oven into pot. Sowly add pasta water while stirring to emulsify sauce and thus allowing it to cling to the pasta. Enjoy with a drizzle of EVOO and freshly grated Parmesan. For a vegan version simply switch anchovies and sardines with 2 tablespoons or more of Miso paste and omit Parmesan.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: New hit meal at our house. I prepared as directed. More or everything would not hurt it! Tasty and nourishing. Served with Italian bread, roasted red peppers and mozzarella on the side but it needed nothing additional.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I like puttanesca, and I like sardines, but the combination just didn't work for me.  I'll stick with good canned tuna when I want fish in my puttanesca.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We have been using white anchovies in many dishes that call for anchovies---they are delicious, less intensely salty than other anchovies & add protein to any dish.  They are a bit hard to find, but worth the effort.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this sauce to go over gnocchi that failed, so I added it to angel hair with the sardines and it was tasty. I love sardines and don’t understand people turning their noses up to them. They are delicious and so good for you and sustainable. As others mentioned, the only thing lacking for me was a bit of brightness and lemon zest would do it. I did drain/rinse the capers and olives to reduce salt. I would make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
OMG! This is delicious. Tell them it's tuna. Wow I used a tin of sardines a tin of mackerel and a tin of anchovies. Everyone was a member of the clean plate club! Hahaha! Serve w some warm soft bread,
so good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: OMG this was so good. I used a tin of Anchovies, I mean seriously 4anchovie fillets! a tin of sardines and a tin of mackerel. Delicious! Yep watch those tomatoes they spray everywhere! Lesson learned

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love this recipe -- and I've been making a version of this for ages, since I was a student, as it's also budget-friendly. I like things a tad less salty than many, and this recipe has a lot of salty ingredients.  I love the olives, and use anchovy paste, which allows me to adjust the salt level. I've also added fresh herbs, like oregano or rosemary. A great recipe for those who say they don't like sardines!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious!  My market did not have black olives or decent parsley. I subbed in green olives and watercress.  Otherwise, I followed to the letter.  What a big hit at my house.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I feel like green olives would have been better in this

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Absolutely delish, great marriage of flavors. A squeeze of lemon really brings the flavors together.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
A nice variation:
Instead of bucatini, sauté gnocchi until lightly browned.
Cut the cherry tomatoes in half.
Thinly slice a Meyer lemon and halve or quarter the slices
After step 2, add the tomatoes and Meyer lemon. (Skip pasta water) 
Sauté for about 5 minutes until the tomatoes have softened. No need to mash them.
Add capers, olives, and sardines and stir for a minute or so.
Add the crispy gnocchi and garnish with parsley.
Delicious either way; bucatini w/ water, or crispy gnocchi.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious!  Made with 15 oz canned tomatoes since I didn't have fresh on hand.  Only needed 2 c of pasta water.  As someone else said, trust the process, have faith when adding the water and the pasta.  Would like more tomatoes.  Next time may add fresh and canned.  Could use more capers.  Goes in the rotation as a great pantry meal.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We love love love sardines and are so glad to have this recipe, as we love love love pasta puttanesca, too!  THANK YOU!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed this recipe exactly, but left out the sardines. I know, I know! But I wasn't sure how my son would feel about them. I doubled the anchovies to compensate and it was delicious! Everyone agreed they would like to have it again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was really tasty, but too liquidy—judge the juiciness of your tomatoes before adding the water, because 2 cups was a lot for mine.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was fabulous! I feared it was going to be too fishy, but it wasn't at all. Just plain delicious with an incredibly punchy flavor. This one will be entering the rotation!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was outstanding. The fishiness of sardines is balanced by the other flavors and develops into a restaurant quality pasta.  I had some leftover spinach which worked well when tossed in at the end. I would echo the warning about smashing the tomatoes, however.  I burned myself slightly when one exploded.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was a fabulous meal. I didn't have anchovies, but it still worked, though i did season it accordingly. Didn't have long pasta, but worked well with Orechiette which had a good shape to match the olives. At first i worried it was too watery with 2 cups of pasta water, but it really thickened up. Added baby spinach with the sardines, olives etc like another reader and it made for a truly fantastic one pot meal. And easy clean up too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Really loved this recipe! I added in sautéed onions, 
oregano, basil, crushed tomatoes vs cherry tomatoes and put a little lemon juice on top when served. I didn’t use the leftover water at all. Very simple and delicious! Can’t wait to make it again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is one of my favorite dishes. Sometimes i use tuna in olive oil instead. But sardines are probably more healthy and tasty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Loved this...very easy, very satisfying.  I agree with other comments, because there are so few ingredients....they should all be too quality, especially sardines and tomatoes.  I will definitely make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Potato masher on the tomatoes? It worked for me.

A bit too much pasta water to reduce to a sauce; I'd try less next time.

A traditional puttanesca might need more subtle garlic handling, but this is a robust dish that shouts its flavors. I hardly noticed the garlic.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A little red wine added an extra layer to the flavor of the sauce. And I agree with others that some coarse salt elevated everything.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cooked pasta at same time. Pan roasted the tomatoes with oils, garlic, red pepper and anchovies. , deglazed with red wine, added the undercooked pasta with the pasta water to the pan to finish it off. Was fabulous.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Anchovy paste is helpful if you don't want to have leftover anchovies in your fridge.  Also, a splash of vodka in the sauce towards the end of cooking elevates the flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Loved it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I so wanted to love this.  I adore Chicken Puttanesca (over mashed potatoes) so I thought this would be a slam-dunk, but it was too. much. I used Portuguese sardines and followed the recipe exactly. I did appreciate the construction of the recipe and how the sauce all came together at the end after continuously stirring the pasta but if it's possible, this was too much umami and too much fishiness. A total bomb at my house.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow! I am so surprised by this recipe. I’ve made puttanesca many times before and adding sardines never occurred to me. I wasn’t even sure I would like sardines, but I LOVE this recipe. Very easy to make. I was able pull this together in an hour.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I would just use canned tomatoes next time. Didn’t care for the tomato skins. A good way to get your family to eat some healthy fishes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Pasta purists may faint, but this easily works as a one-pot dish …

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was out of sardines and substituted canned smoked trout. Fantastic.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was very sad to see that the can of anchovies filets I used was actually whole  anchovies, so I missed out on that lovely flavor. That being said, we really liked the finished dish.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Read many comments beforehand. Added entire tin of anchovies. Loved it!!! Will try splash of fresh lemon juice next. Keeper recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Amazing flavour and so easy to prepare. Will be preparing this again and again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This elevates my already beloved Puttanesca to a whole new level. One of the best recipes I’ve found so far in NYTimes cooking, and that means a lot.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very tasty. Converted an “I don’t like sardines” brother into I really like this!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Also: Could people PLEASE stop saying "I made the recipe exactly--except for...." Once you change even one thing, let alone half a dozen, you have NOT made the recipe "exactly."

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Guy William Molnar
why not? sometimes, you find interesting improvements - I for one added a little grated lemon, very good ☺️

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used Kalamata olives, but I like a briny flavor and could see green olives being a nice addition. Served with fresh grated parmesan but it definitely works well without. I don't think I boiled the pasta long enough and didn't end up with enough pasta water but made it work.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sweet Jesus this is delicious!!!! I had a can of sardines in tomato sauce, so I used that and one can of plain sardines. I only had one pint of grape tomatoes, but I had an open jar of tomato sauce in the fridge, so I threw maybe 1/3 of a cup of sauce in with the tomatoes when they broke down. I used Kalamata olives. I also usually heavily salt my pasta water, but I only lightly salted it seeing how much of it I needed to use in the recipe. I ended up using just over 3 cups of pasta water. Fabulous!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It is SOOOOOOOOOO good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yum! My husband doesn’t love fishy pastas but he gobbled this up. Me and my friend were also very happy with the dish. Thanks for this delicious, fast and healthy recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You will fall in love with sardines and anchovies after this dish. So good!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This recipe is in our regular rotation now. We love it. I thought I would share my adjustments because I really botched this the first time I made it, juggling the timing of the pasta with the sautéing of the garlic, burning the garlic, having to start over, ugh. I am generally slow chopping and measuring ingredients for any recipe, so I always figure on a much longer cooking time.

As I am boiling the water for the pasta, I am chopping and measuring the ingredients and condiments for a half-recipe. I generally do more than 1/2 of the goods other than the pasta, because we like all that good stuff. I cook the pasta for half the time the package indicates for al dente — in the range of four to five minutes. When it is done, I collect 1-1/4 cup of the pasta water (a little more than needed, to be safe) before dumping the half-cooked pasta in a colander in the sink. Yes, it sits there cooling and sticking together, but it comes out fine when thrown into the developing sauce. Reducing the initial pasta boiling takes all the time pressure off. I cut the little tomatoes in half for quicker reducing, and I throw the red-pepper flakes in with the water instead of into the oil, which fills the whole place with acrid vapor. It’s perfectly spicy without putting yourself through that. Last night I used chopped arugula in place of parsley and it turned out nicely.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Fabulous. I added some shredded spinach and it gave it a little more color.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was a big pot of deliciousness!  Made according to recipe, except used canned tomatoes and a bit of tomato paste in lieu of cherry tomatoes.  We served the hot pasta over a bed of arugula, which wilted.  This is a great way to add fatty fish with bones in into our diets,

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yum! Everyone in my family loved it. I made it without the tomatoes, and added some white wine in place of them to give it some liquid and sweetness. Used the full tin of anchovies because why waste. Green olives instead of black because it’s what I had. Bow tie pasta, same reason. I sprinkled toasted pine nuts on top to serve. So good! Not as fishy as I would have thought. One note, the sardine oil splattered a lot when adding it, as the olive oil had already heated. I will add them in quicker succession next time, which there certainly will be!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Sally Lou I mixed them both together before adding them!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made to the recipe. Boy, my family just did not like it. Too bad because it sounded great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
made twice - my notes:

-cook tomatoes down til soft, then add 1/4 the recommended pasta water and cook down
-lots of pepper 
- add a squirt of lemon. top with parm

it was way better when I made it pop the way I wanted it to. also the more you eat sardines the more your body wants them in the winter imo

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Have had to ration myself to eating this once a week for fear of family getting bored of it. I at least double garlic, anchovy, olives. And 1.5x capers. Basil and/or fennel work well instead of parsley. Easy, insanely delicious, omega 3. What’s not to love?!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Good recipe- everyone loved it.
Will make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not sure where I went wrong, as I followed the recipe to at T but I found it bland and watery.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We decided that we like this without the sardines. There was a nice briny salty umami from the anchovies, etc, and for us, the sardines made it fishy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What anchovies? The recipe does not call for anchovies. Skip anchovies, add sardines.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: BJ, perhaps read the recipe again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I saved the garlic cloves from the "Garlicky Chicken Thighs with Scallions and Lime" recipe (Discard them? Please.) and used 1 15-oz can of diced tomatoes, 1 12-oz can of tomato paste, and about 3/4 c of tomato salsa instead of the cherry tomatoes (all of the supermarket tomatoes around here taste like nothing). I would use about 1 c of salsa next time. If, like me, you're not a big caper fan, 2 heaping T is plenty. I did not add any oregano or wine, but I will next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I thought that this needed a little more zing. Canned cherry tomatoes and the addition of some sun-dried tomatoes was helpful, also some chopped fresh oregano. Cooking the pasta in the sauce was a good touch.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Surprised you don't include brown rice pasta in this recipe. We like its texture better than whole wheat pasta!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: They didn't recommend whole wheat pasta.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love this recipe! I can't wait to try it with my garden tomatoes this summer. Used canned diced tomatoes and it was great! But my house stinks a bit from the sardines and anchovies. It was worth it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Nice and satisfying dish ! It was a hit with chili loving husband and our chili hater child.
Such good value for money ! Will make again.

I added a dash of crushed fennel seeds to the tomato sauce - made from 2 bland winter tomatoes and a little bit canned tomatoes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this the other night.  I used brown rice noodles since I am gluten free.  I made the mistake of adding the sardines with their oil in the beginning with the anchovies and garlic.  It still came out delicious, even though the sardines were mostly just small bits rather than huge chunks.  Will definitely make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Halved the recipe to serve two persons. Delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very tasty. I was skeptical about putting so much water into the tomatoes, but with time, the sauce came together. Added very little water to the pasta as I stirred it into the sauce. Love that the olives, sardines, capers and parsley are added at the very end, not cooked down. The oil from the canned sardines ensures a nice umami throughout.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a super easy dinner with very little prep! I recommend a lot more anchovies (I used 2 tins). It made the sauce rich and flavorful.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Added 1/2 of a peeled chopped granny Smith apple as a garnish -sardines and granny Smith go well together, but didn't work here.  Next time I'll try some golden raisins.  The idea is sweet to balance the pungency.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I increased the veggie quotient by adding a bunch of broccoli rabe, making it a single pot meal. Could do spinach or arugula. I cooked the rabe in the pasta pot while cooking sauce in the Dutch oven, then added it while plating. 
I used the whole anchovies tin, to avoid left overs, and it tasted great - didn’t add any salt. 
Tried sautéing/caramelizing cherry tomatoes before adding the pasta water, but I don’t think I had the heat high enough.
Rich delicious meal, ends up 6 - 8 portions.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I like to riff based on what I have. I only had a few cherry tomatoes so I added some leftover jarred vindaloo sauce. I used berbere and Aleppo red pepper instead of flakes and that really made things special. It took a while for the water to burn off since there wasn’t much tomato to work with but it’s no worse for wear. In fact I’m a bit obsessed and might try to do it this way again! Went light on sardines and added goat cheese. Love!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was a good addition to our weeknight rotation, although I’m a bigger fan of NYT’s lemony chickpea pasta. Next time I’ll try reducing the amount of pasta and serving crusty bread to soak up the sauce. Everyone seemed to comment on the sodium level, so I didn’t salt the pasta water - next time I won’t omit this step as I did have to add some salt at the end.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I had everything in my pantry except only 1 can of sardines so I doubled the anchovies. This is a marvelous dinner that comes together easily and quickly. It’s now on the “repeat” list!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have an allergy to tomatoes but like the look of this recipe. What might be a good substitute for the tomatoes?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made as directed and it was fabulous.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Puttanesca is a flavorful and easy dish. I usually add a dozen little necks but I’ll try sardines next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was so good! Halved the recipe, let the tomatoes cook a little longer in the oil,  but otherwise followed it exactly. We loved every bite. It's a keeper! Served with a little parmesan on top.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Do pierce and sauté tomatoes before adding pasta water so they neither explode nor steam. Double anchovies. Reserve some parsley for garnish. Fabulous!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If one is stuck with winter store bought cherry tomatoes adding a tablespoon of date molasses to the sauce will compensate for the lack of sweetness in the tomatoes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Great. Easy to make, good for a weeknight (though next time I'll do the prep in advance.) Big hit. This will go into the rotation.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Divine.  Used a little less garlic, and subbed green peppercorns for the capers -- added a perfect kick.  Highly recommend, will make this regularly.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: quite good but i felt like it would have been a lot better with breadcrumbs! also could use more anchovies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If using a GF Pasta (Trader Joe's Tagliatelle) only cook for 5 minutes & start sauce same time as pasta. Next time, will use more olives & not make mistake of adding sardines with anchovies (only the oil). Still, loved it!  Topped w/handful arugula dressed with lemon juice & olive oil to add brightness.  So fast and easy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m making this tonight for guests and I wanted to know if I’m supposed to use Kalamata olives. Someone said yes, but why didn’t the recipe call for them? Thanks!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yummy. Cut recipe in half but for some reason took me a while to boil out water. Omitted olives

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Excellent. I wouldn't change anything, although I might have been a bit heavyhanded with the garlic.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 😋😋😋😋😋a definite 5 YUMMIES EZ, Tasty, appropriate meal for this cold late Fall night. Perfect as written Thank You

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe is easy and delicious! I will make often. I splurged and bought the expensive handmade pasta at the grocery store (Tortello if you’re in Chicago) and it soaked up the sauce really well. The other ingredients are inexpensive, so the splurge is definitely worth it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is so easy and absolutely delicious.  Essentially a two pot meal.  Clean up was a breeze.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Made pretty much as written. Happened to have sardines in tomato sauce so used that. Went perfectly. 
All the ingredients blended perfectly. Delicious and will definitely make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I also used sardines in tomato sauce, and liked this very much. Actually I doubled the sardines and cherry tomatoes, and that made for a lovely gooey, saucy dish. Also added more red pepper flakes at the table. I would say it was flamboyant!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Easy to cook , we just loved this great and very tasty dish!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a fantastic creation!  My whole family loved it.  It's a delightful way to eat more of the 'lowly' sardine!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made it as directed and it was good, but the balance was off--next time I will double the most or all of the sauce ingredients. But please help me with the timing; I must be missing something. The recipe calls for starting the sauce when the pasta is almost done, but from the oil hitting the pan to adding the pasta to the sauce is a minimum of 12 minutes. I started the sauce early and still had pasta sitting and waiting long enough to turn gluey, and I had to rinse it thoroughly with hot water.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Guy William Molnar
they say this so you have cooking water ready for the sauce I think - but if you do it, you should definitely drain them when they are cooked (and keep some of the water)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Guy William Molnar
(actually it is mentioned in the recipe)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wowsa! Really good. So easy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Amazing recipe, thank you!! definitely the best way to use canned sardines

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Fortunately my menfolk like sardines, so this was a hit and will work for a weeknight. I happened to have grape tomatoes on hand, which worked pretty well. As for the pasta, I opted for thick spaghetti. Don't skip the fresh parsley!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
My advice: go easy on adding the pasta water, maybe limiting yourself to half a cup at a time and only adding more when the existing water has been completely absorbed.  Otherwise, you end up with sardine/anchovy/cherry tomato soup.

Some commenters found the dish too salty. I, on the other hand, didn't find it salty enough, despite adding more anchovies than the recipe called for and not rinsing the capers. I suppose tastes and ingredients differ.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If you like canned sardines, this is a great dish!! I added green olives and pickled sweet peppers from the garden for extra tang.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sauce never fully came together for me. I added some acid in the form of wine and lemon to the sauce because I found it a bit cloying.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious and easy dish that comes together quickly. Tho I do use fewer garlic, only 2 large cloves, and remove them after browning. I’m eating more sustainable fishes and this fits into my repertoire nicely.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We really enjoyed this, though we used tinned mackerel instead of sardines and omitted the anchovies (we didn't have any).  It didn't taste fishy this way.  Next time we'll try it as suggested and I'm sure it will great, extra fishy or otherwise

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was a "crowd pleaser" but the next time I make it, I'll add plumped golden raisins.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: DELICIOUS!  This was a simple but amazing dinner during a snowstorm!  Comfort food at its best.  With homemade bread.  A little fresh parm.... SO GOOD!  Will make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Phenomenally delicious!   Even my partner who said he would never eat sardines, snarfed it down.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Everyone needs a fuss-free pantry pasta in their repertoire, and this beauty has become mine. I usually make a half-portion with chickpea noodles and that winds up being enough for 3-4 meals. The use of sardines (or any canned fish) makes this a flexible and affordable option for all, there's barely any chopping, and the rest of the ingredients are pantry staples that effortlessly amp up the flavour and take this into wow territory every time. Easy to adapt to include additional veggies, too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Easy, tasty, satisfying homey dinner for two: one can of sardines, what was left of a box of Sugo, one fresh tomato, and the rest of the ingredients.  I poached the garlic in oil in the microwave then smooshed it in.  Added bread crumb topping (garlicky sourdough)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Maybe I'm a purist, but I prefer either traditional puttanesca or pasta with sardines and capers (ala Mark Bittman's NYT recipe). This hybrid didn't do it for me. The sardines were mostly lost, while the other flavors were less punchy than a typical puttanesca.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Did not have fresh tomatoes so I used two cans of Mutti baby Roma. Worked well. No anchovies so could be missing out on flavor. Next time. Had some arugula and threw in at the end. This is an excellent pantry items meal. Quick and tasty

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this as written, the only change I made was 8 ounces of chickpea pasta. With that change, 2 cups of water gave us more liquid. However, my husband did not mind at all, especially since he had some wonderful bread to help sop up the extra sauce. But next time I would maybe cut back a little on the water for 8 ounces of pasta. A great dish with very good flavors.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Looks super salty which I don't love. Any suggested modifications?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m sure a good recipe, and made as directed, my family found this unpleasantly fishy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used the oil but next time I’d ditch the sardines. Just too fishy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Okay, so we walked into this thinking what the heck, we have left over pizza, I had bought some sardines from Costco, and I wanted to try something different. This is awesome and not good at the same time at least for my pallet. I am going to try again, halve the sardines, add some kielbasa, and a bit of good parm, I will update you. The recipe is as promised so even though I didn’t love it, I am giving it 4 stars. 

Oh, I used an immersion blender and I liked how that worked for smoothing the sauce.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was unpleasantly fishy. Did not like it at all.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I feel like there wasn’t quite enough sauce for the pasta, I might halve the amount of pasta next time

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is delicious. Made as written but added some sundries tomatoes. Quick and very tasty. Used my sardines in lemon brought from my last trip to Portugal.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love this recipe, but FYI it's not for those who aren't into tinned seafood already. Also, I cook down the smashed tomato sauce an additional 15 minutes or so, makes it nice and thick and more "saucey."

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Two cups of water was too much; I would start with one.  Good recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Which kind of black olives do you all recommend?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed this recipe to a t and it did not disappointment. The flavors melded perfectly. It was filling and nutritious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was so delicious! I added some thinly sliced onions (against the grain, super thin half moons) with the garlic because I had some that needed using up. I also used some leftover passata as I did not have any tomatoes, and just let it cook down a bit before adding the pasta and cooking water. Fantastic quick, tasty pantry meal.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious! Used 10 oz fettuccine, needed about 3 cups pasta water. I’ve made this twice - 2nd time I accidentally bought water-packed sardines, so added EVOO to compensate. Definitely best with oil-packed. A whole small tin of anchovies with their oil works great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Rachel Yes! The sardine oil comes to life when sauteeing the aromatics!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is excellent - flavorful and not at all difficult. I used whole wheat spaghetti to increase the fiber content and it was delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used anchovy paste instead of whole anchovy filets. Canned tomato because it’s January. Really nice!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is the second time I made this great recipe. I wish the cherry tomatoes were easier to skin! That was a bit of a pain. I added chopped basil, chives in addition to the parsley. And just pressed the garlic in with a press. Yum

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @jillene Since the recipe did not call for skinning, I just smashed then right on the pot when the softened!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was delicious! Very unctuous. Didn’t have cherry tomatoes but a can of diced tomatoes and less pasta water worked fine and made it a pantry meal. Used Castelvetrano olives.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was really tasty and so easy to make. Definitely great for a weekday meal. The only change I made is I was out of parsley so I just substituted it for dill.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
During the process I was worried it was going to end up too soupy and bland, but it worked out ok! (Did no have anchovies, though). 

Next time would make half the recipe, as in the middle of cooking I had to switch to a larger pot! Now have tasty pasta for the weekend! 😀

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This dish was so incredibly salty that I had to stop eating even though I was still hungry. I feel I’ve gotten a month’s worth of sodium in one meal. I was looking forward to trying a new protein, but this dish was not it. Maybe without the capers it would have been okay? Who knows. I had to throw it out.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Outstanding dish!  I try to make a new recipe exactly as written the first time - and have often wished I had added the ingredients or omitted some I thought of while making it.  Not this one! It is delicious as is and I will be adding this to our regular rotation. I am ordering more sardines today!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow this was amazing! I didn’t have olives but had everything else in the house so gave it a whirl. I used Miracle Noodles so didn’t have pasta water; added a splash of vermouth instead. Didn’t change anything else and it was fantastic.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I didn't have the black olives that I wanted so I used giant caper berries instead. They are the size of olives and add a lovely lemony hint.  They worked very well and I will definitely use them when I make this again with the proper olives as well!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A perfect dressing for this salad is (clutch your pearls everyone, there is a bottled dressing suggestion ahead) is a half and half mix of Thousand Island dressing and a hot as you like it salsa.  You're welcome.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My mother used to make a taco salad with Thousand Island dressing.  I don't particularly like Thousand Island on a green salad, but it worked with the taco seasoning on the meat.  Never thought of mixing it with salsa, but it sounds good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: While this is not a healthy meal, Fritos and Doritos are terrible junk food - so high in salt they’re almost inedible. Try a good quality blue corn chip like Bearitos.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: How about using Quest chips?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Kan
The description says, “ You can use any corn chip for this recipe…but opt for Fritos if you want that authentic taste.” It was created by Charles Elmer Doolin, a cofounder of the Frito Company at his Disney Land restaurant, so of course he used Fritos.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is the quintessential Seventh-day Adventist meal and is referred to as "haystacks." I prefer mine with homemade guacamole, black beans (or chili beans, or both), salsa, a blend of romaine and spinach, cheese, sour cream, cilantro, and Chili Cheese Fritos.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Matthew That’s right!!! My brother still makes “haystacks”  for parties or any gathering over 5 people. We love them

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
1/2 sour cream and 1/2 salsa (herdez please) and a squirt of lime all blended together is the salad dressing for this at our house!
Also any Mexican cheese instead of cheddar, and soy curls for the veg version.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I skip the meat and season warmed pinto beans with cumin.  I add it to romaine lettuce, shredded cheese, avocado, red onion, tomatoes, tortilla chips and (horrors) Catalina dressing.  It's a go to for a quick, meatless, summer meal....been loving it since the 80's.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: There’s a Girl Scout version of this recipe called “Walking Tacos.”  Start with a small individual bag of Fritos, add cooked taco meat and whatever additions you like: beans, onion, tomato, avocado, lettuce, etc. Clean up was easier and the kids loved it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I use a stainless dough blender or potato masher to breakup ground beef when cooking. Also, browning half a pound at a time (while lengthening prep time) allows for more pan contact and better browning.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Add fresh off the cob corn that’s slightly cooked.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: homemade guajillo tomatillo salsa. I toast dried guajillo chilies, dried Chile Morita, and dried Chile Pasillo and 2 garlic cloves in a pan with a tbsp canola oil. Then I put six tomatillos and one sliced white onion in the pan until bruised and soft. Last, place all in a blender, with two tablespoons white vinegar, and two tablespoons water. It beats Pace Picante store bought brands ("New York City!")

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Skip the corn chip base and stick with crumbled on top to make it a bit healthier anyways, I uses red salsa and sour cream as a dressing. And sub Juanitas corn chips- any Oregonian will agree!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: To crumble ground meat, first add a few tablespoons water to pan and then add meat. Stir around vigorously when water evaporates the meat will be loose and will brown easily. Also, the leaner the meat is the harder to break up.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: When I was growing up we did this with Doritos.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It's the summer version of a Frito pie. You can just put everything on the table and let folks assemble their own. I like a nice dose of pickled jalapeños on top.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I grew up eating this in the 80s. Our version came from the back of a french dressing bottle. Need to add the dressing to take this up a notch, as well as Ranch style beans (rinsed) instead of plain beans. No avacado or sour cream needed. It’s the best!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I will make just about anything Sohla El-Waylly tells me to. I loved the method she used to brown the meat, it worked well and I plan to do that any time I have to brown ground beef. The flavors were perfect, and reminded me, as many other cooks, of the taco salad of my youth. 

I followed the recipe as written, and though I may make some changes for my own taste preferences, it is a solid recipe as written.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Forget the Fritos, serve it piled on a mound of white rice, and you have Okinawan Taco Rice. So good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The Ta-Cup was one of my favorite things about going to Disneyland as a kid. I think it helped to shape and elevate my immature palate. Thank you for eliciting some fond memories.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: As a Texas girl, a taco salad is one of my favorites! I use ground turkey for my meat seasoned with home made taco seasoning. Salad must also include: cilantro, beans, corn, and avocado or guac. The rest depends on what I have in my refrigerator. Dressing: part Catalina dressing, part salsa and lime juice. If you can start with a margarita, all the better!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: SO GOOD! I agree that cilantro would be a good addition (but I'm colombian and cilantro and lime are like salt for us). Jalapeños and jalapeño sauce of course were a requisite. Corn kernels would also work... there is no ruining this recipe, and I did not feel guilt over it. We love you, Sohla <3

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Growing up, my father would make this once a week with Doritos and Kraft French dressing. I don’t know why that dressing worked with this combination, but it did!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A lime juice vinegarette with a few tablespoons of a good salsa works, too, and it's not as sweet as French or Catalina.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We used ground turkey and swapped out pinto beans for refried beans. Guacamole and pico de gallo replaced the avocado and tomato. Delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
FABULOUS recipe!!!  Sooo delicious.
Over the years, have tried a few different taco salad recipes, this one is the absolute best.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is exactly what it appears to be, haha. An extremely solid taco salad! It was really popular and the fam requested that it show up again. I used coconut oil so I could really crank the heat when browning.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Let cool after cooking and pulse a few times in your food processor

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow! This has been a Go-To for me for over 60 years. Grew up in SoCal but never connected this with Disney (about 15 miles away).  The only differences from my version are that I break meat up in pan during frying, rather than doing so afterwards...and serve it with my own fried corn tortillas on the side.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was lunch every Thursday at my high school!  We called it haystacks, always with Fritos, but it was the vegetarian version (sans beef).  It was my favorite meal!  Yumm.  Too, I never knew about the Disney connection.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My mom made a version of this growing up but added a can of Old el Paso enchilada sauce to the mix!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I’ve had this as a staple for years, but can’t stand Fritos. Local tortilla chips (California) are way better. Also add a lot of cilantro, but I realize people differ.
The fun of making this an easy group dish is that the ingredients can be prepared and assembled, and each person can “build their own”

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Homemade Catalina or Western dressing is delightful on this salad.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with plant-based meat? Curious how it tastes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This won't appeal to everyone but we made it with crumbled baked marinated tofu - followed the rest of of the recipe.  It was delish.  
Next time I will try it with the suggestion of Thousand Island dressing and salsa!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In the summer in the Midwest, it's easy to find "Walking Tacos." I just had one at a graduation party. We built our own (in the open chip bag) so each guest could add meat, mushrooms, lettuce, cheese, sour cream, pickled jalapenos as she wished. A delicious version for a crowd.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I only eat veggie, so yes Susan, any “fake meat” works perfectly. I also up the chili powder and don’t use oregano (love it but not in tacos). I dump a can of petite diced tomatoes (unsalted) and let it simmer for a while, too. Garlic is a must as well! Roasted corn and any kind of beans are delicious—I’m partial to pintos. Fritos are not my choice (whatever people like is what they should enjoy!), but the generic grocery store tortilla stuff is great!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love these comments, that most don’t disparage but take it seriously, reminisce about it, etc. Thank you. ❤️

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Shredded cabbage vs lettuce

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My husband grew up with a version of this and makes it to this day. No pinto beans - he uses both kidney beans and garbanzo beans. No Fritos - any tortilla chip will do. His go-to dressing is Kraft Catalina. We jazz it up with chopped up red peppers and sliced black olives. Yummy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this for lunch today, and it was a big hit.  The Fritos make the dish, and we all agreed we wanted to try it with chili cheese Fritos next time.  You can tweak this to your own taste, adding scallions, sweet peppers, olives, etc.  It would good with some chopped cilantro, too.  This served four with a lot of left overs!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Quick and easy vegetarian version for hot summer nights. Chopped iceberg lettuce. Drained and rinsed canned black beans. Defrosted frozen corn. Diced tomatoes. Shredded cheese. Avocado cubes. Sliced black olives.  Crumbled tortilla chips. Toss everything in a big bowl with Aldi's chipotle ranch dressing. Serve with a side of extra chips and salsa.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love fritos but when they're put in a dish like this all I taste is salt and chemicals. Take the extra 5 minutes and fry some stale tortilla cut into strips in a little oil.  You get a much nicer corn taste  and a better texture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made with Impossible plant-based meat and it was delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Olive oil for browning beef please! Pico de gallo, fresh cilantro, and a lime crema make it more authentic to a taco, as do high quality tortilla chips, also made with good oil, at least in our kitchen. Fresh lime for serving a must!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yes, pico de gallo!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The things you miss when you grew up in San Antonio but have lived in Europe for 20+ years: Middle-school Frito. Though it needs saying that an Avocado was beyond exotic in Texas in those days. I had to import the Fritos through a friend in Mexico City. Well worth the trouble for primal comfort food.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Excellent! Wasn’t sure if the layering order was important but I did it as stated (I may follow recipes too literally the 1st time!). We had leftovers, so for lunch the next day I did corn chips on bottom, then lettuce, then the meat and toppings on top of lettuce (crumbled chips on top), and that worked best to get some of each in every bite. Will make this again and again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this salad with suggestions from others: added red bell pepper, corn, avocado, pickled red onions, and the best ingredient - left over bruschetta mix. Also added a bit of salsa verde and used a bit of greek yogurt instead of sour cream. Crowd pleaser for sure!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was fine but we found the seasoning underwhelming. If we make it again, we'll add much more seasoning to the meat and beans. We did like the addition of the beans to the meat mixture. My husband's family always served this with Catalina dressing

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a dry version of the Frito Pie, we walked around with made IN the lunch size frito bag at fiestas in Santa Fe. And the origins, while debatable where, definitely predate this one

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Seemed dry when I made it. If I did it again I would add a can of diced tomatoes after the meat is browned. Spices also need to be taken up a notch.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The beans should not be added to the meat mixture.  The beans should be the bottom layer, topped by the seasoned meat and the other toppings, each forming a separate layer. Fritos are spread around the edge of the beans and on top of the whole stack.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
For better browning of  ground beef ... to 1 pound of meat:
Stir a quarter to a half teaspoon of baking soda into one tablespoon of water. Mix into beef.  Let it sit for five minutes. And you are good to go.
Use in any recipe calling for browning ground beef.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this last week, and introduced the YA offspring to an OG taco salad. It was a hit. Echoing others' comments, I think this recipe calls out for a dressing, and I vote for ancient Catalina or French. Without the dressing, the balance of ingredients -- the Fritos, the fairly dry beef and bean mix, the cheddar cheese, the sour cream -- tilts the mouthfeel too far toward the dry/sticky end of the spectrum. A dressing, especially if you spike it with some hot sauce.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Other additions include pepita seeds, jicama, black or red beans, red onions, jalapenos, and/or corn.  It tastes wonderful to mix equal parts salsa with ranch dressing!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Works really well with ground turkey as well. You can also do a vegetarian version with adding black beans along with the pintos and using non dairy cheese and sour cream. My family likes both versions.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I tossed the greens, tomatoes, and cheese with juice from half a lime and some red wine vinegar and thought that really kicked it up a notch. Crushed Fritos on top add a great crunch to every bite.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Making this tonight— a vegetarian tofu version.  As many have noted already, this was a staple dinner in high rotation when I was growing up in the 70s/80s.  Ours always had Catalina dressing.  Tonight’s version will have a homemade Catalina dressing to keep the tradition alive!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was such a fun throw-back to my childhood!   I made as written but used ground turkey.  My mother used to make with taco seasoning mix.  Felt much more grown up using my own spices.  As others have noted, fresh salsa and hot sauce were perfect additions, as was the squeeze of lime.  Thanks for this!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: There were not enough spices in the "taco seasoning" in this recipe including, most notably, paprika, garlic powder and onion powder. Is much better with the addition of these. Also needed red onions and where is the Catalina dressing?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m honestly a little ashamed how often I make this. And I look forward to it every time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I use shredded cabbage vs lettuce. Easy to make dressing: mayo, ketchup, mustard (basically thousand Island). Important to have meat/beans hot when tossing so cheese melts.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very good; I used turkey meat to keep it healthier.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My only tip is not to forget the lime. This is the taste of childhood in every possible good way. The first time I had this was at the 1982 World's Fair in Knoxville, Tennessee, when I was a kid. My mom quickly made her own version of it and this NYT-featured version tastes so much like hers. What a great comfort, this comfort food is.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Skip the chips and spend five minutes frying stale tortilla strips. Less salty, more corn taste and they stay crispy longer.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Here in NM it's just called a Frito Pie. We raised $ at schools instead of cupcakes.
My FAVORITE is at the Plaza Cafe [santa fe plaza].....it's called the Upside down frito pie
cause they put the fritos on the top!!!! more crunch!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used ground turkey instead of beef and guacamole instead of avocado.  Kept the recipe exactly the same otherwise.  It turned out fabulous!  This one is definitely going into the dinner rotation.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I grew up in the 1980s with this on the solid home dinner rotation.  Mom always served with Catalina dressing.  And that is how I shall serve or this Christmas—with homemade Catalina.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I added thin slice zucchini and fresh corn. Went with the salsa/sour cream dressing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I wonder if anyone knows a good way to crumble up ground beef? …it takes forever to crumble with a wooden spoon. But maybe I’m just lazy

Also, I like to make guacamole, and for a kick, I make a guajillo Chile sauce with roasted tomatillos, onions, jalapeños, and guajillo chiles, as well as toasted ancho and pasilla chiles. I put them all I a blender with white vinegar and some water. They add a nice touch that imo beats the pace picante store bought versions (“New York city!”)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: does anyone know a better way to crumble ground beef than using a wooden spoon? It always takes me forever.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Jay, I put ground meat into hot oil/skillet, let it sit to brown and then turn it over. Then bring in the potato masher. You can crumble the meat evenly. I use a potato masher all the time, for breaking down split pea soup, etc.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My mom used a round potato masher/ ricer— once the meat is broken into large clumps, rub the meat with the masher in small circles around the pan. I think it works great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A wooden sauté spatula is a bit more efficient than a spoon. OXO makes a nice one.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A Zulay meat chopper works well for ground beef!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I got this recipe from a friend in the 80’s….it is delicious but a bit different.  Tomatoes, shredded lettuce, shredded cheese, crumbled Doritos, Western dressing along with the cooked ground beef, spices and drained beans.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I use a pastry cutter to mix and crumble the meat.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I've made this salad for years with salt free corn chips and bottled Kraft Green Goddess dressing - really gives it a zing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We used to make this in the '70s.  It had Doritos and the dressing was Catalina.  Funny, we were jus.t talking about this yesterday

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made mostly as directed minus Sour Cream and fried my own tortilla strips. Delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Bland. Not enough flavor in the seasoning mixture. I tried to amp it up but at the point I did, the beans started to disintegrate and the mixture became mushy. In all it was just ok. Kids ate it so that was good. I would just use a powdered mix for the meat adding the onion part. Good time saver and kid proof. Make sure to use really fresh crispy lettuce.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I always add extra spice too and add some powered chipotle, cayenne, smoked paprika.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was a terrific recipe. I made as directed in the recipe with two small exceptions. My avocado wasn’t ripe enough so I added some pickled jalapeños. I also thin the sour cream so make it glide off the spoon easily. The hot sauce I used was Valentina’s extra hot. I’m careful only to dot it about sparingly. I will definitely be making again. The recipe doesn’t state what kind of oregano, so I used the Italian oregano that I had. I may try Mexican oregano the next time just to experiment.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
