commentBody: At least double the seasoning recommended or buy a taco seasoning pack to go with it. Not very flavorful as written but relatively easy to throw together.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really good!  Made it exactly per the recipe.  It does not need any additional dressing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We have been living on "Old School Taco Salad"  for the last couple hot days. We use Penzys bold taco seasoning w beef (sometimes we use Morning Star chorizo crumbles for beef). Pickle red onion, jalapeno, radishes. We add corn, red pepper, cilantro, duritos (oft just tortilla chips), taco sauce, sometimes quinoa pilaf, doctored up canned beans, oft baby spinach w romaine. It's a summer favorite for sure esp when it's hot and you can eat leftovers for several days.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made as directed, found the seasoning underwhelming. Added more salt, cumin, and chili powder. Hot sauce helped... and it was great. Used Doritos "taco flavor" instead of Fritos.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: To take this dish to a new more tasty level, I put the chips, meat mixture and the cheese in the oven to warm prior to adding the other toppings.  Makes it all delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe would be improved by using greener, healthier lettuce, like romaine. For years I pondered what sort of salad dressing to use that would complement taco salad and I finally figured it out. This is more technique than recipe: use half sour cream, half mayonnaise, (amounts to be determined by how many servings you are making), adding smokey paprika and sriracha to taste. It’s a game changer; try it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Instead of beans I used an ear of fresh corn as I didn’t have beans.  Followed recipe pretty much as written but used morning stars vegetarian crumbles. Perfect for a summers supper. Will make again for sure.  With beans?  Maybe, although the early corn was awfully good.  Yes I know, not as nutritious as the beans.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious and satisfying salad. Will definitely make again.  Added cilantro.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We used Impossible meat, romaine and tortilla chips as substitutions. So good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I typically cook 1.5 lbs of taco meat(minus the beans). For the first night it's beef tacos. For the next night it's basically this version of taco salad, with the exception that I simply drain and wash the beans in a colander and add to the salad. It's a great way to cover two dinners with little effort. My go to dressing? Catalina dressing all day long.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Use tortilla chips, taco seasoning, cholula sauce and salsa

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made as is this is pretty bland. Definitely needs pickled jalapenos, guacamole and salsa but then most things would taste OK with all of that added!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this as written, and it came out really bland, like it was missing something. I added an envelope of Sazon Goya, and it immediately improved it. Was a nice changeup from my usual Taco Tuesdays. I presented it buffet style and let everyone make their salad how they wanted it. Had some pickled jalapenos and additional salsa on hand as options as well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It's been so hot,  I used sliced iceberg ad the main ingredient and added others as flavorings. Delish! Agree with other comments that more seasoning (cumin, oregano, chili) is needed but that's easy enough to doctor. So refreshing on a hot day.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Pleasantly easy weeknight dinner. Don’t skimp on the line juice, it’s the only thing that really brightens, and be generous with the salt in the beef. 

I added about a third of a cup of water to better accommodate the simmer step. Served with chopped spinach instead of iceberg. I’ll make again with quick pickled red onions on top.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Great salad... I made chips from corn tortillas.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Kraft Catalina dressing, yes the red stuff, is another popular dressing for this salad. As shocking as that may sound, it's really good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This brings back memories. When I was at Ohio State, "Texas straw hat" was everyone's favorite meal!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was delicious! The perfect quick, easy warm weather dinner. We rarely have Fritos in the house and my husband ate nearly the whole bag before I had planned the dinner for later in the week! We added some Red Duck taco sauce on the top. Making it again this week!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is what I grew up calling Haystacks, and my family still loves it half a century later. It’s best with Fritos, which I love, really, It’s still good with any sort of taco chip. You don’t even need the beef. (My family always used canned Vegeburger in a soupy mixture with smashed pinto beans (not refried). You can start with lettuce, then add pinto or black beans, avocado cubes, tomato cubes, fresh salsa, grated cheddar cheese, sour cream if you like. This cuts the calories (and flavor).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Refried beans and cheese are all the protein you need for this. Guacamole instead of avocado. Romaine instead of iceberg for nutrition sake. Sliced olives. We have this on the couple of too hot to cook nights we have every summer. Eaten outdoors of course! Enjoyed since a spring quarter party at UCSB in 1976!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Catalina dressing and finely crushed Doritos

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We loved this. We live in Spain where chili powder is quite mild so upped to 1 1/3 tablespoon. And them did a whole tablespoon of cumin. Also, black pepper and about 1/4 teaspoon of cayenne. But the meat cooking method is great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In case anyone was wondering this works really well with impossible meat

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Excellent as written. Happened to have cilantro so popped some on top!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Bland, bland, bland. Came here looking for a good DIY taco seasoning mix (to avoid all the salt/chemicals). Ended up adding a lot more chili powder plus some smoked paprika and some tomato paste just to get it to taste like something. I'll keep looking.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Swapped the ground beef for tofu crumbles & added a can of black beans to further amp up the protein. Also added fresh chopped jalapeños to the garnishments to take the spice to the next level. Delicioso!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Quick easy meal, especially to use up some drying tortillas….  Toasted them in the oven to crisp them.  Mashed up kidney instead of beef to make this a pantry meal.  Would recommend

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Easy and good for. Quick dinner. Next time I will probably add some cayenne pepper or use jalapeño pinto beans for a bit more kick.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious and easy to make

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’ve been enjoying this all my life (though without lime or garlic) and thought I knew everything about the history of Fritos. Born and raised in Dallas, I also am a lifelong consumer of Frito pie. But I had not heard of Ta-Cups. Thanks.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: pretty good salad but i used chicken instead of the beef (mistake)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Excellent!  Made exactly per the recipe and it was so good.  Definitely a keeper. - may add a little more heat next time (jalapenos?).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just served this to our collection of two teenagers and two adults. Loved it. We used quality tortilla chips instead of Fritos and added a little salsa, so  no need for salad dressing. Don’t forget the squirt of lime! I will say this: it would take four huge appetites to consume all of that hamburger and bean mixture in one go. We have enough left over for four smallish luncheon salads tomorrow.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We do ours with chopped romaine, and a quick delicious dressing is 1/3 taco sauce (your preferred spice level), 1/3 ketchup and 1/3 mayo.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Oh yes, also I do one with leftover chicken, pref dark meat. Thin tortilla chips vs Fritos.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I grew up eating this, and my mom’s version was very similar. I still make it a lot, but have added diced cucumber and red bell peppers to mine.  They’re a nice fresh addition!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My mother always used tortilla chips but…

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really good. As my wife finds beef hard on her I used a good quality ground pork with fat in it and used that. Skipped the avocado as neither of us are fans and used corn chips. Easy and taste was very rewarding.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Instead of Fritos, heat a super size corn tortilla in a hot pan, place it on the plate, and then spoon on the meat mixture and other ingredients. We prefer this method to using the unhealthy Fritos.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this with Impossible meat and added cilantro and fresh corn. It was delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Save yourself some dishes and time and just dump the can of beans in. I strained mine and measured the juice, which came out to slightly over a half cup.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Diabetic? Looking for old school comfort food? To make this blood-sugar friendly, omit the corn chips. Keep cheese/cream moderate for heart-health balance.
Then add extra veggies or seeds/nuts to bring back crunch and lift the nutrient profile.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious. I got a skeptical look from my son when I said we were having taco salad for dinner, but he loved it.  The bean/meat mixture was enough for five servings. Worried about health aspects?  All you need is a small a,punt of Fritos for plenty of crunch. We used about 2 oz for two salads. Skip the avocado and use low fat sour cream. Delish!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I am an experienced cook & was very disappointed in this recipe. It is unnecessarily messy and under seasoned and under spiced. Also needs chopped chilies. Use real corn not corn chips, except for some crunch on top.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I haven’t made this recipe but watched the short video showing how it’s made. Did anyone else see a bug crawling in the lettuce or am I hallucinating

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @MP saw bug for sure

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: made a salad dressing from another recipe which it needed; simple avocado oil, 1/4c lime juice, 1/2 t of chili powder, sprinkles of dried oregano and cumin....was just right.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I subbed lentils for the beans because I don’t love the texture of beans and I can’t detect lentils and it was great. I love recipes like this because they’re so easy to modify to your liking or what you have in your pantry! It was delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love this recipie - and Sohla! I serve with tortilla chips and Fritos to have options. Also, you can add any veggies you like - I add diced red peppers and black olives. I use salsa as the dressing and pretend it’s healthier. I also like to make it more salad than chips - I’m watching my figure. I love that it uses some pantry spices instead of taco seasoning mix. I’ve also found you can add the same amount of water if you don’t want to use the bean liquid; you may just need to reduce a few minutes more.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Although this salad sounds great (with the exception of the cheddar, which I cannot eat) raw broccoli is not appealing. I always briefly parboil chopped broccoli then plunge in ice water to stop the cooking if I plan to use it in a salad. Not only does this improve the flavor, it brightens the color to a very pretty and palatable bright green.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: i agree - raw broccoli doesn't sound pleasant to eat.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Briefly parboil? How brief?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Sue omg! This was my first thought upon landing on this recipe

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: A note on raw broccoli: Melissa Clark notes in her recipe for garlic broccoli salad with sesame dressing that the vinegar « cooks » the raw broccoli, kind of like happens with the fish in ceviche. I’ve tried it and it’s really good; it’s not like plain raw broccoli at all. I recommend that all the skeptics try it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
@sally agreed. I have a sensitive stomach— raw veggies cause problems. The dressing in this salad does indeed ‘cook’ (break down) the broccoli.

I love this salad and have been evangelizing about it for a while. Friends w similar issues can eat this without problems. (I do not parboil the broccoli).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Followed recipe exactly and it was perfect.  So glad I did not follow advice to parboil the broccoli. This is really good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Instead of eating raw broccoli, I drop it into boiling water for a few minutes ( 3 to 5).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Even though I’m not a fan of raw broccoli, I gave this recipe a try. I used toasted walnuts because I didn’t have any pecans. It was absolutely delicious! I will absolutely make this again. I grated the cheddar because it’s easier than chopping. Extra sharp cheddar adds a very special taste to this dish.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Chop the broccoli finely and let it macerate in the dressing a few minutes as you prep the remaining ingredients. I was skeptical at the outset but the raw broccoli worked great and the result was delicious. I did hold back some of the quinoa because the recipe as written makes such a large quantity. This means mine was even more broccoli forward. I cooked the quinoa in the Instant Pot, which also made the whole process easier.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is very similar to one from Bon Appetit magazine that uses almonds, dates and scallions with cheddar and it’s easy to riff on the combinations based on what you have on hand (nuts, dried fruit, cheese type.) The raw broccoli makes the salad texturally, try it before you decide to parboil.  The warm quinoa and the dressing soften the broccoli and the salad keeps well, it’s delicious with a fried egg on top!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Also nice to add some heat (finely chopped chili I had around) - and some herbs (a tiny bit of mint and parsley in my  case).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! Bright flavor and very satisfying. The only change I made to the recipe was to squeeze the juice from the whole lemon which probably helps soften the broccoli and adds extra tasty tang to this delightful salad.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly, and it's delicious. Which is good because the recipe should have specified that it serves 4 to 6 distance swimmers during an Olympic training camp.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
Can any other Gen-Xers make this salad without singing "She's chop'in broccoli, chop'in brocco-li, chop'in brocco-la, chop'in brocco-laa-aa, she's chop'in brocco-lay, chop'in brocco-lay?" 

Every time. Can't help it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Tim H Dammit! You just had to go there—and take us with you on this earworm!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This recipe totally delivered. I decided to try it because I had everything in the recipe already. And I was surprised that I didn't need to change anything - no tweaking necessary. Really delicious. And it wasn't like eating raw broccoli. It's such a complex mix that nothing stands out and everything blends well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This salad would be excellent with the Unexpected Cheddar from Trader Joe’s.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Really delicious, and certain to become a weekday lunch favorite.  I was pleasantly surprised with how quickly the broccoli 'cured' in the dressing.  I soaked the quinoa overnight (first time for me) and it turned out nice and fluffy with none of the bitter/soapy taste of past quinoas.  I didn't have cheese and so added some green onion to bump up the savoriness of the dish.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I bet this would be fantastic with wild rice subbed for quinoa!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I have made this for the last 9 months almost weekly. If you use the raw broccoli, it keeps for a week, allowing me to make it on Sunday and parse it out into individual containers for worktime lunch. Hold off on the dressing until the morning you take it, though - dressing soaks into the broccoli, and you can't taste it if applied a day in advance. For more grain taste, I use barley rather than quinoa. Orzo and pasta don't hold up for 4 days, so orzo can be used if eating in 48 hours.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight and it was delicious. Used dried blueberries because did not have cranberries and they were great. Such a bright tasty salad on a cold snowy January day.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This salad is like a party in your mouth. I added more apple (1 1/2 apples) and cranberries (3/4 cup) as I liked the way the fruit played against all that broccoli. I could only find broccoli crowns, so I used about 1 1/4 pounds. (Do not be tempted to cook the broccoli; it's crunchy and perfect just marinated in that fabulous dressing.) I thought it needed a bit more dressing, so I made 1 1/2 times the vinaigrette recipe and added a bit more lemon juice. More of Sohla's spoon salads, please!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This turned out great. But I wish that recipe writers would say what the trimmed and finished proportions of the ingredients should be. I had broccoli crowns instead of a large bunch of broccoli with stems. So, as I discovered, 1-1/2 pounds of finely chopped broccoli was WAY too much in proportion to the dressing amount. I used 1 pound of it instead. It would be much more helpful to tell us the AMOUNT of chopped broccoli recommended in the ingredient list, to get the proportions right.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Used sour dried cherries instead of cranberries, and manchego instead of cheddar. Used what I had in the pantry.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
Excellent but a bit sweet as written, so I scale back the honey.

FYI backpackers: This makes a fantastic, calorie-dense cold-soak meal. Dehydrate quinoa, broccoli & apple; mix with nuts & cranberries; add dry mustard, crystallized lemon, sumac (in lieu of vinegar) salt & pepper; portion dry ingredients into baggies. To eat, soak in 1 c. water for 45 mins. (a Talenti gelato jar is great), stir & add oil & cheese (Babybel—or aged cheddar is OK on trail a while). My favorite John Muir Trail meal!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Followed others advice and didn’t cook the broccoli, and added extra lemon juice. This is so much better than similar broccoli salads from the grocery store deli. I did keep the pecans separate to add to individual servings, since all nuts tend to get soggy or gummy after sitting in a salad for awhile.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was a recipe I didnt cook, but was served by a friend this week and insured me to subscribe  to the cooking app. Delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Addendum: perfect amount of dressing (to our taste) is 1.5X that given in the recipe.  2X is a little too much (but still delicious!).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Agree 100%.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: One could blanch the broccoli in the boiling water and, after removing the vegetable, use the boiling water for the quinoa. Italians do this when making a variety of vegetable and pasta dishes. I'd cool the broccoli somewhat in the ice water, and drain it well before adding it to the dressing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Raw-broccoli skeptics: be brave. Chop it small. This is great as written!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have quinoa on hand but my husband buys those I cup Minute Rice thinks and happened to have two of mixed Brown Rice-Quinoa. Zapped in microwave and left to cool while chopping up the rest. Came out great. Would maybe crumble some crispy bacon on top next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
The three of us spent the whole meal talking about why we didn’t love it. First and foremost, it was too vinegary, and I had already cut the vinegar from 3 Tablespoons to 2. The rest of the recipe I made as is. 
The ingredients didn’t seem to go together properly. They just didn’t fit or blend or complement each other. Just not our favorite.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Vinegary totally agree. It just doesn’t work.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I followed this exactly; it was hearty and delicious. It makes a generous amount! It keeps well in the refrigerator for several days. One recommendation: if you aren't' going to consume it all at one meal, I suggest doubling the dressing, put half of the dressing aside in the refrigerator, then proceed with the recipe as written. The next day the salad is quite dry and needs more dressing; you can stir in the reserved extra batch of dressing the next day (or second day).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Really good! Was looking for recipes for TG sides that are a bit healthier. This is perfect! I made everything as is except I didn’t rinse the quinoa because I forgot to do so before putting it away the day before making the rest of the recipe. Didn’t seem to matter. Fun fact: recently learned that eating broccoli with mustard increases health benefits of broccoli so tastes extra good knowing I’m doing good by my body!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Teresa that fact about mustard seed and broccoli was so interesting. I looked it up, turns out it is restorative to cooked broccoli, as it puts back in what the cooking process takes out. Glad that I know about this now, thanks!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I thought this recipe looked great and so I decided to make a double recipe for a potluck, following the directions exactly. Big mistake! The combination of flavors really didn't work for me, especially the sharp cheddar cheddar with the lemony dressing. Nobody else liked it either, although this is a crowd that readily embraces adventurous vegetarian fare. All the other potluck contributions were gobbled up, but not mine! It's going into the compost pile. What a waste of good ingredients!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I'm thinking I'd prefer to use feta cheese.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
Winner winner, salad for dinner!!  Loved this recipe, made it exactly as written with the exception of subbing Gruyere for cheddar because that’s what I had on hand.  

I did NOT parboil the broccoli, but added some extra dressing to ensure the broccoli “cooked’ thoroughly.  

Got rave reviews from dinner guests.  Just ate some leftovers for lunch on a bed of arugula and topped with a boiled egg and some pan-fried halloumi.  Delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Five stars for giving me exactly the flavor I wanted from reading the ingredient list. Lots of flavors and textures that mix wonderfully. I wish I'd paid more attention to the comments about doubling the dressing though--the amounts given are for people who like very lightly dressed salads. I like heavy dressing, so next time I'll double it. But there WILL be a next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Subbed Pom seeds for apples cuz at last minute apples were so sad looking! Fab recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe rocks. I wasn't sure how it would be on a winter day, but it offered a change that is refreshing in the midst of winter. Conversely, I'd make this in an instant in summer as well.

The broccoli chopped finely, including peeled stem was beautifully tender. I second the comment to let macerate while preparing the rest of the ingredients to add. It's going to Snowshoe Camp next week!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: this is exactly what I think. Finely chop the stems, and let them macerate in the honey vinegar dressing. This is a really, really good salad.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I was skeptical of this salad - looked like a ‘healthy’ salad I see at the deli counter of the local supermarket. But it was actually very tasty, if a bit chewy. I made exactly as written - glad I did not cook the broccoli as some notes suggested - the dressing broke it down enough. Make sure to cut the broccoli even smaller than you think. Eating again now for breakfast - it’s a little addictive.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I cook quinoa with a 2:1 ratio of water to grain, always turns our perfectly with no need to drain and less chance to overcook. It's done when the water's 95% absorbed and there's a sheen of water at the bottom of the pot. Fork the quinoa out of the pot into a large bowl to cool.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This dish just planted itself into heavy rotation at our house. I can see easy variations in the future depending upon what is available. For the most part, I made the recipe as described. I didn’t have Dijon, so I used a small amount of Yellow Mustard and a bit more black pepper and salt to compensate. Another thing… I did not rinse the Quinoa. I never do that part. I loved mixing the warm quinoa with the cooler other ingredients. One extra bit… I topped the bowl with some steamed beets. YUM!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe - I blanched the broccoli first because I prefer it on the softer side. Works well with sliced almonds too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was a big hit, but I did make a few changes. After cooking the quinoa, it seemed like too much. I reserved a cup for another meal. Had walnuts on hand, so used those instead of pecans. Husband hates cranberries, so I added raisins. Also made another batch of the dressing to serve later. All in all, a big hit!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe except used walnuts for pecans and dried sour cherries for cranberries. Loved this salad. I chopped the broccoli into small bits and after just 30 minutes blending flavours, it was really, really good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made to spec except used walnuts bc I didn’t feel like going out to buy pecans. It was tasty, but not the flavor explosion I expected from the comments. I ended up making a third more dressing and added that and it helped. Next time instead I would use half the quinoa and leave dressing as is. Used a nice very sharp cheddar. Wish it had a little more flavor. Maybe I’ll try adding scallions if I make again. PS this recipe makes A LOT! I would halve for my little family next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Just made this and want to confirm - do NOT be afraid of the raw brocc! It stays crisp but isn't woody, and soaks up the dressing so nicely. I substituted with vegetable oil in the dressing bc I was out of EVOO, subbed roasted salted pistachios instead of walnuts and farro instead of quinoa because it just seemed yummier, and can confirm that it was. TRY THIS. IT YUM.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Prepared as written, except for the cranberries.  I only had golden raisins on hand.  It was delicious and easy to make.  The broccoli was great raw.  It gives it a nice texture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
I made this as written and it was so-so.
Then it became asparagus season.  We grow our own and were inundated.
So I made this with chopped asparagus and did not mix in cheese but topped with Feta.
It was fabulous!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I made a half recipe exactly as written and I'm really glad I didn't waste any more good broccoli, pecans or cheddar cheese.  This salad had no taste.  Maybe I chopped the elements too small, but my salad looks pretty much like the salad in the picture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made this following the recipe exactly and didn't love it. The raw broccoli was totally fine - I really did chop it finely. The dressing was just okay but in general I wanted the salad to be a lot more tart and bright. Did not love the cheddar cheese or apple. I think if I were to do something similar to this, I would leave out the honey and sub in feta and leave out the apple. Would probably also add way more lemon. Cranberries added the right amount of tart sweetness that I was looking for.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
For true blue vegetable lovers, I'd recommend cutting quinoa to 3/4 or even 1/2 of a cup -- I'm missing some of the broccoli flavor I got before I added the quinoa. 

However, everyone (no matter their palate) should trust Sohla on the raw broccoli; I normally find raw broccoli, as on a veggie plate, dry to the point of being flavorless, but peeling the thick skin and chopping finely completely transforms the texture and flavor. Do not parboil, I promise you won't regret it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was terrific. Cheated and hit some of the broccoli in the Food Professor because spoon sizing all that broccoli was a pain in the butt. I kind of thought a little more cranberry, pecans and apple would have been nice, but definitely not necessary. Also thought maybe 25% more dressing would have been cool, but my lovely wife disagreed. Will absolutely make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made this for our Super Bowl party.  Perfectly adequate, but everyone agreed it was a bit bland.  It did have a nice lemon flavor, & perhaps would be good to balance out a spicier dish in a menu.  Notes:  do not parboil the broccoli.  The recipe calls for the broccoli to be finely chopped, and it does not taste too firm raw.  I used a good cheddar, but it didn't add much taste given the amount of quinoa.  This will serve more than 6 since people will only want a small portion.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Double the dressing!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, easy, and toddler approved. I put the apples and broccoli in the food processor and did not toast the pecans because I don't have the time or the energy 🤣

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I love all these items individually, but together in this salad, it was a bit underwhelming. I should say, I'm not a huge fan of dishes that lean towards the sweeter side (I love spice, salt, acid) and so perhaps being deeply satisfied with this particular dish was going to be hard to achieve. I'm not sure I'd make it again, but if I did, I think I'd go with others' suggestions to use feta and maybe use less apple.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I had exactly the same experience, this salad was not punchy enough for me as written.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I recommend doubling up on the vinaigrette if you’re making this the day ahead. Add the amount the recipe calls for as the “marinade” and then add some to the salad shortly before serving. It’s loses its vibrancy of flavor/acidic notes in the fridge. The extra dressing freshens it up and adds some moisture back. Also, added the apple and the chopped cheese the day of serving as well in order to avoid slimy cheese and soggy apples.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: The mustard flavor sort of took over, I thought.  I wished I would have put in a little less.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Made as written. Unlike other grains, quinoa is a complete protein, so this salad is a good vegetarian main course and will serve 4 to 6 as stated. But if you are serving it as a side dish, this will yield at least 12 servings.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Nice summer lunch or light dinner. Whirred broccoli in food processor - light pulses to get it small. Subbed in what I had - dried cherries and parmesan for the cranberries and cheddar.  Upped the lemon and ACV slightly.   Tasty.  Could see it working well with crunchy, firm red grapes. Feta'd be a nice alternative too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed.  (I like raw broccoli.) It’s good but I found the dressing too sweet.  Next time I would omit the honey.  I don’t care for any sweeteners in salad dressing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was so amazing and delicious when it was freshly prepared but it was not as good just a couple of hours later and did not keep well at all in the fridge. Would recommend to eat quickly or maybe make it but keep the dressing separate/i.e. don’t mix the dressing into the whole batch but just add the dressing to each serving as you go.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is wonderful! I agree with other commenters that the broccoli, which I finally diced, was "cooked" and softened by the acidity of the vinaigrette. I didn't have cheddar cheese so grated Manchego cheese into the salad mixture, which I found to be a tasty substitution. This truly is a wonderful salad, especially in the winter months, and really, a complete meal.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This is a little bit putsy if you’re lazy like me, but it is soooo well worth it. The depth of flavor is incredible.  I serve it slightly warmed. The only change I made is I pulsed the broccoli until it was almost riced, which is just a personal preference.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This makes a LOT; well more than 6 servings unless this is a main course for big eaters.  It is a tad sweet; I added the juice of the other half of the lemon and a splash more apple cider vinegar. Used roasted salted pecans. Used manchego instead of cheddar. All in all, quite delicious.  Would probably use less honey next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I agree with the note about the raw broccoli - finely chopped raw broccoli the way the recipe intended is best. This was a good recipe, including the sharp cheddar cheese. I shall make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Huge salad, next time make half. Overall good but cut back on the honey next time, also I didn't add the cranberries (too sweet already). Add the nuts and/or cheese per serving for variety.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I will admit I was skeptical about the raw broccoli but I followed some other tips and let it macerate for a few and I have to admit, winner winner chicken dinner!! Absolutely delicious, crisp, and pairs well with just about anything. Since I was shredding the cheese I did the same for the apple and it blended really nicely. Definitely adding this to the rotation. Thank you!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This has become a staple. The honey mustard dressing is great with broccoli. I usually use lentils in place of quinoa. Works well with any dried fruit, nut, and legume /grain.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Endless variations on this recipe have been my favorite spring, summer, fall food for years. I like it with roasted broccoli florets, raw slivered broccoli stalks, feta cheese, tofu, lemon/oil vinagrette, lemon poppyseed dressing, various nuts, whatever fresh fruit is lingering in the fridge, etc. The constant is the quinoa and cranberries. The tartness of the cranberries balances out whatever sweet ingredients happen to be there. Also makes a great sandwich if stuffed into pita bread.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
You can get shredded broccoli stems that look like slaw at Trader Joes or other groceries. I find it's not as hard on my stomach as the broccoli crowns.
I've made a salad like this for years but used farro which is nuttier and chewier.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I have made this several times with no changes to the recipe.  I may fudge the amount of broccoli and/or quinoa depending on what I have, but this recipe is awesome.  I use maple syrup if honey is not available.  Still awesome.  It is part of my meal rotation.  There is no need to parboil or blanch the broccoli.  The apple, cheddar and dried cranberries give the flavor depth and variety.  I use Trader Joe's sweet and spicy pecans (my pantry is NEVER out of it!). Again, awesome!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is fast, easy, uses ingredients common to kitchens, and is very nutritious.  Good protein source, a "complete" meal in one bowl, plus it tastes delicious.  I followed the recipe, but I held back adding some of the cooked quinoa - just seemed like too much.  Next time I will use 1/2 cup dry quinoa rather than 1 cup.  The crunch from raw broccoli, apple, and pecans complemented the softer texture of the cheese and quinoa.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Darn good as written. Makes a lot, more like 8-10 servings for meat-and-two meals. Would be good as a base for a salad topped with smoke salmon or shredded chicken.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I made this with bulger.  It's marinading in the fridge now for tonight's supper. Tasted the dressing, while prepping the veges.  It is outstanding!.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night exactly as written but used the farro option instead of the quinoa. It was delicious! I definitely vote for using raw broccoli. It added a lovely crunch. Love the combination of textures and flavors!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Great summer lunch. Used kohlrabi instead of apple which worked well. Cut the honey in half and the balance worked for me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: 1.5 dressing for single recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Agreed that about 1.5x dressing is needed. Agreed that the raw broccoli cooking in the dressing is perfect! Left out cheese as we are dairy free—still awesome!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This salad makes a delicious meal. I almost doubled the sauce, and that really brought out the flavor. I live alone and was surprised at how much it made- I will be eating it all week!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Definitely a keeper! I subbed in pan-fried halloumi, dried cherries, and roasted almonds because that’s what I had on hand. Love this salad and excited to eat it for WFH lunches for the next few days.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Chopping broccoli releases cancer-fighting phytochemicals; chop and stop to maximize this chemical reaction.  Perfect recipe for this! For me, broccoli crowns are more easily digested.  If broccoli gives you issues, avoid the big stem.  I love this recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious and well-received.  My only note: perhaps I'm slow in the kitchen, but this took me substantially longer to prepare than the 30 minutes it estimates (found this recipe in their list of "quick vegetarian meals").

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This is just lovely. Used my food processor to finely chop the broccoli using the pulse function because my small dicing knife skills are not the best! Subbed the dried cranberries with chopped Medjool dates. Simple, quick, healthy and delish? Checks all my boxes!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
Like, well …. I didn’t feel like
going to the grocery
store ( oh dear)
so instead of peas, I used celery 
(Because that’s what I had )
and I didn’t have lettuce, so I used cabbage, oh and also…. Instead of
dried berries
I found dried ants in
the pantry, but other these
changes… oh wait…..
for the dressing, was out of olive oil,
So subbed some
Maple syrup….
Turned out
Spectacular!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Sky Island Brilliant. This captures exactly how I feel about reading reviews when people list the 96 things they did differently and talked about how much they loved the recipe and will be making it again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I cook like this, too.........

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
I like the combination of ingredients for a salad, but I agree with others that the recipe as written makes a bland, uninteresting dish. After reading comments, I used 3/4 c quinoa, doubled the dressing, and reserved half of it to add before serving. It was still lacking, so I added another lemon, half a scallion, and more pepper, which helped. 

I will try it again with 1/2 c quinoa, triple the dressing, two lemons, green onions, and other spices as needed.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: As someone who really needs a recipe, I have found the encouragement from the description and others to use this as a "base" to be absolutely true! I've used whatever grain, cheese, nut, or dried fruit I've had around, including feta/walnuts/raisins instead of what is listed (though always sticking to the dressing and the broccoli). The 30 min prep time is accurate and amazing for lunch prep for the week, highly recommend!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This salad is delicious! Sometimes I omit the quinoa if I another carb on the menu. I also have begun shredding the cheddar to sprinkle on top instead of mixing it in.  The cheese can get a little mucky. It’s great the next day especially if the cheese is added as a topping. I have also used dried Michigan cherries instead of cranberries. I make this recipe frequently! It travels well and is great for picnics.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
@Carol
I can attest to this salad’s deliciousness!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
I love this recipe and make it 2-4 times a month. It makes a great lunch for work the next day!

I love to add more lemon juice, and put some grilled chicken in it for extra protein.

Enjoy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This would have been so much better without the cheddar, or with feta instead. Wish I tasted before adding the cheese.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious! Tastes so fresh. The secret is to take the time to finely chop the broccoli.  Just enough dressing to add all the flavor but doesn't overwhelm the freshness of the salad. Great as a stand alone or a side dish. I'm taking to our next office pitch-in!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Roasted the pecans and finely chopped the cheese, but didn’t mix in. Let guests top with them on their own.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: My tastebuds and stomach were at odds on this salad. I really enjoyed the taste but my stomach did not agree with the raw broccoli. Next time, I will just use the florets of the broccoli. Maybe I didn't get peel off enough of the woody part of the stem when I made this the first time? I will trust that when the food goddesses, Sohla and Melissa Clark, say that the broccoli does not need to be parboiled, they are correct.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Really, really good with wild rice rather than quinoa.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful! I only had slivered almonds, and they worked well. Next time I will try Bleu cheese or goat cheese.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I accidentally made this with three cups of quinoa instead of one and it is great. I imagine that if I had only used added one cup it would have been too dressing-forward.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This is a remarkable dish that allows for so many swaps. I almost exclusively use Farro rather than quinoa and it holds up so well over a few days, which is all we get as we eat it nonstop! No cheddar? Try crumbled Feta, or add some sunflower seeds. So many ways to use a few of those items in your pantry. When you know a recipe by heart, it’s a keeper!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Next time I will less aggressively salt the quinoa water.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made/brought ingredients to a friends house to make this salad. Instant hit and was asked for the recipe immediately. Agree with other comments that the raw broccoli makes the salad! However if you don’t like that, parboiled broccoli I’m sure works for people with texture preferences.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Subbed farro for quinoa, walnuts for pecans, and hard cotija for cheddar as they were what I had on hand, and it was all just great. Definitely do not parboil the broccoli--as others have said, it macerates nicely and quickly in the vinegarette. This recipe also makes A LOT of food. Luckily, it is delicious and healthy and was very easy to make.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Really delicious. Makes enough for a small army if using as a side.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Do wish your recipes would be for smaller portions, as for 1-2 persons.  Always a challenge to have to half/quarter amounts.  Discourages one from trying the recipes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Thought this was pretty good especially when the quinoa is still warm - took other folks’ advice and left the broccoli raw. It was fine since it was diced up small! Felt healthy and fiber forward. I doubled the dressing and that helped (also a good way to use up two already-zested lemons), probably used about 1.5x and saved the remaining 0.5x for leftovers. It does make a lot!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This just didn't come together as we anticipated. The cheddar was definitely "lost" in the finished product, the pecans also vanished. And, way too much quinoa for the amount of dressing and combination of other ingredients. Even keeping at least half the quinoa back and doubling the dressing didn't quite work for the anticipated consistency. Feta and toasted slivered almonds might be better choices. That said, we probably won't do a repeat on this one (part of it is still in the refrigerator).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Love this. Usually don’t go for uncooked broccoli, but trusted the recipe and it was amazing. Made as written. It doesn’t taste like uncooked broccoli. It tastes bright and sweet and tangy and crunchy and definitely eat w a spoon because u will want to shovel this in your mouth. AND u can feel good about yourself for eating something healthy. Trust the recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I oftentimes pick new recipes to try based upon ingredients that I already like, and so tried this recipe.  Sooo disappointed, it lacked flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This salad delivered, and then some. Used what I had knocking around the fridge - super mature Gouda instead of cheddar, raisins instead of cranberries and Dutch mustard instead of Dijon. Turned out a straight up banger. Served it with hot olive bread, hummus and a fruit salad as a bit of a Saturday-picky-bits dinner. The whole family loved it - and that includes teenagers. This is gonna go in heavy rotation. Might try adding chickpeas or lentils as well next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was amazing. I did use the whole lemon in the dressing. The dressing was drinkable it was so delicious. Note if you don’t want to heat the kitchen the quinoa can be cooked in the instant pot in 1 minute with a natural release.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This makes an absurd amount of food, FYI.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I found the dressing to be too sweet and agree with the other reviewer that raw broccoli isn’t great, and maybe a quick blanch would help.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I will make this as written because I trust Sohla. Then I’ll make a riff substituting feta and chopped dates for the cheddar and cranberries.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I’m not a fan of raw broccoli, but this dish is very delicious! I used toasted walnuts instead of pecans and a very sharp cheddar. So delicious! Grate the cheese. It’s easier than dicing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Yum! I followed the instructions but was hesitant to serve it all combined. I ate it combined and love it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I followed the directions and ingredients exactly except for the cheddar, as we are vegans. The salad is very good! I'm sure it will improve with the mellowing of the raw broccoli as the hours pass. I am going to add a few more cranberries and pecans. I I think that will kick it up even more.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I enjoyed this although did not add cranberries and parboiled the broccoli. I also used almonds instead of pecans as that’s what I had. Next time I’d add chili flakes for heat. I think it was a bit too heavily dressed and wish I hadn’t added all the dressing

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This salad was fabulous. I did use walnuts instead of pecans because that's what I had. I also don't like raw broccoli, but the dressing made it seem less "raw." I just loved it, and my guests did too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I had to make more dressing and add it to the salad. It absorbed all the flavor after the first couple of hours, and the tang and thrill was gone.The broccoli didn't soften up. Perhaps blanching briefly is the key. I would advise doubling the dressing and adding more as needed, because the recipe makes a huge amount of salad.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Having now made this, I am 100% team cheddar + raw broccoli. The cheddar pairs classically with all of these ingredients (think broccoli/cheddar soup; cheddar + apple; cheddar + nuts/honey/dried fruit). But it's also the texture that works really well--the little creamy-chewy cheddar bits contrast nicely with the crunchy broccoli/apples/nuts. I had some plain and some smoked cheddar, and so used both. Also gave a few chops to my bizarrely huge dried cranberries to get them in line. 5 stars!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I'd suggest that you let the quinoa cool before adding the cheese so it doesn't melt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
I make a raw broccoli salad not dissimilar to this and it’s lovely particularly as I’m not too keen on cooked broccoli as a whole!
But raw it’s great,I do however soak for a little while dried cranberries-as we can’t get fresh in NZ,in earl grey tea.about 5 mins and they have a slightly different flavour which always gets great comments.
But I’ll make this version tonight as we’ve beautiful broccoli in our summer garden.
Thanks Melissa for another great recipe

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I made this for lunch today and thought it was great! Quite healthy, but the nuts, cranberries, and Cheddar all give it enough richness to make you excited about eating it. One addition I might try for the leftovers: some scallions!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
Excellent! No need to cook the broccoli.   And I will try some of the other grains in future salads. I just cubed the cheese, quite small , very easy, and it doesn't leave a grater to clean up.
Also, a good dish to take when you are headed out to a friend who is making dinner, or  for a potluck.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: We made this for dinner last night and thoroughly enjoyed it. Like some of the other comments, I was skeptical about the raw broccoli but it wasn't a problem for me at all. We added roasted hazelnuts instead of the pecans and it was a great addition. Also next time I make this salad I will make a little more dressing. It seemed a little dry with the amount the recipe calls for.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made this for dinner tonight and it is GREAT.  I made a half recipe which was still a lot of salad.  I used raw broccoli and made sure to cut it into small pieces.  I mixed the raw broccoli with the dressing and let it sit for an hour or so before finishing with the other ingredients.  Subbed dried cherries for the cranberries.  Otherwise, followed the recipe.  It is a keeper!! Everyone loved it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I LOVE broccoli and will make this again! Next time I will chop the apple and cheddar more on the chunky side rather than fine. Recipe as written makes more than 4-6 servings in my opinion.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Great idea for a healthy salad - dressing underwhelms, unfortunately. Kept tweaking with extra acid and salt. Good - not great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Holy Stromboli!  Made per recipe & had enough for a small army.  Very good first day.  Looking forward to seeing how it tastes 2nd day & 3rd since we'll have it that long.  Not so much a fan of raw broccoli but this was intriguing & with enough good cook's notes (thanks to those who left them) to try. Keeper to make again, although cut by 1/2 if for just four.  Would be good dish to take along when asked to bring veg or salad. I think dry cherries or white raisins could sub for cranberries.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: The raw broccoli is great! When you add the hot/warm quinoa, it softens the broccoli ever so slightly and it’s delicious. I didn’t have pecans so I added pumpkin seeds. It was a hit!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as written. Wouldn't change a thing. I was amazed at how the dressing softened and enhanced the raw broccoli. I allowed it to remain dressed for about an hour, then added everything else. That kept the apple and pecans crisp, giving a nice variety of textures. The dressing was delightful, the meal hearty enough to not need a separate entree. We have plenty leftover for lunch, for which I'm very happy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: In my opinion, a little heavy on the quinoa ... which makes all the other flavors/colors not so vibrant. Cheddar is definitely not necessary -- I agree with the other reviewer that feta might make a more interesting addition. Definitely parboil the broccoli -- for color and texture. Raw broccoli is indigestible!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Having seen other shoppers sneeze and cough before picking up broccoli and other similar vegetables, I rinse and steam or cook it before consumption.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made just as is, and it is delicious. Good quality cheddar important.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Just made this exactly as written other than subbing red wine vinegar for the apple cider vinegar. absolutely delicious! Do not par boil. The broccoli - the dressing does the trick.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Am eating this now as I type!  I agree that only 1/2 cup of quinoa is needed. I’m vegan so left off the cheese(not a big fan of vegan cheese) and roasted pepitas cuz no pecans on the house. Also- no apple as I didn’t have one. Thought the dressing was bland so I added a little bit of chili crunch which was hanging out next to my Dijon. I love it with a bit of heat!!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Briefly Rinse quinoa in a sieve under running water before cooking to avoid bitter taste.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made the recipe as written using red quinoa and smoked cheddar. Very pretty, fresh, crunchy salad. I would say the red quinoa helped pick up the visual interest and the smoked cheddar added a bit more nuance to the flavor profile.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was very good. I made it as written. I found that it made a huge quantity—more like 6-8  . It calls for a lot of quinoa and broccoli. I have started halving recipes now b/c it’s just my husband and I. If it’s just two of you, definitely cut this in half. I’ve been eating this twice a day for 3 days now……

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: All I have to say is..Make this! Perfect combination of sweet and tart and fresh flavors! I did indeed eat it with a spoon....and fully intend to put this in front of anyone I meet. Perfect for a side to seafood....or as a stand alone chopped salad!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Used feta instead of cheddar and added grapes, sunflower seeds, and blueberries, omitted dried cranberries bc I didn’t have any. Delicious. The dressing is excellent!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made the recipe as posted (although subbed toasted walnuts for pecans because that's what I had on hand). Tasted after cooking and it was good. Next day for lunch, it was incredible as a cold salad.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: My family loved this from the get go.  Easy to chop everything that needs chopping in a food processor with the grater blade.  This would be a great salad without the quinoa as well.  Will definitely be making this again and again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I had an excess of beets and no apple, so used finely chopped raw beets in lieu of apple. Skipped the cheese, nuts, and cranberries.  Delicious.  My non-veggie loving self and spouse both ate it up with enthusiasm for several days.  And yes, the vinegar "cooks" the broccoli.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I meal prep my weekday lunches -- would appreciate anyone's thoughts on the staying power of this salad! With a side of roasted chicken, it would equal a great meal!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Great, even with some tweaks. My lemon was bad and apparently I had no AC vinegar, so I added some white wine vin + white balsamic. I'll definitely be making this again for lunches!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This salad is fast, healthy and tasty. I'm in the raw broccoli camp. The dressing is flavorful, the quinoa is a nutrition boost, and there are lots of options to change ingredients based upon what is available. I made this the first time to go with baked salmon fillets for a company dinner and then had it the next morning for breakfast on top of cottage cheese. This is a perfect cooking assignment for a young or inexperienced cook.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious. Notes: 
--Like someone else here, I thought there would be too much quinoa for the salad, so, once it was cooked, I scooped out two cups, which will be saved to make quinoa chocolate cake. 
--I'm not a fan of raw broccoli, but if you chop the broccoli very fine, it's great!
--I used the juice of one whole lemon, as the one I had had extremely thick skin and not a whole lot of juice. 
--Had dried tart cherries on hand, so threw some of those in, too. I will definitely make this again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Delicious.  I used a combo of cheddar and feta.  Also instead of quinua I used Trader Joes Harvest Blend.  Might try adding roasted butternut next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Very very good. I added a grated carrot and subbed raisins for cranberries and barley for quinoa. I did not use any salt but had some on the table; no one added any because the cheddar made it salty enough. Do like the idea of feta, maybe next time. And there will be a next time!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I love every ingredient in this recipe, and yet there’s something missing… I’ve been eating leftovers and trying to tweak the dish a little and just can’t make it work. I followed the instructions and the broccoli was perfect raw. I won’t make this again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I plan to make this with jicama instead of the apple. It sounds delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I’m not sure I see this as a meal, but I’ll definitely make it again for picnics or potlucks. It’d fit well next to coleslaw, potato or pasta salads on a buffet.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
This was very tasty.  The broccoli does soften after it's been in the dressing for awhile -- but be sure to chop it finely.   It also makes a TON of salad, so good for lunches all week.

Make sure you have plenty of dental floss handy afterwards!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: We used kerry gold cheese. This is good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Instead of a whole head of broccoli I used two bunches worth of broccoli stems I had saved up—spiralizing the main stem and chopping the smaller parts. Definitely saving this one.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: We loved this salad. Ate it alongside bowls of creamy pinto beans with cheddar and scallions stirred in. Great combo.Increased dressing by about a quarter and it seemed just right. Fine to juice whole lemon. May cut quinoa  to 3/4 cup next time. Broccoli was not too crunchy. Can’t wait to try with cashews and some diced raw zucchini, or roasted winter squash cubes. Dried apricots or golden raisins would work fine instead of cranberries. Yum !

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Kicking myself for not reading the comments - waaaaau too much quinoa- going to get more broccoli - I imagine this is amazing as I do broccoli salads and that dressing was great- looking forward to the second try lol

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I concur with other reviewers in preferring a higher vinaigrette-to-broccoli ratio than called for in the recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I had to double the dressing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
I found this recipe to be like a kitchen sink cookie, or a recipe to entice a child to eat his vegetables, so laden is it with ingredients that mask the flavors of the main ingredients.  It is an inundation.  My husband was drawn to it because it features quinoa and broccoli.  Little did either of us realize the Inordinate qualities of cheese, pecans, and mustard, among other ingredients, that were called for.  It had a good flavor. Nevertheless, we won't be making it again.
.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: No, just no. I’m a huge fan of grain salads, and I love all the other ingredients, but this just didn’t come together in any meaningful way for me. The textures were weird, from mushy quinoa to crunchy broccoli and the dressing did nothing to amalgamate the flavors. In all these years of cooking NYTimes recipes this is the first one I won’t make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: It's delicious just as it is, but added some onions were a good addition.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This salad is delicious. I made it as per the recipe with the exception of the nuts. The only change I would make is with the portions. As a side dish it serves much more than 4-6.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This salad is addictive, an no, you don't have to blanch the broccoli!  I added chopped kale (had some I needed to use up) to the dressing to tame it before combining everything else together.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: We made this for lunches this week, I added chicken jalapeño sausage for more protein and it was absolutely delicious. I added the whole lemon instead of half as well! If you are meal prepping this for lunches, I would recommend adding the pecans morning of as they lose a little bit of their crunch sitting in the quinoa. Will definitely make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Yes, raw broccoli is not appealing. Cheddar cheese in a salad? No. As I'm making this salad, I questioned WHY would I cook something I know I'm not going to like. Thankfully I took the leap of faith and prepared it as is. I did not parboil the broccoli as suggested by others, and against my better judgement I added the cheddar. Ya'll... it turned out so good. Quick, easy, and delicious. I finely chopped the broccoli and used grated Kerrygold cheese. It really only took 30 minutes. Try it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Delish! I think next time I’d use less honey as I found it a pinch sweet. Maybe will try with feta instead of cheddar or at least very very sharp cheddar as the type I got blended a little more than I would want. I was skeptical but agree that the raw broccoli is fantastic in every way. Would be curious to add a protein, maybe even cured?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I had leftover steamed broccoli so I looked for recipe ideas and found this yummy salad. Followed the recipe except the cheese. I always have crumbled Gorgonzola blue cheese on hand so I used a handful of that. The tangy flavor of the cheese is a wonderful contrast to the sweetness of the cranberries. The dressing is very good and I plan on making it for any salad!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: If you're not going to eat the salad right away, I recommend reserving the cheddar cheese and adding it at the last minute. I've found that after several hours, the flavor of cheese leaches out, and the cheese loses its texture if left in a moist salad.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: UPDATE to my original post: flavors blended nicely overnight. Overall, salad was a bit dry so I made a second batch of dressing and used the whole thing - much tangier and tastier! Great foil to a rich entree. This would be a great salad to bring to a party or potluck or just please the folks at home. Recommend making this in advance.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is very good BUT: it makes an enormous amount of salad, suitable for a party but not for everyday; rec halving the ingredients but keeping the amount of dressing THE SAME.  Also: use a food processor!  Grate the cheese instead of chopping it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: My husband made quick work of this salad, a swimmer and a hiker, he has a hearty appetite!  Did not use the lemon, overkill!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
I’ve made several times now. I don’t always add cheese. Sometimes I’ve used substituted cheese and nut types. Very delicious for a quick lunch!
I’ll use a large broccoli bunch and cut as small as I can. The dressing softens the broccoli and is good texture. Just leave out the very stiff dried out bottom part of the large stem. Definitely use the smaller stems!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made it just as directed, the raw broccoli is not an issue, the secret seems to be to chop small.  I think cooking it would have made it mushy.   Very wonderful blend of flavors...BUT, it makes a ton!  Way more than I expected, could have halved it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Husband couldn't believe the broccoli was raw. For his heart health, no cheese, less oil and fewer pecans. Still extremely enjoyable. Like did others, I used less quinoa and a few extra lemon squeezes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Strongly recommend halving the honey :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made this following the recipe with raw broccoli as per other comments. I used only 3oz cheddar which was more than enough for me and substituted dried cherries for cranberries. Will add more apple next time for better sweetness balance. Lots of prep time but pretty tasty!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: We enjoyed this and will make it again. Instead of cheddar, I added bleu cheese at the time of serving, I used raisins instead of cranberries and and toasted walnuts instead of pecans. Add all of the lemon juice. It kept well over a few days.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I love this salad! It’s easy enough for ME to make successfully (this means it’s easy enough for you to make, no matter your skill level)! It’s a great meal prep lunch recipe and the mix of textures and zippy flavor mean you won’t get tired of it over several days! It’s perfect as written and I highly recommend.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Add more apple and more (double?)the vinaigrette- for vegans, substitute agave (or agave/ maple) for the honey. Swap out vegan cheddar or similar vegan cheese for the cheddar.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Step 1 is cook the quinoa. Clearly 1 cup of uncooked quinoa.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
I just made this for the first time and almost forgot to add the quinoa, because it looked so beautiful and whole!

Trying it with half the quinoa and will adjust.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Oddly, there was no broccoli available today. We subbed broccolini and consider the result to be excellent. We’ll do this one again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
I had a very juicy lemon, and I still added the juice from both halves (not just one).  It needed salt and pepper to finish, and I think I'll try making a greater quantity of dressing for next time.  The amount of dressing was "light", but good.  I just like a little bit more. 
 Also, instead of dried cranberries (too sweet, I think), I used dried Montmorency cherries.  Loved the sweet-tart bite of those.  Overall, very tasty and definitely a winner.  We will be making this again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
I do a sales like this with shaved raw brussels sprouts.
Also works well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This is incredibly easy and so delicious. I made exactly as written. Had to sub golden raisins and pine nuts as that’s what I had on hand. Next time I’ll double it as I almost ate the entire bowl by myself. Thanks NYT!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: When I make this — and I will — I’ll substitute crumbled Gorgonzola or some other blue cheese for the cheddar.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This salad is delicious. I'lll be making this salad a lot.  I did make an additional third of the dressing because I needed a bit more for my taste. Highly recommend it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Substituted pumpkin seeds for the nuts since I'm allergic to nuts and worked great.  About 1/2 cup.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I was skeptical at first about the raw broccoli but a comment here made me more adventurous and I did make this salad, but without the quinoa or any added grain.
