commentBody: I seem to be sensitive to quinoa so I substituted freekeh for the quinoa. Otherwise followed the recipe exactly. Served some to my sister and she loved it too—this is now in regular rotation at our homes. Excellent recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Just curious about how or why the apple doesn’t brown if the salad is made in advance or kept as leftovers. Thanks!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Stephanie it’s the acidity of lemon juice and vinegar that helps the apple to maintain its original color

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This salad slaps. It’s going into my regular rotation. Good with or without the quinoa. Otherwise, don’t change a thing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was great! I only had frozen broccoli so used that, and it was too wet (my fault). Definitely making this again with raw broccoli. Used buckwheat, really good! Farro would be great as well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Easy on the lemon juice and vinegar

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I have made this several times.  I agree with other contributors -- 1.5 x the dressing is perfect.  I have made it with and without the cheddar and I prefer it without.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: We roasted the broccoli first.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I've made this salad (without cheddar) several times. The quinoa and broccoli soak up the dressing, making it rather dry by the next day.  I am now doubling the dressing recipe, and keeping half of it in a separate glass container to add to the salad as needed.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I made this a couple of weeks ago and it was delicious. I thought the recipe called for way too much mustard but my husband insisted on trying it as written and it turned out he was right.  The flavors meld together and none of the individual flavors overwhelms the others. I would note that this recipe makes a lot of salad,-8 standard side servings at least. The leftovers are just as great as when originally served, but for the two of us, it took days to finish it off.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Great salad and I will be making it again.  Thank you.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I am confused. The recipe indicates to finally chop the broccoli, apple, and cheese but the picture shows larger chunks of broccoli and what it looks like small cubes of cheese. These ingredients do not look like they were finally chopped. Did everyone finally chop or did you do as shown in the picture?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe as instructed and I genuinely could not taste anything other than mustard. It completely overwhelmed every ingredient. Never again

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I love this salad and make it on the regular (though I'm often too lazy to zest the lemon). Some twists I enjoy to mix it up are replacing the quinoa with buckwheat kasha and using dried blueberries instead of cranberries

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful kid salad. Dubliner cheddar works perfectly as it blends well with the Granny Smith Apple in aesthetics. A white cheddar prevents this from reminding/tasting/looking like melted velveeta on broccoli.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. My partner made it a couple weeks ago and I’m glad she did. This is a really good salad for hot summers and it’s surprising how refreshing the dish is.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Does anyone have a quantity of chopped broccoli in cups instead of weight they recommend?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: 2nd time making - so good! I cut the quinoa a bit and add extra lemon.  Otherwise made as per recipe which is very unusual for me :-).  I let the broccoli “cook” in the dressing while making other items - it’s cooked without the stove! Also we add lentils on the side to this salad for extra protein.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: simple, healthy, delicious. Thank you!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Really really good, and yes a bit addictive. Perfect for work week meal prep. Made as written.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Chop broccoli more finely than picture -- almost into sand. Don't need to par-cook the broccoli this way. Finely diced apples, cheese, nuts until almost quinoa size.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Wow!  Better than I expected.  The dressing is great and the salad is yum.  Agree with others that the broccoli doesn't have to be cooked in advance.  In fact, I think that would detract from it.  Chop up fine and it softens in the dressing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Typically I'm not a fan of the pungent flavor of raw broccoli, but the vinegar, lemon & mustard collectively neutralize the intensity of it, and the salad as a whole is delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I made this salad today, lightly blanching the broccoli and substituting blue cheese for the cheddar. I also added dried tarragon to the dressing. YUM!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This is absolutely delicious. The mustardy dressing really makes the dish. I followed the recipe exactly, with the exception of the suggested swap of farro for the quinoa. It was a very welcome change after the heavy dishes over the holidays.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
It’s New Years Day and I saw this recipe when I wondering what to do with little bit food on the fridge and a long day of putting away Christmas decorations, the tree etc. I had to make some adjustments and substitutions but it was still yummy.
I had half the amount of broccoli between regular and broccolini so I halved the recipe, substituted walnuts for pecans, and used more apple for the cranberries.
My partner wanted “protein” so I thawed frozen shrimp, sautéed it in a bit of lemon infused olive oil  and we had that on the side. 
Made the dressing first and let the chopped up raw broccoli marinate while I did the rest - yum. I also didn’t  cool the quinoa or mix it in - we just scooped some onto our plates and and put the cold salad on top. It was really good like that
Next time I’m going to try it with feta
Added to the rotation as a fav!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Em that's exactly the cheddar I paired it with! quite yummy

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Borrowing from the Brits, “unimprovable”.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
This was really good.  I reserved a little of the dressing and brushed it on some salmon before roasting, then served the salmon and salad together.  

The salad was a little bit sweeter than I typically prefer.  Next time I’ll probably reduce the honey a bit.  Overall really good though.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Tried this last night and wasn't a fan. I should have read the comments on parboiling the broccoli as the raw flavor was overwhelming. There was no flavor to the quinoa at all and I felt there wasn't enough dressing for the salad. I added more dried cranberries, oil and the rest of the lemon later which helped. I think maybe a champagne vinegar would be better than apple cider vinegar. My largest complaint isn't related to flavor at all. Finely chopping the broccoli makes a huge mess!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Loved this. I  blanched the broccoli, used honey crisp apple and sharpe white cheddar. The salad makes a hefty amount.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: What a great chopped salad! I ate it as a light dinner and loved it! I definitely wouldn’t recommend parboiling the broccoli, as the crunchiness and crispiness are key in this recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Great taste! I used a food processor to chop most of the ingredients and next time I will chop it by hand because it chopped the ingredients too finely and I would like to have more of a crunch when I eat it. I also added chopped bacon and it was delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I find the recipe good as is! No need for parboiling the broccoli, if you chop it fine enough, the vinegar is enough. I substituted farro for quinoa, but I’d probably reduce it down to half a cup of grain next time. Also used golden raisins and toasted walnuts because that’s what I had on hand instead, and these were fine too. Good recipe for meal prepping for office lunches. Thinking of maybe adding bacon or thin pork belly or shredded chicken for more protein next time too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Great dressing! I didn't mind the raw broccoli, but overall the flavors didn't work for me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: 4 ounces was plenty of cheddar for me. I doubled the dressing (because I think it needed it), and doubled the apple (because I wanted it). Soooo satisfying, and great to have in the fridge. Don't worry about the broccoli being raw!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I also followed the recipe as is and found it to be very delicious. The texture of the raw broccoli was perfect after marinating in the dressing for a little bit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This salad is so good, you can take it anywhere and know you are coming home with an empty bowl.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: next time I make this I'll half the mustard, it was making my nose run and eyes water as it was just too much. yum otherwise though!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Less honey , quinoa and less mustard

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made as written once with the cheddar, then another time substituted 4oz of goat cheese. Even my goat cheese skeptical family members much preferred it the latter way - we found that the cheddar, even good sharp cheddar, got lost and the recipe really benefitted from goat cheese's creamy tang.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I love this salad.  I do steam the broccoli briefly before chopping in the food processor.  I double the apple and increase the cheese, cutting it in small cubes (the sharpest cheddar you can find). And my small mighty secret:  add 1 capful of vanilla extract to the dressing. You will not regret it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Quite fast you have a delicious meal, this is a keeper. Perfect for a potluck too. Instead of cranberry I used dried cherry, it was delicious! A perfect Mariahs between ingrideants. Also added a small can of lentils and instead of cheddar I used a matured Gouda. Was afraid of using raw broccoli but I am so happy I tried it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This is so yummy! Made it without the apple due to an allergy, otherwise as written.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Great to make ahead for lunch all week. I used dried apricots instead of craberries because that's what I had on hand. Will be fun to play around with different nut, fruit, cheese and grain combinations.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: At first, I thought this recipe was too much chopping, but all the flavours worked so well together. I especially loved the addition of the cheese, and yes, the broccoli doesn't taste raw. I have no idea how the transformation happens!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Tasty, but took me well over an hour to make, not 30 minutes as stated in the recipe. Lots of cleanup time. I steamed the broccoli slightly, toasted and chopped the pecans, used pepper jack cheese, as I had no cheddar, and added a small amount of chopped chicken breast to add more protein and make this a main dish salad. I liked this method of cooking quinoa, which I've never tried before. Worked well. The recipe made a huge amount!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This is FANTASTIC on Day 2. But know, lotta chopping. I only made half the recipe and the chopping was such I wasn’t sure if I’d ever make it again, but Day 2 persuaded me. Made exactly as written.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I switched out the quinoa for bulgur partially because I had that on hand and also because my people don’t love quinoa. Chopping things properly is the key to this recipe. It was delightful with a grilled salmon on the patio. Thank you!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Note for future: add more lemon to taste, depending on how much broccoli you had time to chop up!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
I made this and it was delicious.
I didn’t  have enough broccoli  so I added chopped curly kale . 
I used a little over half of the called for quinoa as I prefer the greens to the grains.
I will make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Perfect. I used broccolini, the only change. Everyone loved it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This was not great.  Way too much!!!!  And not all that delicious. Made 4/26

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Make the recipe as written. It is that good!!!!! And the acids break down the broccoli so it's not too hard on your tummy. Tastes best on day 2. We leave out the cranberries because I don't like them... but other than that, we make it as written!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Yummy, used what I had on hand too. Used 5T honey mustard as I was out of Dijon.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I make this on repeat. It is delicious. My modifications: no honey, add small chopped shallot. Definitely use crispy quinoa - cook it then crisp it in over @ 400. Add when served. Do NOT forget the lemon. Save some dressing to add as the week goes on; add crispy quinoa. This salad lasts in the fridge. Delish!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Pretty good! Easy, light Sunday supper. I like the crunchy raw broccoli.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: This is great - making it on repeat to have a veggie snack option in the fridge. I didn't get around to toasting and adding pecans the first time and still loved it. Lasts all week in the fridge. We used dried cherries because it's what we had and now I'll always use them for this recipe. I might try adding some white beans next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Great leftovers for lunch as well. I add cheese as we go, and sometimes not at all, as I do limited dairy. Lactose intolerant but love cheese! I have made it twice once with pstachios and dates as I didn't have the correct nuts and dried fruit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made this with faro as we were unexpectedly out of quinoa. And I left out the cheese since my husband can’t have it. Wonderful! I agree with the earlier commenter that mentioned the dressing “cooking” the broccoli. It was perfect!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I am an outlier, I didn't care for the recipe. Too much cheese (which I would reduce by at least 75%), not enough apples or brightness (tartness). On paper it should work but, it didn't for me.  My wife also agrees with me (and she has a better palate than I do.).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I substituted farro for quinoa, toasted slivered almonds for pecans, dried cherries for the cranberries and sherry vinegar for the apple cider vinegar.  Very tasty !

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: @Elizabeth thanks for the idea of using dried cherries!  I love dried cranberries (and this recipe), but variety is nice!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: We love this broccoli salad so much. We make it almost exactly as directed (swap raisins for cranberries) and it is perfect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody:
I made the recipe exactly as is.  My teenagers all consumed it without complaint. A winner!
The one thing I might add is I thought it tasted the best on the first day.  On the second day the individual ingredients became indistinguishable taste wise.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Salad so good it reminded me it is life worth living after all.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Rinse the Quinoa after you cook it?  That seems weird.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I've made this multiple times, both exactly as written an with variations (Jarlsberg instead of cheddar,  cashews and/or almonds instead of walnuts, rice or orzo instead of quinoa). All fantastic. Two tips: cut the broccoli and get the sauce on it first so it begins to soften while you're prepping the rest, and cut everything quite small so you can get some of all of it on your spoon. Great recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: I wondered about the raw broccoli, but kept faith and cut it into pretty small pieces and tiny florets and it was delicious. Absolutely no need to cook the broccoli - unless you prefer it that way. I loved the textures and flavors; it was full of fresh and bright flavor. Loved!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: Made this exactly as written with Trader Joe's Unexpected Cheddar but eliminated the quinoa. Delicious as a side dish; easily fed many more than the 4 to 6 servings suggested.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: My mom made this with couscous and added shredded rotisserie chicken for a succulent non-vegetarian version.  The broccoli was beautifully "cooked" after the salad marinated for several hours in the fridge.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
commentBody: can I use frozen broccoli and hust microwave it for a couple of minutes ? its all i have at home

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I like the simplicity and adaptability of this recipe. I cut the honey down to one T or so, and subbed applesauce for 4 T of the oil. I added a touch extra to make up for the moisture lost by cutting back the honey. Turned it tasty and plenty sweet for me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed this exactly and I am an experienced cook. These look good but have little to no taste. Its not sweet, you can't taste the peanut butter. The salt on top pushes it away from sweet to... neutral. Perhaps this is for "special" diets. It feels like food-by-committee. Just don't. Really.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @American in Paris yes, so I’m looking forward to making these for my toddler, as well as myself as I’m in post partum recovery in need for something quick, high fiber and no dairy due to infant dairy sensitive. We’re very “special.” Wouldn’t touch these otherwise.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @American in Paris I felt the same and I’m an experienced cook, too. Only my dog likes them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @American in Paris same here! I was so confused how all these ingredients that sound like they should go well together just turned out so blah. And the texture is not great either - I think it should’ve had some baking powder.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Chocolate chips made a great addition..and when do they not!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Miranda agreed!!
I added chocolate chips, shredded coconut, and a third banana. Delish.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed recipe, used almond butter in place of peanut butter. No overarching flavor, even the banana gets lost somehow. So bland I looked over the ingredient list several times to make sure I added everything.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Agree with comments these are too salty. Recommend halving the salt, or even less. Like the texture and found them reasonably tasty. I'll make these again, but with less salt and perhaps adding raisins or dried cranberries.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I took to heart many of the comments that these are quite bland, with no discernible flavor profile. So I doubled all the spices, used about 5 tablespoons of honey and used toasted hazelnuts on top. Still, after all that extra love given, these are godawful and should never be made! This recipe should be binned, just like the batch I made.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Reduce olive oil to 2 Tablespoons 
      .Apple sauce  4.5 Tablespoons 
Reduce honey to 1 Tablespoons

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I ignored the negative reviews and made them anyway.  They just don't taste good.  Even with less salt and added coconut I thought I could beat it, but no.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The amount of salt in my opinion ruined this recipe for me.  Couldn't eat it.  I would add half or a quarter of what the recipe called for.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was so good we made another batch three days after the first one! Added a tablespoon of chia seeds to amp up the protein. I don’t get the other comments about too much salt, bland, etc. This recipe is legit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Use bananas that are so ripe they're almost liquid in the peel (takes about a week). Reduce honey and oil to 3 tablespoons. Only 1/2 cup of almond butter. Add a scoop of protein powder. Triple the spices and vanilla (use pumpkin spice instead of plain cinnamon). Add plenty of nuts.

This gets you the perfect "healthy" cake base. I make this weekly with a variety of flavor variations (apple coffee cake, brownie chocolate chip, carrot cake, pear tart, ect.)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I found the cook time on this long so I’ll make it again as breakfast cookies.  I agree with what other people said that they’re bland, I would add some raisins, craisins, and/or chocolate chips next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Based on other reviews I skipped the salt. I love this recipe. Easy, tasty, perfect for a breakfast bar.) If you’re looking for something else, try a pastry.)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these the minute I saw the recipe--great fit with what I needed to use up!. Halfway in, I realized I had lost my 8X8 baking pan so made in a round pan and cut it into wedges.  Was it good? We gobbled the first two pieces, so yes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were super disappointing. Was hoping for something a bit more flavourful with so many expensive ingredients.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It’s just amazing how tasteless these were!  The flavor of each ingredient canceled each other out. I won’t bother trying it again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: After reading all of the other comments, I took a chance and made some adjustments. I substituted 4 tbsp of Greek yogurt for 4 of the tbsp of olive oil and used a neutral oil for the other 2 tbsp instead of olive. I cut the honey and salt in half and mixed the walnuts into the batter. Overall it turned out well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Thank you to those suggesting to cut back the salt. I only added 1/2 tsp and it seemed perfect. 

Besides that I reduced the olive oil to 1/4 cup (my bananas were large rather than medium) and added raisins and chocolate chips and chopped walnuts (rather than on top) to the batter. I used peanut butter for the nut butter. And it’s true that they don’t taste overwhelmingly like bananas or peanut butter, but maybe that’s not a bad thing! The flavors just blend together for something unexpected and more subtle.

My whole family really liked these. I took them out of the oven 3 hours ago and they are almost gone. I thought they were in fact “fudgey” and sweet and healthier than a lot of other bar or cake recipes. I will definitely make them again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Omg I made this based on the four star rating before I read the reviews. Wish I had bothered to read the reviews. I am usually forgiving of “okay” recipes and eat them anyways but this is bad. Something is very wrong with this recipe. I threw the whole pan out and am annoyed that I wasted all those ingredients. Oh, I did sub maple syrup for part of the honey because I ran out of honey…so if the honey was key to making this work…well, I don’t know.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Ama right?? How does this have 4 stars?!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Nope, I used honey. Just bland, not worth all the tasty ingredients

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I should have read the reviews - these are so bad. They’re salty and tasteless. My husband asked if I accidentally plastic into them. I refuse to waste food so I will eat them… resentfully.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Emily I doused them with maple syrup and choked them down of the next few days because, like you, I didn’t want the food to go to waste. But, never again will I make this recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Emily I too don’t waste food I make. I chopped the bars, froze them in a pot (they seperate frozen) and added a piece or two to porridge (oatmeal) in the morning. As they’re frozen, cook porridge 2 minutes less and increase liquid by 1/4 cup, add more spices (1/8tsp good pinch), 1/2tsp sugar, pinch of salt or salted natural peanut butter to flavour extra liquid. I enjoy “bulked out” porridge, because I like bold flavour but I’m indulgent when it comes to breakfast

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is the second time Ive made these bars, I encourage all of you who may have read the negative reviews to give them a go.  We love these, perfect for a ski day snack or with a cup of coffee.  Used 1/4 cup honey, dash of maple syrup, one egg, coconut oil instead of olive,  1.5 tsp Morton salt, 1T cinnamon, 1/4 tsp nutmeg, and a good shake of shredded coconut.  Double the oats to soak up the extra batter, and added a good amount of ground flax seed as well.  Sprinkled pecans on top, no salt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Well this is hardly the recipe, is it? As is, they're pretty bad.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Feeding 5 

I appreciate the effort in the modification friend, but if you have to completely rewrite the recipe to make it palatable then it’s a terrible recipe. 

That being said your modifications do sound like an improvement. 

If enough people try it and comment maybe it’ll get bumped to the top of the reviews and save a lot of people the disaster of cooking this recipe as written.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are just awful. They aren’t sweet at all. The banana flavor is lost, the peanut butter doesn’t come through, the salt is way too heavy. The recipe says they should be fudgey, but instead they are a fall apart crumbling mess. Don’t bother trying this recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Other commenters call these “bland” but I would call them perfect for kids.  We made a few modifications based on preference and availability of ingredients :

- used 3 T of honey instead of 6
- 1 tsp Morton salt
- 1 1/2 cup oats (we used quick oats)
- 2 1/2 tsp cinnamon
- Added 2/3 cup flour (try whole wheat next time)
- Added chocolate chips (a must!)
- Skipped the nuts and extra salt topping

Perfect kid breakfast bars

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these bars according to the recipe; the bars turned out well, but need the maximum baking time or a little more.  I also made these bars with an egg substitute for a vegan family member and the results were the same!  My celiac husband, who is not a big fan of baked goods really enjoys these  bars!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used almond butter and overripe bananas. I thought these were good as a gluten free option for someone who is avoiding gluten and comes over often. I am a little surprised by the disappointing reviews. Regarding saltiness, it is important to note that Morton kosher salt is used in a lesser amount than Diamond Crystal. I don’t know if that was why some had an issue with the bars being too salty or not. Using Morton, I went with 1.5 tsp of salt. These are a breakfast bar, so they are not super sweet and they really aren’t intended as dessert despite their appearance. I would make them again for sure.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I had some overly ripe bananas and was looking for something other than banana bread to make - I got more than halfway through the recipe and then starting reading the comments and boy was I worried. I doubted myself and hastily chopped up some dark chocolate to salvage it. I didn’t need to! I used almond butter and chopped almonds and left out cinnamon. Otherwise I made the recipe word for word and it was great. Even my husband who is a hard sell on healthy things exclaimed how good it was. The things I think really matter - very ripe bananas, rolled oats, and a dry nut butter. It came out with a dense bake like texture and was neither too salty nor too sweet. My almond butter was 100% almonds and almost chalky in texture because I had it upside down in cabinet and I didn’t mix the oil in knowing there was a considerable amount of olive oil in the recipe. I would 100% make this again - the adults loved it and the kid loved it, doesn’t even need the chocolate!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I read the reviews and made adjustments to deepen the flavours (reduce salt, replace honey with maple syrup and dark muscovado sugar, use a spice blend of cinnamon/cloves/nutmeg to bring out banana flavour - see Stella Parks banana bread, mixed 50/50 salted almond butter with unsalted tahini for more flavour?, added pulsed oats (quick oats) because I had a wet batter, no surprise with no flour!!). In the end this recipe is deeply problematic, nothing can fix it flavour or texture. JUST TERRIBLE

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used almond butter, cut back on salt because of reviews, 1 tspn, added 1/2 cup almond flour super fine, just curious, and semi sweet chocolate chips. It is a great recipe, easy to make, healthier alternative to a banana muffin. It’s moist, packs a protein punch and great snack to hold you up in between meals :-). Pour batter into 24 little muffins. If you want more calories and sweetness haha throw a crumble on top. Thanks for all the great reviews, super helpful

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I very rarely leave reviews even though NYT cooking is my go to site.     I made these based on the ingredients and that I am living on a boat in the south pacific and have too many bananas almost all the time.  I was shocked by all the negative reviews.   These are great and good for you and a nice pick me up in the middle of the day.  I cut them into 16 squares and find it is a much better size.   I did cut down on the honey a bit but otherwise - made them as written.    Thank you Sohia

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I wanted to love these. Everything that goes into them is something I love. The bake seems good, and they smell great. But the taste is… strange, and definitely not good. Disappointing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I read through the comments just after baking these bars and grew very nervous as I read through reviews remarking on their blandness and saltiness.  I tasted one and they're exactly what I was expecting them to be - a not too sweet, nutty breakfast bar.  I could still taste the nut butter and banana, it all blended really well. I did take into account to reduce the salt (use Morton Kosher not Diamond) and the 15 minute wait time for the eggs to work their magic with the banana.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I would bet most people made these without a scale. I measured out all the ingredients following how many g or ml indicated, and it seemed way off from the cup, tbsp, tsp amounts. I followed the recipe exactly and it came out great, full of flavor!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are not great. Super oily. Lots of honey — there’s got to be a better choice. Was waiting for that ‘fudgey’ texture, but none. Don’t normally use salt but this time I’m looking for it. Two banans is abt 212 g.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I don't know what all the hullabaloo is over this recipe. I made them and they're really good.  They do have a 'fudgy' texture and they are 'satisfying', as described. If you want to cut down on the salt go-ahead, I might do next time but there is nothing overpowering in this recipe and I didn't find them bland at all. It really hit the spot and I will absolutely make it again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: delicious and super filling. you people swapping out the oil are crazy. they have a great texture and sweetness. they taste like a combo of banana bread and salty peanut butter. so perfect for taking to work if trying to avoid packet snack food.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The minute I took it out of the oven, it burst with flavor. The walnuts are crunchy and not overly sweet. It's a keeper and I like that it is loaded with nut protein. I won't tweak or change anything.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made these and they were tasty, but I made some changes. 

I didn’t see all salt comments before make mine, but luckily I forgot to add the salt to my batter! My peanut butter was plenty salty, so that took care of things. I am guessing the issues with the salt level relate to the type of salt people are using (the author uses Diamond Kosher with is MUCH lighter than other salts), and nut butters vary significantly on saltiness. So, my recommendation is to just use salted peanut butter. I did put Maldon on top. 

I made three other variations that: I complete eliminated the olive oil. It just seemed superfluous to me with all the peanut butter fat. I am guessing it is adding a strong flavor that, as some cooks noted, overwhelmed the peanut butter and banana flavors.  I also used maple syrup (100g) instead of honey and used both cinnamon and ginger.

These aren’t huge changes, but they probably improved the flavor significantly. Texture is great—no meed for the extra fat from the olive oil.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thank you. I will sub the eggs with a vegan alternative.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I absolutely adore Sohla and have employed the egg-banana method in other recipes. That said, this recipe should be flagged for removal. These were awful and I will not make them again. I’d also say they’re “squidgy” rather than “fudgy.” My preschooler and husband were both displeased, so these became compost.

The finished product was something that maybe you tried from a blogger in the early aughts who doesn’t test recipes but had an idea turn out once and then tried to remember the steps.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Terrible recipe.  It tastes like a lightly sweetened, bland, oily, salty mess.  Do not try to adapt the recipe, make something else.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Perhaps it was the ingredients I used or the fact that I like food that is less sweet, but these came out great for me. Here’s what might have done the trick:
• I only have quick oats on hand, so they probably picked up moisture pretty quickly and blended nicer.
• Teddie peanut butter with ground flax seeds. I can see how a lightweight sugary peanut butter would disappear here
• three bananas, so probably an extra quarter or half cup to what’s written
• I don’t measure cinnamon, I eyeball it and go a little heavy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This is the third time I've made this recipe. The first time I put salt in as directed but didn't recognize that my nut butter was also salted so it came out way salty. 

Last two times I've doubled it and used quick cooking oats and a 9 x 13 glass baking dish. It comes out more like a granola bar and it's the perfect cooking time at 35 minutes. 

I've used a mixture of almond and PB, natural and with soybean oil, and it's very forgiving. Add more spices for taste.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’ve made these a few times and I always put a lot of walnuts, and chocolate chips. This time I added pepitas and flax seed meal as well, I’ve used agave instead of honey and it’s great, these are solid, but I always use Celtic sea salt so they aren’t too salty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: there’s so much hate for this one that I had to try it out to see for myself. I thought it was good and as described.  Made as written except avocado oil instead of olive oil. Used Diamond crystal salt and the 2 teaspoons were fine. 2 teaspoons each cinnamon and vanilla-how could it possibly be bland? Not sure why there were so many complaints. I used almond butter too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I see a lot of negative comments here, but I actually like the recipe. However, next time I would doctor it up with a lot more ingredients to make it much more of a hearty bar. I’m going to try adding more oats, some seeds, raisins, and maybe some flax. I cut the salt down to 3/4 of the original recipe since the peanut butter is already so salty and it was just fine.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I usually love the recipes from the NYT. But this one is  unfortunately terrible, even with chocolate chips.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are WAY too salty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Agree with the comments that these are way, way, WAY too salty. And we used unsalted peanut butter. Nest time, we’ll cut the salt as other’s have suggested.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These bars were easy and fun to make. I finally read the comments once they were in the oven and realized I probably shouldn’t have wasted my time. Things I noticed, they tasted a little different than what I expected once they were out and cooled a little. I cut them and put them in the fridge. Had one cold this morning and they were better. I think next time if there is a next time, I will add 1/2 c flour and some chocolate chips. The recipe definitely needs more substance. Also, I had substituted applesauce for the eggs. Next time I will do that and add more to replace the olive oil to not waste my good olive oil in a cakey thing where there’s not even enough to add flavor. Also would skip the honey since mine comes from our bees and is a treat here at our house and I would use brown sugar. So basically, I am making it more of a cake situation and who doesn’t love cake for breakfast!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We agree with all of the bland and salty comments.  They went right in the trash bin.  This recipe should be removed.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this with very ripe bananas, good quality peanut butter, olive oil, etc and really no flavor! And kind of mushy. I wouldn’t really call them bars.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm not an experienced baker so I followed the recipe exactly. All of my ingredients were good. These are awful tasting! Not sure what the problem is, but the recipe went immediately into the recycle bin.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are excellent! Would be very adaptable. I only used 1/2 tsp of salt in the recipe and eliminated the flaked salt on topping. These bars would be great for winter hike, snow shoe or ski activity.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
January 15, 2025.  I used 3 peanut oil, and 1 tablespoon safflower oil. Cut too 1/2 t kosher salt (may not even add that much next time), added 1/2 c. sugar and 3 T molassas (no brown sugar but thta would be nice).  I think this is best made night before and served cool!  I will make it again.  Probably change it up each time..maybe add fresh cranberries halved?
I also added a yummy neufatchal cream cheese/yogurt/milk/vanilla frosting I found on the 'net.  Family ate it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Unsuccessful for me, too-- trying to figure out why, giving Sohla a little benefit of the doubt. Wouldn't the KIND of PB have a huge bearing on texture and taste? The fat level of PB varies widely, doesn't it? And if you are using shelf-stable PB, would stabilizers and emulsifiers influence results? I am surprised the recipe does not advise about what KIND of peanut butter to use.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this spontaneously cause I didn’t know what to eat for breakfast. Indeed they tasted a little bland. But with some jam or yoghurt added they were fine. I didn’t use so much sweetener and less salt. And they were a little dry in the end but nice sort of banana bread vibe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love Sohla El-Waylly's recipes but something terrible happened here. I note the recipe is "updated" Jan 17, 2024. Sad to say the entire product had to be trashed. The only good thing is the beautiful picture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My husband and I really enjoyed these! Not sure what the less satisfied bakers did. I used a little less olive oil than what the recipe calls for - about 4 tbsp instead of 6 - but otherwise followed it to the letter. I wonder if maybe folks were expecting something with the sweetness and texture like a cake, but this recipe is intended to be for breakfast bars! So yes, they are fudgier and not quite as sweet as a cake or bread. They are tasty and satisfying!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was very happy with the taste and texture. I loved the peanut butter/banana flavor combo. I reduced the olive oil to 5T (and will try to get away with 4T next time). I also cut back the honey to 4T as my peanut butter already contained sugar. I used raw honey. I used 1/2 tsp of fine salt, not a full teaspoon, and only one tsp of cinnamon because I find cinnamon can overpower everything.  Definitely will make these again and enjoy more experimenting.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Well I thought these were lovely! Made sure I used overripe bananas. The only sub I made was Bobs Redmill vegan egg replacer to sub the eggs, and still found the flavor to be the perfect light sweetness for breakfast. I used 1.5 tsp of an off brand salt (are people complaining about saltiness following the Diamond Crystal recc?) and didn’t add the top salt. Maybe this recipe just isn’t for those who prefer a traditionally sweet breakfast, but it’s a great grab and go option for me!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was almost put off by the mixed reviews, but that also challenged me to make this exactly as written to find out for myself... and as always, those bananas to use up.  The negative reviews,  prepared me for the results: Not dessert, not banana bread... but satisfying, healthy breakfast bites.    My four year old grandson who has started to be food choosy, happily had seconds.  I don't seek out gluten-free or vegan, but this is a bonus for those who do.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed recipe; three tasters agreed "just not good" despite all the healthy ingredients.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Mediocre at best. Gloopy and no taste of banana. Would benefit from more fruit and some flour for cohesiveness. Had to increase bake time to 50 min and still underdone. Skip it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were just terrible.  They went in the trash after a bite from my wife and I.  Cooked to a T, and we don't take throwing food in the trash lightly. How did this make the #1 recipe today?!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not good. Followed the recipe and added a handful of chocolate chips, but it came out both bland and salty. Double checked to confirm that I’d added the correct amount of the right kind of salt. But I think this’ll go right into the compost.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Found these bvery salty and little else. A disappointment.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: How could this list of wonderful ingredients make such a Meh bars?  What’s wrong , I don’t know everything.  I spent time thinking of how to improve: dried fruit, nuts in the batter, chocolate chips.  Finally the conclusion i came to was find a different recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were way too salty and terrible. Waste of time, effort and cleaning dishes!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I agree with everyone who writes that these are too salty, and flavourless otherwise. The texture is off, too. One of the most disappointing NYT recipes I have made so far. They are so wrong that I, too, looked over the ingredient list once they had cooled and I had tasted one. I wanted to rinse my mouth out afterwards. They leave a cloying oily and salty taste that lingers. Ugh.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I don't usually write reviews, but I thought this time I should.  What a disappoint this recipe is. It certainly is easy and it smelled great coming out of the oven, but it was tasteless, except for salt.  And that was even with adding chocolate bits!   I ended up throwing it out.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Don't waste your time. I followed this recipe exactly. This combination doesn't taste good. Too salty. Texture is not good. I'm not sure what would help it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Husband made these tonight and they were basically inedible. Seriously too much salt, and the texture was just weird. A sad waste of a lot of ingredients . . .

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were way too salty. I halved the salt based on comments and they were still too salty. Otherwise I made them exactly as specified and found them to be too oily and not particularly tasty. One of the worst recipes I've made from NYT Cooking.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I don’t know what happened! It tasted like salty nothingness - all of the ingredients somehow canceled out. Love Sohla’s other recipes but this was a miss for me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Came out salty but otherwise bland. An unfortunate waste of ingredients.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So salty, and the texture was like a half baked cake. Followed the recipe exactly and I did bake for the full 40 min.  Ended up in compost as we couldn’t eat it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these twice, bc the recipe is exactly the kind of thing I am always looking for and I had forgotten on round 2 that I had already tried this once. Really disappointed, both times. Do not taste good despite the ingredients being ones I'm excited about. Texture is claggy despite ensuring a thorough cook. And do not keep well, get soggy within a day.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I rarely leave comments, but this was sadly just not good. I made it exactly as the recipe, and found it incredibly oily and tasteless. I wanted to like this, but it wasn’t worth the ingredients or time - save your bananas for banana bread!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Overall, really like these for a grab no go brekkie. Especially as someone newly gluten free. Used half honey, half maple syrup since honey was running low. 
Has anyone done this with coconut instead of the walnuts on top? I feel like the walnuts are a little too heavy for the fluffy texture of the rest of the bar. Next time I’ll tray large shredded coconut instead.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We aren't too impressed with this recipe. The peanut butter overwhelmed the banana but did not have the yumminess of a peanut butter cookie. I won't make it again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I should have read the reviews - these are so bad. They’re salty and tasteless. My husband asked me if I accidentally melted plastic into them. I did not. I refuse to waste food, so I will eat them - resentfully.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is not a good recipe. These are greasy, salty and not delicious. One of the worst recipes I've seen on NYT cooking.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were just plain bad.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Added extra over ripe bananas (which was the raison d'etre for the recipe), used almond butter, flaky salt on top, served warm, definitely captured the attention of all my co-workers and they really enjoyed that it wasn't that sweet. The squares held together well. I think it was a good choice over the usual banana bread which was going to take too long to do in the morning before work and didn't require to be slathered with butter or cream cheese:) ! Thank you for the idea.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed the recipe and thought these were really good. I’m not sure what all the fuss is about with them being too salty. Mine turned out perfect. Perhaps people are using salted peanut butter?? These tasted like healthy banana and peanut butter bread in bar version, which matched my expectations. Also came together super easily and fast, so bonus points for that.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly, it came out a weirdly flavorless oily meh, and a waste of ingredients. Almost composted it but decided to put it outside for the raccoons instead. They ate them, but that's hardly a high bar.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
what do you all eat for breakfast every day? pancakes and bacon? this is a perfectly tasty breakfast for folks who like to start their day with something healthy.

subbed coconut oil for olive oil. 1/2 eggs was a flax egg because I only had 1 chicken egg.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I found these bars delicious and can’t believe others called them bland. They have a wonderful honey nut flavour. I highly recommend them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The first time I made these they were not nearly as flavorful as they smelled while I was baking them. The second time I made them I decided to apply a generous amount of salt at the end to sprinkle on top before putting it into the oven and found it made all the difference! I do not agree with other reviews that it was too salty but in fact the opposite. More salt adds a nice contrast between the sweet of the banana/honey.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Don’t know what everyone’s complaining about. I think they’re tasty. Not too salty. Pretty strong on the peanutty flavour. A nice healthyish morning bite. I did run out of honey and sub in some maple syrup.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yum!!  Made these w pure almond butter (pure=ground almonds. No added yucky oils) plus an extra tsp of cinnamon. Baked at convection 340 for 27 minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Reading these reviews and trying to decide if I dare make this recipe. I have a hunch that people’s choice of nut butter might be the swing variable. The question would then become *which* nut butter is the best fit for a happy outcome?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these exactly as written, and they came out perfectly! They make the perfect pre-workout snack! So yummy!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These were really good - not sure what the other home cooks did. I used a maple walnut pecan cashew butter and added an extra 25g oats. I skipped the salt. 

Poured them onto a sheetpan and baked. You could probably bake this in a loaf pan. Mine came out like banana bread.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was nervous after all the comments but went ahead with it. I omitted all added salt, used super ripe bananas, and underbaked a tad. Perfect!! Moist, delicious, and filling. Will make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these vegan with an egg substitute, reduced the oil to just 2 tbl, subbed maple syrup for the honey and added 4 finely chopped dates. Upped the cinnamon and added some five spice too. Family loved.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I reduced the kosher salt slightly because the brand we have in the house is saltier; not all kosher salt is the same. Also used a natural peanut butter that has only peanuts and salt. Taste is good to me. Lots of negative comments, but I am glad to have a substitute for packaged granola or protein bars that I sometimes grab on the way to work.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I don’t understand the negative reviews here. If you want a muffin, then just make the darn muffin. If you want a scone, then just go make or buy a scone.  To each their own I guess. I am on a high fiber diet and this recipe checks off a lot of boxes for me and it’s also not loaded with sugar. I ended up going with almond butter and cut out almost all the oil by subbing apple sauce. I did end up adding several diced and pitted Medjool dates and that added a wonderful texture and density akin to a packaged breakfast bar. I stored in an airtight container during the week and noticed that the flavor developed as these sat longer. I would go with riper bananas next time and am thinking of subbing out the eggs with hydrated chia seeds and maybe adding some Greek yogurt for added flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious. A bit of common sense will have you read the recipe first and halve the amount of salt. I've made this twice and find it flavourful, fudgy and highly adaptable - once I didn't have oats and used plain flour, another time i didn't have honey and used soft brown sugar, and bot times it turned out fantastic.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed the instructions exactly, except I baked it 10 more minutes because center looked wet. The texture is fudgy and it tastes like an unsweetened blondie with a salty top. Next time, I’ll add mini chocolate chips or coconut flakes on top.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not bad, but a little too salty. Next time I’ll probably add only half the salt and skip the salt sprinkle on top. I also added a few chocolate chips on top to intice my kids to try something new.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What amount of chocolate chips did you add Miranda?  That sounds like a great addition to me!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I just want to say thank you to the community who makes time to provide info and improve recipes! It is appreciated!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were pretty bad. Too salty, not sweet at all, no banana flavor. Really disappointed.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Awful.  I made it with almond butter.  I used 4 tbsps of olive (not 6 tbsps), used half of the salt noted, used a bit of nutmeg as I didn’t have any cinnamon.  The taste (granted with nutmeg) was bland and still too salty.  I would not make this again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not good. It comes out bland and salty, even when I added a handful of chocolate chips. I even double checked to ensure I’d out of the correct amount of the right type of salt. This will go straight to the compost, sadly.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe was awful. I used flax eggs bc of an egg allergy, but even with eggs, it’s hard to imagine this ever tasting good. It was incredibly salty! Omit the salt on top and maybe this recipe will improve, but it’s not sweet and like others are commenting, it’s almost savory and bland. I don’t want to try this recipe again, but if I did, I’d add heaping amounts of cinnamon and extra sweetness more honey or brown sugar perhaps. Better off just baking banana bread…

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Agree with American in Paris, I followed recipe and just not very tasty. If you’re going to try them, skip the salt on top for sure, I think took away from the flavor of the honey and peanut butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: very disappointing. wasted very good ingredients. Flavorless except for the overwhelming saltiness. Save your ingredients and make something else!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I suspect there are at least two key reasons some report dissatisfaction with their output: one, they didn’t time a full 15 minutes for the banana-egg mix and missed out on that chemical reaction; two, they didn’t try the bars the next day. I bet the flavor improves over a day or two. Or their oil was old. Or their walnuts were stale or not toasted lightly first. Anyhow, my panful smells better and fuller than any banana bread or cake. Can’t wait to cut into them tomorrow morning.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Okay! The morning after, finally tasted these bars. What a unique, delicious variation on banana bakes. Agreed with the baker who called them akin to Lara bars: that cheek-by-jowl, peanut butter-banana combo is spot on. Texture is fluffy and will be familiar to those who make those two-ingredient banana pancakes. I look forward to their slight maturing in flavor in a day or so. Used sorghum instead of honey, BTW - out of honey. Really good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Too salty if using PB or nut butter with salt already added. Adjust accordingly! Love the texture of these. Will make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Way too salty

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Agreed with other commenters who found these bland. They are also oily in texture. I wouldn’t wake up craving one of these!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Similar to what other commenters wrote, I found these incredibly bland. Only the salt really came through. They’re also calorie dense - so I did not find them worth the effort or the calories.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: After reading all the comments, I made this, substituting with what I had on hand: Skippy peanut butter and a scant 1/4 c. olive oil; no salt; 1 T. pumpkin pie spice, used 4 T. maple syrup, 1 T. dark agave syrup and 1 T. brown sugar for sweetener; sprinkled the top with chopped honey-roasted peanuts. I waited until it was completely cooled to taste it. I think the texture is really luscious and the flavor is subtle but really good. A keeper for me!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Cate I make this every couple of months. Last time, I used almond butter, white sugar, almond flavoring, and sprinkled the top with sliced almonds. Its a very forgiving, flexible recipe that we enjoy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Few points:
- Indeed, the texture is nice and fudgy, but if I had a hankering for something banana-nut flavored, I wouldn’t turn to this. 
- Used handmade chunky peanut butter and can barely detect it, unfortunately. 
- While the squares aren’t bad I wouldn’t make this for guests or for part of a special brunch. 
- Really glad I skipped the additional salt. Even though I’m a fan of salty things, the 1 tsp of table salt was almost too much. I’d do 3/4 or a 1/2 plus the flaky salt on top next time

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Based on comments I was unsure how these would turn out- but they turned out great! Added pecans instead and folded them into the batter along chocolate chips. Will make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I actually liked these a lot more than it seems others did. Incredibly simple to whip up. Took the tip to add chocolate chips. Delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I thought these were really good. Based on the comments about lack of flavor, I added semi-sweet chocolate chips but I think the flavor would have been great without them. I might do half and half in the future. I didn't add the salt on top and liked them without it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Loved the uniqueness of this recipe but WOAH are they salty. Perhaps my brand of peanut butter was saltier than Sohla’s. I’ve been looking for a nice and dense, portable, high calorie but homemade snack for hiking and this really fits the bill! I loved they texture best of all- lots of homemade granola bars are impossibly crumbly. Next time I’ll at least halve the salt, and I want to try with blended dates instead of honey.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I rather liked these. For anyone who didn't know this already, use about half as much Morton's kosher salt than Diamond Crystal. I used sea salt this time (vs table salt) and was happy with the result. I also used a natural peanut butter. I half wondered if I could use Nutella, but didn't try it. I will probably try a little less oil next time to compensate for what's in the natural peanut butter. These have more fat than I'd like for breakfast but they are yummy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Was excited to make these for my toddler son and I to have as a morning snack but they are almost inedible by how salty they are. It’s a shame and a big waste of time and ingredients.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I kept checking the ingredient list to see if I missed something...this turned out terrible, I had to throw it away.  Totally wet and mushy even after baking an extra 10 minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Based on others' comments about flavor, I added chocolate chips and thought these were really good. I think they would have been good without the chocolate chips. It's subtle flavor. In the future I would do half with chocolate chips and half without.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were great with some tweaks. I used three bananas instead of two because the first two were well short of a cup when mashed. I also added chopped dates. These baked for a little longer than the recipe said, about 45 minutes in all. A solid make-ahead, pack-ahead breakfast.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Baked in a muffin pan for 25 minutes and they came out perfect

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I loved these bars! 
I doubled this recipe as I wanted to make use of 4 unripe bananas. Substituted maple syrup for honey and used only about 8 tbsp. 
Also did not completely double the unsalted almond butter requirement. 

However, I listened to the comments and reduced the amount of salt. I truly wish I had trusted Sohla as she knows how to please the Asian palate. I added flaky salt on top but it was just short of being enough.

I also added fresh ground cardamom and nutmeg to my recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Why didn't I read the comments first?? Made a double recipe to get rid of some over ripe bananas and these are WAY TOO SALTY. AM just throwing them out. Dang - that's a lot of wasted honey, almond butter and olive oil $$$

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These were not very flavorful at all, as others have noted. I would recommend reducing the salt by at least half and not throwing any on top. Also, don’t use natural peanut or almond butter because it makes them too soft.

These have potential but as is they are pretty terrible. We threw them away.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was so salty I threw it out. I’m going to stick with banana bread.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is an awful recipe. It has no banana taste, even though I used banana extract, but mostly, it  had way too much oil and the finished product was greasy. The bars also were quite soft, and not crisp, as I had expected.  Don’t waste your time and ingredients on this (although I do usually like NYT recipes).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I scored the recipe low because as others have noted, there really wasn’t any taste despite the ingredients.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Agree with other reviewers that there’s too much salt and no overarching flavor. I hate that I used a $10 jar of almond butter on this. Won’t make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The salt was to taste,  so I went light on the salt. The only thing i did differently on the recipe was use 2.5 bananas to get a full cup. I won't make these again. The olive oil flavor permeates. I used cold pressed virgin. Very light tasting. The end product was totally disgusting.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Lining the pan with parchment paper and overlapping the sides made it very easy to lift these out of the pan and into the garbage. Mostly tasteless with hints of straw.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was incredibly salty and mushy. Please heed the warnings by other reviewers to decrease the salt by half or more. We used kosher salt too, so we didn’t think it would be that overpowering (unfortunately, it was). Trust your instincts—if it seemed like the salt was too much when making this, you’re likely right. If you enjoy moist baby food, this is your ticket (although with the high sodium content, I wouldn’t feed this to any kid or adult with HBP).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I found this recipe absolutely delicious as is - perfect for breakfast. I suspect those who found it bland or salty were expecting the sweetness of muffins or a typical sweetbread, which this is not. They're super moist and satisfying without being too sweet. If that's what you're looking for in breakfast, these are for you.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m not understanding the other comments about no flavour and too much salt. I didn’t have kosher salt so I followed the recipe of 1tsp fine salt and thought it was perfect. I also loved the topping of flaky salt. I love the fudge texture and the walnuts on top to contrast. I could taste the sweetness, banana, peanut butter, and a hint of olive oil. I love the balance of flavours in the recipe. A great treat to start the day.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I reviewed the other notes and made a few changes.  I used brown sugar instead of honey and added some mini chocolate chips and a little coconut.  We really enjoyed it.  Our bananas were very ripe which added to the sweetness.  Will make it again for sure

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These taste of olive oil and salt, not banana, honey or peanut butter.  Bland and not worth repeating.  Rather disappointing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Halfway through mixing the batter I scrolled down and read the notes, and thought “yikes, oh well, might as well go for it”. So I had low expectations, but I was happily surprised. I think these are great! Just the right amount of sweetness. I used quick oats because that’s all I had. I love the walnuts on top, will use more next time. Used just a pinch of salt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I think the outcome of this depends greatly on whether your peanut butter is good or not. Because all I can taste is subpar peanut butter, but I guess that is kind of on me. Chocolate/chips would make it better.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe was very disappointing.   Nobody in my family liked it.  I think it really needs flour.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These bars are healthful and delicious and my 2 year old loves them. Yes, they’re not that sweet and the salt comes through. And yes, 2 bananas makes them less bananaey than you might have wanted… if you just don’t like the taste go make something else, don’t call it a bad recipe lol. Sohla rules. Coarse ground almond butter here and a flavorful olive oil, just 100g honey, and pecans - legit

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cooking time for me was much longer than recipe called for.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: agree with all the comments on salt- wished that I would have only use 1/2 teaspoon-I unfortunately followed the recipe and the salt ruined the taste and the dish

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This came out great! I went by the weights in the recipe. It did take a bit more than 2 bananas to get to 240g for me, which of course depends on the size of the bananas. I used natural peanut butter that only contains peanuts, and I used unsalted walnuts. I imagine it would affect how salty and sweet this is otherwise.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Agree with the reviews.  Bland, bland, bland.  Love all of the ingredients so I anticipated that these would be delicious.  Very disappointed.  Make a banana walnut bread instead.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These smell amazing going but taste disappointing when they’re done. I was so excited to make this recipe and it was a let down. I thought about adding chocolate chips, and I wish I had. These just don’t have any distinct flavor. I’ll still eat them because I made them and don’t like wasting that much food. Hoping some warm butter will help.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The mess and hassle with the ingredients is one thing but these have no taste.  Based on the other reviews, a recipe like this should have never been included in the Sunday supplement this week of breakfast recipes.  I wish I would have read the reviews before I spent the time making.  In addition to no taste, too much salt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I, too, am a very experienced baker. I usually get rave reviews from NYT recipes. I should have skipped this one. Too salty. Little taste. Nothing positive to recommend. Will not make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Ghastly, nobody ate more than one bite. Banana, peanut butter, vanilla, and cinnamon were completely   masked. I’ll retry with half the amount of oil, using a neutral oil instead of the olive oil, only 1/4 to 1/2  teaspoon of salt, and skipping the flaky salt on top.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Subbed coconut oil for the oil and maple syrup for honey. We were not especially impressed immediately after cooking but they were so much better flavor after a day! Will plan for this another time. Might add either golden raisins and/or coconut flakes next time. Great portable breakfast!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I should have done what I often tell others to do, and read the comments first! These are waaay too salty! Maybe because my peanut butter was unsweetened? But even then...excessive.  It will be a great excuse to slather maple butter on top before consuming, as otherwise, the nuttiness and texture were great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: made today and would not make again. i don’t hate them as much as most, but they’re pretty underwhelming. for me, they are not too salty, but they do veer salty. there just is not alot of flavor, despite two flavor-packed ingredients: banana and peanut butter. if you’re needing to use up a banana, here’s a better choice: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1012904-banana-everything-cookies. That recipe works well with olive oil, sugar reduced to 1/2 cup and adding a handful of chia seeds.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I agree that they are too salty and bland - however the texture was perfect. Not sure what I would do to “fix” it

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Meh at best, as per everyone in the family. Made them according to recipe. No real taste, too much cinnamon and vanilla, definitely too much salt, even though I cut that one in half. Came out mushy and crumbly at the same time, baked per instructions until a toothpick came out clean. The formula could probably use a small addition of a starchy binder and a dab of leavening, just to lift the very compact appearance and taste. Maybe someone will try it, to me it was a waste of good ingredients.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We liked them, but prefer something not too sweet and filling for breakfast or a mid day snack.  Used about 4T honey,coconut oil instead of olive oil,  a little more cinnamon, a little nutmeg, and half the salt per other reviews.  I did end up having to add about twice the oats to thicken up the batch, and I let it sit for an hour, so the oats oils thicken up, before popping it into the oven.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love to bake & try new recipes.  Made these this morning and no one in our house enjoyed them.  I realized they were gluten-free and brought them over to a neighbor who is gluten-free and thought maybe she'd enjoy them?  Saw another neighbor along the way and offered one to her.  Neither she, nor my other neighbor (who both tried to be kindly polite) could stomach them.  On a positive note, my dog and each of the neighbors' dogs thought they were scrumptious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: no!  tastes awful.  don’t bother

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Mushy and bland. Threw it away, seemed undercooked even though I baked it 20 minutes longer.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
almost didn’t make this because of all the bad reviews, but I’m so glad I did because it was exactly what I was looking for: a wholesome breakfast treat that you can eat on the go! Maybe everyone was disappointed because they actually just wanted dessert?? I made a couple small tweaks and thought the result was so good AND they kept really nicely. The changes I made were:

6 tbsp melted butter instead of oil
Maple syrup instead of honey
1.5 cups oats 
Dried cherries

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: After reading these reviews, I suspect the challenge with this recipe is which peanut butter to use. There is a huge amount of variation in texture, taste, and ingredients, particularly salt and sugar. I won't cook a recipe calling for peanut butter unless it at least specifies whether to use natural or commercial varieties.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Way too much salt. I couldn’t eat them and wouldn’t serve them. I thought I must have made a mistake until I read the notes and saw all the similar comments.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Replaced the honey with 1/4 cup brown sugar and the olive oil with 4 tbsp coconut oil. Was not as bland as others described, or overly sweet

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love Sohla's recipes! This was great. I did reduce salt a tad and threw in some semi sweet chocolate chips, flax seeds and raisins. Yum

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Oats are not suitable for Celiacs or anyone truly requiring a GF diet. This recipe should note this or be untagged "gluten free".

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Allison oats don’t contain gluten though. They can be contaminated with gluten during processing (farm, packaging factory). Some coeliacs are affected by avonin (sp) in oats but it isn’t specifically gluten, it’sa derivative I think

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is really bland. It doesn't taste of bananas, or nuts, it just tastes like something "healthy" that you'd buy in a health store a few decades ago. I followed the recipe closely, and I'm really disappointed. I threw half of my batch away.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed this recipe exactly. These bars were bland, oily and salty, and I thought the texture was more undercooked than fudgy.  I ate half of one & tossed the rest.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wish I had read the reviews.  I think it is the almond/peanut butter that ruins these.  Also, using salted or unsalted almond or peanut butter makes a huge difference in the saltiness.  Mostly, though, they are not "fudgy" as promised and they don't taste very good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The one time I failed to read the notes before making the recipe...regret. Agree with the comments about too much salt and little flavor. I give it a solid "meh." Removing from my "Recipe Box" now.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe is way too salty. I used 1.5 tsps (rather than the two tsps called for) and they were still almost inedible.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Too salty. Kids did not like them.  Maybe ok with chocolate chips added, but definitely not a hit here with my fam.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious but came out very goopy - would have added another 1/3 of oatmeal. I had to stick them in my freezer for them to set.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Way too much salt. Will try again with less salt and more bananas.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I loved these! After reading the comments, I omitted the salt. I think I could have used 1/2 tsp. I reduced the oil by 1 tbsp because my dough was looking gloppy. I mixed the walnuts into the dough, and also mixed in semi-sweet chocolate chunks. I had VERY ripe bananas. I think the chocolate is a must if you want these for a healthier sweet treat. The texture is similar to a dense carrot cake or pumpkin bar. My toddler likes them but they’re not rich enough for my older junk-food loving kids.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I think they’re delicious, have been making them every week with no edits to the recipe. A great replacement for the boxed breakfast bars I used to buy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Added cocoa powder, chocolate chips, and chia seeds and it was delicious. Did not think it was too salty or bland with these additions

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: nice texture but too many flavours. Could not taste banana or peanut butter. I would remove cinnamon next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: First batch as written, pretty lousy. But such a great nutritional profile! Second batch much better: Omit cinnamon, use 1/2 tsp. salt, chill before cutting.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: P.S. 8x8 pan, stir walnuts in, mix batter before preheating oven. (So the oats have time to absorb some liquid.) With these revisions, it’s a keeper!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I thought these were delicious - the fudgy texture is great, and ripe (key word) bananas make this sweet along with the honey. Healthy but not low calorie.
