I think it accomplishes its mission to be a healthy, nutritious breakfast bread. The negative commenters must not be very used to this sort of baking. It's not meant to be very sweet.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
overall i liked this... but...

a) always let your bananas get extra ripe, not just semi-ripe. they'll be sweeter and more 'bananay'

b) despite the comment above, i don't like overly sweet baked goods so i cut the amount of honey by ⅓

c) i also cut the amount of nut butter in half so the flavor doesn't overpower the bananas, which nut butters tend to do. i used PB w-no added sugar

d) to enhance the banana flavor even further, i added a tsp of banana extract, but that's just me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I found these were not that flavorful until I added more salt on the top. Made all the difference! When you sprinkle salt on at the end you really have to be generous. @American in Paris

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I despise bananas. What fruit could I substitute please?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I can't eat bananas. I'm thinking...mabye avocado?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Maybe sweet potato?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Claude apple sauce?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed recipe almost exactly except for: 1) reduced the salt to 3/4 tsp, 2) added a 1/4 c of high quality chocolate chips and 1/2 c of unsweetened coconut, 3) used pecans instead of walnuts on top. I think they’re good! Let’s see what our teens say.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these because I grew up eating healthy foods, so bananas, honey, oats, etc seemed ideal. For many recipes  I never measure precisely, and tend to approximate, in this case what was left in the jar of my favorite raw, crunchy, sprouted almond butter. As for salt, just a few twists of the grinder (I actually did not even look at the amount of salt stated in the recipe). I loved the recipe..had a couple bites soon after it came out of the oven and thought it was really quite good!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These weren't as awful as the comments may be, but mainly because I made some changes since I had doubts going into the recipe. I used three, not two, ripe bananas. I only used 5 TBS of olive oil and next time I'll cut that to 3 or 3 1/2 TBS.  Also, I used a pinch of Maldon salt both in the batter and atop the nuts; cutting way back on the salt. Only 4 TBS honey. I added a cloves with the cinnamon and about 1 1/4 cups oats, so it held together better. Next time, I think I'll add chopped dates.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I added chocolate chips and coconut and a third banana  - it was delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I forgot to add the oil to these. I had used a little more banana so they’re still very moist. I added chocolate chips and flaked coconut. They’re pretty good! A little sticky but tasty. I was worried after reading all the negative comments and happily surprised.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Believe the reviews that these have no taste. And they are gooey, not a breakfast bar.  Threw them out.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Maybe it would be tastier to sub 1c. of white whole wheat flour in place of the oatmeal & use only one egg.  IMO, Oatmeal is flavorless no matter what you add to it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Tastes very salty. Would cut that down.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I always read the comments first- what a mistake not doing that with this recipe! Throwing it away. Too salty, too bland.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @angie. Glad all I did was print it and read comments while doing so. It’s scrap paper now!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Replaced eggs with 2 tablespoons of flaxseed meal (grind 2 tablespoons of flaxseed and soak in 5 tablespoons of water for 15 mins), excellent egg substitute

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Did not like this...only NYT recipe that I haven't liked so far.  Too much salt..not great flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I added one finely grated carrot, which gave it a little more moisture and created a pleasant texture. My toddler is insane for these.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I accidentally forgot all of the oil in my haste. I  substituted pumpkin butter for the almond butter. I used overly ripe bananas  (I.e. blackened). I also used 5 TBSP of honey, and added the walnut right into the batter instead of on top. As well, I sprinkled chia seeds and cranberries  on top as well as a handful of semi-sweet chocolate chips. Overall, it is a little salty but not overpowering. The accidental omission of oil perhaps enhanced the other flavours?
There is noticeable banana flavour. As well, the incorporated chopped walnuts gives it some crunch. In summary , these bars now taste like a low-fat oatmeal “sweet and salty”  type of soft  granola bar.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these this morning and I thought I did something wrong because they were terrible.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I found the amount of oil to be very off-putting in the flavor. Too heavy, too sweet, not tasty at all. Bummed to have wasted all my ingredients on this. My kids didn’t like it either.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just made this recipe and wished that I had read the reviews first. It looked great coming out of the oven, but tasted terrible. The texture was bad, too. The worst NYTimes recipe I've ever made. Unfortunately, I had to throw it out.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Way too salty, probably would be good if the salt was reduced by 1/2 or more. Not sure if these are going to be eaten. Maybe a little bit on top of ice cream? Sad to waste all those ingredients.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I checked the comments about 30 seconds after adding the salt - and agree this is WAAAYY too salty. (& I like salt!) TBH I can’t give much more of a review as I kept adding in SO. MANY. things trying to cover that up. More banana. Yogurt. Applesauce. Almond flout. Craisins. Another egg to balance all that. I took a tiny piece to try and couldn’t finish it. So it’s going in the bin. 
Sorry NYT this was not a win! 
(I do think the salt issue may be variable based on the type of peanut butter you use - most have salt added so if you were using unsalted it might be ok).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Do not waste your time or the food ingredient costs. I followed this to the letter and they were literally inedible. The only pro: the cooked product looked delicious out of the oven. That's it ... that's the pro. the cons: incredibly salty (and I used no-salt peanut butter,) the honey scorched (as so often happens in baked recipes... but I went against instinct and followed the recipe,) the texture was atrocious (gummy, pasty, stuck to roof of my mouth but not in a good "peanut butter" way,) the bars somehow both squished and crumbled (gummy squish with the cut edges falling into little pieces) when sliced. I usually keep opinions to myself regardless of whether I personally enjoyed the recipe because, hey, we're all doing the best we can and tastes are unique and personal. But these were SO BAD, I am shocked they made it into the app.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Worse, they made it into a calendar with daily recipes!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yah I made this without reading the comments first. The texture is gross, and it is too salty. I would have vastly preferred making banana bread with my ripe naners rather than this dud.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Like Miranda, I agree that chocolate chips are a great addition!  I used light olive oil and because I used salted peanut butter, I cut the salt to 1 teaspoon and omitted the flaky salt. I also added 3/4 cup chocolate chips, used a full tablespoon of vanilla extract, and added in an additional 2 T of honey or sugar.  Without these tweaks, I found it to be a little meh -- not sweet, not savory.  But with these changes, it turns into a lovely gluten free, dairy free dessert!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I  followed the recipe exactly and had to throw the entire thing out. It was awful.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious and simple. Used 1 tsp fine sea salt and 3 smallish very ripe bananas.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The negative reviews lead me to believe that people were hoping to bake a treat, like banana bread bars. If you think of them as more like a Luna bar with ingredients from home, then you'll have a better sense of what to expect. I enjoyed them, but I can see how they wouldn't be for everyone. My husband despises walnuts, so I used salted sunflower seeds instead of flaked salt or walnuts, to good effect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thanks! I’ve wanted a guideline to make such breakfast foods. I don’t add any sweeteners beyond fruit, so i’ll remove honey and add in some soaked dried fruit

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Perfect gluten free recipe for soft bananas.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I wondered given the ingredients about the calorie count.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Bland. Neither sweet nor salty nor nutty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly, and I also found it lack in taste. I think the culprit is the peanut butter. Next time I will use a roasted peanut butter such as Trader’s Joe’s. Reduce the amount of olive oil, it prevents the squares from firming up.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Cal 388 cal

Protein 9.9 g

Carbs 30.3 g
Fiber 3.48 g
Sugars 15.08 g

Fat 26.8 g
Saturated fat 3.86 g
Unsaturated fat 13.6 g

Cholesterol 53 mg
Sodium 133 mg
Potassium 158 mg

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I like this recipe, it's a kind of cakey Lara bar. I would leave out the salt next time, i just wanted the sweet flavors.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I would add some raisins or other dried fruits chopped up and maybe some orange zest and vanilla extract.
I like the suggestions for applesauce instead of oil.  We’ve already got some fat via the nut butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just made this with defrosted frozen very ripe bananas. Delish! Didn’t have enough honey on hand, so added a half cup of mini choc chips on the top to sweeten. Also cut salt a bit. Delish!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Pretty bland, needs maybe more cinnamon or chocolate chips?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was overly salty for me, and I love salt. I will cut back to half a teaspoon of kosher salt next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed recipe almost exactly. Only deviation was I added 2 tablespoons of coconut flour. These are delicious and low in sugar. I put the walnuts on top with the salt flakes and that for me makes them a stand out snack.  I for sure will make again!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was JUST trying to find something for Bananas and oats this morning and how great was it that NYC cooking posted this :) We enjoyed it, I added a little bit of coconut sugar and chocolate chips instead of nuts based off comments and we enjoyed it! I think the only adjustment I would try next time is whizzing the oats in a blender to make it smoother (I've got a kiddo who struggles with texture sometimes). Otherwise it will be back on our bfast rotation!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used almond butter, left out the olive oil, used maple instead of honey and did one banana and the rest mashed roasted sweet potato. Increased the oats by 20 grams. Heavenly.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love these…but it helped to just barely toast the walnuts first. They add a lot of depth, and yes I’m getting the caramel notes and fudgy texture. Didn’t find them bland, but I also used frozen and thawed extremely overripe bananas. Maybe that helped!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Mine came out really salty. Otherwise, they were good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made earlier this week! My 7 year old loves them and I feel like there is plenty of flavor- definitely used old bananas. After days of her not wanting any breakfast I know she has some good energy for her school day.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were delicious and so easy to make! The bars have a perfect balance of sweet and salt, great texture with oatmeal, and the banana comes through just enough. Perfect with coffee, or as an afternoon snack. Bonus: my son loves them too. I subbed 4tbsp apple sauce for oil bc that's what I had, dropped honey to 4tbsp, used almond butter, and added 1/2c unsweetened coconut bc why not? Will definitely make these again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Don't skimp on the salt. It really needs it. I used peanuts on top due to tree nut sensitvities.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This was so fun and it tasted great, even after I completely screwed up the order of additions to the banana egg mixture. 

I threw all the next ingredients in to the bow except the flaky salt and it still ended up tasting great. I will do it right next time.

Easy grab for breakfast through the week. Win Win

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These have taste but they are pretty salty. I'd make them again, but with about 1/2 the same. Definitely would skip the sprinkle on top

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I added extra vanilla, halved the salt, and did half honey and half brown sugar, but more than recipe called for as many commenters said it was too salty and not sweet enough

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious! I used natural peanut butter so the flaked salt on top was the perfect finishing note.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are delicious if you aren't expecting a sweet breakfast bar. I wonder if the author meant to say "unsalted" peanut butter, because the amount of salt is way too much if you use peanut butter with salt. I have taken others' suggestions and am using part olive oil and part applesauce to make it less rich an a bit sweeter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I don't understand other reviewers complaints? I feel their bananas may not have been ripe enough or they they did not let the egg banana mixture sit long enough, because this was really good and nicely sweetened. Subbed the 4 tbsps of oil for 4 tbsps of apple sauce and reduced the PB to 1/2 cup. Would definitely make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: After reading the comments, I made the recipe as is and then made a blueberry sauce to pour on top after serving. I enjoyed it with the sauce and might make it again. A good breakfast recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used coconut oil instead of olive, just 1/2 tsp salt (skipped the flaked salt on top), and it came out perfectly! Will absolutely make again- super easy way to use up some browning bananas!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I halved the salt and cinnamon; I loved them. Will make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: good but a little oily for my taste in baked oatmeal bars.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Nice flavour and texture, but like others I found these a tad too salty AND too sweet. I’d recommend using 1/2 to 3/4 of the honey and only a 1 tsp of salt. It’s easy to overdo it with the sprinkling salt. To remedy this, I also sprinkled some chunky granulated sugar on top… sweet and salty! I used a mix of nut/seed butters (pistachio, sunflower and tahini) to spice up the flavours a bit. Peanut butter everything is so 2011…

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My family loved these! Based on other comments, I used unsalted almond butter, omitted the flaky salt on top, and added a handful of mini chocolate chips. Ours had just the right amount of salt, and they’re very banana-y. Maybe bringing the eggs to room temperature first made a difference too? Anyway, we’ll make these again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I read the Notes after the batter was mixed. Added raisins but the salt was already in. I ate it warm and thought it was delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I saw a lot of comments saying that there was far too much salt, but I used the full amount of salt, made the recipe as written, and did not find it too salty. I found that these were not very sweet at all, but I don't want a lot of sugar in the morning so I personally really like them and will be making them on a regular basis! 
The sweetness/saltiness also might vary depending on the type of peanut butter you are using, I use all natural peanut butter that contains only peanuts and salt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A few changed: I did half coconut oil / Half olive oil. I halved the walnut salt and put no flaky salt on top. I added 1/8tsp nutmeg, 1/8 cup raisins, and about 1/8 cup buckwheat flour. It was plenty sweet and not too bad!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I could not agree more with American in Paris. I cooked the recipe exactly as described - everything about the it  turned out heavy handed with a dull result, just like when you mix all colors and get a dull brown,  Pass on this one,  but if you must, reduce the peanut butter by half, use stone cut oatmeal and cook it 45-50 minutes (test at 40 minutes, as the reduction in peanut butter may reduce cooking time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I too cut the honey down and decreased oil to 5 T (and added 1/3 c chocolate chips).  To make as 12 muffins: oven temp to 375F, bake for 23-25 min.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were great! They're good for having on hand to eat quickly in the morning without having to rely on commerical granola or other breakfast bars. I did reduce the salt to 1 tsp and no sprinkle. Added 1/4 c mini chocolate chips but they weren't necessary. Surprisingly filling. Note - no flour in these!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these with a few modifications. I used almond butter (150 grams not the amount written). Added two large handfuls of chocolate chips but would add more next time (approx half cup). Was generous with vanilla. Skipped the salt on top. I was worried these wouldn’t be good given the reviews but my kids enjoyed them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these with a few modifications. I used almond butter (150 grams not the amount written). Added two large handfuls of chocolate chips but would add more next time (approx half cup). Was generous with vanilla. I was worried about these given the reviews but my kids liked them!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are great and tasty!!!  I used almond oil and Skippy crunchy peanut butter and made no other changes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I loved this, as did everyone I shared it with, followed recipe exactly except for only 1 tsp salt due to using salted almond butter, hits the spot. I cut in into 12 squares and shared for pre skiing energy, perfect and lasted until lunch.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Despite the harsh comments, I really enjoyed these! They reminded me of banana pancakes and had a lovely texture. I added golden raisins bc I had them and if I make these again I’ll add shredded coconut and maybe increase the oats. Thanks Sohla!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this recipe as directed and found it perfect - it is meant to be salty/sweet. Delicious. Especially the day after making. Highly recommend as a snacking cake.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I thought this recipe was actually great. I actually added 3 bananas (352 g), less oil, a few more oats (130 g), less honey,  and got spiritual with the salt. Because I am pregnant, I also added psyllium husk powder, lol. Delicious and fudgy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made these last night and kind of agree that they taste like nothing, but not in a bad enough way that I won’t make & eat them for a quick breakfast on busy mornings.  Some modifications I made:
-double batch in a 13x9 pan bc I had 4 bananas I had to use up (only had about 410 grams but was okay) 
-subbed plain Greek yogurt for ALL of the oil to reduce calories & add protein
-used an immersion blender for all but the oats 
-left out the walnuts, I hate nuts in my baked goods

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Agreed that these are far too salty as called for especially if you use salted peanut butter. Just a pinch is needed. Cut sweetener down to just one teaspoon. We enjoy them as a snack or for breakfast!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made a few changes, and it's yummy!

4 table spoon of olive oil.
3/4 cup of brown sugar (instead of honey)
1 tea spoon of vanilla
a pinch of salt

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these with peanut butter. My husband thought they were awesome, but the kids didn't like them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow, cannot believe that people find these bland! They are moist and delicious! Yummy PB flavor! Making again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 10 April 2023.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used this recipe as a starting point and added some spices after reading the other comments about blandness. Tossed in some almonds, sunflower seeds and dried fruit. Substituted flax "eggs" for eggs. The final result had a good flavor and texture, and could have used some chocolate.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
this does not taste like banana and it’s salty even though I skipped the salt on top 
I don’t mind the flavor but the olive oil makes it savory.  I would tweak this with less salt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I substituted coconut oil

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used half the olive oil, honey, and salt recommended in the recipe. Added plumped raisins and bits of a chocolate bar. Tastes good but definitely don’t expect it to be like a dessert.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm always on the hunt for a low-ish sugar option for breakfast with all the good stuff (bananas, oats, nuts) and this one fits the bill.  Delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Maybe use unsalted nut butter?  Way too salty!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These were just OK; 
Should have punted and made banana bread.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Good, super easy. I was a little weary given all the notes after i had already put this in the oven.  I added some coconut flakes, some mini choc chip, and mixed in the walnuts.  I was a little unsure of how soft this would be because the batter was very very loose.  I added a couple of tablespoons of gluten free flour and it came out great and very moist.  Easy to cut. 
Would make it again the same way.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I really like this recipe and will make again. I don’t think it’s bland like some commenters, has a nice banana nut flavor. In the future, will cut back on salt, 2 tsp is way too much. Will add extra nuts on top and maybe mix in some dried fruit (cranberry, apricot, or date).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: only needs 1/2 tsp of salt, otherwise salt is the only thing you can taste

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could you use prunes instead of bananas?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these today varied in two respects. 1st reduced the oil to 4 Tablespoons. 6 is too much with addition of the peanut butter or almond butter for all you fancy folks out there. The other I did was add another 1/2 cup of oats. 1 and 1/2 cup of oats overall gave more consistency to recipe and added a little more substance into the batter. This recipe firmed up nicely. I will say that I did not follow the ingredient sequence to the T and it still turned out.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I feel like this is some kind of experiment to test to see if people will notice when one ingredient in a list is waaaay out of proportion. But yeah, way too much salt, which is more annoying if you kinda thought that as you were making, but didn't trust yourself. I need more Martha Rose!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this for a 10x15 pan. Made no substitutions except obviously doubling the ingredients and adding pecans instead of walnuts. Bar baked beautifully but definitely salty like everyone else is saying. Reduce salt by half for sure!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I thought these were great. Added chocolate chips and next time will add hemp seeds and chia seeds. Wasn’t bland at all. Skipped the salt on top, based on others’ complaint of saltiness.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious, flavorful and super moist. Making them for the third time today to satisfy a craving.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
The salt ratio in this recipe is wrong! It should always be 1:2 kosher salt: fine salt. Making it as listed results in it being too salty.

Adjusting the salt, I love this recipe but think of it as a base to riff on. Definitely need to add nuts, dried fruit, seeds, chocolate chips, coconut, or anything else you have on hand that could go on a granola bar.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yes, these are WAY too salty.  Half or 3/4 of a teaspoon instead of two full teaspoons would have been much better.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made this with less salt and honey as many suggested. I also added various nuts and seeds to the batter which helped with texture quite a bit. Between the oats, banana, peanut butter, and nuts, this had nice flavor. I enjoyed these quite a bit. Reminded me of banana bread. 

I even made it a second time with homemade apple sauce instead of bananas. Less satisfying than with bananas. Next time I’ll try both fruits. 

Perfect homemade, healthy, fiber rich, grab and go breakfast

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed the recipe; it made a gooey, heavy bar. We didn't enjoy it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were delicious, but I'm glad I read the notes first. I reduced the oil to 3 tablespoons and did 4 tablespoons of honey, 2 of maple syrup. I added a few dark chocolate chips and did cinnamon sugar instead of just cinnamon. They came out great, but I imagine they'd be super greasy with more oil, so definitely reduce. They were super flavorful--I'm surprised so many people mentioned no flavor! Maybe the maple syrup helped.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are great. I riffed on them from the first, adding coconut flakes, poppyseeds, coco powder, skipped nut topping most of the time. A great base for playing around with. Does anyone know if we really have to let it sit? And if so, could they add that to the recipe?? My brain needs to know whyyyyy!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: the second time i made these i got it right. I pre roasted some almonds and cashews, and added a handful of top notch belgian dark chocolate chips for good measure. thank you Sohla, this is a keeper.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed the recipe but at the end mixed walnuts into the batter instead of just on top. Perfection! Mine were not bland; maybe the use of very overripe bananas helped.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I added store bought pecan pralines to the top 5 min before they were done instead of the other nuts. Would recommend!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Loved it! This was super easy and flavorful. Only two modifications: 1) I ended up with almost two cups of bananas because I had four going bad at the same time and 2) I added 1/2 cup chocolate chips. I didn’t experience the blandness (maybe the chocolate chips helped), the lack of banana flavor (maybe 1 cup as listed in the recipe isn’t enough), or saltiness (I like things on the saltier side so this was a bonus) that others commented on.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are incredibly salty - next time I'd only use a half teaspoon.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Remember to mix egg and banana and let sit. 
Reduce salt to 1tsp. 
Reduce honey to 1/4 cup. 
Add chocolate chips.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I only had fine sea salt, used 1/2 tsp after reading the comments. Plenty sweet and not too salty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I found these to be quite oily. Reducing oil, adding additional oats and some additional liquid would have made this a better overall recipe. Agree with those who recommend cranberries & perhaps skip the salt on top.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I loved these, with modifications based on other’s comments. I reduced oil to 5 Tbsp and salt to 3/4 tsp, with no added salt on top. I eliminated honey and added no sugar to recipe, but did add raisins. Put a mixture of walnuts and pecans on top. Came out beautifully moist after 30 minutes with a rich peanut butter/oatmeal taste. I will make these again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 9 servings at 391 cal. each.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: would decrease sweetener ( we used maple syrup) and decrease salt

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The nutrition info is shown in the drop down list located underneath the list of Ingredients.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Honestly starting to grow on me! Thomas really liked them too

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are great and Cecilia likes them too. Go with 3 T each honey and oil, and 1/2 t table salt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Way too salty will try one more time using unsalted nut butter, and maybe add another 2 tablespoons of honey.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Regarding feedback saying it’s bland — anybody try going to 3 bananas, maybe other nuts (pecans), etc.?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can one omit the olive oil altogether? One serving of this has more fat than my usual full day total. Would applesauce or flaxseed meal replace the eggs and the oil to further reduce the fat?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Agree with others that the olive oil is a strange addition to a breakfast cereal bar. It seems to drown out the banana flavor entirely. Next time I’ll use melted butter. And the addition of dried cranberries or raisins would add a bright bit of chewiness and sweetness

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made this but did not use any salt
Added 1/2 cup golden raisins, 1teas of ground chia & same amount  of ground flax seed
They are tasty, moist and delicious!!!
Nothing bland about this recipe 
Used 9x9 pan lined with parchment 
At higher elevation so i took it out after 30 minutes
It was crispy around the sides-easy to cut
Will make thus again!!
Thanks for this deliciousness

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: OMG!!! These will be addicting if I don't watch out! I used about 3 1/2 T of honey and added mini chocolate chips. I used half the salt and it seemed like enough for me. Not bland at all...I really loved them!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly as written and they turned out a soft mess, even after 45 minutes in the oven. The tester came out clean but they just wouldn't set. There is nothing to bind them other than the oats, and they don't really bind anyway. If I was to try them again, and I won't, I'd add a half cup of flour.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow!  What a range of reactions!  Same for my household. Made the recipe exactly. Shared with my husband who told me nobody would ever ask for the recipe. I was neutral on the result, questioning if I had undercooked it. Rereading the intro, the word “fudgy” jumped out. Yes!  These are not like coffee cake or granola bars. Since I will not waste ingredients or my effort, I have been eating them each morning or for a late afternoon snack. They satisfying and curb my appetite. Will make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were delicious - but too salty. I would halve the salt next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These worked well for me with some alterations, subbed pb powder for the peanut butter, used chocolate chips in place of honey and added some apple sauce.  Also def half the salt!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These aren't as bad as some of the comments suggest. That said, I think if I make 'em again, I would use either butter or veg oil in place of the olive oil, and omit the flaked salt on top. Maybe add a couple of tablespoons of flour? Other than that, I liked the peanut butter/banana/oat combo.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Loved this. Reading the other comments, I bumped up the spices- powdered ginger, and a bit of cloves and allspice. I have trouble with honey and used maple syrup instead. It tastes delicious. I can taste a hint of banana, but not so much the peanut butter. It’s hefty and just right for breakfast or a solid afternoon snack.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious, but I’ll use half the oil next time. Plenty of moisture with the peanut butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious! Dimensional and filling, perfect with afternoon coffee.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Used maple syrup. Misread directions and mixed in nuts instead of putting on top. I used slivered almonds. They were super moist and almost too sweet for my liking.. one day in Tupperware they were almost bread like, moist.
My toddler and i devoured them as snack and breakfast bars. Cooked them again 3 days later just to have in the freezer!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love these. Froze most of them and pull them out as needed. Swapped 4 tbsp apple butter (homemade) for 4 tbsp oil, replaced honey with 1 tbsp maple sugar, and added ¼ cup each coconut flakes, carob nibs, and dried cranberries. Used defrosted frozen bananas. Baked 30 minutes. Very hearty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just made these. They are really good. I followed the recipe as it is published. They will be the perfect breakfast bar. For the honey I used Hero’s Orange, a filtered orange honey.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was expecting more crunch on the exterior portions, they were tasty enough. I am a novice baker, but even I thought that 6 tbsp of olive oil was too much.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed the recipe and these turned out a little salty and bland to my taste. That said, it’s an easy treat to make and enjoy. Might try adding chocolate chips and less salt if I make them again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not sure about all the negative comments, I quite liked these! We reduced the salt by half per suggestion and added crushed pecans and chopped toffee on top instead of walnuts. It was delicious, I couldn’t stop eating them!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If this recipe was updated on January 17, 2024, what were the updated and how will the update impact the comments from last year?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a great breakfast bar, versus a desert. It is everything that people are commenting negatively on… salty, not sweet, etc. But that makes for a lovely breakfast treat. I made mine with almond butter and added a bit of unsweetened coconut. Perfect. Next time I may try to add some protein powder too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not sure why there are so many negative comments. I used 2.5 very ripe bananas, subbed 4 T of applesauce for 4 T of the oil (still used 2 T oil, but I was also worried about the high fat content), used almond butter, and added 1/2 cup of chocolate chips - and they came out delicious! Both my 6 yo daughter and very particular husband loved them. Tasted the bananas, didn’t detect too much salt, and loved the fudge texture. This was a win for us! Easier way to use up bananas than banana bread.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were delicious!!! I couldn’t stop eating  them. I used natural peanut butter and no walnuts, followed the recipe. It’s also a perfect and nutritious snack if you’re breastfeeding :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A lot of the flavor of this bar comes from peanut butter. Does your peanut butter have salt? Is your peanut butter already sweetened? Does your peanut butter have your favorite PEANUT taste? Do you prefer other nut butters? Maybe you should use your favorite ___ butter in this recipe. Same with bananas - did you use seriously ripe banana that smelled strongly of banana flavor? I substitute out honey for a preferred syrup combined with solid coconut oil (melted). Use FRESH/raw walnuts for flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These definitely tasted better on day 2 as flavor deepened. I used less salt and added chocolate chips on mine.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are very good. The negative reviewers may have been expecting something more like banana bread, which these are not. We tried them because we need an alternative to our morning oatmeal routine. Used maple syrup instead of honey, toasted pecans instead of walnuts and added raisins as some reviewers recommended. They are not overly sweet, but are delicious with a cup of coffee and berries for breakfast. The nut butter adds a protein punch and keeps hunger away until lunch.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these last night and ate this morning, honestly they are yummy and super moist!  I followed the recipe almost exactly, using unsalted peanut butter.  I did cut the salt and still found them salty.  So I would definitely cut the salt in half, more if your nut butter has salt.  Added just a few chocolate chips on top instead of walnuts because that’s what I had.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed verbatim.  Not flavorless at all, rather the taste of peanut butter and olive oil, which while not unpleasant, wasn’t pleasant.   Maybe it was the brand of peanut butter. Too many other recipes to try versus working on modding this one.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Yes, I agree with everyone else, 2 tsp salt is way too much; one tsp is plenty. Next time, I'll use less olive oil and add some sugar or maple syrup. 
Pumpkin seeds on top in addition to the walnuts is nice too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Disappointing recipe. I cut the honey to 4T, significantly cut the salt and probably did half the nut butter. Had a long cook time, baked for 40 minutes- edges were browning almost too much but center still wasn’t done. Taste was okay but too oily. If I make these again I will try applesauce and cut the oil. Took this recipe out of my recipe box. Agree with others, effort and ingredients weren’t worth the recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Way too sweet. Probably don’t need any honey, let alone 4 TBs. Maybe sub in some unsweetened coconut.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Adding some love to balance out all the haters here, because I LOVE this recipe. It’s definitely not for everyone, because no, it’s not sweet — it’s salty-sweet-savory. But if you recognize the ruse that banana bread pulled off by convincing millions that it is not a cake but a breakfast food, and if you also would prefer your breakfast more on the savory side than the sweet, then this is the overripe banana recipe for you! It’s become my weekday breakfast of choice.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used a combination of peanut and almond butter, out of necessity. Overall there was little flavor except oat and salt, and I omitted the salt on top. Just ok.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I added nutmeg and cloves and cranberries and chocolate to enrich flavor.  Yum!  Pecans worked better than walnuts.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I did not have Diamond Crystal so I cut the salt back to 1 tsp.  I subbed in avocado oil because I had some I wanted to use up, cut the honey back to 100 grams, used a natural peanut butter (only dry roasted peanuts and salt), used very ripe bananas and cut the squares into 16 instead of 9.  Both my husband and I thought these were pretty yummy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not sure what all the negativity is about. My husband is allergic to peanuts so I made the bars with almond butter. Followed the rest of the recipe as written. The bars came out moist and flavorful. Perfect for a quick breakfast.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This isn’t banana bread - it is more of a moist peanut cake. Made as directed, a bit leery of all the oil and salt. Very oily, salty peanut flavor, but not the worst thing I’ve ever made. Agree with comments to reduce oil and salt, maybe add some chocolate chip. Use neutral oil instead of olive oil. Maybe mix the nuts into the batter next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed the recipe and used almond butter. I am surprised at the comments as I found the bars to  be very tasty, although I did not taste  the almond butter. It is exciting to find a desert recipe with no flour or sugar which are inflammatory ingredients. I used to be a pastry chef and LOVE baked goods. But I  prefer to concentrate on healthier foods the majority of the time . I plan on making  this recipe again and experiment with persimmons and apples.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Omit salt except 1/4 tsp in batter.  Add 1/4-1/3 c dark raisins.  Use smooth peanut butter vs crunchy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this as is but added chopped chocolate for a topping. It is greasy. It is sweet. It is salty. An absolute perfect pre x country ski breakfast or as a pre meal for any other long haul sport. Will keep engines running all morning.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Made a few times & a hit in our house with changes culled from others:
Added 1/4 cup oats, dark chocolate chips and pressed some toasted coconut into the top before baking (to keep it from falling off).
Reduced oil to 2 TBSP, salt to 1 tsp, Maple instead of honey. (Also, peanut vs almond butter weirdly didn’t taste much different and flax eggs vs real with no detectable difference.)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 4 bananas over ripe

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly. The texture threw me off. It's like banana bread. I was expecting it to be more firm.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were delicious but salty. The recipe called for WAY too much salt so I cut it down to 1 tsp although next time I’ll only use 1/4 tsp. Sprinkled chopped walnuts on top.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are easy to make and are great to have available for breakfast all week. Followed the recipe except used maple syrup instead of honey, almond butter, and used only 4 TBSP oil (I was worried my almond butter was too oily). Mine came out a bit dry so next time would use the full 6 TBSP. For anyone who thinks the banana flavor is lost - make sure to weigh your ingredients, my bananas were small so I ended up needing 3 (and still only got to 220g).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this yesterday and my husband I I polished off two bars each this morning. Neither of us understand the hate for this recipe. I halved the honey and added nutmeg   cloves, and left everything else as written. Filling, tasty, not too sweet.  Will make again. Travels well for breakfast on the go.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We thought they were amazing! Reduced salt to 1/2 tsp and added an extra 1/4 cup of oats. I don’t know why people are saying they have no flavor—I thought they were full of flavor! This recipe will be on repeat for us.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I liked these but the consistency was less cake than I'd hoped.  The flavor was good but somewhat bland, neither sweet nor salty.  It tasted too much peanut butter so perhaps 3/4 c peanut butter is a bit too much and the almonds made it very nutty.  I wouldn't rush to make it again but I agree that adding chocolate chips would give it nice flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these pretty much to the recipe with the exception of cutting back on the salt by half as well as the honey. I also mixed the walnuts in the batter so added some extra on the top after noticing my mistake. I did not add salt to the top. One thing I should have taken into consideration was that my peanut butter had no sugar. The texture was fine but as others have commented they were boring. I will make again and use the original amount of salt, perhaps use agave syrup-lower GI than honey.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Added cinnamon sugar to the top at the end instead of salt, and also added chocolate chips to the mixture. Turned out really good and my friends liked them as well. Good gluten free option.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Pecans substitute
325 convection  fo 45 min

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: While I don’t hate these, they are not a favourite. I read comments after I made them and have to agree that there is too much salt and not enough of a dominant flavour. If I make them again I think I will add dried fruit and maybe stir in some extra nuts.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Anything with cinnamon I quadruple the amount. I halved the honey and used hemp sead oil. Without my extravagant use of cinnamon I can see how they would be bland. I love the idea of adding chia. Overall, I'm a fan!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This is the second time I have made this recipe. The first time it turned out perfectly and it got amazing raves from friends.  It was moist and dense like a brownie but banana bread instead.  

To adjust the protein/carb/fat ratio I made the following changes:
4 egg whites for the eggs
Mixed nut butter for the PB
1/2c vanilla protein powder   1/2 c ancient grains GF flour. No oats
No honey or sweetener.

The batter tastes incredible so far.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Based on other comments, I decreased the oil and the honey - but I stirred in about a quarter cup of chocolate hazelnut spread. : ) No cinnamon, so I added a dash of allspice and about a half teaspoon of ginger. Husband just went back for another piece.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was nearly discouraged from trying this recipe by all the negative comments. But I really like these bars. They’re not sweet, which is exactly what I want in a breakfast option. They have heft thanks to the oats and peanut butter. And a nice crunch and nuttiness from the walnuts. The salt complaints, I would guess, come down to Morton vs Diamond Crystal (or other brands) — I had no problems using Diamond in the amounts recommended. I’d make this recipe again and happily enjoy it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wasn't sure what to expect from this given the split between the 4-star overall rating, and a number of negative comments, but tried it anyway, and quite liked it! Mine came out tasting of both bananas & peanut butter, with a very soft consistency ("fudgy," as described). Easy. Good recipe to have on hand for using up ripe bananas. Made exactly as suggested except I used cashews on top because I was out of walnuts.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I should have read the comments first. Even after trusting my intuition and cutting the salt by 1/4tsp these were inedibly salty. That's the only flavor they have, actually. Somehow, the saltiness was enhanced over night and we had to throw them out. This is the third time now I've made a NYT Coking recipe that called for ludicrous salt. What's the deal? Going to try again but omit the salt except for a topping and add some chocolate chips. I think there's a decent recipe here it just needs help

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I reduced honey to 4T and threw in a bunch of mini chocolate chips. Then mixed the walnuts in with the batter and topped with sliced almonds. Delicious

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Too salty. Next time I will cut the salt in half and maybe add some chocolate chips as some have suggested here.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My son doesn’t like peanut butter so I subbed tahini. Came out great!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I read the notes first, and then made the following modifications: used a combination of almond butter and peanut butter, cut salt by half (used sea salt though), doubled the vanilla, used a mix of cardamom and cinnamon, cut the honey to 3 T, and added less than 1/4 cu dark chocolate chips.

Smells wonderful, tastes good.  It did not come out oily.  It would have been even more flavorful if my bananas were a bit riper.  It’s not my favorite baked good but it’s tasty and a lot healthier.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These aren’t bad, but two tablespoons of salt is way too much, and I like my baked goods with some salty flavor in them. A bit bland (used almond butter, not PB), and if I hadn’t added chocolate chunks I don’t think I would have liked them. I also used a very fruity olive oil with great flavor, but the other ingredients (mostly the chocolate) drowned it out. If I did it over, I’d use more banana, half the salt, peanut butter and less expensive olive oil.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Recipe calls for 2 tsp, not 2 Tb of salt!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These weren’t bad but neither were they great. The flavors are muddled, the texture heavy. Meh.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I liked the idea of this recipe (whole grains, healthy oils, natural sweeteners) but it fell short of my taste expectations. I have made this twice and I agree with other reviews that say the bars are bland. The first time I followed the recipe. The batter was thin and the finished bars were oily. I switched to sunflower oil and cut it back to 2 tablespoons the second time, but the bars were a bit dry and again the batter was thin. I'm an experienced home cook who started baking in the 1970's.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these today despite the poor reviews, and they came out fine. I decreased the salt, and  also used *unsalted* almond butter. I suspect many of the reviews saying the recipe is too salty might be because folks are using salted peanut butter. I decreased the honey to 4 Tbsp and added about 1/4 C of hemp seeds. I also used a hand blender to combine the mashed banana and eggs; chunks of banana would make the bars "claggy" as one review put it. Overall, a nice flour free breakfast item.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I thought the flavors of this were good. I skipped sprinkling salt on top based on the comments and subbed the cinnamon with British mixed spice that I had leftover from another recipe. My biggest complaint is with the texture. It's very loose.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So simple and easy to make! Came out super fudgey and moist. Great healthier alternative to banana breads with loads of gluten and sugar!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I accidentally left out the salt (except the flaky salt on top) and thought they were great! Added some bittersweet chocolate chips too. Delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this before reading the reviews. I think it depends what you expect…it tastes good with unsweetened unsalted peanut butter and is moist and breakfasty. It’s not a cake or dessert. Glad I trusted my instinct and halved the salt but otherwise followed recipe to a T. Not sure I’d run out to made it again but the reviews seem overly negative

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These tasted better than expected based on the reviews but the texture is very craggy. Wouldn’t make again. Somehow too sweet and would be much too salty with full amount of salt

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: To me, the most prominent flavors here are salt and peanut butter. I used weight measures to make this, and my bananas were ripe. I used Morton’s kosher salt, and my peanut butter was homemade with lightly salted peanuts. I left the flaked salt off the top, and this was still too salty for my liking. This isn’t something I need to rush to make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I found this recipe unusual! The bars work well for me as a substantial, nutritious and tasty portable breakfast. They hover in an interesting space between sweet and savory, leaning toward sweet.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I read the reviews about it being too salty and bland. So, I left out the salt, was a little heavy handed on the honey, let the bananas over-ripen, and used pumpkin spice instead of cinnamon for extra kick. It turned out great- very fudgy, sweet enough, and dense/filling. I think I could up the spice by a half teaspoon- but it’s very good as is.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Messed around with the recipe due to comments about bland flavor. Added some greek yogurt, chocolate chips, and extra PB. Cooked closer to 55min. I love that there is no flour/baking powder in this. Feels more substantial for breakfast and less of a mess on my counter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a great base recipe that is easy to enhance to your personal taste.  Try adding fruit like blueberries or tart cherries (buy packaged dried, then rehydrate).  Additional spices like ginger or cardamon are also good.  Adjusting the quantity of rolled oats and/or adding the chopped nuts directly to the mixture will change the texture of the bars.  I enjoy them with morning coffee right from the freezer and thawed just enough to eat with a fork.  Yum!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Added  the chocolate chips as someone suggested! Really liked them!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I cooked with recipes from the NY Times all the time, some recipes have not been entirely to our liking but as a whole they are really good. This recipe particular recipe was terrible, barely eatable, tasted too much like peanut butter and it was so bland that I threw the whole thing out in the garbage.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We were disappointed.  All the ingredients sounded like they would work together, but nope.  Lesson learned, read the notes/comments.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Supremely disappointing. Crumbly, too cakey, salty, little of the sweetness I’d expected, nut butter and banana didn’t come through, even the oats were lost in the breadiness. I MIGHT try it again, but this batch is like eating some kind of medicinal, eat-your-vegetables blah.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Agree with the less than glowing comments - not worth the calories to eat something lacking in taste.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very salt forward. No to little banana flavor. Not much peanut butter flavor either. I used salted PB.   The recipe didn’t specify if it should be salted or not. But the added salt was a lot. So keep this in mind! Add more banana, half salt and unsalted PB in my opinion.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These came together really easily but the flavor was off for me. The peanut butter dominated (in a bad way) and the texture was more cake-like than bar-like. There was little to no banana flavor and the salty-sweet elements almost canceled each other out.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Note to self: go easy on the salt here. It’s not like a brownie or a choc chip cookie where the more salt the better, banana things can be too salty very quickly

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
3T olive oil, 3T applesauce
6T peanut butter, 6T applesauce
1T date molasses, 1-2T honey
0.3 cup extra oats
2.5 bananas
Add handful of raisins and walnuts in the batter
Added walnuts & sliced almonds on top
Spices: cinnamon   cardamom   nutmeg (very little)   pumpkin pie mix

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I was looking for a baked breakfast that wasn't too sweet, but this actually turned out a little bit too sweet for me.
I made the recipe close to written (subbed maple syrup for honey), but my bananas were WARM (about 50°C - I needed to defrost them). I can see why it'd fail to sweeten if your kitchen/eggs are too cold. Try microwaving the bananas to lukewarm before mixing in the eggs.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I got halfway through the recipe before reading the comments and unfortunately there was no going back. I decided to make these with the following changes: no salt, 3medium bananas, 3 Tbsp. Chia seeds, 1/4 cup of coconut flour, 1/4 cup unsweetened shredded coconut, two cups oats, and 1 Tbsp. Unsweetened Cocoa powder. I didnt add any nuts since im making these for my 1.5 yo daughter. Theyre delicious and i’ll be making them again. :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: After reading the comments, I made some modifications. I added raisins, and chopped crystallized ginger. I also cut way back on the salt. I added a little extra cinnamon, some cardamom, extra vanilla, and peanuts to the top. It really helped add some flavor. Not bad for something with no flour.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I liked this recipe- I read the comments while it was baking and got nervous, but I liked that they were a bit more salty than your usual baked oatmeal square. The banana adds moisture, more than flavour- and mine has a strong peanut butter flavour. Perhaps helpful that I used a high in sugar peanut butter. Not sure if they’re in the regular rotation, but it definitely wasn’t a waste and enjoyed. No subs needed and baking time was true for my oven.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed recipe exactly and it’s way too salty but with that being said, I still wouldn’t like them with less salt. Sorry but this is a no for me!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Dawn how much and what kind of salt did you use? I see the recipe offers two options for kosher vs table salt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a clean recipe but lacks in flavor.  I would try again and consider adding some chocolate chips and possibly coconut to add some depth and contrast.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are...just wrong.  The lack of a leavening agent leaves them raw in both texture and flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Is there eggless version on this ??

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Well, I'll make this again for sure! If you can accept it as a breakfast cake instead of a dessert treat, it fills the bill nicely. 

My changes, after reading comments: reduced the honey by 2 tablespoons, halved the salt, increased the rolled oats to 1 1/4 cups, and added 1/4 cups of whole wheat flour. Used almond butter.

It took 45 minutes in a convection over to test dry in the center, but I'm at 4600 feet elevation, so maybe that's why it needed more time in the oven.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Too salty

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: How can I make this vegan?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Swapped dark brown sugar for the honey and they were very flavorful - texture like a brownie. Whole family enjoyed for breakfast.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: so blah tasting, I threw the whole thing away

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Had some bananas I needed to use up - can confirm like others that these are not tasty! Find another way to use up those bananas.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not for me, sticky and not in a good way. I agree they looked nice when cooked but who cares about how they look if the taste is not there. Going back to banana bread.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Whoops. Forgot to say I reduced the salt to 1/2 teaspoon regular salt and grated some rock salt over the top...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Too salty!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I do not recommend this recipe. 
Way too salty and bland flavors.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I don’t understand all of the negative reviews here; these were excellent. Subtle spices and a nice spongey/fudge-like consistency. The only changes I made were using just one teaspoon of salt (which was the perfect amount), and I lost count of the tablespoons of honey so it may have only been five. I also didn’t measure out the weight of the bananas and may have ended up with a little extra from two regular size bananas. Next time I might try adding some shredded coconut or a little more oats, but it doesn’t really need any changes. I have been enjoying them for breakfast all week.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yikes. These are so bad. How do they have four stars? What a waist of ingredients. Entirely too salty. All I could taste was the salt and peanut butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed the recipe, except didn’t add the extra  salt on top and I added 3/4 cup chocolate chips. I love the flavor and didn’t expect the texture to be like a moist cake. Absolutely delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These have no flavor, don’t bother.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I think they are great.  I’ve made with almond butter and then I did half pb & half almond, I also added hemp seeds which made it a little more cake like.  Overall, I love them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is an easy-to-make breakfast bar. I used butter instead of olive oil, crunchy peanut butter & no salt. It's quite dense and sweet. It would be a good snack before or after exercise, or pack for hiking. I would like to adjust it so that there is more protein. Currently, it's slightly oily. However, it's very tasty and filling.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: They're not bad. I didn't add as much salt and added shredded coconut. I actually kind of like them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made it first with peanut butter, and then with almond butter. MUCH better with almond butter. I found it too sweet and, well, peanut buttery with Jif.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could this work with cashew butter?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sadly I have to agree something is wrong here. Perhaps the walnut can’t withstand the heat for that long? It was barely done inside but also burnt tasting; all ingredients were fresh so I don’t believe it was quality control.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These worked out for us! Added a third banana and halved the salt but otherwise followed the recipe and the texture and taste were good. I used the weight measurements for the ingredients, not sure if that played a role in why this worked for us versus the commenters who had a bad time with this recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are fine, not great not bad. I left out all salt since I don’t like much to begin with. Added chocolate chips which is great. As people said, this just lacked a strong flavor. Nothing shone through. I’ll eat what I made but won’t make it again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was not my favorite recipe - granted I believe I undercooked it slightly but the flavor was not great to me. I couldn't taste the PB at all - they were just super blah.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I almost didn’t make these because of the negative  comments. I’m glad I decided to take a chance on this recipe! I used natural peanut butter and 1 tsp of fine salt. I forgot the flaky salt.

I made sure not to skip the step of letting the eggs and bananas rest after mixing. These bars were rich, moist, and just salty and sweet enough. The peanut butter flavor really comes through.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Reading the comments on bland, and realising I was using natural peanut butter, I added the salt and half a tsp of baking powder to the banana egg mix before letting it sit. Not bland at all, and I can definitely cut down on the honey next time.

Good way to use up the random half packets of nuts I have lying around

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just awful. Only flavor was salt and I didn't even use as much as the recipe called for. Threw the whole thing away. Not even sure how there is a 4 star rating.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If you regularly cook from sketchy recipe blogs, you’ll like these! If you have a modicum of skill or taste, you’ll think these are shockingly bad. I’m in the latter camp. Love Sohla and many of her recipes, so my cognitive dissonance is almost as unpleasant as these disgusting bars.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I think the flavor and texture here is actually pretty good (I used natural crunchy peanut butter and overripe bananas that had been frozen and defrosted). They are so so salty, though. I thought it was my error - that I had accidentally used 2 tbsp’s of salt instead of tsp’s, but no. Made it again triple checking I was using the right measuring spoon and they’re still so salty. 

I’ll try one more time with half or a quarter of the called for salt…

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Perfect recipe - don’t listen to the haters. Use ripe bananas and follow the recipe exactly by weight. The sprinkle a little sugar on top along with the salt because we deserve nice things.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Unfortunately I did not read the comments before I made. Love the ingredients so figured what could be bad. Followed the recipe and agree it was tasteless!! The salt even made it worse. 
Wouldn't bother doctoring. Just don't make!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I’ve made these several times and really enjoy them. Some things I’ve learned:

-using almond butter will yield a LOT less flavor
-account for the sodium in your peanut butter brand and adjust salt accordingly 
-only use 4-5 tbsp of honey 

I love these for breakfast with yogurt!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these at the beginning of the week and read comments while they were in the oven and thought I’d made a grave error. I have enjoyed having something easy to grab that’s low sugar and high fiber for breakfast. I even pop it in the microwave for 10 seconds before consuming. I did measure by weight and used Teddie salted creamy peanut butter. I also used quick oats, as that’s all I had on hand. I don’t think they’re bland or waste of ingredients as many have stated. I don’t know if they’re a staple for me yet, but could be! Was a nice way to use up some ripe bananas that wasn’t banana bread or freezing them for smoothies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Tasteless. Would not make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sorry I didn’t read the reviews before making these. When the smell of them baking nauseated me I realized they were not going to be good. Way too salty. Kind of a slimy texture and the taste is really unappealing. Very nutritious ingredients but they just don’t go well together.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: There’s no nice way to say this… but these were so gross. I took one bite and immediately threw the whole thing away. I should have just made regular banana bread.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: read the comments and thought "how bad could it be?" it's not terrible...but not great? even after halving the salt, the top flavor is...salt. wouldn't make this again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I find if I cook the elderly bananas a bit before hand for any banana bread type recipes, I don't have an issue with too much moisture in the finished product.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We thought they were very yummy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Had read the comments prior but gave it a shot. There's little flavor, if any. Each ingredient seems to cancel out the other and you are left eating texture. Echoing others, I hate to waste thus am dripping maple syrup and pairing with a hot cup of dark coffee.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are delicious and have lots of great stuff in them.  Flaky salt on top is an important part of the recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: After reading the comments I almost decided not to make these, but I am glad I did! I followed the measurements and instructions (with the exception of the vanilla and cinnamon--I just eyeballed those) and they turned out well. I like the banana flavor and the crunch from the walnuts. The peanut butter flavor is subtle, but present. I think chocolate chips would be great in this as well. I'm really don't know why it turned out so differently for others!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We LOVE these!  Followed the recipe exactly, except for the walnuts on top.  Used almonds instead.  I will say the key is the Kosher salt and using the weight measurements (thank you for those!).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
We enjoyed these and will make again. Met needs to use overripe bananas and reduce refined sugar intake. It’s not dessert, but IS a satisfying, dense kind of bar good for breakfast or after workout. Makes me think of some protein bars I like - The ones that don’t pretend to be a candy bar…. 

I had accidentally bought almond butter with honey (Justin’s), so reduced additional honey a bit to account for. And my bananas didn’t weigh in quite as much as called for. Didn’t taste them specify, but I’m sure added some sweetness. 

If ur looking for a sweet treat you won’t like these, but if looking for a healthy snack, these are good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I agree this is too salty but those who are finding it way too salty might want to check the ingredients of their nut butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just made this and it's beautiful! Yes, bake twice. Do not skip the longer drying step: that will generate the support necessary to hold the frangipane on the top. I would suggest slicing the sides of the dough off with a very sharp knife so the whole thing puffs up evenly. Mine was more pillow shaped bc I didn't do this.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I doubled the frangipane and I have no regrets

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If you make the frangipane in advance and store it in the fridge as directed, make sure you bring it to room temperature before spreading it on the puff pastry.  The cooked puff pastry is quite delicate and it tore easily while spreading the filling. This was not much to look at, but at least it was tasty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So, so impressive looking and delicious. I inadvertently browned the butter, but used it anyway. Yummy goodness. Other than browning the butter, I followed the recipe and served my family flaky almond love. One caveat: the recipe says serves 12. It serves 4. Or maybe only 1, if you don’t want to share.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Haha, kinda agree, 4-5 generous servings

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
A traditional almond croissant is fully baked, split, filled with frangipane/almond cream, then baked again.

Frangipane goes in the middle AND on top. It shouldn’t burn. The frangipane has to bake through and will prevent the pastry from burning at the same time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe needs a visual or better guidance on how long to prebake the puff pastry. I did as instructed, all-butter pastry, 10 minutes at 425, then 22 at 350. At that point it was puffy with an even dark golden color. When I later went to split and fill it, it had the consistency of a cracker. It was so dry and crisp; it shattered as I cut it. The final product was edible but not croissant-like; edges tasted singed. Also, 1 tsp kosher salt was too much; I’d use 1/2 if making again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Try hazelnut spread, jam, marmalade, lemon curd…this is versatile recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I wondered, since my husband is allergic to almonds. Thanks!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Absolutely delicious, but you MUST double the filling! Trust me, you'll want it. I found it easier to spread by using an offset spatula.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
The following changes, also recommended by other users are a no-brainer:

- Make 50% more filling
- Definitely cut the pastry in eight before the pre-bake (it will be significantly easier to slice the pieces in half. Try re-freezing the rolled out sheet for 30 to 45 minutes before cutting to prevent edge compression
- Use a pastry bag to fill the inside and top of the pastry - so much easier! The "strips" you're pastry bag dispenses will blend together and flatten once baked.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I loved this recipe! I followed it exactly and it worked perfectly. I used the recommendation to cut the ends of the puff pastry prior to cooking (thanks for the tip!).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You want almond flour that is blanched, not bleached. This means it’s made from blanched almonds, with the skins removed so it’s a uniform light color. If it’s made from unblanched almonds, it’s usually labeled almond meal and is speckled brown in color. Blanched almonds, finely ground in the food processor, are a fine substitute.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The second time we made this, we added some chopped dark chocolate on top of the filling spread inside and on top of the outside and it was even more amazing!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I cheated. I needed to feed hungry bellies quickly and still wanted to do this recipe. So I want to the market around the corner who makes good-enough plain croissants. Bought a few then went home to start at Step 2. In about 45 min (including trip to the store) I had delicious half-homemade almond croissants. Everyone loved it. 

If you’ve got a need for speed, this hack can work nicely.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @coco genius! Thanks for the tip. Could work in a pinch.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The pastry is baked first, filled and baked again? Seems like puff pastry would burn on the second bake. I know from using puff pastry sheets they bake and burn easily. Why not use two sheets and bake with the filling?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Confusing recipe. I agree. What? Cook twice?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The frangipane filling spread edge-to-edge prevents the outer edges from burning. The ratio of pastry to filling would be wrong if you used two sheets.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Julia, I just finished the first bake; my pastry puffed up to about three inches, so one sheet really is what you need.  I was worried that it would be difficult to slice a large, thin sheet, but it is no longer thin.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The first time I made this I followed the recipe exactly and, though it turned out perfectly, it was too sweet and too salty.  I've made it again today, reducing the sugar to 1/4 cup and the kosher salt by half.  Much better!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I cut the puff pastry into 8 pieces and shortened the baking time. Double frangipane. And used a high quality puff pastry. 
When I made it as one big square, it was so dry and crumbly that slicing it made a big mess.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It's very good. Kudos to Gardner for suggesting to trim the edges before baking. I substituted ¼ teaspoon of table salt for the Kosher salt. Next time I'll skip the flaky salt on top.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can we get the ingredient proportions for the 2 variations shown on the video, please? (Great video btw)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I left the puff pastry in the fridge overnight to thaw, but even so, when I unfolded it, it broke/tore into three pieces.  I didn’t try to mend them, did the first bake and put two away in an air tight container. I proceeded with only one of them, and it made a perfect amount for a small treat for our family of four.  Made the second one a couple days later (warmed the frangipan for about 8 seconds in the microwave first) and it was just as yummy.  The third will be a hostess gift today.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was concerned after reading comments but went ahead. Glad I did - delicious. No issues with crumbling. Made sure my butter was soft, I chilled overnight - 12 hours, used a sharp knife. Sliced easily and baked to perfection. They were attractive and delicious. (I also used volume measurements and didn’t adjust anything)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I trimmed the edges of the pastry sheet and cut the sheet into four equal-sized pieces before the first baking. Much easier to handle, cut, and serve. I cut each piece in half and put the top half top down and spread that with filling. Then I put the bottom half top down on that and spread the rest of the filling on the top layer of pastry, which started as the bottom. Made for uniform looks. Delicious, easy to make in advance. It's in regular rotation here.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I did not prick holes in the puff pastry before baking and it rose to high in the oven. This made it difficult to spread the frangipane on top and it collapsed. If you’re not used to working with puff pastry, suggest pricking a few holes with a fork before baking to avoid this from happening.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this today as written. It’s is fabulous. Just take the slicing slow with a good serrated knife. All in all, it is very forgiving and if it over-flakes in a spot, who cares. The same is evident in the photo. My favorite kind of rustic dessert. Don’t skip the salt on top if salty-sweet is your jam. Thanks, Sohla, for another winner!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Are the recipe amounts for the chocolate hazelnut version from the video listed anywhere? I'd like to try that one but not seeing the recipe. The classic almond came out perfect...what a treat. Thanks!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used about 2 cups of hazelnuts and the same amounts of sugar, butter, etc. as the almond recipe. I did have to add a little water (scant quarter cup) to the hazelnut paste because it was too thick to easily spread.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For anyone that struggled halving the puffed pastry, cutting it width wise into thirds really made filleting the pastry much easier once cooked.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is delicious but VERY salty. I’m making it again tomorrow (it’s that delicious!) but will use salted butter and omit all salt in the recipe. Otherwise, I would suggest using unsalted butter, 1/4 tsp. table salt, and omit the kosher and flake salts.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used rum instead of vanilla and it ended up having a really strong rum flavor that I didn’t enjoy. Go for the vanilla option unless you’re a big rum ball flavor fan!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It was the height of the holidays and the store was out of puff pastry sheets, but they did have individual shells in packs of six. I pre-baked as directed in the recipe, then carefully removed the center "plug" added the filling, closed it up, any extra filling on top and "Bob's your Uncle" It worked great!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I like the idea to cut before the first bake. That was my problem: flakes everywhere!! Cut while warm? Running into the almonds creates a problem too. The extra salt over top was — over the top. Try again! Like the  reviewer who used shells!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This has quickly become one of the most requested desserts/breakfast treats I’ve ever made for the handful of mouths I delight with my kitchen’s creations. 
I agree - double the recipe for the filling and use a heavy hand with the powdered sugar! Mini chocolate chips are a great addition to sprinkle inside the two layers before the second bake!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I toasted the almond flour in a skillet for a couple minutes to deepen the flavor; watched it closely and let it cool completely. I also used brown sugar instead of white granulated for a flavor boost… not sure it makes a difference. 
I used salted butter, the full amount of kosher salt recommended, and a slight sprinkle of Maldon salt on top — really nice recipe! Wasn’t exactly like breakfast in France but will be pretty enough on my (North Carolina) brunch table. :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
