commentBody: Fabulous. It would probably have been better had i used almond extract instead of vanilla, but I didn’t realize I was out of it until I started. I, too, doubled the filling but did not double the salt and sugar. With the confectioners sprinkled on top at the end, it was fine. I used Bob’s Red Mill almond flour. I’m pretty sure it wasn’t bleached. I made the filling first and it sat out until the pastry was ready and it was easy to spread.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Have to wonder how many times web chefs have to make something until it's ready to show. I followed directions. It looked right but collapsed in minutes, before slicing/filling. Dufour says that means it's underbaked. Baked some more, top blackened. Collapsed again & more as I sliced/topped it. Looked nothing like hers. Tasted great -- used mac & pine nuts along with almonds inside & out, & orange marmalade inside. Note to self: do not eat while sitting in recliner; add more marmalade next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really easy recipe! Was in a hurry and wanted a smaller amount for a quick breakfast treat…Instead of splitting the pastry, I rolled it thin, added the almond mixture, folded over the puff pastry and baked it all in one shot.  Used vanilla instead of rum, and omitted the confectioners sugar and added salt.  Delicious!  Some of the mixture oozed out and made a yummy cookie.  Will definitely be my go to for a quick breakfast pastry.   Looking forward to trying it with apples and walnuts.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This was so easy and so good. My puppy thought so too, as she grabbed a piece off the counter and happily trotted on in to where we were eating, carrying it in her mouth, and then stopping, looking at us and slowly realizing that wasn't the best plan. :)

Pretty much just followed the directions, except that instead of dark rum I used bourbon that I'd soaked vanilla beans in for two months.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made one of these yesterday and another today.  I follow the recipes to the letter.  However, as written, IT'S WAY TOO SALTY!  And this from someone and friends who shared it like salt.
One suggested leaving out the salt all together and I did.  What an improvement!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
My family loved this!  Made it for Christmas morning and again for New Year’s brunch.

The second time, I added slices of ripe pears and crisp apples on top of the almond paste in the middle. Terrific.

I also tried trimming the edges of the puff pastry before the first bake.  The pastry puffed more evenly, although not as high.  It was easier to cut, too.

All in all, this will be in the repertoire.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It’s just that easy! Hahaha you’re so cute, you’re my new favorite. I CAN’T WAIT to do this for Mother’s Day! Thanks so much.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this, but with a mix of pistachios and almonds (2:1 ratio), and all in a food processor. First ground the nuts really finely, then added the sugar, butter until creamy and blended in eggs and vanilla and almond extracts, to form a smooth paste. Much easier and faster. And it was delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Mine came out too flaky and almost burned. I think the bake times are too long. I might try again with adjusted bake times.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Tried this with houseguests, they couldn't believe how fun, delicious, and interactive. Especially loved the video, this recipe is an instant go-to. Thanks Sohla! On the downside, it took me an extra twenty minutes to fall asleep because I had variations running through my head.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This turned out just like the picture and was delicious!  It is somewhat time-consuming, so  I recommend at least baking the puff pastry beforehand.  I made almond flour by putting whole almonds in boiling water for 1 minute, then ice water til cooled, and slipping off the skins.  Then I put them in my food processor until the almonds were ground to sand-like-sized particles.  It took about 2 cups of whole almonds for the recipe.  Spread can be made early, just wait to add the egg.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have this in the oven right now on its second, almond-filled bake. I have no doubt it’s going to be delicious but geez, did anyone else have trouble spreading the filling on the top with the amount given? I will most likely bake this again and will definitely double the filling recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this for my wife for Mother’s Day, and it was a hit!  Having satiated my sweet tooth with an abundance of almond croissants over many years, I felt that I had the upper hand of experience to deliver the goods. The secret ingredient however was the buttery and delectable Dufour puff pastry from Bronx, NY. I followed the package instructions and convection oven baked at 350 F, cooled, divided in two, spread the filling and top with frangipane, assembled the top, sprinkled a small scattering of Maldon flaked salt and copiously covered with sliced almonds. The second bake again at 350 F took about 20 minutes. The result was absolutely spectacular, rivaling the best almond croissants I’ve ever had in Montreal! Best of all, I made my wife happy! A keeper for sure. Enjoy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was fabulous, although mine certainly did not look as beautiful as the one in the picture! The layer of puff pastry was very crackly and hard to separate neatly. I will add that I also thought there was too much salt. Half as much would have been plenty, and yes, I used Diamond kosher salt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Doubled the frangipane filling as others suggested and cut into 16 pieces for a brunch. Otherwise,  didn’t change anything. The filling and pastry together were divine. It got rave reviews!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Would love the recipe for the hazelnut filling Sohla does in the accompanying video.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Using the same Dufour puff pastry shown in the video, I found that baking it at the higher temp initially just led to a burned top. Baking at 350 for 25 minutes  led to a nicely browned, fully baked puff pastry.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: After watching the "how to make" video, I realized the Pepperidge Farm puff pastry I had to use (live in a rural area-only frozen puff pastry available) was way smaller in size and would not bake up nearly as thick as the one used in the video.  I used both PF sheets, stacked them and baked them.  Supper easy to split in half like a bagel!  I doubled the Frangipane.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You can’t say “puff pastry any size” and then expect the filling to be the right amount. I used a 14-inch puff pastry—probably 14X10”—and should have doubled the filling recipe at least.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: THIS is the most amazing recipe! Favorite party trick going forward... can't wait to try the ham and cheese for bowl games!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Five stars because giving something one star for semantics is silly. Also it’s good and easy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This was a success using tips from previous posts.
I used a sheet of Dufour puff pastry (14 oz.)
I did not prick/dock, and baked till dark golden
I had/used slivered almonds, ground, and made 1½ of the frangipane, which I piped on.
I used ¼ tsp. of salt in the filling, none on top.
After the first bake, the pastry was thick enough to slice easily. The top layer did collapse a bit in the process, but I don't think that was a bad thing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this as directed and it was wonderful!  I did not have almond flour but did have almonds, so I blanched one cup of almonds for a minute, removed the skins and then pulverized them in a food processor until the pieces were similar to sand.  Other than that I followed the directions.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Amazing! Doubled the filling and added 70% dark chocolate inside, it balances the frangipane filling perfectly. Will divide into 12 pieces next time, 8 was too large. 10/10 would make again to fill a crowd, I’m thinking Boxing Day brunch.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could one use pistachio cream instead of almonds? I have a jar and don’t know what to do with it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made two of these for a brunch today and they were delicious! I followed the recipe as written. I’d recommend cutting into 9 (or less)pieces instead of 12. All that cutting destroyed the delicate pastry. Also, when spreading the frangipane over the cut pastry, I found that pressing down slightly with the spatula and not spreading back and forth helped keep the pastry from shredding in the process. Definitely a keeper!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I came across this recipe and thought I would make it right NOW IF I had all the ingredients. I found one piece of puff pastry in an opened box in the back of my freezer. It’s probably 5 years old. Also found an ancient bag of almond flour, the kind the recipe says not to use. Amazingly, a tiny bottle of almond extract. My puff pastry sheet seemed smaller than the one in the video, so I made the frangipane and it was the perfect amount. The puff pastry was barely defrosted. My almonds were in an opened package I found that were mixed with dried cranberries—-it was a salad topping.  I baked it for a little less time. This thing is delicious. I will admit I’ve eaten half of it myself. I can’t wait to make it again with fresher ingredients!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Dufour, Dufour, Dufour! Pricey but worth it

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Awesome.  Use a quality all butter puff pastry such as Dufeur or Publix Greenwise brand.  Trader Joe's brand is seasonal.  Definitely cut the puff pastry before baking the first time, made it easier to cut in half and fill.   Watch the baking and adjust the time to prevent overbaking.  Added a tiny bit of almond extract to the frangipane.  Next time I will warm the frangipane to make it easier to spread inside and on top of the pastry, and add some shaved dark chocolate on the inside.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You have to split the baked dough.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sounds delicious and so pleased to have metric measurements included. I’m assuming that almond flour is what we in the UK call ground almonds, ie very finely ground almond meal, not actual flour?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yes, same thing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm probably going to be making this, sounds nice, but I'm aware that the marzipan mix will be very stiff - and as a pp has already noted, it tears up the delicate pastry if used from the fridge. Thinking it'll be like trying to spread cold butter on fresh white bread?  I routinely warm butter in the microwave for 10 secs to get it to a spreadable consistency so the bread doesn't tear. Maybe doing this will work for this too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I already have a bag of almond flour (I assume not bleached) that I rarely use, so I don’t want to buy a bag of bleached (assuming I could even find it).  If it makes the frangipane too stiff, as the note warns, what would be a good way to loosen it?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Is there a reason that one has to mix by hand, rather than with a hand or stand mixer on low?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Roughly what dimensions was your uncooked puff pastry before you baked it? Ours (I'm in BC, Canada) comes in small-ish blocks and needs to be rolled out.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Yes.
Some of the recipes I use frozen puff with ask that it be rolled flatter.
I didn't do this, and it's puffed to about three inches.

With only the top-view photo, I can't tell if that's what's wanted here.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I wonder if you could use Solo brand almond filling, which I love.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is delicious! I followed the recipe exactly. Baked the puff yesterday, then pulled the frangipane together, spread it and baked it this morning. It makes a dramatic entrance! So perfect with coffee or tea.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I did as directed and the first round of baking had the pastry coming out light brown. I was able to cut easily and fill, but putting it back in the oven, the edges were too crisp and burnt to eat a second time, and I cut the baking time in half to 10 minutes. The inside was delish, just the edges were not edible because too crispy. Maybe turn the temp down from the start.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
So insanely delicious. I was skeptical having pre baked the puff pastry and the filling. I thought it would turn into a crumbling mess but the filling/topping are magical and turned a crusty pillow of pastry into a soft and delicious breakfast that was devoured in an instant. I did have to warm up the filling as mine was straight from the fridge but 20 seconds in the microwave was perfect. 
This will be a staple for brunches and holiday breakfasts.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I realized that it might be easier - and would save more buttery flakes of crust - if I sliced the cooled puff pastry into 3rds, where the fold line was. The horizontal cutting was much easier.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Easy to make, however, it is very salty for those who are sensitive to that taste.  I will nearly eliminate the salt the next time.  The croissant was best after sitting, i.e., on the second day.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I could only find the puff pastry cups and it worked beautifully! Might have even been less messy because there was no reason to cut before serving

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Don’t give up! The puff pastry had an uneven puff on first bake, making it hard to slice “bagel style”. We put almond filling on the top, layered the sad pieces on top, and flipped back upright. The pastry leveled out on second bake. A beautiful finish and all’s right in the kitchen. For inexperienced pastry baker, I wasn’t expecting the weird shaped first bake to even into a lovely finished product.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This tasted fabulous, but aspects of the recipe were challenging. For one thing I found it almost impossible to cut the pastry without it crumbling, especially the top, let alone spread a filling on it. Also I felt like the filling was a bit skimpy in quantity, given that it was supposed to be spread edge to edge. However I had just made some almond paste, so I added some of that to fill in the gaps. In any case the result wasn't gorgeous, but it was absolutely delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Would be nice to have some extra filling for the top. After using 2/3 on the inside, it was a stretch to, well, stretch the filling to cover enough of the top. Also need to let us non-pastry cooks (speaking for myself) know that the puff pastry is going to collapse and flake as you spread more on top. The picture shows this perfectly with thin slices of the finished product.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was incredible. All the stores were out of puff pastry - but it turns out it's no harder to make puff pastry than pie dough. When I sliced the baked pastry in half, it was a little too moist on the inside so I popped it, open-faced, into the over for 10 min and it was perfect. Filled it, baked it, devoured it. YUM! Can't wait to experiment with other fillings like Nutella...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The recipe works well and  yields an impressively tasty and visually appealing return on a minimal time and effort investment. The amount of salt in the filling is a little excessive and I would cut back to a half teaspoon next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Double the amount of filling --> twice as good :-)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Found Trader Joe's butter puff pastry to get unpleasantly dark at these temperatures. Also, next time will almost refreeze it after rolling pastry sheet, and cut into 8 pcs. for easier handling.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A simple recipe that yielded delicious results, but could use a little adjusting. I would make 1.5x the filling next time -- I found the recipe as-is only barely covered the top and middle. I would also bake just slightly less than 'deeply browned' on the first pass - my puff pastry grew shattery after the second bake and although delicious, was a total mess to eat. Nevertheless, dead easy to put together and even suboptimal results were tasty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Much easier to buy in a pastry shop.  Our first try with splitting the first baked pastry (ha ha "like a bagel") was a total disaster. Just a big bubble flake on top. I was forced to lay on a new layer of puff pastry over the Frangipane, and bake for another 20 minutes.  The result was almost edible. The amount of salt in the Frangipane is way too much, basically ruining it for me and the people to which I served our Frankenstein version of this recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I've made this 4 times.  I don't add any salt, and after the first time I skipped the powdered sugar.  Wonderful recipe, so easy, and 
 appreciated by everyone whose eaten it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Doubled the filling.  Heaven.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sohla made variations (chocolate hazelnut and ham & cheese) in a video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wvt0TkhO1CI

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The language in the recipe is a bit confusing, but there is a very helpful video of this on NYTimes Cooking Youtube channel, including two variations - not sure why it is not linked in the recipe: https://youtu.be/wvt0TkhO1CI

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just made this: it's delicious and fairly easy to make. I thawed the puff pastry on the counter at RT, by inverting it as Sohla showed in the video, and it didn't break at the seam. In terms of ingredients, I followed other people's advice and doubled the almond paste, while cutting the sugar in half, i.e. 1/2 cup sugar, 1 1/2 cups almond flour, 2 eggs etc. I added a very thin layer of nutella under the almond paste, and sprinkled some shaved dark chocolate on top. Sweet enough for my taste!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My six year old asked, “can you make this everyday?” Enough said. It was beautiful, easy, and yummy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was amazing. It looked like a professional pastry chef made it and it tasted better. Really easy too. I am a convert to puff pastry. Crispy, soft, sweet, salty, crunchy and aromatic. What a flavor bomb. I will use this at my next fancy dinner party for sure.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'll add to the chorus of those saying the filling can be doubled! My friends went wild for these things -- they were very popular and so delicious. I also used a mixture of rum and vanilla in the frangipane and was NOT sorry!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Easily adapted to accommodate vegans. Delicious and beautiful! Used individual puff pastry shells (accidentally vegan) and made the filling vegan with Miyokos and Just Egg. Served with fresh raspberries. A lovely dessert.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could you use fresh berries or preserves for the filling or add them to it?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If using two pastry sheets (Pepperidge Farm 17oz. Pkg.) doubling the filling recipe seemed to be necessary. The recipe specified “any” size puff pastry sheet, and I was not sure if one or two would be enough, but I am a a novice baker.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe is dead simple, crispy/chewy buttery almond deliciousness. Watch the video if the instructions don't make sense. Made 2x frangiapanne and used about 1.5 in the croissant - will find a good use for the yummy remainder. TJ's all butter puff pastry (which I had in freezer from the holidays) puffed beautifully. Though it cracked when sliced horizontally, the filling glued the pastry back together on final bake and no one knew or cared.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Impressive results for so little effort, great for brunches or out of town guests.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is similar to a Galette des Rois, which has almond frangipane sandwiched between two layers of puff pastry, but is baked only once. Why is this baked twice?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can this be baked and filled, baked again and stored for consumption later in the day or the next morning?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Absolutely. I baked this is the early morning, kept it at room temperature for a few hours and then warmed it again for about ten mins at 300 and it was perfect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Our raspberry patch is overflowing this year. I added ripe, falling-apart berries along with the frangipane filling the last time I made this, and it was transcendent! It was also so pretty on the plate!

Use all-butter puff pastry, if you can find it. The difference is profound—enough so that I may try to make my own next time. Pepperidge Farm puff pastry just doesn’t do justice to this charming recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was the perfect thing to make with friends. Very little equipment and concentration needed so we could chat and drink tea and then have a beautiful and impressive and delicious snack by the end.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Loved this even without doubling the frangipane. Thanks to Joe for the suggestion of using a piping bag - I think we would have destroyed the puff pastry had we tried to spread it. Skipped the finishing salt and thought it turned out perfect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
The comment about using a pastry bag was brilliant.   I used a plastic bag with a corner cut out.

I cut off the top horizontally, but I fear I cut too high.  A good bit of the "flesh" of the top became part of the bottom, leaving a hollowed out very thin shell on top.  I cut the top flesh from the bottom, set it aside, put the frangipane on the bottom, and then manually placed the flesh of the top over the bottom and then place the "top shell" on top.

Will use more frangipane next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Has anyone tried a savory filling like ham and cheese?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: She does a ham and cheese variation on the video.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What is a good brand of all butter puff pastry.  I think I will have to order it from somewhere as all we have in our stores in town is Pepperidge Farm.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Dufour puff pastry is amazing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I found all-butter PP at Trader Joe's. The sheets are small, so rather than one large sheet, I made two and cut each into four pieces.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could you please provide the measurements for the Hazelnut Chocolate variation? Looks fabulous!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My puff pastry puffed in one corner but the rest stayed flat and cooked solid like hardtack. It was thawed in the fridge and went straight into the 425 oven.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly, used Pepperidge Farm puff pastry. The trick is to thaw slowly before you unfold it. The video really helped, this was so easy to make!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Made for Xmas brunch-very easy and festive. Family loved it. I agree w/others comments to reduce salt--and doubled the filling.
Also made the ham and cheese variation.
Thanks for this festive fun recipe

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Doubled the frangipane, could probably 1.5 it? Would add a little almond liqueur to filling too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this last year for a New Year's brunch that we've decided to make a tradition! It's on the menu again by unanimous consent! I followed the recipe as is and it was great. Professional chefs in attendance said they wouldn't change it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used a store bought puff pastry (store brand) since they didn’t have anything else, it came out terrible! A BIG cracker! I knew it immediately while it was baking, next time I’m going to search for a good brand of puff pastry!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very good, easy.  Would leave out the salt on top, halve the salt in the filling.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: How do I get the quantities for the chocolate hazelnut variation that Sohla shows in the video?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My friend and I made two versions of this recipe and hosted a breakfast for some other friends - we  ALL loved this giant croissant.  We made the almond croissant according to the recipe and it was fabulous.  I decided to try a different filling. I baked the croissant and filled it with a hommade vanilla custard and strawberry jam.  I didn't do the second baking, obiously.  We can't wait to try the chocolate version.  One piece of advice though is to use the all-butter puff pastry.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The first time I made this the puff pastry was too high and crackly and it was too salty. The second time I halved the salt, doubled the frangipane, and spread it evenly over the two halves of puff pastry and sprinkled with almonds, then baked them as separate pieces. They are more almond squares than croissants, but far easier to cut, eat, and store. I will make them like this from now on. They are so delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: After eyeing, rereading this recipe, the comments and watching the video for months (obsessive much, yes), I finally made it yesterday. Previous tips, especially trimming edges (Gardner, thank you!) were key in my case. My Dufours pastry had sat in the freezer for a year+ and I couldn’t find a best by date on the packaging. When I opened it, the edges were a bit dry, so trimming was particularly relevant. Even though my pastry’s age was suboptimal, loved the results and will make this again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I baked it for 30 minutes after turning oven down to 350 degrees.  Used all butter puff pastry.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was wonderful. Made double the frangipane, which was a bit much for the sheet of Pepperidge Farm puff I used, but it was still terrific. Stayed very good for a few days.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have made these a lot in the past year.  Recently, I decided to cut the sheet into before baking, trimming each side of 8 pieces.  The result gives a totally different taste, level of crispness and height to the tart.    I understand the reason for the giant tart idea, but now I prefer cutting up the sheet. I made a hazelnut Nutella tart ( go easy on the Nutella) and the almond which I love.  But can’t wait to try other variations!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this for Mother's Day and made the filling the day before as suggested but it didn't work out.  The filling was too thick and hard to spread.  I would recommend more liquid (a second egg?) and letting it get to room temp before trying to spread it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I also found that the first bake time for the puff pastry was way too long. Even cutting it down, mine ended up overly browned. I doubled the filling because it was too salty (I did miss the note about kosher salt). Tasty ingredients save the day, but I would take a different approach if I actually tried this again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What size puff pastry is this recipe for? I grabbed the only one available at my store, but looking at the video it appears to be half size? Is there a standard size for puff pastry or does it come in multiple sizes?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Question About Puff Pastry she used Dufour? Con be bought at WF TJ etc!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: How can I substitute almond paste in the frangipane? I think I can just look up a recipe for frangipane and use that. But ideas are welcome

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Family loved this! But too salty. Next time 1/4 tsp salt & no flakes on top. Keep an eye on your puff pastry-ours browned much quicker than recommended bake time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
DELICIOUS. Made it for Christmas breakfast and again for New Year's. Would suggest bumping the frangipane up by at least 50%, or double for almond lovers. I trimmed and scored the pastry the first time, and didn't the second - I don't think it's necessary.

And as always, it's helpful to watch the video.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Super tasty! I would just add a bit less salt next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So easy & delicious! I’ve made this recipe once a week since I discovered it. It works with gluten free puff pastry and stays fresh for a few days. My family loves it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Fantastic crowd pleaser and "easy to pull off"as advertised. Sohla El-Waylly, well done!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I wish the exact ingredient list including amounts were listed for the chocolate hazelnut and ham and cheese variations.  They look so good but I need to know how much hazelnuts to buy and if I need to buy both raw and roasted hazelnuts and how much ham and cheese to use.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The recipe says that any puff pastry will do but unfortunately the one I purchased did not work for this recipe. Like another commenter, I followed the instructions but my pastry barely puffed and hardened to the consistency of a cracker well before the cooking time was done (10 minutes at 425 and another 20 at 350). The almond filling however was delicious so I smeared it on some halved day-old croissants, baked them for 25 minutes (along with the sliced almonds) and enjoyed the results.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Great and easy. Loved by all on Mother's Day. I look forward to making this again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My partner had to be at the airport at 4AM on Mothers Day, so I legit whipped this faux croissant up in less than an hour! Divided it up into two cute boxes and had a wonderful addition to the hanging baskets I had bought my Mom and MIL.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Great recipe, though color guideposts would be hugely beneficial. 

Thanks!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was great! Already shared with a few other New York Times cooking aficionados. Made this for a brunch birthday party and was met with rave reviews. Cooked the puff pastry and mixed up the frangipane a day before assembling and baking (yes you bake it twice - like traditional almond croissants!), and it was super easy and delicious! 10/10

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Has anyone made this with a pumpkin filling/topping?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’ve made this several times and love it. I use gf pastry (Simply Wise in Australia) and use one sheet that I cut into 4 before the first bake. It’s such an easy and delicious way to once again be able to enjoy a pastry that I’ve missed for years!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is kind of a better idea than it is a recipe or result. I feel a little foolish for thinking it would taste like something more than puff pastry with frangipane (which in the recipes defense is exactly what this is) but it didn’t. The texture was just off for me. Too flaky and messy and a pretty mid flavor. There’s also not enough cues to know how long to do the first bake. I think you could read this recipe and have equal chance of an under or over baked result. Maybe doubling the filling as everyone says is the move but honestly, I’d never make this again. It just made me want an actual croissant. And yes, I used the good all butter puff. Just an underwhelming result for something so pretty in the photo!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My comment is not about the recipe itself (though it seems like a wonderful idea and I look forward to trying it). My comment is about the way the salt measurement is expressed. Why on earth would you give measurements for different kinds of coarse salt, which yes, we know vary in their crystal size, but not one for regular table salt? The whole business about salt has become so very complicated lately, with what seems to me as a snobbery against table salt in cooking. WHY?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! I used the Dufour pastry dough which made this even better.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Has anyone tried this recipe using unbaked, frozen puff pastry instead of the pre-baked? Wouldn’t the puff pastry puff no matter what the filling?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe already calls for unbaked, frozen puff pastry. You bake it twice, once as a full sheet, next after splitting it in half horizontally and spreading the filling inside and on top.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I think after slicing the cooked pastry, you flip them so the browned side is on the inside. it shouldnt burn then, maybe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: No, that is incorrect. The filling goes on the inside, (not on the browned bottom), and the top is placed over the filling, browned side up, and then spread with the remaining third of the frangipane.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Okay, holiday baking pals, I'm hoping to tah-dah! the family with this one on Christmas morning, but I have questions. In Step 3, do we spread frangipane on the "outer" halves of the pastry (the upper part of the lid and the underside of the bottom part), or do we spread on the "inner" half of one (exposed only after splitting) and the "outer" part of the other? I'm crossing my fingers for a video showing Sohla's method.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Spread two-thirds of the frangipane on the inside of the bottom half. Then cover with the top half and spred the remaining third on top of the puff pastry.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: do you mean one thin sheet, or one pack? one sheet seems really wrong!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: puff pastry, not phyllo

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: One package that already has the butter between the sheets.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The recipe says one sheet.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
So no filling on the bottom half at all? 
“Flip the top of the pastry over, then spread about two-thirds of the almond filling evenly across the bottom of it, taking care to spread it all the way to the edges.”

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Looks like you spread filling on the underside of the top piece, so it's like the peanut butter in a sandwich when you flip it back onto the bottom piece.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Or spread on the top of the bottom half. You want layers of (on the bottom) pastry, almond filling, pastry, almond filling (on top).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Is there a workaround if I don't have almond flour?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If you have blanched almonds, grind them with the sugar in a food processor.  This will prevent them from becoming greasy.  Or use other nuts and do the same.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: No almond flour here. What can I use instead??  Ground almonds??

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have some almond flour that I rarely use, so if it’s not bleached, which I assume it’s not, I really don’t want to buy I bleached (assuming I could even find it).  So what would be a good way to loosen the frangipane if it makes it too stiff as the note says?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You're looking for "blanched" not "bleached" almond flour.  If you can't see small bits of brown in your flour, then the almonds were blanched (that is, the skins were removed). You would have to add more liquid of some sort.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: How well would this turn out with a blitz puff pastry rather than a store bought frozen puff pastry?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Blanched almond flour is not available in my area - possible substitutions?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: At what temperature is the second bake?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Step 3: 350 degrees

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: How thick should the puff pastry be before putting it in to bake? Here I can only get puff pastry in a block that has to be rolled out before baking.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can I just use a can of Solo Almond filling instead of making the frangipane?  Anyone?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: probably but it won't taste like the recipe version

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can this be made with chocolate and, if it can, what kind?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I would recommend getting the Pastry sheet. They smell delicious and I'll see how everyone likes them. A little too dark for my liking but nothing powdered sugar can't fix. Merry Christmas!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I don't understand this direction in Step 3. How can you rotate the baking sheet without spilling the topping?
"Bake until the nuts are toasted and the topping is brown, rotating the baking sheet once during baking, 20 to 25 minutes."

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You just turn the pan slowly, like a record.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Rotate horizontally, so that the side of the pan that is closest to the back of the oven ends up closest to the front of the oven. Do this slowly and carefully, and nothing will spill.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It was ok. Not too much effort to do as I was baking it whilst doing other Xmas prep which is good, but the puff pastry on the bottom ended up a bit dark and hard for my taste. Frangiapane is always tasty but I wouldn't bother cooking this again

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Judy - I made it with chocolate chips on 1/3 of the of the pastry puff and put the almond mixture on the other 2/3. I put sliced almonds only on the chocolate side top, with the almond mixture and almonds on the other topside. But sides were delicious!
What a great recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Has anybody tried this recipe with commercially available gluten-free puff pastry?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just tried with gluten free and it is a half inch tall after a full bake. Don't recommend.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed the instructions but trying to split it length-wise, it just kept flaking and breaking. Finally cut horizontally and used half in top. Baking it now. Could you double the filling and use two sheets? I might try that next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This turned out lovely! Dufour pastry box said 325 on convection, so when it was time to lower the temp I lowered to 325 — when the oven hit that temp, I turned on convection. Puffy, sturdy with a nice fluffy inside.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed recipe but did cut off a tiny bit of the edge all the way around as suggested.  Also, popped the once cooked pastry in the fridge for about an hour before slicing in half.   Used some sea salt I got in Hawaii for the flaky salt on top.  Delicious!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My husband requested this for Christmas morning and it did not disappointed. Groans of happiness were heard all around the table. It was surprisingly easy to make just as written. Highly recommend!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sohla for the win! Loved this. If you haven't seen "The Big Brunch" be sure to check it out. It's a lovely show (think "British Baking" in tone). Sohla is the tough love judge of the three and you'll definitely learn a thing or two.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Dumb question:  What size baking sheet is this for?  I’m assuming half-size, but I want to make this for a quarter-size sheet.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: depends how big your puff pastry is

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We made homemade puff pastry (King Arthur recipe) and separated dough, baking on two different pans. Watch time in oven; ours would have burned. It’s delicious. Not two sweet. A Christmas keeper.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Amazing!  We made it for Christmas morning.  It was beautiful, smelled amazing and was absolutely delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This is awesome, easy and fun

It took much longer to brown than specified, but was worth the wait

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m in the midst of baking this. My puff pastry is about five inches high. I did not poke holes in the pastry dough….was I supposed to!? I’m still forging ahead but wondering how this will turn out!?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It turned out great. It was easy to cut in half since it was so high.  Once I put it back in the oven with the frangipane inside and on top and started baking it again, the middle sink in so it ended up looking more like the picture. Everyone enjoyed it and it really is very easy to make. I will definitely make it again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I think of this as more a mille feuille than a croissant, which is a yeasted pastry. I decided to add raspberries to the interior and serve it as Christmas dessert. My puff pastry developed a steeply slanted top which broke under the weight of the frangipane, so I ended up mixing frangipane and puff pastry crumbs and hoping the almonds would hide the problem. Tasted good, but wasn’t very pretty! Next time I might make my own puff pastry, even though it’s so much work.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A Christmas miracle. Agree with comments about groans of happiness and fun to make. (Make your own puff pastry? Your call.) I made the first bake of store-bought pastry and the frangipane two days ahead of time, assembled and final bake day of. It is beautiful and scrumptious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made exactly as is, came out perfect and was a huge hit. Any imperfections in baking/slicing/filling didn’t matter at all, one of the easiest showstoppers I’ve ever made!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I baked as directed and ended up with a pillow. I should've known that puff pastry needs to be pierced in some way. If you don't, you end up with a big shattered mess when you try to spread the topping on.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I scanned the recipe, rather than read carefully, and that led to a time crunch. I see now how easy it is to partially bake, and even fully bake in advance.  The crispness lasts, so this recipe is fully dependable if cooking for an event.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
My husband called it "Christmas in your mouth"!
Truth be told, i did put all the filling in the middle and did and egg wash on the top.  Really good, but if you do that, there's a good chance that when you cut the pastry into pieces some of the filling will run out, making this easier eaten with a fork.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made it with organic gluten free almond flour, the only kind I could find. It did become rather thick and hard to spread. I heated it up a little to soften it a bit, but it helped only a little. Next time I’ll try adding more melted butter.

If you ask me, you can easily double the amount of filling in the recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Super delicious! The only sub I made was using monk fruit sugar instead of granulated, and it tasted great!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This tasted fine, but trying to cut the baked pastry in half while not letting it crumble to pieces is nearly impossible, as is trying to spread the thick frangipani on the brittle pastry. In this respect this was, well, a recipe for disaster.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really good and very easy!  My future edits:  Will use 1/8 of the filling and much less brushed on top.  Other than that, delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was incredibly easy and very tasty.  Made it Christmas morning (did the frangipane 2 days earlier, then let it come to room temperature before trying to spread it).  I wouldn't change a thing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Making this for the second time. I love the texture and taste. One question, however. When the oven temperature is reduced from 425 to 350, does the pastry puff stay in the oven or should it come out so it doesn’t get too brown?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It looks like it stays in for another 20-30 minutes. The 425 is to brown the outside and get it puffed, and then the additional time at 350 for the first bake is to dry out the inside!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This turned out so well. I made a half batch of the puff pastry from Baking with Julia.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very easy to put together. Quite delicious, will definitely make again. I agree with a previous comment on reducing the salt in the filling, next time I will add 1/2 tsp.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made as directed but my puff pastry (TJs all butter) was square and not the oblong the photo shows.  Given the smaller size, there was too much filling for my taste.  Next time I’ll roll it out a bit longer if the pastry will permit.  Or I’ll try with PF pastry.  It was, as previously mentioned, a pillow.  Nonetheless, it was delicious and I’ll play around with the concept.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: One word: Amazing

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this with phyllo dough, because that’s what I had. Worked well, but would recommend brushing top and sides with melted butter before first bake, and sides before second bake.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: One more note...would dispense with the salt on top. This amazing piece owns "sweet." Don't recall any salty flavor from any such croissant in France...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just made this this morning and it is amazing! No changes needed whatsoever (except you’re going to wish you made two!) Loved by all!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed the recipe with two exceptions — I used vegan puff pastry and I doubled the filling recipe, so I’d have plenty, plus leftovers (which I baked alongside the pastry in a ramekin). Delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I couldn't get puff Pastry sheets and subbed puff Pastry Shells. Per shell packaging, I did NOT thaw prior to 1st baking. Apart from that, followed all recipe  instructions. DELICIOUS and pretty as individual filled shells.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could I substitute Almond paste for the filling?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What is frangipane?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It's the filling?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I tsp salt is too much salt. I find it very salty and unpleasant.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I would love to make these but don’t understand how it is folded or cut. Would love a video or different explanation.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Follow directions exactly. It is incredibly easy AND delicious. One family member asked me to double the filling next time; I won't. It's good as is.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm sure it will be delicious.. I followed the recipe.. 10 min at 425, then 20 at 350..baked beautifully...however it flaked like crazy which puff pastry likes to do.. doesn't make a lot of sense spreading the paste on something that wants to fall apart.. wondering if it would've been smart to underbake it somewhat and then do the spread? After all it's not like it's a traditional croissant dough recipe..

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My almond flour must have been very dry as the frangipane came out very crumbly, more like a crumble topping. I added a bit of water to make up for the 2t. missing because I had used vanilla extract instead of the rum. But it was still crumbly. In the end, I scattered the crumbs all the way to the edges and on top, along with the sliced almonds. Because I suspected that it would be messy to eat, I cut it into smaller pieces for serving. It was delicious, but messy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Forgot to mention that I agree that 1t. kosher salt is too much. And I love salt!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed directions exactly as Ash did. Mine was a mess. Tasty. A little to brown and shattered. We ate of course but it should state light brown.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My sheet was folded in thirds and it definitely caused the pastry to form in three distinct pieces which broke on slicing. It was also difficult to spread the frangipane on the domed tops. Will try rolling the creases before the first bake next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was so easy I was afraid I wasn't doing it right, and so good I'll make it again and again.  Extremely well-received by whole family!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a great recipe, but 1 1/2 teaspoons of almond extract is too much!  It is a very strong extract, and half that amount would have been better.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So easy, so delicious!!!  It is so easy to put together in the morning for breakfast.  My family devoured it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thinking I will roll the pastry a bit to get rid of the trifold lines on top.  Doubled the filling and it was too much. Yummy still but 1.5 times would be better. Was amazingly good and easy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this exactly as written except for the sprinkle of salt and powdered sugar at the end. I did trim the edges of the puff pastry as suggested and did not roll or press the dough prior to baking since that wasn’t mentioned. Ended up with a square pastry rather than rectangular but it easily cut into eight pieces. Next time I may reduce the sugar in the filling by half to see if we still love it when it’s less sweet. Impressive finished product for very little effort!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 1/2 the sugar!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly, turned out excellent, though it was a bit too salty for my taste.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I would use a lot less flaky salt on top than you think it needs. Store-bought puff pastry (Pepperidge Farm) is already salty, and so is the almond filling. I sprinkled chocolate chips over the almond filling before replacing the baked pastry top. Tasted great (except for the excess salt).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Like somebody else, I, too, was in trepidation, but no need, it came out superbly and easily and was a huge success w. my guests. I did make 1 1/2 times the filling and was v. glad I did. Don't skimp on it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was divine.  And easy.  Don't overthink it.  Read directions and BAKE! It's not that hard.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a genius idea. I’ve made it twice. Fantastic on a lazy Sunday with a cup of hot coffee.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So good!  Next time, will trim the edges very slightly prior to baking to ensure an even puff. I did double the filling.  I used slivered almonds on top which gave more of a crunch. Love it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Fantastic! I did use an all-butter puff pastry and extended the initial baking time by an extra 7 minutes. Great for a brunch party.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this morning but I didn't take out my puff pastry until the day of. I let it sit on the counter for 20-30 minutes (per package directions) and it unfolded just fine. This recipe was easy to make, tasty...just messy to eat!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made the chocolate hazelnut version(see video), would probably cut the sugar or maybe I had too much chocolate, is that a thing?  Was delicious, love how easy it was definitely going to make fancy puff pastry sandwiches

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Too salty as directed. Will reduce salt by half next time

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I wanted to try this but where do I buy the right kind of puff pastry? I went to three different grocery stores: the first two said they didn't know what it is. The third one was trying to be helpful and got me a roll of "fillo dough" which were thin sheets of flour separated by thin sheets of papers. After taking the papers out and baking the rest for 10 minutes, they came out like sheets of dry, almost burnt papers. Edible but not useful for my purpose. HELP!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yeah, fillo dough is NOT puff pastry. Any major supermarket should have at least Pepperidge Farm puff pastry in the freezer -- look maybe in the frozen rolls or desserts section -- but PF is made with oil, not butter, though if that's all you can get it will work just fine. If you're lucky enough to have a Trader Joe's or Whole Foods, they usually have butter puff pastry, and not only will they know what it is, but also WF (mine at least) has the Dufour brand she uses in the video.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Also, if PF is all you can get, the taste will be fine with all the filling and you won't be comparing it with butter. And WF charges $13 for Dufour while PF is half the cost.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Dufour All-Butter frozen puff pastry. Accept no substitutes (although I hear that Trader Joe's is good too, and probably half the price). Dufour is probably available in a specialty or gourmet store in the dessert freezer. If you're in New York, OF COURSE it's at Kalustyan's. It's also at Garden of Eden, Whole Foods and Westside Market.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My mother made this for Easter breakfast. This makes it hard to weigh in because she is known for eschewing specifics in recipes,but she swears up and down she followed it exactly…it was INEDIBLY SALTY. We narrowed it down to a combo of possibly too salty puff pastry and the flaky salt sprinkled on top. It was painful. Also the puff pastry was browned and cracker-dry as baked per recipe direction. When it crumbled and sank into the finished bake, it was greasy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 1 teaspoon of salt is excessive.  I'd cut it to 1/4 or 1/2 at most.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Make sure filling is in middle of rows, save enough for topping

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe is a keeper. Delicious, flaky pastry that looks amazing and tastes as good as any pastry I’ve purchased from the top bakeries in the Bay Area. It’s seriously impressive. I did double the frangipane, because why not? Go big or go home, right? Enjoy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The cooking guide video is helpful and very fun.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this with a leftover sheet of puff pastry (about  8.6oz), so mine was on the smaller side. Although the center collapsed under the weight of the frangipane and almonds during the second bake, the error was masked after being sliced and coated with powdered sugar. The frangipane is very good, and I’d like to incorporate it into other baked goods.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I think the instruction “continue baking until dry, crisp and deeply browned” is off. I baked both times as directed - on the short end of baking time - stopping at browned (slightly more than golden). The finished pastry was overly crisp/dry and crumbled apart when cutting it into pieces and trying to eat it. Texturally it did not resemble a croissant at all, more like a cracker. Flavor wise I found it to be too sweet (though I normally love the sweetness of almond croissants).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Definitely use all butter puff pastry, used Pepperidge Farm because it’s what I had and it didn’t puff enough. Who knew there was no butter in it?! Don’t cook until dark brown, just golden as in video. I doubled the filling, it’s rich with double filling and will feed more with smaller pieces. Next time I’ll try all butter puff pastry (TJs or Dufour from WFs) and just single filling to compare. Despite overdone pastry it was really delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made for Sunday brunch this morning. "One of the best pastries I've ever eaten," from the taste tester. I feel like doubling the frangiapane would be smart (for one sheet of Pep Farm), Very flaky and not too sweet. Super easy. Impressive, Delicious. Also going to try with Nutella and play with a chocolate version. Definintely a keeper!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made the ham and cheese version and it was so good! Easy to make and perfect for brunch. I found the same puff pastry Sohla used and it was delicious. Can’t wait to try the other versions!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Absolutely delicious, have been meaning to make this for a while and so glad I did. I used amaretto instead of rum and it was excellent. Also used 25g less sugar and 25g more almond flour, and very happy with the result. There's just about enough frangipane to pastry ratio, but if feeling indulgent, I'd double the frangipane as that's the best bit. I've put half the pastry made up with frangipane (pre-second-bake) in the freezer, in the hope I can bake from frozen next time for guests.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Very, very special. Beautiful and delicious for company, perfect for brunch
Doctored it a bit to our tastes- 
I didn’t bake twice only once
Doubled the filling so I could have more on the top
Used salted butter and reduced the kosher salt 
Used vanilla instead of the rum and liked that taste

commentBody: Made this for brunch this morning using Dufour puff pastry. It was incredibly good, but I may try cutting the puff pastry in half (in other words, bake the puff pastry in two pieces instead of one) before baking next time as I found it a bit unwieldy to cut the whole thing crosswise when it came out of the oven after the first bake. The almond filling is delicious. Baking times are spot on.

author: Sohla El-Waylly

It was a bit difficult to cut the puff pastry in half while leaving enough layers on bottom half to hold the frangipane but I managed all right. Stored in a ziploc bag. Leftovers were excellent the next day. Maybe would cut a few minutes of baking time

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
One star because it is labled "croissant."
Croissants are named for their historical crescent shape.
They are not rectangular slabs, even if they are a buttery, flaky, viennoiserie pastry.
It may taste good, but find a name appropriate to its shape. Perhaps pan almond.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Can this be made without that one egg? What can be substituted for that egg without ruining the texture/taste?

My wife has a severe egg allergy, and it seems every pastry recipe calls for one or more eggs to be used. I have to ignore them all, but I love almond croissants...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I haven't made this recipe but a vegan replacement for egg is one tablespoon ground flax seed mixed with three tablespoons water. I don't see any reason why this wouldn't work in this recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Ruth no one is being snobby about salt at all. Diamond Crystal salt is the 'standard' used by chefs & recipe developers around the world; if you look at Morton Kosher  Salt you will see the sodium content is vastly different from Diamond Crystal Kosher salt. That's why recipes differentiate between the two. As far as table salt is concerned, every brand has a different sodium content as well, so there's no consistency.

I hope that answers your question WHY!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Sohla's cookie recipes seem to create wet doughs and and moist cookies. But I have learned to trust her!! Last year's ginger cookies quickly became legendary, far beyond others according to aficionados of the genre. Get the right ingredients, follow the instructions, chill the dough, re-chill the shaped cookies, let baked cookies cool completely on the racks, even overnight. The result will be better than you imagined.  Sohla's sweets bring complexity and depth of flavor hard to find elsewhere.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I don’t know why this cookie doesn’t have more reviews. It is THE best cookie I’ve ever made, and I’ve made lots of cookies. I refrigerate for a few hours than just scoop regular size cookies onto parchment paper and then freeze and then bag up for baking on demand.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: These are very sweet with a salty undertone. Maybe reduce the sugar to ¾ cup and a bit less salt. Also VERY big if you follow the size in recipe. I made about 40-45 gr. size and they're about 3 in. wide. Even so, they are tasty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I’m getting ready to make these, and reading the notes makes me wonder if 2 teaspoons of kosher salt *must* be Diamond Crystal salt. If so, the equivalent amount of Morton’s kosher salt would be about 1 1/4 teaspoons. This might explain why some would think the cookies too salty if they added 2 tsps of Morton’s?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Actually, cashews work pretty well in place of macadamia nuts. The taste is more similar to macadamias than walnuts.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody:
These won my company bake off for what it’s worth. 

The batter’s consistency is wet before it rests. As a work around, I portioned everything out, let it rest overnight, then formed the discs from the portioned dough. Worked like a charm.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: This is an amazing cookie recipe. Make the dough as written. Put the dough to rest in the bowl in the fridge for 30 plus minutes. Scoop with normal cookie scoop. Don’t worry about waiting days, don’t worry about rolling them, pressing them down, adding extra chocolate/nuts/salt.  Just make them like a chocolate chip cookie and they are AM-azing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Horrifyingly delicious. Make them, do not hesitate.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: These are fantastic made as is - nutty, salty, vanilla scented goodness - or made with any combination of toasted sugar, caramelized white chocolate, and a tablespoon of malted milk powder added with the nonfat milk powder. Marcona almonds worked well in a pinch when I was short on macadamia nuts. My dough has been pretty easy to handle - never too wet - but the overnight rest really helps make a moist cookie.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: These cookies were extraordinary. I made them without variation to the specified recipe. They are among the single best cookies I’ve ever had. I am confused as to why there are only four stars, but I hope that my words will echo in eternity and those searching for great cookies shall look no further.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: One of the best cookie recipes I've ever made. You must follow the instructions, as variations will not turn out as well. The only change I made was to use malted milk powder (had in my pantry) instead of the non-fat milk powder. Pastry chef Stella Parks refers to malted milk powder as the "umami bomb" for sweet baked goods, and it does boost the flavor of the cookies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I don't have the space in my refrigerator for trays of preflattened cookies so I rolled balls (44g each) and refrigerated those in a bowl. Was too lazy to warm them to shape them so just baked the balls as is. The ball shape made it so they needed a little extra time in the oven but they still came out cookie shaped and very tasty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: No notes. Studied and followed the recipe to a tee. The vanilla bean brown butter was a game changer and resulted the most delicious bite that's come out of the oven. Thank you for this banger!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I don’t know why this recipe isn’t talked about more or featured in the newsletters—because my gosh, these are so good. Sohla, thank you for being a true one: not only did you create something unique, but you also brought me back to those mall trips with my grandma for white chocolate macadamia nut cookies at Mrs. Field’s.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I was very worried these cookies wouldn’t turn out. Adding hot butter, that I borderline burned, to sugar is not my usual cookie recipe. I also didn’t realize until it was too late, that I used malted milk powder instead of plain milk powder. No regrets. Sohla created an amazing cookie recipe that I will cherish until the end of time. I’ve wasted so many wccmn cookie cravings on subway T_T

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Omg DELICIOUS. Wow. I selfishly want to keep this recipe a secret! It’s so good. My only change was to use malted milk powder since it’s what I had. Worth buying macadamias for this recipe!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Don’t over salt the dough. Do less or exactly as much as recipe says bc macadamia nuts being a lot of salt too

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Best cookie I've ever eaten.  It's *that* good.  A little more complicated than your typical cookie but WORTH IT.  Tip:  get your vanilla bean in bulk from Amazon - so much cheaper than the supermarket cooking aisle.  I got like 5 and vacuum packed the rest of them for the next times I make these!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I would refrigerate the dough for at least 30 mins before rolling into balls. I actually like the taste better when the dough is refrigerated in a mass but it does require letting the dough come closer to room temp before making balls. Either way I think the dough is too lose initially to now allow a chill.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: this recipe is incredible!! the result is a RICH, delicious, flavorful cookie the likes of which i've never had come out of a home kitchen, which is definitely a function of the milk powder and the long mixing process. i would not skip chilling the dough (though i made do with just 6 hours and not 12) bc the dough was a little too loose to work with just after mixing. and i also would not skip the flattening step, as that helps the cookies get started spreading out. thank you sohla!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I’m wondering if I could use malted milk powder, which I have on hand.  I think it would work.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I preface this with the fact I hate when people completely butcher a recipe and then rate it. However I have made this dough 3 separate times, all different and the dough itself is wonderful. No white chocolate or hate white chocolate, use whatever chocolate you want.  No vanilla bean, doesn’t matter, make it without.  Macadamia nuts not salted? No problem, make it.  My only suggestion is that if you are salt sensitive like me, maybe use less.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Made these cookies and they were delicious. Brown butter definitely adds a nice toffee flavor. I think next time I would follow the other reviewers suggestions on cutting back the almond extract. It was quite pronounced. Also maybe try cutting back on the sugar a bit as well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I used malted milk powder and it was even better. Make this cookie!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: This is a fabulous recipe. It has a hint of butterscotch to me.  I let the vanilla bean steep in the butter overnight.  The only caveat I have is it is fairly expensive to make.  Vanilla beans are ridiculously expensive and macadamia nuts are as well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I have always made white chocolate macadamia nut cookies for Christmas but with dried cranberries. Has anyone tried adding dried fruit to this recipe? The ingredients are expensive, so I don’t want it to flop if I try it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: These are excellent. For some reason everyone around me seems grossed out by white chocolate (????) which meant I got to eat all of these haha. I think I chopped the chocolate and nuts too coarse, so I'll make sure it's finer next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Wow! After baking so many different recipes for the quintessential white chocolate macadamia nut cookie, I finally found THE ONE. I knew this cookie would be different with the use of milk powder and actual vanilla beans. I’m not going to lie. I did cut down the sugar  down a bit (i figured the sweetness from the white chocolate would be enough and I was right) and the salt from 2 teaspoons to one 1.75  and it worked out just fine. If you want more salt at the end, just add a little Maldon salt on top at the end.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I recently added freeze dried raspberries for a Valentine’s Day themed treat and it was beautiful and delicious

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I bet they would be even better with walnuts! (Also, because I don’t have any macadamias.)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Then they would be White Chocolate Macadamia nut cookies

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: My husband makes these every year, they are delicious. However he has always added fresh grated ginger and it takes these already yummy cookies and puts them over the edge. People love love love them.  This years batch the ginger must have not been very gingerly and they are just okay. I miss the ginger.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Amazing cookies. Just follow the recipe and Chill all the dough in the bowl for 30 minutes. Then scoop like normal chocolate chip cookies. You do NOT need to roll in balls or flatten or add extra choc/nuts/salt or wait fir days.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Best ever cookies!! Made as directed than chilled dough in fridge in kitchen aide bowl for 30 mins. I then scooped like a normal chocolate chip cookie onto parchment paper lined 1/2 sheet. You do NOT need to make balls, flatten, chill for days, add stuff on top, etc. make them!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: These are incredible! I substitute the milk powder with equal amount of grounded macadamia nuts cause couldn’t find it and honestly didn’t really want to use it, and naturally pink ruby chocolate for the white chocolate since I find the flavor is very similar to white chocolate and I like it better, plus looks beautiful. The crunchiness on the outside soft middle is perfection. Will definitely make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: These cookies are the bomb! My absolute favorites.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Like several of the recent cookies featured  on this site, they appear very flat.  Is that intentional?  I always thought there was a flaw in technique or proportions when this occurs.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody:
Since this uses vanilla extract, don't even waste your money on a vanilla bean. There are so many blind vanilla taste tests with professional chefs. None could distinguish the difference between vanilla extract and imitation vanilla in baked applications. The only application where chefs were able to make a distinction was in cold applications, such as mousses, ice cream, etc.

I’ve been a baker for over 20 years, and I cannot tell the difference.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: This is the best cookie I have ever made and truly gave me the Mrs. Fields cookie vibes. I’ve been following Sohla for awhile and she is a genius. Her recipes are well thought out- there’s a reason why she’s asking you to seek out milk powder and let the dough chill for 12  hours. Want just an average cookie? You can totally skip those steps and it will still turn out great but if you are seeking the ultimate, flavor and texture enhanced cookie, just do it- the results will be amazing!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: And now that they're baked, I'll say that they need more time than 13-15, at least in my kitchen, and also that when I put them on a wire rack, they fell through and then fell apart because they were so soft! Recommend cooling them on something flatter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I am a cookie obsessed baker and these immediately caught my attention. Made them 3 times: 1st time as recipe called but used 1-1 gluten free flour (because celiac) worked amazing. 2nd time I ran out of the full amount of macadamia nut so used unsweetened coconut flakes to make up the difference - so chewy and delicious yet again. 3rd time made as instructed but ran out of my white choc chunks so had to use chips and they give the same affect maybe just not as artistic.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is lovely. My only note is that maybe if gifting these cookies, I would cut the almond extract to 1/8 teaspoon. Even with only 1/4ts, I would walk by the cookies and the almond extract scent would waft into my nose. It was a little at the forefront of the flavor and next time I’ll cut it back a little. That said. The cookie was stinkin delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: These are the best white chocolate macadamia nut cookies I’ve ever tasted! I’ve tried to make brown butter WCM cookies for a long time and never could get the portions right. Everything about this recipe is perfect. The dough is wetter/stickier than most cookie doughs, but the chilling makes them easier to handle. I found them to be the perfect balance of sweet and salty. The flaky salt really ties everything together.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: 1/4c cookies are huge! I made a 1.5x recipe and got from it 28 'normal' sized cookies

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody:
i liked the idea of this cookie and dissolving the sugar in the butter definetely gave them a special texture i not yet tried. 
i made them a little bit smaller and substituted the macadamia with cashews and they turnes out alright. although i dont know how but my cookies didn't really flatten out like on the picture, they stayed more in clumps..

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: A little more involved than the basic drop cookies I normally make, but wow! The pay-off was worth it. I rarely have cookies come out perfectly, but these looked like cookies I would buy at a bakery. Tasted fantastic too. Made the recipe almost exactly as stated (accidentally bought only  a half cup of nuts at the store) and with the optional vanilla bean. Life got busy and these spent two days in the fridge before being baked.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: 45g portions were large/normal.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: So good. Never used vanilla bean before so fun to try something new especially since they turned out to be very very yummy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful tasting cookies that I will add to my favorites.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I would rate this recipe 6 stars if I could! This is my favourite cookie recipe now. It’s got such a mature balance of sweetness and toastiness and saltiness. Love love love!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: The best cookies I’ve ever made!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I have made a lot of cookies through the years and these are tied with my favorite cookie of all times.  These things are amazing - the browned butter flavor really comes through.  Just make them, you won’t regret it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: These won my office bake off, highly recommend!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I made these and thought they were quite good. Watching the video was helpful. Sohla is knowledgeable, welcoming and thoughtful. A superb instructor. I especially wanted to make these because the ingredients include milk powder. Sohla explained precisely how to incorporate it. I think she's terrific.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I didn't think there was a way to improve these cookies until I accidentally reversed the white chocolate/macadaemia nut ratio. Oh lord.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I made these and accidentally addicted my friend who will periodically text to ask for them. I use macadamia nuts from Costco as they are good quality and much more cost effective. I also chop up white baking chocolate in a bar or brick. I don’t find the chips to taste as good. I’ve made this with a bunch of different sugar combinations, most recently all dark brown. Gives it a chewy Carmel-y flavor. Can’t go wrong

author: Sohla El-Waylly
