recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Excellent. 5 stars!! Wouldn’t change a thing. That said, these would also be delicious with the same dough base substituting the type of nut and/or chopped dark chocolate as mix-ins. The butter and milk powder took a long time to brown, perhaps because I made a double batch, but though I was concerned that the long melt/simmer time would cause too much liquid to evaporate, the final cookie texture was perfect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: These are ridiculously good. After an overnight fridge rest, 50g cookies still took about 13 min to bake. They came out thick and chewy with a delightful shiny crackly top like a brownie. So glad to have a freezer stash of these for when the craving hits!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I will use salted butter which imho always helps balance the sugar and chocolate chips, in addition to the flaky salt garnish

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: There is something wrong with either ingredients or instructions. The dough came out quite wet - so I’m not sure how one can roll them into balls (it’s runny). I had to refrigerate dough before dividing it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I’m interested in how ~ much/ many vanilla bean you are getting from a bean. I have about 6 pods that I’ve found from my move from Maui to the mainland abt 5 hrs ago & they’re pretty dry but still aromatic! Using them but would like to know as I’m planning to use as many as needed for this recipe 😀

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Love these but couldn’t get the butter/powdered milk mixture to brown at all. Tried higher heat and that didn’t help. Any ideas what I did wrong?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: surprising! I feel like its impossible not to get it to brown, its simply "burning" the butter and milk. did you wait until the butter foamed? ideally, wait for the butter to melt completely, foam and then once the foam settles add the powder. I actually like to keep the heat on medium-low to really control browning too fast/overly burnt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Mine didn't work either! Not sure why--I thought maybe I was nervous about burning so I was moving the heat around a lot, but it just wouldn't brown.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: @Joan glad to hear it wasn’t just me. I thought my butter wouldn’t brown at first because I was doubling the recipe and maybe there was too much in the pan. There was a lot of steam coming off, So I feared that the evaporation would affect the consistency in the final dough. Nonetheless, I persisted and eventually got the butter and milk to a beautiful, nut brown toasty shade. My fears about the liquid ratio were unfounded. Final cookie was perfection! Patience, a whisk, and constant attention made all the difference in getting the butter and milk solids browned to the right color without burning.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Made these today and they’re actually pretty tasty. I used dry milk (because I couldn’t find milk powder) and it worked okay, but I would probably skip using it altogether. Make sure to use good nuts and chocolate and you will be fine. I think chilling the dough was absolutely necessary because this dough is very moist. Anyway, slay!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe with delicious results, worth buying quality ingredients. My only note would be that you should taste your dough at each stage to check if the salt content is to your liking. Between the kosher salt and the salted macadamia nuts, a garnish at the end would've been overkill for me. I'd consider either knocking back the kosher salt or buying unsalted macadamia nuts in the future for a little more control.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody:
These cookies were delicious, but mine didn't spread and had a crumbly texture.  I'm measured my ingredients very accurately so I don't think that's the problem. I used a Cuisinart to chop the nuts, which came out quite finely. Do you think that's why the batter was stiff? Also, could it have something to do with the milk powder? Never used that before.
Thanks!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: When I tasted the batter before adding the flour I was surprised to find out I had created drinkable Werther‘s Originals, wow!! Although the cookies turned out perfect they were - for my humble European taste - too sweet and too salty at the same time…

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody:
The dough also tastes like Werther's Echte! 
(Fellow European here, I often halve the salt in cookie recipes)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I had a similar situation as someone above where my dough became very stiff when I added the flour, and my final cookies bake up thicker (still freakin delicious though). They also had almost a glossy crust. I rewatched Sohla’s video and can’t quite figure out where I went wrong. The only difference is a used a low fat milk powder versus a non fat milk powder, it was all I could find. Anyone have thoughts on the texture difference?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: For some reason my butter just would NOT brown--I think I got spooked by what felt like a very strong boil and I was nervous about burning--so my cookies are pale yellow...but they smell amazing!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Sohla, what brand of white chocolate did you use?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Will be making these this week, was just wondering if the cookie dough could be frozen?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: A friend has just given us tonnes of shell-on macadamia nuts that her Kenyan friend gave her from his tree and that she can’t find a use for. They taste incredible, like butter. I’m going to make these cookies with them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: made the cookies twice already and I absolutely love them. The cookies are a bit sweet, but if you reduce the amount of sugar by 30 g, really go for it with the salt and brown the butter really well, the cookies really shine. i'm making another batch today with pistachios. I have a feeling these will taste great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Such a fun recipe! I am looking forward to baking the cookies tomorrow.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions on what constitutes “good” white chocolate?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: This is the kind I used and it was absolutely delicious! I had enough to do a x2 batch with a little leftover. https://www.amazon.com/gp/aw/d/B01CSX2UPY?ref=ppx_pt2_mob_b_prod_image

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I made these with half unsalted and have salted macadamia nuts. I also omitted the salt on top of the cookies. I think including all of the salty components may be too much. Otherwise, these cookies were a huge hit. So delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody:
These were unrelentingly sweet to us. Very buttery, and there thankfully was a salt component but in the future I would make these with half the sugar and hope it didn’t mess up the texture too much. 

I stan Sohla el-Waylly and every other recipe of hers I’ve cooked I’ve absolutely loved! I think this is just our preference. If you like a sweet, buttery, nicely chewy cookie here you go!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: These are the white chocolate cookies I’ve ever tasted! I’ve tried to create my own brown butter version of these cookies and I could never get them quite right. These are so flavorful. Not too sweet for me. I took another person’s recommendation and added only 1/8 teaspoon of almond extract. The flaky sea salt really pulls it all together. If you’re a big white chocolate macadamia nut cookie fan, these are perfect. Another Sohla winner!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: These were amazing! My dough seemed a little too wet to scoop well, but after a chill they were perfect. Sohla wins again. Perfect chew, crisp edges.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody:
I baked half the cookies after a 6 hour chill and half after an overnight 20 hour chill. The longer chilled cookies were firmer in texture and had a better flavor.  

One oddity of these cookies is that they don't brown much in the oven since you already browned the butter on the stove. 

As others have noted, do taste the cookie batter before salting again as in the instructions. I used Morton's kosher salt and the cookies were already plenty salty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Maybe it’s because I roasted and salted my own nuts, but these were a bit too salty, and I didn’t even put the flaky salt on top! And I love salty baked goods! So just be sure to double check the seasoning in the dough, like I didn’t, if you’re having to roast your own nuts. Or maybe just don’t salt them at all.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Milk powder does not seem to be available where I live. Could I use rice powder? Or just omit it?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: My husband doesn’t like white chocolate so more for me!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made these 4 times now and 3/4 times they’ve turned out really puffy instead of spreading out and being chewy. What am I doing wrong?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Made these as directed and they were amazing. The only thing I would change is that the macadamia nuts were a little to big for easy consumption, in my opinion. But overall great cookie and I highly recommend.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody:
Sohla’s done it again - these are delicious! I followed the recipe, including browning the butter with the vanilla bean pod skin, and also rested the dough for one day: these turned out amazing, with a beautiful vanilla undertone. 

I saw some comments about the dough coming out too wet. I didn’t have that issue and weighed everything out. The dough had some slack, but wasn’t too wet.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Chewy without tasting underdone, nutty, salty, exactly what I wanted from this cookie. Perfect! I let the butter cool a few minutes before I poured it into the sugar lest it liquify the sugar entirely, and I froze the dough and cooked straight from frozen. I didn't have a vanilla bean, but they're still decadent and flavorful.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Sohla, my cookies don’t look like that… in all fairness it might be because of the time frame. We will see, but I can say omg gooood

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Double almond extract

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: If yout hink I am going to buy milk powder just to make one batch of cookies, - not.  Haven't bought dry milk since nuclear bomb scares in the 50s. Maybe that box is in the back of the cupboard somewhere .  I should go look.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: @Rose K. I bought it at a bulk food store and just got the amount needed for the recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: @Rose K. Dry milk power continues to be used in modern day, especially many South Asian and Middle Eastern desserts. Online, you can find recipes for western treats like Rice Krispies, frostings, and more that use it as well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Waaaayyyyy too salty with the amount of additional salt quoted in recipe. Felt like I was eating salt dough with mixings

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: @D you must use diamond kosher salt. Any other salt will be too salty

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: These are the best biscuits I have ever ever made

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: This is a great recipe! I cut the size of each cookie in half and shortened the time in the oven to 12 minutes. They turned out perfectly, and everyone loves them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Do you think they would be good with hazelnuts instead of macadamia?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: What brand of white chocolate do you reccomend?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Has anybody tried making these at a higher altitude? I live at 5200 feet above the sea level and am wondering if I'd need to make any high altitude adjustments

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Found the recipe disappointing. Followed all the instructions as written and ended up with very cakey cookies (not my style), didn’t enjoy them and felt like the white chocolate to dough ratio was low and disappointing as well

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Made a few different cookie recipes from NYT this holiday to give to friends, these were the best! Great flavor, and flawless shape/look. Left me feeling like Mrs. Fields over here. I let them rest in the fridge overnight, which worked wonders for them to hold their height. Wish they were a little chewier on the inside, but all in all I’ll be making this again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Made as is expect omitted the dried milk since it was only sold in a large container and I had no use for it after this. Still really good. I rolled the dough into a log overnight instead of on a sheet pan and turned out fine. I did once batch with flaky sea salt on top and it was way too salty. The next batch i didn’t top with salt and it was much better. Made a lot smaller cookies than suggested and it turned out delish.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I learned in year 10 of my relationship that this is my spouse's favorite kind of cookie. Imagine his surprise when I made this recipe! These cookies are perfect in every way.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: I made these as directed. They are delicious, although the sugar mixture was hot enough to melt the white chocolate pieces. Next time I’ll wait for it to cool, a step not included in the recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: White Chocolate Macadamia Nut Cookies Recipe
commentBody: Echoing others to cut the sugar. I cut in half and it was still very sweet since white chocolate is so sweet. I found white chocolate bars with strawberry and used those 👌

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: I’m not much of a winter squash fan. That said, this recipe was phenomenal!  The sweet/sour/spicy blend of the gravy over the squash with the salty yogurt as a dash of creamy flavor made this a huge hit. I did cut the cayenne to 1/2 tsp, and the heat was perfect for us. I anticipate making this often as winter rolls on. Next time, I may double the gravy recipe. The amount was adequate, but having extra to soak into the rice would be a definite plus.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody:
No peeling needed and I always roast my butternut or delicata squashes with the seeds. The seeds are delicious, crunchy, and super good for you. I don't even scrape them out. I just cut up the squash, keep the seeds attached to the flesh, roast it all, then add them with the squash pieces to whatever I'm doing with the squash. Yum!! 

I'm so excited to try this awesome recipe. Thank you!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: What about the stringy pulp around the seeds? Do you eat that? It doesn't look very good, but I'm always willing to learn something new.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Sohla recipe dropping is like an album release for my tastebuds. This is probably one of the best things I've ever made this year

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: This, as I modified it, was phenomenal. The balance of sweet and salty was notable.  Made it with (unpeeled) Japanese sweet potatoes on hand that I sliced and roasted. Doubled the onion gravy components and served with rice and salted yogurt. Will keep this recipe in the box.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: I was looking for something new to do with all my adorable honeynut squash and was intrigued by the great reviews for this recipe. It came together so easily and while I thought the onion gravy tasted just ok out of the pan, the combination of rice, squash, sauce, yogurt and cilantro all together was insanely delicious. I did add a little finely chopped cucumber to the salted yogurt for texture which I thought was a nice addition. I will be making this recipe over and over.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: I highly recommend not using a nonstick pan so you get the browned bits the recipe refers to. Also, be sure to cook the spices at least the one minute called for otherwise they may end up tasting somewhat “raw”. Delicious and different.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: So is salted yogurt just that? Or is there a recipe? Thanks!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Definitely double the gravy!!! Also cut the squash into bite-sized cubes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody:
No Miriam, the squash doesn't need to be peeled. The skin is edible. However if you feel it necessary,
peel away.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night but added Brussel sprouts to the squash, to add a green vegetable. I also doubled the onion gravy to use tonight with chicken. I used olive oil. Delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: These flavors are complex and warming, especially on chilly winter evenings. I made this recipe with delicata squash. The squash, onion gravy, and cilantro paired wonderfully together. That said, this recipe had too much cumin for my taste (and I love cumin!) and was on the spicy side even with only 1/2 tsp of cayenne. I'll use less of both in the future. I served it with sour cream because it's what I had on hand.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! Glad I doubled the gravy part per the recommendations of others. I have no regrets. This was easy to make and a new favorite comfort food. Took a bit longer then I expected but still easy and worth the wait.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: I would increase the onions - ratio was too low for me

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: I think the gravy called for far too much cumin - and I like cumin!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Next time I would peel the squash.  It was a bit spicy for me, even though I cut the cayenne pepper in half.  I wasn't sure what they meant by "salted yogurt," either.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Made this for my roommates last night and they loved it. The spicy, salty gravy was a perfect combo with the sweetness of the squash. I realized too late that I had run out of yogurt, so I had to substitute sour cream on the side but it still tasted great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Oh so good. Used farmers market honeynut. I love the juxtaposition of the tart gravy and the sweet squash. Had with plain basmati and also left over OTK rice mushroom pilaf. Outta this world!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Made this exactly as instructed (using oil instead of ghee) and it was so, so delicious - no notes. Thanks Sohla!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was terrific and not difficult or overly fussy to make. I could get Kashmiri chili powder only by mail-order so I substituted paprika. Ate with brown rice. I love squash and caramelized onions, and next time, I might try some of Ali Slagle's caramelized onions that I have in the freezer just because I have multiple sclerosis and can't stand for a long time (the freezer is your friend, Yotam O says in one of his cookbooks, and it has been for me with my illness!).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Doesn’t the squash need to be peeled before roasting?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Most squash can be roasted without peeling. I don't peel kabocha or honeynut unless the kabocha is kind of warty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: This has great flavor, but I would reduce the cayenne to 1/2 teaspoon next time. 3/4 tsp. was pretty intense!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody:
Sara— Ghee is safe up to 450 since all of the milk solids (the part that burns) are removed as well as any water— it’s pure butterfat with a nutty flavor

Elisa— I used whole plain yogurt and mixed in a little bit of a saline solution I made (kosher salt and water) to thin it out a little bit and make it slightly salty. Not sure if this is the intended garnish, brief google search did not turn up a clear answer, but Im sure any kind of yogurt or your sour cream will achieve the desired result!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: I basically followed the recipe. I used about 3/4 T cumin instead of 1. Changes I will make: use ghee for roasting the squash but a neutral oil to fry the onions and spices and peel the squash before roasting. It doesn’t take much time to peel butternut with a vegetable peeler and I prefer it peeled when eating it. I was skeptical about using a whole cup of water, but it did cook down and the onion “gravy” was good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Butternut squash ravioli in my fridge and what to do but combine the onion gravy for a sauce to the ravioli, so no baked in the skin squash. Added basil from my window garden and dry sherry plus the vinegar and water. Really yummy with the spice and blackened onion sauce, and together with a dry Riesling made for a nice dinner. Many thanks for a lovely fall dinner.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and easy. I doubled the sauce, added a can of chickpeas and had a complete meal. My cheapy brand of cumin and coriander had a bitterness that better spices probably don’t have, but the sweet squash and the sour from the vinegar was the perfect counterbalance.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious, the onions bring the whole dish together. The recipe was easy to put together and I even forgot one of the spices and it still tasted good. Really nice with yogurt over rice.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: I would really suggest using the Kashmiri chili pepper if you can get it. The cayenne felt like it overwhelmed things a bit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: This was good. I made it with oil—ghee at my supermarket is $14 for a small tub.  The gravy, doubled per comment suggestions, was very good but the butternut squash was disappointing (it was one we grew—others have been sweet and tasty). Turmeric may not be the best seasoning for it, or maybe the ghee is really necessary.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: I added some chickpeas for protein

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Definitely double gravy/onion.  Myself, I'd up the chili/ red pepper; I used Korean chili.  But this is really tasty, and not too hard to produce.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Made with butternut squash for Thanksgiving. Best squash dish my family-in-law has ever tasted, and that’s saying something: they aren’t “vegetable” people. Also: Absolutely eat the skin. Full of flavor and a nice flaky, chewy counterpoint to the mushy, heavenly crisp squash innards.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Can a vegetable oil be substituted for ghee? I am lactose intolerant.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody:
A suggestion when prepping the sliced squash— put the slices directly into a large bowl after slicing, and toss with ghee/turmeric/salt before putting onto the baking sheet. 

We served this as bowls with basmati rice and yogurt— the hubs declared it a must-add to our winter dinners rotation!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: I roasted two chicken breasts on top of the squash. Fat cooked into it and added to the gravy. Yum!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: We loved this, even the squash-ambivalent in our household. Served with salted yogurt and rice, with a quick dal and some cabbage I roasted with the same seasonings as the squash since the oven was on anyway. I found that simmering the gravy a little both thickened it if that’s your jam and smoothed out a slight graininess from the spices. Prep is by far the hardest and most time consuming part of this. Will try skin on squash next time!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Does anybody know what salted yogurt is??

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: @LLA just plain yogurt with some salt in it. in south asian cuisine, yogurt is much more commonly served savory

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I added a can of chickpeas soon after the onion for added protein and it worked well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: I want to cook this for Thanksgiving, a high-stakes time to try it.  I've never cooked with ghee.  Will it hold up to the 450 degree temperature when roasting the squash without smoking?  What oil might work as well as ghee?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Do yourself a favor and make it at least one time before Thanksgiving like try it this weekend or something. Don’t do something brand new on such a stressful cooking day.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: An internet search of different oil smoke points says ghee's is above 450 (which I presume our professional recipe developer Sohla took into account during testing and before publishing). My understanding is that because clarifying the butter removes protein and other compounds you're left with purer fat and there's not much left to "smoke." Unless you avoid dairy for ethical reasons, ghee has its own flavor worth trying out here.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: This was easy and delicious.  Maybe add a little flour or cornstarch when adding the water so that the sauce has some thickness.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Is salted yogurt just yogurt w/salt? It sounds good, I use ‘Mexican Style Sour Cream’ -- it has more salt than reg sour cream as well as tapioca starch as a thickener. The dish sounds intriguing!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Make your own ghee -- it's easy and far less expensive.  Lots of recipes on line.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: When a Sohla recipe drops its like an album release for tastebuds, this was so good

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: interesting, but we found it too spicy. If I try it again, I'll watch quantities more carefully (and remember that my family are very fond of unadorned oven-roasted squash)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: This was great with crumbled goat cheese. Also, 1/4 t of cayenne provided more than enough heat!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: very tasty! thought the gravy needed a little bit of sweetness so added about 3/4 teaspoon of maple syrup and it was very nice.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Squash is easier and less dangerous to peel after 5 minutes in the microwave.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was a hit. Added some chipotle powder for some smoke, but otherwise no changes. Loved the salted yogurt— a new staple in our house.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody:
Used 1 butternut and 1 koginut squash. Left skin on both.
Doubled the gravy.
Used olive oil for the ghee.
Served with rice.
Very good, especially with the yogurt! And quick to make.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: We used butternut as kabocha wasn't available. Turned out great and the yogurt really adds the final touch  The spicy gray was fantastic and we decided to cut the onion in half rings. Will probably double the gravy next time out.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: I was skeptical about this one but now I’m convinced.  Absolutely wonderful.  I added in a bit of fancy hot curry powder and a couple drops of liquid smoke. I served it with homemade pita. I added garlic, lemon & diced cuke to the salted yogurt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Anyone else finding the gravy rather grainy? Any ideas on how to smooth it out? Great recipe otherwise.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: This was soooo delicious. Doubled the gravy as others have suggested and added chick peas. I did not double the cayenne and did 1 1/2 times the cumin and cider vinegar just because they are such strong flavors. Used two teaspoons of sea salt in the doubled gravy. I was afraid it would be too salty, but once everything is combined it's perfect. Served over rice with plain greek yogurt on the side. Super satisfying vegetarian dish.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: One of the best NYT recipes I've cooked to date, hands down. Made exactly as written, served over basmati rice and salted yogurt. There were no leftovers.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: The squash was nice, however, the sauce was dreadful.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Disappointing, which was surprising. After all the positive reviews here. It took me longer to cook than I expected. I didn't like the texture that the turmeric gave the roasted squash, and the onion gravy was grainy from the ground spices, with the vinegar flavor predominating. My kids did not like it, and usually they like everything, including vegetables. Oh well!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Gravy usually has something like flour to thicken it. This seems to be missing something…

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful, complex flavor. I used half cayenne and it was still a bit too much heat. Did not have apple cider vinegar so used half sherry vinegar and half apple cider. Will be in our monthly rotation.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: I agree, double the gravy and cut up the squash before roasting. I found the gravy a little thin tasting so added a tsp of Better Than Bouillon. Simmered it a bit to thicken. Terrific!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody:
This was so amazing! My boyfriend and I both simultaneously said "wow" after our first bite. 
I second the suggestion of doubling the onion gravy. I also followed another commenters idea of having this with dal and that was a great accompaniment. 

This is a likely contender for the Thanksgiving table this year for us!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: this was easy and delicious, but I used an acorn squash and next time would peel it as the skin was a little unpleasantly tough after roasting.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Can this be made ahead of time?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: my leftovers, reheated, were delicious if that's any help.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, have made twice already exactly as directed, but with oil, rather than ghee Will be making for Thanksgiving this year--but will use much less cayenne!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Roasted Squash With Spiced Onion Gravy Recipe
commentBody: Big hit with all the family! Next time, we'll reduce the baking time for the squash a little next time to keep it slightly firmer.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This method is so much easier than the original version, which is the source of great agony every Xmas. Makes for a little merrier holiday.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For all of us non food-processor people: I've found that the nuts really do need to be ground finely for the bottom crust to have adequate structural integrity, BUT 1) pecans are soft enough to "grind" (smash) with a rolling pin in a heavy plastic zip-top back, and 2) pecan meal IS a thing you can buy. The rest can be done with a knife and a wooden spoon.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I subbed almond flour because I had it for some reason, and hazelnuts for pecans as they are impossible to find here. I also got the proportions wrong in step 3 and had probably too much dough to crumble on top, I didn't use as much raspberry jam as the recipe called for, and subbed a 24cm, 9 1/2" springform for the 9" square, and despite all that, they were delicious. A 5-star recipe. But I must confess, the idea of a thin layer of marzipan in there someplace did occur to me....

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What do people think about using apricot preserves/jam? Would that work well?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Ruby It’s 2 years too late, but you can usually swap flavors of jam with no real issue. I love apricot, I think it’d go well!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Try with rhubarb jam, cardamom, and hazelnuts

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The recipe is well-written, easy to follow and the results were perfect.  That being said, 13 oz. of raspberry jam overwhelmed all the other flavors.  It didn't slow us down from eating them all, but next time I will half the jam, which should give a nice balance and bring out the nuances of the lemon, cinnamon and pecans.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Mark agree too much jam. Should have used the smaller jar.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So actually the Linzer Torte has it‘s origins in Linz, Austria (hence the name). Vienna is a completely different city!! There is no vieneese Linzer Torte - only a linzer Linzer Torte . It’s like saying it’s a New York style/Miami pizza. Yes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Writing from Vienna, this is exactly the comment I wanted to make. Many thanks "Linzer"!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this with hazelnut flour and the Bonnie Maman mixed berry jam Costco sells. Delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I think apricot jam/preserves would be great!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was thinking the same thing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: That's exactly what I did! In the oven now...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I agree!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made four (4) of these today (for a large holiday cookie sale) using raspberry, apricot, cherry, and strawberry preserves. All sold very quickly; we sell by the pound so these are very lucrative.  Super easy, but do cut the bars as directed using a long, thin, wet knife; any smaller and they can crumble, and must be fully cooled first.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Any thoughts on using almond flour (ground almonds) instead of the pecans (which also sounds delicious) for the crust?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I actually have pecan flour which I bought in a Florida grocery store.  Now I can use it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Definitely will come back to this recipe! Used almonds as I had leftover almond flour. The trickiest part of this recipe is getting the top to brown without the bottom layer over-baking.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Ruby, I’ve made a similar recipe with apricot preserves and it was delicious. 

I’m very interested to try the cardamom suggestion!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If I make this again, I’ll try cutting the sugar from 2/3 cup to half a cup. The preserves added so much sweetness that I felt it didn’t need quite that much sugar. Brought these to a party and they were a huge hit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I thought these Linzer bars were amazing! So easy to make and they were delicious. I couldn’t find a way to put the crust on the parchment paper, it just would not smooth out so I sprayed the pan again and then pressed in the batter with my fingers. I let the bars cool overnight and the whole thing came out of the pan pretty easily. I just ran a spatula around the edges and it popped up.  Other than that, I followed the recipe exactly and was very pleased with the results.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this twice and both times got rave reviews. I used a 10" square pan, which worked fine, and the second time used two sheets of parchment crossed in the middle, so I had parchment going up all four sides of the pan. This made it MUCH easier to get out of the pan and slice. The dough is fairly soft, so not easy to pat into the pan or crumble on top, but the results speak for themselves!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In years past I've made traditional Linzer cookies, lovely but what a pain to make. So these looked like an easier alternative and they were. I doubled the recipe, because why not given, it is the same amount of work to get double the cookies. I used Bonne Maman mixed fruit jam and it was superb, though next time I will use two jars for a jammier bar.  I loved the notable lemon background notes in these cookies. They stored well and tasted delicious. I will make these every year from now on.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with hazelnuts?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yes I have. Pecans can easily be replaced with hazelnuts or walnuts!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I added extra flour to the cookie dough/pecan mix to make it a bit more crumbly before sprinkling on top. Made it easy to sprinkle over the jam.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m so pleased to find a recipe to use up all the jars of jam I have in my fridge. I mixed a lot of them together. They were a bit too sweet so I added a quarter teaspoon of citric acid. Delicious recipe I’ll be doing this again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I tripled the recipe for a party and used a half sheet pan to bake.  I did all the math so the area would be comparable.  Cutting 9x6 gave 54 almost square pieces.  Made as written except used almond flour instead of grinding my own pecans.  Worked out great!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Original (Austrian) recipe uses powidl (a kind of plum spread prepared without additional sweeteners or gelling agents). Few other fruit spreads have the same heft or aromatic depth. :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Why specify a metal pan?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My guess is it shapes how the dessert browns and bakes, and/or is the type of pan used in testing this recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made as written, but with a GF flour blend and apricot jam. Delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I think apricot jam would be delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: People just cannot stop themselves from being know it alls rather than letting the recipe speak for itself.  Keep those comments to yourself, please.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have a very small food processor. It could only fit the sugar, butter, etc. Dumped it out into a bowl and folded the flour in. Tasted just fine out of the oven!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just made these.  Adapted my pan with a roll of foil—11x9 became 9x9.  Delicious.  I am freezing them so assume they will be great for a collection of sweets.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made them exactly as the recipe described. As with many baked goods I thought these were even more delicious the day after I made them. They are quite sweet so I cut them into smaller squares. They look absolutely beautiful — a fine addition to a tower of cookies for a holiday tea.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are fabulous.   Made one batch with raspberry preserves and another with apricot.  The apricot was even better.  I mixed all nuts in the crust and did not do nuts on the top only crumbled crust.  Was great!!  Thank you!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Excellent with a nice combination of flavors.  Also, relatively quick and easy to make.  Based on another review, only used half of the amount of preserves (blackberry) and would do so again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these three times this holiday season along with several other cookies.  This was the cookie that everyone begged for the recipe and talked about.  This recipe is an instant classic and has been added to my holiday cookie list.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Was excited to find use for dark cherry jam from MI cherries. Result was underwhelming and the process tedious. Mine were crumbly even though I weighed and measured carefully so didn’t present well on cookie tray. One time effort.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very easy and tasty. I followed exactly but had to use Raspberry spread instead of jam, a fine substitution that didn’t give me any trouble. Next time I mighty add more lemon zest. A note on appearance, I found that the dough came out more grayish rather than the described golden. I sprinkled powdered sugar so didn’t wind up being an issue, but was taken aback. Also I found the dough a little too sticky to be “crumbled” on top (even with the reserved pecans added in) and just did little globs

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Use the 9” pan as recommended; 8” pan makes the crust too thick. Also, don’t scrimp on the jam layer; spread it nice and thick!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this with almond meal and white sugar, as that's what I had, and they turned out pretty good. For the topping, I added enough almond meal until it seemed crumbly. Maybe a little less jam next time but everything was quickly eaten.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
i gave this to my neighbor and her partner, and the first thing her partner said the next morning was, “i’m still thinking about how good that pecan thing megan gave us last night was.”

also, i toasted my nuts beforehand and added half of the lemon i zested in the jam mixture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Two things:  1) I get stubborn when a recipe suggests a kitchen implement I don't have, such as an offset spatula. But when I finally bought one, the recipe was SO much easier to make.  2) I kept finding the sweetness and amount of raspberry jam overwhelming and destabilizing.  So I tried an 8.82 oz jar of Fior de frutta reduced-sugar raspberry jam:  the bars held together better, more diverse flavors came through, and I got better comments on the results!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Step 5 "Evenly crumble over the remaining cookie dough" was not clear to me. Makes it sound like you're crumbling the crust you've already baked and putting it on top of the unbaked dough. I 'crumbled' the unbaked dough on top of the crust and jam layer, but mine was too wet to crumble, so more like put little pieces on and spread it out. Didn't get the same look as in the photos but tastes good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So, this - "Evenly crumble over the remaining cookie dough...," - means to crumble the remaining cookie dough on top of the jam layer, correct? So, "Evenly crumble the remaining cookie dough over the jam layer."

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Those who are gluten free, your thoughts on better to use a 1:1 GF flour mix, or better to use almond flour? Thank you.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this with my all-purpose gluten-free flour mix. 1:1 and they worked out great!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very easy and fun to make! Made these with my 10-year-old with peanuts and a mix of strawberry and homemade blackberry jam based on what we had on hand. Very tasty!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used seedless blackberry jam (because it was all I had), and it was a game changer! Highly recommend trying different fruits jams. Followed the advice of another comment, and ground the pecans very fine with no problems on the bottom crust.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Those must be tiny bars if you are dividing a 9 x 9 into 18.  Three by six means long narrow strips of bar -- hmmmmm ... A lot of work for two bites.  Who would eat just one?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made it in a pie tin and used 250 grams of tart cherry preserves as that’s all I had.  I’ll definitely put this in my holiday rotation. Delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So are you substituting 1\2 cup almond or pecan flour for the 1\2 cup pecans in the beginning of the recipe?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'll be making these for Thanksgiving with my cranberry-persimmon-apple-pear compote.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are very good and pretty simple! I made them almost exactly as written; only difference is that mine had to bake an extra 8 minutes before I saw any bubbling. Just a difference in ovens, I suppose. Hoping they freeze ok as a pan was too much for two of us to eat in time!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can these be frozen?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yes! I made them and since there are just two of us I froze half the bars. They were great. Took about an hour or so to thaw.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this per the recipe.  They turned out very tasty.  I would not change anything. but you could easily modify with a different jam or nut.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I make a lot more jam than I can eat or give away. I subbed apricot jam here- not the same taste as a real Linzer, but very tasty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wouldn't describe the top cookie dough mix with pecans as crumbly.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
The jam I planned to use was moldy, so I heated some frozen berries with 1/3 cup sugar and 1/2 cup water and let it simmer for 30 min-1 hour. 

I used a blender on pulse mode for my food processor. 

4/5 stars only because they came out drier than I planned, I think somehow the butter didn’t fully incorporate with the dry ingredients.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Baked in Nov. ‘23 for bookclub

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Baked in Nov. 2023

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can these be frozen? How are they once thawed?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made as was directed and thought it was plenty sweet before I added any powdered sugar, so I skipped that addition. These were delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
To"Su"--you can also use a blender to grind those nuts. I don't have a food processor either and my "old school" blender works just fine.

Lee

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Another brilliant dessert recipe from Sohla! Had to bake in a cake pan and slightly altered the bake time, jam amount, etc accordingly. Came out just fabulous all around! I can recommend adding a little extra lemon zest (maybe 1/2 tsp) to the reserved topping dough when you mix in the chopped pecans. Lovely :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Freeze the fresh ginger root and keep in freezer then microplane it when ready for use.  Keeps the ginger juices intact; keeps longer than fresh ginger in the fridge.  Microplane is easier and quicker than grater although your fingers can get pretty cold in the grating process.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@David
I always do this too!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@P.Kaye Hanifee 
Me too and no peeling needed

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
In the end, these came out great, and I will absolutely make them many more times. Crispy outside, chewy center, sweet and spicy. 10/10.

However, like others have already said, be careful when adding the fresh ginger to the browned butter -- it will foam a ton (like, way more than when making caramel). Either brown the butter in a larger-than-you-think-you-need sauce pot or move the butter to the bowl of the stand mixer first and then add the spices. It'll save you a mess.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wish I had seen your comment beforehand!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Sean legit! Had massive spillage and fire alarm fun, even after reading notes, and using a pan I reckoned was large enough.
Err on the side of caution, fellow amateurs. I was not expecting that!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Sean they should really add this note to the recipe. I had quite a mess to clean up!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
MAKE THIS. I just finished my third cookie (don't judge me). With  no substitutions, these are by far, the most delicious ginger cookie I've ever eaten. These have an amazing kick, and come out beautifully crackled. 

MAKE AHEAD: I made the brown butter the night before, and let it come to room temperature before the mixing. 

PAN BANGING: I banged the pans at the 5 min mark, and right after they can out. Stored in an airtight container to retain chewiness.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The recipe is not super clear, but the butter needs to be fully cooled and semi-solid. If it’s still at all runny, your cookies will spread and not have compact chewy texture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yes. This will take closer to 40 minutes in the fridge in my experience. Very important to feel for warmth through the bottom of the bowl. Thanks for the tip.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Beth J - I think this is where things went wrong for me. I chilled the brown butter and spice mixture in fridge for 20 min, but everything was still liquid and the bottom of the bowl was warm. Put it back in for another 10-15 min. Edges were solidifying but middle was still liquid. Bottom of bowl was cool. I moved ahead and then chilled the mixed up dough overnight in the fridge. They spread out pretty thinly and while there was some chew in the middle, not enough thickness for me. I may try these again, but more likely to go for a less fussy recipe. I also wondered if these should have baking powder rather than soda to puff up more?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are outstanding and have a really nice bite. For serving, we spread vanilla ice cream (softened in the fridge) between two of them, then refroze them as ice cream sandwiches. Outstanding!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @James I did the same but then rolled my ice cream sandwiches in chopped crystallized ginger. Over the top delicious and pretty to look at too!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I cannot stop eating these cookies. I love the kick this cookie gets from the fresh ginger and all the spices. I don't always have it in me to grate that much fresh ginger, but the 50g was absolutely worth it. This is my new favorite winter cookie!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Butter Goddess I buy frozen, pre-portioned grated ginger (and garlic). Brand is Dorot and they sell at Trader Joe’s.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: At the risk of annoying purists, I used leftover preserved ginger instead of the fresh so I expect my cookie was a little sweeter than the recipe.  I can't find Diamond Crystal salt where I live but the Canadian substitute seemed to work OK.  Likewise, my choices of molasses included neither blackstrap nor true molasses.  Using my "cooking molasses", I plunged ahead.  Took a bit longer and spread more than as written (or perhaps exactly as cautioned) but absolutely delicious nonetheless.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Canucklehead
Fellow Canadian here, the equivalent to unsulphered molasses here is called “fancy molasses” or “table molasses”. In Newfoundland and a lot of Atlantic provinces they use it as pancake syrup. Diamond Crystal salt can be purchased at some of the very large grocery chains. I purchase it at our local European deli/grocery.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow! These are fantastic. Chewy center and crispy, lacy edge. Cookies were the same whether I followed the directions to refrigerate for 2  hours or  froze the dough 15-20 minutes, instead. I was worried this would affect the outcome, but it didn’t. I used Trader Joe’s frozen, grated ginger (Dorot), and the flavor was excellent. Have fun eating these: they are the perfect ginger cookie.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies were really good.  Very spicy from ginger and definitely chewy.  Only downside is that they're a little greasy.  Also make sure to leave your cookies way farther than 1 inch apart (that must be a type because these cookies spread a decent amount so my first batch became one giant mega-cookie). I think you can only fit 8 cookies to a baking sheet.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Be sure to use a large enough saucepan for the butter browning!!! Mine was too small and the whole thing foamed up over the top (and all over my stove) when I added the fresh ginger + spices.  A rookie mistake for sure.  Next time I'll go straight into the stand mixer bowl. 

Didn't start over (not enough ingredients) so I tried to make up for lost butter 
by weighing it out and re-browning the difference, accounting for 15% loss due to water evaporation. We'll see how they turn out....

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I needed to cool the butter in the refrigerator for much longer than the recipe says. If you don’t cool enough, your cookies will be way too wet. Also, I suggest balling, freezing overnight, and then cooking. When I cooked the frozen balls, I ended up with the look of the cookie in the picture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Freeze the fresh ginger root and keep in freezer then microplane it when ready for use. Keeps the ginger juices intact

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Amazing cookies! Other commenters have noted, and I agree: 
If you don't have Diamond Crystal salt and use Morton's instead they do come out a bit saltier.
Use all of the ginger the recipe calls for and freeze it before you grate so that the grating goes faster. Always grate against the grain so your ginger isn't "hairy". 
VERY IMPORTANT: These cookies spread out while baking so space them more than 2" apart for a perfectly round shape. I could only fit 5 cookies per sheet.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These spread a lot for me. Put far enough apart so they don’t run into each other.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are perfection for ginger lovers.  I was lazy and only used two Tb of fresh ginger, also too lazy to grind a full tsp. of pepper, chilled in the freezer for 45 minutes, and still got some close to perfect cookies.  Will definitely try the recipe as written.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Oh lordy, Sohla! These are SO good! The only change I made was to scoop smaller portions (I adjusted my baking time accordingly). Mine weighed in at an avg of 17g. It's really hard to imagine these any bigger than that. They are exactly as advertised: crispy at the edges and almost-fudgy at the center. Plus, they leave me with a very pleasant mouth-buzz from the ginger and black pepper. I'm freezing most of this batch in ~17g balls to bake off at the slightest whim in the next few festive weeks.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Eureka! Orange/clementine zest takes these fiery devils (which I love and have baked numerous times) over the top!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these exactly as written and they are legitimately the ginger cookie of my dreams. So much ginger flavor but still very balanced. I chilled my dough overnight, rolled and coated in the sugar mixture the next morning, put them back in the fridge covered until I was ready to bake about an hour later. I baked one sheet at a time keeping the rest of the dough balls in the fridge. I baked each tray for 11 minutes since the dough was cold. Perfectly crispy edges with a near fudge like center.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were wonderful! I would use less fresh ginger next time and about half the black pepper. I also liked the batch I baked for 13 minutes better than the 10 minute batch, which had raw dough in the middle. But this is a really good ginger cookie recipe! The vanilla and brown butter are soooo good with the spices.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: To make it gluten-free, I used 1 cup of 1-to-1 replacement all-purpose GF flour and about 1 and 1/3 cup of fine ground almond flour. Also rolled them in coconut palm sugar with the ground ginger, since I was short on regular granulated sugar. They came out perfectly and look just like the picture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Butter foams up a lot when you add the ginger: beware!

They will not look done at 10 minutes but should be taken out to remain soft.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Have the mixer bowl close by to catch the overflow.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The flavor of these cookies is amazing ! They are sure to become a holiday favorite (though not for the faint of heart!) Only disappointment for me is that the tops of mine didn’t ‘crinkle’ as in the photo. Not sure what I need to do or not do to achieve the lovely crinkle.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Seriously some of the best cookies I've ever made. Made as per recipe (plus added cardamom) - no issues with spreading when baked from chilled; also tested baking from frozen and was very happy with the results (bake 13 mins). I added 3/4 tsp ground cardamom; I'll roll them in cardamom sugar next time. The ginger sugar mix for rolling makes double the amount needed for one batch of cookies. Not a particularly child-friendly recipe based on my test cases - more for the adults! ;)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I added cardamon too! (1/4 tsp) and halved the black pepper. My 6 & 4 yr olds were very happy with the cookies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Way too peppery. I put extra pepper on everything but these cookies are terribly peppery. Inedible!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just bit into my first attempt and jesus lord they are perfect. Somewhere and angel is weeping; beneath her, the ground is swollen with rain. I followed the recipe exactly, this is the perfect ginger molasses cookie 😭

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love these! Like others, I cooked slightly longer (12-13 minutes) to get the nice cracking, rotating the sheets halfway through (both top to bottom and front to back). I generously spaced them out around 2” or so and ended up with 12 on one sheet and 15 on another with just a couple barely touching after baking. I measured 30g exactly, which got me 27 cookies. One key here is to make sure NOT to press your cookies down but leave them in a ball. A slight press just to avoid rolling!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I've made these twice and love them. The first time I used blackstrap molasses and the wrong kind of sugar and they spread like crazy, very thin. Still delicious. Second time, with the right molasses and powdered sugar, plus careful measuring, they were perfect. I banged the trays when they came out and got some lovely crinkles, also threw in some finely diced candied ginger. This recipe is a keeper.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Disastrous. I’ve made for a dinner party, and the two tablespoon amount of dough place one inch + apart resulted in one solid sheet of cookie. Of less significance but worth noting: cooking from frozen per note in the recipe took several minutes longer than the 11 to 13 minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I found the cookie dough to be quite hard and difficult to scoop after I left it in the refrigerator overnight. Tha meant it took me a while to prepare each cookie. And when the cookies baked, they spread quite a bit. So, for next time, I plan to scoop the dough right after it’s made and then put the individual scoops under a covered in the refrigerator. That way, when I take the dough out of the fridge, all I have to do is roll each scoop and then put them on the baking sheet and into the oven

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Chris T same thing is happening to me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Chris T I highly recommend scooping and rolling, then putting in the fridge already shaped in balls! I popped them in the freezer for about 30 mins and baked the same day and they worked great!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Disclosure - I cannot help but improvise. Leaving notes in case other bakers have similar preferences. 

Rounded down on sugar (7/8ths) and twice as much molasses. Used blackstrap and rounded up on baking soda. Made cookies smaller and baked at 350 for 7min. Tried first batch at 375 for 8 min and they didn’t crinkle. Second batch came out perfect. Like ideal cookie perfect. The spice mix is excellent. I doubted the amount of black pepper and I there was no need to.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I adore these. Absolutely nothing to change. However I strongly recommend you bake almost exclusively from very very cold, or frozen because the texture yields better. I let it sit in the fridge after making the dough intentionally for 3 days and the flavor built was spectacular!

My husband however wanted his glazed, lol. So a quick glaze of milk, sugar and fresh ginger was made and applied.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this with the exact recipe ingredients, except I weighed out 25g balls of dough, placed them on a quarter sheet pan, and froze them. Then I placed them in a freezer bag. By baking them from frozen I made cookies that look like the recipe's picture. These are terrific-tasting and so very gingery and crispy-chewy. I agree with many readers who say the dough balls need to be at least 2 inches apart. I placed 6 on a half-sheet pan and they did not run into each other.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I don’t  know what to say. I’m blown away and lost for words, but I can croak out the following…..this is some evil skulduggery by someone who clearly knew that if we made these we would be seduced after just the first morsel and made a slave to this cookie and its recipe.  I humbly submit my willpower. You win.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yum!  The intense ginger/pepper flavors broke through my lagging post-covid tastebuds.  Followed the recipe but for ground nutmeg from the spice rack and Mortons kosher salt (missed Reid's note about the different weights of Mortons vs Diamond; didn't mind the added saltiness). I've frozen the remaining dough to bake up a fresh batch for cookie-loving guests. This recipe now joins the must-bake holiday cookie file.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made as written. Made 25 cookies at 30g each--bonus for the cook! I've been making what I thought were the best ginger cookies of all time for 3 decades. This recipe SLAPS!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This cookie just won my office-wide cookie competition! It’s fabulous!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I took all 430 comments so far, put them in my brain blender, & came up with the most surprisingly delicious spicy (but not too) cookie that is the perfect counterbalance to the holiday sweetness: 
1) left dough in fridge overnight
2) reduced pepper by 1/4 tsp
3) coating: used 1/2 granulated & 1/2 turbinado sugar, reduced ginger by 25%, & added the fresh rind of one orange, then mashed it all together until the whole thing was orange in color
4) baked for 12 minutes.

Perfection.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thank you, Nancy for doing this, my brain blender has reached its limit this year.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Space them out more than you think you should

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Outstanding recipe. Refrigerate for as long as you can stand (or your fridge real estate can bear).

I disagree with the recipe that these are best on the day they are baked--if they're allowed to rest, the spice picks up a bit and the ginger flavor comes through more clearly.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I make pumpkin ice cream and make them into ice cream sandwiches and they are pretty much to die for. It’s not easy to find a cookie that won’t get too hard when it’s frozen but these stay slightly chewy, making them a perfect ice cream pairing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These are great. I made these gluten free with King Arthur Measure for Measure gf flour. The dough was extremely hard after chilling overnight, and I had to bring it back to room temp before I could scoop it. Stuck the portioned dough in the freezer for 30 mins before baking to ensure the butter was cold again before baking. (I definitely recommend portioning the dough prior to chilling to avoid some headaches with scooping and re-chilling.) My cookie scoop was only about 1 tbsp, so I adjusted the cook time to 8 1/2 mins (with tapping at 6 mins and after removing from oven) and cooked the dough from frozen. Cookies were soft and chewy on the inside and lightly crisp on the edges. 

Note for gf bakers: I think some people may be having issues because they’re using all purpose gf flour and not gf one-to-one. Gf one-to-one contains xanthan gum and is formulated as a one-to-one replacement for recipes using all purpose (wheat) flour. If you use regular gf all purpose flour in a recipe that was not originally formulated to be gf, you need to add xanthan gum and adjust the dough hydration to achieve the right texture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious! Definitely use a medium saucepan as the butter foams to about 4x it’s volume when the ginger and spices are added.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I accidentally used blackstrap molasses, and these were still incredible, so I’m not sure what the warning is all about.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Lovely cookies! Made 25 gram balls, baked up to the perfect size, IMO. 6 per sheet. My cookies were flatter than pictured - I was sad b/c I'd refrigerated the dough overnight and even bunged the rolled balls into the freezer before baking - then I remembered one baker's tip to bang the pan down at the halfway mark during baking and once again when done. That was the trick! Came out much more like the picture. It's weird to bang them at 5 minuts when they look raw, but trust the method!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Super tasty but the black pepper makes them very spicy. Reduce pepper to make more palatable for kids!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In the middle of doing this recipe. I’ll add my comments as needed. It took about 10 minutes to get the butter an Amber/brown color. Took the pan off the heat and added the ground fresh ginger as instructed. The butter literally over flowed from the pan when I added the ginger. Salvaged as much as I could, hoping I will have enough butter and ginger to finish the recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’ve made these cookies for the last 3 years for my Christmas cookie box. They always come out perfect as written and are a crowd favorite. I do find they are the best the day of baking or the next day as they start to lose their pizzazz after a day or so.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m popular because of these cookies. I add 2 cups of big raisins after the flour, fork them 4-5 minutes into baking at a 360 oven, and I’m running for mayor. Thank you

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were fantastic! Fantastically chewy and delightfully spicy. The grated fresh ginger brought these to a whole new level above just using powdered spices. I enjoyed the addition of cardamom and a bit of clove, but that’s just my personal preference. I’ve tried plenty of chewy cookie recipes that aren’t actually chewy, but these absolutely met my expectations for chewiness. They were a little thinner than I expected but I think I probably just did a poor job measuring the flour!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Do you have a cookie contest you want to dominate? In-laws or co-workers to impress? This bold, unforgettable cookie will cement your status as a baking legend. I followed this precisely except used Morton kosher salt. Yielded 29 cookies. Thank you to those who warned about browning the butter in a larger sauce pan or transfer it to your mixing bowl before you add the ginger and spices to avoid having the butter overflowing out of the pot.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow! Spectacular!! Took Sean's (& other's) advice to mix in ginger & spices in the large mixing bowl - not saucepan.  Left the dough in the fridge for 3 days (unintentionally - "Oh, what's in that bowl in the back again?") - then baked 9 w/out rolling them in the sugar/ginger mix & ate all 9 cookies in 1 evening.  They were that delicious & not too sweet.  I left a good 2" spacing & a couple of them still mated.  So good & dare I say - refreshing?  Which is why you can't eat just 1.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I usually buy the tube of already-grated ginger for most recipes. Did anyone use that instead of grating their own? It always takes me forever to get all the skin off of the ginger.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Liz I made it with skin on—used the microplaner to grate. No issues with that!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
In Europe the only molasses I could find didn’t specify what type it was. 

I also cooked the butter in an enamel pot and it took a lot longer to brown (10 min?).

The recipe came out tasty, but the cookies were thinner and spread more than the batch I made in the US with the correct molasses. I recommend freezing the balls to keep them thicker, and watching them closely so they don’t burn. 

Also I didn’t peel all the fresh ginger before I grated and they came out nicely.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I discovered that in the UK (and perhaps in other places in Europe), "treacle" is an equivalent for molasses.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cut powdered sugar by half a cup and it was still plenty sweet. Also reduced butter to 12 tbsp. Delicious! With above changes, texture  was slightly cakey on inside and crisp on outside.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these for 2nd time today. Refrigerated dough overnight. Scooped & rolled in sugar, put balls back in the fridge until I baked them. The refrigeration completely solved the spreading problem I had on my first attempt. Banged the tray mid way through cooking & at the end to get nice cracks/the appearance I wanted. My Indian husband’s relatives gave these cookies effusive praise and he asked me to make them again, saying we gave too many away the 1st time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made the recipe as is- the cookies spread out a lot in the oven, and became crispy and thin, unlike the photo. I suspect it’s because I didn’t allow the butter to completely resolidify. 20 minutes in the fridge is simply not long enough. 
Next time I will make the spiced brown butter a day in advance and keep it in the fridge and let it get to room temp before using.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are very important cookies. Everyone asked me about them; you can tell they’re special. I made the browned butter spice mixture a couple days in advance and let it sit out all day before using it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
altitude adjustments:  extra egg, extra 3 tb flour, leveled baking soda a bit below the line
Baked on Silpat.  Always feel like parchment paper absorbs too much moisture in Denver.
8 minutes was good.
Read the frozen ginger comment after the fact, will try that next time.
Good flavor!  Love this cookie!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Jen these worked out perfectly, thank you for posting!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I had A LOT more success baking these from frozen. They kept their shape and had the perfect chew at 11 min with a hard smack on the counter when they came out of the oven.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are the best of the NYT Cooking cookies IMHO. I like to freeze the dough balls, along with a bag of the ginger sugar, with brief instructions. So they’re always handy and fresh and chewy/crispy. Life should be so easy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I have had some excellent ginger cookies in the past, but these are absolutely sublime.  That gingery sweet heat hits you right away, and the crunch of sugar coating on the exterior gives way to a soft chewy interior.  The perfect cookie bite.  A 10 out of 10!! 

A couple of tricks from the notes helped me make them as pictured:  let browned butter and spice mixture rest overnight at room temp, added 2 TBS extra flour.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious and spicy gingerbread! My new go-to recipe. Note: does not form into gingerbread men very well. The cookie dough spreads, and becomes more like some gingerbread men that need to go on a diet!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: To make gluten free use 1 cup 1:1 flour and 1.25 c almond flour

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Caroline do you mean bobs 1 to 1?  Thanks

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Tastes great, but they spread a lot in the oven so they were thin. The 20 min in the fridge for the butter to solidify wasn't enough time. When I checked at 15 mins, it was still fluid so I moved it to the freezer for 5 min. Upon removal it was slightly solid so don't know the reason for the spreading. Didn't change much of the recipe other than subbing half the black pepper with cardamom. Definitely an adult cookie.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are fantastic!  Definitely a keeper. Used a microplane to grate the ginger. Very easy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Freeze the fresh ginger root and keep in freezer then microplane it when ready for use. Keeps the ginger juices intact; keeps longer than fresh ginger in the fridge.

Diamond Crystal salt and Morton’s weigh differently even if you use the same volume. That’s why it’s helpful for recipes to identify what it was made with. One teaspoon of Morton kosher salt weighs 4.8 grams; one teaspoon of Diamond Crystal kosher weighs 2.8 grams.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hi everyone!! First time gingerbread cookie maker here, and would love to know if there was a way to make this recipe something that doesn’t spread as much and I can use a cookie cutter with? My toddlers love to shape and decorate cookies, but I hate a hard cookie, so trying to find a delicious recipe that also works to decorate and shape!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
