commentBody: We used a gingerbread cookie cutter with ours, and they turned out great. I like the substance of the original suggestion though. The kids preferred the gingerbread shape. If you use a cookie cutter, try playing around with the weight...we found that using 40 grams made a fluffier shape for the cookie cutter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Swapped out black pepper for white pepper and added chopped crystalized ginger. 11/10 for spiciness and pizzazz!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were good! I made them with cup for cup gluten free flour and blackstrap molasses and they turned out beautiful. In the future I will omit black pepper - for me, made them taste too much like a turmeric latte (healthy) - and  also cut down the sugar.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Julia I’m interested to hear your gluten-free cookies didn’t behave like mine…must be the flour I am using . I’m using one that is a mix of maize starch, rice flour, potato starch, sugar and some thickeners. What kind did you use?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Baking refrigerated dough for 12 min (vs 10 min) yielded a more crinkly cookie that was still chewy. 10 min created a more doughy cookie.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are my new all time favorite cookie!! Bold and spicy and they don't get stale. Great chewy texture. My daughter made a second batch right after the first because we ate them so fast.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I did them at 375F for 10 minutes, slamming the tray halfway through. They spread better than at 350 and still retained the crispy/chewy texture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Subbed ground ginger for fresh and they still turned out perfect

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Make sure butter is fully solidified before continuing. Liquid butter will make cookies spread

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Does anyone have a gluten free suggestion for this recipe?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are amazing! Be forewarned—I used a very small saucepan to brown the butter and once I added the ginger & nutmeg it bubbled over furiously. So opt for the bigger pan. And I used blackstrap molasses as that’s all I had and they are awesome. Best ginger cookie I’ve ever made. Intrigued by the brown butter step and all the confectioners sugar but the result is magic. Thanks, Sohla!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Pretty good. I made as directed, but added 1/4 tsp of clove and 1/4 tsp cayenne pepper. This made a very spicy cookie! I also poured the hot browned butter from the pan to the mixing bowl of the KitchenAid before adding the spices. 

The requirement to use confectioners' sugar is a bit fussy. I wonder if they'd turn out using standard granulated sugar?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I am no expert, but I suspect that granulated would melt and result in a very loose crumb. Syrup or a concentrated sugar solution might avoid that?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Aaron K.
“Pretty good”? Is the understatement of the decade :)) !  These are amazing.  I read elsewhere that regular granulated sugar would melt and spread more, being thinner, would not be chewy in center. Frequent bakers often have confectioners sugar on hand.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I've made this recipe a few times and they are always so good. This Christmas my sister was in town and so I made them vegan. I subbed a 1/4 cup apple sauce for the egg and Miyoko's vegan butter and the cookies ended up just as flavorful and fluffy as the original version, they were a hit! The vegan butter also browned really well I was surprised.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these with butter and they were not chewy at all, tasty but very crisp. I substituted a vegetable oil spread the second time, nice and chewy on the inside, crisp edges.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are amazing. I followed the recipe exactly except that i reduced the sugar to 200 grams and that was perfect for me. I also got a little lazy and didn't make the coating, instead rolling them in a little turbinado sugar. I also made them smaller so I'd get more cookies out of the batch. For the smaller size, I found a slightly lower oven temperature (360) for about 8 minutes worked well. Regarding the fresh ginger, two pieces that were about 2" each got me to 50 grams.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
My family and friends said these were the best cookies they ever had! We all love ginger!
I appreciated the notes on the different kosher salt measurements to use for different brands…
I will always bake this when having family or friends over or going somewhere when I need to bring something!
Delicious, despite the fact that I substituted canola oil for butter as we have a genetic cholesterol problem.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm perennially in search of a recipe that recreates a treasured childhood memory: chewy ginger molasses cookies from a mall coffee chain Paradise Bakery. This recipe nailed the flavor but mine came out dry and airy. I followed recipe to the T. What am I doing wrong? Baking at sea level in a gas oven.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Listen to the comments… these stick together. An inch isn’t enough. I did mine from frozen like the note says and they still spread! I measured to the gram, no substitutions. I have a full sized baking sheet. They all fit with 1.5” clearance. Next time I would bake in 2 batches. I got 4 pretty cookies. The other 22 are all square edges and ugly. Not winning the Christmas party this year for sure. 

They are delicious though! The spice is warming and perfect salty sweetness. Not too harsh. Cozy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these vegan for my kid with milk and egg allergies and they were soooo good! Used Miyokos brand unsalted vegan butter (contains nuts) and Bobs Red Mill egg replacer. The vegan butter browns nicely, but it doesn’t fizzle out the same way real butter does, so no need to wait for that cue. An it took closer to an hour for the butter mixture to set back up in the refrigerator before adding the other ingredients. We all loved them, and you’d never know they were vegan!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Same as a previous commenter, mine looked nothing like the photo.  They were much lighter in color and puffed up.  So flavorful though! I followed directions to a T except using Alessi Coarse Sea Salt instead of Diamond. I was looking forward to a darker, flat cookie but am no less satisfied; they are delicous! Thanks to others' hints as to watching out for spices bubbling up in butter.  Also, I'd never heard of banging the cookie pan, researched it and tried it.  Still puffy cookies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Adore these cookies!  I think the key to their intense yet smooth flavor is blooming the spices in the hot browned butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If I cut these out with a gingerbread man cutter, will they make adorable puffy Michelin Men or blobs? Asking for a friend...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For my fellow dairy-free bakers - I knew dairy free butter wouldn't brown, so I improvised! I ended up just mixing the ginger and spices with the butter and carrying on like a regular cookie. Nope. Super greasy and flat, but still a great flavor. So I salvaged by warming up dough and adding more flour - until it was barely sticky. Then re-chilled and baked. MUCH better results - not exactly chewy, more like crisp on the outside and pillowy on the inside. Super delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I tried a gluten-free version, using half almond flour and half oat flour. Cookies were extremely runny/spready at first (maybe because I didn’t let the butter cool enough, based on other comments) and also were very quickly getting bubbly & lacey @ 350. To fix, I used *very* small scoops of dough (about 1 tbsp, 2+ in apart) and I turned the oven down to 300 — they cooked slowly, and instead of the chewy centers, this resulted in a beautifully crispy, light melt-in-your-mouth texture. So good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were fantastic. Mine came out completely deflated so were named Flat Stanley cookies. Not sure what happened though I did not have the right amount of powdered sugar and had to add a bit of granulated. As one commenter pointed out, they were pretty buttery for this type of cookie. Not sure why the note says they are best eaten on the day of baking. Three days later, mine are still extremely yummy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: SUCH a good recipe. I ended up accidentally purchasing about half the ginger required for this and it turned out completely find and totally ginger-y enough. Also made 12 cookies instead of 24 (they were 2.4oz each) and they were a great size. Everyone was asking for the recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these twice w gluten free flour. They come out terrible but taste delicious! Stick to regular flour!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
To make these, one requires patience, the determination to see things through, and a willingness to try new things. It’s analogous to trying to win the heart of a beautiful woman. worth it though, these are delicious, delicate, cozy, and Christmassy. Anyways:

Ingredients: Canadian here, huge thanks to my patriots in the comments. Confirming that it’s fancy molasses and icing sugar. 
Dough: I used a peeler and then a grater. I’m not sure why there are so many people complaining about this, it goes fast. I didn’t freeze the ginger. I learned from the comments and used a pot 4x as big as the butter. Weirdly, when browning the butter foamed up to near the rim so definitely use a big pot - but, I took it off the heat for 4 min and then added the spices, and there was no impact. 
Baking: I rolled mine half the size (one tbsp), froze, and then baked directly from frozen at 375 for 10 min. I made a double batch, was about 75 cookies. watch the last min as these burn fast. Also they do indeed spread a lot, leave a good two inches. 

*These are FUSSY, they need a lot of attention when baking. You need to pan bang, rotate, cool for a very long time, etc. 
* My only regret is not rolling these into cylinders so that the cookies were taller in the middle. I did spheres and they’re flat. Still delish but not as chewy as they could be. 
*These have a real kick to them and are NOT kid friendly. I made them for a family Christmas get together and had to make chocolate chip for the kids.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: No one else noted that the butter foams like crazy and overflows while you are browning it, before putting in the ginger. I opted to use a small saucepan and put the ginger in later, and in the browning process the butter foamed over. I had enough for a double batch, but after the butter overflowed from my saucepan I only had enough butter for a single batch. Beware of butter overflow!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Chewy, gingery, taste explosions! These were quite popular here, great accompaniment to hot cocoa on a cold day. The weight notation on the fresh grated ginger is appreciated--50 grams is a large mound! Next time I may replace some or add chopped candied ginger for another ginger note and some texture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used blackstrap molasses even though it says not to and they were amazing— so if that’s all you’ve got, don’t worry about it. I put them in a Christmas cookie box and everyone was requesting more of these ones. I have made them quite a few times now and after baking for 10 min they were slightly underdone. I think 11 minutes is really the right number. That could be because of the blackstrap molasses.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have noticed recent recipes from the Times asking for Diamond Crystal kosher salt. I don't know why they single out this brand. I just buy kosher salt and if happens to be Diamond Crystal that's fine, and if not, also fine. I've never had a problem with any kosher salt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: They specify because Diamond Crystal is coarser than Morton's (probably the main other readily available kosher salt), so if you use the same amount of Morton's salt as is called for in Diamond Crystal salt, it'll be a lot saltier.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Diamond Crystal salt and Morton’s weigh differently even if you use the same volume. That’s why it’s helpful for recipes to identify what it was made with. One teaspoon of Morton kosher salt weighs 4.8 grams; one teaspoon of Diamond Crystal kosher weighs 2.8 grams. There’s a good Cooks a illustrated article on the difference.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
My 16 year old son and his girlfriend made these and they were absolutely perfect. They literally looked straight from the picture and tasted
 delicious. After refrigeration, they needed to warm up a bit to be able to scoop them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This really is an excellent cookie! So gingery spicy, but soft and deliciously sweet! Will make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Increased the recipe by half without difficulties. Replaced a teaspoon of cinnamon with a half tsp cloves and a half tsp fresh ground allspice.

These were a huge hit at my holiday party. Warning - HIGHLY addictive.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love this cookie. I want to serve them at a dinner party with something else but unsure what would be the best - Ice cream? After dinner drink? Has anyone found the perfect companion?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: After dinner drink?  Try a White Russian.  Or RumChata on the rocks.  Or Baileys would be a nice companion to these cookies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Whiskey or bourbon on the rocks. Perfection!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Oh yeah, these are delicious. My mods were 3 heaping tablespoons of fresh grated ginger and a teaspoon of just plain ol' regular ground pepper. They came out perfectly.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I grate fresh ginger, after peeling, in a mini food processor and use as needed in a variety of recipes, sweet and savory. It can be frozen and thawed as needed. (I do this with garlic as well.)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Has anyone tried substituting the confectioner sugar with granulated sugar? I prefer more of a ginger snap texture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies pack a ginger punch, in all the best ways. Mine spread thinner than I was expecting, but who cares when the flavor is this good. I made them per the recipe except that I didn’t have enough fresh ginger so I use 2/3 fresh and 1/3 candied ginger. Maybe that explains the spreading flat? I’ll definitely make these again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Omg these are AMAZING. I did not make any substitutions but some notes:

I read in another NYT recipe not to bother peeling ginger before grating. I did the same here - did not peel - and it made no difference.

The first time I tried browning the butter, it burnt. I tried again; this time as soon as it got slightly brown, I took it off the heat, and it kept cooking to the right color. 

I thought 2 tablespoons seemed to much - but no, it was right! 

Can’t stop eating these!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: As I was making these, I didn’t like the smell. But they turned out to taste wonderful!  I need to stay in the oven more than 10 minutes

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Best ginger cookies full stop. Don't deviated from the recipe because the method is what makes these shine. After rolling the cookies I put them back in the fridge for 30 minutes to make sure they didn't spread too much

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My cookies turned out overspread and flat! Any tips on what I might have done wrong? Followed recipe as written…or so I think!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These tasted fantastic. But they spread a lot and were enormous. Next time I will space at least two inches apart and will make them half the size (15 g).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If you only have Blackstrap molasses, use ½ blackstrap and ½ dark or light corn syrup (ie 1 ½ T of each). It works just fine. The dough is very stiff after refrigeration. Helps to let it warm up a bit. The cookies are great!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: LOVED this recipe! Agree that smaller is better and they spread a great deal even though I refrigerated the dough for 3 hours. Thanks - a new favorite!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are excellent! Totally worth the effort of grating ginger, and extra chilling. My family is loving them!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a cookie for grownups! Loved the balance of ginger, pepper, and molasses.  WHEN BAKING GIVE THEM ROOM. One inch isn’t enough. I made them smaller. 20 grams or so. Favorite cookie this season (and I’ve made 8 kinds)!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I cooked for 10 minutes and they were a smidge under cooked. Wondering if I should cook for 11 or use convection.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: OMG, make these cookies! I didn’t read the instructions well before starting to make them and was dismayed at how long it took me to microplane 3 TBSP of fresh ginger. And then browning the butter and letting it chill was quite the task as well but it was totally worth it! I definitely agree with some others that they should be made smaller than it says and keep in mind they spread quite a bit. It says at least 1 inch apart, but it should be more. A few of mine spread into others. But so tasty..

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: great great flavor but mine didn't look at all like the photo. they spread so much they're like little gingerbread crepes. very disappointed as they were going to go in my Xmas cookie boxes but they look terrible

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Do not attempt to make these with Cup4Cup. Beyond fail. They’re incredibly greasy and flat and gross and look nothing like the picture. I hate having to adapt recipes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could only find blackstrap molasses here in Ireland, so did 1 1/2 TBS of blackstrap, and 1 1/2 TBS of maple syrup and they were phenomenal. Mine didn’t spread at all refrigerated overnight and then again while oven preheated after rolling cookie balls. They needed 13 minutes in my oven.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I used a #40 scoop and got almost three dozen cookies out of the batter.

One thing to watch out for is that the butter foams a lot when you add the spices.  Use a large saucepan to avoid overflow.

Delicious cookies, with intense ginger flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I added cardamom instead of black pepper. Terrific cookies - my new favorite!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used the exact type of molasses they said not to use, and it still turned out good

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: no more than 12 TB of butter, maybe less.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Cookies spread - use at least 3 pans
Plan ahead - two cooling periods
Fresh grated ginger can have a lot of moisture -- will spatter if put directly in hot butter. Cool butter a bit after browning

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @pam - thanks for the pan banging suggestion.  I have been baking so many cookies this season and some without parchment that for my first test batch in the Breville countertop oven I didn’t use parchment  and I had no issues.  Batch with parchment and no pan banging spread more

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used vegan butter and JustEgg and they came out perfect. Stick to the baking limit even if they seem super doughy when they first come out, they harden a bit as they cool

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Used only half the raw ginger because I ran out, and they were still killer good. 
Used Blackstrap molasses and they were still great.
I recommend doing what Sean does - put spices in the stand mixer and then add the browned butter to that; it does froth up quite a lot. 
Don't bother trying to roll them in powdered sugar to attempt a crinkle; the fat content absorbs too much of the sugar and they don't look pretty.
A golf-ball of dough will yield a thicker, chewier cookie--but space far apart!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We did two batches of these at Christmas.  This was the best liked cookie I gave out this year.  I personally loved the kick from the ground black pepper.  We followed directions exactly and I would not change a thing the next time I make these.  The effort it takes to follow the butter browning process is the one thin that will keep this recipe as something I do just once or twice a year as it’s time and labor intensive. That said, the results do justify the effort that goes into these cookies!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Roll the dough into a log before refrigerating. Then when ready to bake, cut off equal sized pieces (I got 26).  Sooooo much easier than using a cookie scoop on cold hard dough!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love these. My cookie scoop made 40 cookies, baked for 8’. Didn’t need even half that much ginger sugar.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I don't know why this cookie has no baking powder in it. I think it needs some. 

I followed the recipe exactly and got cookies that spread, charred on the edges and was inedible.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I thought I had molasses, but didn't.  So I subbed with golden corn syrup/brown sugar/coffee flavoured extract.  The cookies turned out great. (And molasses is now on my shopping list!)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these exactly as directed and was told by everyone who tried them that they were the best ginger cookie they had ever eaten.  What a hit!  Lots of work, but clearly worth the effort!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We made this recipe at altitude using Bob Mill’s All Purpose Gluten Free Flour. To adjust for altitude we baked at 390. After 2 minutes in the oven the dough was becoming soupy and bubbly and the dough balls started pooling together. We were horrified. We left them in the oven to bake until done (10mins). Our result was a very thin sheet of cookie balls that melted together. Given that the recipe is, in fact good, the result is perfectly edible. Don’t use gluten free flour!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Excellent cookies! I didn’t really know what the butter was supposed to look like when it was done so I just cooked it the appropriate amount of time and the cookies came out perfect. Spread just the right amount, nice and chewy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I couldn’t find molasses anywhere so instead used maple syrup. To make up for the lack of molasses-flavour, I rolled the cookie dough balls in brown sugar instead of white. 

I made these for a holiday party and friends couldn’t stop complimenting them! These are probably the best cookies I’ve ever made.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are good, but I'm always trying to make darker ginger cookies. I can see that sometimes the issue is the lighting/contrast in the photograph. But overall, why are my cookies always, like, blonde?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I didn’t have unsalted butter so just didn’t add any salt. Love this cookie! Place about 3 inches apart on the baking sheet. Grating all that ginger is for strong arms.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: No rise, no subtlety to flavors, leaves terrible aftertaste, don’t repeat this recipe, tossed the entire results

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Need to impress your friends? Your family? Offer aliens evidence that mankind should be spared? This is your recipe. Spicy, delicious, buttery cookies! Seriously I don't know if my family will ever recover from how good these are. To really make these extra especial, you can add candied ginger to them!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This has become my go to cookie from fall to early spring. So delicious and warm, thank you!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Help!  Nothing i do will bring the wrinkles! And it's just not a ginger molasses cookie without that iconic face. I tried banging the pans at 5 min and at the end; I tried sprinkling water  by fingertips before putting in the oven. I followed the recipe perfectly and carefully. Checked the temp with a thermometer.   Could it be that my gas oven doesn't work as well as an electric. Tried undercooking them a minute or two. Nothing worked. And YET, they ARE the most delicious molasses ginger cookie

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Re: freezing ginger, I do this all the time and highly recommend as an everyday strategy! I pop the whole piece of ginger in the microwave for 30 seconds before grating, makes things even easier, including peeling if you want to do that (I just sort of roughly shave off the sides with a knife cuz I’m lazy)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies have been incredible every time I’ve made them. The addition of black pepper adds a complexity that I didn’t know my ginger cookies were missing. When making them yesterday, I realized after starting my mixer that I was out of molasses and used the closest substitute I had, date syrup. They were still amazing and nobody else even noticed the difference.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Need to space more than one inch apart. Cookies spread a bit more than anticipated on upper third rack. Probably should have done center since I only did one pan at a time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If portioning out for freezing to bake at a later date, so you need to thaw before baking?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Winner recipe! Just what I wanted! Chewy, warm gingery afterglow. So pleased. Thank you. Couldn’t make them look like the photo - and followed recipe: chilling of the butter then the dough. Oh well. Will repeat again and again

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are incredibly delicious. I've made a batch for my workplace two years in a row now (third year this year!) and my boss told me "it was the best thing he ever put in his mouth" (and not in a creepy, I-need-to-talk-to-HR kind of way). They are so, so gingery-spicy in such a good way, and the texture is unreal. Can't recommend these enough!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were a fail for me and i have no idea why! I was so excited. They turned out like hockey pucks once cooled. Baked for 10 mins at 375. Maybe too much butter? Or i didn’t wait long enough for butter to cool (30 mins in fridge)? The flavor is amazing but these turned out more like ginger snaps on my end.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Scoured the notes to figure out how to maintain shape / reduce spread. A few things that worked for me
- let the butter mixture sit for 3/3.5 hours at room temp - it was mostly solidified but still creamy 
- put back in fridge for an hour  after rolling
Couldn’t get the crackle but didn’t spread!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For those interested in making this a GF cookie, I substituted Pamela's Gluten Free All Purpose Flour Mix (1 to 1 mix) with no adverse results. Cookies came out perfectly crunchy on the edges, soft in the middle with a beautiful crackle.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are so good. Like the others said, the ginger was perilous when added to the browned butter, so use a bigger bowl. Also, grating 50g of ginger is not for the weak, but neither are these cookies. I did cut the black pepper in half because I’m a chicken. I also made them gluten free with better batter original flour. (Cup4Cup would also work.) They are so good and perfect and crispy and sweet. And then after you eat the cookie, you get a punch of the ginger spice. A MUST make.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
V good - rave reviews
Pepper is strong - grind fine 
Baked 12 minutes

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is an amazing recipe, but somehow I always forget that the butter foams and bubbles over every single time I add the fresh ginger to the browned butter. Maybe I'm doing something wrong, but the recipe doesn't mention this at all, and it's always pretty scary when it does! Be warned.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these tonight and they’re SO good. The texture is perfect! I didn’t want to wait to refrigerate the dough so I froze it tor 30 minutes and it was totally fine. The only other thing I changed is I used ground nutmeg (1/4 tsp) because I didn’t have any fresh. Such an awesome recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I make these cookies every year to rave reviews. They are really the best gingerbread cookie for serious ginger lovers. HOWEVER, you do need to follow the directions! The last sentences in steps 1 and 3, if followed, should get you a cookie that looks like the photo. Good luck and Happy Holidays!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The spice is great in these cookies, but the texture is disappointing...very flat and greasy even though I put in fridge overnight.  I think they need more flour-maybe the 125g/cup of flour designation is too low.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The first time that I made these cookies, I loved the flavor but was not as fond of the very soft interior. For the next bakes, I made them half-sized (15 gm),  rolled them in sparkling sugar rather than the ginger/sugar mixture, and baked for 10 min, which yielded crunchy cookies with just a slightly chewy interior. Perfection (at least to me).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
My cookies looked like the picture, and this recipe is very tasty as is. 

The hint about adding the spices to the butter after moving to mixing bowl was good.  

They also do spread out.  I ended up with 18 cookies, 2 inches apart on two sheets, baked 11 min.  Not sure 30 grams was accurate?  

If you put two cookie sheets in the oven on the top and bottom, the bottom ones may end up a bit softer than the ones on top, so you might want to bake about 30 seconds longer.   This was my finding.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Wildly tasty. The flavor and texture are absolutely perfect. Having tasted the very spicy dough before rolling them in the sugar coating, I chose to cut the ginger in the sugar coating almost in half - I used just one teaspoon but it was generously scooped, probably 1 1/3 tap or so. This was perfect for me, still super gingery.

The ginger pepper spice blend works together so beautifully. Reminds me of good chai served at a Indian restaurant.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Have made these twice now- just fantastic.  Soft and crispy= the best cookie texture combination.  Flavorful as can be.  I did have to remind guests that grated ginger is very fibrous, so if they found a ginger fiber, don't think it's a dog hair in their cookie (cuz there's that possibility in our house too)
(For the record, we are a hygienic people)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were really superb! We have made countless gingerbread cookies and these are the clear winner. The only change I made was to cut the black pepper - I have an aversion to it. I increased the ginger a bit to make up for it.The taste of the grated fresh ginger really came through and the texture was great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these with my friends at a Christmas party, absolutely delicious!! I stopped counting how many I had. Air on the side of underdone when taking them out, and u left out the black pepper and some of the ginger so the littles could enjoy as well!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Too sweet! Nice spice but it’s overpowered by the sugar, I would try and cut out a half a cup.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies have the perfect texture.  Probably the best I have ever had.  As others have noted, the hot butter rises a LOT when adding the fresh ginger.  I made some according to the recipe and some without black pepper and with less ginger coating.  Both were great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Forgot vanilla and these were still amazing!  Also, had some chai mix (mainly sugar and chai spices) and used this as the coating. Yum!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were too fiery for me. I fresh ground the pepper finely and the pepper was very potent. Next time,  I’ll reduce the black pepper to 1/4 tsp-1/2 tsp and maybe reduce the fresh ginger to 2 T.  That being  said, I still ate 2 immediately after cooling with a glass of cold milk- a perfect match. I love the texture and other spices and will make them again with said reductions.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are delicious; whole family loves them. Adult son said these are the best ginger cookies he has ever had; he did not expect them to be so good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are the best cookies I’ve ever been involved in making. Yum.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made as per recipe, delicious.  Substituted honey for molasses, texture worked out beautifully.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Eat these when they’re about 10 minutes out of the oven. They’re brownie-chewy in the middle and slightly crispy on the outside. You can tasted the brown butter best then. Om nom.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I can't believe I am going to say this but....these cookies are too sweet!  It's a strong sweet taste with a separate strong ginger taste, they exist side by side but not together.  Not my fav ginger cookie recipe

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: BUTTER WILL FOAM OVER like a chemistry experiment of a volcano when you add in spices. This recipe needs to be rewritten to instruct bakers to use a large saucepan, and to warn about this chemical effect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I saved this recipe just for the fact the measurements are given in metric as well

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We re-named them Ginger Slaps because they slap you in the face with ginger. In a good way. These are SO good it is unbelievable and impossible to stop eating them. They are POTENTLY gingery.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Adding 1 cup of crystallized ginger is also a tasty move. It intensifies the whole thing. These are by far my favourite cookies. Also don’t skimp on the pepper. It is a pretty crucial add.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have made this recipe many times and it is just the best. So this time I decided why not make it gluten-free, just to be fancy. Well, they are still delicious, but it’s more like a ginger snap: they spread out so much, almost ended up like lace cookies. I put the dough balls in the freezer for 15 minutes to see if that helped the spreading: no. I’m a novice gluten-free baker so maybe I made an obvious error. To be clear I will still be serving them!! So good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Followed the recipe and pulled out very flat cookies like others in the comments. I’m shrieking. Whyyyyyyyyyyy. What did I do wrong? I chilled the butter and chilled the dough overnight. 

I would recommend Gimme Some Oven’s Ginger Molasses Cookie Recipe for those looking for a similar cookie. 🥲

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Chewy center and slightly crunchy outside with delicious flavor with slight modifications that I made after reading the comments (so glad I read them ahead of time!). I used 12 Tbsp butter instead of 16, and they’re still very rich and slightly greasy when warm. I used the full 3 Tbsps of fresh ginger (which weighed about 40 grams, not 50) and reduced ground ginger to 2 tsps. I was also careful with the salt. They are perfect!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is such a delicious recipe! I did add an extra 20 grams of molasses and cook them closer to 13 minutes for a little more color. This is a definitely keeper for my regular cookie list. So addictive!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Amazing!!!!!! I couldn't get the butter solids to brown, but these are still deliciously buttery with a warm ginger flavour. Delicious with a cup of tea.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used 45 grams of frozen grated ginger and banged the pan when it came out. Spicy, chewy, and delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I am not sure where I went wrong…followed the recipe to a T and got the most spread out/thin cookies as a result. This is so disappointing as the flavors and smells were unreal

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yes, it's easier to grate the ginger when frozen but boy does it lose its kick. My first batch with fresh was incredible. The second batch with frozen was... okay. Won't make that mistake again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @M 100%!!! I’m no scientist but if using frozen ginger should you increase the amount? Does the frozen water/ fluid it take up more space so 1 tablespoon frozen doesn’t equal 1tablespoon fresh? If you’re weighing it you probably don’t have this problem. (Nobody laugh at me for not knowing my science 🤣)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I overbaked these by about 5-10 minutes because they seemed very doughy and underbaked at 10min. As a result, they were very hard when they cooled- (but still delicious!) I read a comment on Reddit about re-hydrating overbaked cookies by keeping them in an airtight container with a slice of apple— I tried it and it totally worked! Three days later, and these cookies are PERFECT. I will reduce the powdered sugar a bit (10%?) next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Amazing!!! I recommend cooking for a 13 minutes instead of 10.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The recipe should have you bake in multiple batches. I tried to bake 12 per sheet pan and they all merged. I tried again, baking 6 per sheet, and they came out perfect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I've made these twice in the last week. Every single person who tries them loves them, including a 2 yr old and a 4 yr old who both wanted more. I did halve the pepper, but that was the only change. Do make sure the dough is cold before scooping and baking, although even very thin and flat, these cookies taste amazing!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The best flavor ever!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was looking for something with a good KICK. These fit the bill. Made them according to recipe. Grated the ginger using microplane, gotta add about 15 minutes to prep time just for that. Now I've read the hint about freezing so will do that next time. I probably didn't cook butter long enough because it didn't explode when adding the ginger, but my second time it did. Lucky me used medium saucepan. These cookies are exceptional!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Amazing. I only had 1 and 1/3 C of confectioners sugar but they came out perfectly. One of my favorite cookies recipes so far.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have made these several times and they are always a huge hit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very tasty, reduced butter by 2 tbsp as recommended by several reviewers. When baked for 10-11 minutes they are very delicious but a little too soft/doughy in the middle for my liking, I would perhaps bake a minute or two more.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are exquisite. Even though they require a lot of ingredients (definitely measure and set them out in prep bowls ahead of time) and take some patience to wait for the dough to chill twice, they're worth it. And once the balls of dough are completed, they will keep in your freezer for a long time and can be baked straight from there.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I've never added spices to browned butter like this for a cookie- the sensory experience of this was top notch, will do again many times a+

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just made these as written but I did add 1 heaping teaspoon salt f clementine zest as another commenter suggested and pulled out the sheet at 5 min to slam the pan.  Next time I’d skip the black pepper.  One man’s gingerbread is another’s snickerdoodle I suppose, but the pepper is too heavy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Full disclosure. I have never been a fan of ginger, but… I made these recently as a thank you for a group of very special people after being told their favorite cookie is ginger. All I can say is WOW! I did replace half the pepper with cardamom, though. Thanks, Sohla, I am now a fan and plan to make these again n the near future.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a god-tier recipe. I’ve made it four times, honestly one of the best things I’ve ever made. Only change I suggest is also adding chopped up crystallized ginger for added texture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Space these bad boys 3 inches apart. If you space them 1 inch apart you’ll end up with a sheet-pan cookie.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: looking forward to this but....after chilling 2 hours the dough was not at all pliable enough to form into balls.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used sea salt (table salt) and used half of what was listed on the recipe (half teaspoon per batch). Worked great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have made these three times now and each time the dough is super hard and the cookies don't spread when cooking leaving them very hard. What am I doing wrong? Also at about 5300 ft elevation of that makes a difference?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Jac this also happened to me. I’d love to know why.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wowser.  I am sooo happy.  These cookies finally met my expectations for gingerbread cookies, especially as I tweaked the recipe by reducing the vanilla to 1 teaspoon, adding 1 tablespoon of gineger beer concentrate I use for Moscow Mules, and added 1/2 cup of crystallized diced ginger cubes I purchaed online.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are a delightful texture, but the description isn’t exaggerating when it says they are maxed out on spice. My kids asked is I could make cookies that “weren’t spicy.” Most of them went uneaten by the kids, so maybe make them if you want a whole batch to yourself!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: the BEST cookies!!!! i made them exactly according to the recipe. don’t change ANYTHING—they’re perfect. this is going to be a forever recipe for sure

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Second year making these for the holidays. They are AMAZING. Only thing I suggest is chilling the dough after rolling into balls to minimize spreading.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are delicious cookies. The large amount of fresh and ground ginger, and the freshly ground black pepper give them a wonderful zippy kick, and their chewy texture is fantastic!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I agree with the majority that these are truly 5 star cookies.  No changes to the recipe except I skipped the sugar topping.  Not too spicy, crispy tender, 10 minutes perfect in my oven.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My first batch came out great- I got the crinkles in top and the chewy center immediately out of the oven. On cooling they were more like ginger snaps which I also like. I would def go for less sugar next time (put in less this time but might go for one cup only) and not sure I correctly measured the grated ginger - would err on side of more. I probably also should have been more conservative on the time. I made small ones and baked for 8 min. Next time maybe 6?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These have ruined all other gingerbread cookies for me. The spice mix is perfect. They're a fan favorite in my home. Sometimes take them out of the oven a minute early, and they are chewy enough to make into ice cream sandwiches with homemade chai ice cream.... MMM! Heaven to me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Per instructions, I cooked the butter for 7 minutes at the beginning and got so much black/brown bits that I thought I ruined it. Followed the recipe exactly and these are so amazingly good.  The flavor builds with each bite you take: heat, spice, sweet, warmth - I've never had a cookie anything like this!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Did anyone have issues with spread? First batch came out perfect. Let butter cool overnight in the fridge then brought to room temp. Did the same thing on second batch. Cookies came out gunner with lots of spread. Why?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Michelle R maybe the second batch sat out on the counter too long? Other posters from years ago said that baking them from the fridge or freezer worked out best. Today will be my first attempt at this so we’ll see what I find.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Spreads more than expected! Space em up.  Frozen prechopped ginger is weak. Double it if you go that direction like I did.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Maybe the best cookie I’ve ever made. Made one batch and then promptly threw more. 30g is too big for a pan, 25 is doable, 20 is perfect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It's the first time I'm baking these cookies. The flavor is delicious--an intense ginger flavor yet not burn-inducing-- and the texture unparalleled for us chewy cookie lovers. The only thing I'll mention is that even directly from the fridge, the cookies ran into each other in the oven. I suggest being more liberal in spacing them out than the recipe recommends.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: To make gluten free I reduced the butter by 30% and used King Arthur GF 1:1 flour but these still spread a little too big so I would recommend putting the cookies farther apart on the baking sheet

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It is a lot of ginger to grate but SO worth it. Also they stayed soft so perfect for an ice cream sandwich

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Christmas crowd pleaser!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I am not a baker. I followed this recipe exactly and all I can say is… wow these were amazing. Definitely will make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I over baked the first sheet and ended up with gingersnaps. They were great once dunked into a hot cup of coffee. The second sheet had a firmer edge and soft, chewy center. However, they didn't have the fissures featured in your version. I'll make them again for sure. I confess I gave the crisp versions to friends and kept the chewy batch for me. It's difficult to stop eating them. I'll do a double batch next time. Also, I froze half of them and they baked up beautifully.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wouldn’t make these again. They spread out way too flat, doesn’t look like the pictures. And the brown butter has no flavor in the finished product.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: my experience as well sadly

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So yummy! Don’t recommend freezing the dough, though. The texture got kinda crazy

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Subbed Maple syrup for molasses, no problem, lots of complements on these cookies , will make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Don't make the balls too small.  The cookies will be too thin, too crisp, and possibly burned.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Unusual spicy recipe, more time consuming than usual, but we followed it to the letter and the results were perfect. We couldn't stop eating until all were gone.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: lol i went to make these and realized that i’ve been using granulated sugar instead of confectioner’s in these cookies for the last couple years. they’re a little bit grainier with granulated but i actually kind of prefer it. maybe it’s just because i’m used to it now. anyways great recipe great cookies

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Any thoughts on the effect of subbing  powdered monk fruit sugar or stevia instead of sugar in these?  And almond flour as a wheat flour substitute? Trying to be better about carbs.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: That's a really bad idea here, because both the confectioner's sugar and the wheat-based flour are important to how the cookie spreads and to its final texture. You're best off looking to a shortbread that's built to work with almond or coconut flour and whose texture relies primarily on butter to hold the flour together, where you can replace the small quantity of sugar more or less safely.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
@Chdgreene
Substituting wheat flour in many cookie recipes is undetectable. I’ve been making lots of tried and true recipes with 50% wheat flour 50% AP flour. The results are consistent with 100% AP flour. Tasers cannot tell the difference. There are some articles about this out there—maybe America’s test kitchen, but I can’t remember.

You will not have a problem with the flavor or texture of these cookies if you use wheat flour. You could always start with a 50-50 blend.

I can’t speak to the sugar substitutes. Personally, if I’m going to eat sugar, I want it to be in a great dessert. This is personal choice, of course. So I don’t put sugar in anything else and I don’t eat dessert every day. I’d rather moderate everything else in my nutrition and enjoy a delicious cookie.

Happy baking!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I would recommend you only coat the top side do the cookie. The high sugar content leads to easier burning. So if you do not cover the bottom you can get a more even bake. Otherwise awesome recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are the best gingerbread cookies I’ve ever had!  Their spicy ginger flavor is addictive.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My cookies definitely needed more than 1” apart. I ended up with a beautiful flower of pull apart cookies! The taste is amazing and I will try it again. Maybe slightly less butter?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used the frozen cubes of grated ginger that I always have on hand, and based on how delicious these are, I don't think fresh or even frozen and microplaned would have made a difference.  I love those cubes of ginger/garlic/etc. from the grocery.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Anybody tried to veganize these? I'm about to try--plan to sub Miyoko's and chia "egg". I'll be mixing by hand, too--hope this works! They sound incredible!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: How did it go? So curious...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could I use almond flour?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I have a silicone gingerbread man mold that makes 24 small but thick cookies.  Does anyone have any idea whether this recipe would work?

And what would happen if I left out, or reduced, the pepper?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Nothing will happen if you leave out the pepper, it's just there for flavour. 

I suspect your gingerbread men will be more like man-shaped blobs, as this is a cookie that spreads. They'll still taste great, of course.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very spicy cookie - loved it and will definitely be saving this recipe for more batches. For some reason they were pretty underdone for me at 10 minutes and needed another 5 minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My husband took these to an office cookie exchange (Oh, Covid, your ways are not our ways anymore, evidently). I was afraid they would be too gingery, because there's ALOT of ginger in there. But they were a huge hit. The best loved cookies of the day, with requests for the recipe. Thanks, Sohla, for an amazing new Christmas cookie for my rotation.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: One of the best gingerbread cookies I’ve had in a long time. The brown butter adds a nice depth of flavor which complements the ginger beautifully. The ground ginger add a nice amount of ginger spice without it becoming overpowering. I wasn’t sure about mixing the sugar with ground ginger to coat the dough as the mixture became clumpy. I may use ground ginger powder for the sugar mixture next time. This is definitely going into my Christmas cookie mix!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Is confectioners sugar granulated sugar?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: No, confectioners' sugar is also sold as powdered sugar and is ground much more finely than typical granulated sugar. Most grocery stores should carry both.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I really loved this spicy ginger cookie! I also thought they were too big — next time I’ll try 20 gr per cookie — and they definitely need more than 1 inch between cookies unless you’re going for a sheet pan cookie! I will definitely make these again (though I think my brother-in-law thought they were too spicy).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If I use blackstrap molasses (because I'm halfway through baking these and realized it's all I have) will these still be ok? Or will they be awful enough that I should sub dark corn syrup for some instead? Or some other substitution? Going to the store to get regular molasses is not an option :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Great cookies! Definitely give at least TWO inches apart; even chilled, these fused together when I baked them about an inch and a half apart. Still good though!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Same here. Any ideas why.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with reduced sugar?  That is a lot of sugar for 24 cookies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Add some cut up candied ginger…triple g dose!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I wonder if one could make this using ginger preserve? Without adding as much sugar. Ginger preserve is easier to retain, and has other uses. It can also serve to bring out subtler flavours in the spices.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are delicious. But something went wrong. The cookies were completely flat. They didn’t rise at all. I weighed everything and followed the directions closely. I made one deviation, I doubled the vanilla. Was that enough to cause them to fail?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are delicious cookies.  The large amount of butter has a lot to do with that.  I like the added pepper but if I make them again I will not use koshering salt.  That’s just a personal preference- I don’t like the grittiness.  In my oven they took longer than ten minutes to get done - more like 12-13.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wonderful

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
if you freeze ginger to grate, do you peel it and then freeze?  Or do you grate with skin on?

Regarding Salt weighing different amounts, if recipe ingredients were shown by weight (kg) it would be so much easier!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I peeled the ginger first, then froze and it worked great!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Tasty. But after refrigerating the dough, covered, overnight, it developed the strangest texture. Very hard and crumbly. I think this is the combination of the butter and the powdered sugar. Recipe says to scoop or spoon cold dough onto cookie sheets but the Hulk couldn't have done it! Instead I hand-rolled the dough into lumpy little planetoids, using the heat of my hands to soften it. Didn't affect texture of finished cookie. But next time I won't refrigerate it so long.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not a fan. Too greasy. The size was off. When I switched to tbsp. scoop they came out better. Too sweet for me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Lovely recipe but way too much pepper!  Looking at the proportions of other spicy gingerbread recipes, one would do better with using only half of what's recommended or forgoing the pepper altogether. Make sure that you use the finest setting when grinding.  Even with a slight pepper aftertaste though - these cookies are scrumptious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love David's tip of freezing the peeled ginger root. This made the microplaning much more efficient and the cookies nice and spicy! I had to send most of these off to work with my husband...my willpower is strong, but no match for a batch of these cookies just sitting on the counter...staring at me...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I wish I had read the comments about using a big pot for the butter! I used a small pot and lost some butter when i added the fresh ginger (who knew?!).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love Ginger and the taste of these cookies is perfect! I agree Microplane is easier than grating. I mixed everything in a food processor and this helped to smooth out the spices and almond flour that was substituted for a gluten-free cookie! My new favorite cookie!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love gingerbread, and these cookies truly encapsulate the cozy feeling of the holiday season and what I love most about a good gingerbread cookie. They're so good! I didn't have fresh ginger or nutmeg, so I just continued the recipe with only the ground spices and they came out great. I got a little impatient and only let them sit in the fridge for about 1 1/2 hours, but I think they turned out pretty good! I highly recommend this recipe- it's a Christmas staple!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these yesterday. They were a hit! This was my first time using fresh ginger in a cookie recipe, and all I can say is, wow! After reading the recipe and all the reviews it was a call into action! Love this cookie, and so did the family!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Refrigerated covered overnight and w/ just a little strength I was able to scoop and roll from cold the next day, still smooth. I used the advice to microplane frozen ginger and it worked well. These are definitely very spicy but delicious. I smacked mine on the counter and they're a little too thin now, probably let them deflate on their own. 
The sugar coating called for at the end yielded 2x too much, I have the rest in a little tupperware for some other use (maybe another batch...)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: WOW. These might be the best holiday spiced cookies I've ever made. Or consumed. The ginger is such a delightful bite and the pepper really adds a tiny (but delightful) kick. They also turned out to be the most attractive cookie I've ever made. The roundness and wrinkles were simply perfection. I'll be making these every opportunity I get for sure. Sohla, you're magical.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is my favorite ginger cookie recipe to date. When rolling the dough balls in sugar and ginger before baking, I subbed some of the granulated sugar for demerara sugar (25 g of each). Compared to the granulated sugar + ginger coating, the demerara sugar addition complemented the crisp edges.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Bleached or unbleached flour?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: if i want to use slightly less fresh ginger, just to cut down slightly on spice, do you recommend adding moisture in some other way to compensate?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 10/10 Best Ginger cookies I've ever had.. combo of *fresh* ginger & black pepper is divine—will be on repeat every Holiday :) We made these gluten free with a 1-to-1 all purpose flour and they were perfect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are the best ginger cookies I have ever eaten. The black pepper makes the recipe. Delicious and easy. These won’t just be for the holidays in my house.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are very good, but a bit fussy to put together in my opinion. Like others have noted, I added the spices and ginger directly into a smallish saucepan of melted butter only to find the mixture foam uncontrollably, and lost a considerable portion of butter down the sink. I estimated around 25% loss and modified other ingredient amounts accordingly. End result is enjoyable but I will stick to "Grammy's Spice Cookies" for my holiday spice cookie needs.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I didn't have ground ginger so I added more fresh & more pepper. I could have cooked them for another couple of minutes for additional crispness. I just ate two and they were fantastic!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Divine. Thank you to Jenna for idea to use (thawed) ginger cubes from Trader Joe’s. I rolled chilled dough into 19g balls and froze. Baked direct from freezer at 375° (not convection) for 9 1/2 mins. Perfect!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I don't care about too many cookies - I make a ton of them, especially this time of year, but I very rarely eat more than just a taste for flavor. I could not stop coming back to these! The spice is such a lovely and delightful punch.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Excellent flavor.  I made the dough the night before so that I could spread out the steps. Mine look nothing like the photo and spread a lot.  Not sure what I did but the flavor alone means I would make them again.  Perhaps a bit smaller next time though.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delightfully spicy. I added fresh ground cardamom and cloves to the recipe, because I got excited. I made the cookies 20g each, and banged the pan about 2/3 of the way through baking. Cookies are great - chewy, spicy, and exactly what I want from a gingerbread. Thank you Sohla!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It says these are best eaten on the day.  I am making them as part of my Christmas gifts.  Will they be bad?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My absolute favorite Christmas cookie. It gives me speculoos vibes. I froze my ginger before grating, which made the process a lot easier. Sohla has done it again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
1) Weigh the freshly grated ginger; don’t use a tablespoon measure. Otherwise quantities can be wildly different depending on compaction. 
2) Make these much smaller. I’d say 20 grams max. 
3) These spread a lot. Six to eight on a sheet max at 30 grams. 
4) The balls need to be slightly flattened when you put them on the sheet so they don’t roll and touch. 
5) Ten minutes was not enough for 30 gram cookies (and my oven temperature is accurate). More like 15.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Exceptional. I added some crystallized ginger to the batter but it wasn’t necessary. Absolutely delicious cookies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Excellent recipe. Lots of zesty flavor. Wouldn’t change a thing. 

The tip on a medium-sized pot for the spices-in-butter step proved helpful. Props!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are perfect. Love the punchy flavor. Take care not to use a small saucepan as others mention but these hit it out of the park! Perfect complement to eggnog on Christmas Eve.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made Xmas 2021, drizzled with chocolate glaze

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made these for the first time for Christmas this year.  They are a universal hit!  I reduced the sugar and substituted eurythriol for the remainder.  I was then able to use sparkly decorative sugars. But the spices seem to make it a more grown up flavour.  
I had to use blackstrap molasses, but it worked.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have been craving a really good, chewy ginger cookie and these were just PERFECT. I don't often bake, but broke out of my mold for these and even went so far as to weigh every ingredient. Surprising how "off" a few of the ingredients were from tablespoon and cup to weighed, specifically the fresh ginger and butter. Since it's just my husband and me, I only cooked a few cookies and will keep the rest of the dough frozen to have on hand for later...better yet they will be warm from the oven!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I misread the recipe and left out the tablespoon of ground ginger from the dough -- thought it was just for the coating. Ridiculous, I know. I did add about 1/8 tsp each of cloves and cardamom. Even with my error, these are some of the best cookies I've ever made. Tremendously flavorful, excellent aftertaste, and exactly the chewy consistency I wanted. Banged the pan before cooling. Looking forward to making them again with that missing tablespoon back in the mix.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For extra kick, add a half-teaspoon red pepper flakes

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Many steps no prob. but the actual timing...with cookies SO buttery - what are "browned" edges..they just sort of get darker and wrinkled while center stays lighter and puffy - out of oven they collapse..but not so "brown" as pix.  then others I cooked a bit longer - bottoms BURNED...testing to see if tops could get darker.  last few was sort of mid-day...but definitely something you have to watch. All ingred. measured on scale so my ingredients were correct.  maybe just do 1 sheet at a time ??

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’ve been looking for a good ginger molasses cookie for awhile now, and this is my new go to. The flavor has some “bite” - which I love - and the texture is exactly what I was looking for. One guest said, “I’m not a ginger cookie guy, but these are REALLY good”

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The winner of our holiday cookie lineup this year, just perfect with no changes made!! Simply crisp, chewy and extremely flavorful. I must reiterate that there’s no need to peel fresh ginger, just grate it as is for a quicker process.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thanks for the tips. I weighed my Morton kosher salt and it came to one teaspoon. I weighed my flour and it was less than 2 cups. But that's what I used. Yes, even for me, the dough balls needed to be 2-3 inches apart. A bright pan cooks the cookies a bit slower than a dark pan - perhaps 11 minutes rather than the perfect 10 in the recipe. I simply slid the parchment paper to the rack with cookies still on the paper. These are delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made a quarter recipe and weighed all ingredients. I also made smaller balls. They were picture perfect and will impress a ginger cookie lover to tears.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are the best! I’ve made many ginger cookies in my time, but this recipe is our favorite.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: As I wrote previously, these are delicious. I learned that it's quite a time saver to put the fresh ginger in a food processor; I prefer my mini-Cuisinart, the bowl in the large one needs to be scraped too frequently to finely chop. I weighed all my ingredients.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Fantastic recipe! These were delicious, and I'm going to make them again today. I used 1.5 cups of sugar instead of the full 2 C, and that was just fine. Also cut back on the amount of sugar for the coating. I prefer my cookies a little less sweet.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Good, a very spicy chewy cookie. Divided dough in half and froze one half.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Love the flavor of these cookies, but I'll need to try again to get the right shape/consistency.

My cookies spread way to much, to the point that they are pretty much flat.
Things that may have gone wrong?

1. Wrong molasses? I used Grandma's Original (best buy 2018...). Doesn't say it's blackstrap.

2. Melted butter too long at too low a temperature? 

3. Dough to warm? I chilled the dough over night, but maybe they warmed up too much when rolled into balls or waiting on the counter to bake.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Best cookies ever!  Froze half the batch since there's only two of us and SO looking forward to baking a few at a time whenever the spirit moves us!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I've been obsessed with these since they were listed in "24 Days of Cookies".  The first batch turned out flat and greasy. Since then I have reduced the butter to 12 tablespoons, otherwise mix as instructed.  Don't refrigerate dough, scoop size ~17-18 grams, roll into balls, coat with sugar/ginger mix, freeze on rimmed baking tray.  Bake from frozen 8-10 minutes.  Perfect shape and consistency.  My new favorite cookie!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe is a new ‘winner’ at our household. Love the bite from the fresh ginger and black pepper. Didn’t let the brown butter mixture cool completely down, before incorporating the other ingredients and they turned out just fine. Letting the cookies rest on the baking sheet for a couple of minutes allows them to set up. Love, love, love these cookies!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are incredible.   Made as directed except used Demerara sugar with blitzed crystallized ginger for the coating.  Dynamite.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love this recipe and expect to be making it periodically. I didn't have fresh ginger and added a bit of extra ground ginger. I also added small pieces of candied ginger and it was amazing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is truly the perfect cookie. It's work to make, but all totally worth it by the end. The first time I made it, I dumped the grated ginger in the melted butter as soon as it came off the heat and it frothed up. The end result was good, but the next time, I waited a couple minutes after taking the butter off the heat to let it cool a tad before putting in the ginger, the end result was a cookie with a sharper ginger bite. I always add a little bit of ground cardamom as well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wish I knew that my postal scale wasn’t as sensitive as I needed it to be to measure out the grated ginger lol!  I PACKED my tablespoons and it still didn’t come close to 50 grams, so I just used 3 packed Tbsp of the grated ginger and hoped for the best.  The cookies had quite a bite to them, so I’ll probably go lighter next time.  I also used the suggestion to roll the dough into a log before chilling… so much easier/cleaner.  The texture was great fresh out of the oven. Thanks!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I rolled some of the dough into a log per reader suggestion and sliced it before baking. I rolled the rest of the dough into balls, as directed in the recipe.  The balls definitely had the better texture and looked more like the recipe pic.  The sliced cookies looked more like ginger snaps you buy at the grocery store.  Texture was still nice and chewy out of the oven though.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Maybe it's just me but I needed close to like 3-4 cups of flour! These were absolutely delicious!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Great flavor, a touch too salty for someone who doesnt cook with very much salt (note: i do cook with salt). I might scale the salt back to 3/4 tsp next time or use diamond (as instructed) instead of kosher salt (which may have been the issue).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this exactly as written. The cookies are good - BUT there are many ginger cookie recipes out there that are equally deep with respect to spice as well as soft that are MUCH easier to make. No need to over complicate a good thing

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Agree. This was one of the most complex  cookie recipes I have ever made.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Butter took longer than expected, but foaming did stop, and it did turn brown. The solids were the size of coffee grounds and not visible until I poured it into the bowl. For shaping, because the texture was quite crumbly, I scooped it up with my hands. Shaped. And weighed on the scale so each was exactly 30g. Taste was quite good. Crystal diamond salt gave an unusual added flavor spike. Just not sure if it was worth all the trouble. Who eats each cookie batch in ONE day?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: (Added a bit of cornstarch since I cut back powdered sugar, also didn’t want to use half egg so only used yolk)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I tried this recipe exactly, and these are incredibly good. I'm wishing for some pregrated ginger though. I never fail to grate a knuckle.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The bake needed more than 10 minutes for me, maybe watch them through your oven window. First batch I used the prescribed recipe time, the bottom was crisp, the middle was soggy. Second batch I added 3 minutes. 13-14 minutes seemed to be what I needed, but they taste so good I am going to eat all of them. I subbed 2 cups powdered sugar for regular sugar blended with corn starch, it was fine but a little more grainy. these are incredibly sweet and taste like those Gin Gin candies. Great to share

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Make these cookies. Don't wait. They are the best ginger cookies I have ever eaten.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these for the first time to be my gingerbread component for cookie boxes, and there is a definite learning curve with the butter- mine spent way longer on the counter, then fridge, than freezer, and I still probably started working with it too soon judging by the fried looking cookies I pulled out of the oven. I’m leaving these out of the box, and am freezing the rest to make and add to ice cream another time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed another baker’s suggestion and made 17 g cookies. Fit 12 on a cookie sheet. Still baked for 10 min. Recipe made 48 cookies. Perfect size. I also pressed the top of the cookies into some candied ginger granules I had. Very good and so gingery!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very good cookie. My oven required more like 14 minutes or they would be underdone in the center.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Excellent recipe. The flavors and texture are spot on. I followed the recipe exactly. Thanks for a great cookie!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these Gluten free and omitted the pepper for my 10 year old. OMG so good!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: When grating your frozen ginger, wear your thinner gardening gloves.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Based off of comments I cooked longer than the directions - mistake! Take out when they still feel VERY soft if you want a chewy cookie.  I also scaled back the black pepper and was glad I did, but I think it's actually the amount of ginger in sugar the coating that really gives a kick!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Departing from the other glowing reviews, these are disappointing. They spread to thin greasy crisps and the ginger flavor is quite muddled. Skip this recipe and go with something else.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I found the ginger too spicy, although I do love ginger. It gave the cookie a bit of a bitter aftertaste for me. Next time I would tone that down. I liked the texture so would keep the rest. I also don’t get the obsession with browning the butter, it just adds more work and washing. I don’t notice the flavor so don’t see the point.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these last year and they were perfect but then i made them again and they ended up being too cakey/puffy and not flat or chewy… does anyone have an idea what I may have done wrong? They still taste good tho :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is my new go-to gingerbread cookie recipe.  I love how spicy it is, and I love how it uses fresh ginger.  The cookies were crisp at the edges and chewy in the center -- perfection.  And they kept well -- I'm on my third day of enjoying them and they are still as good as on the first day.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: try 12 tbsp butter, 40 g ginger, full amt pepper, 70 g molasses, 300 g flour

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are very tasty and a crowd pleaser. They turn rock hard the day after baking them. I’ll be searching for another similar recipe in the future. One that we can enjoy for several days.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These are good, but they spread a lot maybe i didn't let the butter chill long enough maybe the recipe could use more flower. Given the incredible depth of sent of dough of these cookies I am a bit disappointed in the flavour. 

Thank you NY times for putting recipes also in grams!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So so so good

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Amazing ginger cookies.  Perfectly chewy and crisp all at the same time.  Everyone raved about them

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these cookies last year and we're absolutely my favorite! This year I have tried twice and the batter ends up being rock hard which makes it so I can't even bake them. I thought it was the step of cooling the butter and not having it cool enough, but the second time around I was more patient. Any ideas?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not sure where I went astray, but these didn’t crackle at all for me! Delicious sharp ginger flavor though. They didn’t turn out pretty, but tasted delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wish i'd seen the tip to use Trader Joe's frozen grated ginger, but what's a little ginger hair between friends? I made these bigger, because I don't have an 1/8 cup measuring cup, and I also don't have a cookie scoop. Eyeballed it, overshot, but just cooked them longer and made adjustments for space based on the comments here. Mine did not run together. They smell divine, and they taste fabulous.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Not blackstrap molasses regular unsulphured molasses like grandmas

Add a touch more sugar like 1/4 c 
Reduce salt if using Morton’s

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were amazing! The cookies were strong in flavor, perfectly soft in the middle, and crispy near the edges. I had a little leftover dough and ended up making about thirty-five cookies. This was most likely because I made the dough balls small; about the sizes of grapes. This was good for the spread, though, since most of them didn’t stick together. Thank you for this recipe, I’ll now bake the cookies often!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I found these to be way too spicy (and I love fresh ginger). Perhaps I had a particularly spicy root, but I used less fresh ginger than the recipe called for and still found these unbalanced. Maybe I accidentally used blackstrap molasses? The jar didn’t specify. Not really sure what was off as there are a lot of positive reviews for this recipe, but I personally found these cookies disappointing and will likely not make them again as the recipe is written. I did like the texture, though.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are PERFECT. I will be making them every Christmas.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The cookies tasted amazing but they didn't look amazing like in the picture.  But since taste is the most important thing I would make them again.  They really are best eaten on the same day.  I had a couple left over the next day and they were hard as rocks but I saved them to make a crumble on something else.  Also it seemed to take hours to grate 3 tbsps. of fresh ginger so when I got tired and still didn't have nearly enough I substituted more dried.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These spread out so much. Absolutely no crisp to the edges. May toss.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Maybe the best cookie I’ve ever made. Great, great flavor and texture, intensely gingery but also balanced, so good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Use 3/4 sugar, extra grated ginger (1/2tbsp)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Next time I will substitute Turbinado sugar (raw sugar) for the granulated sugar

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very spicy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Substituted 1 cup coconut sugar for the confectioners sugar in batter. Amply sweet. Used only 1 YBsp per cookie, and they were big enough. Two tablespoons per cookie would be gigantic. Cooked for 9 minutes. Delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
