commentBody: Okay huge slay… I used about 30 grams of pickled ginger cause i only had 20 grams of regular and they’re deluxe. I also added white chocolate chips <3

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I've made this recipe twice and immediately thought if the batter is any sign, this willbe scrumptious!  And I was right. EXCEPT, there was nothing I could do to get crinkles! The cookies are smooth and flat. Both times. It tried banging the pans at 5 mins and at the end. I tried sprinkling water from fingertips before popping the cookies in the oven.  Do you think that a gas rather than electric oven could be the problem. I can't get the crinkles no matter what recipe I've used.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The most incredible gingerbread cookies ! This recipe is perfect. Will be making every year.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious cookie! I used half stevia and half sugar. We baked them as is, in little mounds. We also took 30-40 grams and used a gingerbread cookie cutter for Christmas cookies. Found that upping the amount closer to 40 grams works well if you are using a cookie cutter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cut the sugar down to 200g and let the dough rest overnight. Otherwise this recipe as written worked out perfectly to make some very tasty cookies!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed the recipe, but wonder... Maybe half black pepper and half cayenne. Will try if I bother to make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: so good and flavorful!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Ditto on time. 10 min came out raw in the middle. Extended to 12 min for just right bite.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are delicious!!  To kick it up a notch, I added some crystallized ginger to the top mid-way through baking.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I think i like cooks one better.  These are a lot of work and get hotter each day after baking

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 10/10 *but* Made as written, mine also spread more than expected so they looked like a crinkly pan banging chocolate chip rather than the picture, which is the shape and texture I associate with the *perfect* chewy ginger cookie. Next time I'll try adding a bit more flour to see if this helps me reach the mountain top.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Wow these were delicious lovely combination of spicy, softness.
Followed exactly as written. Wouldn’t want to change a thing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are truly amazing. Mine turned out pretty good except for losing some browned butter because of the overflow that others warned about when adding ginger (didn’t see it until after the fact!). Mine did turn out a bit spicy however…perhaps ‘cuz I lost some butter but otherwise they paired perfectly with coffee!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really finicky cook time. At the recommended time these were underdone. One minute over they became hard as a rock when cooled. Good flavor but textually greasy and hard and the same time. Not worth the fuss.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
As others have noted, adding the ginger/spices to hot brown butter can cause it to bubble over (wasting your pricey ingredients and your hard work grating / grinding). I also got a hot butter burn on my hand while making these. 

They're still worth it. Amazingly delicious!

NYT, please update that step to help people avoid this risk.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sooooooo good! While I usually wouldn’t mess with a Sohla recipe, no one in my family likes molasses, so I replaced it with Lyle’s Golden Syrup and I dialed back on the fresh ginger a bit and added some cardamom. Mine came out a lot thinner than the picture and they spread a ton, but they are still a spicy chewy dream. I want to play around more with this technique because the texture (crackly yielding to a fudgy chew) is so good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly as written.  I weighed everything on my scale and chilled the dough.  My cookies spread together and were a mess.  Anyone have any thoughts as to why this happened?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: no, but the same happened to me. My butter never really browned though I simmered it for nearly 15 minutes. Maybe temp was too low? Also, the ginger never foamed the butter up like everyone complained.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I found I had to watch the cooking super close to not over bake.  Love them!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used half the pepper, cooked 11. In, smaller balls. Used about 3-4 th ginger which still wasn’t 50g

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The fresh ginger is amazing in these – so spicy, perfect level of ginger.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Although the flavor is delicious, find another cookie to make for your ginger fix. After following all directions precisely and noting other’s comments, my cookies were a runny mess and not suitable. What a disappointment

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 11 minutes from the fridge

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Oh, these are so lovely! They'll be my go-to gingerbread cookies from this day forward. Started grating the ginger, realised the effort involved, and then decided to just juice it and mix the pulp back in. Worked perfectly. Thank you for inventing these gorgeous cookies!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are delicious! Moist and chewy and they definitely have a bit of a kick.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hard to beat these cookies for explosive flavor and chewy texture. Outstanding. As others have said, recommend to transfer the browned butter off the stove into a large mixing bowl - either of a stand mixer or for hand mixing. That butter foams like the dickens once it hits the hot butter so you need some space to quickly whip down the foam in the bowl.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Once the fresh ground ginger hits the hot butter it foams considerably so recommend transferring to a large mixing bowl of a stand mixer or if mixing by hand.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Good! Theres certainly a kick, so best for an older audience. The cookies didnt turn out quite like the picture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is the second batch of these I've made - part of a christmas present for my ginger-loving mom.  Unfortunately, I realized too late that I was about 1/2c short of confectioner's sugar, but I subbed in 1/2 c of light brown sugar and they still came out absolutely delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: :-( I don't know what happened. My cookies came out flat and crispy, not chewy.i followed the recipe exactly.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: OMG the ultimate ginger cookies. I made them for Christmas this year / my family loves ginger. Next year I'll try the frozen ginger method and make them a more bite-size. GREAT recipe. BTW, I used 3/4 teaspoon of sea salt b/c I ran out of kosher salt but I also used salted butter. Thank you whoever's creation this is!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious. Make sure to brown the butter and use all the spices posted, they all really balance each other.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Make this- so spicy and good. Perfect cookies for those who love something with a kick and not too sweet. Amazing!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made a double batch and froze half. Cook just fine in the toaster oven!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Flavor of these cookies was amazing! We had a little trouble with the texture, couldn’t quite get the chewy wrinkly texture right. Any suggestions?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I tried everything (well, almost everything) from pan banging to shifting spots in the oven. Baking longer, baking shorter. And my cookies were never as pictured. Over-baking produces a cookie with a bitter aftertaste.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are by far the best ginger cookies I have ever eaten!!  For the rolling sugar, I used candied ginger and ran it through the food processor to get it smaller.  Then I added a small amount of coconut sugar to dry it out and get a more granular texture - they were delicious (by all accounts)!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Only had 1 heaping tablespoon of fresh ginger and still came out amazing. Agree with other reviews that the butter will bubble up A LOT so definitely use a large pot or bowl. Will be making again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Phenomenal! They spread way more than I thought they would and 1 inch spacing wasn't enough but it was easily remedied. Nice spicy bite to it and the perfect balance of chewy and crispy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I experienced brown butter spill over when adding ginger and spices. Will certainly making these again because they were delicious and chewy with a lot of great gingerbread flavor. Also will space more than 1" apart to allow spread when baking.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Further apart than suggested - maybe 10 per blue baking sheet.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What an interesting recipe! My first batch was too much for me - too greasy, too gingery, too pepper-y. However, I see the potential! I’m going to try again and reduce some things and increase the flour to butter ratio. They’re definitely not boring!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Super easily adapted to gluten free. Swapped flour for Pamela’s 1:1 with 1/4tsp xanthan gum and they came out PERFECT. Also threw in some chopped non-crystallized candied ginger chopped up super fine, which was killer.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are overwhelmingly gingery, in the best way. Definitely the punchiest gingerbread cookie I ever tried. Mine came out quite cakey but with a delicate crisp from the gingered sugar coating. I used jarred grated ginger from my local Korean alphabet mart, which has a touch of salt in it. I’d already frozen it in ice cube trays and stored it in cubes in my freezer, so the grated ginger part was a snap. (Pause for tsk-ing from the purists.) Try cream cheese (or CC icing) with them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Fabulous!
I did make slightly smaller (20g) cookies and place farther apart on cookie sheet. Grated 40 g of ginger until I ran out. Had to eat them hot off the cookie sheet cuz they smelled so good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Is treacle an appropriate substitute for molasses for this recipe?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
They were good, 50 grams of fresh ginger is A LOT of ginger. I got three large knobs (a little less than .2 lbs) and after peeling it, it ended up not being enough. I ended up substituted the rest of the weight with some frozen ginger. My cookies didn't end up getting that beautiful crackle like in the photo which was disappointing. 

If I made them again, I would leave out the black pepper. These were too spicy for my Chinese in-laws during Christmas lol.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’d recommend for a spicier cookie tripling the amount of cinnamon and adding some allspice but it’s an overall good cookie

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These turned out pretty good but i think i prefer a less oily/buttery/gooey cookie in favor of more texture and caki-ness 
The flavor is pretty intense, which i loved. These would be great as one choice on a cookie platter rather than a whole batch

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Finally, a ginger cookie ginger-y enough for me! Excellent textures: chewy inside with a solid texture around the perimeter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These are great cookies. The recipe tells us to freeze the balled cookie dough, then bake and serve as needed - meaning they are great for experimenting.
I made a double batch then studid the baking process: oven temperature, dark vs. light colored cookie sheets, silicone pads, convection versus non-convection and, of course, cooking time. For me: 375° with a silicone pad for 10 minutes was optimal. Readers should experiment. By the way, a teaspoon of orange zest in the dough is delicious here.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I read the notes first then made these for our after church service coffee chat.  I made the balls 15g (instead of 30g) and they came out as 50 beautiful 2.5in-diameter chewy-ish cookies with a little crunch.  I could easily put 12 balls on a standard baking sheet.  I had only half the fresh ginger on hand, but they still have the ginger burst of flavor we all love.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This recipe is labor intensive but so worth it. I thought I could grind up crystallize ginger and sugar to roll the cookies in before baking. It caused the cookies to spread as the ginger melted. The cookies really spread out. I ended up
Using them for ice cream sandwiches with vanilla ice cream. They were good but not as good as using plain ground ginger and 
Sugar. .

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Flavor packed! But mine looked nothing like the picture! Much lighter and didn’t fall like a typical cookie - just puffed up. That said, flavor was amazing. I didn’t know if they would be worth the effort, but flavor was definitely there. I added 1/3 c chopped, crystallized ginger because it’s my fave. Can’t imagine them without it. But I think I’ll tweak spices of my Williams Sonoma recipe to better match this, and keep with the simpler method.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Reduced the black pepper by half, did not need to. Excellent cookie. Pan banging good addition halfway through. Cookies will be too soft to directly transfer to rack. I pulled the entire sheet of parchment on to rack at first.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: these were incredible! A hit with my whole family at Christmas last year. I intend to make them again

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have no idea what I did wrong but these were a total fail for me. I followed the recipe to a T and used irish butter, which never really turned 'brown'. Maybe that was it? Kept in the fridge overnight and baked the next day. They spread out and took closer to 20 min at 375 rather than 10. Still chewy and flavor is good (spicy!) but they look nothing like the picture!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Make your house smell amazing!!! These taste great! I followed the recipe and the only problem I had was that the cookies spread on the pan, so put less cookies on the tray next time. Use more than 2 trays. DELICIOUS!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Great flavor but the texture/appearance turned out all wrong, despite following the instructions to a T. The cookies spread flat and didn’t have any crinkle to them. Would not repeat this recipe, especially given that they’re more laborious than comparable.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Suggest at least 2 inch spacing.  At 1 inch apart or you'll end up with each of them fusing together. 
That's what just happened to me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really delicious! Used Penzeys minced ginger since I didn't have fresh, and did just a few grinds of black pepper and added cardamom. They are very buttery and I would be inclined to cut back on the amount of butter by a couple tablespoons and see if that helps? I plan to mix up enough dough to keep in the freezer all winter long for easy access to these cozy cookies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I committed a mortal sin and used ginger paste from a tube instead of fresh. These cookies *still* turned out incredible and I can’t imagine a world in which I have the time and the will to change this method in the future. Besides that, I followed the recipe exactly. These came out sweet and spicy and are so wonderful for winter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are incredible. Spicy, chewy, and delicious. I was very skeptical of the coloring until they cooled. Absolutely divine.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed the recipe as is and they turned out great! As others suggested, once the butter is browned, put that straight into a larger bowl or mixer bowl and then add the grated ginger and spices to avoid bubbling over. Will be making these again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Spicy…. 50 g is a lot of ginger

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Pulse slices of frozen fresh ginger in blender to make a fine powder. No microplane needed.  Super easy!  Golden milk powder instead of ginger powder gave a fantastic earthy flavor.  Loved these cookies and will definitely bake again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My holiday party guests could not get enough of these cookies. I had multiple people ask me for the recipe and then follow up later to get it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are delicious. Definitely be careful of spacing as mine bled into each other.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used 50 grams of ginger paste from a tube for convenience, the cookie turned out great. This is such a nice holiday cookie.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Seriously be careful about the salt if not using diamond crystal.. these are delicious but im kicking myself for accidentally making them way too salty!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Salted butter
2 c (or slightly less) swerve confectioners zero calorie sweetener
1 tsp baking soda plus 1/2 tsp baking powder
No salt
2 cup almond flour

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Measuring out 30 grams of dough per cookie i was able to make 27 cookies. Will update once done baking as to how they came out.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These came out great

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is our favorite cookie!  It is outstanding.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are delicious! I followed advice from others, thank you, and cut the size to be baked in half to 15 gm per cookie. I overbaked as the cookie edges did not look browned - in for 10 minutes. Still delicious but more crispy then I wanted. I also left the dough in a very cold fridge for two days and had no difficulty with sticky dough when rolling the cookies. Baked some today and froze the rest for next week. A keeper!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Oh mah gawd!  Bake them exactly as the recipe says.  I don't know how they could be any better.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly with the exception of substituting finely diced candied ginger for the fresh.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are insanely good! My notes: Used 2tbsp less butter (so 12 total), and baked for ~2-3 min longer.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Smells and tastes like Christmas! Skipped the ginger in the rolling sugar and only used a few cranks of black pepper. Still plenty of ginger and spice kick. My cookies were perfectly set at 11mins. Def give the pan a bang on the rack before you take them out for a nice ripple.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It was taking me awhile to microplane the ginger so I got out my $20 coffee grinder that I used for herbs. It worked much better and was less time consuming.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I did the same thing!  I let my frozen ginger defrost and it was impossible to microplane.  The mini grinder worked quickly, although I did use a knife to chop extra fine to be sure there were no big bits.  Delicious cookies!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Third year in a row that I have made them, and third year in a row that they are the resounding favorite in the holiday cookie box! Grating fresh ginger is worth the effort.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
For me, the output didn’t meet the effort.

Did the fresh ginger or browned butter make it much better than the average gingerbread cookie? Meh. Not to my bourgeois palette. Even using a big pan there was massive spillage when I added the fresh ginger to the butter. It was a fun experiment for me, but other recipes would’ve yielded similar results with much less effort.

The cookies are good, but I won’t make them again. p.s. I love The Times’ chefs and learn something new from each of your recipes - thank you!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Don’t put on lower third of oven. Will burn.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are my new favorite cookies. I was a little worried when I tried the dough, because the dough before baking does not taste great, almost added more sugar. But I trusted the process and they are Delectable. Must makes if you like ginger cookies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are the best cookies I have ever made - chewy, slightly spicy, ginger-molasses flavor bombs. Everyone loved them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these as instructed and they turned out perfectly and were a favorite among all who ate them! While these cookies are for sure best on day one (as all cookies are), ours held up really well for  about five days.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are delicious!  I was worried about burning the butter so I did not cook the butter solids until they were deeply brown.  I will definitely be making these again!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These ginger cookies were the hit of the holiday! I was concerned my son would be turned off by the strong flavors, especially the pepper, but he wasn’t. Ginger are his favorite and these were a clear winner. If I could change anything, I would hold a touch of the pepper.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Rechill dough after rolling in balls. Or, roll balls directly after making the dough and chill in ball form. Bake the cookies in 2 separate batches. The first time I baked them together and got 1 pan of glorious cookies and 1 pan of weird, spread out ones (even after rotating pans at 5-minute mark). Also, make sure the brown butter is no longer liquid before starting the dough-making process. Think the still-melted butter made the cookies greasy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I am an avid baker and this is by far my favorite cookie of all time. Do not change a thing! I use the fresh grated ginger in a tube in the herb section of grocery store. Be sure to make enough to freeze into balls for your nightly cookie fix. I keep the ginger sugar in a Tupperware container and roll the frozen balls before I bake.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Amazing ginger sweet taste and zingy bite. Wish I had this recipe years ago! Recipe works great as is, especially the tips. I’ve frozen 10 balls for a future “ fix”.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just absolutely divine. Follow instructions and they may just be the best cookies you have ever had. I took them out oven at exactly 10 mins and it perfect timing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This is magic: "Cookie dough portions can be frozen, then coated in sugar and baked from frozen as needed. No need to thaw; bake frozen portions for 11 to 13 minutes."

I froze some, they got buried in the freezer, I found them 9 months later, baked them off in the toaster oven wondering if they'd still be any good and OH MY GOD THE BEST COOKIES EVER. I'm about to make another batch for the freezer and this time I will not let them get lost.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: can you shape these into gingerbread men?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Michelle no. they will spread and not hold shape.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can someone share with me what might have happened to my cookies?  They are more cakey than to my liking, more even through the texture rather than crisp on the edges and chewy/gooey inside--definitely gingerBREAD but not dry.My variations/failures in doing the recipe:  I used blackstrap molasses and measured it rather than weighing it; all else by grams and as called for. I may have under-mixed slightly.   I microwaved for 5 seconds the butter/spice because I thought it was too chilled.  So..?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Marguerite Hi.. do not use blackstrap molasses, use unsulphered molasses as instructed in recipe. Also, I wouldn’t put the re-solidified butter in the microwave. It will soften up once out of the fridge and mixed with the molasses. Good luck!! They are sooo good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are fabulous.  Instead of sugar and ground ginger for the coating, I ran sugar and a few chunks of candied ginger through my mini food processor.  I think that was a good call, though I'm sure they're equally fabulous exactly as written.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: remarkable...added 1 cup of pecans

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Love this recipe. I add espresso powder and cayenne pepper to mine. 
People call them the "Spicy christmas cookies" and ask me to make them every year.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can I substitute sorghum for the molasses?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
The comments under recipes makes the subscription to NYT worth it! Loved how the cookies came out, but I have to admit I made a much smaller ball and was able to get about 50 cookies to the recipe. Each cookie weigh about 
12 grams vs 22g and was a perfect size reducing the guilt having 1-2 or even 3.  I left out the pepper by accident and they were still TOP DOG!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can I make these cookies now and freeze them until Christmas?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these by weight and they had way too much butter. Excellent flavour though. Would reduce the butter by 50g if I tried it again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made the recipe as written, no substitutions.  They were amazing.  The only thing I’d note is to make sure the cookie dough balls are spaced outside sufficiently when you place them on the baking sheet.  My cookies kind of melded together as they cooked.  These were so good though.  Highly recommend!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Didn't wait the 2 hours for them to chill and they still turned out fantastic!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are SO good! The perfect amount of sweetness and ginger kick, holiday spices, etc. Balance of chewy and crunchy, exactly what I was craving. I didn’t make the sugar coating at the end and just topped with flaky salt. No notes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are insanely delicious.  I didn't have enough ground ginger so I substituted 1 teaspoon with allspice. I used plain sugar to roll them in. They needed to be spaced a little farther apart and a little more time cooking in my oven.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this recipe with weight measures. As others noted, the butter foams a lot when you add the ginger and spices, so be careful and use a large pot. I also use an oven thermometer, so I have an accurate oven temp read. Ten minutes at 375 degrees resulted in underbaked cookies for me. Twelve minutes was much better and looked like the picture. It’s a good, spicy cookie overall. I’m usually need to double the salt in recipes, but I actually found these a touch too salty, so I would maybe go with 3/4tsp to 1tsp.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these cookies and they may be the best ginger cookie I have eaten. Having a cookie scoop is key here since the dough becomes really stiff in the fridge. You can just scoop and put on the cookie sheet with a flat bottom. Make sure not to over bake, I had one batch that went 2 mins over and they were a little too crunchy. I want to add a little candied ginger next time I make them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I make these every year, they are the best gingerbread I’ve ever had

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these exactly as the recipe and these are spicey but so yummy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I baked these exactly as instructed but they are cakey rather than chewy. Does anyone out there know why this happened?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was able to make these without the stand mixer, and while they're not crackly on top, they are fudgy in the middle and crisp on the edges, which was everything I hoped for. And the flavor is out of this world!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Way too much work for the final product. 30 grams of cookie dough seemed like a lot, so I cut it to 25. Even so, they spread and connected. I cut back to 20, then 17. Some were soft in the center, some crispy. They still spread. I did chill the dough in the fridge for 24 hours. Not sure what happened.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: just incredible!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These came out exactly as advertised--soft, chewy with an intense flavor. Not too sweet.  If you were raised on hard-as-rocks Ginger Snaps, this is a tasty surprise.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Way too much ginger, almost inedible.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Oh. My. Goodness. These cookies are amazing. If you love ginger snaps, these are a wonderful chewy version.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My cookies came out like hard lumps and didn’t spread at all. I followed recipe exactly. I cooled the butter mixture overnight and then brought to room temp before using. The dough was quite still and crumbly. Cooled the dough in fridge a few hours and then cooked when cold. Don’t know what I did wrong.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Victoria I’m no expert, but you say your dough was dry and crumbly - our dough before chilling was soft and pliable, so it sounds like something in the measurements was off?  Too much flour?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Don't you need to peel the ginger before grating it? Appreciate the help here.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Mame no, peeling ginger is generally not required

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It seemed odd to me also, but I grated it unpeeled and it worked well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: freeze dried ginger works great, i bought it on MilkStreet.com@Butter Goddess

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious and easy in a stand mixer, despite the steps and waiting. Used half whole wheat flour. 2nd time I made them, used the powdered sugar measure as written, would decrease 1/4c less. Will also try 12 tbs butter instead of 14 next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are the ginger cookies I make

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Amazing recipe, truly! I didn’t realize that there’s a call for fresh ginger so omitted it, but they were still perfect. Next time, I’m going to chop up crystallized ginger and add that in for an extra kick (and probably still omit the fresh ginger).

Also, I scooped and rolled them in the sugar mix, then froze them for 30 mins, then baked them (same day) and they were perfect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hands down, the best Gingerbread cookie you will ever eat!  Next time will have to double the recipe - I couldn’t eat just one!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I make these before the holidays and freeze them in balls and bake them whenever we have guests. They make the house smell great. And they are PERFECT!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love recipes that include the scoop size for allocating perfectly even sized treats. Thank you for focusing on the details!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are amazing and potentially addictive. So flavorful and chewy. My first time making gingerbread cookies and I won't try another recipe. The fresh grated ginger and black pepper is the key. If you don't like the spice of fresh ginger/black pepper, these may not be for you. But we loved them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thanks for all of the helpful comments! I froze the ginger root and microplaned - worked fabulously. I also took the advice regarding the ginger foaming and added the butter to my mixer bowl and then added the ginger. My tongue does not like black pepper at all, so I added just a shake or two. The cookies still had a kick, were super delicious and received good comments.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
incredible cookies. this recipe is a keeper. i chilled my dough overnight. i also used sorghum syrup in place of molasses because it is what i had in my pantry. other than that i followed the recipe ingredients and process. 
the blend of spices is delicious. looking forward to trying these with hot chocolate or with atol

based on other reviews, i used a stock pot to brown the butter. that was big enough to accommodate for the foaming that happens when the spices are added.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
simply the best (ginger, and possibly any) cookies around. Followed exactly as the recipe stated. 

Instead of using a scoop, I rolled my dough into a log, chilled, then cut into even slices before rolling into by hand which yielded PERFECTLY round cookies all the same size. Also, second the comment about freezing ginger before grating on the microplane - easy peasy. Can't wait to make these again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are delicious!  I folded in about 1/2 cup of King Arthur's mini diced ginger pieces that I had left over from Christmas, and swapped turbinado sugar for regular in the ginger/sugar mix.  They were perfect after 12 minutes in my oven at 375.  The black pepper adds a perfect kick and there's no such thing as too ginger-y in my house.  Love it and will absolutely make again.  p.s. my kitchen smells amazing while they're baking!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Soft chewy GINGERBREAD cookie!
We love ginger but the amount used seemed a bit more than I would use for a small batch of cookies. That being said, 
I did reduce the fresh ginger to 1T & added an extra 1/2 tsp of ground ginger to the dough mix.
Next time, I will only use 1/2 tsp of ginger to the 1/4c sugar, to roll the dough balls in,  as it seemed a bit bitter per friends/family.
Baked for 14-16 minutes to achieve dough “set”. They were still soft and chewy and delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are fantastic. But I disagree that they are best day of baking. They are good for a solid week after baking. Perhaps even better. The flavors come together more and the texture holds its crisp/chewy deliciousness!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 3Tbs molasses is only 45g…which do I use?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I've made lots of ginger cookies over the years and these are by far the best ones. The extra work to brown the butter is totally worth it. As others have said, use a bigger pot as the butter foams up big time once you add the fresh ginger. Your house will smell incredible afterwards. The only adjustment I would make is to the sugar-ginger coating at the end. I ended up adding doubling the sugar as I found the ginger-sugar ratio in the recipe wasn't coating the cookies fully in sugar.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Oh My these were over the top fantastic. So much flavor - between the browned butter, the spices and rolling the dough in sugar and ginger…  mmmm, mmmm good. I did add candied ginger that I chopped super fine in a coffee grinder because I had some and figured, Why not?  Will make again and again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 50 grams of ginger is more than 3 tbs. I went with the weight, and the cookies were too gingery. Otherwise, the cookies were delicious. I will make them again, but go down to 40 grams of ginger.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Really good recipe. I’ll make again. Tastes exactly like the ginger snaps I remember as a kid. 
Love the chewiness. 
I needed to bake about 11 min w my oven. Next time I’ll only put in one tray at a time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Freeze dried fruit is a dried fruit, no sugar added, and has a lighter, crunchy, completely dry texture…not chewy and moist like raisins or dried apricots. Not always easy to find in grocery stores but I’ve had luck at Trader Joe’s for a good price. Great to flavor frostings without adding moisture or sugar!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are delicious. But I don't know how the yield is 60-70 cookies.  The logs and slices would have to be TINY! I got around 20, though I did make a larger log.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: When I saw the picture of this it made my mouth actually water. Can't wait. This will taste good after a wrap with fried chicken, sriracha , lettuce, and the lemon chutney from Indian instant pot cookbook by Pitre. Love NYT Cooking, it's a great place to take a break from you know what.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! I used freeze dried raspberries (can be found at Trader Joe’s) and these turned out so well. I am going to make these instead of our usual snowball/bullet cookies for our cookie boxes this year. They have the same texture but with a fruity/tart twist that is a nice spin on a traditional cookie.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were amazing!  Such a nice light crumb and the fruit flavor is strong, but not overwhelming.  I blended freeze dried strawberries, blueberries and raspberries (heaviest on the raspberry) and they are to die for.  So cute and tiny and WAY too easy to pop in your mouth!  This one's a keeper.  I froze one log for closer to Christmas and got exactly 35 out of one log, so the yield was spot on for me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used freeze dried raspberries which were great, but after blending them into a powder I should have put the powder through a sifter to get the seeds out. Still good, but probably better sans seeds.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used raspberry powder on hand from Kitchen Alchemy that I used for macarons. It was lovely here.  Let the cookies cool slightly before tossing in the sugar or it will melt into a delicious, sticky mess.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Tasty recipe. Yield is correct when making the size described. We used multiple fruits for experimentation. Cranberry, Pineapple, Strawberry, Raspberry, Apple, Coconut and Blueberry. Mixed and matched. Milder flavors were blueberry, pineapple, coconut, and apple. Cranberry was best mixed with apple. If milder flavors are used exclusively, the cookie is not flavor forward, but more of a later flavor. Not recommended alone. Pair mild flavors with bright flavors! Great with GF flour.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Is freeze-dried the same as dehydrated?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Yes.  I’ve gotten them at Trader Joe’s, they have several different fruits. I made these today with raspberries, and they were delicious. Definitely not the usual Christmas cookie!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Target is also a great source as well - lots of unique fruit flavors.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: No. Freeze-dried has a completely different texture, sort of like crumbly styrofoam. It would be pretty much impossible to grind regular dried fruit into a fine powder.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this recipe exactly as specified (using freeze-dried strawberries) and got 72 gorgeous and delicious little cookies as a reward for my efforts! It's an easy recipe with two exceptions: grinding up the fruit (my blender worked way better than my food processor or mortar/pestle); and "tossing" the cookies (I just used my fingers to "dip" each cookie's top and bottom into the powdered-sugar/powdered-fruit mixture). I'm definitely going to be making this one again, maybe with blueberries!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Which fruit was used in the pictured cookies?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Freeze-dried fruit is available on Amazon in many flavors.

Pulverizing the dried fruit (and sifting out any seeds) yields a beautiful, flavorful powder that is sensational in cookies, frosting, cakes, or even just sprinkled on toast!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I've made these with raspberry several times and they are amazing, but I tried with mango today and I wouldn't recommend it. Far too subtle for me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were alright. I made them with freeze dried blueberries. They were definitely a cool way to use freeze dried fruit and kind of an interesting cookie to put in a mixed box, but not mind blowing. Maybe try something a bit punchier than blueberry if you're going to make these. Sohla has way better recipes than this one!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made the blueberry ones too. I was apprehensive after reading your comment, so I decided to increase the processed freeze dried fruit by 5 grams. They ended up being gobbled up by people who don't like very sweet things!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Tangy, sweet, buttery, brightly colored treats! I'm a complete novice when it comes to baking and I found the dough very easy to work with. I went with freeze dried strawberry and would definitely recommend.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Absolutely loved this recipe!  Used Trader Joe’s dehydrated mixed berries.  Leftover powdered sugar berry mixture is lovely sprinkled on buttered toast!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These were really excellent. We made mango and blueberry and we’ll definitely make them again. So simple. 

I do wish the instructions had said to wait about 5 minutes after taking them out of the oven before tossing with confectioners/fruit and also specified a vague diameter for the log. But I do love this recipe and we learned quickly enough how to do it properly. 

Highly recommend.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Our family loves these. Just made a second batch. Someone may have already provided a method using a food processor, however, I really enjoyed a “one-bowl” procedure: 1) pulverizing  at least 40 of the dried fruit; 2) mixing 20 fruit powder with confectioner’s sugar for the sugar/fruit toss after the bake, cleaning bowl and reserving mix; 3) proceed with the cookie dough recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: beware of the extra sugar at end ... i would sift over cookies lightly - cookies are already sweet enough w/o being rolled in more sugar.  also easier..

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these for Christmas, in addition to the (humble brag here) fourteen other kinds I always make. They were the star of the cookie platter! Simple dough, easy to slice and bake. I did save some raspberry/powdered sugar mixture to give them another coating before plating.  Winner of a recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Question:  Does "freeze-dried fruit" mean frozen fruit from the freezer section of the supermarket, or dried fruit, such as cranberries, dates, mango slices?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Freeze dried fruit is neither frozen fruit or typical dried fruit like dates or raisins - it's the type of dried fruit that is extremely light in weight (no moisture at all) and melts in your mouth.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I just made these with FD raspberries, because I had a bag that needed using up. The dough was initially VERY soft, but firmed up well. They taste good, but I would like a more buttery or richer flavour. Relatively easy and fast to make, and adds a zippy surprise in the Xmas cookie tin. I might experiment by adding melted, cooled white chocolate into dough next time, obviously will need to adjust other ingredients…

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Insanely good. What a nice break from too-sweet! I used raspberries. Going on our every-year list for sure.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Somes times you can get freeze dried fruit powders from King Arthur Baking

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were such a fun, colorful, fruity addition to my holiday cookie baking. I made them with vegan butter and they worked perfectly. I split the batch and did strawberry and blueberry, with 60+ little two-bite cookies total. I'll be making them again for sure.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these with freeze dried strawberries from TJ. Ground in a spice grinder (grind rice beforehand to get rid of spice odors). No need to sift. Got sidetracked and let dough sit in the fridge for a few days before we got around to baking. Maybe helped deepen flavor? Tart strawberries balanced out powdered sugar well and made these addictive. We had to toss multiple times in sugar to get a good coating.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this for my cookie boxes this year. A standout for sure. Yes they are small, but lots of flavor. I used the Trader Joe’s Mixed Berry freeze dried fruit. I did use a sieve to get rid of the seeds. Took about 1.5 bags between dough and coating. The dough was quick and easy to make and froze beautifully. My daughter said it’s a new favorite…she loves fruity cookies. Even my husband who doesn’t go for fruity desserts liked them. Not too sweet and full of flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were fine. They lacked the fruity punch I’d hoped for—- I used strawberries though and maybe that’s the issue. I upped the salt after the first bunch and added an additional Tbsp of lemon juice to try for something more tart but they were still a bit lack luster. Fine. Not bad. They just didn’t have that tart, fruity finish.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Try upping the processed freeze-dried fruit. I weighed mine, so I know that I added 23g for the strawberry batter and 25g for the blueberry batter. I'm guessing it made a difference because they both turned out to be successfully tangy and approved by those who don't like cloyingly sweet biscuits.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these last week, did raspberry and strawberry, both so delicious. Rave reviews from every one that tried them. Looking forward to trying with other flavors next time I'm in a baking mood. Thanks for a great recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Added 2 more tbs strawberry fruit powder to sugar coating
Sift powered sugar before mixing with fruit powder

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was underwhelmed with these. I followed the recipe, but for the effort it isn't worth the result.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used raspberry as freeze dried fruit, quite tart tasting, and upped the amount when mixing with the confectionary sugar - the result was still nowhere near tart enough for me - added more ground fruit to the mix AND some lemon juice and dunked the cookies in that. An improvement imo.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I mixed freeze dried raspberries and freeze dried strawberries together and cut down the powdered sugar for dusting. Delicious, dainty, and very pretty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these for the first time during the Holidays this year. Trader Joe's was having a production issue (still is) w/freeze-dried raspberries which I really wanted to use; I used strawberries & blueberries instead. They were a hit! I weighed my ingredients (not vanilla & salt, obviously) and decided to increase the processed berries to 23-25g instead of sticking w/the 20g each for the batter & for the coating. Next time, I'll add complementing citrus zest to the batter. YUM!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My brother-in-law made me promise that I'll make these for his future birthdays. I see no problem with that...as these are fairly easy to put together! Plus, the freeze-dried raspberries should be back in stock at Trader Joe's by then! I am going to try lemon or orange zest with the blueberry batter; lemon zest with the strawberry batter; orange zest or finely minced candied orange peel or candies lime peel with the raspberry batter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
NOTE: use only dehydrated products that have nothing but the fruit in them. Big Lots carries an excellent line. The flavors are tart and the product comes out beautifully powdery. Do NOT purchase Aldi brand. The product is mixed with fillers and doesn’t taste as good, nor work so well.

I loved the tartness and beautiful powdery look of the finished cookie so much that I began using finishing coat on some of my spiced sugar cookies. Made stripes on the candy canes- gorgeous!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Aldi does carry the Simply Nature brand which is only fruit, no fillers.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These are delicious and way too easy to eat by the handful.

I made a split batch with three different freeze dried products: strawberry, mango, and roasted sweet corn (found the corn at Whole Foods). 

The mango was the least impressive, just kind of a generic "fruit" flavor. 

Strawberry was amazing, will definitely be making that version again come summertime.

The surprise hit was the corn, though a bit grey looking, I added some Tajin spice to the dough and sugar coating, tasted like elote!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these with raspberries, and only realized after I had ground them up that I should remove the seeds, and so I didn't end up with enough to coat them. Luckily the logs lasted in my fridge well until I could procure more, and everyone loved the cookies. I made sure my logs were 10" and though I didn't count, it made A LOT of cookies. I cooked the first pan a little long and they were a bit dry - next time I'd err closer on the side of underbaking. But can't wait to try more flavors!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: made last minute for a cookie swap using Karen's Naturals Just Strawberry. I ground them with my wand blender which was a little messy but no big deal. They were easy and unique so I think they worth trying. Flavor was intensely sweet and tangy. Not my favorite nor the kids' for that matter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are gross.  Dried-raspberry flavored butter cookies taste very strange.  And the bright red fruit powder bakes up to odd-looking muddy pink cookies.  Once you dust them with the sugar/fruit powder mixture, the cookies look... interesting.  Bright Barbie pink, which I guess is adorable?  But the dusting mixture tastes weird and dries out your mouth.  It's like eating fluoride-flavored desiccant.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made them with freeze-dried raspberries, which I found at Target. I ground them in a coffee blade grinder dedicated to spices (wiping it out before and after the raspberries) and sifted them through a strainer to remove the seeds. The sifting goes faster if you stir with a spoon.

The cookies are tart, sweet, and delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Fabulous recipe. I used the mixed berry pack from TJs, no salt for dietary reasons and the cookies were loved by all. Delicate and delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this with freeze dried mango. They were good, but I would double the amount and perhaps add some lime zest. Good solid recipe - would like to try with other fruits that have a more assertive flavor

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Delicious!  I made these with strawberry and the cookie dough looked a little like spam. I might add a little food coloring or use a different fruit next time for a more vibrant color.  

For the powdered sugar, I would let them cool for only a minute or two before tossing. And only touch the sides of the cookies once they are in the powdered sugar to avoid getting fingerprints on the surface. 

I'd make these again!  They are easy and look cute and people love them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Think twice about making these with freeze dried strawberries... They're nice but they really do look like weird salami slices... Kinda hilarious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used freeze-dried strawberries ($10 worth!) that I chopped up in the blender and then put through a sieve. I tried so hard to get a fine powder so I could avoid the sausage look that other commenters had, but it didn’t work. I was about 20 grams short with the powdered sugar but had enough for coating. I baked one tray at a time as suggested by others and agree that’s necessary. My cooling rack made marks on the bottom of the cookies so I cooled the second batch by moving the parchment paper to the rack, which also kept the counter clean.  They were so tasty and were a big hit with my family and were different from anyone else’s at my cookie exchange. The strawberry flavor comes through intensely. Made 67.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’ve had success using a bean grinder for grinding the dried fruit. Used a sieve to remove remaining seeds. Lovely recipe.  Thank you!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hugely popular in our cookie boxes.  We did Blackcurrant, lychee, plum, and plum-raspberry.  Next year I'll make the logs ahead and freeze them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Where did you find freeze-dried blackcurrants, lychees, and plums?  They all sound delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: these were ok. they tasted like shortbread. maybe I didn't cost them correctly.  I tried rolling then in preferred sugar sis veggie baking,  right after baking,  after they were cooled.. I never got them to look like the picture.  and more importantly  taste like the picture.  they were crisp and fruity, but they didn't melt away.  I think i prefer regular shortbread.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
If you like tangy stuff- these are for you. Made in raspberry and boy were they good, could eat a dozen!! 
Love these

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm very confused. The introduction mentions the importance of cornstarch, but cornstarch doesn't appear in the ingredients list or the recipe. Am I missing something?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It says that classic lime meltaways use cornstarch, but in this recipe, "the cornstarch is swapped for your choice of freeze-dried fruit finely ground in a spice grinder or blender."

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The recipe claims to make so many because traditionally this kind of cookie is very small. See the boxes of "lime coolers" in gift shops.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Truly delicious. Made one batch with strawberry, another with mango. Huge hit. Will absolutely make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I couldn't get a 'soft dough' to form, just lots and lots of crumbly bits. I did you a hand mixer instead of a paddle one, but thats the only thing I did differently. Worried they won't harden enough to make actual cookies. 

I guess worst case scenario I'll just end up with DELICIOUS ice cream toppings.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Question - the recipe says 1 3/4 cup / 224 g of flour. King Arthur flour says 1 cup = 120 g, which would be 210 not 224. Do we follow the provided weight, or use the weight equivalent based upon King Arthur flour’s direction? (Eg 224 g or 210 g). Thank you!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I would use the weight that is provided here. I'd like to think that the recipe testers weighed the amount of flour they used.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The weight would depend on the brand of flour you're using.  If you have King Arthur flour, use their weight.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It depends on the container you store your flour in and if you fluff the flour and then dip and sweep OR if you spoon it into your cup measure. I weigh my flour, so I always get 140g of flour when the amount specifies 1 cup.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Has anyone used freeze-dried fruit powder instead of freeze-dried fruit?  It seems like that would save a step of having to grind up the fruit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can you use raspberry or strawberry powder instead?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You had me at "the cookies will keep for 2 weeks at room temperature".

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: only if you hide them!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Anyone tried these with GF flour?  Curious if they'll stay together.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hi! I successfully made these gluten- and dairy-free with no issues. I used my favorite gluten-free flour blend King Arthur's measure for measure and Miyoko's vegan butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I get freeze dried fruit from North Bay Trading Company. Lots of different kinds of fruits in many quantities. You can even buy it already ground up.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: They become much more red after covered in the final sugar mixture. Don't be disappointed, as I was, if they're a bland pink right when they come out of the oven. The sugar mix will transform them!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sift out seeds after pulverizing the fruit. Necessary with strawberries? We’ll see.  It’s to let cookies cool a bit before coating makes sense so sugar doesn’t melt. Maybe use a split open cardboard roll to help dough chill with no flat bottom.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Baking these tonight. Put the dough overnight in the fridge and the dusting mix is prepped, but I may add more pulverized freeze-dried strawberries to that for a darker color and more of a punch of fruit. Hope they come out as good as they look in the photo!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used fresh lime and freeze-dried tart cherries left over from a cake project ... great taste and color!  Next time I am rolling balls instead of cutting a log.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I misread the butter quantity on this one and ended up with a melted mess, but it's turned into nice crumbles I'll use as an ice cream topping. It was disappointing, but based on reviews, I will try again because I want to love this cookie!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We had trouble getting the dough to form logs as the recipe was written. It was too dry and "scratchy," as my bread-baker daughter said. We added 1 tablespoon more of lemon juice and brought the vanilla up to 1 full tablespoon, in accordance with a lime meltaway recipe on Southern cooking site. This moistened the dough enough to shape soft logs, which are now in the freezer.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These looked amazing so I was excited to try them. I used targets freeze dried cherry blueberry and grape blend. My Cuisinart pulverized most of the fruit into powder. I did strain out the seeds. The cookies looked like little cut sausages before going in the oven. They taste very bland there is no color at all in the powdered sugar coating. They are really flavorless. I think the fruit you get must make all the difference.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these with raspberries, should have mixed them longer to make them stick together better but they sliced well. Dipped in plain icing sugar; a bit boring. Filled as sandwiches with leftover raspberry buttercream (from macarons) and that is tasty. Will try the second log dipping in the called for icing sugar and fruit mix to see if it livens it up a bit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: One oz of dried fruit pulverized is enough for the dough and also the sugar coating.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I agree that grinding up the dried fruit is the most challenging part.  The first time I used my blender - it was difficult to pick the ground up stuff out of the bottom, and it didn't grind it up evenly so there were still some larger pieces.

The second time, when I needed to make more for the powdered sugar topping, I just put the fruit in a ziplock bag and took a hammer to it - that worked much better.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I didn't think these had enough of the fruit flavor.  I used freeze dried strawberries and found the flavor just to be faintly reminiscent.  I would try these again because of the nice shortbread texture but with more fruit in the cookie itself.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious, but took 15 minutes to bake, and I think might have gone longer

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These turned out great!  Pretty as a picture, and easy as can be to prepare, slice and bake.  Make 70, and they were small, but I prefer a smaller cookie anyway, because it means you don't fill up on just one cookie!  Will make them again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious! I only refrigerated the logs for 25 minutes and it no issues. The cookies look exactly the way the dough looks (no spread or puff-up) so make sure the dough coins looks like what you want. I counted on the dusting of sugar and fruit to cover up some flaws but it did not. I tasted the batter with the 3tbs of fruit powder and decided to double+ it for a fruity punch. Maybe my freeze dried fruit (strawberry) wasn't as strong so adjust accordingly after a dough test.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I chilled only 1,5 hours instead of 2 because they seemed adequate and holding at that point. No clue how one is supposed to get 60-70 cookies out of this though. My cookies came out maybe 2 inches in diameter and I got about 40.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were wonderful!  I found the freeze dried fruit at Trader Joe’s. I could not understand why the description mentioned classic lime meltaways but the recipe called for lemon juice, so I squeezed a lime instead. The favorite on the holiday cookie tray!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: They have lemon meltaways too!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were a hassle to make (grinding the freeze dried fruit in the spice grinder) and even with an overnight in the freezer they spread out. Don’t know what I did wrong, from what I can see no one else commenting experienced any spread. Still taste pretty good, but didn’t turn out for me and not worth the trouble.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these and they are delicious.  Everyone who has tried them, loves them.   I made mine with died cherries and raspberries.  I have dried cranberries and will be making those next.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m not much of a baker but I’m kind of proud of  these. Made with freeze dried strawberries and @bobsredmill  1-1 gf flour. I had extra strawberries so I ground them up to a fine powder and, using a tiny clean incense holder, tapped a little extra strawberry powder on each. My GF siblings better love them!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Went to TJ's and they had mixed fruit and mangoes for freeze dried fruit so I made a half batch of each.  the mixed fruit came out amazing!  The mango had surprisingly weak mango flavor.  Go for the mixed fruit!  Plus, the color is better.  Since I was making a Half recipe, I went ahead and added the flour b4 the fruit and it was fine although it bit harder to get the fruit mixed in well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I just made these and they are delicious.  My batch yielded 40 cookies and I baked them for 11 mins. 30 seconds.  They melt in your mouth so not all the cookies are going to make it to Christmas dinner today.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made these cookies with freeze dried strawberries and found them easy to crush with my fingers in a tea strainer without the bother of using a blender.  The strawberries quickly absorb moisture from the air though which renders them almost impossible to crush, so do it while they are fresh from the bag.
I found they did not pick up much of the powdered sugar/strawberry coating even hot from the oven until I sifted it over them, again using the tea strainer.
Will double the fruit next time!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these with freeze dried raspberries. They were delicious

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I just threw the confectioners sugar and dehydrated fruit in the food processor. Works great!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made with dehydrated blueberries from Target's Good & Gather line and thought these were just OK, nothing special. Yield is correct if following recipe's precise dimensions. Not sure if the recipe would be better with a different brand/type of dehydrated fruit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Gorgeous. !!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Gorgeous !!    The dough freezes like a dream too.....

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could you use fruit powder? It is freeze dried, but has the consistency of flour.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used fruit powders from nuts dot com and both texture and taste are fabulous.  Will be trying mango and açaí powders next, just to see how they taste.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Has anyone drizzled chocolate on the finished cookies?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These are so amazing.  The recipe was easy to follow, and I love that there are so many future adaptations to experiment with in the future!  This time around I had dragon fruit powder on hand, so I did a light dusting with that on top of the original recipe - looked and tasted really wonderful.  (a note that all sorts of different types of quality fruit powders are at nuts.com)  

I don't have a mixer - no problem.
I also like that they are small bites.  Hard to stop eating them, though!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow these are so good! I used freeze dried strawberries from Trader Joe’s. The dough felt kind of dry when I first wrapped it up but after chilling and setting it back out for 20 min, it cut so easily. I think I cut slightly thicker that 1/4” and came away with about 45 cookies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not sure what happened—as far as I can tell, I followed the directions carefully, and I’m not a novice baker—but mine spread out so far that they became one big cookie (I spaced as directed). Disappointing and unsightly, but tasty. Others didn’t seem to have this issue, so it might be “user error.”

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It is best to bake these one sheet at a time. By the time I finished with the first tray of cookies in the extra sugar, the second tray was too cold for the sugar to really stick. Other than that they’re quite delicious. I made them with the freeze dried raspberries from Trader Joe’s

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Tart and melt in your mouth! Definitely took 13 min for me, don’t want these to be underdone. Make the log an inch wide or even thinner. I used raspberries and shifted the powder to get out the seeds.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Good recipe! I used Koyah freeze dried organic raspberry powder and added some lime zest/juice  and extra salt. I also made them gluten free with Bob’s GF flour. So good that I ordered Koyah freeze dried mango, lemon and banana powders to try with this recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are pretty addictive. They’re kind of like a grown up version of sweet tart candies. I made them a little small, which made them fun to eat. I used a combination of strawberry and raspberry powder. As others have said, it’s a good idea to strain out the raspberry seeds.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Success on first try (for me, that means it was easy). Made mine with freeze-dried raspberries. Nice change from all the chocolate, nuts, and butter for the holidays and really bright, fun color. Great addition to my holiday cookie lineup, but don't have to be limited to the winter holidays. I'll make these any day of the year. Looking forward to trying them with blueberries!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is my second year making these cookies! I use freeze dried cranberry and apple and the texture on these is to die for. I use the brand Super Garden ordered on Amazon and they have a lot of interesting options for freeze dried fruit. This recipe is so easy and adaptable, thank you Sohla!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You can find dehydrated strawberries at Aldi's any time of year. Got two bags and will try this recipe this weekend.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were nice- kind of shortbread/teacake. I prefer the nuttier flavor of Russian tea cakes, but overall a fun twist. Might pick a mildly sweeter freeze dried fruit than strawberries next time. Was thankful for dusting (added a few fine colored sugar sprinkles) bc otw the cookies looked like large sliced Vienna sausage going in and out of the oven….

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So delicious. I agree with Halina - before the dusting, my raspberry cookies looked like sliced salami lol. I really enjoy the sweet-tart flavor, definitely adds a new flavor to a holiday cookie platter. Next time I would try with a different color fruit!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a delicious recipe. Used mixed berries and gave it great color and flavor. Sugar costing tasty, but not necessary. Will try with rose extract next time for additional depth of flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were so bright and flavorful! I made them with freeze dried strawberries for my daughter's daycare holiday party. However, I don't think they were the most popular item in the lineup because they looked hilariously like slices of sausage link. In order to get 70 cookies from one batch they were the exact diameter of a typical sausage. They could have easily won a prize in a contest where they looked like one food but tasted so incredibly strongly like another. But still yummy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these while babysitting! We rolled them out and used cookies cutters, and they perfectly withstood all the manhandling a very young child could throw at the dough. Just chilled again for 10 min before we put them in the oven and they came out tender and delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Very small.  Roll shorter, thicker logs.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I tried using a blender and a mortar and pestle to grind the fruit and thought the mortar and pestle worked much better than the blender and  it was easier to clean!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made a half recipe of these and they are OK.  I love the fruit / sugar dusting but the cookies are too tiny IMHO.  If I make them again, I will make them much larger.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I wish I had used a 1" cookie scoop to get a different shape.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Yes, they definitely look like sliced salami coated in confectioners sugar. I'm going to drizzle with chocolate to improve the appearance. 
A coworker suggested making up a "cheese and crackers" plate using a bar cookie cut into cubes and shortbread that looks like crackers.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 12 min was to long in my oven. I'll try 10 but I do think you need to really pay attention at least the first time and watch to see when they're done.  let them cool almost completely before you dip in the powdered sugar, or the sugar melts.  I did mine Luke warm and the sugar layer was very thin and then melted away.  I repeated 3 times but never really built up a visibly thick layer.  however,  when I ate them, it was definitely there. it's nice and tart. I do try choosing when cooked and baking less next time.  Has potential.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Flavor 5-stars; texture 3-stars; degree of difficulty - not work the bother. Unlikely to re-make these.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made as directed using Trader Joe's freeze dried sour cherries.  They are fine--nothing earth-shattering.  It's a butter cookies with a little tang from the cherries. You can't actually taste the cherry though--it's all butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Eight types of Christmas cookies made. All devoured except this one. Days after the holiday, these finally made their way to the compost bin.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Has anyone mad these with a cookie press, instead of the roll/chill/slice approach?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
No, but that is an interesting approach.  

I'll probably try that with citrusy meltaways before I try it with this fruity version though.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: who has a freezer that can accommodate 3 sheet pans????

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Exactly my question! Great recipe if you live in a text kitchen. Otherwise...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I did a lengthy chill in the fridge before the egg/almond topping, then returned to the fridge to re-chill before the oven.  It seemed to work.  I didn't have any spreading.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Live in Canada, and then you just put them outside :P -35 degrees Celsius here this week

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just made these cookies and they are delicious.  I love how snappy they are, and the brown butter frosting is phenomenal.  I would cut back the sugar just a tad in the frosting as I found it a touch too sweet.  Also I rolled the dough out between sheets of floured parchment, which I find prevents sticking better.  The cookies themselves (sans frosting) make great little biscuits for tea and coffee.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Making these with salted pecans on top and bourbon in the filling.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: How about dividing the dough and shaping into logs about 1 1/2 inch in diameter than slicing into rounds.  Saves time, no rolling and no scraps.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Sharon - Thanks so much for the suggestion! I now make these every year and they are much easier this way. I roll them into a log, slide the log into an old paper towel roll and then slice thinly. Works great and since the people demand these every year now, I am glad you threw in that advice!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Rachel do you slice the cookies while still in the paper towel roll or just use the roll to measure the thickness of the dough?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Elizabeth-so sorry-I missed this- wrap them in plastic wrap and then put in a paper towel roll. Once you are ready to slice remove the roll and plastic wrap, let it warm just a tiny bit and then slice. Hope that helps and enjoy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Ok first, cookies are amazing. Second, these are a pain in the butt to make and you need patience. Third, doesn’t make the amount it says. I only got 24 cookies out of this. Lastly, when you take these out of the freezer and start the egg wash, the egg wash freezes fast and almost no time to get the the almonds on so have the almonds in your other hand as soon as brush the egg wash. Lots of steps and time to make these but my god the taste is unreal!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In the video, the dough had a slight crumble that she was able to bring together with a bit of coaxing. My dough came out impossibly crumbly, no amount of coaxing could save it.  It finally came together for me after adding both an extra tablespoon of butter and an extra egg white. The modified dough rolled out easily and baked up snappy. I got 68 2-inch cookies. Sweetness level of icing was right for me, love the brown butter. Tastes best next day.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
My dough came out incredibly crumbly as well. But I did exactly what you suggested and it helped immensely! They baked up snappy and delicious and even without the frosting, are PHENOMENAL with a cup of Earl grey tea.

Thank you for sharing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Anyone brave enough to try these with GF flour and want to report back?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I nominate you!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hi! I successfully made these gluten-free and dairy-free and they were fantastic! I used my favorite all-purpose gluten-free flour, King Arthur's measure for measure gluten-free flour. I also used coconut milk hard toffee and Miyoko's vegan butter (also hands-down my favorite!). You can absolutely do it! I had no problems whatsoever. I had a dinner party with a picky boyfriend and guests who all could eat gluten and dairy and they loved them!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Did anyone else’s dough come out of the mixer very crumbly? Even when I mix by hand it doesn’t seem to come together…any suggestions?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Mine came up very crumbly - I ended up almost "kneading" it together for after taking it our of the bowl and it stuck together better.
Rolled dough was still super fragile and prone to cracking but if you're gentle they stay together! Turned out great

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My dough was crumbly, too. I added about 3 tbsp vodka in 1 tbsp increments to moisten it enough to get it to come together like you do for pie crust. It worked and these cookies are delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Mine was also totally crumbly and not a dough (I read and re-read this recipe 100 times to make sure I didn't miss something). I refrigerated the dough hoping some rest time would help, but it's still not workable. I'm going to try adding another egg white and some butter like another person tried and see if that helps.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow, these were delicious. A fun project. For the filling I didn’t use a pastry bag but simply scooped with a teaspoon.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious!! Made using 1 1/2 inch round cookie cutter, good size for sandwich cookies. Rolled the chilled dough out between parchment and used a 2 mm rolling pin guide to get thin enough. Baked 8-9 minutes. This made 60 sandwich cookies. I wish the cookie had just a little more salt to bring out the salty-sweetness. I doubled the salt in the icing and added 1 1/2 tb whiskey as seen in another comment here, this toned the sweetness of the icing down. Add whiskey in when browning butter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Had to make my own brickle, everything turned out wonderfully! No way will this make 72 2” cookies, everything including the icing will make 36 halves. Also not sure why the urgency to be ready to pipe the icing, it’s just buttercream.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My icing dried pretty quickly once the cookies were together and stuck like glue! Thing that's the reason for the rush

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These were a hit - the cookies would have been good on their own and the frosting worked.  Not sure if the almonds added much depth, but they did not hurt either.  

For those without the freezer space or number of pans (like me), I cut the cookies up and then stacked them between folded parchment to freeze.  Then I took the disks out when it was time to bake.  Didn't have any issues.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can these be frozen once baked?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these with pecans instead of almonds and the are absolutely delicious. The recipe worked really well. I kept the dough in the fridge over night and there was absolutely no sticking (used enough flour for rolling).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The first time I made these the dough was too cumbly. So I tried the extra egg white.  But also, after watching Sohla's Youtube video I noticed that when she ground the toffee and flour in the processor she ground the mix very fine. I think that's important. My dough rolled out with no problems. But the cookies swelled up in the oven, which I'm attributing to the extra egg white, which I won't use next time. Also, the baking time to "brown like pretzels" seemed to be more like 15 minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have officially named this The Best Cookie I've Ever Made. Yes, it was a pain, especially cramming three cookie sheets into the freezer (had to stack one on an angle), and there are a lot of steps, but soooooo worth it! I was nervous about the icing given the instuctions, but it was not a big deal. I am being very choosy about who I share these with :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hey, as I am outside the US and Britain - would mixing any other brittle toffee work here? thanks!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
