commentBody: I believe it would. My local candy store makes the best toffee I've ever eaten. I am going to break his up in a Ziploc using a meat hammer, and it should work perfectly. I will likely be making these in mid-December and will report back!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If I use convection-bake do I rely have to do all the pan rotating? Thanks for any tips.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I also use a convection oven and I do the rotation. Unless your oven is way better than mine (Thermador Wall oven 3 yrs old) I would do it here as well. Even though convection does indeed move the air around you still get hot spots near the fans. At least I do.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The dough didn’t come together in the mixing bowl I was tempted to add a few drops of water, but the first time I use a recipe I like to follow instructions, it did come together by hand, the only change is I baked one tray at a time, they didn’t come out as dark as the picture, but these cookies are very good…only made 32, the scraps became to dry, again may be adding drops of water would help… I will next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this recipe twice yesterday and both times the dough did not come together, it was very crumbly and sandy.  I won't make these again, something wrong with my process or the recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I actually had the same problem and eventually added a few drops of water to get the dough to come together. I think my egg white was too small?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These look amazing and I'm looking forward to trying them. I will do one thing differently, though. Instead of rolling out the dough, I will make a 2-inch log, wrap in plastic and chill, then slice. Much easier and you get the same result!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I doubt this this will work, the cookies need to be very thin and even. Your knife will crumble the dough as it slices. You're trying to outsmart Sohla who thinks every step of her recipes through, if something could have been simplified she would have thought it through already.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Roger, I make other cookies this way and it actually works very well. Chilled enough, using a very sharp knife, and, very important, use parchment paper.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Woah. These are seriously good. A perfect Christmas cookie. For the busy amongst us bake the cookies on day one, then make the filling and assemble on day two.  Sohla is the cookie queen!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I used soft toffees as we don't have toffee bits where I live and the dough came out perfectly malleable like Play Dough. The cookies were snappy and sweet after baking. For me they are probably top sweet with the buttercream so I am probably going to fill them with red currant jam.
I was too lazy to roll out the floured dough a third time and formed very small balls out of the remainding dough just as I would with regular cookies but smaller. Worked very well!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are awesome! I didn’t have room I’m my fridge so I just chilled them in my garage. I couldn’t find any toffee w/o nuts so I made toffee from scratch. Couldn’t put nuts on them so ideas about toppings would be great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Amen. Who has a freezer that can accommodate three sheet pans? If you live in a cold climate, the great outdoors will provide plenty of space to do the trick!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are really tasty cookies, well worth the effort. I got only 24 cookies from the recipe, added a little bourbon to the icing, and am definitely putting these into my regular rotation.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Bourbon in the icing sounds perfect!  What a good idea.  Thanks for sharing the idea.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What is the total baking time for these cookies?  Ten-fourteen minutes total seems like more than enough, but the way this is written it seems that 10-14 minutes is the halfway point.  The directions do say “deeply browned like a pretzel,” but almost a half-hour in the oven is one well-done cookie!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are my new favorite Christmas cookies! I wish I could give them more than five stars, they are so good. Some notes for other bakers: In order to get 72 cookies, I had to roll out the dough about 1 or 2 inches wider than a standard piece of paper. Also, the recipe makes it seem like piping the cookies needs to be mega urgent, but you can take your time. The frosting didn’t harden for me until about 10 minutes after it was made.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Good but not great, probably not worth all the effort. I had no trouble getting the dough to come together, but the resultant cookies’ surface was smooth, not crackly looking as in the picture, and they were crisp like gingersnaps. Should they be? I inadvertently doubled the salt in the dough. Oops. Could that have made then crisper? It did no harm to the flavor, at least. I reduced the powdered sugar in the filling, thinking it would be too sweet as written, which was wise. Loved the filling.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Absolutely the most delicious cookie I have ever eaten. Followed the directions exactly (over two days - cookies then filling) Worth every minute of the effort.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These are unquestionably one of the most delicious cookies I've ever eaten! Time consuming but not particularly difficult and absolutely fantastic! 

As others have mentioned, the egg wash froze when we brushed it on the frozen cookies. The next day, when we baked the remaining two sheets, we used a silicone brush rather than one with typical hair-based bristles and it did not freeze. So either time in the freezer, brush type, or both may solve that issue.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I found the dough to be very very dry and crumbly. I ended up adding a small amount of water and a lot more butter. They ended up tasting great but I wouldn't make them again without changing the recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made these again for the holidays because I enjoyed making them for the first time last year. This time I actually did end up with 36 sandwich cookies (last year I think I got around 24) but I made sure to really roll out the dough to 1/8”. It’s a lot thinner than you might think but it holds up. The cookies are much snappier this time around because of it too. 

If you have limited room in your freezer like me, I stacked my sheets between parchment paper and that saves a ton of space.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are amazing, and really not that much effort.  Completely worth it - they are special.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: these. are. amazing. i didn’t find them difficult at all. made plenty. icing is amazing. i think i will be making those every year.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious! Rolling the dough out and then chilling it in the fridge before cutting significantly helped in maintaining the cookies' circular shape.  Buttercream needed a smidge more salt than called for.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are delicious! Agree I couldn’t get 72 halves with 2” - maybe I’m too scared to roll to thickness of two Pennie’s? They were still delicious. My dough was sticky but I didn’t freeze it I just rolled it and did the three sheet pans in freezer after. Agree with egg wash freezing - did we have too much water? They are a pain but directions are methodical and clear and cookies are delishhhhh

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Made these cookies yesterday and froze overnight. Today I baked. My oven is fussy so had to watch carefully. I did use my convection option just to keep the temp up as it will drop to 300 or lass in the bake mode at 350 .  Miscalculated butter amt and melted 2sticks of butter instead of 1 1/2. So had to add more p.  sugar and decided on 1/2 tsp of. Bourbon vs vanilla for the xtra amt.  

Everything turned out well, very tasty, but will make more when I’m not making other bake goods.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made the recipe as is. I ground the toffee chips a lot as someone else recommended and the dough came together nicely. Also I rolled the dough into (2) 2" logs and froze them. After an hour, I sliced and did the egg wash and sliced almonds, much easier and more manageable in the freezer!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I thought these cookies were delicious, but making them was tough. I thought it was incredibly difficult to roll out the dough because the toffee was so sticky that the dough could not be easily rolled out. I ended up eyeballing the size and just rolling them out like you would chocolate chip cookies. I found once I did that I felt much better about the whole process. Happy cookie making.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I am so excited to try this recipe. Thoughts on adding almond extract as well?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m going to be trying these Gluton Free.  I always mix various GF flours.  My plan for the flour is to use 2 parts Authentic Foods superfine brown rice flour, 2 parts Bob’s Mill All purpose GF flour, and 1 part almond flour.  And then let the dough rest in my cold kitchen for at least an hour before rolling out.  I would dust with the rice flour.  It’s always an experiment!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have a problem being gluton free as I love sweets.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The Gluten Free plan worked that I suggested two weeks ago.  Oh my god.  So delicious! I always mix various GF flours. I used 2 parts Authentic Foods superfine brown rice flour (90 g), 2 parts Bob’s Mill All purpose GF flour (90 g) and 1 part almond flour (45 g). And then let the dough, wrapped in plastic, rest in the fridge for an hour before rolling out. I dusted with the rice flour.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Does browning the butter really make it safe to keep these cookies at room temp for a month?  USDA says regular butter can be left at room temperature for "several days" before it goes rancid. The longest time recommended I've seen for keeping butter at room temp is two weeks - and only then if the room temperature doesn't exceed 70F.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I don’t know whether the browning makes a difference, but the sugar and salt in the recipe almost surely do.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these cookies today and WOW are they are amazing!! VERY worth the effort to make them - they are quite different from most typical Christmas cookies. I also didn't get as high of a yield from these cookies as other people, but I definitely didn't roll my dough thin enough (as I found out after I'd rolled them all out). It made 26 sandwich cookies for me, but I think I could have gotten to the 32 by rolling them even thinner.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What if I live in a country that doesn't sell toffee pieces? :D

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I will be making these with sugar free & low carb ingredients for a diabetic. Wish me luck.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My frosting is super runny - does just cooling it work or should I add more sugar?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These came together for me in the food processor. I just continued the recipe after it said to move the crunbled heath bits, etc into a mixing bowl. I just didn’t move it. Certainly less stuff to clean up. I also found the biscuits great but the filling too sweet. I used chopped almonds as I didn’t have sliced ones. Beware putting too much egg wash on. It made the tops an odd yellow in the spots without nuts and the leftover few I didn’t fill got soggy after a day or two.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sohla is queen. This recipe is a masterpiece. These cookies are absolutely incredible.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just made these and they’re worth all the steps! I have to say though I haven’t yet made the icing and I’m not sure I want to — they’re so good on their own. And like most others I also did not get that many cookies out of this recipe, maybe only 36 pieces total

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I wonder if using thin blanched sliced almonds would burn them in the oven. Has anyone used those? I’m having a hard time finding the unblanched regular thickness sliced almonds in the pic & video?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used blanched slivered almonds, they turned out just fine, lightly roasted and look equally good. My cookies were in the oven for 12 minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
The cookie itself is loaded with sugar but not too bad, toffee crunch is enjoyable.

However sandwiching with that buttercream frosting was not a good idea, i find it cloyingly sweet and not good :( (I only added 210gm sugar to frosting and even upping the salt didn’t do much, too bad).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I stacked the sheet pans to solve the freezer space problem.  My issue was that my dough was a bit crumbly and I only got about 50 cookie sides/25 constructed cookies.  I left the dough in the fridge overnight -- perhaps I dried the dough out (even though wrapped in plastic).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are delicious and look impressive without an excessive amount of work. As you might expect from a toffee cookie, they are a little bit over-the-top sweet (be warned!). I also found it difficult to roll out to the thinness of two pennies. It worked much better when I tried rolling between sheets of wax paper.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Well i used a whole stick of butter instead of 3 Tb.... so we will see if the butter melts out... any suggestions to this dilemma? I do not have any more toffee to double the recipe... so more flour??? Another egg white?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are good! I agree with the comment suggesting cutting back on the sugar. They are sweet and rich. The first batch I baked burned, so watch them. I think they should definitely be baked for 10 mins (with rotations halfway through) and no longer.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just made them and they are fabulous!  Dough came together easily for me.  Like others, I didn’t get as much yield as indicated. It made 28 sandwich cookies for me.  I found it quite challenging to roll the dough out to the same thickness each time, with the result that the cookies varied in size a bit.  That made it difficult to get them all browned to the same extent, even with pan rotation. But they still look pretty.  And that brown butter filling is phenomenal, not too sweet IMHO.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: On the egg wash and almond application, I did one cookie at a time and had no problem getting the almonds to stick firmly.  On the piping of the filling, I piped a half dozen cookies at a time and then topped them.  One irksome thing is that the cookies, being so lightweight, tend to stick to the filling as the piping bag is lifted up.  Need to figure out how to pipe with one hand while holding the cookie with the other.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed the directions exactly and ended up with delicious cookies! I saw some people saying the yield was low was able to get 34 cookies from this recipe and just the right amount of filling as well. You do have to be careful when applying the egg wash though, as it freezes quickly on the cookies and there may not be time to do fun designs with the almonds. I highly recommend this recipe and will be making it again in the future!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Rolling thin wasn't a problem for me and I ended up with over 40 cookies. I used my smallest scoop (1 tsp) for the filling and that was plenty.  I had just enough for the higher yield of cookies.

When I rolled the scraps it was really cold dough and cracked.  I had to let it warm a bit to roll out nice and thin without cracking.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I found the dough was way, way too crumbly, so I had to another 2 tablespoons of butter to the dough at the end. As a result, my cookies weren't particularly crisp, but at least it was possible to roll and cut the dough.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Accidentally got crushed heath with chocolate... will this be a problem??

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Freeze the Heath Bits with chocolate before using - bet they will be delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these exactly as written and they were delicious—maybe the best cookies I've made this year. Perfect blend of crunchy cookie and rich filling. They were a bit crumbly to roll out, but if you persevere and roll the pin in different directions the cracks in the dough will come back together. I'll definitely be making these again next year.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could the egg wash and almonds be put on Before they go into the freezer?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I did make these and the taste is incredible but not sure if maybe I didn't cook them enough??   They are very hard to bite into so thinking they needed to stay in the over more and become crisp?  Was afraid they would burn.  Will try again as they are that good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made some changes too. My dough didn’t get fluffy or creamy so I added more egg white and that did the trick. I also used only 1/2 cup sugar and used dark rum instead vanilla to enhance the toffee flavor. I rolled out 35 pairs so my numbers were similar; these are super thin. I used an egg white wash on the almond half, did not freeze them first and they were done in 8 minutes. In the icing, I cut down to eight T of butter and 1 1/2 c powdered sugar and applied with a small knife. Yummy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I love these cookies.  But then, I love toffee/butter pecan type profile.  So I figured I'd get to eat most of the batch.  Not this batch....served at a small family gathering with a good assortment of cookies and everyone ate at least two.

I was able to freeze the cut cookies in stages, as I could only get two sheets in my freezer.  I filled the two sheets and froze them.  After a couple hours at most I could put them into a freezer bag.  Then I finished rolling out the scraps.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I rolled the dough out between sheets of floured parchment, which I find prevents sticking better. The cookies themselves (sans frosting) make great little biscuits for tea and coffee.
Making these with salted pecans on top and bourbon in the filling.
 when you take these out of the freezer and start the egg wash, the egg wash freezes fast and almost no time to get the the almonds on so have the almonds in your other hand as soon as brush the egg wash.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delightful cookie! Watching Sohla's instructional video on YouTube was helpful. The dough was very forgiving IMO - I stuck one half in the fridge as I was rolling out the other. I also didn't have room in the freezer but 45 min in the refrigerator did the trick. Next time around, I would probably try to roll the cookies a tad more precisely (I was a little over 1/8"). The brown butter frosting is delicious - I will add slightly more salt in the future, but it's not necessary at all.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Reilly and others - my dough never came together. It literally fell apart in my hands.   I did see a note below that an extra egg white and/or more butter might have worked.  I was so disappointed; I had to scrap the whole project. :(

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really great texture and flavor. Added a little more powdered sugar to the filling bc it was super loose. These are a great festive cookie.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these as written and they are absolutely delicious. The dough came together nicely for me and I would have been able to cut out 72 cookies had my toddler not stolen a small hunk of dough to play with. I didn’t have room in my freezer for the cookies, but an hour in the refrigerator prior to baking worked just fine. These are a bit of a project, but by far the best cookies I made this year.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these yesterday and they are delicious, but I overbaked them because I read the instructions too quickly. (Even at too much time, they are delicious.)  Would appreciate other bakers’ thoughts on optimal baking time at 350.  Thanks!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies are legendary. The crispy, caramelized, cookies combined with the creamy, buttery filling is a recipe for obesity and delight. However, do NOT add any liquids to the frosting! I was like "ima be smart and add milk so that it hardens less quickly" and it caused a disastrous oily mess cuz the filling separated after the addition of the liquid. All in all though, these are delicious and would highly recommend. They are worth the effort!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe is so fantastic!! Long time reader, first time commenter just to say how much I love these cookies. I “tested” 3 before I could even get them on a plate. The rest were devoured in record time. Glad I bought a second bag of heath bits so I can make them again as gifts (if I can resist!).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Our dough came out of the mixer crumbly. Based on folks comments and our assessment of the done cookies, we will add butter next time. Icing is too sweet like others said. The flavor is phenomenal. We got 26 cookies, not 36.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: No issue with dough as others stated. Cut my butter into cubes and left it on the counter for about 30 minutes along with the egg white. Followed the suggested mixing times, if anything I should have put it in the fridge to firm up a tiny bit after it came together.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I could only find the heath bar pieces with chocolate at my grocery store but they still turned out great! the dough wasn't crumbly at all for me (i weighed my ingredients so maybe that helped?) I also used salted butter and I think that balanced the sweetness better. Seconding the rolling between 2 pieces of parchment!
Overall, great flavor and yes, it's a little more work than the standard cookie but it's worth it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wish we knew to roll the dough out before freezing overnight sooner.  The freeze/thaw process was the only challenge with the recipe. We didn’t know and it gave us a tough time.  The cookies are great though!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
If you didn't get enough cookies, you're probably either not rolling the dough thin enough, or using a cookie cutter bigger than Sohla's. I used a 2-inch cutter and the recipe yielded 80 cookies. 

Another tip -- in the video, Sohla says you can chill some of these in the freezer and some in the fridge if you don't have enough room. Then, to avoid the frozen egg wash issue that Tommy mentioned, egg wash the refrigerated ones, not the frozen ones.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The toffee flavour of these cookies was outstanding! They’re a little fussy to make but watching the video really helped my process (especially the bit about working fast with the frosting). Sadly, I did over-bake the biscuits a tiny bit (totally my own fault for not knowing my new oven) but they got very hard, almost inedible and weren’t quite the hit I was hoping they’d be on my holiday cookie tray. That said, people were taking them apart and dipping them in their tea so not all was lost!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: OMG, these are SO GOOD!  Had to special order the toffee bits from Amazon, even 2 weeks before Christmas; don't know if that's a COVID supply chain thing or what.  Like all cutout cookies, these take time, but totally worth it!  I have a new addition to my Christmas cookie "must bake" list.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I couldn't find toffee bits without chocolate, which I think was the main problem behind my extremely crumbly dough. Once I added an additional eggwhite, the dough came together perfectly. The cookies were a huge hit!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Did anyone cross-test this recipe because it sure doesn't seem that way. Putting hot browned butter into a standmixer to make icing does not work. You just end up with an oily soup as expected - mine is sitting on a -20C balcony chilling off after I second guessed myself and followed the written directions.

Echoing others, the dough is way too dry to come together without additional hydration or oil. 
It's tasty, but a very flawed recipe that requires some wizardry to make work.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I used homemade toffee for this recipe and it worked wonderfully! However, I have two notes: 
1. The brown butter frosting should cool a little before being put into a piping bag (it will be boiling hot right after being done). Don't worry about it becoming too cool- I evaporated all the water off of my butter, but it never got too solid in the piping bag. The heat from your hands will keep it pliable enough. 
2. Probably obvious, but the thicker you make your cookies, the more they spread!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Ok I'll admit that I could not find English toffee, so instead used Wether's Originals (blitzed into a powder)....the taste was...fine? I mean basically it was a Wether's Originals cookie. Super sweet, chewy...but probably NOT what it was supposed to taste like. Likely because of the WOs, the dough was very dry and required addtl butter and water to bind. Only made ~25 very small sandwiches.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Unlike others, I did get exactly 72  2 inch rounds. Like others, I found the snap excellent and the flavor very good (the brown butter icing is wonderful), and the time required excessive. Though the flavor was pleasantly different from most cookies I have made, they are excessively sweet. I'm not sure how the sugar level can be decreased without changing the texture.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If you're like me and didn't look carefully at the recipe before grocery shopping, the toffee with chocolate works out fine. I found it hard to roll out the dough thin enough to get 72 cookies. I also thought it was too sweet (maybe because I used the chocolate toffee), and I halved the amount of frosting. It's a good cookie, but I probably wouldn't make it again -- too sweet and too finicky.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These ere impossible.  Th dough is so crumbly that it couldn't be rolled out.  I tried everything, including water and another egg.  Finally just ended up throwing it out.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The first time, I added the butter into a bowl containing the sugar and ended up with a very soupy mixture (eventually saved by cooling and adding more sugar). The next time, I put the butter in the mixer bowl and slowly added the sugar to it and it came out with a perfect texture (way too hot to pipe, but an offset spatula worked well). So follow directions.... Also be aware that the butter will violently boil when the vanilla is added to the bowl; make sure the bowl is large.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were delicious and unfortunately incredibly fussy to make as other commenters have already noted. I encouraged everyone I gifted them to enjoy as they would not make it onto my yearly Christmas cookie list.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cool brown butter before adding the powdered sugar. There is plenty of filling-cut a smaller hole in the pastry bag and pipe the filling on in a circle (still well inside the edge of the cookie) to make it show evenly all the way around.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these last year 2021 and they were great! I’ve tried twice this year and failed both times - the dough won’t come together. Did anyone else have problems? I honestly wonder if there’s an error that slipped into the recipe over the last year.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Are you weighing the dry ingredients? That can make a big difference, especially with flour. And maybe try an XL egg white instead of a regular one? I've baked two batches with an XL egg white with great success. Hope it works -- these are so good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Update for 2022.  I loved these so much last year, I had to dive in again. Like last year, I did have to add a bit more butter to get the dough to come together.  I found the almond topping, while pretty, didn't add much flavor.  So this year I topped with a sanding sugar/flaky salt mixture for a little bling. I also added 1/2 tsp almond extract to both the dough and the icing to amp the almond flavor. Thank you Sola for such a nice recipe.  It's sure to be a classic in my house!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: An absolute labor of love- these cookies are delicious but oh boy do they take some time. Don't make these if you are short on stamina or time since it will take a fair deal of both, but they are so tasty they are completely worth it. I have people begging me to make them again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Perfect! My brother (who doesn’t like sweets) ate three and claimed they were the best cookies he ever had.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made these with no problems. The dough was a bit crumbly, but I just smooshed it together & put it in the fridge overnight. Rolling it out takes a light hand & I  actually stacked two pennies along side the rolled-out dough to check the thickness.  Also, I added extra salt to the filling, about 3/4 t., to cut the sweetness. Next time I’d make an Italian buttercream. Not a fan of powdered sugar. They are very sweet. 

Weighing your ingredients is key with this recipe.  I made 42 cookies.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Was able to make 72 cookies come out, but they were thin. Cookies were too hard too quickly. Was hoping for a chewier cookie. Between the toffee and the sugared icing these were too sweet for my taste. Guests loved them, but it would probably need some more tweaking before I made them again. 

Also, 1 1/2 hours is a lie. Was closer to 2+. Time consuming.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious cookies that I would make again! They are very crisp and sturdy for storing and shipping as Sohla states in the video. Wonderful with a cup of coffee or tea.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I ended up not sandwiching after frosting. Worked out great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: On my third (fourth?) time making this recipe I accidentally bought the heath bits WITH chocolate although Sohla said not to use those…anyway it turned out fine. I wouldn’t do it on purpose but the cookies are still good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Forgot to mention my dough was fine, just a tad softer than my biscotti dough and easy to handle.  Perhaps if the flour is beaten too long it gets crumbly.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These will now be a staple in my household. Two changes: use the entire egg it addresses the crumblyness, and double it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I was nervous about these after reading the comments, but they turned out great! 

I weighed the flour and rolled the dough out between parchment, then chilled two trays at a time.

Pay extra attention to the 1/8 inch thing. I ended up rolling out my scraps 3 times to get the full 72. 

For the Canadians reading this: I couldn't find Skor pieces anywhere so close to Christmas, but 5 Macintosh Creamy Toffee Bars smashed then blitzed up worked well.

TLDR: Fiddly and take a *while*, but delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These are crispy and 
delicious! The brown butter filling is melt in your mouth phenomenal. A couple notes: cut down on sugar(in frosting and batter... a tad too sweet), did not get 72 cookies (more like 44), I did not freeze them, kept in refrigerator for 30 minutes and they baked beautifully. Worth the time!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really good but a lot of work to roll out and cut. Sweet but no way around that

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Overall I loved these. I made my own toffee due to almond allergies. I cut with a 2 inch cutter but they spread quite a bit so I would go to 1.5inches. The brown butter frosting was fantastic and really worked well with the cookies. I took one pan out too early and they were chewy. Those that I cooked long enough stayed crisp for days. Since I could not decorate with almonds I sprinkled on some fine crushed toffee but it melted in. Maybe a little large grain sugar would have been better.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
1. Best cookies ever. Everyone will rave about them when you share them.
2. They shockingly make the specific amount of cookies, and I never end up with even a scrap of excess (you must use the exact size cutter, roll out to exact thinness)
3. Because I have a small freezer, I freeze on parchment sheets and layer.
4. You can even make your own "skor" if you want, although it does change the texture slightly for the snap.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can I make these successfully without freezing them? My freezer is way too small for even one try to fit in.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For anyone looking for this advice, I couldn’t find non-chocolate coated toffee for an affordable price near me (only shipping), and so I used Heath bits. It worked AMAZING. I think the milk chocolate gave it a lovely warmth. The color of the cookies was ever-so-slightly darker, but honestly… not by much at all.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I froze the cookies on my smallest pan touching one another and transfered them to a tupperware to stockpile them due to freezer space constraints. like other people noted, I could not get 72 cookies out of the dough, I think I got around 60 (so 30 cookies when everything is sandwiched). I found the dough easy to work with with a little chilling and a floured cutting board. I think if the dough is too crumbly you might need to procces the toffee bits for longer.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These are amazing! Lots of steps but worth it. My husband gobbled a bunch of the individual cookies before I could even fill them and turn them into sandwiches. So from now on, will definitely make a batch just for the cookie part for him. 

Btw, first time around, Heath Toffee Bits were all sold out. So I scraped down Almond Roca (used the chocolate almond scrapings in another recipe). Worked fine. Second time around, same problem so I made my own toffee (which really isn’t that hard to make). Worked out pretty darn good too.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Amazing. We make these every year and are in love with them. Sure, they take some time but it’s really not difficult at all. There’s great stopping points in the recipe so you don’t have to make it all in one day. Three cookie trays in the freezer is a little tough, but in Wisconsin winters we’ve got outside. We have also alternated and left one dough disk in the fridge while we roll, cut, and bake one batch. Highly recommend these!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I make these smaller than suggested, using a 1” cookie cutter rather than a 2”. The bigger size just feels too big. 
I bake for about 8-10 minutes. Always remind myself to let them get darker than I expect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really good. I used half brittle & half candied pecans to reduce the sweetness and added whisky to the buttercream.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My first time baking this recipe so I undoubtedly did things "wrong" but we found them DELICIOUS and too rich to eat an entire one.  Debating changing the size or making them iced, not sandwich, next time.  So yummy though.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love Sohla's ginger cookie so had to try this one. My confession: I added the whole egg (I am a lazy baker) and I just made the cookies, no filling. And lemme tell you THESE ARE TERRIFIC. Easy recipe, the cookies are snappy and major hit (already) in my house. Thanks Sohla!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Everything I imagined these cookies would be: crispy, crunchy, creamy, and deeply brown-buttery! There are many steps here but the recipe worked perfectly. Well worth the effort. They look amazing too!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody:
Why are people so resistant on using cardamom without even trying the recipe? Why even comment?

Absolutely delicious. I would make this a thousand times over as is.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody:
I made this pie for a family gathering (with a couple modifications) for a family gathering and it received compliments all around. I was a bit nervous that I wouldn’t be able to pull off the caramel but Sohla’s instructions and a quality pan made the difference. 

I caramelized the bananas to intensify their flavor, pulling from the  Caramelized Banana Pudding NYT cooking recipe for this. My family has pretty vanilla taste and wouldn’t like cardamom in the pudding so I used, well, vanilla.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: Matt, regarding your question about using Cardamom in this recipe:   Green cardamom pods is not a spice staple in my cupboard.   While considering this recipe and researching the ingredient, I decided to purchase a small amount from Penzey’s and was glad I did.   The spice added a complex layer of flavor while remaining inoffensive.  It did not overwhelm the dish as I was afraid it might.   I’m not sure I personally could have accomplished the same flavor profile using ground cardamom.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: @Jamie that’s where I get my cardamom too and it’s perfect !

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: This was not as much work as it might seem. The cardamom is subtle and without it the custard would be quite boring. It tasted better after a full 24 hours in the fridge. The one issue is that even though the caramel was quite firm, it became very weepy once assembled. I end up spooning it out of the pan and onto the slices. Mistakes I made: packing the crust too tight, not using all the bananas called for because I didn’t think the custard would fit (it did and would have with all the bananas).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: When someone actually makes this with the cardamom, can you share your verdict? Occasionally like a little cardamom in coffee, but I'm struggling to imagine whether this is a good idea or one of those perilous bits of culinary frippery that spoil a simple pleasure.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: @Matt - I’ve made this recipe many times exactly as written, with the cardamom. It’s the pie everyone loves and keeps requesting. Fabulous as always—no alterations.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: Just a heads up! remember to find the little Silica Gel Packet/Moisture Absorber Drying Bags before blending your freeze dried banana slices. I did not remember this until it was too late…Happy to report that the cardamom IS delicious and subtle in the custard.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody:
This pie took work but it was one of the most amazing things ever to come out of my 
kitchen

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: Hands down one of the best pies I’ve ever had. We had about 68 people over for thanksgiving (we have a huge family) and everyone who tried this says it was the best pie they’ve ever eaten. Highly recommend! I will be making this again very soon!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe for Thanksgiving this year. It was generally a hit. Two guests don't care for bananas, but they ate the pie. The others liked the interesting combination of flavors with the cardamom, the cream filling, and the (for them) not-overpowering banana flavor. I had not made caramel sauce for many years and had to dump out my first try at it, but otherwise I was able to follow the directions and make a delicious dessert.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: I say no to the cardamon. Use a vanilla bean instead. Caramel, banana and dark chocolate. What more do you need?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: I made this pie with graham cracker crust, and it was delicious!  the bittersweet chocolate shavings were delicious.  I would use about half the cornstarch next time, and cut back a bit on sugar in the filling.  Caramel was a great modification.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: Suggestions: Added a generous sprinkling of Maldon salt on caramel layer. For filling, except for bananas and butter, put everything else into bullet blender, cooked as suggested, then strained filling to remove any cardamom pod detritus. Caramelized bananas and deglazed pan with a Tbsp of bourbon.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Gorgeous. Over the top dessert. Make it from scratch. You’ll be so proud of yourselves.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: Easiest way to evenly disperse a crumb crust into a 9" pan is to use another 8" pie plate to press gently over and into the 9" pan filled with cookie crumbs (before baking.) Prevents the thicker build-up of excess crumbs ithroughout the pie plate providing an even dispersement. And it's quick!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: Nothing about this one worked for me, unfortunately, even though I usually have good luck with NYT cooking.  The custard was too stiff, the caramel intruded far too strongly on the rest of the pie, and the cardamom was unnecessary.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: The whipped cream topping is a disaster. Just sprinkle the crushed banana on top of regular whipped cream.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: Definitely needs more cardamom. The caramel is a bit overwhelming - - not sure if leaving out the honey would have fixed that. I couldn't find freeze dried bananas so just made plain homemade whip.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: Instead of Cardamon, can I use ground Cardamon instead and what measurements would I use for for the ground Cardamon?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely amazing. I couldn't find cardamom pods at my grocery store, so I just put a scant 1/4 teaspoon of ground cardamom instead. I also added a tsp of vanilla extract to the custard because I like vanilla.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: @stacy  you didn't make this

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: LOVED the flavors. Burned the caramel on the first try. Needed lower heat. Cardamom flavor was incredible, I will probably use 1.5x next time but I am a real cardamom fan. Could not find freeze dried banana in my small town so I did 2 layers of fresh banana and it was great. This is an interesting and delicious pie and worth the work!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody:
This was a HUGE hit at a potluck!! I made mine in a springform pan and it held together beautifully. The cardamom was subtle and added a nice floral note to the pudding layer — if you’re unsure about adding it I encourage you to give it a try, it’s not obtrusive at all!

The only modification I made was reducing the sugar in the whip topping by 1 tablespoon because I could only find pre-sweetened dried banana chips. Worked like a charm. This was a great project recipe with a great payoff.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: As a liquid, milk is measured in millilitres (ml), not grams.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: unless you happen to have a balance...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: The recipe already measured the milk in cups rather than milliliters.  Measuring by weight would be more accurate although I doubt that such level of accuracy is necessary.  To paraphrase former Secretary of Defense Donald Rumsfeld:  you cook with the ingredients and equipment you have, not with the ingredients and equipment you wish you had.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: Haven't tried this yet but I think I may cheat and buy the caramel at Trader Joe.  They have a great one.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: So good. I think it needs a little more salt, but otherwise great and, honestly, not that much work if you break it up.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is absolutely delicious. I only had biscoff cookies which made for an excellent base. I was worried about the cardamom, but it was perfect. A little different and very inoffensive, even to guests with a very vanilla palate (pun intended). 1.5x to fill my deep dish pie to the brim, arranged my banana coins in offset spirals, and it was a stunner.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: I used one teaspoon of cardamom and it turned out great! The pudding in the middle tasted savory with the cardamom which was complimented nicely with the sweet whipped cream and sugary crust. Also, instead of using freeze dried banana I folded in one mashed banana into the whipped cream.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: This was a truly phenomenal pie and an absolute joy to make! I couldn't find vanilla wafers so I substituted equal parts graham crackers and it was delicious. Also, I could only find "solar dried" bananas which were chewy rather than brittle, but they worked well in the topping. The textures of the crisp crust, chewy caramel, the bite of the banana, and soft custard and cream were lovely. Not too sweet, and the cardamom really adds a depths of flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: Due to an egg allergy, how should I go around replacing the egg & egg yolks in the filling? I'm not much of a baker.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: I whipped whipped cream, too long despite the strong morning. It still tasted great. If you’re worried about an untraditional flavor, I would leave out the honey in the caramel and add an extra tablespoon of sugar! We also left out the cardamom.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: I wouldn’t make this again. The honey caramel didn’t taste good at all. It also made this pie far too sweet. The pudding has far too much corn starch and was stiff as a board. I recommend finding an alternate recipe. I did like the cardamom flavor, but there wasn’t enough of it. After tasting the pudding, I added more ground cardamon.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: Your guests are sure to love the complex flavors of this banana cream pie. At out Thanksgiving meal, the pie was a favorite among all!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: loved this dessert, and it was fun to make. only used 3 cardamom pods because it was the birthday cake for my friend who has the palate of a 13 year old. remember to take some extra lactaid.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: Putting the hot pudding directly on the caramel causes it to reheat and become displaced. I also think the addition of the honey made it less stable, so it ran out of the bottom of the pie when cut. I would just make a regular caramel, and perhaps more of it for a more distinct layer.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody:
Overall a fine recipe, but felt like an unnecessary amount of work for an unbalanced and overly sweet pie. When others commented on the caramel weeping, I thought this was only when poured on top, so I skipped this and had folks add caramel on top of each slice they took. Unfortunately I found that the base layer of caramel also has this issue. It really wets the crust as it daws moisture from the bananas. 
Wafer crust and sweetened cream top make it all too much imo

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody:
This was probably the best pie I ever had! However, there were a few things I would have liked to know in advance:
1. You should add two more pods of cardamom at least if you want a strong flavor (and you should, because straining the custard is very inconvenient).
2. Speaking of straining the custard: Instead of a sieve, you should use a cheese cloth
3. I didn't end up using much banana. Maybe my pan was too shallow? I did two layers of slices
4. Add a THICK layer of chocolate shavings

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
commentBody: @Matt also try the cardamom banana break from Milk Street which is the only banana bread I make now

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Watched Sohla's video before deciding to make this recipe—so glad I did! Of course, the look and description are winning, but the basic ingredients and prep clinched it. Solha's instructions are uncomplicated but precise. This is a great weekend at home recipe, and frankly, every day of this pandemic is a day at home. I wouldn't ding this recipe on prep time as three of the four hours are completely hands off. That time is spent coasting the meringue bake in the oven at a very low temp. Enjoy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I would make this again, but cook the shortbread for a shorter time, the long  bake with the meringue made the cookie a bit more crumbly that I would like.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed the recipe faithfully and the end result was really dry, really crumbly cookies that don’t cut well. Disappointed with the end result so I won’t take the time to make them again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was really great! I did read some of the comments and so I cooked the shortbread for 15 mins and the meringue for 1 hr 30 mins. It tastes delicious and looks beautiful.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
It’s way too salty using 3/4 teaspoon of Morton’s kosher salt. The recipe must be expecting you to use Diamond Crystal; 1/4 teaspoon is probably enough for Morton’s. 

The shortbread is very crumbly and the meringue doesn’t want to stay attached. 

The meringue layer is really good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Pretty easy recipe, but Step 9, the meringue never gets dry while baking. I kept in an additional 30 min to try for dry. Still never felt dry or crisp. Turned off oven and cooled as directed and finally got dry and crisp. When cutting the cookies the meringue cracked and completely separated from shortbread. Tastes great, but failed for xmas cookies to share. Too bad. Now I have to eat them all.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have to agree it is too salty using Morton's kosher salt. 1/2 tsp would seem more reasonable. Not impressed with the shortbread it's self. After all the cooking it was chalky tasting and too crumbly.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: For some reason, my meringue was hard after only an hour of baking, so I stopped it then! I think that prevented it from being as dry as other reviewers said. It’s crumbly but in a good way, at least to me!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I love shortbread cookies and I love that Sohla gave me a chocolate shortbread cookie! And you don’t have to add the peppermint to the icing...  you can do whatever you want with it! this can be very versatile! Sohla always says do what works for you! That’s why I love watching her cook and I love her recipes! She always makes them so versatile that you can make it how you like it!! she just gives you a really good foundation! 
Thank you so much Sohla!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these today; biscuit is far too dry and way too salty. The meringue topping worked well and was very tasty. Would not make these again; far too much effort for a mediocre treat.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can this recipe be frozen?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The cookies were extremely dry and disappointing. I pulled the shortbread out 15 minutes early during the initial bake based on the comments of others. The end result was still super dry. I don’t think I would make this again- but if I did I would pull it out after 1 hr 40 min so the meringue would still be kind of chewy and not so hard.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have made these twice and both times they looked beautiful out of the oven but shattered into crumbs when I tried to cut them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The texture of the shortbread was really chalky and dry. I’d love to know if it was because of the temp of the butter - really room temp and not cold, or because I used King Arthur’s Double Dutch cocoa. I baked for only 30 minutes and at correct oven temperature. I had to scrap the meringue topping because the peppermint oil i used was overpowering even at 1/4 tsp. So I tried the shortbread before restarting the meringue recipe. I just didn’t like it and decided not to spend anymore time on it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This didn't work for me. I followed the recipe faithfully - I even watched the awful video - and ended up with a dry, crumbly shortbread that resulted in a pile of crumbs and ragged pieces. 

I liked the meringue and the finished product tasted good, but I wasn't able to cut it into anything presentable even though I used a sharp knife and thoroughly cleaned it between cuts. 

For me, not worth the time and effort and I won't make it again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Chocolate shortbread was disappointingly dry — a real shame because it smelled so good! Meringue also did not want to stay put on top of cookie. Won’t be making this again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This smells and looks lovely, but it’s way too dry and crumbly. I could only manage 4 pieces when cutting because the rest turned into a pile of crumbs. It tastes pretty good, but I wouldn’t make this again. A lot of time for a very underwhelming result.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It is very rare that I bake cookies that are inedible, but these were IT.  The shortbread has a horrible texture and harsh taste. The meringue did not rescue it, and for the most part did not stay attached. A ROYAL MESS.  I bake cookies all the time, from shortbread to sugar cookies, to macarons, and springerle.  No operator error here. The recipe is simply not good. AT ALL.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The meringue layer is beautiful and tasty, but I would skip this recipe. The shortbread is so dry that I was embarrassed I had shared these with others. They lose the charm of the swirled top because the cookie disintegrates when you touch it. I even reduced the bake time from reviews above.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So taking all my fellow commenters’ advice, I made a few adjustments: cut the kosher salt in the chocolate layer to 1/4 tsp, baked it for 15 minutes, and I baked the meringue layer at 215 degrees for about half hour. The textures were perfect (not too crumbly or over baked) *but* the meringue cracked on cutting it, making it unserve-able. Oh well, guess I have a whole pan to myself!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Unfortunately the end result was very disappointing. I followed the recipe carefully, but the shortbread was chalky and very dry, almost inedible. The merengue had a nice taste, but was also a bit too chewy. I made this for a cocktail party and will not be serving this - may have been some unknown error on my part, but this was quite a bit of effort for a disappointing result.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow, should have read the comments. These were totally inedible. Texture of chalk like everyone says. Just absolutely awful to bite down on.  This recipe should be taken down.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made these tonight & they turned out great! My meringue topping wasn't quite as smooth after baking but still looked gorgeous 
I also doubled the recipe and it fit perfectly in a 13x9 with a little meringue leftover (made meringue cookies with it!)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I LOVED the idea for this recipe, so I tried it out for cookie boxes I wanted to gift to friends. When I made it though (exactly as written), the meringue did not stick to the chocolate base after baking, so when I cut it into shards it just crumbled off and I was unable to use it in my boxes since it was just a big mess. Additionally, it's very dry. I know meringue is supposed to be fairly dry and so is shortbread, but the level of dryness here impeded the flavors.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wanted these to be as amazing as they look. I even watched the video. Honestly these turned out to just be OK for the effort.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Shortbread is so dry and not sweet enough. I actually wondered if I messed up the recipe until I came to the comments section and saw that this is a shared experience. I just wish I would have read them first. After such a time investment to have inedible shortbread is such a disappointment.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The cookies look a little better than they taste. I found the shortbread too dry and the merinque tasty but not worth the 3 hour cooking time. I agree with the comment that the cookie tasted chalky and was crumbly. It was also difficult to cut without ending up with cracks on the top. Not a repeat.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was really excited to make these cookies after watching the video and planned on shipping them to my family because we can’t be together for the holidays. Unfortunately the shortbread came out so crumbly it wouldn’t hold its shape when it was cut. The meringue came out great but the texture of the shortbread made it so the meringue topping separated from the shortbread. All in all, I was pretty disappointed and can’t ship this crumbly mess to my relatives.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: GF bakers: I made the shortbread with King Arthur gluten free measure for measure flour and it came out perfectly with no other subs or modifications! Go for it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Appreciate all the notes about the salt - adjusted down to 1/2 tsp of Morton’s.
Also adjusted down the peppermint extract to 1/4 tsp and it was still VERY Minty, enough to linger long after tasting.
I cooked at 350 for 30 minutes checking at 25 minutes. I used the guidance in the video about looking for the smell and dry centre for doneness. It was still slight soft in the centre, but I was hoping to avoid the dry/crumbly cookie some of you mentioned.
Not too sweet! Great for cookie boxes

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used less than 1/4 tsp peppermint extract (because I ran out, not by design). I imagine these would be inedible had I used the full amount. Mine turned out well and fairly close to how the texture looked in the video. My meringue was crispy and my shortbread dense and crumbly.  After storing them in an airtight container at room temp. the shortbread and the meringue are decidedly more chewy. Which IMHO is an improvement. These were fun to make and the video is a delight. Won’t make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Shortbread was a dry, powdery mess.  The meringue was a nightmare. I have never had any trouble making meringue.

Recipe was "made for a stand mixer" but the volume of the meringue mixture was such that the whisk does not even touch the substrate. Add a third egg white and increase the other ingredients accordingly. Made two attempts, this could probably be rewritten into an actually useful recipe but it is absolutely not worth the time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Like others: dry, crumbled when cut (both cookie and meringue). Will make excellent ice cream topping, but otherwise low ROI. Too much bother to ever make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Similar end result as others - far too dry and half was reduced to rubble when I tried to cut it.  I guess a good ice cream topping?  Don't know where it went wrong.  I love chocolate-mint combos so was hoping to add this to my holiday baking repertoire.  Not without some tweaks.  Flavor was good, though.  I was originally thinking of foregoing the meringue and just making a peppermint infused white chocolate drizzle - that may be my next attempt.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
i read all the notes beforehand and the recipe was great! it is quite lengthy, so i made them in the evening and left them overnight to cool. a couple of things i changed:
-baked the cookies for about 25 minutes and i also left them in the tin when cooking the meringue so the edges would not dry out
-i also don't have a cooking thermometer, so i was nervous the meringue would not work, but i just heated it for about 8 mins and it whipped up fine!
-i reduced the amount of salt to ~1/4 tsp

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Really not a good recipe… very dry. No short bread should be in an oven that long- and I under cooked it for fear of dryness per the other comments. I would definitely discourage people from making this. I made the nigella cinnamon squares to replace them. Excellent.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I had the same issues as other commenters: I baked the shortbread and meringue for the amount of time as written, and they both came out very dry. It was my first time making meringue, so maybe I messed something up elsewhere, too. Everything crumbled as soon as I tried to cut it, and some of the meringue topping separated from the shortbread. The flavors were good, but I had to leave them out of the cookie tins.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Note to self: ALWAYS read the comments before you embark on a recipe. The dough is too salty, the meringue not enough to hold the cookie together. I have no idea how the picture on this recipe is possible. We will eat it but it will not be made again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Unfortunately all the other commenters are right, this ends up far too dry! By the end of the baking process the shortbread is so dry it is practically inedible. It looks amazing when first completed with the beautiful meringue swirls on top, but as soon as you try to cut it the cookie crumbles to dust and each bite is like eating peppermint chocolate sawdust. We even shaved 5 minutes off the initial cookie baking time but it didn’t help. Love Sohla but sadly this one is a flop.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These were a lot of trouble for no reason.  You can make meringue as you do for a "forgotten" cookie with fine sugar.  No cooking at all required, and the meringue forms soft peaks with no trouble.  When I followed the directions in this recipe, mine was runny as it is in Solah's video. Finally, it's easy to firm up the meringue by putting it in a preheated 350 degree oven, and turning it off when you put the cookie in.  Leave it for two hours and you're finished.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Boy the shortbread was dry as dust after cooking the meringue!  We also had an issue with the meringue itself when we added the peppermint extract, which made it collapse a bit.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The comments worried me, so I doubled the recipe, but kept the same surface area and cooking time, and it turned out very nice! Some of them crumbled at the corners, but overall a very fun and unusual cookie and the chewy minty meringue at the top was perfect. I'll make them again because they are just a notch fancier and lighter than the usual Christmas cookie.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I hacked this a bit. I put the peppermint extract in the shortbread base and used marshmallow buttercream frosting for the top, swirled in some red food coloring and crushed candy cane for crunch. Delicious. Recommend baking the base for about 22 minutes. For those commenting on the salt, use half of the recommended amount for kosher salt if using Morton’s.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe took a long time and was a disappointment.  I agree with the others - the shortbread was cooked so long that it became super dry and crumbly.  Not even worth eating. The meringue itself was good.  I made this over a week ago over my family and this dessert is still in my kitchen - will probably toss the rest today!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm a big fan of Sohla's and I was excited to make what looked like a beautiful cookie. Followed the recipe exactly (accounting for her advice on the video as well) and was sorely disappointed. The cookie itself was dry, hard, flavorless and a bit salty. It didn't cut or present well either. This one's not going into the rotation for me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A lot of work for a failure in the end. So unfortunate and disappointing. One of the first Times recipes that I had to throw away after baking it for hours. Don’t bother with this dry crumbly tasteless recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Follower the directions and the brittle meringue slid right off the cookies. Next time I’ll follow the advice below and coon shortbread for 20 minutes, shortbread   meringue for 90 minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I wish I had read all of these notes before I decided to make these to put in xmas cookie gift boxes!  Had the same experience as others with the dry, crumbly texture.  Also found it near impossible to get the meringue to 175.  I would make twice as much meringue next time as I didn't have enough to cover the shortbread.  And I also found that the meringue didn't stick to the cookie.  Bummer!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was alot of effort for little return - dry and crumbly and not in a good way - the top did not attach.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It turns out that when you bake something for 3 hours it tastes dry as dust.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
The instructions here are great but honestly, these cookies are dry and brittle. I would not make these again — the cookies baked for way too long (I should've pulled them out sooner) but I don't think even if they weren't so dry that they would've paired well with the meringue topping. 

The topping tastes nice. I've made meringue frosting before, but never baked it, but I think it came out how it should. However, paired with the dry cookie, it's just not a nice cookie, unfortunately.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I ruined 2 batches of meringue by adding the peppermint extract as instructed. They went from perfect peaks to completely flat and runny. Ultimately I opted to use crushed candy canes for the peppermint flavor.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These are fairly labor intensive and are not worth the effort. The meringue would not harden fully and the shortbread did not have much taste and was crumby as other commenters have noted.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Warning- these are a ton of work, it’s impossible to make a neat cut on the meringue, and most of all they taste awful!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: honestly these slap. they’re drier than other cookies but that’s bc they’re shortbread!! the flavors are great and i love the texture of the meringue.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Looking at a lot of the tips, I solved the dryness of the shortbread cookie issue by baking the meringue separately on parchment paper. And then stuck together the shortbread and the meringue with a very thin layer of tempered chocolate. I also sprinkled the meringue with crushed candy cane prior to baking. 10 out of 10 would recommend.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: These cookies were so bad that my boyfriend who usually eats anything didn't want them. Do not make. There are better ways to get your chocolate mint fix.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Yeah, should have read the comments. Loads of labor for the same crumbly/dry result as others
here. Also, no point in the small amount you get (doubling did seem a good idea, but I was wary of wasting a lot of it did not turn out well). I’m a fan of crumbly shortbread and meringue so this is fine for the family, but not for giving to anyone, and I won’t make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So very disappointing--overly dry, chalky taste, does not cut well and meringue does not stay attached to chocolate shortbread. A waste of time and ingredients.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This sounds divine, but I’m allergic to chocolate.  Any thoughts on another recipe shortbread or otherwise that could be topped with the peppermint meringue?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thinking about the taste, for me any shortbread would be good, including pretzel. (There's a great recipe here for pretzel shortbread.)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made half of this recipe- the cookie half. That is because I had not read through and didn't realize it was meringue on top- when I got to that part I just made royal icing flavored with peppermint instead.  So they are still drying, no verdict on that yet. That being said, the shortbread seems a bit disappointing as shortbread. Like everyone says, dry and crumbly and a wee bit salty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Nailed the meringue, and the shortbread is tasty. However, it is completely dried out and crumbly (like many reviews state!) Don't shy away from the long cooking time, as most of it is hands-off. Not sure what I will use the crumbly cookie for, but it is too good to waste! The meringue is delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was really excited about trying out this cookie.  However the shortbread came out dry and somewhat bitter from the cocoa powder. (not enough sugar maybe).  I couldn't get the egg whites to stiff peaks despite whipping for a long time.  I was disappointed in this recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sohla's flavors haven't let me down yet and this recipe is no exception! Read the comments prior to making and pulled back on the shortbread cooktime (checked at 20, removed at 25). The meringue came together nicely but was not crisp throughout even after the full bake and cool time. LESS IS MORE with the meringue, otherwise you're left with perfect edges and a gooey taffy like center that will rip out your dental work.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Following the comments about how dry it got, I baked the shortbread for only 25 minutes. The whole thing was still rather dry, but it cut reasonably well (the meringue was a bit crumbly) and it's pretty tasty. Since this is the first time that I did anything peppermint flavoured for Christmas, it's a nice treat. Probably will do again next year.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was super disappointed by these! Great idea, but didn’t taste good. So dry- they tasted chalky. I cooked them for less time and still had this issue... They were quite a bit of effort, and I ended up throwing them out. The meringue wasn’t chewy and melt in your mouth- it was just hard. Not worth the calories.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These taste like the best thin mints you’ve ever eaten. The lightness of the meringue along with the richness of the chocolate shortbread is a beautiful marriage.

My only complaint is that the meringue was so delicate that I was unable to put the cookies in tins for my neighbors without breaking them. Guess I’ll have to eat them all myself!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made these this afternoon and overall I'm pleased! 

I took other peoples comments to heart and only baked the shortbread for 25 minutes, which made it a bit fudgier than I think it would have before hand. I wonder if it couldn't use some other fat (oil?) or another egg yolk to make it less dry.

I also underbred the meringue at 1hr 45min & didn't let them cool in the oven which made the meringue much chewier which I prefer. I would probably not make them again as it just took too long.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Ugh I wanted this to be good, and I think maybe I dont have the bakers touch, but it certainly did not turn out well. Merengue never dried thoroughly, shortbread was fine but by no means good. :( Did not look pretty either. but Sohla's look amazing!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Maybe a little less peppermint. Next time I would try quarter teaspoon

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Like others, I followed the recipe precisely and there was too much salt in the shortbread and it baked so long, it crumbled when sliced. My pretty meringue topping turned to peppermint dust!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made this today (as part of a christmas cookie box gift) and it's a delight! Yummy and pretty too!
Even using an electric mixer, and thoroughly cleaning my utensils before use my meringue (similarly to Sohla) didn't form stiff peaks but was still delicious.
I also didn't cook for as long as recipe suggests (20 minutes for base, 1 hr 20 for meringue) and I'm super happy with the consistency. 
It's a fiddly recipe but good fun if you have the time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: DRY. Perhaps add two yolks? Less initial bake time? My meringue also lost its peppermint potency after baking. I wanted to like this so badly. Maybe I’ll play around with it and make some adjustments.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The shortbread was way too dry and didn’t have much flavor. The meringue would have been nice by itself but combined with the shortbread just contributed to the crumbly mess. To top it off, my food coloring was plant -based and so when baked it took on an unappetizing purplish hue. Big waste of time and money. I wish I would have read these comments before I tried this recipe. Maybe it will be good as ice cream topping? Still love Sohla and will keep trying her recipes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Agree with others below-- dry and crumbly, didn't cut well/easily. Tastes good but not worth the time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The flavors of this cookie are so well balanced! For those who say the shortbread is too salty - I skipped the salt and used salted butter and had no issues. I also tried a bit of the shortbread before I added meringue and at that stage it wasn't dry at all. I would be interested in remaking with a peppermint royal icing, or maybe using meringue powder to make the meringue instead.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Ingredients were easy to get and got started right away! Whisking by hand was fun but I wish Sohla was more specific at the end with the meringue. I was going back and forth between the video and the recipe on here but found no answer for baking the meringue:(, was super excited when I got to the last step but my meringue ended up browning :( Not sure what I could have done differently. Anyone know what we had to do for making the meringue crisp?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: use waxed paper to press down the shortbread. if you can’t find peppermint extract, use 1-3 drops of peppermint essential oil instead.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have tried this twice and both times the shortbread came out dry and burnt. I thought I rolled it too thin the first time but had the same issue even going thicker the second time. I wanted to love this but based on the experience of others I don’t think I’m alone here. Would not make it again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The shortbread was very crumbly and impossible to cut neatly. The meringue was tasty, but I would reduce the peppermint extract since it was overpowering. I would recommend cutting the baked shortbread into largest pieces (into thirds, for example) first, as Sohla did in her video. That helped reduce the amount of crumbling. I probably won’t remake these in the future as they were totally underwhelming.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sohla’s video is a family affair, and I loved it! Really entertaining but also great instruction. It definitely inspired me to make the cookies. Thanks Sohla and especially Clementine.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I enjoyed this recipe. I’ve been never tried making meringue this way (I’ve only done it with raw whites) but the texture and quality after cooking the whites and sugar was great. I agree that the texture of the cookie is crumbly and dry, but I mean it’s a meringue! The two small changes I made was keeping the shortbread in the oven for 25 minutes and only adding a splash of peppermint extract to the meringue itself. I will most likely make this again next holiday.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These comments are rough, but the cookies are delicious! I baked them as written, only having read the comments after they were in the oven with the meringue. Everything turned out beautifully, and I love that the not-so-sweet shortbread balances out the sweetness and peppermint of the meringue. 

It did take me much longer to get my egg whites to 175 degrees (10 minutes), and the meringue whipped up to peaks in just 3 minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I am not an experienced cookie-maker and didn't have a stand mixer, so I made this by hand. But I would not recommend, despite how much i love Sohla (sorry Sohla!! ily!)
The cookie ended up crumbling apart and the merengue topping fell flat (perhaps because I didn't use a mixer) and was not as easy to cut into pieces as it was in Sohla's video.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Recipe worked great but once cut, the meringue separates from the base. Was very disappointed and not able to include in my cookie gifts.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Tripled this recipe by putting it into two odd-size pans. After reading the notes here, I took the shortbread out about 5 minutes early from the initial bake, but otherwise followed the recipe exactly. They were great! The meringue is really minty and compliments the cookies really well.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
