commentBody: Wow! I wish I had read the comments before baking. I love Sohla and all of her recipes; however, these simply crumbled in our fingers as soon we tried to cut them. While the flavours in the meringue are lovely, the shortbread is dry and overbaked by the end of the process. Perhaps baking for less time would improve the texture, but I do not think I will be trying these cookies again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So disappointed with the end result! The shortbread is way to crumbly and dry. Merengue tastes amazing. Not making this again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Made this recipe and read some notes here that I incorporated. Added an extra yolk to the batter to make it less crumbly (the shortbread batter was far too wet and basically ended up coming out like a thin brownie...) and only added a few drops of peppermint to the meringue (this worked well, I would've added a touch more).

My meringue unfortunately came out very chewy in the center where the edges were definitely done. Can't give these to anyone, but they're alright to eat ourselves

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can I use unsweetened cocoa powder?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I just made these and they turned out great! no problems with saltiness or the meringue falling off the shortbread. Of course, it was a bit crumbly around the edges where I cut it... but you would expect that with shortbread/meringue. These are the modifications I made: salted butter and no added salt, 25 minutes for the first bake, 1/4 tsp peppermint extract rather than 1/2, didn't whip the meringue all the way, stopped when it was thick and glossy but not in peaks, 1hr 15 mins for second bake

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this today, and it took a while, but not more than the amount of time stated. It looks beautiful. It will look beautiful on my dessert presentation. It really doesn’t taste like “all that”. Other than for visual effect, I don’t think I would do it again. But I’m glad I tried!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I doubled this to fill a 9x13 pan, the first batch was beautiful but salty dry and crumbly with scrambled egg white bits in the meringue :( .  Agree with others that there’s way too much salt if you’re using Morton’s; I used 1/2 tsp total for the double batch.  I substituted peppermint oil for extract and used a scant 1/4 tsp for the whole double batch of meringue and only cooked the meringue to 160.  Turned out great!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
1) I only baked for 20 minutes based on comments I had read. My cookie was more like a brownie, but I actually liked that. 
2) I used a dye made from a powder, and the dye ended up coloring my whole meringue so I had a pink topping
3) Trying to cut after drying seems like a bad idea at least if one is trying to make pieces as pretty as the picture. The meringue is so crumbly and the cookies leave crumbs. It seems like it might be a better idea to cut before baking the meringue

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: my family loved this recipe! I doubled the batch, and then I cooked the shortbread for about 15-20 minutes and cooked the shortbread with merengue for about 1hr15 because a few comments said the cookies come out a little dry. I've never made merengue before and it turned out a little soft but overall everything was great! The cookies were crumbly but it's shortbread so that's normal. I also thought the saltiness of the cookies was perfect with peppermint. I'll definitely make this again

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: used less salt (1/2 tsp kosher salt instead of 3/4) and only put it in the oven with the meringue for 1h 30 instead of the full 2. end result wasn't too salty, meringue stuck, and cut well if a little crumbly! watching sohla's vid beforehand was also helpful as an inexperienced baker to help me know what to look for. thank you sohla!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used my black cocoa powder. And a wrong sized pan and slightly undercooked the base. And was too lazy to get the candy thermometer from the garage so I winged it on the meringue temperature. It is so delicious. But I have managed to eat half of it already on Xmas morning before my tea had brewed.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Labor intensive but so worth it. Made with salted butter on accident but it makes the contrast with the meringue so delectable, would definitely repeat that substitution.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Like the short bread and used less 'regular' salt.  I love meringue but this didn't adher to the shortbread when it was cut.  Tastes good.  Will probably work on a chocolate peppermint short bread or add a swirled royal icing next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these for Christmas and we loved them! They are crumbly, but they are chocolate shortbread, so I’m not sure what others expected. I doubled the recipe and made them in a 9x13. I followed Sohla’s advice to pay more attention to smell/feel/look than timing for the recipe to know when they were done. The meringue was the perfect complement and mine held together well. Will definitely make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I used black cocoa powder and was rewarded with intense chocolate shortbread.
Appreciate the comments on the shorter bake for meringue and time in the turned off oven. I turned the oven off after an hour at 225 degrees and left it in the turned off oven for an hour. The shortbread was a little crumbly but not too dry.
I used coarse kosher salt at the amount in the recipe and end product was not overly salty.
Delicious and addictive!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: End result not so beautiful as photo because meringue browned very slightly and they do not cut very neatly.  Shortbread was a bit more like cake, fluffy and loose.  Good flavors though.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Excellent cookie!  I am usually not a fan of mint with chocolate combos, but this was a keeper recipe for my annual Christmas cookie tray.  I did skip the last portion of the recipe (had no time--baking 8 varieties of cookies set  a premium on oven cooking time).  The meringue hardened after a few hours of rest on its own and was perfection.

Will be adding this cookie to my annual holiday baking, and year round for that matter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We watched the video first and then decided to give it a shot.  I was worried about the meringue, but it came off without a hitch.  I didn't have peppermint oil or red food coloring, so I crushed up a candy cane to add the peppermint flavor and some flare.  When I cut the cookies, the meringue split off of the shortbread.  Both the shortbread and the meringue were crisp and we saved the tiny shards for on top of ice cream.  It was a good recipe, but was a lot of work for an "okay' payoff.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I feel like I had...less meringue than I should have? In the picture it looks like a nice little pillow on top of the shortbread but I had just enough to scrape on top of the cookies. Besides that, the taste was amazing!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love Sohla's personality, she always makes me want to cook whatever she's making and this recipe was SOOOO good!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I definitely overbeat my meringue, and the chocolate shortbread was quite dry. I love Sohla and wanted to love these so badly but they just didn’t do it for me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Went Off Script and substituted pulverized freeze-dried raspberry for the peppermint and food coloring. Mmmm.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made a similar egg yolk cookie and it was perfect after a day or two in the Tupperware container. The cookies were crumbly in a good way. Also, I used powdered egg whites to make the meringue.  Simple and quick without no need to cook the meringue.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Too dry with timings. I baked the short bread for 20 minutes and baked the meringue for 1 1/2 hours, and it was still too dry. Disappointed, but will try again with a brownie base and an hour for the meringues.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m sure 1/4 teaspoon peppermint extract is sufficient; 1/2 tsp tasted medicinal. I agree with other reviewers- shortbread is dry, even with only 30 minutes of bake time. Although pretty in the pan, these aren’t really shareable- they tend to crumble and not cut.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cook shortbread for 15 min and merengue for 1.5

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I baked this cookie for my holiday cookie platter just to include variety, and this cookie turned out to be my absolute favorite!  My only complaint is that the batch this produces is way to small---can this be doubled?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this recipe exact.  The shortbread was a bit dry but the meringue solves that issue. Watch the video, it’s very helpful.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You can also substitute the meringue with white chocolate and add peppermint extract to the shortbread

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The result is okay, but it’s more like a brownie than shortbread. I think it would be better if it was actually shortbread and had Royal icing instead of meringue.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a beautiful looking cookie (before you try to cut it) that desperately needs to go back to the Test Kitchen.  Followed the recipe faithfully - dough was dry and missing any chocolate flavor, and it turned into an ungiftable mess upon cutting:  the meringue separated from the cookie, the cookie broke into shards and crumbs, and what few pieces appear presentable break on handling.  I like the concept but this recipe is simply not repeatable at home in its current state.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody:
I read this recipe and kept it in mind for a light Friday lunch, so did Steps 1 & 2 when was putting away Thanksgiving leftovers.  

I used radishes, celery, carrots, Asian pear, and a Granny Smith apple on romaine and radicchio with some diced leftover turkey.

The dressing is sooo good.  I overdid it on the greens (2 heads romaine and 1/2 head of radicchio),  and had to triple the dressing.  I used salted sunflower seeds so omitted the kosher salt.

Thanks for the lovely customizable recipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: The cranberry vinaigrette is fantastic. When I first read the recipe I was skeptical but gave it a try.  I used homemade cranberry sauce (the sauce had ample sugar in it) so I reduced the honey to a teaspoon and increased the olive oil by 1/2 a tablespoon.  Awesome outcome. I may have to stock canned cranberry sauce just to make this vinaigrette during the summer.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Vinaigrette was delicious and very easy to make!  Definitely make sure you leave the stuffing in the fridge at least overnight and packed tightly or the croutons won't hold their shape.  Will make it again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: The stuffing croutons turned out amazingly well. I kept them cold until right before cooking. I think that helped. Dressing seemed too thick so I added some EVOO and it was fantastic. Added radishes and celery to the salad but wished I’d had some fennel to add. I topped the salad off with some broiled shrimp as my husband really loves protein with his meal.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody:
Love it!  Making the stuffing croutons was easy and they held together pretty well.  We didn't have cranberry sauce, but I had a red currant jelly in the fridge for a substitute with a bit of lemon juice for tartness. Radicchio, little gem, fennel, honey crisps, and chopped dried cranberries rounded it out.  It was a delicious lunch and a new way to use stuffing left overs.

Thanks, Sohla!  :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This was fantastic. I had an extremely flavorful and rich cornbread-sausage stuffing, which worked great as these bits of flavor explosion amid the greens and vegetables. All I had on hand was celery and apples so that’s what went in with the greens. No pepitas so I subbed sunflower seeds, which worked fine. I wasn’t sure about the cranberry dressing but once it was tossed with the salad it was perfect. Added some leftover turkey and a bit of crumbled goat cheese and it was so satisfying.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This is a healthy and refreshing idea for Thanksgiving leftovers. Depending on what’s in your stuffing you can add or subtract ingredients.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Um? Leftover stuffing? I've never seen that happen.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Wow! This recipe is an incredible and creative use of leftovers! The cranberry vinaigrette and stuffing croutons are next level. We made it with Deb Perelman’s Challah Stuffing. So good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody:
Excellent post Thanksgiving salad!
Substitutions with what was in my refrigerator: spinach for greens, shaved carrot, julienned pear, blueberries.  Sunflower seeds for pepitas + nuts. Some diced turkey and fresh sprouts from my deck!  

Now for the smidgen of pumpkin pie and apple-cranberry tart!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This is really good, going to be part of my holiday/winter repertoire from now on.  Pretty to look at, too, as well as flexible as far as the ingredients go.  Mandoline makes prep easy on the crunchy stuff.  I happened to have some sunchokes on hand and they are a nice addition.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Try as I might, my stuffing did not become "panzanella." My cubes crumbled in the pan.  Perhaps my mother's sausage and mushroom stuffing is more chunky than most.  Not to be daunted, I made stuffing fritters instead by binding it with a couple of eggs and then frying little mounds I gently flattened in the hot pan.  I proceeded with the recipe as written and found the dressing very tasty and colorful.  It was a great post-Thanksgiving use of the remaining stuffing!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: The dressing is delicious! I usually struggle to use up cranberry leftovers, but no more. Thank you for helping me tackle the trickiest leftover!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Ok - as a salad, this slapped! Excellent dressing and I loved it with shaved raw beets and carrots. However, this crouton technique abbbbbsolutely failed for me. Embarrassingly ugly. Add an egg and smush by hand maybe would work?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This is simply the best ever use of extra stuffing and cranberry sauce. Even though my stuffing was compressed for only 4 hours, it held together beautifully for the croutons. The vinaigrette is delicious. I used carrots, fennel, brussels sprouts, and radishes for my vegetable mix, arugula for the greens. We topped the salad with a bit of turkey.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Why would you grease the pan and then use parchment paper on top of the grease? I’ve read the recipe through a couple of times and I just don’t understand.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t make the full salad but used the dressing on greens with pear, and it  was delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This salad and dressing are very good and will make again. The recipe allows for lots of variations. Personally, I don't feel the stuffing "croutons" were worth it. Just dump the stuffing on the top, or better yet, put the salad, with some cranberry sauce and a little bit of stuffing in a wrap. Peace.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: For the dressing-is this jellied or whole berry cranberry sauce? Or does it matter?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: This is pure genius! Easy to dry out the stuffing in a toaster oven and then crumble over the salad. Any variations of crunchy veggies and greens work beautifully.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I've not really got a reason to make stuffing right now-Canadian Turkey Day is over-but I think I'll make one so I can make this salad.  It sounds really good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: I did not make this recipe.  But it inspired me to fry the leftover stuffing in lots of olive oil, and then toss it on the leftover salad with a teaspoon of leftover cranberry sauce and a little salt and vinegar.  Made my Friday morning!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: Excellent and appreciated since I always make way too much of a pretty rich stuffing including chestnuts, sausage, neck meat, apples, cranberries - the light crunchiness (carrot, fennel, celery)  and tart dressing were a perfect balance.  Don’t worry about stuffing cubes coming apart in the pan - it’s the crunchy bits that are the best!  You could get away with just frying up some looser stuffing and compressing it with spatula tin pan like a big hash brown.  The only modification we made was in serving size as this whole recipe made two main course lunch salads which were devoured down to the last crumb.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
recipeTitle: Stuffing Panzanella With Cranberry Vinaigrette Recipe
commentBody: @Katherine MD did much the same today! Fried dressing and the cranberry vinaigrette are a great starting point for many variations of the salad. Found that some bitterness (endive in my case) nicely balances the other elements. I’m going to start making the vinaigrette on its own even after I run out of stuffing!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Dumpling tip: If you’re a chunky-stuffing maker (if you’ve got sausage or chestnuts or anything that doesn’t break up easily) and you’d like a smoother dumpling consistency, feel free to toss the warmed (but not hot!) stuffing and cooled extra broth in a blender with the eggs. Then I just ice-cream scooped the dough directly into my soup.

Also, feel free to vary the amount of liquid added. If your stuffing is moist, you might not need much - the eggs alone could be an adequate binder.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Would this work just as well with cornbread dressing?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I make it all the time with cornbread stuffing and it’s amazing. The soup also freezes well. Happy Thanksgiving!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Leftovers keep longer than 2 days. Most leftovers will keep safely 5-7 days.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made with leftover cornbread dressing and Thanksgiving Turkey, peas instead of kale, because that’s what I had. Absolutely delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My only question is:  who has leftover stuffing?  Lol

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Alicia this is hilarious because I share your name and also your sentiments (are you, me?). Although this year my stuffing was not at its best, so leftovers! Dumplings it shall become.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Alicia  Right??!! My thoughts exactly!! Who are these lucky people??

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm generally not one of those people who deviates from the recipe and then comments, but I'm making an exception! I think of this as "Thanksgiving leftover soup." Use whatever you have on hand. The real "recipe" here is for the dumplings, which are great! I find it best to refrigerate the "dough" for a few minutes before plopping into soup. In my experience, they hold together better that way.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I don't eat meat so I had no turkey leftovers.  I used store bought veggie broth and added a splash of apple cider vinegar and it was still absolutely great.  I had a leftover container of "holiday hash" from Trader Joe's (diced celery, onion, butternut squash, sweet potato, plus herbs) so I subbed that in for the sweet potato and it came out great.  I honestly might make stuffing again just to make this soup!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is brilliant! I used rutabagas instead of sweet potatoes and added about 1/4 cup of leftover gravy with the turkey. I will keep this recipe in mind in planning my turkey stuffing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I use a similar recipe, but roll walnut sized, flattened pieces, and  saute them in butter or olive oil 
until crispy before topping soup with them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this with store-bought vegetable broth and it was still surprisingly flavorful. We really enjoyed it and have no regrets about sacrificing some of our stuffing leftovers.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Oh...my...goodness. This is hands-down the best soup I have ever made. The flavor is outrageously good - deep flavor and LOVE the vinegar in there!! Dumplings are to die for. My spouse doesn't like sweet potatoes so we left them out and substituted swiss chard and stems for the kale. This will be our new post-Thanksgiving (and post-roasted chicken dinner) ritual!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this today after making the stock in the InstantPot. I only had the carcass from a turkey breast so it fit perfectly. I pressure cooked it for 2 hours and then slow cooked it for another 2 days. I don’t like sweet potatoes so I used carrots instead and added celery and kohlrabi (because I have it growing in the garden and need to use it!). This soup is very good and so flexible. The dumplings are good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Fantastic recipe and fantastic idea from Sohla- so excited to see her featured on nytimes!!! 
One note:Definitely sub out sweet potatoes for carrots! 
Would be a bit too rich, I think, with sweet potatoes. 
Though I imagine it would be delicious if you were using cornbread (my stuffing was the Ina sausage and apple classic!)
Kale was glorious. Making them big like Sohla does is key for tender fluffy dumps! I formed them by hand (messy-yes) and delicately tucked them in!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I really wanted to like these dumplings. Sadly, we found them to be too heavy and salty to be enjoyable. The soup was quite good without them.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I recommend chilling the dumpling batter for 10-15 min before putting the dumplings in the boiling broth.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Can you make the dumplings and freeze on their own, then make the soup at a later date?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this yesterday with the leftover turkey. I used carrots instead of sweet potatoes because I was tired of sweet potatoes. Amazing and filling. This is the first time I have made dumplings and it was easy!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made a vegetable soup my way from my leftovers and took only the dumpling portion of this recipe. I'm not all that familiar with making dumplings on top of soups/stews, so I wasn't sure how to know if they were done. I simmered them for about 23 minutes. They were fantastic! What a great way to repurpose stuffing/dressing! As a reference, my holiday dressing is always "Simple is Best Dressing" from Bon Appetit. (torn bread pieces, celery, onions - very traditional)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used year old stuffing, frozen and thawed, from last thanksgiving. Hopes were for something edible. It exceeded my hopes and received great reviews from family.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Hello, are there any suggested alternatives if one does not have a pressure cooker?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sure, just make it on a stove-top, as you would make any stock.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Take your turkey bones and put it in a crock pot for 8 hours with water.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The recipe says, or 3/12 - 4 hours on top of the stove.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I saw the video and cant wait to  make it. I did a turkey carcass gumbo last year Post Thanksgiving this recipe seems easier.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm going to do this, but, I already threw away the carc of the turkey, so I think I'll try and use up some of the gravy that I made and possibly dissolve it into the water, if necessary toss in bullion cubes or something. Worst case scenario, I'm only out the gravy. We will see what happens! Thank you for the idea to use a lot of dressing that might otherwise go to waste. :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We always return the turkey carcass to the oven after dinner, then make stock with the roasted bones and a few aromatics. I do it on the stove top. Lots of leftovers this year ... just the two of us! We subbed butternut squash for the potatoes but otherwise made this soup as written. It was so good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I was so excited to try this given we had no matzo meal on hand for our traditional post-Thanksgiving matzo ball soup. I must have done something very wrong because the dumplings all mushed together into one soggy mess. It was inedible.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm making this today. With cornbread dressing, though I haven't reached the dumpling-making stage yet. Still simmering the bone broth. Can someone explain the purpose/effect of the cider vinegar? Thanks.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have read elsewhere that the vinegar helps to leach the minerals out of the bones.  The bones will get soft and the broth will get more gelatinous and mineral-rich.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Michelle F. That is actually the point of adding vinegar to stock, to break down the bones for minerals. You really don’t get a vinegar flavor in the stock.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Just made this recipe and it turned out amazing!!!!! Definitely going to use it each year :) I also used leftover cornbread dressing, and the dumplings were great.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I messed up the dumplings on this and they completely dissolved in the soup, BUT it was still amazing! Everything you could want in a warm winter soup.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this yesterday. Only change was to use already roasted sweet potatoes since that's what I had. Put them in at the end of cooking. Really good and a great one dish meal. BTW, roast sweet potatoes in oven, 400 degF, and don't wrap them. They might ooze but won't explode and come out extra sweet.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Truly delicious idea. Sohla is awesome.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: When exactly do you add the cubed turkey to the soup?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: At the end of Step 6 ;)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My dumplings also fell apart in the soup.  I just scooped out the big chunks and considered the rest a thickener!  Still a great recipe and I’ll practice my dumplings another time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this soup with leftover chicken and cornbread dressing and homemade chicken stock. Absolutely delicious!! I used baby kale from my garden, and didn’t add it until the end. Worked fine. The sweet potatoes were fairly mushy. Next time I’ll cook them alone for only about 5 minutes in the stock since they keep cooking while the dumplings cook.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used Japanese sweets, sausage, carrot, bay leaf. Milk in dumplings instead of broth. Kale had vinegar in it but added a bit more.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed this recipe from the carcass stock to the dumplings and it was amazing!  Made this with leftover bread/cornbread dressing and the dumplings were light and flavorful.   Will take other posters suggestion and freeze what’s left of the soup.   This will be a Thanksgiving staple now.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was incredible! So flavorful and satisfying. I made it with leftover roast chicken. If you have leftover roasted squash or sweet potatoes (like I did), you can dice them and add just before the dumplings to warm them through. This used up all the leftovers in my fridge (except the cranberry sauce).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Followed the directions exactly as written, except we had already pitched the turkey carcass, so I just used store-bought broth. It turned out absolutely delicious anyway!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What a wonderful way to use leftover dressing! Our dressing is very traditional, plain (onions, celery, bread, butter, broth and seasonings), and the dumplings were so tasty. Oh, one swap: we had butternut squash and not sweet potatoes left after Thanksgiving. The soup was still amazingly good.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Excellent. Made with the NYT Roasted turkey stock (because I have just made 10 quarts of that). Terrific.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This soup is so delicious!!! A great way to breathe new life into leftovers.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Making the dumplings is messy, i.e. not like mixing drop biscuits. As soon as the baking powder hit the eggs and flour, the mixture started to expand and get very sticky. Thought you'd mix it into the mashed dressing by hand? Think again. Use soup or serving spoons. And, this is said by someone who doesn't mind glop. (Traditional dressing w/ bread cubes/onions/celery took a while to mash.) We didn't do a turkey this pandemic year, so used veg broth for the soup. Worked well. Will make it again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This was a brilliant way to use Thanksgiving leftovers!  Probably one of the best soups I ever had!  My husband makes the Paul Prudhomme dressing recipe so it has plenty of spice. As one reviewer recommended, I used carrots instead of sweet potatoes. I also added some celery. 
Otherwise, I followed the recipe as noted. I can’t wait to make this again next year!  Scrumptious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I did this with sage cornbread dressing made in a pan, the southern way, not stuffed in the turkey, and it was unbelievable!  My husband could not stop exclaiming over it.  I think he liked it better than the actual Thanksgiving dinner :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I used roasted chicken cubed and made broth with 'better than boullion' chicken base for speed and its what I had. I added a touch of Cavender's Greek seasoning and it turned out lovely!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: BEST thing I have ever made with Thanksgiving leftovers!! Used my food processor to lightly pulse the stuffing/dumpling ingredients. Will definitely be using this recipe every year!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was VERY good. I’d make it again anytime that I have leftover stuffing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious broth. I augmented mine with frozen leftover home made chicken broth. Our turkey was smoked this year, which added a great flavor to the finished soup. I found the dumplings rather too heavy, but maybe I find nearly all dumplings too heavy. I'd make again without using the turkey dressing for dumplings. I'd make lighter dumplings from scratch or use egg noodles, home made or store bought. Appreciate thoughts about perfecting the dumplings.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This IS an amazing recipe. My Mississippi Delta Mother made amazing cornbread dressing. It was her pride and our joy. I give it the old college try and work hard to recreate her dressing. We can never consume the mass quantity I prepare. This year I made these dumplings and turkey soup. Do it. Make these dumplings. They are brilliant.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made this soup with the leftovers after thanksgiving and it was delicious!
My husband went hunting yesterday and shot a wild turkey.  He’s been slow cooking the legs all day and they smell terrific. I want to make this soup again but I only have Stove Top available. Has anyone tried this?  Should I leave out the broth in the dumplings?  Stove Top is much wetter than my homemade stuffing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I will never toss a turkey carcass again. This was so delicious.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We loved this dish! It was my first time making dumplings and they turned out great. It's a nice and light dish but also very comforting.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: did not go well--dumplings, which were GF, disintegrated; perhaps the mixture needs to be drier or the broth not boiling. Turned my beautiful stock and stuffing into SOS looking mush.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Incredible. Will start a new tradition of making this each year. My stuffing was already very moist and the dumplings ended up tender and nearly weightless. I added lots of chopped parsley to the dumpling mixture. I didn’t have sweet potato or kale so I just made a traditional celery/onion/carrot base for the soup and it was wonderful (but would love to make exactly as written next time!).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Will not make this again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Ohhh, this is sooooo good.  And I am so thankful to find a way to use up leftover stuffing!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This wanted to be a savory soup but the sweet potatoes fought it and it ended up being too sweet. Also, my stuffing dumplings disintegrated into a mushy mess but that could just be the type of stuffing I use.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love this recipe.  I made a breakfast strata (which is basically stuffing) for Mother's Day.  There was a ton leftover so I used it to make the dumplings for the soup.  Delish!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Spinach
Amino Acids
Soy
Apple Cider Vinegar

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I love stuffing dumplings but this year made the wild rice cranberry sausage stuffing by Nancy Harmon Jenkins on this site to try something new (was afraid it wouldn't satisfy in the same way but it is good) and I'm debating whether it would be remotely possible to turn that leftover wild rice into dumplings. I did make some pancakes with it the day after Thanksgiving and was not overly impressed. Maybe better to just stir the stuffing into the soup in this case. Anyone have thoughts?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Added a couple Tablespoons of soy sauce and powdered porcini to my broth; chard instead of kale… very satisfying!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Made this off season (not thanksgiving) with a few substitutions- it was delicious! Changes made:

Ground turkey breast - added an element to the broth that elevated the final product

Stove top stuffing - solid dumplings, simple living

Will make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Dog’s breakfast.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If stuffing is moist, may need only one egg.  Definitely needed refrigerating before putting into the soup.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this last night using the bones of our whole turkey breast and probably more stuffing than the recipe called for. The first few balls fell apart in the water, even using an ice cream soup; I persevered and used a firmer hand, rolling and gently placing the remaining balls in the broth. Those remained intact, and early disintegrated ones added a velvety luscious to the broth. The final product was absolutely delicious, both familiar and a little different, and possibly one of the best recipe adaptations from a Thanksgiving leftovers. Definitely a keeper.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I get annoyed with people who have to change every recipe. However, I didn’t make the soup as written but from leftovers I turned 2 legs, a partial thigh and a wing into a soup with added onion, carrot and parsnip. I then made the dumplings as written. Wow. Great dumplings and worth making extra stuffing so I can make these again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: That was a lot of suggested salt! I stopped adding additional salt after sautéing the onions and sweet potatoes. I added some mashed potatoes to the dumplings. Added some left over gravy and beans to the soup for flavoring

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made as directed. Great second batch even after all  the dumplings were done.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I cooked this well after Thanksgiving, with stuffing frozen after an oversupply from a public Christmas Dinner.  It was well received.  I used Better than Bouillon vegetable and roasted garlic for the broth since I didn't have a turkey carcass, and frozen chopped kale for simplicity, cost, and speed. One pot got lentils for the vegetarians, another shredded chicken breast and their juice for the carnivores.  I kept the dumplings separate for the gluten avoiders.  Lots of people took all three.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m seventy, and there has never been leftover stuffing at my house.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sounds like in the future you need to start making more stuffing.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We always have leftover dressing because I make it in its own roaster.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So skip the bread and mayo, along with the cranberries, and bring out the gravy and a poached egg. At our house we look forward to breakfast the next day with eggs "mcStuffin" off the griddle. We take the left over dressing and form into patties--adding a beaten egg and or milk/stock if needed to bind and form. Stufifng patties are griddled golden brown not he outside and topped with a poached egg and a big spoon of gravy--

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious, no doubt. But too much trouble. The weekend after Thanksgiving should be a lazy one! I have made leftover sandwiches for decades with a schmear of mayo and a layer of cranberry sauce, followed by a layer of squooshed down stuffing and sliced white meat turkey. Then lettuce, for cool crunch and a brief nod to healthy eating. Don't neglect salt and pepper. And you have a huge mouth and feeling ambitious, add bacon and tomato for a leftover BLT. Then wallow happily in ongoing excess...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @adbwest83 I do similar. Microwave the layers of stuffing and mayo turkey beneath provolone then slide it onto toasted sourdough smeared with cranberry relish and mayo, Top with arugula and more mayo on top bread.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Why not use the stuffing as the bread? You could mix in a little beaten egg and put it in a wafflemaker. Sandwich the turkey, sides, and cranberry mayo inside the stuffing waffles.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Shades of Dagwood Bumstead

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: all works good together, but its too big for the mouth and goes sideways when compressed. It became a mess upon use. No Kudos.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My family’s tradition, going back four generations at least, is toasted white bread, a smear of mayo, stuffing, cranberry sauce, and leftover turkey. Add some salt and pepper and you’ve good to go. As a kid, I enjoyed that sandwich much more than the meal on the big day.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Put the pressed stuffing in the freezer up to 10 minutes to get a nice chill on it instead of an overnight refrigerated situation. This is the perfect sandwich for someone who didn't cook and took leftovers home. Texas toast is the right kind of bread for the hearty build. This is also a sandwich you need to eat with a fork. It's perfect and I love it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Yes, Turkey Sandwiches MUST have dressing on them!
My favorite condiment for them is Stonewall Kitchens Cranberry Horseradish Sauce....or just make your own with combo of mayo, horseradish sauce, and whole cranberry sauce.......delish and divine!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Best ever cranberry sauce to add to this - Mama Stamberg’s - from NPR’s Susan Stamberg. Great story too. Don’t let the ‘Pepto Bismal’ color put you off. It’s delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Susan May she rest in peace.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Gilding the lily. Favorite bread, favorite Mayo, turkey, cranberry, stuffing = Favorite sandwich

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
The context provided by the intro is generally helpful for understanding the NYT recipes. In this instance, it's featured in an article on innovative uses of leftover stuffing.  The recipe might seem fussy to you but given that context, it makes sense. The sandwich is written around a technique for, and to highlight...you guessed it--stuffing. I come to NYT Cooking for new ideas, techniques, ingredients and inspiration. And they deliver. 

I ate this and it was good. I can be lazy on my own.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thanks. Got my own version of what I call a Sunday Dinner Sandwich that I've been making for 40+ years. Had to save this recipe though, because the comments are just to rich to pass up. Keep 'em coming... I'll check in with you all in 2022.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made a modified version of this sandwich and it was delicious! Truly best post thanksgiving sandwich I’ve ever had. 

I just smashed down stuffing right before cooking (didn’t chill overnight) and crisped it up as directed. 

Prepared the white meat and dark meat shredded and basted in butter and gravy—used the same pan—added a little salt and pepper. 

Used cranberry sauce mixed with Durkeys (Mayo/mustard combo) on lightly toasted sour dough. 

Did not take that much time and was tasty!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This looks SO good but after spending 8 hours in the kitchen making all the things only to have my guest barely touch her dinner, I’m doing this: two slices of good white bread slathered with mayo and dusted with salt and pepper, topped with thinly sliced cooked turkey breast.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Have noticed that, the time invested vs time spent enjoying is way out of whack.  Hours and hours of preparation, and yet, how long do guests linger?  It's not like a three-course meal, that is actually leisurely and enjoyable, more like a gobble and dash.  For some of us this traditional feast needs a rework.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Cynthia we were just saying the exact thing last night after we were done spending an exhausting two days prepping not to mention the shopping in the cleaning at the house. And then it’s all over within half an hour. And then everybody’s exhausted and wants to go to sleep. next year we are doing things differently… What that’s going to look like we don’t know but we’re tired of doing this year after year. Happy Cranksgiving lol!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It depends on the family. 2pm meal guests arrive 10a-1p, sideboard is loaded, turkey is paraded to obligatory oohs and ahhs, removed and carved, Grace and feast. Two hours and 2-3 platefuls later the leftovers are divided and packed for each guest and the carcass is back on the table for picking. Latecomers get a nuked plate. Most stay at the table, some go to the living room for football/nap and return. Dessert is around 5-6p, most clear out by 8-9p. They've all passed now. Miss them deeply.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: To take this sandwich to the next level, save the drippings from the turkey to make an au jus for dipping. Absolutely divine.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sounds delicious, but if I wind up with the turkey carcass it goes into a big pot with onions, carrots, celery, bay leaves and big sprigs of fresh thyme. Fill that pot with water to the brim, simmer it all day until it’s reduced by half. Strain out the solids, tossing anything still good back in the pot. Add shredded turkey, sliced leeks, more carrots, orzo, and a handful of chopped greens (kale, spinach, beet greens, whatever) at the end! Delicious, and the aroma all day is sublime.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Great recipe. The cranberry Mayo is delicious. I didn’t prep the stuffing the night before so I combined stuffing with mashed potatoes and fried them as written. No leftover green vegetables so I just used salad greens. Will make again!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This was fun to make and hilarious to eat. 

Which is exactly the point. 

I make my own cranberry sauce and it perfect with the mayo. Did I finish it with a knife and fork? Absolutely. No regrets.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: In the old days, before the cancellation of meatless Fridays, we used to wait up until Midnight then tuck into the Thanksgiving sandwich. In some ways, it was better the the big day itself. Nobody we had to tolerate and fewer dishes to wash. My recipe, don’t overthink, it just put anything you want into it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We make “Stuffles” or stuffing waffles in a waffle iron. Layer with warmed Turkey, douse with gravy (or St Hubert Sauce if you are from Québec) and serve with cranberry compote and leftover scalloped or mashed potatoes. YUM

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This is my preference

2 slices good sourdough bread slightly toasted
Kerrygold butter 
Slice of Turkey breast
Dollop of cranberry sauce 
Sausage stuffing which was cooked in the neck of the Turkey
Slice of slow Baked ham (24 hour low temp cook in cider)
Dijon Mustard

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
First time I put dressing/stuffing on my day-after turkey sandwich at my first Thanksgiving at my new in-laws house in SC, I got laughed out of the house.
"Look at him - he's he's putting " bread" on his bread. He's got a "bread sandwich." Mortified, but stuck to my guns and repeatedly made and thoroughly enjoyed my "bread sandwich" every year. (My in-laws remain 2 of the finest people in my life).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Rich S nice comment about your in-laws. They are smart as well. I asked myself the same thing!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I make burritos with Thanksgiving leftovers: turkey, mashed potatoes, stuffing, gravy and cranberry sauce wrapped in a buttito.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Have your in-laws ever had a club sandwich? That's a 3rd slice of toasted bread between two slices of toasted bread.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Impressive.  Try on garlicky Texas toast. Yum.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Is this The Moist Maker sandwich that Ross loved?  Asking for a friend.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A burrito with leftover turkey, mashed potatoes, stuffing and gravy is a favorite in our house. Add sides an condiments as desired.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: toasted sourdough roll, slices of turkey, avocado, and micro greens

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is “the best” only if the sole criterion is more is always better. I prepared one of these “everything sandwiches” a couple of years ago and concluded that such a combo cancels out the various individual subtle flavors that comprise Thanksgiving dinner. You might as well jam in a hunk of pumpkin pie too! Very much to the contrary, I recommend a simple turkey and cranberry sauce—with maybe a touch of quality mayo—on a good sourdough bread, at least if you prefer yummy taste to the kitchen sink.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Toastymaker vegetarian version: stuffing, brie, cranberry sauce- stuffing. let it get golden til the cheese oozes out. the best.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A local restaurant here in San Diego has a similar sandwich on the menu for the entire month of November. It is SO GOOD I’d consider devious acts to obtain it. I can’t be arsed to make it for myself but for those who can, your taste buds will be rewarded handsomely.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
It’s really not complicated. I suggest folks try before commenting. 

These sandwiches are heavenly, I look forward to making them every time we make a turkey and inevitably the response is overwhelmingly positive.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Put a little prepared horseradish in that cranberry mayo in honor of Susan Stamberg's mother in law!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
My initial comment was, “this is too involved, too much trouble,” which I said to my son. 

His reply was, “looks fantastic,” and I was soon (eagerly) pressed into service as sous chef, busily shredding turkey while he did the rest of 

And it was fantastic. He didn’t think the sweet potatoes and green beans could have fit, but I’d still like to see how it would taste together. 

More than a recipe - it’s family time!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: There’s nothing like some of that left over sliced turkey, any color that’s left over, on sourdough, with mayo lettuce and a slice of American cheese, a sour pickle and six or seven potato chips on the side. A tiny wedge of pumpkin pie after. Preferably, after midnight.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The photo is definitely Instagram worthy.  Look at the color of that cran-mayo! I'd like to replace the stuffing (this solves Linda Broere's problem of no leftover stuffing) with a buttery, pan-fried mashed potato cake.  The gooeyness of the potatoes cakes, in their crisp and buttery glory, will help hold the sandwich together.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: A little cheese would also help the mashed potato layer hold together. Just sayin'.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Masterful recipe from Sohla. Love the flourishes including compressing the stuffing into a delicious flavor brick. The reason each ingredient is heated the way she specifies is it brings back the best flavor/properties from each element. It’s not an “average” sandwich it’s her “best” sandwich! Worth the time and effort if you want to dazzle and delight.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious sandwich! Wouldn’t change a thing about it!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Oh wow this certainly takes the cold cuts and salad leftovers to a different level!!!!I can barely wait for Christmas Day to be over so I can hook out the leftovers and make these sandwiches!!
Christmas in NZ is somewhat different due to the weather and all I can say is it’s been on heck of a year,but this recipe( I subscribed to NYT cooking during our own lockdown)is to have something to look forward to!
Happy thanksgiving to you all!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Dagwood and Sohla! What a team!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Anybody can make a turkey sandwich but this is amazing! I love the idea of putting Thanksgiving table between two slices of bread...at a whole new level. Love the technique for the stuffing squares and the idea/color of the cranberry mayo. Makes sense to treat the white and dark meat differently. Best thing: ordinary becomes extraordinary.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Long time NYT recipe devotee, first ever comment! I love the passion so many of us feel around the day after (or night of) sandwich. And the devotion to our own ways that is often intrinsically linked to memories of meals long past. Not much to add to the critiques and amendments already made. But for the record - open face here, starting with mayonnaise (which almost everyone agrees is a must) and ending with gravy on top. This was always my dad's favorite Thanksgiving moment. Mine too!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: People protest too much. I made this sandwich tonight for 6 people. Is it a little fussy to do all the griddling of ingredients? Sure. But it was 30 minutes of total cooking and the sandwich was WAY better than the "just throw a bunch of cold stuff on some cold bread" suggested in the comments. "Chill overnight" is indeed unnecessary, and I didn't bother. If you have any skill with a pan, it's not that hard to keep your "stuffing patties" from coming apart.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe takes the “leftovers sandwich” VERY seriously, which is great, because I do too! Feel free to skip any of the steps you find fussy (I thought chilling the stuffing overnight would be overkill) but if you do anything, PLEASE warm the dark meat in gravy as suggested - total game changer. The cranberry mayo too!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: We are big on toasted onion rolls, sliced turkey, warmed stuffing and cranberry sauce.  A great and easy sandwich to make and enjoy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My husband threw our cornmeal stuffing out (he's still apologizing).  Can I still make this sandwich by skipping the 5 steps that involve stuffing and focusing on the other 4 steps?  Honestly, this recipe made me laugh.  Thank you NYT Cooking and commentors for helping to make this pandemic bearable.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I give you permission to do anything you want with this sandwichl.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: What do you think ?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Relax!  In pyrex cup, microwave the leftover gravy until good and hot. Briefly microwave the the turkey, mash potatoes and stuffing on plate. Pour gravy over other leftovers, enjoy. Cranberry sauce optional.  No bread needed.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thanks for this inspiration!  A friend and I decided at the last minute to share an impromptu holiday dinner.  Too late to make my favorite Turketta(porchetta with turkey breast) so we substituted deli turkey.  Crisp bacon, cornbread stuffing run under the broiler, finished with cranberry chutney, and piled on toasted artisan bread slathered with Hellman’s.   A modest Chardonnay and really great dessert - best ever Thanksgiving!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was great! We used sourdough sandwich bread ana it was perfect. It held up under the weight of all that filling and had a good taste as well. We were lucky to have terrific leftovers from children and grandchildren who are superb cooks!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I personally just put two slices of bread in the toaster.  When they are toasted, it's mayo on both of them, some iceberg lettuce, some horseradish and sliced turkey.  Nothing else needed.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Skip the turkey - stuffing sandwiches with mayo and salt are the bomb. Better than the whole meal the day before!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made these sandwiches after Thanksgiving in 2020 and my partner still talks about them. Worth the effort.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Cream cheese instead of the mayo and now you're talking

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
If you want to spice it up, add some pickled jalapeños. I sometimes take a thin spread of mashed potatoes for the bread to complete the sandwich.
If you don’t want to go to all this trouble, simply heat briefly in the microwave the turkey, stuffing, tiny bit of gravy to moisten the sandwich, but make sure you do not over moisten . Toasting the bread makes it much better

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'm certainly not understanding the stuffing steps. Why do you need to grease a pan only to put parchment over it and not actually cook in the pan?! Why are you reheating the stuffing before putting it back in the fridge?! Very weird steps.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Agreed!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Not weird steps: helpful steps. The stuffing is warmed briefly to make it pliable. The pan is greased so the parchment will not stick to it; the parchment is used to left the stuffing out of the pan without breaking. If you have another way that's easier, do it your way.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Here’s how I make my favorite turkey sandwich: apply mayo on slices of your favorite toasted sourdough or other bread, layer on turkey, then cranberry sauce, then cheddar cheese, then sliced apple…enjoy it open faced or not.  Simple, fast, delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You can make just a good sandwich a bit quicker. Simply heat up in the microwave for a minute the turkey, stuffing, small amount of gravy and put that directly on the sandwich. I had pickled jalapeños and a splash of Sriracha with the mayo and sometimes Spread a tiny bit of warm mashed potatoes with a touch of cranberry sauce on the bread. Toasting the bread makes the sandwich almost perfect. You can make this sandwich in just a couple minutes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Have been making these sandwiches,on my own, for over 60 years.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I laughed at the comments .I tried this sandwich even though I too was exhausted from cooking and cleaning up. I was disappointed but I should have realized that it was a hot sandwich. I prefer my traditional sandwich with all the ingredients being cold- stuffing, Cranberry sauce and  the Turkey. The toast is warm but the rest is cold.This recipe was a clever way to have a hot Turkey sandwich in a hand held version not open faced.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Make sandwich open faced.  Form round patties with stuffing,  heat turkey with butter.  Remove turkey, add more butter sautéing stuffing to crispness on both sides.  Have heated gravy ready.  

Toast bread, put cranberry relish on bottom (homemade is easy, I make it throughout the year by vacuum freezing fresh cranberries from this time of year…love it on hamburgers).  Then stuffing, then turkey.  

Pour gravy over the sandwich and enjoy. NYT Cranberry RelishRecipe.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
An old fashioned gooseberry is a lot like this which is a hit in our house. 
Marble rye
Cranberry sauce
Stuffing or mashed potato’s
Turkey in gravy
Thin slices of onion. 
Heated on one plate in microwave. Voila.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I’m with Linda. What leftover stuffing?!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This sounds amazing. I am so looking forward to borrowing your creativity to create this sandwich. Our tradition is to enjoy our Thanksgiving dinner then take a day off with seafood or pizza. By Saturday, we are ready to delve into the leftovers and enjoy it all again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Heck, growing up we made this very simple. A nice slice of dressing between two slices of bread, toasted was optional, there you have it. My mother’s dressing was the best. I thought of it as veggie loaf. Very fond memories.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this on lightly toasted marble rye with only the white meat, stuffing (I fried it in the same butter as the turkey), and cranberry mayo and it was perfect. Leftover broccoli salad on the side.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I'll take a thin slice of breast meat; and a few shards of dark, on two fresh slices of light rye , w/ a good dab of house mayo and a dollop of homemade cranberry sauce....

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thanks for this recipe - love it! Will save for the future -

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is similar to the Amish stand near my work’s everyday Turkey sandwich offering; they are yummy. I think the stand doesn’t fry the stuffing though and I always skip the cranberry sauce. I’ve done it at home. Thanks for the tips.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
This recipe gets off to a good start. If I may suggest...
Preheat oven to 375-400F
Parchment 'sling' line a pan.
Lay in stuffing, press.
Make Roulade of Mayo and Cranberry Sauce. Add some Pecorino or Hot Sauce for some Zing.
Mix chopped meats with gravy, an egg or two and season to taste. Throw in a 3 finger pinch of grated Parm or Mozz or Gruyere or Mexican Blend or ???
Layer on top of stuffing.
Bake for 20-30 minute or until top is a little crisp.
Serve over crisp shredded greens.
Bon appetit

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thought it was worth the effort.  Didn't wait overnight for the stuffing.  Made 2 patties on parchment paper and put it in the freezer for 1 1/2 hrs.  Also used brioche extra large sandwich rolls.  Very filling and had salad for dinner.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I just made this and it was outstanding! I read through some of the comments after the fact and was really surprised that people thought this was too fussy. It only took me about 10-15 min total. I didn’t chill the stuffing overnight because I found this recipe today, but I simply put a patty if it on my panini press and that worked just fine! Otherwise, I wouldn’t change a thing. Definitely not too fussy :) and soooo delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe is really a mess... too much work, it falls apart at the first bite , and , in the end, it is bread on bread ...should have followed the advice of the other people who tried it before me...

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Beaver Brand makes cranberry mustard that is quite good on turkey sandwiches throughout the year.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This sandwich has the components of a turkey pie I make but not with everything.   Beans, carrots, cranberry sauce, stuffing, gravy with a layer of puff pastry (store bought) on top.  Eat as small a slice as you want for lunch on the days following Thanksgiving.  There is no gorging.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was delicious.  I kept it simple and skipped the overnight step with the stuffing. I also kept the sandwich to just the meat, stuffing, and cranberry mayo and it was filling with just those ingredients.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Much easier way to do this, we always look forward to it in my house. 

Toast the bread. While bread is toasting, spread assorted leftovers on a plate, top with dollops of gravy, and microwave until hot. I do turkey, mashed potatoes, stuffing, and veggies from under the bird (best part!).

Spread mayo (plain is fine, cranberry-mayo works too) on bread. Top with leftovers. Optional to add lettuce and tomato too. 

Just as good and ready in 5 minutes :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: An inspiration! My daughter and I loved our “Sandwich aux Leftovers.” I buttered the outsides of two toasted slices of sourdough (nothing special-just from a package from the grocery store.) Then, I smeared mayo and cranberry sauce on the other sides. I layered slices of turkey breast. Added soft cubes of leftover maple-roasted butternut squash; then topped with a smattering of baby arugula. I finished with a couple shakes of sea salt. We smooshed everything down, and ate every bite.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This year we plan the "leftover" turkey sandwich to be our thanksgiving meal, because everyone knows the sandwich is the best part of thanksgiving.  Having a whole meal in the sandwich is exactly our goal!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Agreed with Doug. I just make a plate of Turkey, potatoes, stuffing, veggies from under the bird and gravy and *gasp* microwave it until warm. 

Toasted bread, thin smear of mayo, assemble in layers. Cranberry optional. Easy, divine, rewarding and yes, probably better than the original meal.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Better still, start with a Boneless Turkey Breast, stuffed with herbs(Rosemary and Fennel) and thin sliced Pancetta.  Have the butcher flatten the Breast and pound a little. Then put the herbs on the Breast   and spead them out  and then lay on the thinly sliced Pancetta. Then roll it up and put the net on. 
A 5lb Breeast gets 1/2 lb of Pancetta and cooks in the oven in 1.5 hrs. Then slice it thin. This is Turkey Tamburini, from a Delicatessen of that name in Bologna, Italy. From Bibi Gaggiano

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I created my stuffing layer by shaping it into an oval. I sprayed them with a little olive oil and cooked them in my air fryer for about 15 minutes.  This was the perfect topping for our hot turkey sandwiches along with a little cranberry sauce.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Shortcut: One slice gets the cold/crisp ingredients, the other the hot, like so. Spread mayo thinly on both slices. Toast open-faced on medium-hot pan. Pile what you want hot onto one slice. Remove the other slice as soon as it's toasted on one side. Cover pan so that hot-side ingredients get hot. Pile cold/crisp ingredients on slice you've removed. Once hot side is where you want it, use one or two spatulas to marry sides with minimal slippage. Tuck napkin into shirt collar.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Why grease the pan before you put the paper down? Doesn't it make more sense to butter the parchment paper after it's in the pan?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The grease helps the paper stick to it.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Love the idea of fried stuffing. Will have to add to my repertoire. Surprised to see that no one mentioned my “go-to” ingredients: Mayo with a generous amount of Dijon on one slice and a good shmear of cream cheese (nuked for 10 seconds to make it spreadable) and cranberries on the other slice. To all that I’ve served this to, the flavors of turkey, cream cheese, cranberries together with the Dijon mixed in gets rave reviews.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow what a great sandwich!  I prepared each of the components as the recipe instructs,.....but when I went to assemble the sandwiches, I  had no room for any of the "assorted leftovers"  - or it would have been impossible to eat.   Isn't the idea of a sandwich to get a mix of tastes when you bite into it?  Still, even without the extra leftover-layers,  the white and dark meat, fried stuffing and cranberry mayo were wonderful - this is our new leftover sandwich.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Our traditional leftover sandwich forgoes the stuffing, greens etc. and is what I look forward to more than the main meal.  I make sure to buy what’s needed for the sandwich the day I shop for the dinner.  Two slices of fresh sourdough, smear of mayo, sliced Turkey , lots of uncooked cranberry orange chutney and a layer of Swiss or Jarlsberg cheese. Top with a spoonful of gravy, Bess it with salt and pepper and you have achieved divinity………

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Our family has a long tradition of serving ravioli with turkey on holidays.  So the day after , or midnight, sanwhicjrs are made with turkey, ravioli, Sunday sauce, cranberry, and mayo.  Aunt Tilly sliced through the bowl of ravioli but now we just place 4 ravioli in a single layer    Ravioli provide a little flavor to a turkey sandwich

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It’s quite simple. If you can’t actually pick up a sandwich and take a bite there’s no point in making it. I love sandwiches, but this is way too big/tall for someone to eat it. You can’t take a reasonable bite without straining or clamping down so hard you crush the fillings. Over time, we often learn that less is more!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: British bubble and squeak is easier.  And less messy.  Lots of people here have suggested variations - basically, whatever leftovers in a skillet, add eggs, AND a heathy handful of cheese (that's the part that makes it official) and fry until it, you know, bubbles and squeaks.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Skip the turkey and just have a stuffing sandwich. Plus if you stuff the bird or bake it in a loaf pan it’s sliceable and you don’t have to do all the pressing the night before.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: My favorite thing to do with leftover turkey is keep it in the fridge in a vacuum pack until Sunday rolls along. Then, make a hash with the leftover turkey meat, diced potatoes, onions, fresh thyme, butter, and white wine to top crispy English muffins. Lay a couple of perfectly poached eggs, add hollandaise, and voila!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I could never get my head around using stuffing as a filling for a sandwich. Too much bread. Instead, stuffing can actually be used in place of bread simply by forming it in squares, refrigerating it, then grilling it. Pretty tasty.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Three words - Leftovers Hot Brown
As I type, I'm envisioning a riff on the classic Hot Brown but using leftovers. Start with griddled dressing patties to replace the bread, cranberry-mayo, turkey, leftover sides, topped with gravy. Add a slice or two of Swiss cheese (or cheddar or whatever preferred melty cheese) and slide under the broiler to melt and brown. Top with a couple slices bacon and tomato, as desired.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The only take away for me is cranberry and mayo mixed together.  That I will try!  But the rest is too much work!  An easy alternative: turkey, cranberry sauce, mashed potatoes and/or yams, creamed onions, and gravy smooshed between two slices of toasted sourdough w/ dijon mustard.   Delish!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sounds great but way too complicated, as other commenters have noted.  Unfortunately,  one of our favorite holiday traditions, Grandpa's famous chopped chicken liver (mostly gently cooked hb eggs, cooked and raw onion, and schmaltz) was a sell-out last night, so I can't have my personal favorite, white meat turkey, chopped liver and a thin raw onionslice on sour dough bread. Will have to *settle* for Sam Sifton's quickie!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this three years ago and I still think about it. It truly is the best Thanksgiving sandwich ever!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You forgot one instruction.......SIT on it before you try to eat it 'cuz it is TOO big to get into your mouth!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: With only my wife and I, we opted out of the usual T-giving meal and made shepherd's pie with lamb!  Little work, great meal, and leftovers are just as good!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thanksgiving sandwiches with leftovers are the best part of Thanksgiving, hands down.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Friday: All the leftovers I choose, reheated in a large shallow bowl to hold the gravy, dollop of cool cranberry sauce and stuffed celery on the side.  Saturday: Your favorite bread, schmear cream cheese, cranberry sauce, and sunflower seeds on one slice, mayo on the other.  Turkey, shaved onion and lettuce.  Could add stuffing?  Potato chips and pickles on the side.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: The  sandwich combo you describe is also known in parts as a Peter Stuyvesant sandwich and is delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I think you may have it confused with another sandwich, Nina

I've had the Peter Stuyvesant sandwich at Brothers' Deli in NYC, and it's definitely not this!  A Peter Stuyvesant has chicken, salami, provolone, lettuce tomato, Italian dressing.  It's fantastic!

An extensive Internet search shows no other sandwich with that name.

But you have me wondering what that similar sandwich actually is called, as I'd like to see some recipes for that one, too!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Squirrel Butler: Nina says "in parts', so perhaps those parts are not known to you. I bet a Dagwood sandwich is made differently in various parts.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was INSANE!! Thanks so much!! Made great use of my sausage and herb stuffing and my Ina Garten gravy recipe with Veal Demi Glacé!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: More like s stuffing sandwich with turkey…need much more turkey and half the thickness of stuffing (IMHO)!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Not giving any stars because I didn’t make this sandwich but have been making a rendition of it for years. 

Mine is simpler. Seeded Jewish rye bread is a must. Lightly toast it, liberally spread good mayonnaise, ie, Hellman’s or Dukes, on both sides, and assemble it with layers of turkey, a mix of dark and white meat, cranberry sauce, and stuffing. It probably doesn’t really matter but I always put the cranberry sauce in the middle of the turkey and stuffing to keep the bread from getting soggy

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
I made this. Ugh. The added fat for the frying did nothing for it, it was entirely too tall to eat as a sandwich. I ended up breaking it apart, scraping off the cranberry mayo and eating the rest with a fork. This recipe just tries too hard and doesn't deliver.
Sorry, but we'll not do this again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
We’ve always turned leftover stuffing and mashed potatoes into waffles, turkey and fixings into hash, and served them with a fried egg. Easy and delicious. 
For sandwiches, it’s the whole 9 yards: gravy schmear on toasted bread, mashed potatoes on both slices to grip the stuffing on one side and the fixings on the other. I like to add thinly sliced jalapeños to add some freshness and spice. And finally, the turkey slices before closing the sandwich up. 
Love Thanksgiving!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I cardamom pod is roughly 1/6 teaspoon of ground cardamom

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Delicious as written. The only change I’ll make next time is to use a mini food processor instead of a blender. Could use boneless chicken thighs.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Can I use ground cardamom if I don’t have the pods?
And how much should I put?
Thank you

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This dish is a keeper! This got demolished during dinner. I amped up the spices per others’ suggestions. I used 2 lb boneless skinless chicken thighs, slightly more than 1/2 cup of yogurt, 1 big onion for purée and 1 small onions for frying, 6 cloves of garlic, 2 inch of ginger, 1 cinnamon stick, 5 cardamom pods and 2 bay leaves. If you’re expecting Mughal style korma (creamy and nutty) , this isn’t the dish. This is a Bangladeshi style Korma where the writer’s mom was from.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: One of the best things I've ever made from this site. Make sure to splash frequently throughout the uncovered braising process; it will help preserve the sauce so it doesn't burn off.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Sauce all burns off on this recipe, leaving an intense cardamon flavor on the chicken and certainly nothing to write home about. If I did it again I would use much more of the sauce ingredients except for salt and cardamon.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Saved all the crispy onions for the topping and used leftover sautéed onions to mix into the sauce. Added about 1/4 cup chip broth to keep the sauce saucy. Used Serrano chiles and chopped them into the sauce. Deboned the chicken to serve over basmati rice. Served peas sautéed with leftover red peppers and minced garlic as a side. Delicious! And now Mark wants to try Indian food. :)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this yesterday with lamb shoulder. AMAZING!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Very tasty; family loved it.  I served it with chana masala.  Since I have been baking sourdough, I served it with toasts and we wiped that bowl clean, sopping the bread in the leftover sauce!

The last time I made it, I doubled the yogurt/onions (both fried and pureed)/garlic/ginger, so that there was more sauce.

The key to this recipe is absolutely browning the onions, slices and puree, until deep golden brown.  That and don't be tempted to add water, just a "splash" here and there.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: One trick I learned from an Indian restaurant was to put sliced onions in a heat proof bowl or cup, ad a little water, top with a paper towel and microwave for about 3 minutes to soften the onions completely, cool a bit before blending.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So amazing. Cooked the recipe as written. Sohla is the best

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Fantastic recipe. "It tastes homemade." Also adaptable and forgiving.  I think key step is get the onion puree and spices good and brown before adding Chicken.  Used "more" of everything 2nd time  around with good result.  Used 3 lb bone in thighs.  One entire onion pureed.  1/2 to 3/4 cup yogurt (wanted more sauce).  More garlic and ginger.  Marinated overnight (no obvious difference).  More of the listed spices.  Added garam masala and cumin.  Skipped frizzled onions - yummy but not necessary.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: You really need just the cardamom seeds - not the husks too. I'd also question whether a chicken korma is truly authentic with both coconut (ground or cream) and, especially, ground almonds. The amount of liquid (from the yoghurt) in this recipe also appears very mean: a good korma should be lush and creamy.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Who cares if it's "authentic?"  Make something that tastes good.  The way I make something is authentic to me.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Please note that this is specifically a Bangladeshi korma and Sohla is Bengali-American so likely as authentic as they come!  You may be more familiar with the Indian style of korma which is heavier, creamier, and sweeter.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: @Janet.  Actually some of us care about authenticity a lot.  I know it's helpful if a 'foreign' recipe is accessible, but we lose a lot by thinking that any old way we want is fine.  Moreover, it's really nice to learn how dishes are made and presented.  In this case, you're actually making Amu's chicken - authentic to her.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This might be one of the best things I've ever made from NYT Cooking.  I only had nonfat plain yogurt so I added that with a touch of sour cream.  Otherwise I made it as written using a cut up whole chicken.  I initially thought there wasn't enough sauce for cooking but the released juices really brought it all together.  I never regret using whole cracked cardamom pods in recipes.  I recommend using these if possible, versus the ground.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow. This one is a keeper. I used cubed boneless breast, which is what I had. Next time I will double the recipe. I made white rice and the cucumber tomato salad suggested--the perfect accompaniment. Thank you so much for posting this, Sohla!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I do not know why I trusted this recipe over my truth-telling eyes.  There is no sauce, which is what I love about korma. Monitor it constantly so it does not burn, and your reward will be relatively plain chicken.  Sorry I’m pissy - RBG just died and I made this trying to have comfort food.  Missed the mark.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was fabulous as written. I might make a full onion's worth of the puree next time, because I'm using a fairly large Dutch oven and it was spread thin. I had to watch it pretty carefully to keep it from burning. But it did come together into a very tasty paste; the sauce being drier allowed the flavors to concentrate and caramelize. I also used turkey breast instead of chicken, which made a nice variation.
