author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I didn't have an onion on hand and made it without one. It turned out great, nonetheless. I used Indian mirchi (Rani brand) instead of green chiles.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thank you! I don't eat onions and was wondering if it would still be OK. Appreciate the note!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Loved this. As the recipe states, it’s milder than some other restaurant Indian dishes I’ve had. You can’t really taste the individual spices (except cardamom if you try), because they’re a lot of adjacent flavors and they merge. I think adding a bit more ghee and/or yogurt would work, depending on the desired sauciness. Next time I think I’ll try adding some additional Indian spices like cloves or turmeric.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Wow! This is so wildly good.  The smell of the onion paste and spices cooking is ethereal. Marinated overnight and took the meat off the bones for serving. Added some roasted peppers and cilantro to the suggested salad. Big dollops of yogurt in hot pita. Wowza!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this last night. Great flavor! I used bone-in chicken thigh, skinless. I crashed the cardamon, otherwise as written. A keeper.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Amazing flavor. Not as “wet” as other chicken kormas I’ve made, maybe because it’s a Bangladeshi variation. Either way, this dish was so good as is. It’s the perfect combo of creamy, sweet and spicy. Thank you Sohla!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could you please specify which type of green chili pepper to use? Looking extremely forward to making this dish. Thank you.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If you google Indian green chili you'll see images of a long, dark green chili, also called finger chilis sometimes. Those are the kind we typically use in our cooking. They are available at Asian markets. Only use these if you are comfortable with spicy food, and still best to deseed them to bring down the level of heat.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I would think you could easily use tempeh, but you may need to make up for the lack of fat and savoriness that would’ve come from the lamb.  So maybe some coconut oil, additional spice, and maybe add some other sort of flavorful vegetable to it to give it some extra oomph.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Could i serve this with polenta? I know this is a culture clash, but i’’m trying to go with what i have & I have pot of leftover polenta.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: If I made this again I would debone the chicken and cube it before cooking in sauce. The seasonings should be heightened and sauce volume increased. The way it is, the chicken is fairly bland. It may be authentic but I didn't really like it as written.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I didn’t even make this right, accidentally skipping a step and adding the onion purée to the marinade. It still had such an amazing depth of flavor I can’t stop thinking about it and will definitely make it again. Will try the correct version next time!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I cardamom pod is roughly 1/6 teaspoon of ground cardamom

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Want to replace the yoghurt with something non-dairy. What do you suggest?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Do you put the cardamom pods in whole, crush them and use seeds and husks or just the seeds?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: So disappointing...  I expected a burst of flavor in my mouth and this was very meh.  I wouldn't make again.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I made this with boneless chicken cut into large chunks. Had only about a pound of meat (recipe calls for 4). I halfed all other quantities and it turned out great. The onion pure is a super interesting approach to get a dish started. Had no idea!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: OkAy Sohla is a literal queen this was a hit no notes.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I adore this recipe as is. It’s absolutely perfect. Leftover make a delicious sandwich with white bread and Mayo, just as Sohla said.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
Enjoyed this as is using a whole cut up chicken. For my taste next time I think I will use all dark meat and use a whole pureed onion in the sauce, double the yogurt, bump up the spices a bit and a smaller onion to caramelize. (I'm a saucy gal). 
Overall, delicious!!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Up the spice and sauce portions - probably best doubled.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: FWIW, a karahi is similar to--but much smaller than--a wok.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made this with cut up boneless thighs and it was delicious. Don’t understand those saying it came out dry - mine was very saucy.  Used a jalapeño bc it’s what I had and somehow didn’t get any heat from it. Will also prob double down on spices next time bc they were more subtle than I thought they’d be. As long as you do a little mise en place, it’s perfect for my new category  “weeknight recipes  I can make while on the PTA zoom.”

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This was outstanding. I actually added a whole onion to the marinade (by mistake but turned out delicious) and doubled all the marinade ingredients so I would have more sauce. I pureed another onion for sautéing along with the spices, and separately caramelized another small onion to add in at the end. Otherwise followed using 3 lbs of skinless drumsticks. Kids and adults gobbled this up. Served with bombay potatoes; squash sautéed with cumin seeds, garlic and tomato paste; garlic naan and rice.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Easily the best thing I’ve made from NYT. We followed directions, used milk, loved it beyond words.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used a combination of breasts and thighs.  Breasts were large so I sliced them in half.  Used Serrano chilies since we like spicy.  Was delicious.  Added some peas at the end and served with rice.  I would make even more caramelized onions for the topping next time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I have been making triple batches of the two purees in the blender, and freezing the extra. Filling the blender up more like this seems to get me a smoother texture on the puree.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Honestly needed a splash or two of water once everything was in the pot, but that helped with the braise. Awesome recipe!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I liked this, but the resulting flavor was a bit surprising to me. The sauce was reminiscent of a  mildly spiced chicken gravy, and despite using lots of onions they somehow got lost. I think maybe I would double or triple the onion, caramelize longer, and then pull only half of it out as a topping, leaving the rest in the dish as it simmers to give a deeper umami to the stew. I also reduced for about 1.5 hrs instead of 30 minutes, and didn't need extra water—the chicken was super tender!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Would this work with a vegan meat like tempeh?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 1. Use chicken thighs, skinless and boneless. 2. Instead of coriander powder, substitute with poppy seeds, gently roasted and ground to a powder.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Must sautee chicken on medium high fire until you can't see any blood. Then turn the fire on low and simmer while covered.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Tried the recipe today and it came out very well. Used extra onion, ginger, cardamom and garlic as my boys like extra curry with their chappatis.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: It's so good. Thank you, Sohla!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Has anyone got any suggestions on how to do this as a vegetarian recipe?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Paneer should work

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Leave fried onions for topping only.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Used diced boneless thighs

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: 1/2 the salt! I didn't use as much as it called for, and it still nearly ruined the otherwise wonderful dish.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I can't eat chillis.  Could I cook this without the chillis?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Consider other peppers to add some heat. Used diced jalapeño with seeds and spine removed - - no heat effect.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: my family and myself found this blah. Easy to make all components ahead of time.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Who would have thought that such humble ingredients - onion, chicken, yoghurt and a fantastic spice mix would pack in so much depth of flavour? My only tweak was cheating by using bought crispy onions. Home cooking at its best.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Prepared as written.  Recipe was good but lacked complexity and was pretty mild (heat wise).

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This is a winner. I would recommend adding more yogurt to increase the amount of sauce, as written it's barely more than a marinade.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I cut the cinnamon by two thirds, added an extra tablespoon of chicken masala, and along with the chicken,  4 large tomatoes cut into quarters and then, during the 30 min simmer, added 4 good squirts of tomato paste. And at the end, I threw on a handful of freshly chopped cilantro. My first time (1) using a food processor, (2) making an NYT recipe, (3) cooking meat, and (4) making anything that wasn’t vegetarian South Indian food. What an amazing experience — thanks guys!!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Different and delicious!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: per others’ suggestions. I used 2 lb boneless skinless chicken thighs, slightly more than 1/2 cup of yogurt, 1 big onion for purée and 1 small onions for frying, 6 cloves of garlic, 2 inch of ginger, 1 cinnamon stick, 5 cardamom pods and 2 bay leaves. (not creamy buttery Korma)

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: About halfway through the 35 min cook I thought it was going wrong and starting to burn; I sloshed in a bunch of chicken broth, scraped up the brown bits and kept it cooking---it's fantastic. Served it with the NYT mint chutney. Worth blowing money on local grass fed ghee
